EDCS-05700034.translation: For the safety of the Augusti. Quintus Caecilius Fuscus, archigal(lus) of the colony of Ostia, gave as a gift a silver image of the Mother of the Gods, weighing 1 pound, with a statue of Nemesis and the standard-bearers of Ostia.
EDCS-05700034.summary: This inscription records the gift of a silver image of the Mother of the Gods and a statue of Nemesis by Quintus Caecilius Fuscus, an archigal(lus) of Ostia.

EDCS-05700035.translation: Quintus Caecilius Fuscus, archigal(lus) of the colony of Ostia, gave as a gift a silver image of Attis, weighing 1 pound, with a bronze statuette of Phryges and the standard-bearers of Ostia.
EDCS-05700035.summary: This inscription records the gift of a silver image of Attis and a bronze statuette by Quintus Caecilius Fuscus, an archigal(lus) of Ostia.

EDCS-05700036.translation: Calpurnia Chelido gave as a gift and dedicated a silver statue of the Mother of the Gods, weighing 2 pounds, for the standard-bearers of Ostia, and dedicated it.
EDCS-05700036.summary: This inscription records the gift and dedication of a silver statue of the Mother of the Gods by Calpurnia Chelido for the standard-bearers of Ostia.

EDCS-05700037.translation: Quintus Domitius Aterianus the father and Domitia Civitas the mother gave a statue of Attis to the standard-bearers of Ostia as a gift.
EDCS-05700037.summary: This inscription records a statue of Attis given as a gift to the standard-bearers of Ostia by Quintus Domitius Aterianus and Domitia Civitas.

EDCS-05700038.translation: To the divine power of Attis. Gaius Cartilius Euplus, by the command of the goddess.
EDCS-05700038.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Attis by Gaius Cartilius Euplus, following a divine revelation.

EDCS-05700039.translation: Aemilia Serapias performed a taurobolium and erected a tauroboliate altar, through the priests Valerius Pancarpus and Anullinus II and Fronto, consuls, on the Ides of May.
EDCS-05700039.summary: This inscription records a taurobolium ritual and the erection of an altar performed by Aemilia Serapias during the consulship of Anullinus II and Fronto (likely 211 CE).

EDCS-05700048.translation: To the great god Helios Sarapis and the companion gods, Lucius Cassius Eutyches, custodian of the great Sarapis, dedicated the foundation, a silver torch, three altars, a many-lighted censer, and two pedestals, out of gratitude, with the permission of the pontifex of Vulcan and sacred buildings, Caius Nasennius Marcellus, and of the IIvir(s) Quintus Lollius Chrysidianus and Marcus Aemilius Vitalis Crepereianus.
EDCS-05700048.summary: A Greek inscription from Ostia recording the dedication of religious furnishings to Helios Sarapis and other gods by a temple custodian, with the consent of local officials, likely dating to the late 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700051.translation: Sacred to Silvanus. Successus, vicarius of Agathemerus, freedman of the Emperor Titus Caesar Augustus, placed this vow.
EDCS-05700051.summary: A Latin inscription from Ostia recording a vow dedicated to Silvanus by a subordinate official, likely dating to the Flavian period (late 1st century CE).

EDCS-05700052.translation: A vow to Silvanus. A sacred altar to the Augustan Annona, to the Genius [---], to Sacomar[ius?]. Publius Aelius Syneros, freedman of the freedman of the Emperor, Publius Aelius Trophimus, procurator of the province of Crete, and his sons Trophimus and Aelianus, [dedicated this] by decree of the decurions. Dedicated on the Kalends of October, when Manius Acilius Glabrio and Gaius Bellicus Torquatus were consuls.
EDCS-05700052.summary: A Latin inscription from Ostia recording the dedication of an altar to Silvanus, the Annona, and other deities by a freedman of a provincial procurator, dated by its consuls to 155 CE.

EDCS-05700054.translation: Felix, freedman of Gaius Atilius Bassus, the priest, apparitor of the Mother of the Gods Magna, donated a statue of Silvanus to the Ostians, with his dendrophores.
EDCS-05700054.summary: A Latin inscription from Ostia recording the donation of a statue of Silvanus by an apparitor of the Mother of the Gods Magna, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05700057.translation: Lucius Agrius Calendio gave a gift to Sol Invictus Mithras. Lucius Agrius Calendio gave a gift to Sol Invictus Mithras.
EDCS-05700057.summary: A Latin inscription from Ostia recording a gift to Sol Invictus Mithras by Lucius Agrius Calendio, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05700058.translation: Caius Coelius Hermeros, priest of this place, made [this] with his own money.
EDCS-05700058.summary: A Latin inscription from Ostia recording the self-funded construction by Caius Coelius Hermeros, the local priest, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05700059.translation: Placed on the 18th day before the Kalends of February, when Quintus Junius Rusticus and Lucius Plautius Aquilinus were consuls. Caius Coelius Hermeros, priest of this place, made [this] with his own money. Caius Coelius Hermeros, priest of this place, made [this] with his own money.
EDCS-05700059.summary: A Latin inscription from Ostia recording the self-funded construction by Caius Coelius Hermeros, the local priest, dated by its consuls to 169 CE.

EDCS-05700060.translation: Aulus Decimius Decimianus, son of Aulus of the Palatina tribe, restored [this] with his own money.
EDCS-05700060.summary: A Latin inscription from Ostia recording the self-funded restoration by Aulus Decimius Decimianus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05700061.translation: Aulus Decimius Decimianus, son of Aulus of the Palatina tribe, restored the temple with its portico, the god Sol Mithras himself, with its marble furnishings and all its cultic apparatus, with his own money.
EDCS-05700061.summary: A Latin inscription from Ostia recording the self-funded restoration of a temple and its furnishings dedicated to Sol Mithras by Aulus Decimius Decimianus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05700062.translation: Lucius Tullius Agatho dedicated a sacrificial altar to the unconquered god Sol Mithras and consecrated it in honor of the god, under Marcus Aemilius Epaphroditus, father.
EDCS-05700062.summary: A Latin inscription from Ostia recording the dedication of a sacrificial altar to Sol Mithras by Lucius Tullius Agatho, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05700063.translation: To Marcus Aemilius Epaphroditus, father and priest.
EDCS-05700063.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Aemilius Epaphroditus, honoring him as father and priest.

EDCS-05700064.translation: Gaius Valerius Heracles, priest, and Lucius Sextius Karus, paid for this statue of the god of the unseen with their own money.
EDCS-05700064.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue of an unseen god by the priest Gaius Valerius Heracles and Lucius Sextius Karus.

EDCS-05700065.translation: Gaius Valerius Heracles, father, and Gaius Valerii Vitalis and Nicomes, priests, set this up with their own money; dedicated on the Ides of August, during the consulship of Emperor Commodus, and VI and Septimianus.
EDCS-05700065.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by the father Gaius Valerius Heracles and the priests Gaius Valerii Vitalis and Nicomes, during the consulship of Commodus and Septimianus.

EDCS-05700066.translation: Gaius Valerius Heracles, father and leader of the priests of the divine, incorrupt, unconquered Sol Mithras, [dedicated] the crypt of the palace, granted to him by Marcus Aurelius, [---].
EDCS-05700066.summary: This fragmented inscription likely records Gaius Valerius Heracles, as father and leader of priests, dedicating a crypt associated with the cult of Sol Invictus Mithras, granted by Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-05700067.translation: Sextus Annius Merops, honored dendrophore of the people of Ostia, gave and dedicated this statue of the Mother Earth as a gift on the 13th day before the Kalends of May, during the consulship of Lucius Cuspius Rufinus and Lucius Staius Quadratus.
EDCS-05700067.summary: This inscription records Sextus Annius Merops, a dendrophore, dedicating a statue of Terra Mater in Ostia during the consulship of Rufinus and Quadratus.

EDCS-05700069.translation: Virtue, from 2 pounds of silver. Iunia Zosime, mother, gave as a gift.
EDCS-05700069.summary: This inscription records Iunia Zosime, identified as a mother, donating a representation of Virtue made from 2 pounds of silver.

EDCS-05700070.translation: [---] gave as a gift to Marcus Cerellius Hieronymus, his father and priest, and the aforementioned leader willingly dedicated them to the god.
EDCS-05700070.summary: This fragmented inscription records a gift being made to a father and priest, Marcus Cerellius Hieronymus, who was also a leader.

EDCS-05700071.translation: [---] quinquennial and patron of the body of dendrophores of Ostia, dedicated with their own money 3 pounds of silver on the 8th day before the Kalends of May, during the consulship of Domitius Dexter (for the second time) and Thrasia Priscus.
EDCS-05700071.summary: This inscription records a patron of the Ostian dendrophores dedicating 3 pounds of silver during the consulship of Domitius Dexter and Thrasia Priscus.

EDCS-05700073.translation: By order, Quintus Ostiensis Felix, caretaker of the temple of Rome and the Emperors, made this.
EDCS-05700073.summary: This inscription records Quintus Ostiensis Felix, the caretaker of a temple dedicated to Rome and the Emperors, commissioning a work by imperial order.

EDCS-05700083.translation: To Germanicus Caesar, son of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, by public decree of the decurions.
EDCS-05700083.summary: This inscription records a public dedication to Germanicus Caesar, son of Tiberius, decreed by the local council of decurions.

EDCS-05700085.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Drusi filius Caesar Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VI consul designatus IIII imperator XII pater patriae fossis ductis a Tiberi operis portus caussa emissisque in mare urbem inundationis periculo liberavit\n
EDCS-05700085.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, was pontifex maximus, held tribunician power for the 6th time, was consul designate for the 4th time, was acclaimed imperator for the 12th time, and was father of the fatherland. By digging canals from the Tiber and letting them out into the sea, he freed the city from the danger of flooding.
EDCS-05700085.summary: This inscription records the achievements of Emperor Claudius in creating port facilities and preventing floods.

EDCS-05700098.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II consul thermas in quarum exstructionem divos pater suus HS XXviginties centena milia pollicitus erat adiecta pecunia quanto ampliuus desiderabatur item marmoribus ad omnem cultum fecit\n
EDCS-05700098.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, held tribunician power for the 2nd time, was consul, and completed the construction of the baths, for which his divine father had promised twenty million sesterces, by adding the additional funds that were needed, as well as providing all the necessary marble.
EDCS-05700098.summary: This inscription records the completion of public baths by Emperor Antoninus Pius, funded by his predecessor and supplemented by his own funds.

EDCS-05700099.translation: To Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, held tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, by the shipowners of Carthage from Africa.
EDCS-05700099.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Antoninus Pius by the shipowners of Carthage, acknowledging his titles and status.

EDCS-05700105.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, son of the deified Antoninus Pius, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, celebrated as Armenicus, Parthicus, Maximus, and Medicus, held tribunician power for the 6th time, was imperator for the 5th time, consul designate for the 3rd time, proconsul, by the guild of carpenters and timber framers of Ostia, which [offers this] through the providence and generosity of the most indulgent princeps.
EDCS-05700105.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Lucius Verus by the guild of carpenters and timber framers of Ostia, acknowledging his titles and their gratitude for his benevolence.

EDCS-05700107.translation: Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus. Quintus Fabius Quintus' son Honoratus, for the honor of the exemption, gave this gift as a dendrophore of Ostia.
EDCS-05700107.summary: This is a dedication by Quintus Fabius Honoratus to Lucius Verus, as a dendrophore, possibly related to an exemption he received.

EDCS-05700109.translation: For the health of Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus. Marcus Aurelius, freedman of the emperor, Armentarius, gave this gift.
EDCS-05700109.summary: This is a votive offering for the health of Emperor Commodus, dedicated by his freedman Armentarius.

EDCS-05700111.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning the deified emperor Pertinax.

EDCS-05700112.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the deified Commodus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, held tribunician power for the 4th time, imperator for the 8th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-05700112.summary: This inscription lists the extensive titles and lineage of Emperor Septimius Severus.

EDCS-05700113.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filius divi Commodi frater divi Antonini Pii nepos divi Hadriani pronepos divi Traiani Parthici abnepos divi Nervae adnepos Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate IIII imperator VIII consul II pater patriae columnam vii sic tempestatis confractam restituit\n
EDCS-05700113.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the deified Commodus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, held tribunician power for the 4th time, imperator for the 8th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, restored a column which had been broken by the storm.
EDCS-05700113.summary: This inscription records Emperor Septimius Severus restoring a storm-damaged column, listing his full imperial titles.

EDCS-05700116.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus. The body of cannophori (reed bearers) of Ostia, one pound of silver.
EDCS-05700116.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Septimius Severus by the cannophori of Ostia, including a silver offering.

EDCS-05700117.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-05700117.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, the guild of cane-bearers of Ostia, one pound of silver.
EDCS-05700117.summary: This inscription records a donation of silver by the guild of cane-bearers of Ostia to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, likely in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700118.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 8th time, Imperator for the 4th time, Consul for the 2nd time, Father of the Fatherland. Stratonicus, a cane-bearer, erected and dedicated this at his own expense.
EDCS-05700118.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Stratonicus, a cane-bearer, to Emperor Septimius Severus, likely during his reign (193-211 CE).

EDCS-05700119.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-05700119.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix, son of Severus, Augustus. Gaius Caesius Eutychion, immune from taxes, gave this gift to the cane-bearers of Ostia, weighing one pound of silver and eight items. On the day of its dedication, he gave bread, wine, and one denarius. Dedicated on the day before the Nones of April, when Asper and Asper were consuls.
EDCS-05700119.summary: This inscription records a gift of silver and provisions by Gaius Caesius Eutychion to the cane-bearers of Ostia, with the date specified by the consulship of Asper and Asper (likely in the reign of Marcus Aurelius, 161-180 CE).

EDCS-05700125.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Severus Alexander Pius Augustus and Julia Mamaea, mother of our lord and of the camps and of the entire divine house. A station for the unit of grain-distributors, a place assigned by Agricola, freedman of Augustus, procurator of both ports, and by Petronius Maximus, centurion of the annona, and by Fabius Maro, centurion. Dedicated the works on the III Nones of August, when Appius Claudius Iulianus and Bruttius Crispinus were consuls. By Quintus Turranius Masila, patron, with Publius Flavius Iunior, son of Flavius, and Valerius Donatus as curators.
EDCS-05700125.summary: This inscription records the assignment of a location for the grain distributors' unit and the dedication of public works during the reign of Severus Alexander and Julia Mamaea, with specific officials named, dated to 12 August 231 CE.

EDCS-05700126.translation: The Emperors Caesar Marcus Aurelius Carinus and Marcus Numerius Numerianus, Pius, Felix, Invicti, Augusti, Germanici Maximi, Britannici Maximi, Persici Maximi, with tribunician power, consuls, fathers of the fatherland, proconsuls, restored the bridge to the people of Laurentum and Ostia, which had long since collapsed from age, with stone.
EDCS-05700126.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a stone bridge by the co-emperors Carinus and Numerian, emphasizing their titles and the bridge's dilapidated state, likely in the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700132.text_edition: \n Quinto Vettio Postumio Constantio pontifici Volkani et aedium sacrarum ddominis nnostris Diocletiano Augusto III <e=I>t Maximiano Augusto cons(ulibus) \n
EDCS-05700132.translation: To Quintus Vettius Postumius Constantius, pontiff of Vulcan and of sacred buildings, during the consulship of our lords Diocletian Augustus for the 3rd time and Maximian Augustus.
EDCS-05700132.summary: This inscription honors Quintus Vettius Postumius Constantius, a pontiff, with the date specified by the consulship of Diocletian and Maximian, likely around 290 CE.

EDCS-05700155.translation: To Marcus Acilius Priscus, son of Aulus, grandson of Aulus Voturia, of the Voturia tribe, and Egrilius Plarianus. Commissioner of roads, tribune of the V Macedonica legion, quaestor urbanus, plebeian aedile, praetor, legate of the provinces of Sicily and Asia, proconsul of the province of Gallia Narbonensis, legate of the legio VIII Augusta. Lucius Vettius Felix and Publius Novellius Atticus, friends.
EDCS-05700155.summary: This inscription honors Marcus Acilius Priscus, detailing his extensive public career from local official to provincial governor and legionary legate, with the monument erected by his friends Lucius Vettius Felix and Publius Novellius Atticus.

EDCS-05700156.text_edition: \n Aulus Egrilius Plarianus pater patronus coloniae praefectus aerario Saturni\n
EDCS-05700156.translation: Aulus Egrilius Plarianus, father, patron of the colony, prefect of the treasury of Saturn.
EDCS-05700156.summary: This inscription honors Aulus Egrilius Plarianus, identifying him as a father, patron of the colony, and prefect of the treasury of Saturn.

EDCS-05700161.translation: To the divine shades. To Tiberius Claudius Antoninus, son of Tiberius, prefect of the V Ulpia Petraeorum cohort, who lived 21 years, 6 months, and 2 days.
EDCS-05700161.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Tiberius Claudius Antoninus, prefect of a cohort, at the age of 21, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700163.translation: To the divine shades. Titus Flavius Claudianus. By his son Titus Flavius Claudianus, to his father and master, haruspex of the LX (schola?).
EDCS-05700163.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication by Titus Flavius Claudianus to his father and master, who served as a haruspex (diviner), likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700165.translation: To Titus Flavius Verus, son of Titus, of the Palatine tribe, a Roman knight. Vibussia Sabina, daughter of Lucius, mother, who orders that she be placed in this tomb and her husband Gnaeus Ostius Hermes, who is not my heir nor the heir of my heirs, nor shall it be permitted to anyone to place in this tomb either body or bones. If anyone acts against this, he shall pay 50,000 sesterces to the aerarium of the Roman people, and likewise 50,000 sesterces to the treasury of the people of Ostia; and he who reports shall receive the sums written above. The hypogeum and the rest are for my freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants. This external monument does not follow the heir, nor can it be made by donation. Frontage: 26 feet. In the field: 35 feet.
EDCS-05700165.summary: This is a funerary monument for Titus Flavius Verus, established by his mother Vibussia Sabina, who also specified burial and inheritance conditions and penalties for violations.

EDCS-05700167.translation: To Gaius Julius Philippus, a Roman knight. The association of naval dockworkers of Ostia, which is permitted to assemble by decree of the Senate, erected this with their own funds. Dedicated on the 12th day before the Kalends of April, when Tertullus and Clemens were consuls. Managed by Calocaerus, freedman, and Gaius Vettius Optatus, Marcus Clodius Minerva... as quinquennial officials in perpetuity.
EDCS-05700167.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by the naval dockworkers of Ostia to their patron Gaius Julius Philippus, specifying the consuls and quinquennial officials responsible for its management.

EDCS-05700168.translation: To Publius Martius Philippus, of the Quirina tribe, curator of the Praenestine road, curule aedile, quaestorian viator, from the treasury, tribune of the naval dockworkers of Portus, the association of naval dockworkers of Ostia, which is permitted to assemble by decree of the Senate, to their most excellent patron. Erected with their own funds. Dedicated on the 12th day before the Kalends of April, when Scapula Tertullus and Tineius Clemens were consuls. Managed by Calocaerus, freedman, and Gaius Vettius Optatus, Marcus Clodius Minerva... as quinquennial officials in perpetuity.
EDCS-05700168.summary: This inscription honors Publius Martius Philippus, listing his various public offices, by the naval dockworkers of Ostia as their patron, and records the dedication date and managing officials.

EDCS-05700170.translation: To Gaius Nasennius Marcellus the elder, son of Gaius, prefect of the I Apamena cohort, tribune of the I Italica cohort of Roman citizen volunteers, prefect of the Phrygian ala, prefect of the dockworkers, aedile, quaestor, duumvir for five years, curator of public works and aqueducts in perpetuity, praetor and pontiff of the Laurentes Lavinates, patron of the colony of Ostia. Nasennia Helpis erected this for her most indulgent patron and for her dearest husband Gaius Nasennius Saturninus, for herself, her children, freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-05700170.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Nasennia Helpis for her patron and husband, Gaius Nasennius Marcellus, detailing his extensive public career and her family.

EDCS-05700171.translation: To Quintus Petronius Melior, son of Quintus, procurator of the grain supply, assistant to the curator of the Tiber riverbed and sewers, curator of the public affairs of Saenae, praetor of the 15 peoples of Etruria, twice military tribune of the III Gallica legion, quaestorian scribe, first chief praetor of the Laurentes Lavinates, quinquennial duumvir of Faesulae, pontiff of Faesulae and Florence. The association of grain measurers of Ostia. The site was granted by public decree of the decurions. Dedicated on the 3rd day before the Nones of February, when Lucius Eggius Marullus and Gnaeus Papirius Aelianus were consuls. The site was assigned by Gaius Nasennius Marcellus, perpetual curator of public works.
EDCS-05700171.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a site by the association of grain measurers of Ostia to Quintus Petronius Melior, detailing his numerous official positions and the consuls and curator involved in the dedication.

EDCS-05700175.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Ulpius Probus, freedman of the Augusti, procurator of the province of Pannonia Superior and Africa, in the district of Theveste. He lived 71 years, 5 months, and 13 days. Ulpia Probitas, daughter of Marcus, his stepdaughter and heir, for one who deserved well.
EDCS-05700175.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Ulpius Probus, a procurator, erected by his stepdaughter and heir Ulpia Probitas, stating his age and previous positions.

EDCS-05700185.translation: To the departed spirits. Honored at public expense by the Emperor on the 3rd day, number 3. Gaius Julius Aithales made [this].
EDCS-05700185.summary: This fragmented inscription records a funerary monument erected by Gaius Julius Aithales, noting imperial funding and the number of days or individuals involved.

EDCS-05700187.translation: Also to Laurentius Lavinas, scribe of the curule aediles, bidentary priest, neocoros of Jupiter Magnus Sarapis. Fundania Priscilla, daughter of Publius, erected this for her most excellent husband and for herself.
EDCS-05700187.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument erected by Fundania Priscilla for her husband, who held several religious and administrative roles, and for herself.

EDCS-05700197.translation: To the departed spirits. To Titus Flavius Botryon and Januarius, servant of Caesar, a homeborn slave and steward, his husband. Caesetia Lanthanusa erected this for her well-deserving father and son.
EDCS-05700197.summary: This funerary epitaph is dedicated by Caesetia Lanthanusa to her husband Titus Flavius Botryon and her son Januarius, noting their status as slaves and Januarius's role as steward.

EDCS-05700198.translation: To the departed spirits. To Titus Flavius Olympicus, from the praetorium, steward. He lived 40 years. Licinia Fortunata erected this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-05700198.summary: This funerary epitaph is for Titus Flavius Olympicus, a steward from the praetorium, who lived 40 years, erected by his wife Licinia Fortunata.

EDCS-05700202.translation: To the departed spirits. Liberalis, freedman of Augustus, made this for Terentius, the actor, who lived 22 years, a deserving person.
EDCS-05700202.summary: This is a tombstone for Terentius, an actor, erected by his former slave Liberalis, a freedman of the emperor, likely in the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700207.translation: To the departed spirits. Aelius Aventinus, equestrian soldier of the emperor, of the turma of Genialis in the colony of Ara Augusta Claudia, lived 29 years, served 11 years.
EDCS-05700207.summary: This tombstone commemorates Aelius Aventinus, an equestrian soldier, who died after 11 years of service and 29 years of life.

EDCS-05700208.translation: Marcus Antonius Secundus Veter, son of Marcus, of the Voltinian tribe, and Antonia Euhodia, made this for themselves and for their sons Marcus Antonius Vindicus and Antonia Secundina, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants. In front 8 feet, in the field 6 feet.
EDCS-05700208.summary: This funerary monument was commissioned by Marcus Antonius Secundus Veter and Antonia Euhodia for themselves and their children and descendants, specifying its dimensions.

EDCS-05700209.translation: To the departed spirits. Antonia Euhodia made this for her most beloved son, Lucius Antonius Theodorus, who lived 7 years and 6 months.
EDCS-05700209.summary: This tombstone is dedicated by Antonia Euhodia to her son Lucius Antonius Theodorus, who died at a young age.

EDCS-05700212.translation: To the departed spirits. Of Caelus Felix, the veteran.
EDCS-05700212.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caelus Felix, a military veteran.

EDCS-05700214.translation: To the departed spirits. Valeria Euplia made this for her husband, Caius Clodius Fuscus, veteran of the VI Praetorian cohort, and for herself.
EDCS-05700214.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Valeria Euplia for her deceased husband, Caius Clodius Fuscus, a veteran of the VI Praetorian cohort, and for herself.

EDCS-05700219.translation: In memory of Caius Licinius Licinianus, a tesserarius of the Praetorians. Aegrilia Probiana, his wife of incomparable quality, [with?] her own money.
EDCS-05700219.summary: This inscription honors Caius Licinius Licinianus, a Praetorian guard, erected by his wife Aegrilia Probiana.

EDCS-05700221.translation: To the departed spirits. Ermes, foster-child of Publius Palaius Romanus, the veteran of the emperors, made this for him, a deserving person.
EDCS-05700221.summary: This tombstone is dedicated by Ermes, the foster-child of the imperial veteran Publius Palaius Romanus.

EDCS-05700222.translation: For Lucius Pedanius Felix, son of Spurius, of the Pollian tribe, from Vardacate, a soldier of the VI Praetorian cohort, who served [3] years, lived [? years].
EDCS-05700222.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Pedanius Felix, a soldier in the VI Praetorian cohort, indicating his origin and length of service.

EDCS-05700223.translation: To the departed spirits. The heir made this according to the will for Titus Sempronius Flavus, soldier of the II Praetorian cohort, centuria of Firmius, who lived 38 years, served 6 years.
EDCS-05700223.summary: This tombstone records the death of Titus Sempronius Flavus, a soldier in the II Praetorian cohort, erected by his heir as specified in his will.

EDCS-05700224.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Sextilius Donatus, father, and Sextilia Maior, mother, made this for their most beloved son, Sextilius Ianuarius, a soldier of the 11th Urban Cohort. He lived for 24 years.
EDCS-05700224.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by parents for their son, a soldier in the urban cohort.

EDCS-05700234.translation: Gaius Domitius Pollio, a veteran from the praetorian fleet of Misenum, made this for himself and for Domitia Pia, daughter of Gaius, and for his freedwoman Domitia Spei, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants. The left side of this monument, for those entering, belongs to the aforementioned family. In front, 16 feet; in depth, 39 feet.
EDCS-05700234.summary: An epitaph and boundary marker for a veteran of the Misenum fleet, detailing property allocation for his family and freedmen.

EDCS-05700235.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Flavius Valens, a soldier of the praetorian fleet of Misenum. He served for 6 years. Of Bessian origin.
EDCS-05700235.summary: A funerary epitaph for a soldier of the Misenum fleet, noting his nationality and service duration.

EDCS-05700237.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Licinius Capito, of Pannonian origin. He lived for 63 years and served for 45 years. From the praetorian fleet of Misenum, a helmsman.
EDCS-05700237.summary: A funerary epitaph for a helmsman in the Misenum fleet, detailing his lifespan, service, and origin.

EDCS-05700238.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Lusius Rufus, a trireme rower in the 'Mercury', a soldier of the praetorian fleet of Misenum. Of Egyptian origin.
EDCS-05700238.summary: A funerary epitaph for a soldier in the Misenum fleet, identifying his role as a trireme rower and his origin.

EDCS-05700239.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Valerius Festus, a soldier of the praetorian fleet of Misenum, of Bessian origin. He lived for 40 years and served for 21 years.
EDCS-05700239.summary: A funerary epitaph for a soldier of the Misenum fleet, noting his nationality, lifespan, and service duration.

EDCS-05700241.translation: A soldier of the praetorian fleet of Misenum, a trireme rower in the 'Sol'. Of Sardinian origin. He lived for 43 years and served for 19 years. Titus Flavius Urbatius Aristo.
EDCS-05700241.summary: A funerary epitaph for a soldier and trireme rower in the Misenum fleet, indicating his origin and service details.

EDCS-05700245.translation: Under the emperors Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, Father of the Country, and Marcus Aelius Aurelius Caesar, consuls. The college of corporati, who contributed money for the expansion of the temple, [are patrons]. Titus Prifernius Paetus Rosianus Geminus, son of Titus, son of Sextus. Marcus Stlaccius Albinus. Trebellius Sallustius Rufus. Marcus Sedatius Severianus, son of Gaius, son of Gaius. Titus Prifernius Paetus Rosianus Geminus, son of Titus. Marcus Sedatius Severus Iulius Reginus, son of Marcus, son of Marcus. Gaius Allius Fuscianus, son of Gaius. Titus Statilius Taurus, son of Titus. Tiberius Aterius Saturninus. Gaius Pantuleius Graptiacus. Gaius Allius Fuscus, son of Gaius. Gaius Sossius Benedictus, perpetual quinquennial. Aulus Egrilius Augustalis, quinquennial. Publius Sulpicius Hera, quinquennial. Lucius Naevius Saecularis, quinquennial, Quintilius and consul. Aulus Egrilius Faustus, quinquennial, Silvanus and Augurinus, consuls. Titus Fisevius Nicephorus, quinquennial, Lucius Naevius Proculus, Laelianus and Pastor, consuls. Titus Fisevius Priscianus, quinquennial, Clarus and Cethegus, consuls. Marcus Magius Marsus, quinquennial, Maximus and Orfitus, consuls. Lucius Helvius Restitutus, Aulus Egrilius Faustus. [He bequeathed] 4,000 sesterces by his will, on the condition that from the interest of the aforementioned sum, on the 5th day before the Kalends of December each year, they should feast. Publius Cincius Apelles. Lucius Naevius Syriacus. Titus Fisevius Felix. Publius Caetennius Epaphroditus. Lucius Munatius Trophimus. Aulus Egrilius Successus. Aulus Egrilius Zora. Aulus Atinius Proculus. Aulus Egrilius Hilarus. Marcus Apuleius Comitianus. Decimus Publicius Martialis. Aulus Egrilius Eutychus. Aulus Egrilius Urbanus. Lucius Naevius Proculus. Quintus Kaninius Crispus. Lucius Naevius Fortunatus. Lucius Bellenius Geminus. Publius Ennius Vitalis. Gaius Rammius Iustus. Gaius Sossius Hermes. Publius Cincius Apelles, son of Publius. Aulus Lucceius Nominatus. Aulus Lucceius Lycomedes. Lucius Naevius Felix. Aulus Egrilius Earinus. Marcus Appuleius Urbicus. Lucius Helvius Sulpicius Restitutus. Publius Varenius Zethus. Aulus Egrilius Hilario. Publius Cincius Florus. Gaius Iulius Corvinus. Tiberius Iulius Epagathus. Aulus Egrilius Chryseros. Lucius Sulfius Benivolus. Lucius Naevius Urbanus. Publius Mucius Fructuosus. Lucius Naevius Apelles. Publius Nonius Hermes. Lucius Sulfius Amfion. Publius Octavius Nynphodotus. Aulus Egrilius Secundus. Titus Fisevius Felix. Gaius Caelius Successus. EDCS-05700245.summary: An extensive list of patrons, quinquennials, and members of a collegium who contributed to the expansion of a temple, with a record of a bequest for annual feasts.

EDCS-05700246.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Antoninus Augustus [...]. Patrons: Titus Prifernius Paetus Rosianus Geminius, son of Titus, of the tribe Quirina. Marcus Sedatius Severianus Iulius Rufinus, son of Gaius, of the tribe Quirina. Marcus Sedatius Severus Iulius Reginus, son of Marcus, of the tribe Quirina. Quinquennials: Decimus Volusius Secundus. Decimus Veturius Lucrio. Marcus Suellius Perpetualis. Corporati: Quintus Vennonius [...]. Marcus Marius [...]. Titus Pompeius [...]. Publius Mummius [...]. Publius Curtius S [...]. Publius Curtius [...]. Marcus Segulius [...]. Titus Helleniu [...]. Titus Hellenius [...]. Gnaeus Turpilius [...]. Aulus Avillius Sab [...]. Publius Curtius Pa [...]. Publius Anteius Sili [...]. Publius Curtius Fel [...]. Lucius Domitius Fa [...]. Marcus Lucceius Ni [...]. Publius Anteius M [...].
EDCS-05700246.summary: A list of patrons, quinquennials, and members of a collegium, likely related to temple maintenance or civic duties, during the reign of Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-05700248.translation: Aulus Iulius Au[...od]us. Statius Priscus. Matuccius Fuscinus. Caelius Fuscian[us]. [...us] Priscus. [...s] Florus.
EDCS-05700248.summary: A fragmented list of names, possibly from a funerary monument or a record of officials.

EDCS-05700249.translation: In the consulship of Manius Acilius Glabrio and Marcus Valerius Homulus, the guild of the boatmen and clerks of the auxiliary forces of Ostia, [honors their] patrons: Marcus Sedatius Severianus, son of Gaius; Titus Prifernius Paetus Rosianus Geminus, son of Sextus; Marcus Sedatius Severus, son of Marcus, Julius Reginus; Gaius Allius Fuscianus, son of Gaius. [Also elected as] quinquennial perpetuus: Marcus Cornelius Epagathus. [Elected as] quinquennial: Marcus Antistius Helius. [Elected as] quinquennial: Marcus Cornelius Secundus, son of Marcus. [Members of the] plebs: Manius Lollius Marinus, Gaius Septimius Trophimus, Lucius Valerius Severus, Gaius Vatronius Iunianus, Manius Lollius Crispinus, Lucius Aebutius Cosmus, Gaius Julius Agathemerus, Marcus Cornelius Epigonus, Lucius Fulvius Cinnamus, Gaius Cornelius Felix, Publius Fuficius Felix, Marcus Cornelius Felix. [Also listed are:] Lucius Julius Memor, Marcus Cornelius Secundus, Titus Cornelius Felix, Aulus Herennuleius Fructus, Lucius Cominius Caecilianus, Lucius Valerius Proculus, Gaius Cornelius Daphnus, Gaius Baberius Marcellinus, Tiberius Claudius Epaphroditus, Lucius Sercudius Proculus, Gaius Tuccius Antiochus, Gaius Masurius Hyginus, Gnaeus Tutilius Martialis, Marcus Cornelius Capito, Marcus Cipius Natalis, Lucius Publisidius Phoebus, Gaius Julius Amaranthus, Titus Cornelius Paulinianus, Marcus Publicius Primitivus, Titus Cornelius Felix, Aulus Herennuleius Proculus, Publius Fuficius Felix the son of Publius, Lucius Furius Marcellus. EDCS-05700249.summary: This inscription lists the patrons, quinquennials, and members of the guild of Ostian boatmen and clerks, dating to the consulship of Acilius Glabrio and Valerius Homulus (likely AD 152).

EDCS-05700250.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Augustus [and in the second consulship of Publius Helvius Pertinax, under Emperor Caesar Lucius Aelius Aurelius Commodus Augustus, for the seventh time], the guild of Ostian boatmen and clerks [honors their] senatorial patrons: Lucius Fabius Cilo Septiminus; Marcus Umbilius Maximinus. [Also listed as] Roman knights: Lucius Furius Marcellus (father) and Lucius Furius Marcellinus (son), Marcus Cornelius Valerianus, Sextus Sextilius Iulianus (son of Sextus) and Sextus Sextilius Iulianus (father), Lucius Valerius Daphnus. [Also listed as] quinquennial perpetui: Marcus Publicius Ianuarius, Marcus Publicius Ostiensis senior, Marcus Cornelius Valerianus, Lucius Valerius Daphnus, Marcus Cipius Victor, Sextus Sextilius Iulianus (father). [Also listed as] quinquennials: Marcus Curtius Victorinus, Aulus Herenuleius Vettianus. EDCS-05700250.summary: This inscription, dated to the second consulship of Pertinax (AD 193), records the patrons, knights, quinquennials, and members of the guild of Ostian boatmen and clerks.

EDCS-05700254.translation: The public familia [of the guild of Ostian boatmen and clerks]. Ostian Hermes, clerk; Dionysius, clerk; Euaristus, clerk; Ostian Eutychus; Ostian Asclepiades; Ostian Liberalis; Ostian Primion; Ostian Polygonus; Faustus; Ostian Epafroditus; Ostian Syntrophus; Successus; Ostian Hermes; Ostian Baeticus; Ostian Alcibiades; Ostian Gemellus; Ostian Eutychus; Ostian Callistratus; Ostian Aciva; Marcius Hermes; Mucius Maximus; Mucius Vitalis; Ostian Palmesis; Marcius Chrysostomus; Mamius Rufus; Ostian Claudianus; Plutius Asclepiades; Helius; Eros; Philetus; Ostian Heliodorus; Vetulanius Felix; Mamidius Euaristus; Vadius Menander; Ostian Chrysippus; Trophimus; Zoticus; Ostian Agathemerianus; Clemens; Ostian Apollonius; Metrodorus; Ostian Gaius. Onesimus; Ostian Callistus; Geminius Trophimianus; Ostian Appianus; Vetulenius Primion; Mamidia Hygia; Ostian Sabinus; Mumius Lucius; Onesimus; Ostian Sanctus; Epictetus; Eufras; Ostian Tertullinus; Rutilius Charito; Aemilius Caelistinus; Fabricius Heliodorus; Ostian Hermetianus; Petilius; Trophimus; Euphenus; Messenius Secundus; Clodius Marcianus; Paulinus; Ostian Dryas; Ostian Aquilinus; Ostian Apollonius; Apolaustus; Sophron; Valerius Felix; Dativus, Vilicus; Mucius Ampliatus; Mucius Ianuarius; Ostian Primus; Ostian Asclepiades; Ostian Eutyches; Ostian Rufinus; Mar Filocyrus; Publius Vedius Epaphroditus; Publius Veius Callistratus.
EDCS-05700254.summary: This inscription lists the members of the 'public familia' of the Ostian boatmen and clerks, likely serving in administrative or clerical roles.

EDCS-05700283.translation: Antonin[us...], quinquennials [who held the office of] custos cannophorum Ostiensium: Flavius Epigonus.
EDCS-05700283.summary: This inscription names a quinquennial official, possibly named Antoninus, who held the position of custos cannophorum (keeper of the pole-bearers) for the Ostian guild, possibly in the Antonine period.

EDCS-05700285.translation: [List of names, possibly members of a guild or religious group:] Glycon(?), Rufus, Dafinus, Sinplicius, Petrus, Crescentinus, Crescentio, Bictorus, Dafinus, Feston, Serpentius, Apronianus, Alexander, Macuca. Leo made [this].
EDCS-05700285.summary: This inscription appears to be a list of names, possibly of members of a guild or religious group, with a dedication by 'Leo'.

EDCS-05700290.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Annius of Ostia, Augustalis sevir.
EDCS-05700290.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Titus Annius of Ostia, who held the rank of Augustalis sevir.

EDCS-05700292.translation: Titus Annius Victor, Augustalis sevir, for himself and Annia Anthis, his wife, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants. 27 feet in front, 30 feet in width.
EDCS-05700292.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Titus Annius Victor, an Augustalis sevir, for himself, his wife, and their descendants, including land dimensions.

EDCS-05700293.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Antistius Agathangelus made [this] for himself and for Titus Antistius Favor, his son, a Roman knight and decurion in the colony of Ostia, who held all offices, and for Titus Antistius Favor Proculeianus, his grandson, a Roman knight and decurion of Ostia, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants. 15 feet in front, 20 feet in width.
EDCS-05700293.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Titus Antistius Agathangelus and his descendants, including his son and grandson who were Roman knights and decurions in Ostia.

EDCS-05700294.translation: [For the deceased] Antoninus, who lived 36 years, [a] Augustalis sevir, also a quinquennial and a dendrophorus of the Laurentine Lavinat(ian?) guild, a good and most pleasant man. Marcia Hilara [dedicated this] to her most sweet husband, with whom she lived 10 years.
EDCS-05700294.summary: This tomb inscription commemorates Antoninus, an Augustalis sevir and dendrophorus, who died at age 36, and was dedicated by his wife Marcia Hilara.

EDCS-05700296.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Antonius Peculiaris, Augustalis sevir and quinquennial of the guild of Ostian craftsmen, for the 25th lustrum, made [this] for himself and for Antonia Soteris, his most rare wife, with whom he lived 50 years.
EDCS-05700296.summary: This funerary dedication is by Lucius Antonius Peculiaris, an Augustalis sevir and quinquennial of the Ostian craftsmen's guild, for himself and his wife Antonia Soteris.

EDCS-05700297.translation: To Marcus Antonius Severus, son of Marcus, of the Menenia tribe, former prefect of craftsmen, former duovir, quaestor of the treasury, quaestor of the food supply, flamen of the deified Vespasian, former prefect of craftsmen of the tilers of Ostia.
EDCS-05700297.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Antonius Severus, detailing his various public offices in Ostia and likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05700298.translation: Lucius Aquillius Modestus, freedman of a woman, magistrate for five years of the guild of tilers of Ostia, in its second lustrum, made this for himself and for Aquillia Aptae, his patroness and daughter of Lucius, and for Decimia Prisca, wife of Spurius, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants, and for the descendants of Decimia Prisca. In front 40 feet, in the field 31.5 feet.
EDCS-05700298.summary: This inscription records the construction of a tomb by Lucius Aquillius Modestus for himself and his family, as well as a patroness, and indicates the dimensions of the plot.

EDCS-05700300.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Arrius Vitalianus, decurion of the Laurentes, of the Augustan district, former duovir of the same place, quaestor of the treasury, flamen, who lived 31 years, 10 months, and 11 days. Vitalianus [erected this].
EDCS-05700300.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Arrius Vitalianus, detailing his age and civic offices in Laurentum, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700301.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Arruntia Dynamis, an Isiac priestess, lived 21 years, 2 months, and 12 days. Arruntia Helpis erected this for her most devoted daughter and for herself.
EDCS-05700301.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Arruntia Dynamis, an Isiac priestess, and its erection by her mother.

EDCS-05700302.text_edition: \n Publio Aufidio Publi filio Quirina Forti decurioni adlecto IIviro quaestori aerari Ostiensium IIII praefecto fabrum Ostis patrono \n
EDCS-05700302.translation: To Publius Aufidius Fortis, son of Publius, of the Quirina tribe, decurion, promoted to duovir, quaestor of the treasury, of Ostia, patron of the Ostians, former prefect of craftsmen.
EDCS-05700302.summary: This is an epitaph honoring Publius Aufidius Fortis, listing his civic and military roles in Ostia.

EDCS-05700304.translation: To the well-deserving Aurelius Sissius and Titus Aurelius Eutyches, seviri Augustales, curator and quinquennial of the Aurelii Quintius and Heliodorus, patron.
EDCS-05700304.summary: This inscription honors Aurelius Sissius and Titus Aurelius Eutyches for their service as seviri Augustales and patrons of the Aurelii Quintius and Heliodorus.

EDCS-05700305.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Lucius Aurelius Fortunatianus, son of Lucius, of the Palatina tribe, dearest son of the first praetor, minister of the rites of Volcanus, who lived 4 years, 7 months, and 17 days.
EDCS-05700305.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young child, Lucius Aurelius Fortunatianus, son of a high-ranking official, detailing his age and parentage.

EDCS-05700306.translation: [Erected] for the Portenses, quinquennial, and also a biselliarius. [Erected] for himself and Aurelia Victorina, his wife, and their sons, and their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. Furthermore, whoever wishes to place a foreign body in this monument shall give 50,000 sesterces to the public treasury of Ostia. In front 50 feet, in the field 71.5 feet.
EDCS-05700306.summary: This inscription details the construction of a family tomb by a quinquennial and biselliarius, including provisions for burial and a payment to the city for future interments.

EDCS-05700308.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Calpurnius Chius, sevir Augustalis and quinquennial, also quinquennial of the body of land surveyors of Ostia and curator, also curator of the codex of Ostia and honorary duovir, also quinquennial of the greater guild of Silvanus Augustus, which belongs to Hilarion, having served as sacomarus, also master to Mars Ficanus Augustus, also in the guild of dendrophori. He erected this for himself and for Cornelia Ampliata, his dearest wife, with whom he lived 31 years; for Calpurnia Phthengis, his dearest freedwoman, daughter of Lucius; for Lucius Calpurnius Fortis, his freedman vernae; for Lucius Calpurnius Felix, his freedman; for Lucius Calpurnius Adauctus, his freedman vernae; for Calpurnia Chia, his vernae daughter of Lucius; for Calpurnia Ampliata, his vernae daughter of Lucius; for Lucius Calpurnius Felix, his vernae son of Lucius; for Lucius Calpurnius Chius Felicissimus, his son of Lucius, Palatina tribe; for their freedmen, freedwomen, and descendants who deserve well.
EDCS-05700308.summary: This extensive funerary inscription records the life and extensive public and guild services of Lucius Calpurnius Chius in Ostia, and dedicates the tomb to his family and freedmen.

EDCS-05700309.translation: Lucius Caltilius Hilarus, freedman of a woman. Caltilia Felicula, freedwoman of Lucius. For himself and for Lucius Caltilius Stephanus, freedman of Lucius, and for his family, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and all their descendants. In front 20 feet, in the field 25 feet.
EDCS-05700309.summary: This inscription records the purchase of a burial plot by Lucius Caltilius Hilarus and Caltilia Felicula for themselves, their relatives, and their descendants.

EDCS-05700313.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Sextus Carminius Parthenopaeus, a Roman equestrian, quinquennial decurion of the colony of Ostia, and of the guild of timber framers of Ostia, and to Carminia Briseis, his wife. Sextus Carminius Plotinianus made this for his well-deserving brother. 12 feet in front, 35 feet in depth.
EDCS-05700313.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Sextus Carminius Plotinianus for his brother, Sextus Carminius Parthenopaeus, an equestrian and decurion of Ostia, and his wife Carminia Briseis.

EDCS-05700317.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Lucius Carullius Felicissimus, a bisellarius and Augustalis, quinquennial of Laurentum Lavinas, and of the guild of wine merchants of the city and of Ostia. He lived 75 years. Lucius Carullius Felicissimus, his father, made this for his well-deserving son.
EDCS-05700317.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Lucius Carullius Felicissimus for his son, Lucius Carullius Felicissimus, who held various civic and guild positions in Ostia and Laurentum Lavinas.

EDCS-05700320.text_edition: \n Publio Celerio Publi filio Palatina Amando decurio decurionum decreto adlectus hunc decuriones funere publico perferendum censuerunt eique honores omnes decreverunt impensam remisit nnorum XXIX mensibus diebus Scanssa sic Antanua sic fecit\n
EDCS-05700320.translation: The decurions decreed that Publius Celerius Amandus, son of Publius, of the Palatine tribe, a decurion, should be carried in a public funeral, and they decreed all honors for him, and remitted the expenses. He lived 29 years and some months and days. Scanssa and Antanua did this.
EDCS-05700320.summary: This inscription records the public funeral and honors decreed by the decurions for Publius Celerius Amandus, who died at the age of 29.

EDCS-05700327.translation: On the Ides of December, to the spirits of the departed. To Caius Modestus Theseus. Publius Claudius Abascantus, freedman of the Three Gauls, made this for his dearest pupil, who lived 8 years, 5 months, and 19 days.
EDCS-05700327.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Publius Claudius Abascantus, a freedman, for his young pupil, Caius Modestus Theseus.

EDCS-05700328.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Modestia Epigone, dearest soul. Abascantus of the Gauls made this for her, who deserved well of him. In the year of the consulship of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Commodus Augustus and Marcus Plautius Quintillus (175 AD).
EDCS-05700328.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Abascantus for his wife Modestia Epigone, dating to 175 AD.

EDCS-05700329.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Tiberius Claudius Cerialis, freedman of Titus Claudius, a quinquennial Augustalis of Ostia. Claudia Primilla, to her most excellent husband.
EDCS-05700329.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Tiberius Claudius Cerialis, a quinquennial Augustalis of Ostia, made by his wife Claudia Primilla.

EDCS-05700331.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Clodius Lucrio, Augustalis, quinquennial, made this for himself, and for his dearest daughter Clodia Victoria, who lived 19 years, 5 months, 18 days, and 5 hours, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. This monument will not follow a fiduciary heir. 30 feet in front, 25 feet in depth.
EDCS-05700331.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Clodius Lucrio, an Augustalis and quinquennial, for himself, his daughter Clodia Victoria, and their freedmen and descendants.

EDCS-05700332.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Clodia Helpis, who lived 27 years. Marcus Aemilius Hilarianus, decurion, flamen, aedile, and duovir, made this for his incomparable wife, along with Caltilia Tyche and Attius Hermes, her parents.
EDCS-05700332.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Marcus Aemilius Hilarianus for his wife Clodia Helpis, mentioning her parents.

EDCS-05700333.translation: Lucius Combarisius Hermianus, a quinquennial Augustalis, made this while alive for himself, and for his wife Combarisia Onesime, and for his sons Lucius Combarisius Marcianus and Victorinus, and for his brother Combarisius Hesperion, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. 19 feet in front, 35 feet in depth.
EDCS-05700333.summary: This is a self-funded funerary dedication by Lucius Combarisius Hermianus, a quinquennial Augustalis, for his family and descendants.

EDCS-05700334.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lucius Combarisius Victorinus, who lived 10 years, 7 months, and 9 days. Combarisius Hermianus, his father.
EDCS-05700334.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Combarisius Hermianus for his son, Lucius Combarisius Victorinus, who died at the age of 10.

EDCS-05700336.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Cornelius Au[---], Augustalis seviri, made [this] for himself and his freedmen and freedwomen.
EDCS-05700336.summary: A funerary monument dedicated to the departed spirits by Lucius Cornelius, an Augustalis seviri.

EDCS-05700338.translation: To the departed spirits. Hilarus, his freedman, (dedicated this) to his patron Sextus Cornelius Eutyches, Augustalis quinqennalis seviri.
EDCS-05700338.summary: A funerary inscription honoring Sextus Cornelius Eutyches, an Augustalis quinqennalis seviri, dedicated by his freedman.

EDCS-05700339.translation: Gaius Cornelius Isochrysus, son of Gaius, Augustalis seviri, and also quinquennalis, made (this) for himself and for his wife, Silia Tyrannis, and for his son, Gaius Cornelius Isochrysianus, and for his son, Gaius Cornelius Silianus, and for his stepson, Gaius Silus Faustus, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. This monument shall not pass to an outside heir. In front 30 feet, in the land 30 feet.
EDCS-05700339.summary: A funerary monument built by Gaius Cornelius Isochrysus, an Augustalis seviri, for his family and descendants, with specifications for its inheritance and dimensions.

EDCS-05700341.translation: To the memory of Marcus Cornelius Valerianus Epagathianus, son of Marcus, of the Palatina tribe, an Equus Romanus, decurion of the most splendid colony of Ostia, flamen, praetor, twice holder of the priesthood of Volcanus, and also a member of the Arulensian association, decurion of the Laurentes vicus Augusti, twice a magistrate, patron of the association of boatmen and clerks of the Ostian auxiliaries, who lived 12 years [and ---] months. Marcus Cornelius Valerianus, son of Marcus, of the Palatina tribe, decurion, made (this).
EDCS-05700341.summary: A memorial erected by Marcus Cornelius Valerianus for his father, Marcus Cornelius Valerianus Epagathianus, a prominent Ostian official and member of various associations, who died at a young age.

EDCS-05700345.translation: To the departed spirits. Cominia Secundina (dedicated this) to Aulus Egrilius Callistio, Augustalis seviri, and also quinquennalis, her incomparable husband.
EDCS-05700345.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated by Cominia Secundina to her husband, Aulus Egrilius Callistio, an Augustalis quinquennalis seviri.

EDCS-05700346.translation: Aulus Egrilius Plarianus, son of Aulus, decurial scribe of wax tablets, and Claudia Hermione, daughter of Tiberius, made (this) for Claudia Irena, daughter of Tiberius, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-05700346.summary: A funerary monument erected by Aulus Egrilius Plarianus and Claudia Hermione for Claudia Irena and her descendants.

EDCS-05700347.translation: To the departed spirits. Aulus Egrilius Ision and Iulia Proba, his wife, made (this) for Aulus Egrilius Secundus Threptianus, son of Aulus, decurion of the Laurentes vici Augustanorum, scribe of the college of Ostian carpenters, who was inscribed as a decurial scribe, member of the Pupinia tribe.
EDCS-05700347.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Aulus Egrilius Ision and Iulia Proba to Aulus Egrilius Secundus Threptianus, a prominent Ostian official and member of the Pupinia tribe.

EDCS-05700348.translation: To the departed spirits. Aulus Egrilius Threptianus lived 27 years and 31 days. Egrilia Vera (dedicated this) to her most dutiful brother. Dedicated on the day before the Kalends of November, in the consulship of Largo and Messalinus.
EDCS-05700348.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aulus Egrilius Threptianus, who died at the age of 27 years and 31 days, dedicated by his sister Egrilia Vera and dated by consuls.

EDCS-05700349.translation: To Gaius Fabius Longus, son of a primipilaris, grandson of Longus, a primipilaris, great-grandson of Fabius Rufus, great-great-grandson of Gaius Grattius. Voturia Agrippa, praetor, for carrying out the sacred rites of Volcanus, decurion by decree of the decurions, admitted as decurion, aedile, duumvir.
EDCS-05700349.summary: An honorary inscription for Gaius Fabius Longus, detailing his distinguished lineage and his public offices including praetor, decurion, aedile, and duumvir.

EDCS-05700351.translation: To the departed spirits. Aulus Fabius Felixs dedicated (this) to his most beloved son, Aulus Fabius Feliciano, son of Aulus, of the Palatina tribe, aedile for performing the sacred rites of Volcanus, who lived 19 years, 1 month, and 29 days.
EDCS-05700351.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aulus Fabius Feliciano, son of Aulus, an aedile, who died at the age of 19 years, 1 month, and 29 days, dedicated by his father.

EDCS-05700352.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-05700352.translation: To Decimus Fabius Florus, son of Decimus, of the Palatine tribe, priest of Sancta Regina, elected by judgment in a majesty trial, the 'decurion of the Laurentes vicus Augustanorum, quattuorvir,' shipmaster of the V corpor(a) of the shipwrights of Ostia, having held all offices and performed all duties, member of the V corpor(a) of the 5 regions of the colony of Ostia. Flavius Moschylus, a most distinguished man, a follower of Isis, erects a statue to him in memory of his piety and chastity, having ordered it by his will to be set up by his heirs, for a patron who deserved well of him. The place was granted publicly by decree of the decurions. In honor of the office he held on the day he became priest (?). Dedicated on the 17th day before the Kalends of [---], under consuls [---]. The place was granted by Julius Faustinus, pontifex of Volcanius, and the administrator of the sacred buildings permitted it, acting for Flavius Moschylus, under the quinquennality of the censorship of Quintus Veturius Felix Felicis, Socrates and Lucius Florius Euprepes. For its dedication, he gave to the decurions 3 denarii with the service of the basilica.
EDCS-05700352.summary: This is a funerary or honorary inscription for Decimus Fabius Florus, a prominent freedman and shipmaster in Ostia, detailing his offices and the erection of a statue in his honor by Flavius Moschylus.

EDCS-05700353.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-05700353.translation: To Fabio Hermogenes, of the public equestrian order, scribe, aedile, decurion, elected decurion, flamen of the deified Hadrian. In whose priesthood, alone and first, he presented scenic games at his own expense. The most distinguished order of decurions honored him with a public funeral and decreed that an equestrian statue with an inscription be erected in the forum at public expense in recognition of his love and industry, and that an aedile be substituted in his place as a consolation to his father, who gave 50,000 sesterces for the honors shown to him. From the interest of this sum, at 5% annually, on the 13th day before the Kalends of August, on his birthday, 5 denarii are to be given to each decurion and 37.5 denarii to the decurial scribes and 25 denarii to the lictors. In the temple of Rome and Augustus, it pleased the order of decurions, in the presence of his father Fabius, that sportulas be distributed on the birthday of his son Hermogenes to those present in the forum before his statues, with a stipulation having been made.
EDCS-05700353.summary: This inscription honors Fabio Hermogenes, a local magistrate in Ostia, detailing his public career, the erection of an equestrian statue, and a bequest for annual distributions on his birthday.

EDCS-05700354.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Fabricius Gallus, son of Lucius, of the Palatine tribe, son of Caesennius Gallus, Roman knight, pontiff of Laurentes Lavinatium, scribe, aedile, having held all offices in the colony of Ostia. Italicus, to his most worthy patron.
EDCS-05700354.summary: This is a funerary inscription (Dis Manibus) for Lucius Fabricius Gallus, a Roman knight and prominent local official in Ostia, dedicated by his patron Italicus.

EDCS-05700358.translation: On his death, Decimus Follius Mela, freedman of Dionysius, for himself and his deceased freedwoman Hilara, wife of Numisia Rufa, and his deceased freedwoman Secunda, daughter of Decimus and Numisia, and his deceased mother-in-law Kara, daughter of Spurius Numisius, and his deceased father Decimus Critonius Dionysius, and his deceased sister Critonia Chila, and his freedman Decimus Follus Protogenes, and his freedman Decimus Follus Alypus, son of Decimus and Numisia, and his wife Fabia Zosime, freedwoman of Quintus. Prisca, daughter of Decimus Follius, lived 1 year, 6 months, 8 days. Marcus Livius Rogatus, son of Marcus, of the Voturia tribe. Marcus Livius Nico, freedman of Marcus, Augustalis. Salinatoria Aucta, freedwoman of Marcus, wife. "Marcus Livius Iustus", son of Marcus, of the Voturia tribe. Marcus Livius Augustalis lived 5 years. Lucius Septimius Malchio, freedman of Lucius. Septimia Tertia. Gaius Clodius Hebenus, freedman of Gaius. Marcus Livius "Rhodinus", freedman of Marcus. Marcus Livius Amemptus, freedman of Marcus. Livia Thymele. Marcus Livius Optatus, freedman of Marcus. Marcus Livius Priscus, freedman of Marcus. Livia Aretusa. 30 feet in front, 22.5 feet in the field.
EDCS-05700358.summary: This inscription is a complex funerary monument (Dis Manibus and indications of burials) for members of the Follius and Livius families, as well as other individuals, in Ostia, providing details of their relationships and lifespans.

EDCS-05700362.translation: To Gaius Granius Matures, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, by decree of the decurions, elected decurion, to whom the order decreed the decurionate and a statue free of charge because of his generosity. Gaius Granius Rufus, Lucius Granius Celsus.
EDCS-05700362.summary: This is an honorary inscription for Gaius Granius Matures, recognizing his generosity with the free grant of the decurionate and a statue in Ostia.

EDCS-05700363.translation: To Gaius Granius Matures, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, by decree of the decurions, elected decurion free of charge, patron of the corporations, curator of the sea-going ships and inspector of the grain dole officials of Ostia. Marcus Lollius Paulinus, son of Marcus, to his friend.
EDCS-05700363.summary: This honorary inscription for Gaius Granius Matures, a patron and official in Ostia, details his administrative roles related to shipping and the grain supply.

EDCS-05700364.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Gaius Granius Matures, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, decurion and duumvir of Ostia, inspector of the corporation of Ostia, patron of the curators of sea-going and river ships of Ostia, of the dendrophori of Ostia, of the [---] of Ostia, of the [---]tinenses.
EDCS-05700364.summary: This is a funerary inscription (Dis Manibus) for Gaius Granius Matures, listing his extensive public and corporate offices in Ostia.

EDCS-05700367.translation: To Publius Horatius Chryseros, Augustalis sevirus, also quinquennalis and immune of the Lares Augusti. By decree of the Senate, the Augustales decreed that a statue be erected to him because he gave 50,000 sesterces to their treasury, from which sum 10,000 sesterces for the honor of the care of Sextus Horatius Chryserotianus and the others, and 40,000 sesterces, excluding a stipulation, from the interest at one half percent, and 2,000 units [of currency?] from the above-mentioned sum, are to be distributed annually on the Ides of March, his birthday, at the second hour among those present, down to the smallest coin, after deducting 100 sesterces for the decoration of the statue and the college of Augustales. If this is not done, then that sum of 40,000 sesterces is to be given to the treasury of Ostia under the same condition as stated above. For its dedication, he gave 5 denarii to the decurions and 5 denarii to the Augustales. Having received this honor, he remitted the expenses for the statue to the order of Augustales. Dedicated the 15th day before the Kalends of January, under the consulship of Mamertinus and Rufus.
EDCS-05700367.summary: This honorary inscription for Publius Horatius Chryseros, an Augustalis and quinquennalis in Ostia, details a substantial donation he made to the college of Augustales for the erection of a statue and annual distributions.

EDCS-05700368.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Bruttius Ianuarius, quinquennalis of the corporation of naval shipwrights of Ostia. For the purpose of donation, a memorial transmitted to him by Maria Callistene, because he undertook to repair it for the purpose of renovation, for himself and Brittia Profutura his wife, and his sons and freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-05700368.summary: This funerary inscription (Dis Manibus) is for Lucius Bruttius Ianuarius, a quinquennalis of the shipwrights' guild in Ostia, and mentions a memorial given by Maria Callistene and his family.

EDCS-05700370.translation: To Gaius Julius Tyrannus, master quinquennalis of the college of roof-timber workers of Ostia for the 22nd lustrum. To him, uniquely and first of all, the entire body decreed that a statue be erected, because of his merits. The place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-05700370.summary: This honorary inscription commemorates Gaius Julius Tyrannus, the master quinquennalis of the roof-timber workers' college in Ostia, who was honored with the first statue erected by the entire college.

EDCS-05700371.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Iunius Euhodus of the Palatine tribe, quinquennial magistrate of the guild of carpenters and roofers of Ostia for the 21st lustrum, made this for himself and for Metilia Acte, priestess of the Mother of the Gods of the colony of Ostia, his most holy wife.
EDCS-05700371.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a magistrate of a trade guild and his wife, likely dating to the mid-2nd century AD based on the mention of lustra.

EDCS-05700373.translation: To Lucius Licinius Heros, son of Lucius, of the Palatine tribe, a Roman knight, decurion of the equestrian cohort of consuls' attendants, quinquennial decurion, priest of the Genius of the colony, flamen of Rome and the Emperors, curator of public works, quaestor of the treasury, aedile, flamen of the deified Severus, Arval sodalis, praetor, first holder of the sacred rites of Volcanus. The ordo of the Augustales [erected this] to their excellent citizen for his merits.
EDCS-05700373.summary: This is an honorific inscription detailing the extensive career of Lucius Licinius Heros, a prominent local official, likely from the Severan period.

EDCS-05700374.translation: To Marcus Licinius Privatus, honored with the ornamenta decurionatus and appointed chief biselliarius, who contributed fifty thousand sesterces to the treasury, quaestor and quinquennial magistrate of the guild of bakers of Ostia and Portus, quinquennial magistrate of the guild of carpenters and roofers for the 27th lustrum, and decurion of the same number, decurial scribe, father and grandfather of decurions, librarian of the Claudian tribe, father of Roman knights, and patron of clients, his great-uncle and relatives. The entire college of caligati of the guild of carpenters and roofers of Ostia [erected this] to their excellent magistrate for his love and merits. The place was granted by public decree of the decurions.
EDCS-05700374.summary: This is an honorific inscription for Marcus Licinius Privatus, detailing his extensive public and guild service, including significant financial contributions, likely dating to the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-05700375.translation: To Publius Lucilius Gamala, son of Publius, grandson of Publius, great-grandson of Publius. Aedile, officiant of the sacred rites of Volcanus; decree-elected aedile by the decurions; freely elected decurion; pontiff; duumvir; quinquennial holder of censorial power, appointed in the assemblies; curator for collecting and distributing public funds; when he received the public revenue for the games, he remitted it and made expenditure from his own funds; he also paved the road with stone at his own expense, the road which joins the forum from one arch to another; he also gave a feast in the triclinia to 217 colonists; he also gave a luncheon to the colonists of Ostia at his own expense twice; he also restored the temple of Volcanus at his own expense; he also established the temple of Venus at his own expense; he also established the temple of Fortuna at his own expense; he also established the temple of Ceres at his own expense; he also made the weights and measures at the market with Marcus Turranius at his own expense; he also established the temple of Spes at his own expense; he also made a marble tribunal in the forum; a gilded statue was erected to him at public expense by decree of the decurions; also a bronze [statue] was erected by public expense by decree of the decurions near the quaestor's tribunal; for the reason that when the state was selling its properties, he donated 15,200 sesterces to the state because of the promise of a naval war. The decurions decreed that he should be carried with a public funeral.
EDCS-05700375.summary: This is an extensive honorific inscription detailing the numerous public works, benefactions, and religious offices held by Publius Lucilius Gamala, a highly esteemed citizen of Ostia, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-05700376.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-05700376.translation: To Publius Lucilius Gamala, son of Publius, grandson of Publius, great-grandson of Publius. Aedile, officiant of the sacred rites of Volcanus; the same person, praetor a third time, decurion, elected by decree of the decurions as an infant; duumvir; prefect of Lucius Caesar, son of Augustus, with censorial power; quaestor of the treasury; pontiff; curator of the archives and books; first appointed curator. He amplified all the games which he held at his own expense; he also gave a gladiatorial show; he also restored the temple of Castor and Pollux; he was appointed curator for collecting and distributing public funds and restored the cella of his father Tiberinus; he also repaired the baths which the deified Pius had built, which had been consumed by fire; he repaired the portico; he also restored the temple of Venus at his own expense; he also made the weights at the market and measures at the wine market at his own expense; he also restored the naval dock which had been built by Lucius Coilus, which had almost collapsed when it was being built; a bronze statue was erected to him at public expense by decree of the decurions; [costing] 16,XXX sesterces.
EDCS-05700376.summary: This honorific inscription lists the extensive public service, religious offices, and costly benefactions of Publius Lucilius Gamala, paralleling the previous inscription and likely referring to the same individual, possibly with some overlap or clarification of his career.

EDCS-05700380.translation: Aulus Livius Strato, sevir Augustalis and also quinquennial, and his wife Livia Eutychia made this for themselves while alive, and for their children Livius Zoticus, Strato, Straticus, and Eutychianus, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants. They acquired this from Aulus Livius Agrippa, Gaius Caetranius Primitivus, and Gaius Caetranius Vitalio. Strato says: friends, may this be for you in safety.
EDCS-05700380.summary: This is a funerary and property inscription for a sevir Augustalis and his wife, dedicating a plot to themselves and their descendants, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-05700383.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Marrius Moderatus, sevir Augustalis and also quinquennial, lived 80 years. Eutyches and Philumenus made this.
EDCS-05700383.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Marrius Moderatus, a sevir Augustalis, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-05700385.translation: Marcus Modius Maximus, chief gallus of the colony of Ostia.
EDCS-05700385.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription identifying Marcus Modius Maximus as the chief gallus (a religious official) of Ostia, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05700387.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Mundicia Felicissima, his well-deserving wife. Mundicius Proculus made this.
EDCS-05700387.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Mundicia Felicissima, made by her husband Mundicius Proculus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05700390.translation: To Publius Nonius Anterotianus, son of Publius, of the Palatine tribe, Livius, honored with the public equestrian order by the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus; elected decurion by decree of the decurions; flamen of the deified Hadrian; Salian of Laurentes Lavinates; aedile; praetor; officiant of the sacred rites of Volcanus. Livia Marcellina [erected this] to her most sweet nephew. The place was granted by public decree of the decurions.
EDCS-05700390.summary: This is an honorific inscription for Publius Nonius Anterotianus, detailing his prestigious career and honors, including equestrian status granted by Marcus Aurelius, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-05700391.translation: To Publius Nonius Livius Anterotianus, son of Publius Nonius, of the Palatina tribe, honored with equestrian rank by the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, admitted to the council of decurions by decree of the decurions, high priest of the deified Hadrian, Salian priest of the Laurentes Lavinates, aedile, praetor, and officiant of the Volcanalia rites; Titus Tinucius Sosiphanes, to [him] most beloved, most dutiful. The place was granted publicly by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-05700391.summary: This is a public dedication of a place for a monument to Publius Nonius Anterotianus, who held numerous civic and religious offices in a Roman colony, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700392.translation: To the gods, the departed. Decimus Nonius Hermes, sevirus Augustalis and quinquennalis, made this for himself and for Nonia Hera, his deserving wife, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-05700392.summary: This is a funerary dedication for a tomb made by Decimus Nonius Hermes, a freedman holding the prestigious office of sevirus Augustalis and quinquennalis, for himself and his family.

EDCS-05700393.translation: Publius Nonius Zethus, Augustalis, made this for himself and for Nonia Hilara, his freedwoman fellow initiate, and for Nonia Pelagia, freedwoman of Publius, his wife. Publius Nonius Heraclio. Proved (worth) 109.
EDCS-05700393.summary: This is a funerary dedication for a tomb made by Publius Nonius Zethus, an Augustalis, for himself, his freedwoman fellow initiate, and his wife, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700394.translation: Publius Nonius Zetus, freedman of Publius Nonius and of his wife, Augustalis. Nonia Pelagia, freedwoman of Publius. Publius Nonius Anthus, freedman of Publius Nonius and of his wife. Publius Nonius Iucundus, freedman of Publius Nonius and of his wife. In the front 30 feet, in the field 25 feet.
EDCS-05700394.summary: This is a property boundary inscription for a plot of land measuring 30 by 25 feet, owned by several individuals with the name Publius Nonius and their freedmen, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700395.translation: Publius Nonius Zethus, freedman of Publius Nonius and of his wife, made this for himself and for Nonia Hilara, his fellow initiate. Oppia [---]. In the front 10 feet.
EDCS-05700395.summary: This is a funerary dedication for a tomb, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE, made by Publius Nonius Zethus for himself and his fellow initiate Nonia Hilara, with a partial inscription for Oppia.

EDCS-05700396.translation: Gaius Novius Trophimus, freedman of his wife, a sevirus Augustalis and also quinquennalis and curator, made this for himself and for his most respected patroness Novia Synerusa, and for his own slave Gaius Novius Amaranthus, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants, and for Novia Synerusa, his freedwoman and wife. To this monument is added the whole of the gardens with their fishpond; enclosed by a wall, it measures 280 feet in front and 28 and 14/24 jugera of land.
EDCS-05700396.summary: This is a funerary dedication for a tomb and its extensive grounds, including a fishpond, made by Gaius Novius Trophimus, a sevirus Augustalis, for himself, his patroness, and his family, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700397.translation: To Lucius Numisius Agathemerus, freedman of Lucius, a sevirus Augustalis, a merchant from Hispania Citerior, and to Numisia Mercatilla, freedwoman of Lucius, his wife, as stipulated in his will. 100 sesterces were spent as agreed by Numisia Mercatilla, his wife.
