Gaius Numisius Severus, son of Gaius Numisius Sucurana, lived 12 years, 7 months, 18 days. To their dearest son, Gaius Numisius Pardalas and Numisia Eupraxia.
EDCS-05700397.summary: This is a funerary monument commemorating a child, Gaius Numisius Severus, funded by his mother Numisia Mercatilla, and dedicated by his siblings Gaius Numisius Pardalas and Numisia Eupraxia, likely in the late 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05700399.translation: To Plaria Vera, daughter of Quintus Plarius, priestess of the deified Augusta, mother of Aulus Egilius Plarianus, father, patron, and consul of the colony.
EDCS-05700399.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Plaria Vera, priestess of the deified Augusta and mother of an important local official, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700400.translation: To Quintus Plotus Romanus, son of Quintus Plotus, of the Quirina tribe, honored with equestrian rank by the deified Hadrian, aedile, priest of Rome and of the Augusti, priest of the deified Titus. The decurions decreed that a statue should be publicly erected for him. Plotus Niger, his father, having received the honor, set it up at his own expense. The place was granted by decree of the decurions. Dedicated on the 16th day before the Kalends of April, when Titus Hoenius Severus and Marcus Peducaeus Priscus were consuls.
EDCS-05700400.summary: This is a public dedication of a statue for Quintus Plotus Romanus, who held various religious and political offices and was honored by the emperor, with the decree of the decurions and the installation by his father, likely in the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-05700401.translation: To the gods, the departed. Quintus Plotus Romanus, son of Quintus Plotus, of the Quirina tribe, honored with equestrian rank, who held public offices in the Ostian colony, died leaving parents surviving him.
EDCS-05700401.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Plotus Romanus, who held equestrian rank and public offices in Ostia, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05700403.translation: To Sextus Pompeius Maximus, son of Sextus, priest of Sol Invictus Mithras, father of fathers, quinquennial official of the body of expatriates, the priests of Sol Invictus Mithras shall always have him because of his love and merits.
EDCS-05700403.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Sextus Pompeius Maximus, a priest of Sol Invictus Mithras and quinquennial official, by the priests of the same cult, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700406.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publicia Aphrodite made this for her deserving husband Lucius Publicius Onesimus, an Augustalis and quinquennial official, and for her son Lucius Publicius Onesimus, who lived 25 years, and for herself.
EDCS-05700406.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Publicia Aphrodite to her husband Lucius Publicius Onesimus and their son, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700408.translation: Salonia Carpime made this for her patron Salonia Euterpe, priestess of the Mother of the Gods of the Port of Augustus and Trajan Felix, her most excellent patron, who deserved well, and for herself, and for Salonius Hermetus, Salonius Dora, Salonia Tertia, and for their children, the left half upon entering. Marcus Cutius Rusticus, a trumpeter of the Mother of the Gods of the Port of Augustus and Trajan Felix, made for himself and for Cutia Theodote and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and descendants, the right half.
EDCS-05700408.summary: This inscription records the construction of a tomb by Salonia Carpime for her priestess patron, Salonia Euterpe, and by Marcus Cutius Rusticus, a trumpeter, for his family, dividing the space between them, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700410.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Sentius Clodianus and Sentia Celeritas made this for Veratia Lacaena.
EDCS-05700410.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Sentius Clodianus and Sentia Celeritas to Veratia Lacaena, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700411.translation: To Gnaeus Sergius [---], Augustalis [---], for Gnaeus Sergius [---], appointed decurion [---], for Sergia Pyrallida [---], for Gnaeus Sergius Ma[---], for Lucilia Opsequens [---], for their freedmen and freedwomen [---], in front 30 feet, in the field [---]
EDCS-05700411.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gnaeus Sergius and other individuals, mentioning his status as an Augustalis and appointment as a decurion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700412.translation: Gnaeus Sergius Anthus, son of Gnaeus, freedman, Augustalis, made this. To his son Gnaeus Sergius Priscus, son of Gnaeus, former praetor, appointed by decree of the decurions for the sacred rites of Vulcan, appointed aedile.
EDCS-05700412.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Gnaeus Sergius Anthus, an Augustalis, to his son Gnaeus Sergius Priscus, who held various civic and religious offices, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700415.translation: The decurions decreed that Gaius Silis Epaphra, freedman of Gaius, the elder Felix, an Augustalis, should be carried out for burial at public expense. His son Nerva, having used the honor, remitted the expense. To Gaius Silis Nerva, son of Gaius, of the Voturia tribe, decreed by the decurions, appointed decurion, duumvir. To Silia Fasta, freedwoman of Gaius, wife. To Lucius Calpurnius Saturnus, son of Lucius, of the Voturia tribe, appointed aedile by decree of the decurions. To their freedmen, freedwomen, and descendants. To Gaius Silis Nerva, son of Gaius, of the Voturia tribe, former praetor, appointed duumvir for performing the sacred rites of Vulcan by decree of the decurions. To Silia Tryphaena, freedwoman of Gaius, his most pious wife. To their freedmen, freedwomen, and descendants. In front 35 feet, in the field 25 feet.
EDCS-05700415.summary: This extensive funerary inscription records the burial honors decreed for Gaius Silis Epaphra, an Augustalis, and details the appointments and family members of his son Gaius Silis Nerva, likely from the Roman Imperial period, also specifying plot dimensions.

EDCS-05700417.translation: Gaius Silius Mo[---], Augustalis [---], made this for himself and Sabin[---], for Gaius Silius Lauro[---], son of Gaius, of the Voturia tribe, for Lochias Magna, freedwoman [---], for his freedmen, freedwomen, and descendants [---], in front 41 feet [---], in the field [---]
EDCS-05700417.summary: This funerary inscription is for Gaius Silius Mo[---], an Augustalis, and lists several other individuals including family members and freedpersons, likely from the Roman Imperial period, also specifying plot dimensions.

EDCS-05700418.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caius Similius Philocyrius, an Augustalis and quinquennial official, master of the 36th lustrum of the college of carpenters of Ostia, and also scribe of the same number, made this for himself, and for Simila Philete, his only wife, and for Similia Romana, his daughter, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and descendants. In front 80 feet, in the field 23 feet. For Gaudentius and Lampas.
EDCS-05700418.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Caius Similius Philocyrius, an Augustalis, quinquennial official, and college master, for himself and his family, likely from Ostia during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700420.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Decimus Calpurnius Primigenius, Augustalis, Lucius Spurius Thiophanes, freedman of Lucius, Augustalis, Lucius Spurius Fortunatus, Augustalis, made this for Spuria Pieridis. And for their freedmen, freedwomen, and descendants, whom Lucius Spurius [---] manumitted. [---]
EDCS-05700420.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication by three Augustales, Decimus Calpurnius Primigenius, Lucius Spurius Thiophanes, and Lucius Spurius Fortunatus, for Spuria Pieridis and her descendants, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700421.translation: Gnaeus Statilius Crescens Crescentianus, a quinquennial Sexvir Augustalis and curator of the order of Augustales, and a Sexvir Augustalis at Tusculum, made this for himself, for his wife Statilia Atalante, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants, on the front 21 feet and in the ground 32 feet.
EDCS-05700421.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by a prominent freedman for his family, specifying plot dimensions, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700426.translation: Gaius Tuccius, son of Lucius, of the Voturia tribe. Attia, his wife.
EDCS-05700426.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph for Gaius Tuccius and his wife Attia, likely from the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-05700427.translation: Gaius Tuccius Eutychus, freedman of Gaius Tuccius Phra and his freedwoman Elate, a freedman of the Augustales, made this for himself and for Gaius Tuccius Phra, his patron, and for his freedwoman Elate, and for his freedmen Beryllus, Atticus, Vitalis, Zosimus, Sabinus, Diogenes, Eutyches, Gati, Hermes, Nicostratus, Diadumenus, Abascanthus, Successus, Cedrus, Nymphicus, Eutyches, Epitynchanus, Secundus, Successus, Trophimus, Delphus, Primus, Nic[---], and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants, on the front [---] feet.
EDCS-05700427.summary: This is a complex funerary epitaph, likely for a wealthy freedman or freedwoman, listing many freedmen and freedwomen as beneficiaries, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700428.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Malchio, freedman of the woman Tuccia, an Augustalis. Calityche, freedwoman of the woman Tuccia.
EDCS-05700428.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Malchio and Calityche, both freedpeople associated with a woman named Tuccia, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700429.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Valerius Firmus, son of Lucius, priest of Isis of Ostia and of the Mother of the Gods of Transtiberim, made this for himself.
EDCS-05700429.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Valerius Firmus, a priest of two mystery cults, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700431.translation: [---]NIEI[---] / honored, immune, and curator of the Traiectus, the ordo Augustalium decreed a statue for this Rusticelius and enrolled him among the biselliarii(?); content with this honor, he paid 50,000 sesterces into their treasury, except for the stipulation that from the interest [---] 2,000 sesterces of the aforementioned sum should be divided annually on his birthday on the Ides of June in the assembly among those present at the second hour, after the decoration of the statue costing 100 sesterces has been deducted; but if this is not done, then he stipulated that 50,000 sesterces should be given to the public treasury of Ostia under the same condition, and afterwards Veturia Rufina, daughter of Quintus, and Quintus Veturius Felix Socrates, decurion of the Laurentes' district of the Augustan neighborhood, and decurion of the Ostians, remitted the expenses for the statue to the ordo, on account of whose dedication he gave gifts to the decurions of 3 denarii and to the Augustales of 5 denarii, under the supervision of Quintus Veturius Felicissimus, his freedman, quinquennial Sexvir Augustalis and curator of the same order.
EDCS-05700431.summary: This is a lengthy inscription detailing an endowment made by a prominent local official (Rusticelius) to the city of Ostia, involving a statue, financial contributions, and distributions of gifts, likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700440.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Euanthenus, freedman of the colony, made this for his most holy wife.
EDCS-05700440.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for the wife of Lucius Euanthenus, a freedman of the colony, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700443.translation: [To the spirits] of the underworld. Onesimus, a Sexvir Augustalis, made [this] for himself (?) while alive.
EDCS-05700443.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Onesimus, a Sexvir Augustalis, who set it up for himself during his lifetime, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700469.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Caesennius Crispus, a physician, who lived 30 years.
EDCS-05700469.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Caesennius Crispus, a physician, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700470.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Cerdon, the most faithful steward. Marcus Caesonius Spectatus.
EDCS-05700470.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cerdon, a steward, set up by Marcus Caesonius Spectatus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700472.translation: To Iulius Admetus, son of Iulia Faustilla and Onesimus the doctor. He lived 10 years and 45 days.
EDCS-05700472.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulius Admetus, recording his age at death, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05700473.translation: To the divine shades. Melior, the calculator. He lived 13 years and was of such memory and knowledge that from ancient times until the day of his end, he surpassed all epitaphs. The individual things he knew could be written in a volume rather than an epitaph, for he made commentaries on his art, which he left behind, and was the first and only one who could have imitated them if the unfair fates had not envied him from human affairs. Sextus Aufustius Agreus made this for his beloved freedman, his most unhappy teacher. In front 2 feet, in the field 6 feet. He died in the year 897 from the founding of the city.
EDCS-05700473.summary: A funerary epitaph for Melior, a calculator and teacher, composed by his freedman Sextus Aufustius Agreus, with the date 897 AUC (145 CE).

EDCS-05700475.translation: Sebasmia at Damascus, Actia at Bostra, Pythia at Carthage, Asclepia at Carthage, Severia at Caesarea, Commodia at Caesarea. To this latter, first the most splendid order of Ostian decurions, at the request of the people, because of his outstanding expertise and great deference to his homeland, decreed that a statue be publicly erected in his colony.
EDCS-05700475.summary: An honorary inscription recording public honors (a statue) bestowed upon Commodia by the decurions and people of Ostia, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700477.translation: To the divine shades. Athenodorus, son of Macedon, from Doritus, made this for himself and for his freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. In front 16.5 feet, in the field 26.5 feet.
EDCS-05700477.summary: A funerary epitaph for Athenodorus of Doritus, establishing a burial plot for himself, his freedmen, and their descendants.

EDCS-05700478.translation: To the divine shades, sacred. Publius Caesellius Felix, a citizen of Sullectum, lived 47 years, 6 months. Pomponia Licinia for her most worthy husband.
EDCS-05700478.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Caesellius Felix, recording his age and his wife Pomponia Licinia, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05700479.translation: To the divine shades. To Titus Flavius Apollonius of Alexandria.
EDCS-05700479.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Titus Flavius Apollonius of Alexandria.

EDCS-05700480.translation: To the divine shades. To Titus Flavius Zoilos, son of Quirinus, most dutiful, lived 20 years. Prisca, daughter of Hecataeus, made this for herself and for Aphrodisius, son of Arpocration, Alexandrian, and for Flavia Artemidora, daughter, and for her own family and descendants, and for Epaphroditus, freedman of Aphrodisius.
EDCS-05700480.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Flavius Zoilos, recording his age and the family members for whom the tomb was intended, including Prisca, who commissioned it.

EDCS-05700481.translation: I, who speak without a voice from carved marble, born in excellent Tralles in Asia, have often surveyed all the walls of Baiae, because of the hot springs and the delights of the sea. My heir, not forgetful of my honorable life, has placed this temple and built these latest chapels for my hands and ashes and descendants, as I wished, with fifty thousand [sestertii]. But to you who read this, I pray only that you say: May the earth be light upon you, Socrates, son of Astomachus.
EDCS-05700481.summary: A funerary epitaph inscribed on a monument, likely a tomb or shrine, for Socrates Astomachi, detailing his life, his heir's commission of the monument, and a final wish for the reader.

EDCS-05700482.translation: To the divine shades, sacred. Valerius Veturius, an African colonist, lived 70 years, 2 months, 9 days.
EDCS-05700482.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valerius Veturius, a colonist from Africa, recording his age at death.

EDCS-05700483.translation: Gaius Julius Pothis, freedman, Amethystus. Trebellia Secunda, freedwoman of Marcus. In front 30 feet, in the field 20 feet.
EDCS-05700483.summary: An epitaph and plot marker for Gaius Julius Pothis and Trebellia Secunda, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-05700484.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Claudia Clide, his freedwoman, made this for her well-deserving patron, Tiberius Claudius Hellanicus, freedman of the Emperor.
EDCS-05700484.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a freedwoman for her deceased patron, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05700485.translation: Elegas, slave of Caesar, lived 24 years.
EDCS-05700485.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Elegas, a slave of Caesar, probably from the imperial period.

EDCS-05700486.translation: Eurema, slave of Caesar, lived 14 years. Fortunatus, a companion of Caesar, set this up.
EDCS-05700486.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Eurema, a slave of Caesar, with the monument erected by his companion, Fortunatus.

EDCS-05700487.translation: Musa, slave of Caesar, lived 22 years. Praesens, son of Peplus, set this up for his most sacred wet nurse.
EDCS-05700487.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Musa, a slave of Caesar, with the monument erected by Praesens for his wet nurse.

EDCS-05700488.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Restituta, slave of Caesar, lived 19 years, 9 months, and 14 days. Ilus Pares and Prisca, the mother, made this for their devoted daughter.
EDCS-05700488.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Restituta, a slave of Caesar, with the monument erected by her parents, dating likely to the imperial period.

EDCS-05700489.translation: Romana, daughter of Beryllus, lived 5 years. Beryllus for himself and his family.
EDCS-05700489.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Romana, a child, with a dedication for her family, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-05700490.translation: Xanthus, slave of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus, Iucundus, son of Xanthus, lived 7 years.
EDCS-05700490.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Xanthus, a slave of Emperor Nero (Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus), with the monument erected by his son.

EDCS-05700494.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Sextus Abenius Aprilis set this up for his most beloved, well-deserving parents, Sextus Abenius Agapetus and Iulia Base.
EDCS-05700494.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a son for his deceased parents, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05700499.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Acsilia Artimidora.
EDCS-05700499.summary: This is a brief funerary epitaph dedicated to Acsilia Artimidora.

EDCS-05700500.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Gaius Marius Felix made this for his well-deserving wife, Acutia Successa.
EDCS-05700500.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a husband for his deceased wife, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05700501.translation: To Marcus Aebutius [---], who lived 41 years. Aebutia Praetorina erected this monument to her well-deserving husband. And Publius Aelius Beryllus, his kinsman, also set this up.
EDCS-05700501.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Marcus Aebutius, who lived 41 years, and was erected by his wife Aebutia Praetorina and kinsman Publius Aelius Beryllus.

EDCS-05700502.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aebutia Septima to her well-deserving husband, Marcus Aebutius Felix.
EDCS-05700502.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Marcus Aebutius Felix, dedicated by his wife Aebutia Septima.

EDCS-05700503.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Marcus Aebutius Felix, Aebutia Septima, to her well-deserving husband. // To the spirits of the departed. To Aebutia Tertia, Aebutius Felix, to his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-05700503.summary: This epitaph records two burials: Marcus Aebutius Felix, dedicated by his wife Aebutia Septima, and Aebutia Tertia, dedicated by her husband Aebutius Felix.

EDCS-05700504.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. To Marcus Aeficius Statilianus, son of Marcus, who lived 31 years, 6 months, and 26 days.
EDCS-05700504.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Marcus Aeficius Statilianus, who lived 31 years, 6 months, and 26 days.

EDCS-05700506.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Ponteia Castula made this for her most sweet husband, Titus Aelius Ampliatus, freedman of the emperor.
EDCS-05700506.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Titus Aelius Ampliatus, freedman of the emperor, dedicated by his wife Ponteia Castula.

EDCS-05700507.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Publius Aelius Arescontis and Licinia Tertia, this burial plot was granted.
EDCS-05700507.summary: This inscription records the grant of a burial plot to Publius Aelius Arescontis and Licinia Tertia.

EDCS-05700508.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius Aelius Alexas made this for his well-deserving fellow freedman, Publius Aelius Atilius.
EDCS-05700508.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Publius Aelius Atilius, dedicated by his fellow freedman Publius Aelius Alexas.

EDCS-05700509.translation: Titus Aelius Demetrius, freedman of the emperor, and Claudia Marina made this for their most dutiful son, Titus Aelius Demetrius, who lived 14 years, 9 months, and 7 days; and for Gaius Cornelius Marinus, their brother, and for their children, freedmen and freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-05700509.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Titus Aelius Demetrius, son of Titus Aelius Demetrius (freedman of the emperor) and Claudia Marina, who died at age 14, and also commemorates Gaius Cornelius Marinus, their brother, and other descendants.

EDCS-05700511.translation: I, Demetrius, having entered life with ample measure, not of long duration, by the stroke of death have I been given to the nether gods. And after the image of death, these temples for myself are pleasingly deserved, and the rewards of light, of flourishing youth, abundant studies, and of life, and having borne death with full reverence, I departed. Indeed, with much goodness, a servant and not eagerly seizing banquets, while I hoped for the glory of life, I was suddenly carried away by a raft to the shades. I was 14 years old, and still formed into one, the middle autumn in the month of October, full and afflicting, broke my sweet soul and shattered my surest hope. However, that chaste and modest reverence often loses the sight of Tantalus, and the fear of Sisyphus is absent, and Ixion and the fear of the Furies. But in the retreat of the nether house's divinity, dutifully at last, he rejoices in his service.
EDCS-05700511.summary: This lengthy and metaphorical epitaph records the death of a young man named Demetrius, who died at age 14 in October, lamenting his lost potential and finding solace in the afterlife.

EDCS-05700512.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulia Syra made this for her well-deserving father, Publius Aelius Epagathus.
EDCS-05700512.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Publius Aelius Epagathus, dedicated by his daughter Iulia Syra.

EDCS-05700513.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Publius Aelius Epictetus.
EDCS-05700513.summary: A funerary dedication to Publius Aelius Epictetus.

EDCS-05700514.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Publius Aelius Eutyches, who lived 7 years, 6 months. Aelius Dafnus and Vivelia Secunda, his parents, made this for a well-deserving son.
EDCS-05700514.summary: A funerary monument for Publius Aelius Eutyches, set up by his parents, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05700515.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Publius Aelius Faustus.
EDCS-05700515.summary: A funerary dedication to Publius Aelius Faustus, with some illegible text.

EDCS-05700516.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Publius Aelius Fortunatus and Flavia Musa.
EDCS-05700516.summary: A funerary dedication for Publius Aelius Fortunatus and Flavia Musa.

EDCS-05700517.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius Aelius Hermes made this for himself, his sons, and their freedmen, freedwomen, and descendants.
EDCS-05700517.summary: A self-funded funerary monument for Publius Aelius Hermes and his descendants.

EDCS-05700518.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Titus Aelius Hermetus, who lived 50 years. Baebia Saturnina, his wife, for her dearest husband.
EDCS-05700518.summary: A funerary monument for Titus Aelius Hermetus, dedicated by his wife.

EDCS-05700519.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aelius Iulianus, a deserving patron, who lived 70 years, 10 months, 10 days. Aelius Eutychus and Paulina [made this].
EDCS-05700519.summary: A funerary dedication for Aelius Iulianus, made by Aelius Eutychus and Paulina, specifying his age.

EDCS-05700520.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Mnemone of the Aelii, who lived one year, 11 months, 21 days, and for Euhodianus, who lived one year, 2 months, 8 days.
EDCS-05700520.summary: A funerary monument commemorating two children, likely from the Aelii family.

EDCS-05700521.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aelius Vitalio, a most sweet infant, who lived 11 months, 10 days. To the spirits of the departed.
EDCS-05700521.summary: A funerary dedication for the infant Aelius Vitalio.

EDCS-05700524.translation: Aelia Doris purchased for herself and for Titus Aelius Onesimus, her freedman, from the donation of Sextus Coelius Fortunatus Iunior, this Vibian monument, which she possessed by hereditary right, with the name written above, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and descendants. In the front 15 feet, in the field 15 feet.
EDCS-05700524.summary: A purchased funerary plot and monument by Aelia Doris for herself and her freedman, specifying dimensions and origin of the donation.

EDCS-05700525.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aelia Helpidis, who lived 16 years, 5 months. Publius Aelius Symphorus, freedman of the emperor, patron and comrade, made this for her, a well-deserving person.
EDCS-05700525.summary: A funerary dedication by a patron and comrade for Aelia Helpidis, who died at age 16 years and 5 months.

EDCS-05700527.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aelia Euresis, whom Gettius Amandus, her brother-in-law, made this for, a well-deserving person.
EDCS-05700527.summary: A funerary dedication by a brother-in-law for Aelia Euresis.

EDCS-05700528.translation: This guardhouse belongs to the heir of Aelia Heuresis, Lucius Gettius Amandus; he and his heir Lucius Gettius Hilarianus, and their freedmen, freedwomen, and descendants, for a front of 26 feet and an area of 32.5 feet.
EDCS-05700528.summary: A property inscription detailing ownership of a guardhouse and its dimensions, belonging to the heirs of Aelia Heuresis.

EDCS-05700529.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For [---], who lived 1 year, 2 months. For [---], who lived 4 years, and [---]s, who lived 3 days, 13 days. For Aelia Nice, for her most beloved parents.
EDCS-05700529.summary: A funerary dedication by Aelia Nice for her parents, also recording the ages of at least two other individuals who died young.

EDCS-05700531.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aelia Ursa, who lived 21 years, 6 months, 10 days. Aelia Sostrate made this for her dearest foster-daughter.
EDCS-05700531.summary: A funerary dedication by Aelia Sostrate for her foster-daughter, Aelia Ursa, who died at age 21 years, 6 months, 10 days.

EDCS-05700537.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Aemilius Posidonianus, son of Marcus, who lived 8 years, 9 months, 11 days. His parents Neptunalis and Veneria made this for their most devoted son.
EDCS-05700537.summary: A funerary dedication by parents Neptunalis and Veneria for their son, Marcus Aemilius Posidonianus, who died at age 8 years, 9 months, 11 days.

EDCS-05700538.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Gnaeus Aemilius Quintianus. Livia Quinta made this for her well-deserving wife.
EDCS-05700538.summary: A funerary dedication by Livia Quinta for her well-deserving wife, Gnaeus Aemilius Quintianus.

EDCS-05700539.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Gaius Aemilius Superstes, who lived 24 years, 8 months, 15 days. His mother Aemilia Nice made this for her well-deserving son. The plot was granted to her by Aulus Flavius Paresiacus.
EDCS-05700539.summary: A funerary dedication by Aemilia Nice for her son, Gaius Aemilius Superstes, who died at age 24 years, 8 months, 15 days, with a note about plot allocation.

EDCS-05700543.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aemilia Fortunata made this for her well-deserving husband, Lucius Tullius Lucrio.
EDCS-05700543.summary: A funerary dedication by Aemilia Fortunata for her well-deserving husband, Lucius Tullius Lucrio.

EDCS-05700545.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aemilia Sabina, daughter of Marcus. Caius Antonius Maximus made this for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-05700545.summary: A funerary dedication by Caius Antonius Maximus for his most devoted wife, Aemilia Sabina.

EDCS-05700546.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aemilia Syntyche, his wife.
EDCS-05700546.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Aemilia Syntyche by her husband.

EDCS-05700551.translation: To the spirits of the departed. [...] Agathemeros to his freedmen and freedwomen, who will be hers by name and whom she will have freed. 16 feet in front, 25 feet in the field.
EDCS-05700551.summary: This inscription records land allocation and manumission by a freedwoman named Agathemeros.

EDCS-05700553.translation: Agathopus made this for himself and his family, and for Salva, his most beloved and most worthy slave-woman, who lived 27 years.
EDCS-05700553.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Agathopus and his slave-woman Salva, made by Agathopus.

EDCS-05700557.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Publius Aelius [...] Ailia Ch[...]
EDCS-05700557.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary dedication to members of the Aelia family.

EDCS-05700559.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For [...]scianus, 5 months, 17 days old. Alcibiades to his most sweet son.
EDCS-05700559.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a child who died at 5 months and 17 days old, dedicated by his father Alcibiades.

EDCS-05700561.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Alledia Herma, Aelius Romulus to his well-deserving wife, who lived 22 years, 1 month, 3 days.
EDCS-05700561.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Alledia Herma, dedicated by her husband Aelius Romulus.

EDCS-05700565.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Anicetus.
EDCS-05700565.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary dedication to Anicetus.

EDCS-05700567.translation: Aninia Hilara made this for her incomparable mother, Claudia Arria, who lived 50 years, 10 months.
EDCS-05700567.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Claudia Arria, dedicated by her daughter Aninia Hilara.

EDCS-05700568.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Anneus Paulus Petrus. Marcus Anneus Paulus to his most beloved son.
EDCS-05700568.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Anneus Paulus Petrus, dedicated by his father, also named Marcus Anneus Paulus.

EDCS-05700570.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Annidia Restituta, who lived 30 years. Gnaeus Egnatius Felix to his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-05700570.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Annidia Restituta, dedicated by her husband Gnaeus Egnatius Felix.

EDCS-05700573.translation: Lucius Annius Felix, freedman of Lucius and (of a woman), Auguralis, for himself, and for Laberia Hilara, and for his son Gaius Laberius Fictor, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their other descendants. In front 30 feet, in the field 25 feet.
EDCS-05700573.summary: This is a funerary dedication from an individual named Lucius Annius Felix, who purchased a plot of land for his family's burials, specifying its dimensions.

EDCS-05700574.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Annius Felix lived 2 years, 3 months.
EDCS-05700574.summary: This is a tombstone fragment for Lucius Annius Felix, recording his short lifespan.

EDCS-05700575.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Annius Mer[---] was made by Marcus Ulpius Suc[---], for his most dutiful and well-deserving brother, who lived [---] years, 7 days.
EDCS-05700575.summary: This is a funerary dedication for a brother named Annius Mer[---], made by Marcus Ulpius Suc[---].

EDCS-05700576.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Annius Serapiodorus, for his most sweet wife Plotia.
EDCS-05700576.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Annius Serapiodorus to his wife Plotia.

EDCS-05700579.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Fabia Felicula made this for her most deserving mother, Antestia Nice.
EDCS-05700579.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Fabia Felicula for her mother, Antestia Nice.

EDCS-05700583.translation: Marcus Antonius Amethystus, freedman of the freedman of Augusta; Antonia Panerotis, freedwoman of the freedman of Augusta; Amor Antonia Panerotis, freedwoman of the freedman of Augusta; for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants. In front 25 feet. Futia Helena, freedwoman of Lucius, for herself and for Aulus Egrilius Felix and Aulus Livius Hermerotus and Lucius Futius Alexander and Aulus Livius Crescenti, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-05700583.summary: This inscription records two families, the Antonii and the Futii, each dedicating burial plots for themselves and their descendants, with a clear demarcation of dimensions.

EDCS-05700585.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Antonius Filumenus prepared this monument for himself, which is within the monument of Caesennius Alexander, with access, right-of-way, and passage, and for Antonia Victorina his wife, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants. In front 5 feet inside, in the field 20 feet.
EDCS-05700585.summary: This is a funerary monument purchase by Marcus Antonius Filumenus for himself, his wife Antonia Victorina, and their descendants, within an existing larger burial plot.

EDCS-05700589.text_edition: \n Antonia Marci liberta Charis fecit Marco Antonio Dracontomeni et sibi et libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum\n
EDCS-05700589.translation: Antonia Charis, freedwoman of Marcus, made this for Marcus Antonius Dracontomenes, and for herself, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-05700589.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Antonia Charis for Marcus Antonius Dracontomenes and for their descendants.

EDCS-05700590.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Quintus Octavius Considianus, for his well-deserving wife, Antonia Laeta, daughter of Gaius.
EDCS-05700590.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Quintus Octavius Considianus to his wife, Antonia Laeta.

EDCS-05700598.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Appuleius Iuvenalis made this for himself, and for Appuleia Acte his freedwoman and wife, and for his most sweet son Lucius Appuleius Augurinus, who lived 4 years, 3 months, 18 days, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants, in the space of a burial chamber granted to him by Detelius Phoebe and Detelius Fannianus.
EDCS-05700598.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Lucius Appuleius Iuvenalis for himself, his wife, his son, and their descendants, in a burial space granted by others.

EDCS-05700603.translation: To the shades of the departed. Arellia Ve[--] lived for [ --] years and 11 months and [--] days.
EDCS-05700603.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Arellia Ve[--], recording her lifespan and likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700605.translation: To the shades of the departed. For Aristia Nicareteni, who lived for 16 years, 9 months, 7 days, and 10 hours. For her most devoted son, Iulio Aristio. For his most sweet wife, Arima. They made this for their well-deserving wife. Eupsychi Nicarete dedicated the place granted by Favia Alexandria.
EDCS-05700605.summary: This funerary inscription records the ages of Aristia Nicareteni and possibly her son Iulio Aristio, and mentions her husband Arimo, with the place granted by Favia Alexandria.

EDCS-05700610.translation: To the shades of the departed. Lucius Arrius Niceros had this made for his son, Lucius Arrius Celestinus, who lived for 1 year, 8 months, and 20 days.
EDCS-05700610.summary: This is a funerary inscription commissioned by Lucius Arrius Niceros for his son Lucius Arrius Celestinus, detailing the child's short life.

EDCS-05700612.translation: To the shades of the departed. For Arria Maximina, daughter of Publius. Her most unfortunate parents dedicated this statue of Venus to their most sweet daughter, who lived for 15 years, 11 months, 11 days, and 3 hours.
EDCS-05700612.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication of a statue of Venus to Arria Maximina by her parents, noting her age at death.

EDCS-05700614.translation: To the shades of the departed. For Caius? Aru[--]timo[--], who lived for 1 year [ --]. Arrun[--] DISI[--]
EDCS-05700614.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for a male individual named Aru[--]timo[--], likely a child, based on the recorded lifespan.

EDCS-05700615.translation: To the shades of the departed. For Marcus Arru[ntius --] Pallad[--], who lived for [ --] years. To her well-deserving [wife/daughter?].
EDCS-05700615.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Marcus Arru[ntius --] Pallad[--], likely a woman, with a portion of the lifespan and a dedicatory phrase.

EDCS-05700618.translation: To the shades of the departed. For Gaius Arruntius Strato, who lived for 40 years. Julia Datiba made this for her well-deserving [husband/son?].
EDCS-05700618.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Gaius Arruntius Strato and indicates it was commissioned by Julia Datiba.

EDCS-05700628.translation: To the shades of the departed. For Atheniqnus, most sweet son, who lived for 2 years, 5 months, and 23 days.
EDCS-05700628.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Atheniqnus, identified as a son, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700629.translation: To the shades of the departed. For Publius Atilius [ --], father [ --] and Atilia [ --]
EDCS-05700629.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning Publius Atilius and Atilia, possibly family members.

EDCS-05700631.translation: To the shades of the departed. For Atilia Politices. Gaius Atilius Augurinus dedicated this to his well-deserving wife. The place was granted by Munatius Onesimus and Munatius Theodo[--]tus.
EDCS-05700631.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication by Gaius Atilius Augurinus to his wife Atilia Politices, with the place granted by Munatius Onesimus and Munatius Theodotus.

EDCS-05700632.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Atilia Istratonice, her husband Atilius Apollodotus, to his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-05700632.summary: A funerary epitaph for Atilia Istratonice, dedicated by her husband, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700639.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius Aufidius Epictetus lived for 77 years, 5 months, and 15 days. The spirits of Aufidius are here now, and he who was once outstanding to all on earth, approved in life, was happy with the gods above, than whom no one else was or has lived more happily, simple, good, and blessed. He was never sad, always happy, rejoicing everywhere, and not similar to old people, he desired to die but feared death and did not think he could die. His wife placed him in the earth and, sad, wept for her wounds, as her husband was so diminished.
EDCS-05700639.summary: A lengthy and poetic epitaph for Publius Aufidius Epictetus, detailing his life, character, and the grief of his wife, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700642.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Augustalia Sabina and L...
EDCS-05700642.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph mentioning Augustalia Sabina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700643.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. In memory of Aurelius, aged...
EDCS-05700643.summary: A fragmented funerary inscription dedicated to the memory of Aurelius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700644.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For [A]urelioria Augus[ti] [daughter?], ...
EDCS-05700644.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph mentioning someone related to the imperial family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700648.translation: To the spirits of the departed. [---]apias to her husband Aurelius [---]us made for himself with his sons [---].
EDCS-05700648.summary: A funerary epitaph with significant lacunae, mentioning a husband and sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700651.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aur(elius) Asclepiodotianus F[---], Victoria Asclepiodote Vi[---], and to his dearest son Aurelius Asclepiodotianus [---] who lived 14 days, and to his son Aur(elius) As[---]no [---].
EDCS-05700651.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicating a monument to multiple family members, including a son who died in infancy, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700652.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aurelius Chresimus, his wife Iaria Irene, with their sons Ianuarius and Chresimus(?), who lived 51 years, 10 months, and 7 days, a well-deserving man.
EDCS-05700652.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Chresimus, detailing his lifespan and mentioning his wife and sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700654.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Aurelius Demosthenes, who lived for one year and 11 days.
EDCS-05700654.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Aurelius Demosthenes, recording his name and very short lifespan, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700656.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Aurelius Faustinus, freedman of the Emperors.
EDCS-05700656.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Aurelius Faustinus, a freedman of the emperors, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700659.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Aurelius Felix to his most sweet son, who lived 11 months and 22 days. Eudoxius, Euchy.
EDCS-05700659.summary: This funerary inscription honors a young child who died at 11 months and 22 days old.

EDCS-05700660.translation: To the departed spirits. Quintus Aurelius Felix and Aurelia Thallusa made this for themselves and their descendants, freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-05700660.summary: This is a self-purchase funerary monument intended for Quintus Aurelius Felix, Aurelia Thallusa, and their descendants and freedpersons.

EDCS-05700662.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelios Heliodorus and Aurhlia Trofime made this for Aurelius Teseus, their well-deserving son.
EDCS-05700662.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication by parents for their deserving son, Aurelius Teseus.

EDCS-05700663.translation: To the departed spirits. For Marcus Aurelius Hyacinthus, freedman of the Augusti, who lived 3 years, 9 months, and 8 days. To the most worthy Aurelia [...].
EDCS-05700663.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Marcus Aurelius Hyacinthus, a freedman of the emperors, who died at a young age, and also mentions Aurelia.

EDCS-05700667.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Aurelius Marianus and Iulia Crescentina to their most sweet son, who lived 8 years and 2 months. Aurelius Cretianus.
EDCS-05700667.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Marcus Aurelius Marianus and Iulia Crescentina to their son, Aurelius Cretianus, who died at the age of 8 years and 2 months.

EDCS-05700668.translation: Lucius Aurelius Priscus, son of Lucius, of the Palatina tribe, made this for himself and for Lucius Aurelius Felix, his father, and for Cloelia Prisca, daughter of Sexus, his mother, and for Aurelia Prisca, daughter of Lucius, his daughter, and for his freedmen Lucius Aurelius Euplus, Lucius Aurelius Florus, Lucius Aurelius Hermas, Lucius Aurelius Atticus, and their freedpersons and descendants. In the field, 25 feet (and a semuncia).
EDCS-05700668.summary: This is a family funerary monument purchased by Lucius Aurelius Priscus for himself, his parents, his daughter, and his freedmen and their descendants, including a land specification.

EDCS-05700670.translation: Marcus Aurelius Secundus made this for himself and for Iulia Aphrodite, his dearest wife, and for his friend Aurelius Romanus, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and descendants. If anyone, after our death, wishes to bury a body not belonging to us here, they shall have paid to the public treasury 50,000 sesterces. In the front, 15 feet; in the field, 13 feet.
EDCS-05700670.summary: This funerary monument, purchased by Marcus Aurelius Secundus for his family and friends, specifies a penalty for unauthorized burials and details the dimensions of the plot.

EDCS-05700671.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Aurelius Threptus made this for Faustina, who deserved well.
EDCS-05700671.summary: This funerary inscription records Marcus Aurelius Threptus dedicating a monument to Faustina, who deserved well.

EDCS-05700672.translation: To the departed spirits. For Marcus Aurelius Trophimus, most deserving, who lived 12 years, [...] months. [...]lia ses[...]
EDCS-05700672.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Marcus Aurelius Trophimus, who died at the age of 12 years and some months.

EDCS-05700674.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelia Candida made this for herself and for her husband Aurelius Sofronius, granting her a pure plot of land with a dining room, ceded by Fanio Oceanus and his sons, except for two bedrooms which are for the use of the aforementioned Oceanus and his sons, their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-05700674.summary: This inscription records Aurelia Candida dedicating a funerary plot with a dining room for herself and her husband, with specific conditions regarding its use by Fanio Oceanus and his family.

EDCS-05700677.translation: To the shades of the departed. Aurelia Prima, who lived 28 years, 3 months, and 14 days, and Aurelia Nice, her most unfortunate mother, made this for their sweetest daughter and [her] husband.
EDCS-05700677.summary: A tombstone set up by a mother for her deceased daughter and son-in-law, providing ages at death.

EDCS-05700679.translation: To the shades of the departed. Marcus Valerius Faustus made this for his incomparable wife, Aurelia Restuta.
EDCS-05700679.summary: A tombstone erected by a husband for his wife, praising her as 'incomparable'.

EDCS-05700683.translation: To the shades of the departed. Axsilia Trophime made this for her most dutiful son, Lucius Axsilio Primitivo, who lived 22 years, 21 days, and 3 hours.
EDCS-05700683.summary: A tombstone set up by a mother for her son, stating his name, age, and duration of life in years, days, and hours.

EDCS-05700685.translation: To the shades of the departed. Her parents made this for Baberia Ianuarie, daughter, who lived 23 years, well-deserving.
EDCS-05700685.summary: A tombstone erected by parents for their daughter, indicating her age and their affection.

EDCS-05700686.translation: To the shades of the departed. Marius Epictectus made this for Bacchis, who lived 13 years, his deserving slave.
EDCS-05700686.summary: A tombstone set up by an owner for his young slave, noting her age and relationship.

EDCS-05700694.translation: To the shades of the departed. For the memory of the son Basilides, well-deserving, who lived one year and 8 months, made by his mother.
EDCS-05700694.summary: A tombstone erected by a mother for her son, specifying his age in years and months.

EDCS-05700695.translation: To the shades of the departed. Bellenius, father, made this for his son, Gaius Bellenius Triumplus Laberianus, who lived 18 years, 6 months, and 17 days; the place was donated by Aufidius Vitalis.
EDCS-05700695.summary: A tombstone erected by a father for his son, including ages and details about the land donation.

EDCS-05700696.translation: To the shades of the departed. Bellicia Marciane [made this] for her well-deserving husband, Gaius Bellicius Kapito.
EDCS-05700696.summary: A tombstone erected by a wife for her husband, describing him as 'well-deserving'.

EDCS-05700698.translation: To the shades of the departed. Caius Litorius Trophimus made this for his well-deserving wife, Blasteni.
EDCS-05700698.summary: A tombstone erected by a husband for his wife, identifying her as 'well-deserving'.

EDCS-05700699.translation: To the shades of the departed. The heirs made this for their well-deserving patron, Gaius Blossius Faustus.
EDCS-05700699.summary: A tombstone erected by heirs for their patron, who is described as 'well-deserving'.

EDCS-05700705.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lucius Cacius Eutactus, freedman of Lucius and [his wife].
EDCS-05700705.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a freedman named Eutactus, dedicated to the spirits of the departed.

EDCS-05700706.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Hilarius, freedman of Lucius Casius and [his wife].
EDCS-05700706.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a freedman named Hilarius, dedicated to the spirits of the departed.

EDCS-05700707.translation: Sacred, holy, chaste, and dutifully dedicated to the spirits of the departed. For Cacia Daphnes, freedwoman of Lucius and [his wife].
EDCS-05700707.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a freedwoman named Cacia Daphnes, dedicated to the spirits of the departed.

EDCS-05700709.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Kacia [---], wife of Lucius Balbilius [---], Kacia Help[is] [---].
EDCS-05700709.summary: This is a funerary inscription, likely for a woman named Kacia Helpis, wife of Lucius Balbilius, dedicated to the spirits of the departed.

EDCS-05700710.translation: For Quintus Caecilius, son of Sextus, of the Galeria tribe, and for Helena, freedwoman of Grania [his wife]. In the front 20 feet, in the field 25 feet.
EDCS-05700710.summary: This inscription records property dimensions (20ft x 25ft) and likely pertains to a funerary monument or plot for Quintus Caecilius and Helena.

EDCS-05700711.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Decimus Caecilius Apronianus, who lived 6 years, 11 months, and 2 days. Aphrodisius, his most dutiful father, made [this] for his son.
EDCS-05700711.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young boy, Decimus Caecilius Apronianus, made by his father, Aphrodisius, around the time of his death.

EDCS-05700713.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Caecilius Felix, who lived 7 years, 9 months, and 4 days. Marcus Caecilius Proculus made [this] for his most beloved son.
EDCS-05700713.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a child named Marcus Caecilius Felix, made by his father, Marcus Caecilius Proculus, around the time of his death.

EDCS-05700716.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For [---] Caecilius Rufinus, who lived 30 years, 2 months, and 19 days. Marcus Caecilius Proculus [made this] for his most devoted brother.
EDCS-05700716.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caecilius Rufinus, made by his brother Marcus Caecilius Proculus, dating to around the time of Rufinus's death.

EDCS-05700720.translation: Died Lucius Caecilius Zabda, freedman of Lucius and Lucius. Died Caecilia Ammia, freedwoman of Lucius and Lucius. Lucius Caecilius Pindarus, freedman of Lucius and [his wife]. Caecilia Salvia, freedwoman of Lucius and [his wife]. Lucius Caecilius Victor, freedman of [his wife]. Lucius Caecilius Machio, freedman of [his wife]. Caecilia Auge, freedwoman of Lucius and [his wife]. Caecilia Lusario, freedwoman of Lucius and [his wife]. Lucius Caecilius Helenus, freedman of [his wife]. Lucius Caecilius Isio, freedman of [his wife]. In the front 20 feet, in the field 25 feet.
EDCS-05700720.summary: This inscription records the death dates and relationships (freedmen and freedwomen) of eleven individuals, likely from a family or household, and specifies plot dimensions.

EDCS-05700722.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Caecilia Felicissima. Titus Salvius Ianuarius, her husband, [dedicated this] for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-05700722.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caecilia Felicissima, dedicated by her husband, Titus Salvius Ianuarius.

EDCS-05700723.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Caecilia Hygia.
EDCS-05700723.summary: This is a funerary dedication on a tombstone for Caecilia Hygia.

EDCS-05700724.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Iulius Faustus made this for his deserving wife, Caecilia Primitiba.
EDCS-05700724.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Caecilia Primitiba, made by her husband Quintus Iulius Faustus, likely from the 1st-2nd century CE.

EDCS-05700726.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caecilia Soteris made this for herself, her son Caecilius Phirrus, Caecilius Primus, and for freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants and future heirs in this monument. [Whoever is received by him...].
EDCS-05700726.summary: A funerary dedication by Caecilia Soteris for herself and her family, including provisions for future heirs and freedpersons, with some partially legible content.

EDCS-05700727.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Decimus Caecilius Aphrodisius made this for Caecilia Tampyris [and?] and Caecilia Laetina.
EDCS-05700727.summary: This funerary dedication by Decimus Caecilius Aphrodisius is for Caecilia Tampyris and Caecilia Laetina.

EDCS-05700728.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Caelius Castricius and Iulia Victorina made this for their son Gaius Caecilius Rusticus, who lived 15 years, 9 months, and 16 days. The plot, conceded by Celsinus and Fortunatus, is located in the right section.
EDCS-05700728.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Gaius Caecilius Rusticus at age 15, with his parents Gaius Caelius Castricius and Iulia Victorina as dedicants, and details about the plot of land.

EDCS-05700730.translation: To the spirits of the departed. They made this for Marcus Cesellius, son of Pietas.
EDCS-05700730.summary: A funerary dedication for Marcus Cesellius, identified as 'son of Pietas', with a plural verb suggesting multiple dedicants.

EDCS-05700731.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Titus Caesellius Saturus.
EDCS-05700731.summary: A simple funerary dedication for Titus Caesellius Saturus.

EDCS-05700734.translation: Aulus Caesennius Herma, freedman of Aulus Caesennius Gallus, and Aulus Caesennius Italicus and Caesennia Erotis, freedwoman of Lucius, his wife, made this tomb enclosed by stone walls for themselves, their own, their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants. In front, 300 feet; in the field, 96 feet, which makes [X] jugera.
EDCS-05700734.summary: This inscription records the construction of a family tomb by Aulus Caesennius Herma and Aulus Caesennius Italicus with his wife Caesennia Erotis, for themselves and their descendants, including freedpersons, and specifies the dimensions of the burial plot.

EDCS-05700735.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Caesennia Erotis. Aulus Caesennius Herma made this altar and statues for himself and for his wife, who deserved well of him.
EDCS-05700735.summary: A funerary dedication by Aulus Caesennius Herma for his wife Caesennia Erotis, recording the erection of an altar and statues.

EDCS-05700736.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Caesennia Galene, dearest mother. Italicus made [this?].
EDCS-05700736.summary: A funerary dedication for Caesennia Galene, 'dearest mother', likely made by her son Italicus.

EDCS-05700737.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caesennia Nymphice made this for her well-deserving son, Caesennius Italicus, who lived 27 years, 6 months, and 28 days.
EDCS-05700737.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a son, set up by his mother, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05700746.translation: For the memory of [---]. Callis[---], freedman of the Palatine tribe, and [---]chani, who lived 11 years, 1 month, and 9 days, on the 24th day before the Kalends of September. Caltilius Epictetus Pa[---]. [---]
EDCS-05700746.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph commemorating a young boy, likely from the Imperial period, with details of his death date.

EDCS-05700756.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Calpurnius Valens lived 50 years and 10 months. For Calpurnius Philero, and Lucius Calpurnius Secundianus, and Lucius Calpurnius Valens.
EDCS-05700756.summary: This funerary epitaph records the age of death for Lucius Calpurnius Valens and names two other individuals, likely family members, from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05700759.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Lucius Calpurnius Reginus, who lived 30 years and 8 hours. His daughter Calpurnia Onesime and Bibia Isias made this.
EDCS-05700759.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Calpurnius Reginus, set up by his daughter and another woman, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05700762.translation: Lucius Calpurnius Valens made this for himself and for his well-deserving wife Calpurnia Psyche, with whom he lived [3 years], and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants, [---] 40 feet of land [in depth] and 60 feet [in width].
EDCS-05700762.summary: This is a funerary monument inscription recording the dedication by Lucius Calpurnius Valens for himself, his wife, and descendants, specifying burial plot dimensions.

EDCS-05700772.translation: Publius Caninius Restitutus, freedman of Publius [Caninius] and [his wife], made this for himself, and for his freedwoman Caninia Nice, and for their heirs or heiress, and for those born to them, and it is not permitted to inscribe an outside name; 9 feet in front, 12 feet in depth.
EDCS-05700772.summary: This funerary inscription records a self-dedication and dedication for a freedwoman, with stipulations about future burials and plot dimensions, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05700776.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Floria Cratia made this for her most sweet Carantiae Nice, well-deserving.
EDCS-05700776.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Carantiae Nice, set up by Floria Cratia, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05700777.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Carfia Marcella, daughter of Titus. She made this for herself.
EDCS-05700777.summary: This is a funerary epitaph where Carfia Marcella dedicates the monument to herself, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05700779.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Carvilia Hilara [made this for] her most sweet and excellent parent, Lucius Carvilius Hilarus.
EDCS-05700779.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication by Carvilia Hilara to her father, Lucius Carvilius Hilarus, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05700780.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Victorinus, freedman of the emperor, [made this] for his well-deserving wife, Carullia [---]a.
EDCS-05700780.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication by Victorinus, an imperial freedman, to his wife, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05700782.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caryllius Charito made this for his wife Caryllia Eutychia, who well deserved it.
EDCS-05700782.summary: A tombstone inscription by a husband for his deceased wife, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700783.translation: Marcus Cascellius Diadumenus made this for himself, [and for] three columbaria and six jars for Cacía Heuhodia, his wife, and his descendants. To the spirits of the departed.
EDCS-05700783.summary: An epitaph by Marcus Cascellius Diadumenus for himself, his wife, and descendants, mentioning burial structures, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700786.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Cassia Ostiensia, daughter of Quintus Cassius, made this for her most beloved brother, Quintus Cassius Martialis, son of Quintus, and also for herself and her freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants, with Quintus Cassius Ianuarius and Tillia Sarnia, parents, supervising.
EDCS-05700786.summary: An epitaph by a sister for her brother, also reserving space for herself and descendants, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700790.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Cassius Trofímianus donated this place for his daughter Cassia Helpis, who lived 11 years. Father.
EDCS-05700790.summary: A funerary inscription by a father donating a burial plot for his deceased daughter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700791.translation: For eternal rest. Carminia Cassia Naeacore [for her mother] Cassia Rufina, who lived 27 years, 4 months, [and X] days. Sextus Carminius Neophytus [for his wife] Carminia Cassia Naeacore, dearest wife.
EDCS-05700791.summary: A funerary inscription for Cassia Rufina and her daughter Carminia Cassia Naeacore, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700793.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Catilius Epagathus made this for his most beloved parents, Lucius Catilius Epagathus and Catilia Hygeia.
EDCS-05700793.summary: A tombstone inscription by a son for his deceased parents, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700795.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Cavarius Epictetus made this for himself and for Cavaria Prima, his most rare wife, and for his daughter Cavaria Matidia, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. Frontage 9.5 feet, depth 9 feet.
EDCS-05700795.summary: A funerary inscription by Gaius Cavarius Epictetus for himself, his wife, daughter, and descendants, specifying plot dimensions, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700796.translation: For Gavia Fortunata and Gavia Felicula, granted to them as a donation by Publius Gasius Restitutus, in full, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. Frontage 24.5 feet, depth 20 feet.
EDCS-05700796.summary: An inscription recording the donation of a burial plot by Publius Gasius Restitutus to Gavia Fortunata and Gavia Felicula, specifying dimensions, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700806.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Chelidonius.
EDCS-05700806.summary: A fragmentary tombstone inscription, likely an epitaph for a person named Chelidonius.

EDCS-05700807.translation: To the spirits of the departed. The sons made this for Chronia, their most indulgent mother.
EDCS-05700807.summary: A tombstone inscription by sons for their mother, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700811.translation: To the departed spirits. Publius Cincius Firmus made this for his son, Publius Cincius Firmus, dearest son, who lived 9 years and 4 days.
EDCS-05700811.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Publius Cincius Firmus for his son.

EDCS-05700812.translation: To the departed spirits. Sabidia Eutychia made this for her foster-daughter, Cincia Marcella, who lived 12 years and 8 months.
EDCS-05700812.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Sabidia Eutychia for her foster-daughter, Cincia Marcella.

EDCS-05700813.translation: To the departed spirits. Cincius Firmus made this for his well-deserving wife, Cincia Trofima.
EDCS-05700813.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Cincius Firmus for his wife, Cincia Trofima.

EDCS-05700814.translation: To the departed spirits and in memory of Marcus Cippius Fortunatus the younger. Marcus Cipius Ostiensis and Aelia Hosia made this for themselves, their dearest son, who lived 6 years, 10 months, and 9 days, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. Front 11.5 feet, land 9 feet.
EDCS-05700814.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Marcus Cipius Ostiensis and Aelia Hosia for their family and descendants, specifying plot dimensions.

EDCS-05700815.translation: To the departed spirits. Labius made this for his well-deserving wife, Cirria Secundina.
EDCS-05700815.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Labius for his wife, Cirria Secundina.

EDCS-05700818.translation: To the departed spirits. [---] for Claudius [---]pinus, a place [---]enus.
EDCS-05700818.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph mentioning Claudius [---]pinus and a 'place'.

EDCS-05700819.translation: In memory of Tiberius Claudius [---]anius, son of Tiberius Claudius, who lived [---] years.
EDCS-05700819.summary: This is a fragmentary epitaph commemorating Tiberius Claudius [---]anius.

EDCS-05700821.translation: Tiberius Claudius Alexander, freedman of Augustus, for himself, and for Carvilia Arescusa, freedwoman of the same, his wife, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and all their descendants. Front 20 feet, land 25 feet.
EDCS-05700821.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Tiberius Claudius Alexander, an imperial freedman, for his family, specifying plot dimensions.

EDCS-05700824.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Claudius Evaristianus and Iulia Felicitas made this for their most dutiful sons, Marcus Claudius Evaristianus and Marcus Claudius [---]ianus, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. Front 20 feet, land [---] feet.
EDCS-05700824.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Marcus Claudius Evaristianus and Iulia Felicitas for their sons and descendants, specifying plot dimensions.

EDCS-05700826.translation: To the departed spirits. Tiberius Claudius Felix made this for himself and for his freedwoman Claudia Faustina, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-05700826.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Tiberius Claudius Felix for himself, his freedwoman, and their descendants.

EDCS-05700827.translation: To the departed spirits. Tiberius Claudius Felix, freedman of the emperors, made this for himself, his freedmen and freedwomen, and their descendants, a portion granted to him by Nobia Helpis, the inner half of the entrance.
EDCS-05700827.summary: A funerary monument erected by a freedman, Tiberius Claudius Felix, for himself and his dependents, detailing a granted portion of space.

EDCS-05700829.translation: Claudius Fortunatus made this for Maria Felicitas, his wife.
EDCS-05700829.summary: A funerary dedication by Claudius Fortunatus for his wife, Maria Felicitas.

EDCS-05700830.translation: To the dearest son, who lived 25 years, 10 months, 18 days, 6 hours. Claudius Heraclianus and Petronia Veronice granted the place with the consent of...
EDCS-05700830.summary: A funerary dedication for a son, detailing his lifespan and naming Claudius Heraclianus and Petronia Veronice as grantors of the burial plot.

EDCS-05700834.translation: To the departed spirits. Attia Thallusa of Ostia made this for herself, for Attius Hermetus, her father, and for their descendants, 3 feet in front and 2 feet in the rear.
EDCS-05700834.summary: A funerary monument erected by Attia Thallusa for herself, her father Attius Hermetus, and their descendants, specifying plot dimensions.

EDCS-05700843.translation: To the departed spirits. Ulpius [---] and Her[---] for his well-deserving wife, possibly for a certain Cl(audius?) Au[---] Andr[---].
EDCS-05700843.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription where Ulpius and Her--- dedicate a plot to a well-deserving wife.

EDCS-05700844.translation: Claudia Chreste lived 3 years, 6 months, 15 days, 7 hours. Claudia Amabilis lived 3 years, 6 months, 20 days, 9 hours. Twin girls, 10 feet in front and 10 feet in the rear.
EDCS-05700844.summary: A funerary epitaph for two twin girls, Claudia Chreste and Claudia Amabilis, detailing their lifespans and the dimensions of their burial plot.

EDCS-05700845.translation: To the departed spirits. For Claudia Marcia, the most sacred, unique woman.
EDCS-05700845.summary: A funerary dedication to Claudia Marcia, described as a unique and sacred woman.

EDCS-05700849.translation: To the departed spirits. For Claudia Victorina, who lived [---] months, [---] days. For the most excellent [---] of Claudia Car[---], her foster-daughter.
EDCS-05700849.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription honoring Claudia Victorina and her foster-daughter.

EDCS-05700851.translation: Marcus Clodius Crescens, who lived 22 years, 3 months, 21 days. The plot was granted to him by Mindius Faustus.
EDCS-05700851.summary: An epitaph for Marcus Clodius Crescens, recording his lifespan and the source of his burial plot grant.

EDCS-05700853.translation: Decimus Clodius Marcellus for himself, and for Servilia Rodinenus, freedwoman of Gnaeus, his well-deserving wife, and for Decimus Clodius Felix, his brother, and for Licinia Themis, his wife, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and descendants, 15 feet in front and 20 feet in the rear.
EDCS-05700853.summary: A funerary monument erected by Decimus Clodius Marcellus for his family, including his wife, brother, and brother's wife, and their dependents, specifying plot dimensions.

EDCS-05700854.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Clodia Domitia made this monument for herself, her husband Terentius Regio, her daughter Clodia Regina, and their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants; this monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-05700854.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Clodia Domitia for herself and her family, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700855.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Cluvius Marcianus and Aurelia Origenia made this for themselves, their sons, and their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants; in front 11 feet, in the field 12 feet.
EDCS-05700855.summary: A funerary monument established by Cluvius Marcianus and Aurelia Origenia for their family, specifying burial plot dimensions, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700856.translation: Lucius Cocceius Adiutor made [this]. He also decrees that no one should wish to disturb or bury a corpse in the left part when entering; otherwise, he will pay 50,000 sesterces to the public treasury of Ostia, and the informer will receive a quarter.
EDCS-05700856.summary: A funerary regulation by Lucius Cocceius Adiutor, establishing penalties for disturbing a burial, likely for a public cemetery in Ostia, imperial period.

EDCS-05700860.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Coelius Iuvenis, who lived 18 years and 2 months.
EDCS-05700860.summary: A funerary dedication for Coelius Iuvenis, recording his age at death, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700862.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lucius Combarisius Alypus, a deserving freedman.
EDCS-05700862.summary: A funerary dedication for a freedman named Lucius Combarisius Alypus, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700863.translation: Lucius Combarisius Zoticus prepared this while alive for himself and for Combarisia Faustina, his incomparable deceased wife.
EDCS-05700863.summary: A funerary monument prepared by Lucius Combarisius Zoticus for himself and his deceased wife, Combarisia Faustina, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700864.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Publius Cominius Flavianus, who lived 3 years, 3 months, and 10 days. Lucius Ostiensis [made this].
EDCS-05700864.summary: A funerary dedication for Publius Cominius Flavianus, recording his age at death, made by Lucius Ostiensis, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700867.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Quintus Connius Agathopus, for his most beloved son Lucius Valerius Valerianus.
EDCS-05700867.summary: A funerary dedication by Quintus Connius Agathopus for his son Lucius Valerius Valerianus, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700868.translation: A pot of Aulus Considius Felix, for his freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-05700868.summary: A funerary monument or burial container established by Aulus Considius Felix for his freedmen and their descendants, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700870.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Considia Donata made this for herself and her family, freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants in this monument after my death, 'I have permitted my own affairs to be undertaken'; 'I have permitted a tomb entrance and drawing of water'; also, in addition to this, for Publius Sempronius Saturninus, a deserving husband. Evil intent is absent from this tomb.
EDCS-05700870.summary: A funerary monument established by Considia Donata for herself and her family, including her husband Publius Sempronius Saturninus, with specific clauses regarding its use and integrity, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-05700871.translation: To the departed spirits. To Gaius Consius Euporus, his most devoted sons Agrippa and Euporus. [Made] for their most devoted father.
EDCS-05700871.summary: This funerary inscription, dating likely to the 1st-2nd century CE, records the dedication by two sons for their deceased father, Gaius Consius Euporus.

EDCS-05700872.translation: To the departed spirits. [For] Cordia, who lived 28 years, 5 months, 10 days. Iustinus [made this] for his deserving wife.
EDCS-05700872.summary: This funerary inscription records the ages of a deceased woman, Cordia, and is dedicated by her husband, Iustinus.

EDCS-05700873.translation: [...] O [...]. [Possibly Cornelius] lived [...]. Believe that death [...] is nothing [...] as I [...] say Cornelius?
EDCS-05700873.summary: This fragmentary inscription, possibly funerary or philosophical, contains a few legible words and many lacunae, with a potential mention of Cornelius.

EDCS-05700878.translation: To the departed spirits. For Lucius Cornelius Ampliatus, who lived 11 years, 4 months, 22 days, and for his mother Cornelia Bacchis, and for his father Lucius Cornelius Epaphroditus. Cornelius Optatus and Cornelia Praetorina, children, [made this] for their sweetest brother and parents. And Lucius Cornelius Evaristus, freedman, [made this] for his deserving patrons.
EDCS-05700878.summary: This funerary inscription honors Lucius Cornelius Ampliatus, his mother Cornelia Bacchis, and his father Lucius Cornelius Epaphroditus, with dedications from his siblings and a freedman.

EDCS-05700880.translation: To the departed spirits. To Marcus Cornelius Callidromus, his sweetest son, who lived one year, 9 months. Marcus Cornelius Callidromus, his father, made this.
EDCS-05700880.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of a young child, Marcus Cornelius Callidromus, and is dedicated by his father of the same name.

EDCS-05700884.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gnaeus Cornelius Eutychus. Flavia Felicitas made this for her incomparable wife.
EDCS-05700884.summary: This funerary inscription records Flavia Felicitas dedicating a monument to her wife, Gnaeus Cornelius Eutychus.

EDCS-05700889.translation: To the departed spirits. To Lucius Cornelius Saturninus. Cornelia Felissima made this for her husband.
EDCS-05700889.summary: This funerary inscription records Cornelia Felissima dedicating a monument to her husband, Lucius Cornelius Saturninus.

EDCS-05700891.translation: To the departed spirits. Of Gnaeus Cornelius Surus.
EDCS-05700891.summary: This funerary inscription is a simple dedication to the departed spirit of Gnaeus Cornelius Surus.

EDCS-05700892.translation: To the departed spirits. For Cornelia Charis. Cornelia Myrsine, her slave, made this for her. She lived 5 years.
EDCS-05700892.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication by a slave, Cornelia Myrsine, for her young mistress, Cornelia Charis, who died at age five.

EDCS-05700895.translation: To the departed spirits. For Cornelia Paulina. Marcus Salinator Livianus arranged for this to be made for his deserving wife, and for Ovilio Aepictetianus.
EDCS-05700895.summary: This funerary inscription records Marcus Salinator Livianus commissioning a monument for his wife, Cornelia Paulina, and also for Ovilio Aepictetianus.

EDCS-05700896.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Cornelius Quartinus made this for his well-deserving wife, Cornelia Severina, who lived 25 years, 10 months, 11 days, and 8 hours.
EDCS-05700896.summary: A funerary epitaph for Cornelia Severina, set up by her husband, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700903.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Caius Curtius Alexander, who lived 40 years and 11 days. Caius Curtius Charito to his fellow freedman.
EDCS-05700903.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Curtius Alexander, dedicated by his fellow freedman, Caius Curtius Charito.

EDCS-05700906.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Cyrilla, freedwoman of Valerius Iulianus, who lived 27 years, she left a daughter of 7 years. Her husband Sempronius Iustus made this.
EDCS-05700906.summary: A funerary epitaph for Cyrilla, a freedwoman, set up by her husband, indicating her age and the survival of a young daughter.

EDCS-05700909.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Decimius Maron for his most holy daughter, Decimia Eutaxia, and also Aurelia Afias for her most sweet daughter, who lived 2 years, 7 months, 24 days, by permission of Titinius Privatus.
EDCS-05700909.summary: A funerary epitaph for two young daughters, one dedicated by their father and the other by a mother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05700912.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Detelius Fannianus, son of Marcus, is laid here. He lived 16 years and 10 months. His mother Fannia Stratonice made this.
EDCS-05700912.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Detelius Fannianus, who died young, erected by his mother.

EDCS-05700920.translation: To the spirits of the departed. I, Gaius Domitius Primus, am here in this tomb, Primus, the most renowned. I drank frequently of Lucrine wine and Falernian, Venus has grown old with me over the years. If I could have this, may the earth be soft for me, and may the phoenix preserve me on the altar, he who hurries to renew himself for himself. A place for burial was granted for Gaius Domitius Primus by his three freedmen, Hermeros, Threpte, and a freedman and freedwoman, Pia and Pius.
EDCS-05700920.summary: A self-penned funerary epitaph for Gaius Domitius Primus, who boasts of a life of pleasure, set up by his freedmen.

EDCS-05700924.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Domitia Cleopatra.
EDCS-05700924.summary: A simple funerary epitaph for Domitia Cleopatra.

EDCS-05700925.translation: To the spirits of the departed.
