EDCS-05701636.summary: This funerary inscription records two individuals, Publius Sextilius Communis and his heir, dedicating a monument to Sextilia Iuliana, a freedwoman.

EDCS-05701639.translation: To the shades of the underworld. The heirs of Servus, son of Sextus, of Carpophorus.
EDCS-05701639.summary: This is a tomb inscription indicating that the heirs of Servus, son of Sextus, and Carpophorus, are dedicating this to the deceased.

EDCS-05701646.translation: To Silvanus the Boy, who lived for 16 years, 6 months, and 5 days. From Gaius Petronius Secundus.
EDCS-05701646.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for a boy named Silvanus, recording his lifespan and mentioning his dedicant, Gaius Petronius Secundus.

EDCS-05701649.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Publius Sirtius Hymnus made this for his foster-son, Publius Sirtius Philocyrius.
EDCS-05701649.summary: This is a tomb inscription where Publius Sirtius Hymnus dedicated a monument to his foster-son, Publius Sirtius Philocyrius.

EDCS-05701652.translation: To the shades of the underworld. For Sphendophoros and Philinice.
EDCS-05701652.summary: This is a fragmented tomb inscription honoring two individuals, Sphendophoros and Philinice.

EDCS-05701656.translation: Marcus Statilius Mercurius made this for himself and for Licinia Vitalis, his most beloved wife, and for my freedmen Statilius Genialis and Statilia Primitiva, and for my freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants, granting the entrance, exit, and all rights to possess what has been granted to me by Titus Flavius Rufinus, in the left half as you enter, 33 feet in the front, 30 feet in the field. Granted to Euphrosynus and Heraclianus, slaves of our Emperor, by Flavius Rufinus in the Iunian monument, which is mine, on the right as you enter, in the triclinium, where there are 14 ossuaries in the wall, and in the triclia, 3 sarcophagi. The total number which I have assigned is of both halves, and to Statilius Mercurius and to the persons listed above.
EDCS-05701656.summary: This is a complex funerary inscription detailing the allocation of burial spaces and rights within a family columbarium, including provisions for the owner, his wife, freedmen, and imperial slaves, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05701661.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Sextus Gavius Scantianus, son of Sextus, made this for his most devoted mother, Steia Fortunata, freedwoman of Publius Steius Felix, for whom he was heir to the extent of one-sixth share. And Sextus Gavius made this for his most beloved wife, Augusta, with whom she married and lived most sweetly for 21 years until the day of her death.
EDCS-05701661.summary: This tomb inscription records Sextus Gavius Scantianus dedicating a monument to his mother, Steia Fortunata, and also mentions his wife, Augusta, to whom he was married for 21 years.

EDCS-05701663.translation: To the shades of the underworld. For Lucius Subrius Felix, who lived 36 years. Laelia Quinta for her most devoted husband.
EDCS-05701663.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Lucius Subrius Felix, who lived 36 years, dedicated by his wife, Laelia Quinta.

EDCS-05701668.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Lucius Sulpicius Maximus and Sulpicia Venusta made this for Lucius Sulpicius Ianuarius, their father.
EDCS-05701668.summary: This tomb inscription records Lucius Sulpicius Maximus and Sulpicia Venusta dedicating a monument to their father, Lucius Sulpicius Ianuarius.

EDCS-05701670.translation: Sulpicia Ampliata donated a columbarium and a space for Pinnia Nebris, her daughter, and for the adjoining area.
EDCS-05701670.summary: This inscription records Sulpicia Ampliata donating a columbarium and an adjacent space to her daughter, Pinnia Nebris.

EDCS-05701673.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Claudia Aegle made this for her most devoted daughter, Thathia Aegle, and for herself.
EDCS-05701673.summary: This tomb inscription shows Claudia Aegle dedicating a monument to her devoted daughter, Thathia Aegle, and also reserving a place for herself.

EDCS-05701674.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius Tadius Saturninus, who is also called Sterceius, made this for Tadiae Euhemeria, his freedwoman and most beloved wife.
EDCS-05701674.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to a freedwoman and wife by her husband.

EDCS-05701682.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Terentia [name lost] who lived approximately [number lost] years.
EDCS-05701682.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the name and lifespan of the deceased, Terentia.

EDCS-05701687.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Tertullius, sweetest son, who lived one year, three months, and twenty-one days. His mother Tertullina [made this].
EDCS-05701687.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of a very young child, Tertullius, dedicated by his mother.

EDCS-05701689.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Publius Thuranus Quartinus.
EDCS-05701689.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to Publius Thuranus Quartinus.

EDCS-05701690.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Thymelenis, his most beloved wife, who lived 25 years and 10 months. Hermes made this for himself and for his sweetest son, Aelius Ianuarius.
EDCS-05701690.summary: This is a funerary epitaph where Hermes dedicates a plot for himself, his deceased wife Thymelenis, and his son Aelius Ianuarius.

EDCS-05701694.translation: Who lived twenty years and four months and twelve hours, of which one year, five months [date lost] with me. Tiberinus [made this] for his deserving wife.
EDCS-05701694.summary: This is a funerary epitaph where Tiberinus dedicates a plot for his wife, detailing her lifespan and time spent together.

EDCS-05701704.translation: Of the Tuccii. Telephus and Alexander. In front [measures] feet. In the field [measures] feet.
EDCS-05701704.summary: This inscription appears to record land measurements, possibly for a property belonging to Telephus and Alexander of the Tuccii family.

EDCS-05701709.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Tullia Charito[n] [name lost], who lived 25 years and [number lost] months, and for Aurelius Assyrius Iu[lius? name lost], and Aurelia Maximilla [name lost]. Marcus Aurelius Adsyrus F[ilial name lost] [made this] for his deserving wife and for himself. [Ownership] [location lost] donated.
EDCS-05701709.summary: This is a funerary epitaph where Marcus Aurelius Adsyrus dedicates a plot for his deceased wife Tullia Charito[n], and potentially others, as well as for himself.

EDCS-05701714.translation: Tutilia Rufa, daughter of Lucius, for herself and for Lucius Tutilius, her father, and Seia, her mother. [Names lost] by the judgment of Lucius Scribonius Cinnas and Fabia Psyche. This monument will not pass to the heir.
EDCS-05701714.summary: This is a funerary epitaph where Tutilia Rufa dedicates a monument for herself and her parents, specifying that it is not inheritable.

EDCS-05701719.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Valerius Euhodus [made this] for Valeria Chrysidi, his wife.
EDCS-05701719.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by Lucius Valerius Euhodus to his deceased wife, Valeria Chrysidi.

EDCS-05701722.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valerius Ianuarius made this for his most devoted wife, Genucia Terpsa.
EDCS-05701722.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a husband for his wife, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-05701723.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Valerius Epictetus and Egrilia Faustina made this for their sweetest son, Gaius Valerius Lamyros, son of Gaius, who lived 10 years and 9 days.
EDCS-05701723.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by parents for their young son, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-05701725.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Valerius Nemorensis made this for his most devoted son, Lucius Valerius Marcellinus, who died in his 20th year on his birthday.
EDCS-05701725.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a father for his son who died young, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-05701730.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Sempronius Postimus made this for his well-deserving mother, Valeria Datiba, who lived 60 years.
EDCS-05701730.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a son for his mother, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-05701735.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Valerius Eutychus made this for his well-deserving wife, Valeria Faustina.
EDCS-05701735.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a husband for his wife, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-05701736.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Annius Marcion made this for his most sacred wife, Valeria Fecunda. The site was granted by Gnaeus Sergius Felix.
EDCS-05701736.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a husband for his wife, including a note about land grant, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-05701737.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Fortunatus made this for his well-deserving wife, Valeria Felicitas.
EDCS-05701737.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a husband for his wife, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-05701738.translation: To the spirits of the departed. The most devoted parents, Flavia Eutychia and Fortunatus, made this for their sweetest daughter, Valeria Fortunata, freedwoman of Titus, who lived 4 years, 10 months, and 4 days.
EDCS-05701738.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by parents for their young daughter, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-05701751.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Salonius Theodotus made this for his incomparable wife, Varenia Diogenis.
EDCS-05701751.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a husband for his wife, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-05701752.translation: To the spirits of the departed. The sons made this for their most devoted father, Vario Eutychetes, an excellent man, who lived 67 years.
EDCS-05701752.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by sons for their father, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-05701753.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Varius Plo[---] and Varia Ep[---], likely a family monument.

EDCS-05701754.translation: To the Divine Shades. Manlia Felicula made this for her most dutiful son Publius Vedius Priscus and for herself.
EDCS-05701754.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by Manlia Felicula to her son Publius Vedius Priscus and to herself.

EDCS-05701755.translation: To the Divine Shades. Publius Vedius Telesphorus made this for his most chaste wife Vedia Chreste.
EDCS-05701755.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by Publius Vedius Telesphorus to his wife Vedia Chreste.

EDCS-05701760.translation: To the Divine Shades. Valeria Veneria lived 28 years.
EDCS-05701760.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Valeria Veneria, who died at the age of 28.

EDCS-05701762.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lucius Ventilius [---] made this for himself while alive, and for Arruntia X[---], and for their sons, freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants, in front 23 feet, in the field [---] feet.
EDCS-05701762.summary: This is a funerary monument established by Lucius Ventilius for himself and his family, including his wife Arruntia, and descendants, specifying dimensions.

EDCS-05701763.translation: To the Divine Shades. Venu[---] Polynices D[---]
EDCS-05701763.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning Venu[---] and Polynices D[---].

EDCS-05701770.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aulus Egrilius Eutychus and Sextus Vestilius D[---] made this for Vestilia Ti[---], deserving of favor.
EDCS-05701770.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by Aulus Egrilius Eutychus and Sextus Vestilius D[---] to Vestilia Ti[---].

EDCS-05701772.translation: To the Divine Shades. Publius Vettius Eutyches and Roscia Procula made this for their sweetest son, Publius Vettius Eutychianus, who lived 2 years, 11 months, 3 days.
EDCS-05701772.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Publius Vettius Eutychianus at the age of 2 years, 11 months, and 3 days, and was dedicated by his parents.

EDCS-05701773.translation: To the Divine Shades. Caius Vettius Hilarus, father, made this for his sweetest son, Lucius Sulpicius Ireneus.
EDCS-05701773.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by Caius Vettius Hilarus to his son Lucius Sulpicius Ireneus.

EDCS-05701774.translation: To the Divine Shades. Gaius Vettius Hilarus has been placed in his property; Gaius Vettius Alexander will place [him/it].
EDCS-05701774.summary: This funerary inscription indicates that Gaius Vettius Hilarus was interred on his own property, with Gaius Vettius Alexander responsible for the placement.

EDCS-05701778.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Gaius Vettius Victor, who lived 14 years and 10 days. Vettia Felicitas, his mother.
EDCS-05701778.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Vettius Victor, set up by his mother, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05701781.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Vettia Ianuarie and Stabereus Primitivus. Titus Staberius Asiaticus made this for his deserving wife and son.
EDCS-05701781.summary: A tombstone for Vettia Ianuarie and Stabereus Primitivus, set up by Titus Staberius Asiaticus, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05701786.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Veturia Marcella, who lived 36 years, 11 months, 9 days, and 7 hours. Gaius Vettius Epicianus, for his incomparable wife, with whom I lived 12 years, 4 months, and 11 days.
EDCS-05701786.summary: A tombstone for Veturia Marcella, set up by her husband Gaius Vettius Epicianus, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05701788.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Quintus Vibius [...], who lived [...] months and 21 days.
EDCS-05701788.summary: A fragmentary tombstone for Quintus Vibius, with some details of his lifespan preserved, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05701794.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Vibie Restituta, who lived 4 years, 2 months, and 20 days.
EDCS-05701794.summary: A tombstone for Vibie Restituta, detailing her young age at death, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05701799.translation: Vipsanius Felix made this for his deserving freedman Vipsanius Felix and for his excellent wife Plaria Tycha.
EDCS-05701799.summary: A tombstone set up by Vipsanius Felix for his freedman and his wife, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05701801.translation: [...]simus / [...]cos / [...] To the spirits of the departed. For Vipsania Fortunata. Vipsanius Menophas made this for his most beloved and most dutiful wife.
EDCS-05701801.summary: A tombstone for Vipsania Fortunata, set up by her husband Vipsanius Menophas, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05701805.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Ulpius Maelis Helpidefori.
EDCS-05701805.summary: A tombstone for Marcus Ulpius Maelis Helpidefori, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05701806.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Ulpius Marcianus, my son, and for Marcus Ulpius Eutactus, my husband. Ulpia made this for her dearest son and husband.
EDCS-05701806.summary: A tombstone for Marcus Ulpius Marcianus and Marcus Ulpius Eutactus, set up by Ulpia, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05701808.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Ulpius Theona.
EDCS-05701808.summary: A tombstone for Marcus Ulpius Theona, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05701813.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Ulpia Fronime made this for Ulpia Ammia, mother, and Ulpia Afrodise, sister, and for her husband Petilius Fructus, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. In the front 18 feet, in the depth 20 feet.
EDCS-05701813.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Ulpia Fronime for her mother, sister, and husband, and their descendants, with specified dimensions for the plot.

EDCS-05701814.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Ulpia Helpis.
EDCS-05701814.summary: A simple funerary inscription dedicated to Ulpia Helpis.

EDCS-05701815.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Ulpia Ianuaria made this for herself and for Titus Aelius Onesimus, freedman of the Emperor, her excellent husband, and for her freedmen and freedwomen, and for the descendants of the same Onesimus.
EDCS-05701815.summary: This funerary monument was set up by Ulpia Ianuaria for herself, her husband Onesimus (a freedman of the emperor), and their freedpersons and descendants.

EDCS-05701816.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Ulpia Thallusa and Ulpia Victorina, his daughter. Probianus for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-05701816.summary: A funerary inscription set up by Probianus for his wife Ulpia Thallusa and daughter Ulpia Victorina.

EDCS-05701817.translation: [Made by] Graapte, freedwoman(?) of Papiria, daughter of Marcus Papirius Umbrinus.
EDCS-05701817.summary: This is a funerary inscription possibly set up by a freedwoman named Graapte for her patron.

EDCS-05701818.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Umbrius Faustus and his most dutiful daughter Aphrodisias, daughter of Modia Hegemonis, made this. She lived 7 years, 7 months, and for themselves and their descendants. For a plot given to them by Publius Fabius Callistratus, 4 feet long, 1.5 feet wide.
EDCS-05701818.summary: This funerary monument was established by Titus Umbrius Faustus and his daughter for his other very dutiful daughter Aphrodisias, and for themselves and their descendants, using a small plot of land.

EDCS-05701820.translation: Voltidia Agathemeris, alumna of Gaius Voltelius Aemilianus, made this for her well-deserving patron.
EDCS-05701820.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Voltidia Agathemeris, an alumna, to her patron Gaius Voltelius Aemilianus.

EDCS-05701826.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Voltidia Priscilla, who lived 35 years. Publius Aufidius Saturninus for his wife.
EDCS-05701826.summary: A funerary inscription erected by Publius Aufidius Saturninus for his wife Voltidia Priscilla.

EDCS-05701827.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. A little one, Anthus, in the embrace of the common, kind parent, fell victim to fate while playing, through the envy of fate. For when the driver of the young bulls suddenly propelled the untutored chariot in a circle, he was thrown out. Both parents, sad after his miserable death, gave the highest offerings to the departed. For Anthus, this tomb was raised by his parents, who shed tears in their youth, and was set up with equal piety. Quintus Volusius Anthus, son of Quintus, freedman of Quintus, made this for himself and for Silius Felicula, his most sacred wife, freedwoman, for Volusia, daughter of Quintus, and for Quintus Volusius Anthus, son of Quintus. In the front 6 feet, in the depth 3.5 feet.
EDCS-05701827.summary: This elaborate funerary epitaph commemorates Anthus, a child who died tragically in an accident with bulls and a chariot, and was erected by his parents, Quintus Volusius Anthus and Silius Felicula, along with other family members.

EDCS-05701828.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Volusia Hermione, daughter of Lucius. Hermes and Heuresis made this for their sweetest daughter.
EDCS-05701828.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Hermes and Heuresis for their beloved daughter, Volusia Hermione.

EDCS-05701831.translation: To the departed spirits, for Gaius Vuotidius Priscinus.
EDCS-05701831.summary: This is a funerary inscription honouring Gaius Vuotidius Priscinus.

EDCS-05701838.translation: Zosime made this for herself and for her well-deserving husband Valerius Marinus, and for Valerius Anacus and Zosimena and their freedmen and descendants.
EDCS-05701838.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Zosime for herself, her husband, and their family and descendants.

EDCS-05701844.translation: To Minallava, his most virtuous and most abstinent stepdaughter.
EDCS-05701844.summary: This is a dedication to Minallava, a stepdaughter, possibly on a tomb or a monument.

EDCS-05701848.translation: [...]isia, a freedwoman, and [---]ne, [a small shrine?] for herself and [---]iae and for her husband [---]anus.
EDCS-05701848.summary: This inscription, partially preserved, appears to record the dedication of a small shrine by a freedwoman namedisia for herself and her husband.

EDCS-05701895.translation: Basilides, accountant for Sabinus, sleeps here.
EDCS-05701895.summary: This is a funerary inscription stating that Basilides, an accountant, is buried here.

EDCS-05701896.translation: Callidromus, formerly an accountant, sleeps here in peace, a good soul, who lived 3 years. T(---)tianus, freedman of Augustus, assistant to the chief procurator. And Seia Helpis, his most beloved children, and Valeria Crescentina, his wife.
EDCS-05701896.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Callidromus, a former accountant, and mentions his family members including his children and wife, suggesting a late Roman period.

EDCS-05701897.translation: Felica, in peace, in the faith of God, who lived 34 years. Supervisor of the mint's middle office, first workshop.
EDCS-05701897.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Felica, who died at age 34, and held a supervisory position in the imperial mint.

EDCS-05701900.translation: To his wife Felicitas, who lived 45 years and [was married] with [her husband] 14 years. [He/She] placed this tablet himself/herself. [---]ano, a very distinguished man, during his consulship.
EDCS-05701900.summary: This inscription is a dedication to a wife named Felicitas, recording her lifespan and the duration of her marriage, likely set up by her husband.

EDCS-05701901.translation: Acceptus [---] returned his [spirit?] by giving back what is his to the master.
EDCS-05701901.summary: This inscription seems to record the release of a spirit or soul, possibly in a funerary or religious context.

EDCS-05701903.translation: The place of Aphrodisia, if the god permits.
EDCS-05701903.summary: This inscription marks a burial place designated for Aphrodisia, conditional on divine permission.

EDCS-05701904.translation: Artemidora, who lived 14 years, 9 months, and 20 days, sleeps here.
EDCS-05701904.summary: A funerary inscription recording the age of the deceased, Artemidora.

EDCS-05701906.translation: Aurelia Severa, mother, and Egrilia Sabina, daughter, sleep here in peace.
EDCS-05701906.summary: A funerary inscription honoring a mother and daughter, Aurelia Severa and Egrilia Sabina, who rest together in peace.

EDCS-05701907.translation: Auxania, pure as a lamb, departed to the Lord. She was in this world 13 years, 3 months, and 9 days. She sleeps here in peace.
EDCS-05701907.summary: A funerary inscription for Auxania, detailing her age and her peaceful rest, indicating a Christian context.

EDCS-05701908.translation: Mother Bruittia made this for her son, Junius Telesphorianus, the sweetest, who lived 4 years, 3 months, and 9 days.
EDCS-05701908.summary: A funerary monument erected by a mother, Bruittia, for her young son, Junius Telesphorianus, specifying his age at death.

EDCS-05701909.translation: Caecilia Creste. Caecilia Ammiadis, mother and sister, sleep here. The patron (perhaps) for the deserving.
EDCS-05701909.summary: A funerary inscription for Caecilia Creste, honoring her mother and sister, possibly mentioning a patron.

EDCS-05701910.translation: Caelidonius sleeps here, who lived 25 years, 8 months, and 19 days, in the places which he himself enclosed.
EDCS-05701910.summary: A funerary inscription for Caelidonius, detailing his age and mentioning his ownership or enclosure of the burial site.

EDCS-05701911.translation: Caelius sleeps here, and Decria, whenever God wills.
EDCS-05701911.summary: A funerary inscription for Caelius and Decria, expressing a belief in divine will for their rest.

EDCS-05701912.translation: Calligenia... was born... was deposited... incomparable, pious... and was deposited.
EDCS-05701912.summary: A fragmented funerary inscription that mentions Calligenia, her birth, deposition, and admirable qualities.

EDCS-05701914.translation: Her children made this for their well-deserving mother, Cipia Augeta, who lived 63 years, 5 months, and 14 days. She rests here in peace.
EDCS-05701914.summary: A funerary inscription erected by children for their mother, Cipia Augeta, detailing her age and peaceful rest.

EDCS-05701915.translation: Cipia Septimia Agape, chaste, faithful, in peace, 27 years, 3 months, and [..] days. And Irene followed after [..] years. Here sleep in peace twin sisters.
EDCS-05701915.summary: A funerary inscription for two twin sisters, Cipia Septimia Agape and Irene, detailing their ages and peaceful eternal rest.

EDCS-05701917.translation: Marcus Curtius Victorinus and Plotia Marcella, while living, made this for themselves, if the god permits.
EDCS-05701917.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument dedicated by Marcus Curtius Victorinus and Plotia Marcella for themselves.

EDCS-05701918.translation: Elpidius, fellow worker, and Irene, his infant son, lived 9 months.
EDCS-05701918.summary: This inscription records the death of an infant, Irene, son of Elpidius, noting his age at death.

EDCS-05701919.translation: My (daughter) Domnica Eusebia, most faithful, lived 24 years, 2 months. Deposited the 6th day before the Ides of August. Evangellia, innocent, sleeps here in peace, who lived 1 year, 11 months, 6 days. Deposited the 13th day before the Kalends of January. My (daughter) Exsuperantia, most faithful, rare, lived 20 years, 4 months. Deposited the 4th day before the Ides of September.
EDCS-05701919.summary: This inscription is a multi-part epitaph recording the lives and deaths of three individuals, likely related, within a family tomb.

EDCS-05701920.translation: Felicitas set this up for her daughter, Muicia Irene, who sleeps in peace and lived approximately 30 years.
EDCS-05701920.summary: This inscription is an epitaph set up by Felicitas for her daughter Muicia Irene.

EDCS-05701921.translation: Felicitas sleeps here in peace, who lived 20 years, 5 months, 17 days. Macsimus made (this) for his deserving companion. Died the 15th day before the Kalends of October, deposited the 14th day before the Kalends of October.
EDCS-05701921.summary: This epitaph records the death of Felicitas, who lived 20 years, 5 months, and 17 days, and was commemorated by Macsimus.

EDCS-05701922.summary: This is a short funerary epitaph praising the deceased named Firmi.

EDCS-05701923.translation: To Fronimus, Victorinus and Severa, most worthy friends.
EDCS-05701923.summary: This is a dedication from Fronimus to his highly esteemed friends, Victorinus and Severa.

EDCS-05701924.translation: Gemellina sleeps here, and the place of Coelitis.
EDCS-05701924.summary: This inscription marks the burial place of Gemellina and Coelitis.

EDCS-05701928.translation: Julia Gelasina and Iulia Vitalis made the burial for Iulia Maxima, their most innocent foster-daughter, who lived 10 years, 9 months, 31 days.
EDCS-05701928.summary: This inscription records the burial of Iulia Maxima, an innocent foster-daughter, by her alumnae Julia Gelasina and Iulia Vitalis.

EDCS-05701929.translation: Fonteius Faustinianus for his dearest wife Julia Quintiana, who sleeps here, and Crescentinus [---]
EDCS-05701929.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Fonteius Faustinianus for his wife Julia Quintiana and Crescentinus.

EDCS-05701930.translation: Lucceius Valerianus, who lived 28 years, and Lucceius Valerius, who lived 12 years, and their mother, if the gods permit, rest here.
EDCS-05701930.summary: This inscription records the burial of two individuals, Lucceius Valerianus and Lucceius Valerius, and their mother, with a pious sentiment about divine permission.

EDCS-05701935.translation: Paula Rogata, who lived 7 years and [---], rests here.
EDCS-05701935.summary: This is a tombstone for Paula Rogata, who died at the age of seven years and some additional days, dated by the style of lettering to the 3rd-4th century CE.

EDCS-05701936.translation: Marcus Salinator Martialis, aged seven years, rests in this place.
EDCS-05701936.summary: This inscription marks the burial place of Marcus Salinator Martialis, who died at the age of seven.

EDCS-05701940.translation: Valerius Cocillius Cyriaccus, who lived fifteen years, one month, and twelve days, passed away on the 3rd of the Nones of November. Valerius Gaionas and Salamina, parents, rest here for their most innocent son.
EDCS-05701940.summary: This epitaph commemorates the death of Valerius Cocillius Cyriaccus at the age of fifteen, listing his parents and date of death, likely from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-05701941.translation: Valerius Ippolitus rests here for Clementiana, his foster-daughter, and for himself.
EDCS-05701941.summary: This inscription records the burial arrangements for Valerius Ippolitus and Clementiana, his foster-daughter.

EDCS-05701943.translation: For Veneria, deserving well, at her repose.
EDCS-05701943.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Veneria, described as deserving, likely for a tomb or monument.

EDCS-05701954.translation: Donatus the bishop adorned the tomb for the holy martyrs and most blessed Eutropius, Bonosa, and Zosima, and also built from the foundations a basilica joined to the tomb for the holy people of God.
EDCS-05701954.summary: This inscription details the construction and decoration of a funerary complex, including a tomb and a basilica, by Bishop Donatus for the martyrs Eutropius, Bonosa, and Zosima.

EDCS-05701961.translation: For Aurelia Gemina, a wife of chastity, purity, and all integrity, to my most sweet wife, Felix the deacon [dedicates].
EDCS-05701961.summary: This is a dedication from Felix, a deacon, to his wife Aurelia Gemina, praising her virtues.

EDCS-05701972.translation: Cominius Marcellinus, who lived 25 years and 11 months, rests in peace.
EDCS-05701972.summary: This epitaph records the life and peaceful rest of Cominius Marcellinus, who died at the age of 25 years and 11 months.

EDCS-05701973.translation: Dicaeus and Chrysogonus and Heliodora live in God.
EDCS-05701973.summary: This inscription records three individuals, Dicaeus, Chrysogonus, and Heliodora, who are stated to live on in God, likely a Christian funerary text.

EDCS-05701974.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Epictesis, likely from the late Roman period, wishing her eternal life in God.

EDCS-05701976.translation: They rest in peace, Faustina, his wife, and [---] their granddaughter, having been brought together by the merits of their parents.
EDCS-05701976.summary: This fragmented funerary inscription records the repose of Faustina and her granddaughter, possibly dating to the 4th century AD, emphasizing familial merit.

EDCS-05701977.translation: Felix rests here, who lived 5 years, 7 months, and 14 days.
EDCS-05701977.summary: This epitaph records the short life of a boy named Felix, who died at the age of five years, seven months, and fourteen days.

EDCS-05701980.translation: Plotius Tertius and Faustina, fellow servants of God, made [this] for themselves in the peace of Christ.
EDCS-05701980.summary: This funerary inscription, likely from the 3rd or 4th century AD, records Plotius Tertius and Faustina, Christian freedmen, providing for their own burial.

EDCS-05701985.translation: [---] for Silvestris, whom [---] his sons [---] and good [---].
EDCS-05701985.summary: This fragmentary funerary inscription seems to record a dedication by sons for their mother Silvestris, possibly from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-05702013.summary: This is a simple dedication, likely funerary or votive, by Aulus Larcius Eutyches, possibly from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-05702016.translation: From the workshop of Nasennus Fortunatus.
EDCS-05702016.summary: This inscription indicates the origin of an object or work, identifying the workshop of Nasennus Fortunatus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05702017.summary: This inscription likely refers to property or a commission belonging to Marcus Aurelius Cleander, possibly from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-05702034.translation: Under the administration of Emperor Antoninus Pius Augustus, through the supervision of Claudius Secundinus, rationalis, and of Annius Phlegons, freedman of Augustus, from the workshop of Demetrius, freedman.
EDCS-05702034.summary: This inscription records the supervision and workshop responsible for a project or construction under Emperor Antoninus Pius (138-161 AD), detailing the officials and freedmen involved.

EDCS-05800015.summary: This is a highly fragmented inscription, likely funerary, mentioning the names Marcia and Hispa, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05800016.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Aurelius Thelephanes made this for Protogenia, his dearest adopted daughter, who was born under unhappy stars, lived for two years, eleven months, and eight days.
EDCS-05800016.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a child named Protogenia, set up by her foster father Marcus Aurelius Thelephanes, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05800018.translation: To the departed spirits. Quintus Sittius Flaccus, tribune of the 2nd cohort of vigiles, and Anicia Caecilia, daughter of Marcus, made this for Quintus Sitti Quintillus, their sweetest son.
EDCS-05800018.summary: This funerary inscription records parents, Quintus Sittius Flaccus and Anicia Caecilia, dedicating a monument to their deceased son, Quintus Sitti Quintillus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05800019.translation: Caius Albius Celer, son of Spurius, of the Colline tribe, and Caius Albius Stabilio, freedman of Caius, Helena, in the field, 10 feet in front, 10 feet.
EDCS-05800019.summary: This inscription likely marks a property boundary or a tomb, mentioning Caius Albius Celer and his freedman Stabilio, and a woman named Helena, with dimensions of the plot, possibly from the late Republic or early Imperial period.

EDCS-05800020.translation: To the departed spirits. Sulpicius Paris and Sulpicia Lais made this for Corinthias, their slave, who lived 15 years, 9 months.
EDCS-05800020.summary: This funerary inscription records Sulpicius Paris and Sulpicia Lais dedicating a monument to their deceased slave Corinthias, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05800021.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Mettius Telesphor and Titus Flavius Leonas, heirs, [made this] for Titus Flavius Phoebus, freedman of the divine Augustus, of the Othoniani association.
EDCS-05800021.summary: This funerary inscription records heirs, Marcus Mettius Telesphor and Titus Flavius Leonas, dedicating a monument to Titus Flavius Phoebus, a freedman and member of the Othoniani association, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05800022.translation: Marcus Iulius Damas and Iulia Rufina made this for their sweetest son, Marcus Iulius Crescentianus, who lived 11 years, 5 months, and 3 days.
EDCS-05800022.summary: This funerary inscription records parents, Marcus Iulius Damas and Iulia Rufina, dedicating a monument to their deceased son, Marcus Iulius Crescentianus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05800023.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Terentius Secundus made this for himself, for Terentia Ampliatiana, his daughter, for Ulpia Faustina, his dearest wife, for Terentia Faustina, his sweetest daughter, and for his freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-05800023.summary: This is a self-dedicated funerary monument by Titus Terentius Secundus, also intended for his wife, daughters, and future descendants, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05800025.translation: Marcus Clodius made this for his most beloved wife, Clodia Successa, who deserved well, who lived 37 years, 3 months, and 12 days.
EDCS-05800025.summary: This funerary inscription records Marcus Clodius dedicating a monument to his deceased wife, Clodia Successa, providing her age at death, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05800026.translation: [---] Numice Lavinas [---] direct [---] pilumni [---] pontifex, votive offering [---] clear blood of bronze [---] greatest of petitioners [---] may you succeed in the outcome [---] the laws of the Laurentes.
EDCS-05800026.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, possibly a dedication or vow, mentioning a pontifex and relating to the people of Laurentum, date uncertain due to damage.

EDCS-05800031.translation: To the divine Antoninus Augustus, the Senate and People of Laurentum, because he not only preserved their privileges but also increased them, with Marcus Annius Sabinus Libo, most distinguished man, as curator, under the administration of the quinquennial praetors Tiberius Iulius Nepotianus and Publius Aemilius Egnatianus, for the people of Laurentum and Lavinium.
EDCS-05800031.summary: This inscription records the Senate and People of Laurentum and Lavinium dedicating a monument to Emperor Antoninus Augustus (likely Caracalla) for upholding and expanding their privileges, administered by local officials, from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05800039.translation: To Lucius, of consular rank, curator of the sacred city, region 4, for the Laurentine and Lavinate territories, Valerius Commagenus, Pullaenius Martialis, Aelius Benedictus, Aemilius Eutycianus, Aurelius Fortunius, Caesius Dulcitius, men of distinguished rank, [dedicate this] to their most deserving patron.
EDCS-05800039.summary: This is a dedication by a group of prominent men to their patron, likely honoring his role in overseeing public works in the Laurentine and Lavinate territories.

EDCS-05800046.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription mentioning Emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar, likely part of a larger dedication or monument.

EDCS-05800047.translation: Marcus Aemilius Alexander dedicated this to the gods and goddesses.
EDCS-05800047.summary: This is a dedication of a cult object or monument by Marcus Aemilius Alexander to the gods and goddesses.

EDCS-05800049.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 20th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, ordered a statue to be made and consecrated to Juno Sospita Magna Regina from gifts of gold and silver that had been corrupted by age, using 3 pounds of gold and 206 pounds of silver.
EDCS-05800049.summary: This imperial dedication by Emperor Hadrian records the commissioning of a statue for Juno Sospita Magna Regina using melted down precious metal gifts, dating to his 20th year of tribunician power.

EDCS-05800065.translation: The Senate and People of Lanuvium dedicated [this] to Titus Aurelius Aphrodisius, freedman of the Emperor, procurator of the Emperor in charge of finances, on the dedication by Quintus Varinius Laevinus, son of Quintus of the Maecian tribe, as aedile.
EDCS-05800065.summary: This dedication by the community of Lanuvium honors Titus Aurelius Aphrodisius, an imperial freedman and procurator, during the aedileship of Quintus Varinius Laevinus.

EDCS-05800068.translation: To Gaius Mevius Donatus Iunianus, son of Gaius, consul, proconsul of the province of Sicily, praetor, quaestor of the province of Sicily, curator of the communities of the province of Sicily, imperial legate with the power of a proconsul in Noricum... to the gods, because of his loyalty?
EDCS-05800068.summary: This is a dedication to Gaius Mevius Donatus Iunianus, detailing his extensive career in various Roman provinces, including Sicily and Noricum, and recognizing his service and loyalty.

EDCS-05800069.translation: Lucius Albius Rufus, son of Lucius of the Fabian tribe, scribe and former aedile, [erected this] to his uncle Gnaeus Ricinius Persa, son of Gnaeus of the Pupinian tribe, scribe and military tribune, and to his grandmother Thoria, daughter of Aulus, and to his wife Quintia, daughter of Lucius.
EDCS-05800069.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Lucius Albius Rufus to his paternal relatives: his uncle, his grandmother, and his wife, listing their names and some of their public roles.

EDCS-05800070.translation: To Aulus Terentius Varrus Murena, son of Aulus, the people of Ptolemais in Cyrenaica [dedicate this] through ambassadors: Itthallamos, son of Apellas, and Simon, son of Simon.
EDCS-05800070.summary: The people of Ptolemais in Cyrenaica dedicate this to their patron Aulus Terentius Varrus Murena, with the dedication conveyed through ambassadors.

EDCS-05800074.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Agilius Septentrionus, freedman of Emperor Augustus, the finest pantomime of his time, priest of the Apollonian Synod, protégé and adopted son of Faustina Augusta, honored with the ornaments of the decurionate by decree of the council, and enrolled among the young men, the Senate and People of Lanuvium [honored him]... on the [date] of the consulship of Crispinius and Aelianus.
EDCS-05800074.summary: The community of Lanuvium honors Marcus Aurelius Agilius Septentrionus, a distinguished pantomime and imperial protégé, with civic honors and enrollment among the youth.

EDCS-05800076.translation: Dastidius Celer, son of Gaius, and his father Gaius Dastidius Apollinaris, son of Spurius, contributed 15,000 sesterces for the repair of the baths, the former for the honor of the aedileship, and the latter for the honor of the flamoniate.
EDCS-05800076.summary: This inscription records a financial contribution of 15,000 sesterces by father and son, Gaius Dastidius Celer and Gaius Dastidius Apollinaris, towards the repair of the baths, associated with their respective public offices.

EDCS-05800081.translation: To Gaius Sulpicius Victor, father of Roman equites, the most innocent man, patron of the municipality, the Senate and People of Lanuvium decreed a statue should be erected and dedicated to him for his unparalleled service and immense generosity towards them; on the occasion of his dedication, he personally distributed 24 sesterces to the decurions and Augustales and the curia, and gave a double banquet to the women's curia.
EDCS-05800081.summary: This is a dedication of a statue to Gaius Sulpicius Victor by the municipality of Lanuvium for his service and generosity.

EDCS-05800082.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-05800082.translation: To Marcus Valerius, son of Marcus, aedile, dictator, prefect of youth, the ward officials of the five compita [dedicate this] because he cleared and repaired a cave three miles long, replaced pipes, repaired both the men's and women's baths from his own funds, gave a banquet and gladiatorial games to the people, [and dedicated] lamps, games to Juno Sospita Magna Regina, all by himself.
EDCS-05800082.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Valerius by the ward officials for his public works, including the repair of baths and provision of games, likely from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-05800090.translation: The Republic of Lanuvium. Hermes made [this].
EDCS-05800090.summary: This is a very brief inscription recording that Hermes made something for the Republic of Lanuvium, possibly a public building or artwork.

EDCS-05800095.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. Publius Aelius Filetianus [dedicated this] to Aelius [---].
EDCS-05800095.summary: This is a tombstone inscription dedicated to Aelius by Publius Aelius Filetianus, indicating a familial or close relationship.

EDCS-05800097.translation: Quintus Agilleius Mundus, 20 feet in the field, 20 feet in the front.
EDCS-05800097.summary: This inscription appears to record land dimensions, possibly for a property boundary or construction project.

EDCS-05800101.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. To Bryttie Helena, most kind and incomparable wife, who loved this place and died here peacefully, lived 70 years. [---]uceius Felix, husband [dedicated this].
EDCS-05800101.summary: This is a tombstone inscription dedicated by the husband to his beloved wife, Bryttie Helena, commemorating her life and death.

EDCS-05800102.translation: [Dedicated] to Florentinus, son of Gaius [---], lived 9 years, 10 months, 6 days. [---]ttia Flora made [this] for her sweetest son and for herself, [for her freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants, and for] Donatus, son of Quintus.
EDCS-05800102.summary: This is a tombstone inscription set up by a mother, Flordia Flora, for her young son, Florentinus, and also for herself and her freedmen and descendants.

EDCS-05800106.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. To Micina, daughter of Duccius. Sitius Felix, father, made [this] by will. To the Spirits of the departed. To Sanpicalus, son. Sitius Felix and Gerulana Ilara [made this].
EDCS-05800106.summary: This inscription contains two parts, with Sitius Felix dedicating a tomb to his son Micina, and Sitius Felix and Gerulana Ilara dedicating one to their son Sanpicalus.

EDCS-05800107.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. To Lucius Myrtilus, foster-son, most dutiful. Flavius Aurelius [---] for his deserving son [dedicated this]. Mainius [dedicated this] to the well-deserving [---].
EDCS-05800107.summary: This tombstone inscription is dedicated to Lucius Myrtilus, a dutiful foster-son, by Flavius Aurelius and possibly Mainius.

EDCS-05800110.translation: [---]tilius Severus, son of Titus, of the Galeria tribe, [lived 6 years, died in his seventh year. Faustus, father, made this for his son and himself. [---] to Lesbius, his brother. [---]ae, freedwoman of the Publii, to his wife Rhodin(a). [---] seven jugera of land and a shop which [---] to protect their income by monument or neglect.
EDCS-05800110.summary: This fragmentary tombstone inscription records the death of a young boy, dedications by his father and brother, and provisions for land and property, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05800112.translation: To the departed spirits. Her heirs [dedicated this].
EDCS-05800112.summary: A funerary dedication by the heirs to Asinia Synhesis.

EDCS-05800118.translation: To Diana Augusta. Sacred to the guild of bath attendants. Primigenius, slave of the public administration of Arcinum and its treasurer, as curator twice, with Marcus Arrecinus Gellianus, his son, as curator once, gave and dedicated [this].
EDCS-05800118.summary: A sacred dedication to Diana Augusta by Primigenius, a slave and treasurer of the guild of bath attendants in Arcinum, and his associate, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-05800123.translation: To Plotina Augusta, wife of the Emperor Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus. Agathyrsus, freedman of Augusta. [Dedicated this]. To Plotina Augusta, wife of the Emperor Trajan Augustus, father of the fatherland. Agathyrsus, freedman of Augusta.
EDCS-05800123.summary: A dedication to Plotina, wife of Trajan, by her freedman Agathyrsus, likely from the early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-05800125.translation: To the safety of the Augusti. Fortunate Nortius.
EDCS-05800125.summary: A short dedication invoking the safety of the emperors, possibly by an individual named Nortius, likely from the late 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-05800126.text_edition: \n Tito Aelio Aurelio Titi filio Epiano legato Augusti legionis X geminae curatori viae Clodiae legato pro praetore provinciae Africae praetori \n
EDCS-05800126.translation: Titus Aelius Aurelius Epianus, son of Titus, imperial legate of the Tenth Gemina legion, curator of the Via Clodia, legate with praetorian power of the province of Africa, praetor.
EDCS-05800126.summary: An inscription listing the extensive career of Titus Aelius Aurelius Epianus, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-05800130.translation: To Aurelius Lupianus, decurion and magistrate of the Aricines. To his most sweet and deserving wife, Poemenes. This too is sweet.
EDCS-05800130.summary: A funerary dedication from a husband to his wife, Poemenes, who was likely a member of the Aricines community, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05800131.translation: Gnaeus Dupilius, son of Gnaeus, of the Horatia tribe, military tribune in the legion, flamen Martialis, quaestor, aedile, dictator of Aricia. Gnaeus Dupilius, son of Marcus, of the Horatia tribe, his father. Marcus Dupilius, son of Gnaeus, of the Horatia tribe, his brother. Mulvia, daughter of Lucius, his mother. Martia, daughter of Lucius, his wife. Gnaeus Dupilius, freedman of Gnaeus, his freedman. Gnaeus Dupilius, freedman of Gnaeus, his freedman. 16 feet in front, 18 feet in the rear.
EDCS-05800131.summary: A detailed funerary monument inscription for Gnaeus Dupilius, listing his extensive public career in Aricia and identifying his family members and freedmen, likely from the late Roman Republic or early Imperial period.

EDCS-05800132.translation: To the departed spirits. To Julius Marcus, decurion of the Aricines, who held every office, who lived 12 years, 2 months, and 2 days, 6 hours. Aurelius Iucca and Julia Hermione, his most loving parents, made [this] for their dearest son.
EDCS-05800132.summary: A funerary dedication by parents for their young son, Julius Marcus, who held the office of decurion in Aricia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05800133.translation: To the departed spirits. To Lucius Sempronius Proculus, son of Lucius, of the Horatia tribe, who lived 31 years. He was aedile, quaestor, member of the Salii college, curator of the Ocranian funds. Luria Secunda, his mother.
EDCS-05800133.summary: A funerary dedication by a mother for her son, Lucius Sempronius Proculus, who had held several important public and religious offices, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05800135.summary: A fragmentary inscription mentioning Gaius Licinius Mucian, likely a dedication or ownership mark, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05800136.summary: This funerary inscription records the maker of a monument, likely Publius Memmius Regulus.

EDCS-05800137.translation: Publius Aelius Symphorus made [this] for Aelius Galaes, freedman of the emperor.
EDCS-05800137.summary: This funerary inscription records Publius Aelius Symphorus as the maker for Aelius Galaes, a freedman of the emperor.

EDCS-05800138.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. [---]ima, bisellarius, [lived] X months and XLV years.
EDCS-05800138.summary: This funerary inscription is for an individual identified as a bisellarius, who lived 45 years and an unspecified number of months.

EDCS-05800146.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Campanius Secundus and Marcus Arrius Secundus, his freedman, made [this] for Cerellia Phoebe, the best wife, deserving of their kindness.
EDCS-05800146.summary: This funerary inscription records Marcus Campanius Secundus and Marcus Arrius Secundus dedicating a monument to the deceased Cerellia Phoebe, described as an excellent wife.

EDCS-05800153.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Gaius Trebonius Ianvarius (of the Palatina tribe) and Clodius Saturus [made this] for their well-deserving friend Titus Flavius Abascantus.
EDCS-05800153.summary: This funerary inscription records Gaius Trebonius Ianvarius and Clodius Saturus dedicating a monument to their friend Titus Flavius Abascantus.

EDCS-05800154.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. [---] Fortunata, [---] to the best [---].
EDCS-05800154.summary: This fragmented funerary inscription is dedicated to an individual named Fortunata.

EDCS-05800155.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Euposia made [this] for Hortesius Synphorus, her well-deserving patron, and for Hortesia Epictesidi.
EDCS-05800155.summary: This funerary inscription records Euposia dedicating a monument to her patron Hortesius Synphorus and to Hortesia Epictesidi.

EDCS-05800156.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Iulia Merope made [this] for her best husband, Tiberius Iulius Apollonius, deserving of his kindness, for herself and for her descendants.
EDCS-05800156.summary: This funerary inscription records Iulia Merope dedicating a monument to her husband Tiberius Iulius Apollonius and also reserving it for herself and her descendants.

EDCS-05800157.translation: Lucius Laelius Agathemerus [made this] for himself and for his most beloved wife Laelia Thallusa, with whom he lived for 50 years without any complaint, and for their descendants.
EDCS-05800157.summary: This funerary inscription records Lucius Laelius Agathemerus dedicating a monument for himself, his wife Laelia Thallusa (with whom he lived 50 years happily), and their descendants.

EDCS-05800159.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Manlius Victor was buried here, aged 18 years, 10 months, and 16 days. Marcus Manlius Thallus and Manlia Helpis made [this] for their excellent son.
EDCS-05800159.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Marcus Manlius Victor at age 18 and his monument made by his parents, Marcus Manlius Thallus and Manlia Helpis.

EDCS-05800162.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Caius Clodius Honerianus set up this monument to his most sweet wife Naevia Libadis, with whom he lived for 25 years, a well-deserving woman.
EDCS-05800162.summary: This is a funerary monument (dis manibus) set up by a husband for his deceased wife, detailing their lifespan together.

EDCS-05800164.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Licinia Bassilla made this for Primo, her most unfortunate freedman, who lived for 4 years and 16 days.
EDCS-05800164.summary: This is a funerary monument (dis manibus) erected by a mistress for her deceased freedman, noting his short lifespan.

EDCS-05800165.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Optatus and Acestia made this for their most holy daughter Tyria Quintillae, who lived 15 years, 8 months, and 3 days.
EDCS-05800165.summary: This is a funerary monument (dis manibus) set up by parents for their deceased daughter, detailing her age at death.

EDCS-05800166.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Cornelia Olympina, grandmother of Lucius Valerius Quietius, son of Lucius.
EDCS-05800166.summary: This is a funerary monument (dis manibus) dedicated by a grandmother for her deceased grandson.

EDCS-05800176.translation: Sacred to Diana Nemorensis and Vesta, during the dictatorship of Emperor Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus, in his third consulship, and with his prefect Titus Voltedius Mamilianus as quaestor. In their second consulship were Lucius Caecilius Ursus and Marcus Lucretius Sabinus, and as aediles Quintus Vibennius Quietius and Tiberius Claudius Magnus. Publius Cornelius Trophimus, a baker from the 14th region, and his wife Lania, daughter of Caius Thionoe, the curator of the Vicus Quadratus, freely fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-05800176.summary: This is a dedication to Diana and Vesta, specifying numerous officials and consuls, indicating a public religious act during Trajan's reign.

EDCS-05800177.translation: Lucius Curius Eutrapelus and Vargunteia Nais freely and deservedly fulfilled their vow to Diana Nemorensis.
EDCS-05800177.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow to Diana Nemorensis by two individuals.

EDCS-05800187.translation: Lucius Sextius Salvius, freedman of Lucius, and Caius Voconius Auctus, freedman of Caius, lived.
EDCS-05800187.summary: This appears to be a fragmentary funerary or commemorative inscription for two freedmen, noting their names and possibly a shared lifespan.

EDCS-05800190.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a dedication or ownership mark, mentioning the emperor Tiberius Caesar.

EDCS-05800211.translation: Felix, freedman of Tychius, freedman of Aulus, and Philologus, freedman of Cladus, and others [names illegible]...
EDCS-05800211.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription listing multiple freedmen, likely part of a funerary monument or property record.

EDCS-05800217.translation: Sacred, by vision and by order of the Good Goddess. Callistus, agent of our Rufina.
EDCS-05800217.summary: This is a religious dedication to the Bona Dea, enacted by an agent on behalf of his mistress, likely resulting from a divine vision or command.

EDCS-05800218.translation: Gaius Faberius, soldier, and Sedilia give [this] to Juno.
EDCS-05800218.summary: A soldier named Gaius Faberius and Sedilia dedicate a gift to the goddess Juno.

EDCS-05800219.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Titus Caesernius Statius Quinctius Macedo Quinctianus, consul, paid his vow.
EDCS-05800219.summary: A consul named Titus Caesernius Statius Quinctius Macedo Quinctianus fulfills a vow made to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-05800220.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Conservator, for the health of our lord and the Genius of the century, Valerius Calendinus, optio, made this.
EDCS-05800220.summary: Valerius Calendinus, an optio, erected this monument dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Genius of his century for the well-being of his lord.

EDCS-05800221.translation: For the health of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, father of the country, and Julia Augusta, mother of our emperor, and of the senate, and of the fatherland, and of the camp, Dasimius Firminus, cornicularius of the legion, and Aurelius Victorinus, actuarius, with the immunes, clerks, and exacti, have rightly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-05800221.summary: Dasimius Firminus and Aurelius Victorinus, along with other soldiers of the legion, fulfill a vow for the health of the imperial family and the state, likely during the Severan dynasty.

EDCS-05800227.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Aurelius Agathyrsus, freedman of the emperor, a clerk, made this for his well-deserving father Hilarus.
EDCS-05800227.summary: Lucius Aurelius Agathyrsus, a freedman of the emperor and a clerk, set up this tombstone for his deserving father.

EDCS-05800228.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Flavia Callisto made this for Epio, freedman of the emperor and clerk of the treasury of inheritances, her most beloved husband, well-deserving, and for herself and their descendants.
EDCS-05800228.summary: Flavia Callisto erected a tombstone for her husband Epio, a freedman of the emperor and an official, and for herself and their posterity.

EDCS-05800231.translation: To Lucius Peducaeus Saturninus, decurion of the group of scribes and clerks, and to Julia Praeconia, by the will and at the discretion of Peducea Severina, daughter of Caius, his wife.
EDCS-05800231.summary: This inscription records a funerary dedication to Lucius Peducaeus Saturninus and Julia Praeconia, arranged according to a will.

EDCS-05800236.translation: Aelius Vitalis, veteran of our emperor, lived 60 years. His wife Aelia Afrodisia and his son Aelius Nicanor, his heirs, arranged for the making of this for a well-deserving man.
EDCS-05800236.summary: Aelius Vitalis, a veteran, is honored with a tombstone erected by his wife and son.

EDCS-05800237.translation: Aelia Victorina, who lived 46 years, 3 months, and 12 days. Antonius Iulius, veteran of our emperor, made this memorial for his most cherished and incomparable wife, with whom he lived 29 years, for her well-deserving soul, and arranged for its making.
EDCS-05800237.summary: Antonius Iulius, a veteran, commissioned a tombstone for his beloved wife Aelia Victorina, who died at the age of 46.

EDCS-05800240.translation: To Aurelius [---], soldier of the II Severian Parthian Legion, Pius, [---] of the princeps posterior. Victorina lived 59 years and 19 days; she made this for a well-deserving person.
EDCS-05800240.summary: Victorina erected a memorial for Aurelius, a soldier of the II Severian Parthian Legion, who lived 59 years and 19 days.

EDCS-05800244.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aurelius Alexandri, scribe of the Second Parthian Legion, Eternal, IVth century, rear hastatus, who lived 32 years and served [years].
EDCS-05800244.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a scribe of the Second Parthian Legion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05800245.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aurelius Auluzanus, soldier of the Second Parthian Legion, who lived 41 years and served 20 years, VIth century, princeps posterior, erected by [his heir] for a well-deserving man.
EDCS-05800245.summary: A funerary inscription for a soldier of the Second Parthian Legion, who served in the VIth century, erected by his heir.

EDCS-05800247.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aurelius Comas[t]us, soldier of the Parthian Legion, from Verona. Aurelius Victor made this for his most beloved son, who lived four years, nine months. For a well-deserving man.
EDCS-05800247.summary: A funerary inscription for Aurelius Comastus, a soldier of the Parthian Legion, from Verona, and his son, who died at a very young age.

EDCS-05800248.translation: For Aurelius Crysomallus of Quintanesis, of the legion, who lived [3?] years, or[tus?] of the IVth century, from the citizenship of Apamea. Placed by himself while alive.
EDCS-05800248.summary: A tombstone set up by Aurelius Crysomallus, a legionary from Apamea, during his lifetime.

EDCS-05800249.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Dassius served in the Second Parthian Legion Antoniniana Aeterna Pia [Felix] Fidelis for 19 years, discharged with an honorable discharge from the aforementioned legion, lived 50 years, 3 months, 26 days. Aurelius Severus, his heir, made this in memory for a well-deserving man.
EDCS-05800249.summary: A funerary inscription for Aurelius Dassius, a soldier of the Second Parthian Legion, who served for 19 years and received an honorable discharge, erected by his heir.

EDCS-05800250.translation: Aurelius Dizapor, veteran, served our Emperors for 27 years, lived 51 years, 8 months, 17 days. Aurelius Agatobolus, his freedman and heir, made this for a well-deserving man.
EDCS-05800250.summary: A funerary inscription for Aurelius Dizapor, a veteran who served the emperors for 27 years, erected by his freedman and heir.

EDCS-05800251.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelia Primula lived 3 years, 4 months. Aurelius Frunitus, veteran of the Second Parthian Legion Severiana, made this for his well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-05800251.summary: A funerary inscription for Aurelia Primula, a young girl, erected by her father, a veteran of the Second Parthian Legion.

EDCS-05800252.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Claudius Maximus, equestrian bodyguard of our Emperor, lived 35 years and served 10 years. Aelius Victor, his heir, placed this for a well-deserving man.
EDCS-05800252.summary: A funerary inscription for Claudius Maximus, an equestrian bodyguard of the emperor, erected by his heir.

EDCS-05800253.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Titus Flavius Quintinus, equestrian bodyguard of the Emperor, selected from the army of Raetia, from the First Flavian Loyal and Faithful Ala, served for six years, lived 26 years. Publius Crescins and Claudius Paternus, his heirs, placed this for a well-deserving man.
EDCS-05800253.summary: A funerary inscription for Titus Flavius Quintinus, an equestrian bodyguard of the emperor from the Raetian army, erected by his heirs.

EDCS-05800254.translation: Pupius Flaccus, evocatus, who lived 55 years, a caligatus for 18 years. Aelia Valentina made this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-05800254.summary: A funerary inscription for Pupius Flaccus, an evocatus (a veteran recalled to service), erected by his wife.

EDCS-05800255.translation: To Septimius Licinianus, dearest son, who lived for 3 years, 4 months, and 24 days. Septimius Licinius, *immunis librarius* of the Second Parthian Legion, his father, placed this.
EDCS-05800255.summary: A tombstone for a young child, set up by his father who was a scribe in the Second Parthian Legion.

EDCS-05800256.translation: To Gypsania Lepida, his incomparable wife, who lived for 19 years, 4 months, and 14 days. Publius Septimius Proculus, *duplicarius* of the Second Severan Parthian Legion, her husband, set this up for her as one who deserved well.
EDCS-05800256.summary: A tombstone for a wife who died young, set up by her husband, a soldier in the Second Parthian Legion.

EDCS-05800257.translation: [---] Siricianus, [---] of the Second Severan Parthian Legion, Pious, Fortunate, Faithful, Eternal(?), [serving in] century VIII of the front *pilus*, who lived 31 years, [---] his heirs [---] set this up for him as one who deserved well.
EDCS-05800257.summary: A tombstone for a soldier of the Second Parthian Legion, set up by his heirs.

EDCS-05800258.translation: [---] Valerianus(?), veteran of our Emperor, lived 50 years. Aurelia Basinta set this up for her dear husband.
EDCS-05800258.summary: A tombstone for a veteran, set up by his wife.

EDCS-05800259.translation: To the departed spirits. Valerius Maximus, soldier of the Second Severan Parthian Legion, his father placed this memorial for his son.
EDCS-05800259.summary: A tombstone for a soldier of the Second Parthian Legion, set up by his father.

EDCS-05800261.translation: To the departed spirits. Pompeia Priscina, who lived 40 years, 5 months. Ulpius Surus, veteran, set this up for his incomparable wife as one who deserved well.
EDCS-05800261.summary: A tombstone for a wife, set up by her veteran husband.

EDCS-05800262.translation: [---]itus, soldier of the Second Severan Parthian Legion, [in century ---] of the front, lived 40 years, served 22 years. Aurelius Clemens, heir, set this up as a memorial for one who deserved well.
EDCS-05800262.summary: A tombstone for a soldier of the Second Parthian Legion, who served for 22 years, set up by his heir.

EDCS-05800264.translation: Marcus Aurelius Zosimus, freedman of Marcus Aurelius Cotta Maximus, freedman. I was an *accensus* of my patron, a freedman, I admit, but my deeds will be read. Cotta, a noble, my shade, who often gladly gave me census payments, and ordered me to raise children whom he would support, and who entrusted his fortunes to me always, and endowed me, and then brought up my daughters as if he himself were a father, and my Cottanus, whom he earned with honor from Caesar's camp. What did Cotta not give, who now gave these sad verses for my tomb, to be seen. Aurelia Saturnina, [wife] of Zosimus.
EDCS-05800264.summary: A tombstone with an elaborate epitaph, set up by the wife of Zosimus, a freedman and *accensus* of Marcus Aurelius Cotta, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-05800267.translation: To the departed spirits. Fulvia Lucina made this for Junius, her most faithful agent, as one who deserved well.
EDCS-05800267.summary: A tombstone for a faithful agent, set up by Fulvia Lucina.

EDCS-05800268.translation: Gaius Julius Eros, freedman of the freedman of Augustus, a baker of white bread, for the years he was among mortals, before that day which was the 11th day before the Ides of May, lived 50 years. Iulia Glaphyra, fellow freedwoman, consecrated this to him and performed the duties for him, in the consulship of Manius Aemilius Lepidus and Titus Statilius Taurus.
EDCS-05800268.summary: A tombstone for a baker, Eros, a freedman of a freedman of Augustus, set up by a fellow freedwoman, with a precise date given by the consuls of 26 AD.

EDCS-05800270.translation: Under the administration of Emperor Caesar Domitian Augustus, by the care of Albanus Alypus, procurator, Esychus and Hermeros, slaves, made this.
EDCS-05800270.summary: This inscription records a construction by two slaves, Esychus and Hermeros, under the procuratorship of Albanus Alypus during the reign of Domitian.

EDCS-05800271.translation: Under the administration of Emperor Domitian Augustus Germanicus, by the care of Albanus Alypus, procurator, Primigenius, a slave, made this.
EDCS-05800271.summary: This inscription records a construction by a slave named Primigenius, under the procuratorship of Albanus Alypus during the reign of Domitian.

EDCS-05800273.translation: Under the administration of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Albanus, by the care of Euporus, freedman of the emperor, procurator.
EDCS-05800273.summary: This inscription mentions the administration of Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Albanus, with Euporus serving as procurator.

EDCS-05800274.translation: Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Commodus Augustus // Asclepius made this.
EDCS-05800274.summary: This inscription records that Asclepius made something dedicated to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Commodus.

EDCS-05800275.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Marcus Aemilius Festibus.

EDCS-05800277.translation: Auteius Fortunatus made this. (Possibly referring to women).
EDCS-05800277.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Auteius Fortunatus, possibly related to women.

EDCS-05800278.summary: This inscription is a dedication or ownership marker related to Lucius Licinius Sabinus.

EDCS-05800279.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Lucius Luscius Philetus.

EDCS-05800280.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Quintus Saufeius Gadia.

EDCS-05800281.translation: Aelius Sabianus lived 2 years and 4 months. Victor made this for his dearest son.
EDCS-05800281.summary: This inscription is a memorial for Aelius Sabianus, who lived 2 years and 4 months, and was made by his father, Victor.

EDCS-05800282.translation: Aelius Secundus made this for his well-deserving son, Aelius Primo, who lived 6 years, 7 months, and 9 days.
EDCS-05800282.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young boy, Aelius Primo, set up by his father Aelius Secundus.

EDCS-05800283.translation: Aelia Baleria, who lived 1 year, and 9 days, her parents made this for their sweetest daughter, well-deserving.
EDCS-05800283.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young girl, Aelia Baleria, set up by her parents.

EDCS-05800285.translation: Apollonius made this for his most dutiful wife, who lived 20 years, 2 months, and 2 days.
EDCS-05800285.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a woman, Apollonius' wife, set up by her husband Apollonius.

EDCS-05800286.translation: To the departed spirits. Urbanus and Soteris made this for their well-deserving foster-son Asclepon, who lived 5 years and 14 days.
EDCS-05800286.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young boy, Asclepon, set up by his foster-parents Urbanus and Soteris.

EDCS-05800287.translation: To the departed spirits. Atilius Agrippinus and Atilia Basilice, daughter of Rutilia Claudiane, [made this] for Atilius Agrippinus, who lived 58 years, 2 months, and 10 days; Rutilia Claudiane, his wife, and his sons, for the memory of their father.
EDCS-05800287.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Atilius Agrippinus, set up by his wife, sons, and Rutilia Claudiane, likely his mother or mother-in-law.

EDCS-05800288.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Bovi and Aurelius B[---]O made this for their well-deserving mother and daughter, who lived 28 years, 3 months, and 9 days.
EDCS-05800288.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a mother and daughter, set up by Aurelius Bovi and another Aurelius.

EDCS-05800290.translation: Marcus Aurelius Lupus made this for his well-deserving brother, Aurelius Felicissimus, who lived 40 years, 5 months, and 7 days.
EDCS-05800290.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelius Felicissimus, set up by his brother Marcus Aurelius Lupus.

EDCS-05800292.translation: To the departed spirits. Zodorus made this for his well-deserving brother, Aurelius Justus, who lived 16 years.
EDCS-05800292.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelius Justus, set up by his brother Zodorus.

EDCS-05800293.translation: To the departed spirits. Aproprnius Felix and Flavia Prima made this for their well-deserving son, Aurelius Salutaris, who lived 16 years and 10 months.
EDCS-05800293.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young man, Aurelius Salutaris, set up by his parents Aproprnius Felix and Flavia Prima.

EDCS-05800294.translation: To the departed spirits. Istercorius, Primus, and Secundinus, the heirs, made this for their well-deserving Aurelius Vitalio, who lived 90 years, and for Publicia Nepos.
EDCS-05800294.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelius Vitalio and Publicia Nepos, set up by their heirs Istercorius, Primus, and Secundinus.

EDCS-05800295.translation: To Aurelia Marthana, who lived 50 years. Aurelia Marthana made (this) for her well-deserving mother.
EDCS-05800295.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelia Marthana, set up by her mother of the same name, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05800297.translation: To Iulia, who lived 1 year and 20 days. Aurelia Artemidora, a most dutiful mother, had (this) made for her well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-05800297.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelia Iulia, who died in infancy, set up by her mother Aurelia Artemidora, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05800298.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Pia Aurelia lived 6 years and 15 days. Her father and mother made (this) for their well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-05800298.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for the child Aurelia Pia, set up by her parents, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05800299.translation: To Aurivia, her most sweet daughter, who lived 17 years, well-deserving.
EDCS-05800299.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurivia, likely set up by her parents, dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05800301.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Bibius Ursus, who lived 13 years, and to his brother
EDCS-05800301.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph dedicated to Bibius Ursus and his brother, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05800302.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aulus Cassellius Halus. Caesellia Psyche made (this) for her patron and husband, well-deserving.
EDCS-05800302.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to Aulus Cassellius Halus by his patron and wife, Caesellia Psyche, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05800303.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lucius Casperius Faustus, son of Lucius Casperius (tribus Palatina). Lucius Casperius Abascantus and Casperia Aeliana, parents, for their most dutiful son. Also Abascantus for Lucius Casperius Candidus Aelianus, freedman(?), son of Lucius Casperius (tribus Palatina) Extricatus, and for his wife Casperia Aelina, his dearest daughter; and for Lucius Casperius Fabius Latinus, son of Lucius Casperius (tribus Palatina), his most holy and dutiful son, who lived 18 years, 3 months, and 9 days; and for Casperia Zotis, freedwoman, and nurse of the daughter; and for Prosdecte, slave. They made (this).
EDCS-05800303.summary: This is a complex funerary epitaph set up by parents and possibly other family members for multiple deceased individuals, including children and a freedwoman/nurse, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05800304.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lucius Casperius Parthas. For his son Lucius Casperius Innocens, who lived 1 year, 1 month, and 9 days.
EDCS-05800304.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for the infant Lucius Casperius Innocens, set up by his father Lucius Casperius Parthas, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05800305.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Casperia Fida, who lived 3 years and 3 days, and for Lucius Casperius Repetitus, who lived 3 years, 9 months, and 13 days. Casperius Hermes and Septima, their children, made (this) for their most sweet children.
EDCS-05800305.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to two deceased children, Casperia Fida and Lucius Casperius Repetitus, by their children Casperius Hermes and Septima, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05800306.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph identifying the burial place of Cladus Arellius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05800307.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Claudius Aviola, his son, made this for Claudia Bassilla.
EDCS-05800307.summary: A son erected this tombstone for his mother, Claudia Bassilla.

EDCS-05800308.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Her father made this for his well-deserving daughter Cretestis. X Beritius Apollinaris.
EDCS-05800308.summary: A father dedicated this tombstone to his well-deserving daughter, Cretestis.

EDCS-05800309.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Helpis made this for Cruso malo, who lived 1 year and 7 months, her sweetest son.
EDCS-05800309.summary: Helpis dedicated this tombstone to her son, Cruso malo, who died at the age of 1 year and 7 months.

EDCS-05800310.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Daphnica. Iunia Veneria and Hlaero, her siblings.
EDCS-05800310.summary: Siblings Iunia Veneria and Hlaero dedicated this tombstone to Daphnica.

EDCS-05800311.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aglinus made this for Helpis, who lived 9 months, his daughter.
EDCS-05800311.summary: Aglinus dedicated this tombstone to his daughter, Helpis, who died at the age of 9 months.

EDCS-05800313.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Iunius Theon made this for his well-deserving wife Flavia Primitiba, with whom he lived for 17 years.
EDCS-05800313.summary: Marcus Iunius Theon dedicated this tombstone to his wife Flavia Primitiba, with whom he lived for 17 years.

EDCS-05800314.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Flavia Rhodina. Her mother Flavia Euche and her grandmother Flavia Melitine made this. She lived 1 year, 2 months, 11 days.
EDCS-05800314.summary: Flavia Rhodina's mother and grandmother dedicated this tombstone to her, recording her lifespan of 1 year, 2 months, and 11 days.

EDCS-05800315.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Epictesis made this for her dearest husband Gaudion, who deserved well. May your bones rest well. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-05800315.summary: Epicteesis, the contubernalis (a close companion or partner, often in a military context or in cohabitation), dedicated this tombstone to her husband Gaudion, including a standard epitaph wishing peace.

EDCS-05800317.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For her sweetest sons, Herennius Crescens and Felicissima. Caesia Helene, their mother.
EDCS-05800317.summary: Caesia Helene dedicated this tombstone to her two sons, Herennius Crescens and Felicissima.

EDCS-05800318.translation: By decree of the decurions. For Ionico, who lived 50 years. Philotea, his wife.
EDCS-05800318.summary: Philotea dedicated this tombstone to her husband Ionico, who lived 50 years, with the dedication authorized by a decree of the town council (decurions).

EDCS-05800319.translation: To the shades of the underworld. To Gaius Julius Felicis, his most beloved husband, Flavia Viandre [erected this].
EDCS-05800319.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Gaius Julius Felicis by his wife, Flavia Viandre.

EDCS-05800320.translation: To the shades of the underworld. For Lucius Julius, Amicia, and Artemisia, his wife, and Amicia, his daughter.
EDCS-05800320.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Lucius Julius and members of his family, likely by a surviving relative.

EDCS-05800322.translation: To the shades of the underworld. To Luria Stratonic, who lived 24 years. Luria Heorte [erected this] to her dearest daughter.
