EDCS-05800322.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Luria Stratonic, who died at 24, set up by her mother, Luria Heorte.

EDCS-05800323.translation: Philippus Marcellinus, who lived 12 years and 56 days. His parents, Talidi and Troianilia, made this for their well-deserving son.
EDCS-05800323.summary: This is a funerary monument for a young boy named Philippus Marcellinus, erected by his parents.

EDCS-05800324.translation: To Nebia Lucilia, who lived one year, 5 months, and 5 days. Her parents, Nebius Iustus and Baleria Bitalis, made this for their dearest daughter.
EDCS-05800324.summary: This funerary epitaph records the short life of Nebia Lucilia, who died before her second birthday, and was erected by her parents.

EDCS-05800327.translation: [Erected] to [a daughter], who lived 5 years and 9 months. Numitorius Primus and Numitoria Prima made this for their well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-05800327.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates a daughter who died at the age of 5 years and 9 months, and was erected by her parents.

EDCS-05800329.translation: To the shades of the underworld. To Titus Pinnio Epaphroditus, who lived 18 years and 5 months. Gaius Rustius Epictetus made this for his most worthy friend, well-deserving.
EDCS-05800329.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Pinnio Epaphroditus, set up by his friend Gaius Rustius Epictetus.

EDCS-05800330.translation: To the shades of the underworld. To Pompeia Severa, his dearest sister, who lived 22 years, 11 months, and 12 days, and to Titus Pompeius Severus, his father, pious and most indulgent, an excellent patron. Titus Pompeius Antoninus made this for his well-deserving parents, and for himself and his freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-05800330.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Titus Pompeius Antoninus for his sister, father, and himself, also dedicating space for his freedmen and their descendants.

EDCS-05800332.translation: Marcus Porcius Sim[---], freedman of Marcus. Porcia Caty[---], freedwoman of Marcus. Lucius Aurelius Agat[---], freedman of Lucius. Aurelia Nym[---], freedwoman of Lucius. On the front...
EDCS-05800332.summary: This inscription appears to be a boundary marker or property record listing several individuals, possibly indicating ownership or division of land.

EDCS-05800334.translation: To the shades of the underworld. To Sallustia Atticilla, his most beloved wife, and to Benerius, his son. Valerius Restutus made this for [them], well-deserving.
EDCS-05800334.summary: This funerary inscription was erected by Valerius Restutus for his wife Sallustia Atticilla and son Benerius.

EDCS-05800335.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Salvidena Corinthia, freedwoman of Salvidena, a beloved pet. Her heirs.
EDCS-05800335.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Salvidena Corinthia, a freedwoman, who is described as a beloved pet, set up by her heirs.

EDCS-05800338.translation: To Quintus Sunturio Eutyches, a deserving patron. His freedmen Ianuarius and Successus Eutyches made this.
EDCS-05800338.summary: This funerary inscription honors Quintus Sunturio Eutyches, a patron, with a dedication from his freedmen.

EDCS-05800339.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Terentia Helena. Lucius Terentius Parnaces made this for his dearest wife, with whom he lived for 15 years.
EDCS-05800339.summary: A funerary epitaph for Terentia Helena, dedicated by her husband Lucius Terentius Parnaces, noting their 15 years of marriage.

EDCS-05800340.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Tertulla, aged 31. Quintus Sabinius Statutus made this for his excellent wife and for Quietus, their son, who lived 2 months.
EDCS-05800340.summary: This tombstone records the death of Tertulla at 31 and her son Quietus at 2 months, erected by her husband Quintus Sabinius Statutus.

EDCS-05800342.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Ulpius Licinus, freedman of Marcus Ulpius, and for his excellent husband, with whom she lived for 20 years. [She] made this. He is buried here.
EDCS-05800342.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Ulpius Licinus, a freedman, dedicated by his wife who notes their 20 years of marriage and his burial.

EDCS-05800343.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Zoes lived 3 years, 11 months. Aquilinus made this for his sweetest daughter.
EDCS-05800343.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Zoes at 3 years and 11 months, dedicated by her father Aquilinus.

EDCS-05800344.translation: To [daughter] Ska, who lived 65 years, 10 months, 20 days. To [son] Aurelius Domitianus, who lived 6 years, 2 months, 1 day. Aurelius Crysomallus made this for his most deserving grandson and [his daughter-in-law?].
EDCS-05800344.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Aurelius Crysomallus for his daughter Ska and grandson Aurelius Domitianus, noting their ages and relationship.

EDCS-05800345.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Titus Flavius, who lived 28 years, 10 months. Flavia, his sister, made this of her own accord for a deserving man.
EDCS-05800345.summary: This funerary epitaph honors Titus Flavius, who lived 28 years and 10 months, dedicated by his sister Flavia.

EDCS-05800352.summary: A short inscription recording the restoration of something by Constantinus.

EDCS-05800374.translation: To Lucius Acilius Eutychetes, son of Lucius, of the Pomptina tribe, a noble member of the *communis mimorum*, a daily parasite of Apollo, a tragic and comic actor, and honored in all theatrical troupes, a decurion of Bovillae, whom they first of all called 'father' after their admission, the admitted members of the theatrical troupe, from pooled funds, because of his gifts and his piety towards them, for which he gave distributions to the admitted members, 25 denarii each, to the decurions of Bovillae, 5 denarii each, to the Augustales, 3 denarii each, and to the women of the honored class and the populace, 1 denarius each. Dedicated on the 3rd day before the Ides of August, when Sossius Priscus and Coelius Apollinaris were consuls. The curator was Quintus Sosius Augustianus. The Order of the Admitted. [Lists of names follow].
EDCS-05800374.summary: This complex inscription records the dedication of a monument or fund by the theatrical troupe *adlecti* in honor of Lucius Acilius Eutychetes, a distinguished actor and official, noting his benefactions and the distributions made in his honor on the occasion of the dedication in 152 CE.

EDCS-05800375.translation: To Gaius Dissenius Fuscus Hadrianus, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe. Gaius Dissenius Fuscus, his father, content with the honour of a statue publicly offered, made it with his own money. // To Gaius Dissenius Fuscus Sabinianus, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, prefect of the engineers, curator and patron of Albanius Longanus of Bovillae, in honor of his merits. // To Gaius Dissenius Fuscus, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe. Gaius Dissenius Fuscus, his father, content with the honour of a statue publicly offered, made it with his own money.
EDCS-05800375.summary: This is a funerary monument with two inscriptions honouring Gaius Dissenius Fuscus, likely a prominent local official, with statues by his father.

EDCS-05800376.translation: A site assigned by Gaius Dissenius Fuscus, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, curator of the public affairs of Bovillae, dedicated on the ninth day before the Kalends of January, in the consulship of Sextus Sulpicius Tertullus and Quintus Tineius Sacer. In recognition of its dedication, he gave to each of the ordinarii 9 HS, and likewise to each of the Augustales 3 HS, because they permitted the painting on his shield, which they placed in front of the new temple, of the image of Manlia Severina, his sister, the greatest virgin of Albani. After her death, he first instituted the elections for creating magistrates here, in the consulship of Marcus Civicus Barbarus and Marcus Metilius Regulus, in the year 290 AUC, with the consent of Gaius Dissenius Fuscus, the curator.
EDCS-05800376.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a public space and mentions a donation to local officials, linked to the curator Gaius Dissenius Fuscus and his deceased sister, dating to 290 AUC.

EDCS-05800382.translation: A small shrine with additions to the vestibules, five golden images, and the paved area. They placed a marble tetrastyle for Empress Faustina Augusta with a statue in the proscenium. Before the dedication of the temple, they provided a feast with scales. They gave to the decurions and Augustales 1 denarius.
EDCS-05800382.summary: This inscription details the construction and dedication of a small shrine, including furnishings and a feast for local officials, likely related to the worship of Empress Faustina.

EDCS-05800388.translation: To the divine shades. Flavia I...cina Aura... Navianus, sons Du... ITDIE
EDCS-05800388.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to deceased individuals, with names that are partially preserved.

EDCS-05800393.translation: Sacred to Hercules Augustus. Delfus, a domestic slave of our Caesar, a freedman and dispenser, willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-05800393.summary: An offering to Hercules Augustus made by a freedman and dispenser named Delfus, who served Caesar.

EDCS-05800395.translation: To the divine shades. For Marcus Aurelius Culino of the twelfth urban cohort.
EDCS-05800395.summary: A funerary dedication to Marcus Aurelius Culino, who served in the twelfth urban cohort.

EDCS-05800396.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Claudianus, Roman knight, an aspiring candidate, wise and innocent, who lived 32 years, 9 months, and 9 days. Claudius Ingenuus, a centurion of the sixth praetorian cohort, his father, placed his body in a sarcophagus.
EDCS-05800396.summary: A funerary epitaph for Tiberius Claudius Claudianus, a young Roman knight, erected by his father, a centurion.

EDCS-05800398.translation: To the divine shades. For Claudia Prisca, his most devoted wife. Eutyches Tryphonianus, a slave of our Caesar and a dispenser at the Mamurrana villa.
EDCS-05800398.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to Claudia Prisca by her husband, Eutyches Tryphonianus, a slave and administrator.

EDCS-05800399.translation: To the divine shades. For Marcus Laevius Severus, of the Fabian tribe, who lived 21 years, 9 months, and 19 days. Iustuleia Priscilla, his mother, made this for her most dutiful son, and for Marcus Laevius Martialis, her husband.
EDCS-05800399.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Laevius Severus, erected by his mother, who also honored her husband Marcus Laevius Martialis.

EDCS-05800400.translation: To Lucius Plutius Eros, freedman of Lucius, a purple-dyer from the Tuscan district. Plutia Auge, freedwoman of Lucius, made this for herself and for Veturia Attica, freedwoman of Gaius.
EDCS-05800400.summary: A funerary dedication made by Plutia Auge, a freedwoman and possibly a businesswoman in the purple dye trade, for herself and another freedwoman.

EDCS-05800401.translation: Of Titus Prifernius Paetus // [Of Titus] Prifernius [Paetus]
EDCS-05800401.summary: This inscription likely records the name of Titus Prifernius Paetus, possibly in a dedicatory or ownership context.

EDCS-05800402.summary: This inscription records the name of Valerius Messala, the purpose of which is unclear without further context.

EDCS-05800403.summary: This inscription records the name of Gaius Valerius Paullinus, likely as a dedicant or owner.

EDCS-05800416.translation: To the departed spirits. Antigonus made [this]
EDCS-05800416.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Antigonus set up a monument for a deceased person, likely a family member.

EDCS-05800417.translation: To the departed spirits. For Parthenopaeus, dearest son, [he/she] made [this]...
EDCS-05800417.summary: This is a funerary inscription where a parent or guardian dedicated a monument to their beloved son, Parthenopaeus.

EDCS-05800419.translation: To the departed spirits. Of Titus Seius Felix. Publius Rapellius Iustus, brother.
EDCS-05800419.summary: This funerary inscription records the deceased Titus Seius Felix, for whom his brother, Publius Rapellius Iustus, set up the monument.

EDCS-05800420.translation: To the departed spirits. Of Marcus Ulpius Primigenius. [They] made [this] for their own.
EDCS-05800420.summary: This funerary inscription indicates that relatives ('their own') set up a monument for Marcus Ulpius Primigenius.

EDCS-05800427.translation: Phileros, by decree of the Thirty, made this shrine to Semo Sancus from his own money.
EDCS-05800427.summary: This inscription records Phileros's dedication of a shrine to the god Semo Sancus, commissioned by the Thirty and funded by Phileros himself.

EDCS-05800432.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Iulianus, in charge of accounts and records, the best father-in-law, Sextus Pedius Iustus...
EDCS-05800432.summary: This is a funerary or honorific inscription dedicated by Sextus Pedius Iustus to his esteemed father-in-law, Marcus Aurelius Iulianus.

EDCS-05800435.translation: While Faustus Cornelius Sulla and Sextus Tedius Valerius Catullus were consuls, on the Kalends of June, Lucius Cornelius Pupillus, son of Aulus of the Falernian tribe, spoke. With Marcus Iunius Monimus, freedman of Silanus, and for his usefulness to the state and his love for the municipality, and that we may see his gifts to the municipality, I propose that the place which is outside the Porta Mediana, from that place where the school was, 67 feet long to the Alban water channel, and from the road inward, where there was formerly a public colonnade, 10 feet wide, be given for his honor to Marcus Iunius Monimus, freedman of Silanus, and his descendants. Then all agreed that since Marcus Iunius Monimus, freedman of Silanus, so loves the municipality and is useful to the state and munificent in making decorations for the municipality, the place which is outside the Porta Mediana, where there was a colonnade, from that place where the school was, 67 feet long to the Alban water channel, and 10 feet wide from the public road into private land, be given to Marcus Iunius Monimus, freedman of Silanus, and his descendants for honor. The decision was made in the council of twenty-six.
EDCS-05800435.summary: This lengthy inscription records a senatorial decree from the consulship of Sulla and Catullus, granting public land outside the Porta Mediana to Marcus Iunius Monimus and his descendants in recognition of his service and generosity to the municipality.

EDCS-05800436.translation: By decree of the decurions, for Marcus Iunius Monimus, freedman of Marcus Iunius Silanus, a public space was granted, 67 feet in front and 10 feet in depth.
EDCS-05800436.summary: This inscription records the granting of a public space to a freedman by decree of the town council, likely for a tomb, with dimensions provided.

EDCS-05800437.translation: Lucius Cornelius Pupillus, son of Lucius, of the Falerna tribe, prefect of the armourers, quinquennial flamen, patron of the people of Castrimoenium, and Gaius Iulius Carus, son of Spurius, of the Palatina tribe, Lucius Cornelius Albanus, son of Lucius, of the Falerna tribe, Lucius Cornelius Athictus, and Lucius Cornelius Eutychus, made this, for themselves and their families.
EDCS-05800437.summary: This inscription records the construction of a monument by several prominent citizens, including a prefect of armourers and a patron, for themselves and their families.

EDCS-05800439.translation: To the departed spirits. For Seberianus, a native slave of the state, who lived 24 years, 7 months, and 13 days. Decimus Rasticanius Abascantus and Sebera made this for their most devoted and deserving son.
EDCS-05800439.summary: This is a tomb inscription for a young man named Seberianus, detailing his age at death and erected by his parents.

EDCS-05800444.translation: For Lucius Aurellius Alexander, freedman of Glabra. Felix, his brother, made this. Lucius Aurellius Alexander, freedman of Lucius, and Asclepiades. Asclepiades Aurellius, freedman of Lucius, Felix, for themselves and their families.
EDCS-05800444.summary: This tomb inscription records the erection of a monument by a brother for his deceased brother, with additional dedications for family members, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-05800446.translation: To Aninia Asteria, for herself and her brother. Gaius Iulius Speratus.
EDCS-05800446.summary: This inscription appears to be for a tomb, dedicated by Gaius Iulius Speratus for Aninia Asteria and her brother.

EDCS-05800447.translation: To the departed spirits. For Aurelius Zosimus. Aurelia Antiochis made this for her well-deserving wife.
EDCS-05800447.summary: This is a tomb inscription dedicated by Aurelia Antiochis to her wife, Aurelius Zosimus, for her deservingness.

EDCS-05800448.translation: for 22 years. Callistus, freedman, and Ennia, his wife, and for themselves, their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-05800448.summary: This is a tomb inscription, likely incomplete, detailing the age of a deceased person and dedicating the monument to themselves and their extended household.

EDCS-05800449.translation: Marcus Captius Philo, Captia Philotea, Captia Erato, Captia Secunda. 16 feet in depth, 16 feet in front.
EDCS-05800449.summary: This inscription likely marks a plot of land allocated for burial, with dimensions provided and multiple names associated with it, possibly family members or owners.

EDCS-05800450.translation: To the departed spirits. For Tiberius Claudius Carpophorus. Tiberius Claudius Silvester, [his] freedman.
EDCS-05800450.summary: This tomb inscription records the dedication of a monument by Tiberius Claudius Silvester, a freedman, to his deceased patron, Tiberius Claudius Carpophorus.

EDCS-05800451.translation: To the departed spirits. For Cornelia Anniana, daughter, already babbling, a two-year-old, not yet who lived one year, 3 months, and 10 days, most sweet. Parents made this from their own means.
EDCS-05800451.summary: This is a tomb inscription for a very young child, Cornelia Anniana, detailing her age at death and erected by her grieving parents.

EDCS-05800452.translation: To the departed spirits. To Publius? Muna?, who lived 5 years and 24 days. Caecilia Musa made this for her dearest foster-son.
EDCS-05800452.summary: This is a funerary dedication from a foster-mother to her deceased foster-son, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-05800454.text_edition: \n Caius Herius Felix patronus Heriae Helpinis sic hic est ille situs Herius Felix coaequalibus cuncteis quei veixit annos viginti quei me reliquit leiberta Helpine annorum natam XIII sibi unice caram quae illius ossa restituit post annos viginti et monumentum nobis aedificavi qui legit discat esse plus\n
EDCS-05800454.translation: Caius Herius Felix, patron of Heria Helpinis, is buried here. This is that Herius Felix who lived twenty years with all his peers, who left me, his freedwoman Helpine, aged 13, to myself, uniquely dear to him, and who restored his bones after twenty years, and built this monument for us. Let whoever reads this know that it is [worth] more.
EDCS-05800454.summary: This funerary inscription records the life and death of Caius Herius Felix, erected by his freedwoman Helpine, who also restored his remains and built the monument, suggesting a bond of affection and gratitude.

EDCS-05800455.translation: To the departed spirits. To Homonoia, daughter of Lucilla?, of Augustus?, a home-born slave, who lived 15 years. Agathocles and [---] to his wife [---].
EDCS-05800455.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to a young enslaved woman, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-05800456.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulia Ionice [dedicated this] to Caius Iulius Aliaeus, her patron, who deserved well.
EDCS-05800456.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication by a freedwoman to her patron, implying a relationship of respect and gratitude, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-05800458.translation: To the departed spirits. Auxesis made this for her husband Caius Valerius Hymenaeus, who deserved well.
EDCS-05800458.summary: This is a funerary epitaph from a wife to her deserving husband, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-05800463.translation: Phileros and Antiochus willingly and deservedly gave [this] as a gift to the Lares.
EDCS-05800463.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to the household gods (Lares) by two individuals, Phileros and Antiochus.

EDCS-05800464.translation: Sacred to Minerva. Lucius Volumnius Verus and Severus, prefects of a cohort, military tribunes, prefects of cavalry.
EDCS-05800464.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Minerva by two military officers who held various ranks, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-05800465.translation: The two Volumnii, Verus and Severus, gave this gift to Diana Invicta.
EDCS-05800465.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to the goddess Diana Invicta by two individuals, Verus and Severus.

EDCS-05800470.text_edition: \n Caio Iavoleno Calvino Geminio Kapitoni Cornelio Pollioni Squillae Quinto Vulkacio Scuppidio Vero consuli proconsuli provinciae Baeticae legato Augusti pro praetore provinciae Lusitaniae legato legionis III Gallicae praetori candidato divi Hadriani tribuno plebis candidato quaestori provinciae Africae tribuno militum legionis V Macedonicae Xviro stlitibus iudicandis\n
EDCS-05800470.translation: To Caius Iavolenus Calvinus Geminus Capitolinus Cornelii Pollionis, consul, proconsul of the province of Baetica, imperial legate with praetorian powers of the province of Lusitania, legate of the III Gallica legion, praetor candidate, divi Hadriani tribune of the plebs candidate, quaestor of the province of Africa, military tribune of the V Macedonica legion, decemvir for settling lawsuits.
EDCS-05800470.summary: This is a detailed career inscription for Caius Iavolenus Calvinus Geminus Capitolinus Cornelii Pollionis, outlining his various high-ranking political and military offices, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-05800476.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio Aspro consuli praetori curatori viae Appiae sodali Augustali tribuno plebis quaestori provinciae Africae curatori aedium sacrarum Sextus Umidius Artemidorus amico incomparabili\n
EDCS-05800476.translation: To Caius Julius Aspro, consul, praetor, curator of the Appian Way, Augustal sodalis, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of the province of Africa, curator of sacred buildings. Sextus Umidius Artemidorus [erected this] to his incomparable friend.
EDCS-05800476.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Sextus Umidius Artemidorus to his friend Caius Julius Aspro, highlighting Aspro's distinguished career in public service.

EDCS-05800477.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio Aspro consuli praetori curatori viae Appiae sodali Augustali tribuno plebis quaestori provinciae Africae Cornelius \n
EDCS-05800477.translation: To Gaius Julius Aspro, consul, praetor, curator of the Appian Way, fellow of the Augustales, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of the province of Africa, Cornelius...
EDCS-05800477.summary: This is a dedication by Cornelius to Gaius Julius Aspro, listing his numerous offices, including consul and governor of Africa, likely dating to the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05800479.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio Aspro consuli praetori curatori viae Appiae sodali Augustali tribuno plebis quaestori provinciae Africae curatori aedium sacrarum provincia Britannia patrono\n
EDCS-05800479.translation: To Gaius Julius Aspro, consul, praetor, curator of the Appian Way, fellow of the Augustales, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of the province of Africa, curator of sacred buildings in the province of Britain, patron...
EDCS-05800479.summary: This text records a dedication to Gaius Julius Aspro, highlighting his extensive career which included roles in Italy, Britain, and Africa, and dating it to the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05800480.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio Aspro consuli designato curatori viae Appiae sodali Augustali praetori peregrino tribuno plebis quaestori provinciae Africae curatori aedium sacrarum provincia Mauretania Tingitana patrono optimo\n
EDCS-05800480.translation: To Gaius Julius Aspro, designated consul, curator of the Appian Way, fellow of the Augustales, peregrine praetor, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of the province of Africa, curator of sacred buildings in the province of Mauretania Tingitana, his most excellent patron...
EDCS-05800480.summary: This inscription honors Gaius Julius Aspro, listing his significant political and administrative positions in various provinces, including Africa and Mauretania Tingitana, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05800481.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio Galerio Aspro praetori tribuno plebis quaestori provinciae Africae curatori aedium sacrarum Cornelius Pla amico incomparabili\n
EDCS-05800481.translation: To Gaius Julius Galera Aspro, praetor, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of the province of Africa, curator of sacred buildings, Cornelius Pla made this for his incomparable friend...
EDCS-05800481.summary: This dedication by Cornelius Pla commemorates his friend Gaius Julius Galera Aspro, noting his roles as praetor and quaestor of Africa, probably from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05800488.summary: This is a tombstone for Celadus, who served as a paymaster (dispensator) for Caesar, likely from the early imperial period.

EDCS-05800492.translation: Atimetus, his freedman, made this for Marcus Pompeius Aspro, son of Marcus, of the Aniensis tribe, centurion of the XV Apollinaris legion, centurion of the III Praetorian cohort, first centurion of the III Cyrenaicae legion, prefect of the camp of the XX Victrix legion; and for himself, and for his son Marcus Pompeius Aspro, son of Marcus, of the Collina tribe, and for his wife Cincia Saturnina, and for his younger son Marcus Pompeius Aspro, son of Marcus, of the Collina tribe.
EDCS-05800492.summary: This funerary monument, set up by a freedman, commemorates Marcus Pompeius Aspro, a soldier who rose to the rank of prefect of the camp, and his family, likely dating to the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05800493.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Acilia Balbilla made this for her most dutiful mother, Acilia Tatia.
EDCS-05800493.summary: This is a tombstone erected by Acilia Balbilla for her mother, Acilia Tatia, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-05800495.text_edition: \n Dis inferis Manibus Albina Tyche fecit Cornelio Agathangelo coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-05800495.translation: Albina Tyche made this for her well-deserving husband, Cornelius Agathangelus.
EDCS-05800495.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Albina Tyche to her husband Cornelius Agathangelus, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-05800507.translation: Claudius Eutyches, his grandfather, and Claudius Pothumenus, his father, and Decia Rufina, his mother, made this for Claudius Irenicus, their sweetest son.
EDCS-05800507.summary: This is a tombstone set up by parents and a grandfather for their beloved son, Claudius Irenicus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-05800528.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Spessina made this for her deserving husband, whom she had with him. He lived 15 years [in marriage?]; he lived 45 years [in life].
EDCS-05800528.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of a husband who lived 45 years and was married for 15 years, erected by his wife Spessina, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-05800529.translation: Varena Sabina gave the place; Marcus Publilius, freedman of Publilia and Caius Vibius Rufus, made it for himself, and for Vibius Spudena, freedwoman of Caius, his most devoted fellow freedwoman, and for Caius Vibius Oecidus, freedman of Caius, and for Varena Sabina, and for their son Gnaeus Caninius Favor, and for her husband Gnaeus Caninius Faustus, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-05800529.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Marcus Publilius, freedman of Vibius Rufus, for himself and his family and freedpersons, likely dating to the late 1st century BC or early 1st century AD.

EDCS-05800540.translation: To Timinia Sitis, dearest daughter, of every kind of sweetness. Posidonius [says this]. What I said to you.
EDCS-05800540.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Posidonius to his beloved daughter Timinia Sitis, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-05800554.translation: To the Dioscuri. Quintus [Flavius] Balbus, consul.
EDCS-05800554.summary: This is a dedication to the Dioscuri (Castores) by Quintus Flavius Balbus, who held the consulship, probably dating to the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-05800558.translation: To Mars Gradivus, Lucius Plutius Pius, aedile, monitor, augur, prefect of the sacred rites, gave this as a gift.
EDCS-05800558.summary: This is a dedication to Mars Gradivus by Lucius Plutius Pius, who held several religious and civic offices, likely from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-05800565.translation: Lucius Caninius Avidus, son of Caius, made [this] willingly for the deserving god.
EDCS-05800565.summary: This is a dedication to an unnamed god by Lucius Caninius Avidus, likely a private votive offering from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05800566.translation: Caius Julius Liberalis paid his vow to the god willingly and with [his whole] spirit.
EDCS-05800566.summary: This is a votive inscription by Caius Julius Liberalis, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05800567.translation: To Fortuna Tusculana, most blessed. Caius Prastina Pacatus, consul.
EDCS-05800567.summary: This is a dedication to Fortuna Tusculana by Caius Prastina Pacatus, who was a consul, likely from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-05800568.translation: Lucius Tettius Hermetio, seviral Augustalis, priest of Isis, paid his vow willingly.
EDCS-05800568.summary: This is a votive inscription by Lucius Tettius Hermetio, a priest of Isis and a member of the Augustales, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05800569.translation: Dedicated [on the] 8th day before the Kalends of July, in the consulship of Commodus Augustus V and Glabrio II. The place was given by Acestius Rufus, supervisor of the works, during the aedilship of Lucius Avilius Metilianus and [---] Vibius Rufus.
EDCS-05800569.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a public space on a specific date during the consulships of Commodus and Glabrio, with details about the officials involved, likely dating to the late 2nd century AD.

EDCS-05800571.translation: [To Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, grandson of the deified Julius, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 34th time, consul for the 5th time, imperator for the 8th time]. [---] Priscus, son of [---], curator of the youth games.
EDCS-05800571.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Tiberius, mentioning his titles and powers, and also records Priscus, curator of youth games, likely dating to the early 1st century AD.

EDCS-05800575.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, prince of the youth, present divinity, restorer and preserver forever of his own life and dignity, most devoted to his divinity, Marcus Aemilius Macer Faustinianus, a most distinguished man. Dedicated on the eighteenth day before the Kalends of September, when Catio Sabinus and Cornelius Anullinus were consuls.
EDCS-05800575.summary: A dedication to Emperor Marcus Aurelius by Marcus Aemilius Macer Faustinianus, a distinguished man, commemorating the emperor's divine status and acts of restoration, dated to 193 AD.

EDCS-05800579.summary: An inscription identifying Quintus Caecilius Metellus as consul, likely a label on a public monument or building.

EDCS-05800581.translation: His wife Opsilia made (this) for Marcus Coelius Vinicianus, son of Marcus, praetor, proconsul, tribune of the plebs, quaestor.
EDCS-05800581.summary: A funerary monument erected by Opsilia for her husband Marcus Coelius Vinicianus, detailing his political and administrative career.

EDCS-05800582.translation: For Mani Cordius Rufus, son of Marcus, praetor, proconsul, lustrális aedile, monitor of sacred rites.
EDCS-05800582.summary: An inscription honoring Mani Cordius Rufus, listing his public offices including praetor, proconsul, and a role in sacred rites.

EDCS-05800583.translation: Marcus Cusinius, son of Marcus, of the Velina tribe, plebeian aedile, prefect of the treasury, later praetor; Marcus Cusinius, son of Marcus, of the Velina tribe, his father; Fictoria, daughter of Caius, his mother; Cusinia, daughter of Marcus, his sister.
EDCS-05800583.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Cusinius, detailing his career as plebeian aedile and prefect of the treasury, and also naming his father, mother, and sister.

EDCS-05800584.translation: To the Divine Shades. Titus Flavius Hermes, quaestorian escort, then my constant attendant, always illuminated my home well, friend. Unio made (this) for himself and for his excellent wife Gallia Tyche, and for his freedman Isidorus, and for their descendants, and for his wife's descendants, and for Isidorus and their descendants, and for his adopted son Marcus Publicius Felix. I ask you, passing by, to pause and read the verses that I dictated and ordered to be written: 'Though earth is light upon me, deservedly, I rest; enclosed in marble, I have returned the deposit. I have always gathered friends; I disturbed no marriage beds; no one will complain. My dear wife lived well with me, always honestly. I provided what I could, always departing without dispute. There was only one friend for me who always honestly provided everything.'
EDCS-05800584.summary: A self-commemorative funerary inscription by Titus Flavius Hermes for himself, his wife Gallia Tyche, his freedman Isidorus, and his adopted son Marcus Publicius Felix, including a lengthy epitaph in verse detailing his life and friendships.

EDCS-05800586.translation: For Marcus Gavius Appalius Maximus, son of Titus, of the Velina tribe, a most distinguished man, an associate of the Hadriatic college, legate of the province Narbonensis, quaestor of Augustus. Gavius Fortis, his freedman and procurator, (erected this) for his merits.
EDCS-05800586.summary: A dedication to Marcus Gavius Appalius Maximus, a distinguished figure who held various significant posts including legate and quaestor, by his freedman and procurator.

EDCS-05800587.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Gavia Helpidia, his most holy wife. Titus Flavius Epaphra, freedman of Augustus, procurator of the Tusculan villas.
EDCS-05800587.summary: A funerary inscription for Gavia Helpidia, the wife of Titus Flavius Epaphra, who served as a procurator of imperial villas.

EDCS-05800588.translation: For Sextus Quintilius Valerius Maximus, son of Sextus, of the Aniensis tribe, legate of the province of Achaia, praetor, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of the province of Achaia, tribune of the legions I Italica and XIII Gemina, commissioner of roads.
EDCS-05800588.summary: A fragmentary inscription detailing the extensive career of Sextus Quintilius Valerius Maximus, including military and administrative appointments in Greece.

EDCS-05800589.translation: For Rubellia Bassia, daughter of Rubellius Blandus. Servius Octavius Laenas Pontianus (erected this) for his excellent grandmother.
EDCS-05800589.summary: A dedication to Rubellia Bassia, daughter of Rubellius Blandus, by her grandson Servius Octavius Laenas Pontianus.

EDCS-05800590.translation: The censors Publius Sulpicius and Gaius Antonius, Publius Peticius Apollodorus, freedman of Publius, [hereby declare (?)].
EDCS-05800590.summary: This inscription likely records a declaration by censors regarding a district or a person within the Galban district, possibly dating to the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-05800595.translation: To the departed spirits. For Publius Avidius Trachalus, prefect of a cohort, also tribune of a cohort, father-in-law, son-in-law, the best.
EDCS-05800595.summary: This is a tombstone inscription dedicated to Publius Avidius Trachalus, honoring his military and familial roles, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05800596.translation: Flavia Vera and her son Claudius Verus made this for Claudius Verus, the evocate, deserving.
EDCS-05800596.summary: This monument was erected by a mother and son for a deceased relative named Claudius Verus, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05800597.translation: To the departed spirits. For Aulus Fabius Proculus, son of Aulus, of the Papiria tribe(?), prefect of the 1st Cohort of Dacians, military tribune of the II Adjutrix Legion. Lucceia Nepotilla, daughter of Marcus, made this for her most deserving husband.
EDCS-05800597.summary: This funerary inscription honors Aulus Fabius Proculus, detailing his military career as a prefect and tribune, and was erected by his wife Lucceia Nepotilla, likely dating to the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05800600.translation: Marcus Acilius [---], [---] GEM, quinquennial aediles, [acted] with money from fines.
EDCS-05800600.summary: This inscription records the actions of quinquennial aediles, Marcus Acilius and another, concerning money collected from fines, possibly from the early Empire.

EDCS-05800601.translation: Gaius Caelius Rufus, son of Gaius, and Gaius Caninius Rebilus, son of Gaius, aediles [---].
EDCS-05800601.summary: This inscription names Gaius Caelius Rufus and Gaius Caninius Rebilus as aediles, likely for a public works project or festival, possibly from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-05800602.translation: [Gaius Cael]ius Rufus, son of Gaius [---], and [---]ea, an emissary channel, [was built?] around that area [---] of Tiburtine stone // and the steps around that area [---] to be placed, [---] of Tiburtine stone with [---] for the games [---].
EDCS-05800602.summary: This inscription describes the construction of an emissary channel and steps near an area, using Tiburtine stone, likely for public works or games, probably from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-05800603.translation: Caelia, daughter of Publius, gave an image of Lucius Saufeius, son of Appius, born from her, as a gift to her municipality.
EDCS-05800603.summary: This inscription records Caelia's dedication of a statue of Lucius Saufeius to her municipality, likely from the 1st century BCE or 1st century CE.

EDCS-05800604.translation: Gnaeus Caninius Amiro and Publius Aelius Felix, as aediles, ordered [---] unequal [measurements and] weights [[---]].
EDCS-05800604.summary: This inscription states that Gnaeus Caninius Amiro and Publius Aelius Felix, acting as aediles, took action against unfair weights and measures, likely from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-05800606.translation: Marcus Durmius [son of ---], Gaius Caelius, son of Gaius, [acted] by decree of the Senate.
EDCS-05800606.summary: This inscription indicates that Marcus Durmius and Gaius Caelius acted in accordance with a senatorial decree, possibly related to a public commission, likely from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-05800607.translation: By decree of the Senate, from the gifts of Gaius Fabius Passienus Saturninus, son of Gaius, augur and lustratory aedile.
EDCS-05800607.summary: This inscription records the source of funds for a public work or offering, originating from the generous contributions of Caius Fabius Passienus Saturninus, an augur and aedile.

EDCS-05800609.translation: To Titus Flavius Hilario, freedman of Titus, decurion of the guild of craftsmen, from his 15th lustrum to the 16th lustrum of the suffragators; quinquennial master of the guild of timber-framing craftsmen from his 17th lustrum, honored from his 18th lustrum; censor twice for the creation of a master from his 19th and 20th lustrum; judge among the chosen twelve from the order from his 22nd lustrum. Claudia Prisca, daughter of Titus, to her excellent husband; and Flavia Priscilla, daughter of Titus, to her excellent father.
EDCS-05800609.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Claudia Prisca and Flavia Priscilla to their husband and father, Titus Flavius Hilario, detailing his numerous prestigious offices within various guilds and civic bodies over many years.

EDCS-05800610.translation: To Flavia Tarentina, daughter of Gaius. Cornelia Secunda, daughter of Decimus, her mother, set this up for her fellow-citizen and comrade.
EDCS-05800610.summary: A funerary monument set up by Cornelia Secunda for her mother, Flavia Tarentina, honoring her status as a fellow-citizen and comrade.

EDCS-05800612.translation: To the divine shades. Antestius Victorinus, Agathemerus, and Asclepiodotus made this for Julius Severinus, patron of the worshippers of Diana, who deserved well.
EDCS-05800612.summary: A funerary dedication to Julius Severinus, patron of the worshippers of Diana, erected by his clients, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05800613.translation: Sextus Octavius Felicianus, son of Sextus, of the Palatina tribe, senator of the town and aedil[is] rex sacrorum, because of the honor offered to him of the prefecture, obtained a part of their school from the guild of dendrophoroi and the town council, and completed the whole work with his own money.
EDCS-05800613.summary: This inscription commemorates Sextus Octavius Felicianus, a senator and aedil[is] rex sacrorum, who significantly contributed his own funds to enhance a building or facility associated with the dendrophoroi guild, likely in recognition of his appointment as prefect.

EDCS-05800614.translation: To Plutia Olympias, daughter of Aulus, comrade of the youth. A place granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-05800614.summary: This inscription indicates that a burial plot was allocated to Plutia Olympias, a member of the youth association, by official decree.

EDCS-05800615.translation: To Marcus Pontius Felix, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, senator, aedil[is] of the town, comrade, and likewise aedil[is] and curator of the comrades, the townsfolk and inhabitants, from pooled money, because of his integrity, diligence, and other administrations, set up on the ninth day before the Kalends of June, with Marcus Antonius Rufus and Servius Octavius Lenate as consuls. For the dedication of the statue, he gave a feast and mulsum to the people.
EDCS-05800615.summary: This is a public dedication honoring Marcus Pontius Felix, a prominent local official and guild member, with a statue, funded by the community, for his service, which was celebrated with a public feast.

EDCS-05800616.translation: To Marcus Tusculanius Amianthus, master of the attendants of Castor and Pollux, who had held the office of Augustalis. Marcus Tusculanius Receptus, son of Marcus, to his brother.
EDCS-05800616.summary: A funerary inscription set up by Marcus Tusculanius Receptus for his brother, Marcus Tusculanius Amianthus, who served as master of the attendants of the deified emperors Castor and Pollux.

EDCS-05800617.translation: Gaius Volcacius Labeo, son of Gaius, and Marcus Plaetorius Macer, son of Lucius, grandson of Marcus, aediles.
EDCS-05800617.summary: This inscription records the names of two individuals, Gaius Volcacius Labeo and Marcus Plaetorius Macer, who served together as aediles.

EDCS-05800618.translation: By permission of the attendants of Castor and Pollux. To Lucius Volumnius Amerimnus, curator and patron.
EDCS-05800618.summary: This inscription, made with the permission of the attendants of Castor and Pollux, dedicates a monument to Lucius Volumnius Amerimnus in his capacity as curator and patron.

EDCS-05800631.translation: Clodius Tertius, physician. Aulus Clodius Metrodorus, physician. Clodia Hilara, freedwoman of Aulus.
EDCS-05800631.summary: This inscription records the names and professions of three individuals, likely related, identified as physicians and a freedwoman.

EDCS-05800639.translation: To Caverasias Pacatilla, daughter of Publius, aged 12.
EDCS-05800639.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caverasias Pacatilla, a young girl who died at the age of 12.

EDCS-05800645.translation: Tiberius Claudius Primitivus and Cornelius Chryseros made this.
EDCS-05800645.summary: This inscription records the makers of a monument, identified as Tiberius Claudius Primitivus and Cornelius Chryseros.

EDCS-05800646.summary: This inscription records the maker of a monument, identified as Gnaeus Cornelius Chryseros.

EDCS-05800647.summary: This inscription records the name Lucius Aennius Fortunatus, possibly the dedicator or the deceased.

EDCS-05800648.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or ownership marker for Numerius Iunius Faustus.

EDCS-05800651.translation: Acratus, freedman of Acratus, freedman of Tiberius Iulius Himerus, freedman of the Emperor, lived 41 years.
EDCS-05800651.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Acratus, who was a freedman of another freedman of Emperor Augustus and lived to be 41.

EDCS-05800662.translation: To Aurelia Calliste, his faithful wife. Aulus Caecina Iustus [made this].
EDCS-05800662.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Aulus Caecina Iustus to his wife, Aurelia Calliste.

EDCS-05800664.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Barneus made this for his deserving freedwoman Lascibe.
EDCS-05800664.summary: This funerary inscription records the maker, Barneus, and the deserving freedwoman, Lascibe, to whom it is dedicated.

EDCS-05800665.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. [For landa?], his alumna, lived 14 years and 4 months. Caecilia Secunda made this for her deserving alumna.
EDCS-05800665.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of an alumna at a young age and her dedication by Caecilia Secunda.

EDCS-05800667.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Calpurniae Terpomene Publius Calpurnius Eumenes sorori\n
EDCS-05800667.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius Calpurnius Eumenes for his sister Calpurnia Terpomene.
EDCS-05800667.summary: A tombstone erected by Publius Calpurnius Eumenes for his sister Calpurnia Terpomene.

EDCS-05800670.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Mamilia Albana for her daughter, Claudia Primigenia, freedwoman of Augustus, who deserved well. She lived 15 years, 4 months.
EDCS-05800670.summary: A tombstone set up by Mamilia Albana for her daughter, a freedwoman of Augustus, who died at age 15.

EDCS-05800673.translation: Eros, freedman of Gaius; Autumna, freedwoman of a woman; Prima, freedwoman of Gaius; Felix, freedman.
EDCS-05800673.summary: This inscription lists four freedmen, likely part of a family group or associated with a household, possibly on a tombstone.

EDCS-05800677.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Fanius Corintus and Sofias made [this] for Fanio Primitibo, who lived 12 years, an incomparable student.
EDCS-05800677.summary: A tombstone erected by Fanius Corintus and Sofias for Fanio Primitibo, a 12-year-old student.

EDCS-05800678.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Flavia Stefanis, a good wife, with whom he lived...
EDCS-05800678.summary: A funerary dedication to Flavia Stefanis, recognizing her as a good wife.

EDCS-05800694.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Januaria, her most devoted sister, who lived 15 years, 16 months. Adventus, her brother, made [this] for her, who deserved well.
EDCS-05800694.summary: A tombstone erected by Adventus for his sister Januaria, who died at the age of 15 years and 16 months.

EDCS-05800695.translation: ...Italicus and S... for their incomparable son, who deserved well, made [this].
EDCS-05800695.summary: A funerary dedication by Italicus and another person for their son.

EDCS-05800697.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Gaius Julius Secularis, son of Gaius, son of Gaius.
EDCS-05800697.summary: A funerary dedication for Gaius Julius Secularis, identifying his lineage.

EDCS-05800700.translation: Iunia, daughter of Marcus; Marcus Junius; Silicia Theo, freedwoman of Silicia Theo; Marcus Junius, son of Marcus.
EDCS-05800700.summary: This inscription lists several individuals, likely members of the Iunius and Silicia families, possibly on a tomb or monument.

EDCS-05800701.translation: Publius Licinius Philonicus, freedman of Publius. Publius Licinius Demetrius, freedman of Publius, for his patron.
EDCS-05800701.summary: This inscription records two freedmen, Publius Licinius Philonicus and Publius Licinius Demetrius, with Demetrius dedicating the inscription to his patron.

EDCS-05800702.translation: To [---]ius Lucretianus, son of Titus, and to Octavia [---]ia, his father-in-law's wife.
EDCS-05800702.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Lucretianus and Octavia, likely a family tomb.

EDCS-05800706.translation: To the departed spirits. Veturrius Apollonius had this made for Narcissus, a freedman and servant of our Caesar, with Apollonius the heir overseeing the work.
EDCS-05800706.summary: This funerary monument was erected for Narcissus, a servant of the emperor, by his heir Veturrius Apollonius.

EDCS-05800709.translation: Gaius Oppius Philargyrus, freedman of Gaius, made this for himself and his family: Hortensia Erotis, freedwoman of Marcus; Gaius Oppius Salvius, freedman of Gaius; Gaius Oppius Albus, freedman of Gaius; and Oppia Alba, freedwoman of Gaius.
EDCS-05800709.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Gaius Oppius Philargyrus for himself and his freedmen and freedwomen, likely a family plot.

EDCS-05800713.translation: To the departed spirits. Caecilia Secunda, an extremely unhappy mother, made this for her son Gaius Pomponius Secundinus, who lived 22 years, 11 months, and 21 days; she lost such a son while still alive.
EDCS-05800713.summary: This tombstone records the death of Gaius Pomponius Secundinus, who died at age 22, and was set up by his grieving mother.

EDCS-05800714.translation: To the departed spirits. For Prima, freedwoman of Atilius Serranus, and her well-deserving freedman companion Euangelus.
EDCS-05800714.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Prima and her freedman companion Euangelus.

EDCS-05800718.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph for Gaius Rustius Diogenes.

EDCS-05800720.translation: Rutilia, daughter of Lucius, the queenly aunt.
EDCS-05800720.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely refers to Rutilia, identifying her by her father and a familial relationship.

EDCS-05800721.translation: Rutilia, daughter of Publius, the grandmother.
EDCS-05800721.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely refers to Rutilia, identifying her by her father and as a grandmother.

EDCS-05800722.translation: Blastus, his freedman, made this for Lucius Sall[---] Primigenius.
EDCS-05800722.summary: This funerary inscription was erected by Blastus for his patron Lucius Sall[---] Primigenius.

EDCS-05800725.translation: Gaius Sulpicius Felix, freedman of Gaius, and Maxima.
EDCS-05800725.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Sulpicius Felix and Maxima.

EDCS-05800726.translation: Taemulentia Hilar[a], freedwoman of Gaius, [erected this for] Taem[ulentius], freedman of [the same woman].
EDCS-05800726.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Taemulentia Hilara, likely a freedwoman, and dedicating a monument to Taemulentius, also a freedman.

EDCS-05800732.translation: Marcus Valerius, son of Marcus, [was born/lived/died].
EDCS-05800732.summary: This is an incomplete inscription providing the name and filiation of Marcus Valerius, likely the subject of a funerary monument.

EDCS-05800733.translation: Gnaeus Velineius, son of Gnaeus, paternal uncle.
EDCS-05800733.summary: This inscription names Gnaeus Velineius, son of Gnaeus, and identifies him as a paternal uncle, possibly on a tombstone or family monument.

EDCS-05800735.translation: To the departed spirits. Publius Aelius Proclus made this for his incomparable and most sacred wife, Vettia Iusta, with whom he lived for 22 years, a woman who deserved well.
EDCS-05800735.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Publius Aelius Proclus for his wife Vettia Iusta, commemorating their marriage of 22 years.

EDCS-05800738.translation: Volumnia Phoeni[c]a, freedwoman of Gaius Volumnius Salvius, made this from her own resources.
EDCS-05800738.summary: This inscription records that Volumnia Phoeni[c]a, a freedwoman, erected something from her own funds, likely a monument or a contribution.

EDCS-05800740.translation: To the departed spirits. [Name, son of Gnaeus], of the position of epulo, made this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-05800740.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for an unnamed man, son of Gnaeus, who held the office of epulo, and was dedicated to his deserving wife.

EDCS-05800749.translation: ]rio / [for] Priscus / [for his friend]lio Festo / [who was] plebeian aedile, and quaestor of the city, / [for] Oppius(?) Quadratus(?), / his most excellent friend.
EDCS-05800749.summary: This is a dedication to Oppius (?) Quadratus (?), honoring his distinguished career as aedile plebis and urban quaestor, and remembering him as an excellent friend.

EDCS-05800751.translation: To the departed spirits. For Parthenius, treasurer of the public business of the people of Lavicanum and Quintanensium.
EDCS-05800751.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Parthenius, who served as the treasurer (arcarius) for the communities of Lavicanum and Quintanensium.

EDCS-05800753.translation: From theDiana shrine, up to the land of Clementianus, [about] a thousand paces.
EDCS-05800753.summary: This inscription appears to mark a boundary or distance related to a shrine of Diana and the property of Clementianus, possibly related to land ownership or a road.

EDCS-05800756.summary: This is an incomplete inscription that begins with the name Gaius Vettenius, likely part of a funerary or dedicatory monument.

EDCS-05800760.translation: Tiberius Claudius Daphnus, freedman of Emperor Augustus, made this, to his freedman and freedman's freedman, Domesticus, and to his partner Moschidus, for whom they had formerly been and to whom they had given thanks for their merits.
EDCS-05800760.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument erected by Tiberius Claudius Daphnus for his deceased freedmen and their partners, expressing gratitude for their services.

EDCS-05800762.translation: To the departed spirits. Quintus Fabius Quintianus Dasumius.
EDCS-05800762.summary: A funerary dedication for Quintus Fabius Quintianus Dasumius, likely a tombstone.

EDCS-05800764.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulia Successa made this for her daughter Iulia Iusta, daughter of Gaius.
EDCS-05800764.summary: A funerary monument erected by a mother for her deceased daughter, Iulia Iusta.

EDCS-05800765.translation: Marcus Popillius Felix and Popillia Crispina, most wretched parents, made this for their most dutiful daughter Popillia Isias, daughter of Marcus, who lived 14 years, 7 months, and 3 days, and for their dearest wife. Gaius Servilius Pallans also made this for his dearest wife.
EDCS-05800765.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Popillia Isias, who died at age 14, erected by her parents and her husband.

EDCS-05800767.translation: To Aurelia Felicitas, deserving [of good things], placed on deposit on the third day before the Ides of April.
EDCS-05800767.summary: This inscription likely records the deposit of a funerary offering or the burial date of Aurelia Felicitas.

EDCS-05800769.translation: Sacred to Holy Hercules. Tullius Romulus, a veteran of the legion, and Venilia Eutychia, his wife, together with their son Tullius Romulus, a Roman knight, fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-05800769.summary: A dedication to Hercules by a veteran, his wife, and son, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-05800770.translation: To the Great Mother Goddess Cybele. Pompeius Rusonianus, consul and member of the board of fifteen for performing sacred rites, performed a taurobolium.
EDCS-05800770.summary: This inscription records a taurobolium (bull sacrifice) performed by Pompeius Rusonianus, a high-ranking priest, in honor of the Magna Mater (Great Mother) Cybele.

EDCS-05800771.translation: Quintus Veranius Mystis made a vow to Silvanus.
EDCS-05800771.summary: A record of a vow made by Quintus Veranius Mystis to the god Silvanus.

EDCS-05800773.translation: Gabinae for Venus the True and Fortunate. Aulus Plutius Epaphroditus, a registrar and initiated official, a silk merchant, made the temple with an air statue in the form of Venus, also four air statues placed in the chambers, and air basins, and an air altar, and all the furnishings from the ground up with his own money. For its dedication, he distributed 5 denarii to each decurion, 3 denarii to each member of the board of six Augustals, and 1 denarius to each merchant doing business within the walls. He also endowed the public treasury of the Gabini with 10,000 sesterces, with the understanding that from the interest of this sum, the decurions and Augustales would publicly feast in their dining halls each year on the fourth day before the Kalends of October, the birthday of his daughter Plutia Vera. If they neglect to do this, then the sum of 10,000 sesterces shall belong to the municipality of Tusculum, and it shall be collected immediately. This was given by decree of the decurions. Dedicated on the Ides of May in the consulship of Lucius Venuleius Apronianus and Lucius Sergius Paullus.
EDCS-05800773.summary: This is a dedication of a temple to Venus by Aulus Plutius Epaphroditus, who funded its construction and provided an endowment for public feasts celebrating his daughter's birthday, with provisions for forfeiture to Tusculum if the conditions are not met.

EDCS-05800775.translation: In honor and memory of Domitia Augusta, daughter of Gnaeus Domitius Corbulo. Domitius Polycarpus and Europe, with a plot of land granted by decree of the board of decurions, built and decorated the temple with statues and other items with their own money, and they permanently entrusted its upkeep to the public treasury under the inscription below. Under Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius (consul for the third time) and Marcus Aelius Aurelius Caesar (consul), on the ninth day before the Kalends of May in Gabii, in the curia Aelia Augusta, the entire board of decurions, with Lucius Vipstanus Publicola Messalla, son of Lucius, and Lucius Setrius Priscus, son of Lucius, the four-man officials with quinquennial power, reported that Gnaeus Domitius Polycarpus, in his own name and that of his wife Domitia Europe, offered to the board of decurions and the Augustan sevirate 10,000 sesterces, having already constructed a temple in honor and memory of Domitia, daughter of Corbulo, and wishing to adorn this act of his piety and benefit our order, he is eager to serve everyone individually and collectively, seeking that the benefit from the income of this money always reach our eternal republic, asking that according to the example of the wills of Claudius Vitalis, with an intervening stipulation, the condition be decreed that from the income of this money, on the third day before the Ides of February, the birthday of Domitia, a distribution be made in public in equal portions in the presence of the decurions and seviri, with reclining, and furthermore, he would contribute 5,000 sesterces for the upkeep and decoration of the temple under the same condition. What it would please to do about it, they resolved as follows: It pleases everyone to accept the money and provide it in perpetuity so that the birthday and memory of Domitia, daughter of Corbulo, may be celebrated, and from the income of 10,000 sesterces, distributions be made and feasting occur in public. If at any time it ceases to be provided, or if the order rescinds its decree or changes the condition, then the entire sum accepted for this purpose shall be immediately repaid to the municipalities of Tusculum under the same condition. This decree was decided after three reports to be inscribed on a bronze tablet and displayed in public, where it can be read clearly from the ground.
EDCS-05800775.summary: This inscription details the dedication of a temple to Domitia Augusta by Domitius Polycarpus and Europe, who endowed it with 10,000 sesterces for the celebration of Domitia's birthday and temple upkeep, with provisions for forfeiture to Tusculum if conditions are not met, as decreed by the local council.

EDCS-05800789.translation: To Marcus Julius Zoticus, decurion, father of decurions and quinquennial sevir of the Augustales of the same order, their perpetual quinquennial dendrophorus and most worthy patron, a place was granted by decree of the decurions for his merits. Dedicated on the eighth day before the Kalends of July, when Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus was consul for the third time, and Publius Valerius Comazon was consul, with Abudius Priscus Cassidarius and Demetrius and Celerinus Statiensis Clementianus as curators.
EDCS-05800789.summary: This inscription is a decree by the decurions granting a place of honor to Marcus Julius Zoticus, acknowledging his status as a father of decurions, a leader among the Augustales and dendrophori, and a patron, likely dating to the reign of Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-05800792.translation: To Aulus Plutius Epaphroditus, accensus velatus, silk merchant, from his freedmen and patron, for his merits.
EDCS-05800792.summary: This is a dedication from freedmen and a patron to Aulus Plutius Epaphroditus, who held positions as an accensus velatus and a silk merchant.

EDCS-05800793.translation: Scyllus, freedman of Messallina, [Pha]edimus(?), set up a gift.
EDCS-05800793.summary: This is a dedication by Scyllus, freedman of Messallina, possibly with a co-dedicant, recording the placement of a gift.

EDCS-05800795.translation: Aurelius Alexander, head of Latin correspondence, Digitius made this.
EDCS-05800795.summary: This is a dedication by Digitius, identifying himself as Aurelius Alexander and holding the position of head of Latin correspondence.

EDCS-05800796.summary: This is a simple dedication by Lucius Clodius Longinus.

EDCS-05800797.summary: This is a simple dedication by Flavia Fortunata.

EDCS-05800798.summary: This is a simple dedication by Quintus Licinius Chrysippus.

EDCS-05800799.summary: This is a simple dedication by Titus Statilius Felicio.

EDCS-05800800.translation: Aulus Cloulius, son of Publius, of the Falernian tribe, lived 95 years. Cluia Clouli lived [ years.
EDCS-05800800.summary: This inscription records the lifespan of Aulus Cloulius and begins to record the lifespan of Cluia Clouli, likely a funerary monument.

EDCS-05800802.translation: To the Divine Shades. Tyche, slave of Publius Vocontius, lived 30 years. Hebreonus made this for his dearest tent-mate. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-05800802.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Hebreonus to his deceased partner Tyche, a slave of Publius Vocontius, including a common epitaph formula.

EDCS-05800811.translation: To Flavia Sabina, daughter of Titus, wife of Caesennius Paetus.
EDCS-05800811.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavia Sabina, wife of Caesennius Paetus.

EDCS-05800813.translation: Alcimus, slave of Nero Caesar Augustus, from the military clothing department, lived 30 years. Claudia Docile made this for herself and her partner.
EDCS-05800813.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Alcimus, a slave in charge of military clothing for Emperor Nero, and for Claudia Docile and her partner.

EDCS-05800814.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Abascantus, freedman of Marcus Antonius Pallans, Augustan official in charge of grain, made this from his own resources.
EDCS-05800814.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Abascantus, an Augustan official, for Marcus Antonius Pallans, to whom he owes his freedom.

EDCS-05800815.translation: To the Divine Spirits. To Lauri. Abascantus, a home-born slave of our Caesar, Augustan official in charge of the annona, and Carpime, his mother, [set this up] for their most beloved son.
EDCS-05800815.summary: This is a funerary dedication by parents Abascantus and Carpime for their son Lauri, both slaves belonging to the imperial household.

EDCS-05800816.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Phoebus Antonianus, freedman of Augustus, for himself and for his freedmen and freedwomen.
EDCS-05800816.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Tiberius Claudius Phoebus Antonianus, a freedman of Emperor Claudius, for himself and his own freedmen and freedwomen.

EDCS-05800817.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Marcus Statio Amerimnus. Statia Cleopatra set this up for her most dutiful and well-deserving patron and husband.
EDCS-05800817.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Statia Cleopatra for her patron and husband, Marcus Statio Amerimnus.

EDCS-05800818.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Caius Valerius Hermetis. Valeria Hermione [set this up] for her most beloved father.
EDCS-05800818.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Valeria Hermione for her father, Caius Valerius Hermetis.

EDCS-05800820.translation: Nicephor, slave of Flavius Sulpicianus, made this.
EDCS-05800820.summary: This is a funerary epitaph made by Nicephor, a slave of Flavius Sulpicianus.

EDCS-05800822.translation: Titus Flavius Epictetus, freedman of Augustus, secretary, official of military supplies, and lictor, rests here. His wife Flavia Tyche placed this inscription for her most excellent husband.
EDCS-05800822.summary: This funerary inscription honors Titus Flavius Epictetus, an Augustan freedman who held multiple administrative and ceremonial roles, and was placed by his wife.

EDCS-05800823.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For my most deserving husband, Virginio, who lived 40 years, 5 months, and 9 days. His wife Dafine made this for you, my deserving husband, for what you owed me to do for me, I know not who will do.
EDCS-05800823.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Virginio, erected by his wife Dafine, who expresses her grief and the unreciprocated affection she feels she deserves.

EDCS-05800826.translation: To Gaius Helvidius Priscus, son of Gaius, of the Arnian tribe.
EDCS-05800826.summary: This is a funerary or dedicatory inscription for Gaius Helvidius Priscus.

EDCS-05800827.translation: For Plautia Quinctilia, daughter of Aulus, [wife] of Publius Helvidius Priscus and [mother] of Publius Helvidius Priscus.
EDCS-05800827.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Plautia Quinctilia.

EDCS-05800831.translation: To Holy Fortune. Atinia Tyrannis, of the seminaria near the triumphal gate, and her daughter Terentia Faustina, gave this gift for her most beloved husband, Publius Terentius Fortunatus, to whom I have always given the greatest thanks. The place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-05800831.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated to Publius Terentius Fortunatus by his wife and daughter, with the gift of the site approved by the local council.

EDCS-05800833.translation: You who dwell near Tarpeian Jupiter, Fortune, always the guardian of my vows, accept the image that piety places before you, deserving one, to preserve my parent, whose name the memorable letter should not be silent about: he is called Caesius Titus Primus. He who dispenses the harvests and new fruits of bountiful Ceres into profit, for whom fame and trust are established, and who is accustomed to pass by riches with modesty, with care and diligence of labor, which shores provide the safest passage for tired sailors. Known in the sacred city, known also in those lands which the Umbrian and the Tuscan plowman are accustomed to till. Every year, according to his custom of vows, he adds hundreds of crowns, with increasing numbers, honoring likenesses of Fortune and altars of Apollo and the secret Jupiter, whose majesty in him promises a long life. Accept, posterity, what you may relate through your ages. Taurinus placed this gift, ordered by the piety of his parent, which neither the sentence of death nor the highest power of fate will be able to overcome, but it will always remain with the people's good repute. To Lucius Ceionius Commodus, Sextus Vetulenius Civica Pompeianus, consuls. The place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-05800833.summary: This is a complex funerary and dedicatory inscription, possibly for a temple or shrine, honoring Fortune, Apollo, and Jupiter, commissioned by Taurinus for his parent, and later associated with the consuls Lucius Ceionius Commodus and Sextus Vetulenius Civica Pompeianus.

EDCS-05800839.translation: Marcius Telesphorus fulfilled a vow to Fortune Primigenia.
EDCS-05800839.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Marcius Telesphorus fulfilling a vow to the goddess Fortune Primigenia.

EDCS-05800846.translation: Marcus Popilius Trophimus, son of Marcus, and his freedwoman Popilia Chreste, and Atilia [---], dedicated a statue of Liber Pater Pantheus with its accessories, and two cupids with their lamps, to Fortune Primigenia.
EDCS-05800846.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription for statues and decorative items to Fortune Primigenia, made by Marcus Popilius Trophimus and others.

EDCS-05800847.translation: Lucius Rufinus, curule aedile, fulfilled his vow to Fortune Primigenia.
EDCS-05800847.summary: This is a votive inscription by Lucius Rufinus, a curule aedile, fulfilling a vow to Fortune Primigenia.

EDCS-05800848.translation: Lucius Sariolenus Naevius Fastus, of consular rank, dedicated this statue of Minerva to Fortune Primigenia, along with an altar, just as he dedicated a statue of Antoninus Augustus [and?] Apollinis Isityches Spei in the triumvirate and in the pronaos of the temple.
EDCS-05800848.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription by Lucius Sariolenus Naevius Fastus, a former consul, dedicating a statue of Minerva and an altar to Fortune Primigenia, referencing previous dedications.

EDCS-05800852.translation: Marcus Ulpius Strato, Eutychia, Maximilla, and Maximus, being freedmen/freedwomen and recompensed by vows, gave this gift to Fortune Primigenia.
EDCS-05800852.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription where freedmen and freedwomen, including Marcus Ulpius Strato, give a gift to Fortune Primigenia.

EDCS-05800866.translation: Caius Appuleius Nymphidianus and his son Nymphidia gave this gift to Fortune Primigenia for Caius Appuleius Diocles, the foremost charioteer of the green faction, of Spanish origin.
EDCS-05800866.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription by Caius Appuleius Nymphidianus and his son, honoring Caius Appuleius Diocles, a charioteer, and dedicating a gift to Fortune Primigenia.

EDCS-05800870.translation: To the Dea Fortuna Primigenia. Valeria Saturnina to her father, Dolutius Marcianus, son of Gaius Valerius, of the Menenia tribe, a former VIvir Augustalis, decurion, who had held all the curial offices and performed all the public duties. A place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-05800870.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Dolutius Marcianus, honoring his public service and filial devotion, with a plot granted by the local council.

EDCS-05800883.translation: Caius Valerius Hermaiscus made this temple of the god Sarapis, the Faustinian school. Gaius Valerius Hermaiscus dedicated it on the Ides of December, when Barbarus and Regulus were consuls. [Greek text] Gaius Valerius Hermaiscus made this shrine of the great god Helios Sarapis and the companion gods.
EDCS-05800883.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a temple to Sarapis by Gaius Valerius Hermaiscus, noting the consuls and including a bilingual dedication in Latin and Greek.

EDCS-05800898.translation: [...] to Titus Aelius Laro, son of Titus, of the Lemonia tribe, a Roman knight, procurator of the emperor, supervisor of the public and private libraries.
EDCS-05800898.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely honoring Titus Aelius Laro for his service as a procurator in charge of libraries.

EDCS-05800900.translation: [...] Trypho, freedman of Marcus Cispius, master of the temple guardians of the temple of Castor and Pollux, master of the [municipality of Tusculum], master for the cult of Mars Praenestinus, beloved of his family. [...] Lucius Baebius [...], tribune of the plebs.
EDCS-05800900.summary: This is a fragmented inscription likely honoring freedmen and officials, possibly a funerary monument or a dedication related to various religious and civic roles.

EDCS-05800901.translation: To Barbarus Pompeianus, a most distinguished man, consular governor of Campania. The entire town council, supported and aided by his beneficence, and also because he took care to have the public buildings, which had fallen into ruin, restored and repaired with benevolent disposition, erected this statue to perpetuate his name and memory in the forum, with everyone rejoicing, in civil attire. This was overseen by Julius Laurentius, a most perfectus, curator of the town. Dedicated on the 5th day before the Nones of March, when Marcus Nummius Albinus and Marcus Laelius Maximus were consuls, under the curatorship of Gaius Valerius Dolutius Marcianus.
EDCS-05800901.summary: This is a public dedication of a statue to the former consular governor of Campania, Barbarus Pompeianus, in recognition of his generous acts, particularly the restoration of public buildings, with a detailed list of officials and the dedication date.

EDCS-05800904.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-05800904.translation: To Titus Flavius Germanus, son of Titus, curator of the most successful triumph of Germanicus Secundus, honored official, most splendid priest, minor pontiff, procurator of inheritance taxes, procurator of the patrimony, procurator of the Great Games, procurator of the Matutine Games, procurator of the city regions, assigned to the office of paving the roads in two parts of the city, procurator of inheritance taxes in Umbria, Etruria, Picenum, Campania, procurator for the grain supply of Lucania, Bruttium, Calabria, and Apulia, curator of the repairers of roofs and public works and sacred buildings, aedile, duovir, flamen of the deified Augustus, quinquennial duovir, patron of the colony. Cerdo, freedman, to his incomparable patron, with his sons Flavius Maximino and Germanus and Rufinus, honored with the public horse.
EDCS-05800904.summary: This is a comprehensive epitaph honoring Titus Flavius Germanus, detailing his numerous high-ranking imperial administrative and religious positions, and his status as patron of the colony, erected by his freedman and sons.

EDCS-05800905.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. To Gaius Gaudius Fortis, former senator, who performed senatorial duties, to his dearest father. [Son's name] son... erected.
EDCS-05800905.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Gaudius Fortis, likely a senator, set up by his son.

EDCS-05800906.translation: The Republic, by decree of the decurions, in honor of the munificence of Quintus Insteius Pupus, son of Titus, quaestor, tribune of the plebs, praetor, consul, consular legate of the province [...], and of his father and brother and son and wife, because he had provided at his own expense for a statue with hospital tablets to be transferred and placed either before the curia or in the porticoes of the forum, dedicated this school.
EDCS-05800906.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a school by the Republic, honoring Quintus Insteius Pupus and his family for their munificence in providing a statue and hospitality tablets for the forum.

EDCS-05800913.translation: Sextus Lutatius Laelianus Oppianicus Petronianus, son of Quintus, of the Pupinia tribe, praetor.
EDCS-05800913.summary: This is a brief inscription identifying Sextus Lutatius Laelianus Oppianicus Petronianus as a praetor.

EDCS-05800914.text_edition: \n Quinto Marcio Publi filio Quirina Victori patri Claudiae Tiberi filiae Taurillae matri Marciae Favonillae filiae Quintus Marcius Faustinianus praetor legatus provinciae Africae tribunus plebis quaestor provinciae Cypri \n
EDCS-05800914.translation: To Quintus Marcius Victor, son of Publius, of the Quirina tribe, father; to Claudiana Taurilla, daughter of Tiberius, mother; [and] to Marcia Favonilla, daughter. Quintus Marcius Faustinianus, praetor, legate of the province of Africa, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of the province of Cyprus.
EDCS-05800914.summary: This is a dedication set up by Quintus Marcius Faustinianus, a praetor and former provincial legate and quaestor, to his parents and sister.

EDCS-05800920.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-05800920.translation: To Lucius Statius Prosper Julianus, son of Lucius, of the Stellatina tribe, a man of consular rank, member of the board of sixteen for judging lawsuits, also a decurion of the second cavalry turma, patron of the colony, beloved by the inhabitants of the market district and worshippers of Jupiter Arcanus. The place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-05800920.summary: A funerary or honorary monument dedicated to Lucius Statius Prosper Julianus, a local official and patron, with a space granted by the town council.

EDCS-05800921.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Verecundus, freedman of the emperor and procurator, made this for his wife Tutia Marcia.
EDCS-05800921.summary: A tombstone for Tutia Marcia, erected by her husband Verecundus, a freedman and procurator.

EDCS-05800930.translation: To Publius Aelius Tiron, son of Publius, of the Palatina tribe. During his fourteenth year, Publius Blandus, his father, in his love for the city, remitted the entire sum and expense for the public benefit, because the emperor Caesar Commodus Antoninus Pius Felix Germanicus Sarmaticus Britannicus deigned to adorn him with his first military service as prefect of the equites of the Braucuni. The place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-05800930.summary: A dedication honoring Publius Aelius Tiron, a young boy who received a military honor from Emperor Commodus, with his father bearing the cost.

EDCS-05800931.translation: Lucius Aufidius Celer, son of Caius, of the Scaptia tribe. A cavalryman in the fourth praetorian cohort, under the centuria of Classicus. He lived 24 years, served 8 years.
EDCS-05800931.summary: A tombstone for Lucius Aufidius Celer, a cavalryman in the Praetorian Guard, detailing his age and service length.

EDCS-05800933.translation: To Domitius Priscus, son of Lucius, of the Fabia tribe. [---] of the 16th Legion 'Flavia Felix'.
EDCS-05800933.summary: A tombstone fragment for Domitius Priscus, a soldier of the 16th Legion.

EDCS-05800934.translation: To Firmius, soldier of the 3rd praetorian cohort, under the centuria of Claudius, from Augusta Taurinorum. Marcus Iulius Virilis, his heir, took care of this.
EDCS-05800934.summary: A tombstone for Firmius, a soldier of the 3rd Praetorian Cohort, erected by his heir.

EDCS-05800935.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Titus Flavius Paternus, soldier of the 10th praetorian cohort, under the centuria of Sabinus, from Iulia Emona. He served 7 years, lived 26 years. Titus Flavius Severus, soldier of the 26th 'Voluntaria' cohort, his brother, and Publius Aelius Candidus, an imperial cavalryman, his heirs, saw to it being made.
EDCS-05800935.summary: A tombstone for Titus Flavius Paternus, a soldier in the 10th Praetorian Cohort, and his brother Titus Flavius Severus, a soldier in the 26th Cohort, with heirs making arrangements.
