EDCS-05800936.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Lucius Geganus Victorinus, soldier of the 9th praetorian cohort. He lived 29 years. His devoted parents made this for their son.
EDCS-05800936.summary: A tombstone for Lucius Geganus Victorinus, a soldier in the 9th Praetorian Cohort, erected by his parents.

EDCS-05800937.translation: Sextus Iulius Rufus, son of Spurius, of the Pollia tribe. An evocatus of the divine Augustus, prefect of the First Cohort of Corsi and of the barbarian tribes in Sardinia.
EDCS-05800937.summary: An inscription honoring Sextus Iulius Rufus, an evocatus and prefect who served in Sardinia.

EDCS-05800940.translation: To Gaius Valerius Florinus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe. Proculus, his brother, erected this for his excellent brother, who was prefect of the 2nd Thracian Syrian Cohort and tribune of the 7th Claudian Pious Faithful Legion.
EDCS-05800940.summary: A dedication to Gaius Valerius Florinus, who held military commands as prefect and tribune, erected by his brother.

EDCS-05800943.translation: To Varus, son of Publius, of the Aniensis tribe, prefect of the artisans, prefect of the cohort of Germans, prefect of the cavalry, tribune of the soldiers of the Fifth Legion; to Varus, son of Publius, of the Aniensis tribe, quaestor, praetor, pontifex, quinquennial praetor. The Capitolini Hernici erected (this) for himself and for himself.
EDCS-05800943.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument dedicated to Varus, a Roman official with military and political experience, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05800947.translation: Accius Germanicus Caesar Vassius... Liguria, duovir. Manilei after... Aulus Minus, quinquennalis. Marius, aedile. X Truttidi... C. Caecilius... Petronius Rufus, duovirs. ...stacilius, aedile. ...orius, duovir. ...ius, aedile. ...cius. Marcus Atellius, quaestor. C. Raecius, duovir. C. Velius, aedile. Marcus Valerius, quaestor. Drusus Caesar, quinquennalis. Marcus Cominius Bassus, quinquennalis, by order of the Senate. Marcus Petronius Rufus, prefect. C. Dindius, Cnaeus Egnatius, aedile. C. Iuc[---] Rufus, C. Thorenas, quaestor. Cnaeus Pom[---]us, flamen, duovir, of the divine Augustus, curator... Marcus Raecius Cordus, quaestor. Lucius Petisius Potens, C. Salvius. C. Dindius, Titus Livius R. Lucius Aiachius Arrius, C. C. F.
EDCS-05800947.summary: This is a fragmentary list of local magistrates and officials, likely from an honorific monument or a record of public service in a Roman colony, dating probably to the early imperial period.

EDCS-05800948.translation: Sedatus, quaestor. ...lus, duovir. ...aedile. ...avius Musca, quaestor. ...Germanicus, son of..., duovir, quinquennalis. ...Sedatus, praetor. ...us, aedile. ...quaestor.
EDCS-05800948.summary: This is a fragmented inscription listing various local officials such as quaestors, duovirs, and aediles, likely part of a public building or monument in a Roman town.

EDCS-05800955.translation: To Publius Acilius Paulus, son of Publius, of the Menenia tribe, six-time Augustalis, quaestor of the colony, aedile, duovir, flamen of the divine Augustus, curator of the grain supply, curator of public works, curator of the treasury. The devotees of Jupiter from the Arkani society of the meat market region, (erected this) to their most worthy patron. The place was granted by decree of the decurions. Dedicated on the 5th day before the Ides of May, when Arrianus and Papus were consuls, under the care of Tiberius Claudius Vitalis, six-time Augustalis.
EDCS-05800955.summary: This is a dedication for a public benefactor, Publius Acilius Paulus, honoring his numerous civic and religious roles, including curatorship of public services and priesthoods, likely dating to the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05800956.translation: Copy of the decree: To the quaestors Cluvius Silanus and Quintus, son of Servius, Lucius Septimius Valerianus, son of Quintus, requesting that a place be granted for setting up a statue to the man of excellent memory, formerly the son of Anatellon, freedman of Augustus, pious and most loving to our republic. It was decided and in honor of Anatellon, freedman of Augustus, who always and everywhere acts for the welfare of our republic, in memory of Aelius Mabbidianus, the best..., that a place for setting up a statue be given to his son by his own choice.
EDCS-05800956.summary: This inscription records a senatorial decree granting permission to erect a statue in honor of Aelius Mabbidianus and his son, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05800957.translation: Marcus Albinius, son of Marcus, of the Menenia tribe, aedile, duovir, quinquennial duovir, Augustalis, master of the Augustales elect, to Valeria Callisto, his wife. Born in her 20th year.
EDCS-05800957.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Valeria Callisto, wife of Marcus Albinius, a prominent local official and Augustalis, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05800960.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-05800960.translation: To Marcus Aurelius, freedman of the Augusti, Agilius Septentrion, the pantomime performer of his time, first in hieronic performance, crowned alone in the city, unanimously by our Lords Emperors Severus and Antoninus Augusti, parasite of Apollo, archer of the synod, six-time Augustalis. The republic of the Praenestines erected this statue to him, because of his remarkable love for the citizens and his homeland, at the request of the people, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-05800960.summary: This inscription honors Marcus Aurelius Agilius Septentrion, a celebrated pantomime performer and local official, with a statue erected by the city of Praeneste, likely in the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05800961.translation: Gaius Aurunceius Cotta, son of Gaius, gave the baths as a free gift in perpetuity for the colonists, inhabitants, guests, visitors, and their slaves.
EDCS-05800961.summary: This inscription records the donation of a public bath by Gaius Aurunceius Cotta for the benefit of the town's residents and visitors, likely in the imperial period.

EDCS-05800962.translation: Gaius Aurunceius Cotta, son of Gaius, gave the baths as a free gift in perpetuity for the colonists, inhabitants, guests, visitors, and their slaves.
EDCS-05800962.summary: This inscription records the donation of a public bath by Gaius Aurunceius Cotta for the benefit of the town's residents and visitors, likely in the imperial period.

EDCS-05800968.translation: Quintus Flavius..., master..., Plaetoria..., Quintus Flavius Q[---]
EDCS-05800968.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning individuals named Quintus Flavius and Plaetoria, possibly related to a public office or family, with an indeterminate date.

EDCS-05800970.translation: To Lucius Herennius, son of Lucius, of the Menenia tribe. Cossutianus, admitted decurion of the Praeneste curia, lived for 17 years. [He lived] for 18 years.
EDCS-05800970.summary: A funerary inscription for a young man named Cossutianus, detailing his tribal affiliation and a brief mention of his life span and civic role.

EDCS-05800972.translation: Sextus Maesius Echio, freedman, made this for himself and for his son Sextus Maesius Celer, and for his wife Maesia Pinoe, and for his freedman fellow Sextus Maesius Potho, and for their descendants. Sextus Maesius Celsus, son of Sextus, of the Romilia tribe, was praefect of the engineers, centurion of the IV Macedonica legion, quaestor, aedile, duovir, and flamen of the deified Augustus, and a diviner of Fortuna Primigenia.
EDCS-05800972.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated by a freedman for his family and descendants, honoring his former master who held numerous prestigious civic and military offices.

EDCS-05800974.translation: To Aulus Munius, son of Aulus, of the Menenia tribe, Evaristus, a distinguished Roman equestrian, patron of the colony. For having admirably discharged all the offices, and upon the people's request for his outstanding public display of a two-day festival, it was decided by decree of the council of leaders to honor him with an equestrian statue. The site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-05800974.summary: A decree honoring Aulus Munius Evaristus, a Roman equestrian and patron of the colony, with an equestrian statue for his public munificence, particularly a two-day festival.

EDCS-05800976.translation: To Publius Oppius... [served as] a quinquennial Augustalis.
EDCS-05800976.summary: An inscription fragment mentioning Publius Oppius, who held the office of quinquennial Augustalis.

EDCS-05800979.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Banausus, freedman and heir, made this for himself, his freedmen and freedwomen, and their descendants. May your bones rest well. For Titus Popilius Epitynchanus, son of Marcus, freedman, quinquennial Augustalis.
EDCS-05800979.summary: A funerary inscription set up by a freedman and heir for himself, his family, and his freed slaves, honoring Titus Popilius Epitynchanus, a quinquennial Augustalis.

EDCS-05800981.translation: [...], Tertius Rufus, duovir? [...], Rufus [...]. [The work of] laying the road [...] they approved.
EDCS-05800981.summary: A fragmentary inscription likely recording the approval of road construction work by local magistrates, possibly duovirs.

EDCS-05800986.translation: Marcus Scurreius Fontinalis, priest of Fortuna Primigenia, chosen by senatorial decree, quinquennial Augustalis, perpetual curator of the sevirate, given by Emperor Hadrian Augustus. With Scurreius Vestalis, his son, he made this from his own money. The site was granted by decree of the decurions. He served as curator of the guild of carpenters and timber workers.
EDCS-05800986.summary: An inscription honoring Marcus Scurreius Fontinalis, who held multiple prestigious religious and civic offices, including being appointed perpetual curator of the sevirate by Emperor Hadrian, and funded this monument with his son.

EDCS-05800990.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. [...] Threptus, priest of the temple of Ops, made this for himself and his [...] and for his freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-05800990.summary: A funerary inscription set up by Threptus, a priest of the temple of Ops, for himself and his family, including freed slaves.

EDCS-05800992.translation: Publius Valerius Mahes, freedman of Publius, magistrate, quinquennial, of the guild of carpenters and timber workers for 13 lustra. Publius Valerius Alcimus, his most indulgent patron, made this for the spirits of the underworld.
EDCS-05800992.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the underworld, honoring Publius Valerius Mahes, a magistrate and quinquennial of the guild of carpenters and timber workers for 13 lustra, by his patron Publius Valerius Alcimus.

EDCS-05800998.translation: To Gnaeus Voesius Apro, son of Gnaeus, quaestor, aedile, duovir, flamen of the deified Augustus, sexvir Augustalis, curator of the grain supply for three continuous years, curator of the gladiatorial games. Because during the time of his offices and curatelles he contributed abundantly to the people's pleasures and benefits with the greatest zeal for munificence, and also publicly offered a gladiatorial training school and a dressing room, purchased from his own funds, it was decided by decree of the decurions that a statue be publicly erected in his honor for his merits, upon the people's request.
EDCS-05800998.summary: A decree honoring Gnaeus Voesius Apro for his extensive public benefactions, including funding gladiatorial games and establishing a gladiatorial school and spoliarium, for which a statue was erected by popular demand and senatorial decree.

EDCS-05801011.translation: From the first magistrate, Cara, daughter of...ius, from Liparus.
EDCS-05801011.summary: This inscription likely records the name of a magistrate and a person from Lipara, possibly dating to the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-05801012.translation: Under the care of Claudius Crispinus the younger.
EDCS-05801012.summary: This short inscription indicates that a matter was overseen by Claudius Crispinus the younger, its context is unknown.

EDCS-05801014.translation: To the departed spirits. Publius Aelius Curtius Pius, a well-deserving friend and physician, erected this for himself. Aulus Curtius Crispinus Arruntianus.
EDCS-05801014.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Publius Aelius Curtius Pius, a physician and friend, set up by Aulus Curtius Crispinus Arruntianus, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05801015.translation: Lucius Aurelius Saturninus, freedman of the emperor, a chamberlain, and his wife Maetilia Severa, made this tomb while alive for themselves and their sons, their freedmen and freedwomen, and their descendants, so that neither their name may be erased nor anyone else may acquire it for themselves through succession, donation, sale, or trust, nor may an outsider introduce a body...
EDCS-05801015.summary: This is a funerary inscription detailing ownership and restrictions for a tomb built by Lucius Aurelius Saturninus and Maetilia Severa for their family and descendants, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05801019.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription that records the name and age at death of an individual.

EDCS-05801022.translation: Quintus Tineius Sacerdos Clemens. Lucius Marius Valens made this.
EDCS-05801022.summary: This inscription records a dedication or commission by Lucius Marius Valens for Quintus Tineius Sacerdos Clemens, its purpose is unclear but likely commemorative.

EDCS-05801023.summary: This is a dedication or ownership inscription for Publius Acilius Attianus.

EDCS-05801025.summary: This is a dedication or commission inscription by Sextus Aufidius Secundus, likely for a monument or building.

EDCS-05801026.summary: This is a dedication or commission inscription by Lucius Coelius Nestor, likely for a monument or building.

EDCS-05801027.summary: This is a dedication or commission inscription by Fabius Paramitus, likely for a monument or building.

EDCS-05801028.translation: To Marcus Primigenius(?) Abascanthus, freedman of Marcus C. N.
EDCS-05801028.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely identifying a freedman and his patron.

EDCS-05801029.translation: Marcus Primigenius(?) Anteros made [this]. For Sosianus.
EDCS-05801029.summary: This is a dedication mentioning the maker and the recipient, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801030.summary: This is a simple dedication by Gaius Vettennius Verecundus, likely marking a tomb or monument.

EDCS-05801185.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a tombstone, identifying an individual and his father.

EDCS-05801294.translation: Marcus Abenna Princeps, freedman of Marcus, ordered this to be made in his will for himself and for his father Marcus Abenna Sabinaeus, and for his mother Abennia Hilara, freedwoman of Marcus, and for Marcus Abenna Faustus, freedman of Marcus, and for Marcus Abenna Lathmus, freedman of Marcus. Caesia Attica, daughter of Sextus, lived for 14 years.
EDCS-05801294.summary: This is a tomb inscription detailing a will to establish a burial place for multiple family members and freedmen, including a deceased child.

EDCS-05801296.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aconia Numeriana, daughter of Tuscus, lived 4 months. [Located in] Caere.
EDCS-05801296.summary: This is a tomb inscription for a young child, Aconia Numeriana, found in Caere.

EDCS-05801297.translation: Aelia Canusina, freedwoman of Caius. 9 feet in front, 9 feet in the field, by a wall along the road.
EDCS-05801297.summary: This inscription appears to define property boundaries, possibly for a tomb or plot of land, belonging to Aelia Canusina.

EDCS-05801300.translation: Anicia Arbuscula, daughter of Quintus, made [this] for her son Quintus Anicius Fortis, who lived 19 years. 20 feet in front, 20 feet in the field.
EDCS-05801300.summary: This is a tomb inscription by a mother for her deceased son, likely specifying tomb dimensions or plot size, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801304.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Aurelius Halys and Aurelia Seleucia [made this] for their sweetest and most devoted son Lucius Aurelius Alpinus, and for Lucius Aurelius Halyi, and for Aurelia Theodora, daughter of Lucius Aurelius Halyi, for their sweetest and most devoted father, and for themselves, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants, Aurelia Theodora made [this].
EDCS-05801304.summary: This is a tomb inscription by a family, dedicating a monument to deceased relatives and themselves, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801305.translation: Sentia Ae[---] for her sweetest Aurelius Con[---] without the consent of [her] living [relatives?], [she] made [this] for herself and hers, [in memory] in [---] in the straight [---] of the city of Rome where [---]
EDCS-05801305.summary: This is a very fragmentary funerary inscription, possibly indicating a woman establishing a memorial for herself and another individual, possibly against familial wishes, within Rome.

EDCS-05801306.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Aurelius Vitalio made this memorial with a solarium and a small street from the ground for himself and Aelia Sofiadis, his wife, and his sons, and Aurelius Mercurius, his brother, with his sons, and Aurelius Berna with his family, and Ulpius Secundinus, my kinsman, with his sons, my brothers, and paternal freedmen and freedwomen, and also for mine and for the descendants of all of them, and for those to whom I have granted the right of passage during my lifetime, I, Syncratius, ask all of you, fellow members, to rest peacefully without dispute. Of the Syncratii.
EDCS-05801306.summary: This is a funerary碑 (sepulchral monument) inscription set up by Aurelius Vitalio for himself, his family, and many other relatives and friends, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801307.translation: To Aurelia Julia, a good soul. Blastus made this for his most holy wife.
EDCS-05801307.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Aurelia Julia by her husband Blastus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801309.translation: Titus Baebius, son of Titus, lived for 21 years.
EDCS-05801309.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the name and age of Titus Baebius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801310.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, likely a tombstone, identifying Titus Baebius Tarula, possibly dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801311.translation: Bones brought in. Of Beatus, freedman of Caius Billienus, from Lunula.
EDCS-05801311.summary: This inscription likely marks a secondary burial or ossuary, identifying the deceased as Beatus, freedman of Caius Billienus, possibly from Lunula, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801312.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Bussenia, daughter of Publius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801316.translation: The black day dragged away Clodius Novianus, who lived for two times ten years, from the light of life for his pious parents, and submerged him in a bitter death. On the 7th day before the Ides of August. Bitalis, his sister, lived for 30 days. Claudius Felicisimus made this for his deserving sons, at the request of memory.
EDCS-05801316.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Clodius Novianus and his sister Bitalis, erected by their father Claudius Felicisimus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801317.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Titus Flavius Asclepius made this for his deserving wife, Claudia Verecunda.
EDCS-05801317.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Titus Flavius Asclepius for his wife Claudia Verecunda, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801320.translation: To the gods of the underworld. For Diadumenus, deserving husband, who lived for 35 years.
EDCS-05801320.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Diadumenus, recording his life span and likely set up by his spouse, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801321.summary: This is a fragmented inscription identifying at least one person, possibly named Dulcitia, and a freedman of Lucius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801323.translation: Flavia Phoebe, daughter of Titus, made this monument while alive for herself, her freedmen and freedwomen, and their descendants. She also dedicated a brick-enclosed building with two and a three-quarter jugera of land for the protection of this monument, as is also indicated by the boundary markers placed.
EDCS-05801323.summary: This is a funerary monument and land dedication by Flavia Phoebe for herself and her freedpeople, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801324.translation: [Flavia] Phoebe, daughter of Titus, for herself [and her freedmen and freedwomen] and for two jugera of land belonging to it, access and entry, as is written on the boundary stones.
EDCS-05801324.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription referencing a funerary monument and land set aside by Flavia Phoebe for herself and her freedpeople, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801325.translation: Land for the protection of a building, left by Flavia, daughter of Titus, Phoebe, as is set out in the inscriptions of the monument and shops. It contains approximately 11 jugera and 600 feet of cultivated land, and also approximately 5 and a half jugera of woods and 500 feet.
EDCS-05801325.summary: This inscription details land reserved by Flavia Phoebe for the upkeep of a building and shops, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801327.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Publius Fufius Dexter for Lollia Iucunda, his well-deserving and incomparable wife, who lived with me for six years, seven months, fifteen days, and three hours, and who in every way advised me well until the day of her death.
EDCS-05801327.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Publius Fufius Dexter for his deceased wife Lollia Iucunda, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801328.translation: Aulus Gabinius Felix, freedman of Aulus.
EDCS-05801328.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph for Aulus Gabinius Felix, a freedman, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801330.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Sextus Geganius Christos, from Gegania Vitalis, his most devoted brother.
EDCS-05801330.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sextus Geganius Christos, erected by his brother, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801331.translation: [Caius Messienus?] Hilarus, freedman of Caius; [Messiena] Chia, freedwoman of Caius; [Messiena] Tryphaena, freedwoman of the woman.
EDCS-05801331.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription naming three individuals, likely freedpeople, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801337.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Lucius Manusius Eutychetes, who lived for 3 years, 10 months, 16 days. His mother Primitiva and his father Arius also made this.
EDCS-05801337.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Manusius Eutychetes, erected by his parents, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801341.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Caius Messenus Maximus, son of Caius, from the Menenia tribe, who lived 7 years, 10 months, 17 days. Caesia Primitiva and Caius Messenus Eutyches for their dearest son and for themselves, their freedmen and freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-05801341.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caius Messenus Maximus, erected by his parents who also included themselves and their descendants, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801344.translation: Of Marcus Mufeius Palicanus, son of Marcus, from the Aniensis tribe. In the front 20 feet, in the land 20 feet. // Of Marcus Mufeius Palicanus, son of Marcus, from the Aniensis tribe. In the land 20 feet, in the front 20 feet. // Of Marcus Mufeius Palicanus, son of Marcus, from the Aniensis tribe. In the land 20 feet, in the front 20 feet.
EDCS-05801344.summary: This inscription specifies the dimensions of a plot of land belonging to Marcus Mufeius Palicanus, likely for funerary purposes, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801345.translation: Sextus Naevius Philemo, freedman of Sextus, and Naevia Arbuscula, freedwoman of Sextus. 15 feet in front, 19 feet in the field.
EDCS-05801345.summary: This is a funerary boundary marker set up by Sextus Naevius Philemo and Naevia Arbuscula for themselves, with dimensions for their plot.

EDCS-05801346.translation: To Ocania Nebridis, who lived 22 years.
EDCS-05801346.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Ocania Nebridis, who died at the age of 22.

EDCS-05801350.translation: To Petronius Stephano, Petronius Hilerio, Unneia, vernae.
EDCS-05801350.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or listing related to several individuals, including slaves (vernae), possibly in a funerary context.

EDCS-05801352.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Annea Vitalis to her well-deserving husband, Lucius Pompeius Hilarus, [a seviri Augustales?].
EDCS-05801352.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Annea Vitalis to her deserving husband, Lucius Pompeius Hilarus, who held a priesthood.

EDCS-05801353.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Pompeia Secunda, who lived [...] years.
EDCS-05801353.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription for Pompeia Secunda, indicating her lifespan.

EDCS-05801355.translation: Of Lucius Popillius, son of Sextus, of the tribe M…
EDCS-05801355.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, likely a personal name with tribal affiliation, possibly from a tomb or monument.

EDCS-05801357.summary: This is a heavily damaged inscription, likely a list of names, possibly funerary or dedicatory.

EDCS-05801362.translation: His heirs erected for Quintus Seveius, son of Spurius, of the tribus Collina(?), Egloge [her freedwoman?], [for her?] ... her heirs [for her?] out of his testament, well-deserving.
EDCS-05801362.summary: This funerary inscription records that heirs erected a monument for Quintus Seveius and Egloge, out of a testament.

EDCS-05801364.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld and to the memory of his most excellent and most dutiful wife, Stertiniae Artemidis. Servius Cornelius Eutychus [erected this].
EDCS-05801364.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Servius Cornelius Eutychus to his wife, Stertiniae Artemidis, praising her virtues.

EDCS-05801365.translation: Caius Sulpicius Thyillas, freedman of Caius, for himself and his family. 12 feet in front, 20 feet in the field. [...] his ancestral property.
EDCS-05801365.summary: This is a funerary boundary marker set up by Caius Sulpicius Thyillas for himself and his family, with dimensions for their plot.

EDCS-05801366.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Terentia Genesis made this for her freedman, Caius Terentius Anencletus.
EDCS-05801366.summary: This is a funerary dedication for the freedman Caius Terentius Anencletus, set up by Terentia Genesis.

EDCS-05801367.translation: Lucius Trebonius Axsius, son of Marcus, and his freedwoman Atellia Hedone, daughter of Lucius. In the field, 16 feet.
EDCS-05801367.summary: This inscription records a land division or boundary, mentioning Lucius Trebonius Axsius and his freedwoman Atellia Hedone, with a measurement of 16 feet.

EDCS-05801369.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aulus Valerius Fortunatus for himself, and for his sister Valeria, and for Neriana Ingenua, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-05801369.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Aulus Valerius Fortunatus for himself and his family members, including freedpersons and their descendants.

EDCS-05801370.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lucius Valerius Stasianus, son of Lucius, of the Palatina tribe. He lived 30 years and 7 months. His mother Flavia Stasime, daughter of Titus, made this.
EDCS-05801370.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Valerius Stasianus, who lived 30 years and 7 months, set up by his mother Flavia Stasime.

EDCS-05801374.translation: For Lucius Vibrius Vementus, son of Lucius, of the Aniensis tribe. For Faustus, freedman of Aulus Sicinius, and his wife Techne, freedwoman of Aulus.
EDCS-05801374.summary: This funerary inscription records Lucius Vibrius Vementus and Faustus, a freedman of Aulus Sicinius, and Faustus's wife Techne.

EDCS-05801375.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For his most dutiful mother Acilia Eutychia and his sister Acilia Victoria, Marcus Ulpius Hesychus, freedman of Augustus, [sets this up].
EDCS-05801375.summary: This funerary dedication by Marcus Ulpius Hesychus, an imperial freedman, is for his mother Acilia Eutychia and his sister Acilia Victoria.

EDCS-05801376.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Ulpia Smyrna. Lucius Camidienus Plocamus [erected this] for his well-deserving wife, with whom he lived for 31 years and 15 days.
EDCS-05801376.summary: This funerary epitaph is for Ulpia Smyrna, set up by her husband Lucius Camidienus Plocamus, commemorating their 31 years and 15 days of marriage.

EDCS-05801378.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Volusia Comices, his excellent wife. Successus, servant of our emperor, Aegestianus [erected this].
EDCS-05801378.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Volusia Comices, set up by her husband Successus Aegestianus, who was a servant of the emperor.

EDCS-05801379.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For his son Urbicus, who lived 6 years, 9 months, [and some days].
EDCS-05801379.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young boy named Urbicus, who died at the age of 6 years and 9 months.

EDCS-05801387.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or funerary mention of Lucius? Arbianus Lentulus.

EDCS-05801397.translation: will be named Ianius; and the atrium of the church of Saint [---], hall of modesty, we have struck the boundaries of the entrance with a sorrowful tomb; tired, we parents [---] the holy clergy and Bishop Iucundus [---] after the rites were completed; the bright, gentle light shines on the innocent and what is innermost, safe [---] is placed to rest, a peaceful soul, freed from care; but we beg you, Saint Agapitus, to receive him, the boy Placidianus, I have worthy spoken of him in verse.
EDCS-05801397.summary: This inscription is a funerary poem for a boy named Placidianus, likely dating to the 4th or 5th century AD, commemorating his burial and requesting divine intercession for him.

EDCS-05801401.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-05801401.translation: Under the third consulship of Caesar, in this place were laid to rest Flavius Aurelius, who died, having lived 52 years and 8 months.
EDCS-05801401.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Flavius Aurelius, noting his age and the year of the consuls, likely dating to the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-05801406.translation: Beneriosus, who lived 3 years, 9 months, and 5 days, in peace.
EDCS-05801406.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph for a child named Beneriosus, indicating the duration of their life and their burial in pace, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-05801407.summary: This is a brief funerary inscription for Dulcitia, indicating her burial in pace, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-05801408.translation: Here lie Herculius and Gaudentius, in peace.
EDCS-05801408.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial of two individuals, Herculius and Gaudentius, in pace, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-05801412.translation: Paulla, who lived five years and was buried on the 5th day before the Kalends of October.
EDCS-05801412.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Paulla, noting her age and the date of her burial, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-05801414.translation: Titite Victorinus made this for his well-deserving virgin wife, buried on the Ides of April.
EDCS-05801414.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a wife named Victorinus, set up by her husband Titite, indicating her status as a virgin and the date of her burial, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801419.translation: To the departed spirits, Lucius Domitius Lupercus, a veteran of Augustus, made this for his sweetest, dearest, most holy, and best wife Roscia Ianuarie, well-deserving, with whom he lived 17 years, and for himself and his family.
EDCS-05801419.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Lucius Domitius Lupercus for his wife Roscia Ianuarie, and also for himself and his family, detailing their relationship and his military status, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801420.translation: To the departed spirits, Flavia Rhodo[pe] made this for her well-deserving husband Publius Aelius Cleruchus, freedman of Augustus.
EDCS-05801420.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Flavia Rhodo[pe] for her husband Publius Aelius Cleruchus, who was a freedman of the emperor, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801422.translation: Fabia Etei, freedwoman of Euche, and Allage, freedwoman, lived 19 years; as she dies, she asks that their bones be in the same tomb.
EDCS-05801422.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of two freedwomen, Fabia Etei and Allage, who lived together and desired to be buried in the same grave, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05801423.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Terentia Genesis and her freedman Anencletus made this for the well-deserving Gaius Terentius Proculus.
EDCS-05801423.summary: A funerary dedication made by a freedman and his patron's wife for a deceased individual.

EDCS-05801424.translation: Iulia Athenais, as magistrate, made this for the Good Goddess Sevina: she made the pavement and seats and workshop, extended the roof, and replaced tiles that were damaged, at her own expense. She also made a bronze altar facing the seats, 190 feet around, enclosed in iron, on the Kalends of June, when Gaius Calpurnius Piso and Marcus Vettius Bolanus were consuls.
EDCS-05801424.summary: A dedication by a female magistrate, Iulia Athenais, for the goddess Bona Dea, detailing extensive building works and a bronze altar, dated by the consulship of Piso and Bolanus (likely 30 BC).

EDCS-05801425.translation: Marcus Munatuleius Marcellus, son of Marcus, of the Aniensis tribe, military tribune of the people. // Marcus Montanus, son of Gaius, of the Aniensis tribe, his father.
EDCS-05801425.summary: An inscription commemorating Marcus Munatuleius Marcellus, a military tribune, and identifying his father, Marcus Munatuleius Montanus.

EDCS-05801426.translation: Of Tiberius Claudius Livianus, praetorian prefect. 19.
EDCS-05801426.summary: A brief inscription likely recording a number, possibly related to an official or military unit associated with Tiberius Claudius Livianus, a praetorian prefect.

EDCS-05801427.translation: For Aurelia Anatole, most holy and most chaste woman, with whom I lived for 32 years, 10 months, and 1 day. Also for myself, Phosphorus, freedman of Augustus.
EDCS-05801427.summary: A funerary dedication by Phosphorus, freedman of Augustus, for his wife Aurelia Anatole, commemorating their long life together.

EDCS-05801428.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Sestidia Rufina, most devoted wife, who lived beyond the death of her most sweet son Vibius Apronianus for 4 months and 9 days. To his most cherished wife and son, Vibius Cemellinus.
EDCS-05801428.summary: A funerary dedication by Vibius Cemellinus to his devoted wife Sestidia Rufina and his son Vibius Apronianus, noting a period of continued life for the wife after the son's death.

EDCS-05801435.translation: For Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the divine Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 35th time, consul for the 5th time. Marcus Castricius Mutro, son of Marcus, of the Menenia tribe, quinquennalis, [dedicated this] at his own expense.
EDCS-05801435.summary: A dedication to Emperor Tiberius, commemorating his titles and powers, by Marcus Castricius Mutro, a quinquennalis, likely for a public building or monument.

EDCS-05801437.translation: By decree of the decurions. For Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus, son of the divine Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the divine Commodus, grandson of the divine Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus, [great-great-great-grandson] of the divine Nerva, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, acclaimed imperator for the 9th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of his country.
EDCS-05801437.summary: An official dedication honoring Emperor Septimius Severus with his full titulature, including his imperial lineage and honors, commissioned by the local senate (decurions).

EDCS-05801444.translation: A vow to the holy Silvanus, seen in a vision, for freedom. Sextus Attius Dionysius, having seen the sign with its base, placed it as a gift.
EDCS-05801444.summary: A votive offering to the god Silvanus by Sextus Attius Dionysius, made in fulfillment of a vow for granted freedom.

EDCS-05801445.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Marcus Aurelius Antiochianus, freedman of the emperor, magistratus ab admissione. His sons Antiochianus and Antiochis and his heirs [made this] for their most excellent father.
EDCS-05801445.summary: A funerary dedication by the sons and heirs for Marcus Aurelius Antiochianus, an imperial freedman and official, honoring him as their excellent father.

EDCS-05801446.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Asclepiades [erected this monument] to his wife Valeria Sabina, well-deserving, an innocent soul who lived with me for 25 years, 4 months, and 10 days. Monument erected by Fucus(?). [---] Menius Malignus. [---] Bebius. Erected the lower monument. [---] Elias gave it to her.
EDCS-05801446.summary: A tombstone for Valeria Sabina, set up by her husband Asclepiades, recording her lifespan and marking the completion of the monument and the provision of an underground burial chamber.

EDCS-05801449.translation: Lucius Maenius, son of Quintus, of the Aniensis tribe, buried.
EDCS-05801449.summary: A simple tombstone for Lucius Maenius, identifying his father and tribal affiliation.

EDCS-05801450.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Ulpia Cales lived 12 years.
EDCS-05801450.summary: A tombstone for Ulpia Cales, recording her brief lifespan.

EDCS-05801461.translation: Marcus Helvius Rufus, son of Marcus, of the Camilia tribe, first-rank centurion, dedicated the bathhouse to the townspeople and residents.
EDCS-05801461.summary: An honorary inscription recording the dedication of a bathhouse by Marcus Helvius Rufus, a distinguished military officer, to the citizens of his municipality.

EDCS-05801463.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Tiberius Claudius Salvius, freedman of Emperor Augustus, made this for his well-deserving adopted son, Tiberius Claudius Eventus, who lived 16 years.
EDCS-05801463.summary: A tombstone erected by a freedman for his adopted son, who died at a young age.

EDCS-05801474.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis censor aedem Victoriae vetustate dilapsam sua impensa restituit\n
EDCS-05801474.translation: The Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, censor, restored the temple of Victoria, which had fallen into ruin from age, at his own expense.
EDCS-05801474.summary: An imperial rescript by Vespasian, documenting the restoration of the temple of Victoria at imperial expense.

EDCS-05801475.translation: To Flora. Tiberius Plautius Drosus, magistrate for the second time, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-05801475.summary: A dedication to the goddess Flora, recording the fulfillment of a vow by Tiberius Plautius Drosus in his magisterial role.

EDCS-05801519.translation: To the most holy Good Goddess Caelestis. Lucius Paquedius Festus, contractor for the Emperor's and public works, rebuilt the temple which had been destroyed, because with its aid he completed the aqueduct of Claudia Augusta under Mount Aeflanus, in the consulship of Emperor Domitian Caesar Augustus Germanicus for the 14th time, on the 5th day before the Nones of July.
EDCS-05801519.summary: A dedicatory inscription by Lucius Paquedius Festus, a public works contractor, recording the rebuilding of a temple and the completion of the Claudia aqueduct under Emperor Domitian.

EDCS-05801527.translation: To Felicitas. Titus Cauponius, son of Titus, and Gaius Aufestius, son of Gaius, as aediles.
EDCS-05801527.summary: An inscription by two aediles, Titus Cauponius and Gaius Aufestius, likely related to a public works project or dedication.

EDCS-05801528.translation: Gaius Julius Anthus, freedman of the freedman of the divine Augustus, restored the estate of Fortuna the Helper, for the well-being of his father Polybius Liberalis.
EDCS-05801528.summary: An inscription recording the restoration of a rural estate by a freedman for the benefit of his father's well-being.

EDCS-05801529.translation: Sacred to Fortune Praetoria. Lucius Mucius Nicephor, the Herculanean Augustal magistrate, and Gnaeus Coponius Epagatus, the first curator, gave gifts from their own funds to the cult members.
EDCS-05801529.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Fortune Praetoria by two officials, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05801533.translation: Gaius Julius Rufus, son of Gaius, of the Palatine tribe, a twice-tribune of the soldiers, a sanctuary curator, and a quaestorian viator from the treasury of Saturn, fulfilled his vow to Victory Hercules.
EDCS-05801533.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Gaius Julius Rufus, an official with military and administrative roles, to Hercules Victor, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05801534.translation: Publius Lucilius Successor, son of Publius, of the Oufentine tribe, from Milan, willingly and deservedly fulfilled the vow he had made to Invincible Hercules, being a centurion, primus pilus.
EDCS-05801534.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Publius Lucilius Successor, a primus pilus centurion, to Invincible Hercules, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05801535.translation: To Victory Hercules. Pomponia Nunnuleia, daughter of Lucius, dedicated this to Gaius Nunnuleius Nudo, son of Gaius, legatus pro praetore. His wife, Pomponia Nunnuleia, daughter of Lucius, erected it.
EDCS-05801535.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Victory Hercules by Pomponia Nunnuleia, likely in memory of her husband Gaius Nunnuleius Nudo, a legatus pro praetore, probably from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05801536.translation: Publius Rusticelius Saltator, master of Victory Hercules.
EDCS-05801536.summary: This inscription records Publius Rusticelius Saltator as a master of the cult of Victory Hercules, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05801539.translation: Titus Allius Medicus, master to Victory Hercules.
EDCS-05801539.summary: This inscription records Titus Allius Medicus as a master of the cult of Victory Hercules, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05801542.translation: Dedicated on the Ides of January, when Julian II and Cristinus were consuls. Marcus Aurelius Zoticus, freedman of Emperor Marcus Aurelius, nomenclator in charge of the census records, gave a gift to Victory Hercules and Certencius, and to the Lares of the Divine House and Fortune Augusta.
EDCS-05801542.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Hercules Victor and others by Zoticus, a freedman and official, under the consulship of Julian II and Cristinus (likely 197 CE).

EDCS-05801545.translation: To Juno Argia. Gaius Blandus, proconsul.
EDCS-05801545.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Juno Argia by Gaius Blandus, a proconsul, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05801550.translation: Publius Flavius Decimus, son of Spurius, of the Camilia tribe, and Publius Flavius Palestricus, Herculanean Augustal, and Marcus Trebienus Tiburtinus, Herculanean Augustal, curators, gave gifts to the cult members of the Divine House and to the Lares of Fortune Augusta.
EDCS-05801550.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Lares of Fortune Augusta and the cult members of the Divine House by two Herculanean Augustal officials, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05801552.translation: Domitia Tertulla made this to the Great Mother of the Gods at her own expense.
EDCS-05801552.summary: This inscription records a private dedication to the Magna Mater by Domitia Tertulla, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05801554.translation: To Good Mind and Safety. Quintus Caecilius Philadelphus, freedman of Quintus, and Publius Aquilius Dacus, freedman of Publius, as quinquennial magistrates, had this made from contributed funds and dedicated the statue themselves.
EDCS-05801554.summary: This inscription records a dedication of a statue and its construction by two quinquennial magistrates in honor of the abstract deities Mind and Safety.

EDCS-05801555.translation: To Priapus, genitor of the potent, powerful, unconquered divinity. Julius Agathemerus, freedman of Augustus, from the care of friends, warned by a dream. Come together, all you who are here; come together, all you girls who worship sacred groves; come together, all you girls who worship sacred waters, and, as you are, with your voice gently say: Hail, holy father Priapus, lord of things. Kiss his phallus and crown him with fragrant garlands, and say again all of you: Hail, holy father Priapus, for he, driving away wicked, cruel men, allows you to go through the woods and through the dark, silent, bloodless places; he keeps away from the springs also the wicked who cross the sacred waters with impious foot and make them turbulent, and who wash their hands and do not first invoke you girls with prayer. O Priapus, favorable, nurturing one, say all: Hail, holy father Priapus, hail. Hail, holy father Priapus, lord of things. Hail to me, grant me blooming youth, grant me that I may please boys and girls with my phallus and with frequent, bold games and jokes, dispelling harmful cares from my mind, nor shall I fear harsh old age, nor shall I be tormented by the dread of miserable death, by which [Avernus] will drag me to its envious home, where the king of Manes holds sway, whence fate denies anyone to return. Hail, holy father Priapus, hail. O powerful, friendly Priapus, hail, whether you wish to be called progenitor and creator of the world, or Nature herself, and Pan, hail, for by your vigor what fills the earth and the aether and the sea is conceived. Therefore hail, powerful, friendly Priapus, hail. Jupiter himself, with your consent, willingly lays aside his savage thunderbolts and his bright abodes, being eager, Venus and Cupido, ardent, and the Graces and the Twin Sisters, and Lyaeus, giver of joys, honor you. For without you, neither honorable Venus nor pleasing Graces are enjoyed, nor Bacchus. O powerful, friendly Priapus, hail. Chaste virgins call you with prayer, that you may loosen the girdle long tied. And the newly married woman calls you, that she may always have a stiff and potent member for her husband. Hail, holy father Priapus, hail.
EDCS-05801555.summary: This extensive inscription is a hymn to the fertility god Priapus, praising his generative power and invoking his aid for sexual potency and protection from death, likely from a private sanctuary or cultic context.

EDCS-05801559.translation: Iulia Gratilla, daughter of Lucius, to Venus the Compliant, willingly and deservedly from her own funds.
EDCS-05801559.summary: This is a dedication by Iulia Gratilla to the goddess Venus, likely for divine favor received.

EDCS-05801566.text_edition: \n Divae Drusillae sacrum Caius Rubellius Cai filius Blandus quaestor divi Augusti tribunus plebis praetor consul procons(ul) pontifex\n
EDCS-05801566.translation: Sacred to the Divine Drusilla. Caius Rubellius Blandus, son of Caius, quaestor of the divine Augustus, tribune of the plebs, praetor, consul, proconsul, pontifex.
EDCS-05801566.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the deified Drusilla by Caius Rubellius Blandus, listing his extensive career as a high-ranking imperial official.

EDCS-05801571.translation: To Faustina Augusta, wife of Emperor Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, chief priest, in his second year of tribunician power, twice consul, father of the country. The Senate and people of Tibur.
EDCS-05801571.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Empress Faustina by the people of Tibur, likely commemorating her visit or some public works associated with her.

EDCS-05801577.translation: To Publius Aelius Coeranus, consul, proconsul of the province of Macedonia, legate of the Augustan Legion VIII, juridical official for Flaminia and Umbria, praetor, tribune of the plebs, candidate for quaestor, quaestor, official with jurisdiction over four towns, brother of the Arval brethren, curator of the communities of Antium and Anagnia, patron and flamen Dialis of Tibur. The decurions of Tibur.
EDCS-05801577.summary: This inscription honors Publius Aelius Coeranus with multiple titles, including provincial governorship and priestly offices, by the town council of Tibur, highlighting his service and status.

EDCS-05801582.translation: Lucius Calpurnius Piso, son of Lucius.
EDCS-05801582.summary: This is a simple funerary or dedicatory inscription identifying an individual, Lucius Calpurnius Piso.

EDCS-05801587.translation: To Senecio, Memmius Galeria Afro, consul, proconsul of Sicily, legate with the rank of proconsul of the province of Aquitania. Lucius Memmius Tuscillus Senecio, to his most excellent father.
EDCS-05801587.summary: This is a dedication by Lucius Memmius Tuscillus Senecio to his father, also named Senecio, who held significant consular and legatine positions.

EDCS-05801589.text_edition: \n Lucio Minicio Luci filio Galeria Natali Quadronio Vero consuli proconsuli provinciae Africae auguri legato Augusti pro praetore provinciae Moesiae inferioris curatori operum publicorum et aedium sacrarum curatori viae Flaminiae praefecto alimentorum legato Augusti legionis VI victricis in Britannia praetori tribuno plebis candidato quaestori candidato divi Hadriani et eodem tempore legato provinciae Africae dioeceseos Carthaginiensis proconsulis patris sui tribuno miltum legionis I adiutricis piae fidelis item legionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis item legionis XIIII geminae Martiae victricis IIIviro monetali aere argento auro flando feriundo patrono municipii curatori fani Herculis Victoris decuriones Tiburtini ex aere collato quinquennali maximi exempli Curante Marco Tullio Blaeso\n
EDCS-05801589.translation: To Lucius Minicius Natalis Quadronius Verus, son of Lucius, of the Galeria tribe, consul, proconsul of the province of Africa, augur, legate of Augustus with the rank of proconsul of the province of Lower Moesia, curator of public works and sacred buildings, curator of the Flaminian Way, prefect of the grain supply, legate of Augustus of the VI Victrix legion in Britain, praetor, candidate for tribune of the plebs, candidate for quaestor, to the divine Hadrian, and at the same time legate of the province of Africa of the Diocese of Carthage, as proconsul, his father, military tribune of the I Adiutrix Pia Fidelis legion, likewise of the XI Claudia Pia Fidelis legion, likewise of the XIIII Gemina Martia Victrix legion, moneyer responsible for striking bronze, silver, and gold, patron of the municipality, curator of the temple of Hercules Victor. The decurions of Tibur, from contributed funds of the quinquennial [magistrates], of the greatest example. Under the care of Marcus Tullius Blaesus.
EDCS-05801589.summary: This lengthy inscription honors Lucius Minicius Natalis Quadronius Verus, detailing his extensive career in military, administrative, and religious roles under the emperors Hadrian and Antoninus Pius, by the decurions of Tibur.

EDCS-05801596.translation: Marcus Plautius Silvanus, son of Marcus, grandson of Aulus, consul, member of the college of seven epulones. For him the Senate decreed triumphal ornaments for services well rendered in Illyricum. Lartia, daughter of Gnaeus, his wife. Aulus Plautius Urgulanius, son of Marcus. He lived for 9 years.
EDCS-05801596.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument commemorating Marcus Plautius Silvanus, detailing his consulship and military honors, and also includes his wife and a young son who also died.

EDCS-05801600.text_edition: \n Caio Popilio Cai filio Quirina Caro Pedoni consuli VIIviro epulonum sodali Hadrianali legato Imperatoris Caesaris Antonini Augusti Pii pro praetore Germaniae superioris et exercitus in ea tendentis curatori operum publicorum praefecto aerari Saturni curatori viarum Aureliae veteris et novae Corneliae et Triumphalis legato legionis X fretensis a cuius cura se excusavit praetori tribuno plebis quaestori divi Hadriani Augusti in omnibus honoribus candidato Imperatoris tribuno laticlavio legionis III Cyreneicae sic donato donis militaribus a divo Hadriano ob Iudaicam expeditionem Xviro stlitibus iudicandis patrono municipii curatori maximi exempli senatus populusque Tiburs optime de re publica merito\n
EDCS-05801600.translation: To Gaius Popilius Carus Pedon, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, consul, pontiff of the Epulones, member of the Hadrianic Sodality, legate of the Emperor Caesar Antoninus Pius Augustus, propraetor of Upper Germany and of the army encamped there, curator of public works, prefect of the Saturnian treasury, curator of the Aurelian roads, old and new, and the Cornelian and Triumphal roads, legate of the Tenth Legion Fretensis, from which office he excused himself, praetor, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of the deified Hadrian Augustus, in all offices a candidate of the Emperor, tribune with laticlavus of the Third Cyrenaic Legion, decorated with military gifts by the deified Hadrian for the Jewish expedition, member of the Xviri for judging lawsuits, patron of the municipality, curator of the highest example, the Senate and People of Tibur, who has deserved excellently of the state.
EDCS-05801600.summary: This is a career summary of Gaius Popilius Carus Pedon, a high-ranking Roman official, likely from the 2nd century CE, detailing his numerous political and military appointments and honors bestowed by the Senate and People of Tibur.

EDCS-05801601.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-05801601.translation: To Gaius Porcius Priscus Longinus, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, a most distinguished man, member of the Xviri for judging lawsuits, admitted among the quaestorians by the Acts of the Senate, curule aedile, admitted among the praetorians, proconsul of Lycia and Pamphylia, consul, brother of the Arvales, curator of the Temple of Hercules Victor, patron of the municipality. The Senate of Tibur. Under the supervision of Aurelius Zoticus, patron of the municipality, and Titus Sallius Romanus, decurion of the public affairs.
EDCS-05801601.summary: This epitaph honors Gaius Porcius Priscus Longinus, a Roman senator and official, likely from the 2nd century CE, and records the individuals who supervised its erection.

EDCS-05801602.text_edition: \n Lucio Roscio Marci filio Quirina Aeliano Maecio Celeri consuli proconsuli provinciae Africae praetori tribuno plebis quaestori Augusti X viro stlitibus iudicandis tribuno militum legionis IX Hispanae vexillariorum eiusdem in expeditione Germanica donato ab Imperatore Augusto militaribus donis corona vallari et murali vexillis argenteis II hastis puris II salio Caius Vecilius Cai filio Palatina Probus amico optimo locus datus senatus consulto\n
EDCS-05801602.translation: To Lucius Roscilius Aelianus Maecius Celer, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, consul, proconsul of the province of Africa, praetor, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of Augustus, member of the Xviri for judging lawsuits, tribune of soldiers of the Ninth Spanish Legion with its vexillation in the German campaign, honored by Emperor Augustus with military gifts: a corona vallaris and muralis, two silver vexilla, two pure spears. For him, Caius Vecilius Probus, son of Caius, of the Palatina tribe, his friend, by decree of the Senate, granted this place.
EDCS-05801602.summary: This inscription commemorates Lucius Roscilius Aelianus Maecius Celer, a Roman official from the late 1st or early 2nd century CE, detailing his military honors and acknowledging a senatorial grant of burial space.

EDCS-05801615.translation: To the departed spirits. To Tiberius Claudius Paulinus, son of Tiberius, of the Palatina tribe, prefect of the First Cohort of Thracians in Britain, tribune of the Thirteenth Gemina Legion in Dacia, scribe of the curule aediles, curator of the people of Circeii. Flavianus and Paulina, children of Paulinus, to their most excellent father.
EDCS-05801615.summary: This is a tombstone for Tiberius Claudius Paulinus, a Roman military officer and administrator who served in Britain and Dacia, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05801616.translation: To the departed spirits. Of Lucius Cominius Maximus, son of Lucius, of the Mantua family, chief centurion twice, procurator of Marcus Antoninus Augustus, prefect of the Second Trajana Fortis Legion (200,000 sesterces), tribune of the Seventh Praetorian Cohort, Fourteenth Urban Cohort, Third Vigiles Cohort, centurion of the First Praetorian Cohort, Tenth Urban Cohort, fifth Vigiles Cohort, beneficiarius of the legates of the praetorian prefects. He lived 82 years and 18 days. His wife, Numitoria Moschis, daughter of Gaius, erected this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-05801616.summary: This tombstone honors Lucius Cominius Maximus, a high-ranking soldier who rose through the ranks to become a prefect of a legion, who lived in the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-05801617.translation: Gaius Numidius Quadratus, a Cilician by birth, a sailor of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum. He served 20 years and lived 40 years. Marcus Postumius Secundus, to the well-deserving.
EDCS-05801617.summary: This is a tombstone for Gaius Numidius Quadratus, a sailor in the Roman Praetorian fleet at Misenum, who served for 20 years and died at the age of 40, likely in the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05801618.translation: To the departed spirits. To Numitoria Moschis, daughter of Gaius, his most holy wife. Lucius Graecius Constans, tribune of the First Praetorian Cohort, and Lucius Graecius Numitorius Constans, his son, her heirs, made this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-05801618.summary: This tombstone is dedicated by a husband and son to their wife and mother, Numitoria Moschis, likely in the 2nd century CE, with the husband holding a military rank.

EDCS-05801623.translation: To the departed spirits. Here lies Lucius Vibius Crescentis, veteran of the Fourth Praetorian Cohort. May the earth be light for you. Cara Salvidiena, to the well-deserving.
EDCS-05801623.summary: This tombstone marks the burial of Lucius Vibius Crescentis, a veteran of the Fourth Praetorian Cohort, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05801628.translation: To the departed spirits. To Aelia Perpetua, daughter of Titus, who lived 3 years, 7 months, and 23 days. Titus Aelius Ampliatus, freedman of Augustus, a clerk at the Tiburtine villa, and Flavia Aphrodisia, parents, to their dearest daughter.
EDCS-05801628.summary: This tombstone records the death of a young girl, Aelia Perpetua, in the 1st or 2nd century CE, and was erected by her parents, one of whom was a freedman of the emperor.

EDCS-05801632.translation: To the departed spirits. To Tiberius Claudius Aelianus Sabinianus, physician of Augustus. Pedanius Rufus, a friend.
EDCS-05801632.summary: This tombstone is dedicated to Tiberius Claudius Aelianus Sabinianus, a physician to Emperor Augustus, by his friend Pedanius Rufus, likely in the 1st century CE.

EDCS-05801635.translation: Gaius Julius Samius, freedman of Augustus, procurator, accensus of the divine Claudius and Nero Augustus, patron of patrons.
EDCS-05801635.summary: This inscription honors Gaius Julius Samius, a freedman and procurator, who served as an accensus for emperors Claudius and Nero and was considered a patron.

EDCS-05801636.translation: Publius Semius, son of Publius, of the Collina tribe, quaestorian scribe, from the sexprimis. Publius Aquilius Gallus. Fannia, his wife.
EDCS-05801636.summary: This inscription records Publius Semius, a quaestorian scribe, and his wife Fannia, likely related to a group called the 'sexprimis'.

EDCS-05801649.translation: Here Eutactus first established the college and built this monument from public funds, and was likewise the first warden of the monument by decree of the decurions and the plebs.
EDCS-05801649.summary: This inscription commemorates Eutactus for founding a college, building a monument, and serving as its first warden through public and local government initiatives.

EDCS-05801650.translation: Titus Herennius, son of Lucius, and Titus Herennius, son of Titus, the IIIIviri iure dicundo, restored this.
EDCS-05801650.summary: This inscription records the restoration of something by Titus Herennius (son of Lucius) and Titus Herennius (son of Titus), who held the office of IIIIvir iure dicundo.

EDCS-05801653.translation: To Marcus Lurio Lucretianus, son of Marcus, of the Palatina tribe, patron of the municipality. The citizens of Tibur, having contributed money, in honor of him having been offered the quinquennality, presented XX pairs of gladiators and a hunt at their own expense. The place was granted by decree of the senate. Dedicated on the 9th day before the Kalends of August, with Lucius Eggius Marullus and Gnaeus Papirius Aelianus as consuls.
EDCS-05801653.summary: This inscription records the dedication of gladiatorial games and a hunt by the citizens of Tibur to their patron Marcus Lurio Lucretianus, in honor of his quinquennial office, with the location for the dedication granted by the senate.

EDCS-05801655.text_edition: \n Caio Maenio Cai filio Camilia Basso aedili IIIIviro magistro Herculaneo et Augustali praefecto fabrum Marci Silani Marci fili sexto Carthaginis tribuno militum legionis III Augustae quinquennali\n
EDCS-05801655.translation: To Gaius Maenius Bassus, son of Gaius, of the Camilia tribe, aedile, IIIIvir, magistrate of the Herculanei and Augustales, prefect of the artisans of Marcus Silanus, sixth tribune of soldiers of the VI Legion Augusta, and quinquennalis.
EDCS-05801655.summary: This inscription honors Gaius Maenius Bassus, listing his various magistracies and military service, including roles as aedile, IIIIvir, prefect of artisans, and tribune, likely in Carthage.

EDCS-05801663.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-05801663.translation: To Titus Sabidius Maximus, son of Titus, of the Palatina tribe, quaestorian scribe, sexprimus twice, prefect of artisans, pontifex, Salian priest, curator of the temple of Hercules Victor, tribune of the aqueducts, quinquennalis, patron of the municipality. A place for burial was granted by the will of the people and decree of the senate of the Tiburtines.
EDCS-05801663.summary: This inscription grants burial rights to Titus Sabidius Maximus, a prominent local official and patron in Tibur, by popular will and senatorial decree, listing his many honors and offices.

EDCS-05801668.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-05801668.translation: Gaius Sextilius Ephebus, freedman of the Vestal Virgin of Tibur, Herculanius, Augustalis, curator. Because Lucius Alfenatius Priscus and Marcus Mucius, IIIIviri, on the 4th day before the Kalends of September, granted access to the basilica Liapta to Sextilius Ephebus, having examined his petition that he be permitted to place a marble base at his own expense under the treasury of Hercules and Augustus and the Esquilina gate, just as it would be pleasing for authority to be declared in writing concerning this matter, they decreed the following: Since Gaius Sextilius, freedman of the Vestal Virgin Saufeia, Ephebus, is not for the first time today being honored for his generosity to the public, with the marble base placed under the treasury of Hercules and Augustus and the Esquilina gate, thanks are given to Sextilius Ephebus, and it is permitted that his name be inscribed on the title and that it be made known by the inscription that our senate has decreed; so that others may more easily follow his example, they decreed thus. Squilla and Titianus, as consuls. Attius wrote.
EDCS-05801668.summary: This is a senatorial decree from Tibur granting permission to Gaius Sextilius Ephebus, a freedman and local dignitary, to erect a marble base at his own expense in a public space, in recognition of his munificence.

EDCS-05801675.translation: Marcus Turpilius, son of Lucius, and Manius Popillius, son of Manius, quaestors, saw to it that the arches were built by decree of the senate.
EDCS-05801675.summary: This inscription records that the quaestors Marcus Turpilius and Manius Popillius oversaw the construction of arches in accordance with a senatorial decree.

EDCS-05801689.summary: This is a simple dedication inscription by Lucius Cornelius Priscus, indicating he commissioned or built something.

EDCS-05801690.summary: This is a funerary or dedicatory inscription stating that Lucius Luttius Thalamus was the maker.

EDCS-05801694.summary: This is a possessive or ownership inscription identifying Publius Marcius Rufus.

EDCS-05801701.summary: This is a funerary or dedicatory inscription stating that Titus Sabidius Helico was the maker.

EDCS-05801703.summary: This is a funerary or dedicatory inscription stating that Titus Sabidius Helico was the maker.

EDCS-05801706.summary: This is a funerary or dedicatory inscription stating that Gaius Tiburtius Verna was the maker.

EDCS-05801710.translation: To the Divine Manes. Marcus Nummius Proculus, the leatherworker, made this for his most deserving wife, Chrysis.
EDCS-05801710.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by Marcus Nummius Proculus to his wife Valeria Chrysis, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05801717.translation: To the Divine Manes. Faustina, mother, made this for Publius Aelius Ponticus, who lived 5 years, 8 months, and 15 days.
EDCS-05801717.summary: This funerary inscription records the life span of Publius Aelius Ponticus and is dedicated by his mother Faustina, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05801720.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aelia Hermione, wife of Aelius Felix, and their children inscribed this for Aelia Germana, nurse of the deified Hadrian, and for her son Aelius [---], and for themselves, their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-05801720.summary: This funerary inscription honors Aelia Germana, nurse of Emperor Hadrian, and her son, and was set up by Aelia Hermione and her family, dating to the period after Hadrian's deification.

EDCS-05801727.translation: To the Divine Manes. For her husband Aug[e] Dextrus, who lived 53 years and 10 days, for his merits.
EDCS-05801727.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication to Auge Dextrus by his wife, noting his lifespan and acknowledging his merits, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-05801749.translation: To the Divine Manes. Publius Comicus Hermes made this for his well-deserving wife, Clodia Prisca, and for himself and his family and their descendants.
EDCS-05801749.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by Publius Comicus Hermes for his wife Clodia Prisca and for their descendants, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-05801755.translation: To Cossinia Caesia, freedwoman of a woman, and Posidonius and Lucius Cossinius Discus, freedman of a woman. To Sextia Eugena, freedwoman of Publius. In the front 11 feet, in the field 15 feet.
EDCS-05801755.summary: This is a funerary boundary stone marking the dimensions of a plot of land for Cossinia Caesia, Posidonius, Lucius Cossinius Discus, and Sextia Eugena.

EDCS-05801758.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Ennius Cosmus and Valeria Philotera, and Marcus Valerius Cosmus, for themselves and their family.
EDCS-05801758.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Gaius Ennius Cosmus, Valeria Philotera, and Marcus Valerius Cosmus, intended for their family.

EDCS-05801761.translation: To the departed spirits. For Fabia Venusta, daughter of Quintus.
EDCS-05801761.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Fabia Venusta, daughter of Quintus.

EDCS-05801778.translation: To the departed spirits. For Herennia Lampas, concubine of Herennius Postumus, whose bones were transferred from Sardinia.
EDCS-05801778.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Herennia Lampas, concubine of Herennius Postumus, noting the transfer of her remains from Sardinia.

EDCS-05801779.translation: Lucius Horatius, son of Lucius, grandson of Lucius, of the Aniensis tribe.
EDCS-05801779.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording the name and tribal affiliation of Lucius Horatius.

EDCS-05801795.translation: To the departed spirits. Laevia Prima, who lived 90 years.
EDCS-05801795.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Laevia Prima, recording her name and age at death.

EDCS-05801824.translation: To the departed spirits. For Primigenius, their deserving fellow slave, they made this.
EDCS-05801824.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Primigenius, made by his deserving fellow slaves.

EDCS-05801838.translation: Here lies the sweet Sallustia Rufa, who always showed worthy service. Quintus Marcius Philippus. Two jars.
EDCS-05801838.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sallustia Rufa, commemorating her service and mentioning Quintus Marcius Philippus and two jars.

EDCS-05801845.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gaius Sextilius Rufus the younger, son of Gaius, of the Camilia tribe, a most sweet infant, who lived 7 years, 10 months, and 10 days. Gaius Tiburtius Alphius and Valeria Potita, educators.
EDCS-05801845.summary: This is a funerary dedication for the infant Gaius Sextilius Rufus the younger, made by his educators Gaius Tiburtius Alphius and Valeria Potita.

EDCS-05801854.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gaius Valerius Corinthus. Arcaeus and Timotheus for the deserving recipient.
EDCS-05801854.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Gaius Valerius Corinthus, made by Arcaeus and Timotheus.

EDCS-05801856.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valeria Athenais made this for Modia Paulina, daughter of Titus, and for herself, her family, and their descendants. She lived 13 years, 11 months. She designated this place while alive, with 7 steps ascending, and 7 inscribed boundary stones. It measures 330 feet in circumference. From the entrance of the upper gate, on the right descending side, it must yield 5 feet to the street up to the 3rd boundary stone, along with a shop. Also, 2 constructed pillars must yield to the upper entrance of the hypaethron in the alignment of the wall and supports, and an altar on which...
EDCS-05801856.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a burial plot and its boundaries by Valeria Athenais for her family, including specifications for its layout and dimensions.

EDCS-05801862.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Ulpius Maximus, who lived 40 years, 3 months, 18 days. Marcus Ulpius Maximus, his son, for his dearest father. Arria Pacata, daughter of Lucius, his mother, oversaw it.
EDCS-05801862.summary: This tombstone records the death of Marcus Ulpius Maximus and was erected by his son and mother.

EDCS-05801915.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Tiberius Claudius Alcimus made this for himself while alive, and for his dearest partner Cornelia Sympherusa, and for his daughter Claudia Primitiva, and for his family and their descendants.
EDCS-05801915.summary: This funerary inscription records Tiberius Claudius Alcimus dedicating a tomb for himself, his partner, daughter, and descendants.

EDCS-05801918.translation: For Statilia Priscilla, daughter of Spurius, and for Statilia Fortunata, daughter of Spurius.
EDCS-05801918.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument or tomb for two women named Statilia, daughters of Spurius.

EDCS-05801923.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Turraniae Philippae Quintus Turranius Callistus coniugi piissimae\n
EDCS-05801923.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Quintus Turranius Callistus for his most devoted wife, Turrania Philippa.
EDCS-05801923.summary: This funerary inscription records Quintus Turranius Callistus dedicating a monument to his wife, Turrania Philippa.

EDCS-05801929.translation: Quintus Canusius Praenestinus made the wall and pottery store for his freedmen and freedwomen.
EDCS-05801929.summary: This inscription records Quintus Canusius Praenestinus constructing a wall and an 'ollarium' (a place for storing amphorae or pots, possibly a shop) for his freed dependents.

EDCS-05801930.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Pacxea Zoe made this for her partner Quintus Cassius Phaebus.
EDCS-05801930.summary: This funerary inscription records Pacxea Zoe dedicating a monument to her partner, Quintus Cassius Phaebus.

EDCS-05801931.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Nicolaus and Pannychis, parents, for their most devoted daughter Iusta.
EDCS-05801931.summary: This inscription records Nicolaus and Pannychis dedicating a monument to their devoted daughter, Cocceia Iusta.

EDCS-05801932.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Flavius Zosimus, freedman of our Emperors, made this for his fellow freedman Titus Flavius Marcion, deserving well.
EDCS-05801932.summary: This funerary inscription records Titus Flavius Zosimus dedicating a monument to his fellow freedman, Titus Flavius Marcion.

EDCS-05801934.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iulia Fausta, his wife, [made this] for her most dutiful Tiberius Iulius Romanus.
EDCS-05801934.summary: This funerary inscription records Iulia Fausta dedicating a monument to her dutiful husband, Tiberius Iulius Romanus.

EDCS-05801935.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Iulius Martialis made this for his daughter Iulia Fortunata, who lived 7 years and 3 months, his most beloved.
EDCS-05801935.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young girl named Iulia Fortunata, set up by her father.

EDCS-05801936.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Tiberius Claudius Atimetus for his well-deserving wife Pomponia Aphrodisia.
EDCS-05801936.summary: A funerary epitaph for Pomponia Aphrodisia, dedicated by her husband.

EDCS-05801938.translation: Decimus Valerius Proculus, son of Decimus, of the Cornelia tribe, as aedile, dictator, and quaestor of the food supply, dedicated a decorated and gilded water-pipe, together with Valeria Fortunata and Valeria Procula, as a gift to Isis and Serapis.
EDCS-05801938.summary: This is a dedication of a decorated water-pipe to the gods Isis and Serapis by a former magistrate and his family.

EDCS-05801942.translation: Titus Titius Tappo, son of Titus, of the Sergia tribe, as a XXvir, Titus Titius Poppalenus, son of Titus, of the Sergia tribe, his father, Gessia Mater, daughter of Mani, his mother, Titia Rufa, daughter of Titus, his sister. Spare the heir, and give to the dead what they want; if the dead have anything, this monument will be mine, the rest will be left according to the judgment of Quintus Umbricius Flaccus, son of Quintus, of the Claudia tribe. This monument will not bind the heir.
EDCS-05801942.summary: This is a complex funerary monument text with provisions regarding inheritance and burial, set up by a family for a deceased member.

EDCS-05801953.translation: [For] Phaedrus, priest of the Nomentan Matres deum Magnae, [lived] 35 years and in good health, [lived] 70 years with full vigor, [a curious person]. And for Melizusa, his own slave girl, lived 18 years, placed here 30 years.
EDCS-05801953.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a priest and his slave girl, with complex dating and life spans.

EDCS-05801955.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Claudia Moschis, daughter of Tiberius, for her most beloved husband, Marcus Li[vius Herm]erotus, a cloth merchant from the Agrippinian granaries.
EDCS-05801955.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Livius Hermerotus, a cloth merchant, dedicated by his wife.

EDCS-05801956.translation: Lucius Anteius Menogenes, freedman of Lucius, [in front] 12 feet, in the field 12 feet.
EDCS-05801956.summary: This inscription records land measurements, likely for a funerary plot, belonging to a freedman.

EDCS-05801963.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Messalla Rutilianus made this for Claudius Felix, well-deserving.
EDCS-05801963.summary: A funerary epitaph for Claudius Felix, erected by his heir Messalla Rutilianus.

EDCS-05801965.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Epityches, freedman of Augustus, for Crepereia Petroniana Marciana, his wife of rare example.
EDCS-05801965.summary: This funerary epitaph honors Crepereia Petroniana Marciana, wife of Epityches, a freedman of Augustus.

EDCS-05801967.summary: This is an incomplete funerary epitaph fragment, likely for a child named Fullonius.

EDCS-05801972.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Quintus Iulius Lollianus, dearest brother.
EDCS-05801972.summary: This is a tombstone for Quintus Iulius Lollianus, set up by a sibling.

EDCS-05801973.translation: Of Aulus Licinius Felix. In the front, 11 feet; in the field, 11 feet.
EDCS-05801973.summary: This inscription likely records the dimensions of a plot of land belonging to Aulus Licinius Felix.

EDCS-05801974.translation: Lucius Maelius Primus, son of Lucius, lived 49 years. His sister Maelia and Valeria Specula, freedwoman of Lucius and Valeria, lived 19 years, pious and chaste. The sister and mother, having suffered extreme grief, made this. Valeria Olia, freedwoman, and Acme, freedwoman, Prima.
EDCS-05801974.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Maelius Primus and his sister Maelia, set up by their mother and another relative, dating to the late Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-05801975.translation: To the Divine Shades. Martialis lived 9 years, 6 months, 5 days. Iulia Nice made this for her well-deserving pupil.
EDCS-05801975.summary: This is a tombstone for a young boy named Martialis, set up by his teacher Iulia Nice.

EDCS-05801978.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Olympias, freedman/woman(?). Philumenus, for himself and for [...].
EDCS-05801978.summary: This is a fragmented tombstone inscription possibly for Olympias and Philumenus.

EDCS-05801995.translation: To Sextus Pedius Hirrutus, son of Sextus, of the Arnensian tribe, Lucilius Pollio, consul, prefect of the military treasury, legate of the emperor, jurist of Asturia and Gallaecia, praetor [...].
EDCS-05801995.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription honoring Sextus Pedius Hirrutus, a high-ranking official, likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.
