EDCS-07000260.translation: Helena, freedwoman of Aulus and the woman, for herself and for her deserving husband Aulus Plotus Lucrioni, and for Gaius Hostius Primogenitus, and for Aulus Plotus Felix the freedman, and for Plotia Veneria the freedwoman, for Aulus Plotus Crescens the freedman, and for their descendants.
EDCS-07000260.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Helena for her family, including her husband, freedmen, and descendants, indicating a tomb or burial plot.

EDCS-07000262.text_edition: \n Lucio Limbriciof(ilio) Falerna V leg II\n
EDCS-07000262.translation: To Lucius Limbricius Falerna, son of Limbricius, of the II Legion.
EDCS-07000262.summary: This is a dedication or tombstone for Lucius Limbricius Falerna, a soldier in the II Legion.

EDCS-07000264.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luciliae Chrysogone quae vixit annos XXII menses IIII Marcius Victor coniugi bene merenti con sic qua vixit annos V menses IIII\n
EDCS-07000264.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Lucilia Chrysogone, who lived 22 years and 4 months. Marcius Victor, her deserving husband, [erected this] for whom he lived 5 years and 4 months.
EDCS-07000264.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucilia Chrysogone, set up by her husband Marcius Victor, providing her age at death and mentioning their marriage duration.

EDCS-07000265.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publi Clodi Celsiani infantis speciosi et blandidi vixit mensibus XV Clodia Ephesia avia et Macedo et Celsa parentes infelicissimi\n
EDCS-07000265.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Publius Clodius Celsianus, a beautiful and charming infant, who lived 15 months. His most unhappy parents, Clodia Ephesia, grandmother, and Macedo and Celsa, [set this up].
EDCS-07000265.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for an infant, Publius Clodius Celsianus, set up by his grandmother and parents, recording his age at death.

EDCS-07000266.text_edition: \n Lucius Fadius Decimi filius Rufus sibi et suis Maria Spuri filia Fausta sibi et suis Marcus Titius Epaphra sibi et suis Quintus Granius Quinti libertus Menophilus sibi et suis Lucius Furius |(mulieris) libertus Isio sibi et suis\n
EDCS-07000266.translation: Lucius Fadius Rufus, son of Decimus, for himself and his family; Maria Fausta, daughter of Spurius, for herself and her family; Marcus Titius Epaphra, for himself and his family; Quintus Granius Menophilus, freedman of Quintus, for himself and his family; Lucius Furius Isio, freedman of the woman, for himself and his family.
EDCS-07000266.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording multiple individuals and their families establishing rights to burial plots or tombs.

EDCS-07000268.text_edition: \n tio Marino manipulario III Minerva natione Surus vixit nnos XXXVIII militavit XVIII Marino fratri heres bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-07000268.translation: To Marino, a standard-bearer of the III cohort, nationality Surus, who lived 38 years, served 18 years. His brother Marino, his deserving heir, made this.
EDCS-07000268.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marino, a standard-bearer in the III cohort, set up by his brother and heir, providing his age and military service length.

EDCS-07000269.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luci Archibi Magni faber duplicarius III Virtute militavit nnos XXX vixit nnos LX Iulia Eustathia uxor bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-07000269.translation: To the departed spirits of Lucius Archibius Magnus, a duplicarius of the III Virtute cohort, who served for 30 years and lived for 60 years. Iulia Eustathia, his devoted wife, made this.
EDCS-07000269.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Archibius Magnus, a soldier who served in the III Virtute cohort, set up by his wife.

EDCS-07000270.text_edition: \n Quinti Saturni Severi Quintus Sabidius Chaerea bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-07000270.translation: Quintus Sabidius Chaerea made this for Quintus Saturnius Severus, his deserving one.
EDCS-07000270.summary: This is a funerary epitaph, possibly for a freedman or close associate, dedicated by Quintus Sabidius Chaerea.

EDCS-07000272.text_edition: \n Naevia Cnaei filia Tiberius Venidius Tiberi filius propola\n
EDCS-07000272.translation: Naevia, daughter of Gnaeus, and Tiberius Venidius, son of Tiberius, a provision merchant.
EDCS-07000272.summary: This inscription records the names and occupation of two individuals, Naevia and Tiberius Venidius, a provision merchant.

EDCS-07000273.text_edition: \n III Kalendas Apriles Caio Calpetanio Sedato Marco Hordeonio consulibus\n
EDCS-07000273.translation: On the third day before the Kalends of April, in the consulship of Gaius Calpetanius Sedatus and Marcus Hordeonius.
EDCS-07000273.summary: This inscription records a date in the Roman Republican calendar, specifying the consuls.

EDCS-07000274.text_edition: \n Holconium Priscum aedilem oro vos faciatis\n
EDCS-07000274.translation: I urge you to make Holconius Priscus aedile.
EDCS-07000274.summary: This is a political appeal urging the public to elect Holconius Priscus as an aedile.

EDCS-07000275.summary: This inscription likely supports the candidacy of Numerius Popidius Rufus for the position of aedile.

EDCS-07000276.summary: This inscription is a dedication, likely funerary or honorific, to Aulus Vettius Caprasius.

EDCS-07000277.text_edition: \n Publium Sittium Coniunctum IIvirum iure dicundo oro vos faciatis \n
EDCS-07000277.translation: I urge you to make Publius Sittius Coniunctus, the IIvir iure dicundo, aedile.
EDCS-07000277.summary: This is a political appeal urging the public to elect Publius Sittius Coniunctus, who held the office of IIvir iure dicundo, as aedile.

EDCS-07000278.summary: This inscription is a dedication, likely funerary or honorific, to Lucius Rusticelius Celer.

EDCS-07000279.text_edition: \n Caio Erucio Heniocho Caius Erucius Faustus libertus sibi et suis et Caio Erucio Oceano conliberto et Publio Annio Plocami liberto Eroti amico\n
EDCS-07000279.translation: Caius Erucius Faustus, freedman, for himself and his kin, and for Caius Erucius Oceanus, fellow freedman, and for Erotus, freedman friend of Publius Annius Plocamus.
EDCS-07000279.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by the freedman Caius Erucius Faustus, commemorating himself, his family, a fellow freedman, and a friend.

EDCS-07000283.text_edition: \n Lucius Antistius Myro et Lucius Antistius Narcissus liberti eius et Titus Flavius Masculus filius eius heredes fecerunt Antistiae Hermaidi dulcissimae\n
EDCS-07000283.translation: Lucius Antistius Myro and Lucius Antistius Narcissus, his freedmen, and Titus Flavius Masculus, his son, as his heirs, made this for Antistia Hermaidi, dearest.
EDCS-07000283.summary: This is a tombstone erected by freedmen and a son for Antistia Hermaidi.

EDCS-07000284.text_edition: \n Lucius Antonius Cerdonis libertus Fortunatus sibi et Noniae Crhesime sic coniugi optumae bene merenti feminae et Lucio Antonio Cerdoni patrono suo et Euhodo liberto et Adiecto liberto et Heraclae libertae et Primae libertae et Noniae Chresimes libertae Fortunatae libertae et Aulo Cossinio Eutycho amico libertis libertabusque suis\n
EDCS-07000284.translation: Fortunatus, freedman of Lucius Antonius Cerdonis, made this for himself, his excellent wife Nonia Chrhesime who deserved well, his patron Lucius Antonius Cerdonis, his freedman Euhodus, his freedman Adiectus, his freedwoman Heracla, his freedwoman Prima, his freedwoman Nonia Chresimes Fortunatae, and his friend Aulus Cossinius Eutycus, and for his freedmen and freedwomen.
EDCS-07000284.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Fortunatus, a freedman, for his family, patron, and associates.

EDCS-07000285.text_edition: \n Lucio Mario Luciano Augustali dupliciario Puteolanorum Thallus libertus merenti\n
EDCS-07000285.translation: Thallus, freedman, made this for Lucius Marius Lucianus, Augustalis, dupliciarius of the Puteolani, who deserved well.
EDCS-07000285.summary: This is a tombstone erected by a freedman for Lucius Marius Lucianus, a former soldier and local official, who was honored.

EDCS-07000286.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Veneriae vixit annos XXIV mater pientissima\n
EDCS-07000286.translation: To the departed spirits. Veneria lived 24 years. A most devoted mother.
EDCS-07000286.summary: This is a tombstone for Veneria, who died at 24, noted for her maternal devotion.

EDCS-07000287.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Pompeia Restituta, who died at 19.

EDCS-07000288.summary: This inscription identifies Mitreus as the leader of a youth group.

EDCS-07000354.text_edition: \n Marcus Tettius Repentinus sexvir Augustalis Venafri sibi et Tettiae Helpidi coniugi suae\n
EDCS-07000354.translation: Marcus Tettius Repentinus, Augustalis sexvir of Venafri, made this for himself and his wife Tettia Helpis.
EDCS-07000354.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Marcus Tettius Repentinus, a local magistrate, for himself and his wife.

EDCS-07400126.text_edition: \n Caius Fabius Placidus vixit nnos XXII\n
EDCS-07400126.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Caius Fabius Placidus, who died at 22.

EDCS-07400127.text_edition: \n Lucius Aemilius Thallus libertis libertabus posteris eorum in fronte pedes XX in agro pedes L\n
EDCS-07400127.translation: Lucius Aemilius Thallus, for his freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants, 20 feet in front, 50 feet in the ground.
EDCS-07400127.summary: This records a land grant of 20x50 feet for the burial of Lucius Aemilius Thallus's freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.

EDCS-07400129.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flaviae Hediae matri Flavius Phoebion Auggustorum l(ibertus) verna vilicus et Flavius Trypho l(ibertus) filii fecerunt\n
EDCS-07400129.translation: To the departed spirits. For Flavia Hedia, mother. Her sons Flavius Phoebion, freedman of the Augusti, overseer, and Flavius Trypho, freedman, made this.
EDCS-07400129.summary: This is a tombstone erected by two sons for their mother, Flavia Hedia, identifying one son as an imperial freedman and overseer.

EDCS-07400134.text_edition: \n Imperatori Aurelio Antonino Augusto consuli III Publius Octavius Nymphodotus et Aulus Egrilius Thallus curatores traiectus Luculli dedicaverunt\n
EDCS-07400134.translation: To Emperor Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, consul for the third time, Publius Octavius Nymphodotus and Aulus Egrilius Thallus, curators of the Lucullus ferry crossing, dedicated this.
EDCS-07400134.summary: This is a dedication of a ferry crossing by its curators to Emperor Antoninus Augustus.

EDCS-07400135.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici Augusto corpus traiectus Luculli\n
EDCS-07400135.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, the corpus of the Lucullus ferry crossing.
EDCS-07400135.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication or dedication of an object related to the Lucullus ferry crossing to Emperor Gordian.

EDCS-07400138.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Cai filius Cocilius Hermes patronus et quinquennalis perpetuus collegii dendrophorum Ostiensium signum Matris Magnae ex argenti pondo III et Z et SS VI milia nummum dono dedit ut VI Kalendas Iunias die natalis sui de |(denariis) CLXXX usuras eorum epulentur et discumbentes sportulas partiantur quot sic is observatum non erit tunc summas supra scriptas honoratis collegii fabrum tignuariorum Ostiensium dari volo sub condicione supra scripta stipulatus est Cocilius Hermes ispepondit sic plebs dedicatum Idibus Ianuaris Maximo et Glabrione consulibus ob cuius dedicationem decurionibus dedit isportulas sic |(denarios) II\n
EDCS-07400138.translation: Caius Iulius Cocilius Hermes, son of Caius, patron and perpetual quinquennial of the guild of dendrophori of Ostia, gave as a gift a statue of the Great Mother weighing 3 pounds and 6 ounces of silver, worth 6,000 sesterces, so that on his birthday, the 6th day before the Kalends of June, from the interest of 180 denarii, they may feast and distribute handouts to those reclining, as is customary; but if this is not observed, then I wish the above-mentioned sum to be given to the honored guild of the carpenters of Ostia, under the condition stated above. Cocilius Hermes himself guaranteed this. The plebs dedicated it on the Ides of January, when Maximus and Glabrio were consuls. For whose dedication he gave handouts to the decurions, namely 2 denarii.
EDCS-07400138.summary: This is a donation by a guild official to his guild, specifying conditions for feasting and distribution of funds, with a backup donation to another guild, dated to the consulship of Maximus and Glabrio.

EDCS-07400140.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Baebi Cai filii Palatina Marciani decurionis flaminis divi Vespasiani vixit nnos XVIII menses X dies XX Caius Baebius Eucharistus pater infelicissimus\n
EDCS-07400140.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Caius Baebius Marcianus, son of Caius, of the Palatina tribe, decurion and flamen of the deified Vespasian, who lived 18 years, 10 months, and 20 days. His most unhappy father, Caius Baebius Eucharistus.
EDCS-07400140.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caius Baebius Marcianus, a decurion and flamen, erected by his father.

EDCS-07400141.text_edition: \n Marcus Rigius Lesbius Herculi Sancto donum dedit\n
EDCS-07400141.translation: Marcus Rigius Lesbius gave this gift to Holy Hercules.
EDCS-07400141.summary: This is a votive offering by Marcus Rigius Lesbius to Hercules.

EDCS-07400142.text_edition: \n Caius Numonius filius Vala quaestor praetor \n
EDCS-07400142.translation: Caius Numonius Vala, son of Caius, quaestor, praetor.
EDCS-07400142.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording the career of Caius Numonius Vala, who held the offices of quaestor and praetor.

EDCS-07400149.text_edition: \n CVius Augusti libertus Diadumenus verna adiutor a rationibus et a commentariis beneficiorum\n
EDCS-07400149.translation: Diadumenus, freedman of Augustus, steward, assistant in accounts and in charge of benefits.
EDCS-07400149.summary: This inscription lists the official positions held by Diadumenus, a freedman of Augustus, including steward and head of accounts.

EDCS-07400154.text_edition: \n Diis Manibus dominae meae Liberae CO amantissimae Felicio coniunx bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-07400154.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For my most loving lady, Libera. Felicio made this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-07400154.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Libera, erected by her husband Felicio.

EDCS-07400162.text_edition: \n Marci Cuseni Anterotis oleari Sexti Publili Dapni carbonari in agro pedes XV in fronte pedes XV\n
EDCS-07400162.translation: 15 feet in the ground, 15 feet in the front, of Marcus Cusenus Anterous, oil merchant, son of Sextus Publilius Daphnus, charcoal dealer.
EDCS-07400162.summary: This inscription appears to define property boundaries for an oil merchant and a charcoal dealer, possibly related to land ownership or sale.

EDCS-07400163.text_edition: \n Sexti Publili Sexti liberti Dapni Marci Cuseni Marci liberti Anterotis oleari in agro pedes XV in fronte pedes XV\n
EDCS-07400163.translation: 15 feet in the ground, 15 feet in the front, of Sextus Daphnus, freedman of Sextus Publilius, of Marcus Anterous, freedman of Marcus, oil merchant.
EDCS-07400163.summary: This inscription appears to define property boundaries for two individuals, both oil merchants, possibly related to land ownership or sale.

EDCS-07400164.text_edition: \n Marco Aurelio Marci filio Palatina Iulio Eupraepeti splendido equiti Romano patrono coloniae IIviro quinquennali pontifici Sanctae Fortunae Primigeniae ludos scaenicos Palatinorum religione dignos Fortunae Primigeniae ededit sic convocatio etiam corporibus collegiorum cum honesta divisione sportularum promeruit hic primus Praenestinorum indulgentia sacra impetrata die lusionis seu con conpositionis sic singulas feras cum paribus gladiatorum ededit sic et die muneris \n
EDCS-07400164.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Eupraepetus, son of Marcus, of the Palatina tribe, a splendid Roman knight, patron of the colony, twice chief magistrate, and priest of Holy Fortuna Primigenia. He presented scenic performances worthy of the Palatine community for Fortuna Primigenia, and also brought together the guilds with an honourable distribution of sportulae. He first obtained sacred rites through the indulgence of the Praenestines, and on the day of the games or competitions, he presented single beasts with matched gladiators, and likewise on the day of the gladiatorial show.
EDCS-07400164.summary: This inscription records the benefactions of Marcus Aurelius Eupraepetus, a prominent local official and priest, who funded games and distributions for the community of Praeneste, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07400168.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Vernaculo actori patri dulcissimo qui vixit annis XLII venustim pi<e=T>tosim Aurelius Cetris et Basdulus fili bene merenti fecerunt \n
EDCS-07400168.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aurelius Cetris and Basdulus, his sons, made this for their most beloved father Vernaculus, an actor, who lived 42 years, most charming and most pleasing.
EDCS-07400168.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Vernaculus, an actor, set up by his sons, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07400177.text_edition: \n Dextro Dextri aeditui et Campaniae Albinae filio Duronio a basilica cum suis vixit annis XXVI mensibus III diebus XIX\n
EDCS-07400177.translation: To Duronius, son of Dextrus the sacristan and Campania Albina, from the basilica, with his family. He lived 26 years, 3 months, and 19 days.
EDCS-07400177.summary: This is a tombstone for Duronius, recording his lifespan and family, possibly dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07400179.text_edition: \n Vivit Luci Novi Luci filii Longi in fronte pedes XIIX in agro pedes XIIX\n
EDCS-07400179.translation: Property of Lucius Novius Lucius Longus, 18 feet in front and 18 feet in the field.
EDCS-07400179.summary: This is a boundary marker indicating land ownership for Lucius Novius Longus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07400180.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Carbo coniugi imi bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-07400180.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Carbo made this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-07400180.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for an unnamed wife, dedicated by her husband Carbo, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07400184.text_edition: \n Publio Brittio Principi fecit Brittia Cale Livi delicio suo hic vixit annos XV\n
EDCS-07400184.translation: Brittia made this for Publius Brittius Princeps, her darling of Livius. He lived here 15 years.
EDCS-07400184.summary: This is a tombstone for Publius Brittius Princeps, erected by his partner Brittia, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07400187.text_edition: \n Pacciae Luci libertae Piluminae ossa hic sita sunt\n
EDCS-07400187.translation: The bones of Paccia, freedwoman of Lucius Pilumius, are placed here.
EDCS-07400187.summary: This is a tombstone indicating the burial place of Paccia, a freedwoman, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07400188.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Stratocleti Stratonici filio iuveni optimo et ingenio bono qui vixit nnos XVII menses VII dies VIII horas V Quintus Vibius Stratonicus et Myrsine parentes filio dulcissimo\n
EDCS-07400188.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Quintus Vibius Stratonicus and Myrsine, his parents, [made this] for their most beloved son Stratocles, son of Stratonicus, an excellent youth of good talent, who lived 17 years, 7 months, 8 days, and 5 hours.
EDCS-07400188.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Stratocles, a talented youth, erected by his parents, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07400189.translation: To Marcus Babullius Philomusus, freedman of Marcus.
EDCS-07400189.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Babullius Philomusus, a freedman, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07400190.text_edition: \n Aulus Varius Anti Aulo Vario Dionysio Aulo Vario Antiocho Terentiae Noviae E Variae C Variae \n
EDCS-07400190.translation: To Aulus Varius Anti, son of Aulus Varius Dionysius, Aulus Varius Antiochus, Terentia Nova, and Varia.
EDCS-07400190.summary: This is a dedication or memorial naming several individuals, likely related, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07400193.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Atilio Agrippae IIII Minerva vixit annis XXXV militavit annis XIIII Lucia Eutuc coniugi bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-07400193.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caio Atilio Agrippa lived 35 years, served 14 years. Lucia Eutuc made this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-07400193.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caio Atilio Agrippa, set up by his wife Lucia Eutuc, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE given the common formula and names.

EDCS-07400194.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording the name and office of Marcus Caecilius Potitus, possibly from a public monument, dating to the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-07600091.text_edition: \n Dormitio Regines sic Gaudenti bene merenti\n
EDCS-07600091.translation: The laying to rest of Regines, for Gaudenti, who deserved well.
EDCS-07600091.summary: This is a funerary inscription, possibly commemorating a burial or memorial for Regines, set up by Gaudenti, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-07600096.summary: This is a dedication to the god Volcano, likely from a shrine or altar, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07600097.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fabio Filadelfo Claudia Hilaritas faciendum curavit patri\n
EDCS-07600097.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Claudia Hilaritas had this made for her father, Fabio Filadelfo.
EDCS-07600097.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Fabio Filadelfo, commissioned by his daughter Claudia Hilaritas, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07600099.text_edition: \n Diis deabusque secundum interpretationem Clari Apollinis\n
EDCS-07600099.translation: To the gods and goddesses, according to the interpretation of Claros Apollo.
EDCS-07600099.summary: This inscription records a religious act or dedication performed in accordance with an oracle or divination from the sanctuary of Apollo at Claros, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07600102.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely a funerary dedication for a woman named Regina, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07600109.text_edition: \n io Cai filio Aemiliano Graptia coniugi ---\n
EDCS-07600109.translation: To io, son of Cai, Aemilianus. Graptia, for her deserving spouse---
EDCS-07600109.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph, possibly for io Cai filius Aemilianus, commissioned by his spouse Graptia, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07600110.text_edition: \n VI GR Antistia Luci liberta Secunda Antist<i>ae Pantuliae LOTA\n
EDCS-07600110.translation: Antistia Secunda, freedwoman of Lucius Antistius, [made this for] Antistia Pantuliae.
EDCS-07600110.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Antistia Secunda, a freedwoman, for Antistia Pantuliae, likely a funerary or honorific context from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07600112.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus <T=F>reboniae Felicissimae Sulpicius Felix coniugi merenti fecit \n
EDCS-07600112.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Sulpicius Felix made this for his deserving wife, Trebonia Felicissima.
EDCS-07600112.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Trebonia Felicissima, erected by her husband Sulpicius Felix, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07600113.text_edition: \n Lucius Licinius Luci libertus Eros medicus Fulcinia Luci liberta Artimisia sic uxor libertis libertabusque mieis omnibus\n
EDCS-07600113.translation: Lucius Licinius Eros, freedman of Lucius, a physician, and Fulcinia Artimisia, freedwoman of Lucius, his wife, [establish this] for my freedmen, freedwomen, and all their descendants.
EDCS-07600113.summary: This funerary inscription records a physician and his wife establishing a burial plot for their freedpersons and their descendants.

EDCS-07600114.text_edition: \n Locus consecratus Otacilio Licino in fronte pedes CXX in agro pedes XXX Trophimus libertus posuit\n
EDCS-07600114.translation: A consecrated plot, 120 feet in front and 30 feet in depth, for Otacilius Licinus. Trophimus, his freedman, erected this.
EDCS-07600114.summary: This inscription records the consecration of a plot of land for Otacilius Licinus by his freedman, Trophimus, with dimensions provided.

EDCS-07600115.text_edition: \n Otacilio Quieto Otacilia liberta Hel sibi et suis\n
EDCS-07600115.translation: Otacilia, freedwoman of Otacilius Quietus, [dedicates this] for himself and his family.
EDCS-07600115.summary: This dedication records Otacilia, a freedwoman, dedicating a monument for her patron Otacilius Quietus and his family.

EDCS-07600116.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberio Roccio Rusoni Euphrosynus libertus posterisque suis in fronte pedes XVII in agro pedes XVII\n
EDCS-07600116.translation: To the Divine Shades. Euphrosynus, freedman of Tiberius Roccius Rusonius, [dedicates this] for himself and his descendants, 17 feet in front and 17 feet in depth.
EDCS-07600116.summary: This funerary inscription records Euphrosynus, a freedman, dedicating a plot of land to the Divine Shades for himself and his descendants.

EDCS-07600119.text_edition: \n ni Stabilionis Veturiaes sic Philumenae pis\n
EDCS-07600119.translation: [This tomb is] for Stabilio, and for Veturia Philumena, his wife.
EDCS-07600119.summary: This fragmentary funerary inscription records a tomb for Stabilio and his wife Veturia Philumena.

EDCS-07600120.text_edition: \n Veveius Comus Chrestio Veveia Fausta uxor Profuturus\n
EDCS-07600120.translation: Veveius Comus [and] Chrestio, [and] Veveia Fausta, his wife, [and] Profuturus.
EDCS-07600120.summary: This fragmentary inscription appears to list several names, possibly for a funerary monument or a shared property.

EDCS-07600124.text_edition: \n Carpinatia Fortunata Veneri votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07600124.translation: Carpinatia Fortunata willingly and deservedly paid her vow to Venus.
EDCS-07600124.summary: This inscription records Carpinatia Fortunata fulfilling a vow to the goddess Venus.

EDCS-07600130.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph identifying Paulla Flavia, daughter of Caius.

EDCS-07600131.text_edition: \n Lucio Caedicio Luci filio Teretina patri duomviro quinquennali Noviae Luci filiae matri Camilliae Pollae sorori Graniae Publi filiae aviae\n
EDCS-07600131.translation: To Lucius Caedicius, son of Lucius, duovir for five years, and to his mother Novia, daughter of Lucius, and to his grandmother Grania, daughter of Publius.
EDCS-07600131.summary: This funerary inscription honors Lucius Caedicius, his mother, and his grandmother, mentioning his political career as a duovir.

EDCS-07600132.text_edition: \n Caius Lactonius Marci filius duovir tribunus militum in Macedonia legionis XXVII\n
EDCS-07600132.translation: Gaius Lactonius Marci filius, duovir, military tribune in Macedonia of the 27th Legion.
EDCS-07600132.summary: This inscription records the career of Gaius Lactonius, identifying him as a duovir and a military tribune.

EDCS-07600133.text_edition: \n Iuliae Luci filiae Valeriae Marcianae Crispinillae clarissimae memoriae feminae arbitratu ipsius Lucius Flavius Luci filius Cleonaeus vir clarissimus VIIvir epulonum Cnaeus Suellius Cnaei filius Rufus Marcianus clarissimus iuvenis Flavia Luci filia Polymnia Marciana clarissima femina Flavia Luci filia Crispinilla clarissima femina matri piissimae fili heredes\n
EDCS-07600133.translation: To Julia Valeria Marciana Crispinilla, daughter of Lucius, of the Cornelian tribe, of most distinguished memory. By her decree, Lucius Flavius Cleonaeus, son of Lucius, of the Cornelian tribe, most distinguished man, member of the board of seven for public feasts; Gnaeus Suellius Rufus Marcianus, son of Gnaeus, of the Cornelian tribe, most distinguished youth; Flavia Polymnia Marciana, daughter of Lucius, of the Cornelian tribe, most distinguished woman; and Flavia Crispinilla, daughter of Lucius, of the Cornelian tribe, most distinguished woman, sons and heirs, [erected this] to their most devoted mother.
EDCS-07600133.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by sons and heirs to their devoted mother, Julia Valeria Marciana Crispinilla, a woman of distinguished memory, indicating the family's social standing and affection.

EDCS-07600134.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antoniae Fortunatae Epaphroditus coniugi karissimae\n
EDCS-07600134.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Antonia Fortunata, from Epaphroditus, her dearest wife.
EDCS-07600134.summary: This is a tombstone inscription dedicated by Epaphroditus to his dearest wife, Antonia Fortunata.

EDCS-07600135.text_edition: \n Loca tria olla Luci Audi Barnaei\n
EDCS-07600135.translation: Three places for pots of Lucius Audius Barnaeus.
EDCS-07600135.summary: This inscription records the allocation of three burial places (ollae) by Lucius Audius Barnaeus, likely for himself and his family.

EDCS-07600137.text_edition: \n Pompeius Adiutor et Pompeiae Adiutoris libertae Primae\n
EDCS-07600137.translation: Pompeius Adiutor and Prima, freedwoman of Pompeius Adiutor.
EDCS-07600137.summary: This inscription records the names of Pompeius Adiutor and his freedwoman Prima, possibly indicating ownership or a shared burial space.

EDCS-07600141.text_edition: \n Publius Vesonius |(mulieris) libertus Phileros Augustalis vivos monumentum fecit sibi et suis Vesoniae Publi fliae patronae et Marco Orfellio Marci filio Fausto amico\n
EDCS-07600141.translation: Publius Vesonius Phileros, freedman of a woman of the Vesonian family, made this monument while alive for himself and his relatives, for Vesonia, daughter of Publius, his patroness, and for Marcus Orfellius Faustus, son of Marcus, his friend.
EDCS-07600141.summary: This is a self-funded funerary monument built by Publius Vesonius Phileros, a freedman, for himself, his family, his patroness, and a friend, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-08000853.text_edition: \n Lucius Marius Hermeros actum Puteolis VIII Kalendas Novembres Cnaeo Hosidio Geta Lucio Vagellio consulibus Vadimonium factum Lucio Mario Hermeroti in I Idus Novembres primas Puteolis in foro ante aram Augusti Hordionianam hora tertia HS |(mille) et HS |(quingentos) et HS dari stipulatus est Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus spopondit Lucius Marius Hermeros actum Puteolis VIII Kalendas Novembres Cnaeo Hosidio Geta Lucio Vagellio consulibus Vadimonium factum Novembras sic primas Puteolis in foro ante aram Augusti Hordionianam hora tertia spopondit Marius Hermeros Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus stipulatus est\n
EDCS-08000853.translation: An undertaking was made in Puteoli on the eighth day before the Kalends of November, with Gnaeus Hosidius Geta and Lucius Vagellius as consuls. An undertaking was made for Lucius Marius Hermeros on the first day before the Ides of November, at Puteoli in the forum, before the Hordionian altar of Augustus, at the third hour. Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus stipulated that HS 1,500 were to be given. Lucius Marius Hermeros pledged. An undertaking was made in Puteoli on the eighth day before the Kalends of November, with Gnaeus Hosidius Geta and Lucius Vagellius as consuls. An undertaking was made for the first day of November, at Puteoli in the forum, before the Hordionian altar of Augustus, at the third hour. Marius Hermeros pledged that HS 1,500 were to be given. Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus stipulated.
EDCS-08000853.summary: This inscription records a legal agreement (vadimonium) concerning a financial transaction of HS 1,500, taking place in Puteoli during the consulship of Gnaeus Hosidius Geta and Lucius Vagellius, likely related to a property sale or debt.

EDCS-08000855.text_edition: \n Vadimonium factum Caio Sulpicio Fausto in VIII Kalendas Iulias primas Puteolis in foro ante aram Hordionianam hora tertia HS D acturus ex empto dari stipulatus est Lucius Faenius Eumenes spopondit Caius Sulpicius Faustus\n
EDCS-08000855.translation: An undertaking was made for Gaius Sulpicius Faustus on the first day before the Kalends of July, at Puteoli in the forum, before the Hordionian altar, at the third hour. Lucius Faenius Eumenes pledged that 500 sesterces were to be given for a purchase. Gaius Sulpicius Faustus stipulated.
EDCS-08000855.summary: This inscription details a legal undertaking (vadimonium) for 500 sesterces related to a purchase, made in Puteoli before the Hordionian altar during the consulship of Lucius Vitellius and Lucius Messalla Poblicola.

EDCS-08000856.text_edition: \n Vadimonium factum Caio Sulpicio Fausto III Nonas Iulias Puteolis in foro ante aram Hordionianam hora tertia HS MMMMMquinquaginta milia maioris summae rem in iudicium deducturus et HS Mmillle depositi anuli arrae nomine Vadimonium factum Caio Sulpicio Fausto in III Nonas Iulias primas Puteolis in foro ante aram Augustam Hordionianam hora tertia HS MMMMMquinquaginta milia maioris summae rei in iudicium deducturus et HS Imille depositi anuli arrae nomine stipulatus est Lucius Faenius Eumenes spopondit Caius Sulpicius Faustus actum Puteolis V Nonas Iulias Lucio Vitellio Lucio Messalla Poblicola consulibus\n
EDCS-08000856.translation: An undertaking was made for Gaius Sulpicius Faustus on the third day before the Nones of July, at Puteoli in the forum, before the Hordionian altar of Augustus, at the third hour. Gaius Sulpicius Faustus pledged 50,000 sesterces of the larger sum to be brought to trial, and 1,000 sesterces for a ring as a deposit. An undertaking was made for Gaius Sulpicius Faustus on the first day before the third Nones of July, at Puteoli in the forum, before the Hordionian altar of Augustus, at the third hour. Gaius Sulpicius Faustus pledged 50,000 sesterces of the larger sum to be brought to trial, and 1,000 sesterces for a ring as a deposit. Lucius Faenius Eumenes stipulated. Made in Puteoli on the fifth day before the Nones of July, with Lucius Vitellius and Lucius Messalla Poblicola as consuls.
EDCS-08000856.summary: This inscription records a complex legal undertaking (vadimonium) involving a large sum of 50,000 sesterces and a deposit of 1,000 sesterces for a ring, occurring in Puteoli during the consulship of Lucius Vitellius and Lucius Messalla Poblicola.

EDCS-08000858.text_edition: \n Vadimonium factum Quinto Baebio Eroti in V Idus Puteolis in foro ante aram Augusti Hordionianam hora tertia HS I et HS MMMMM (quinque milia) dari stipulatus est Caius Sulpicius Faustus spopondit Quintus Baebius\n
EDCS-08000858.translation: An undertaking was made for Quintus Baebius Eros on the fifth day before the Ides of [August?], at Puteoli in the forum, before the Hordionian altar of Augustus, at the third hour. Caius Sulpicius Faustus stipulated that 1 sestertius and 5,000 sesterces were to be given. Quintus Baebius pledged.
EDCS-08000858.summary: This inscription records a legal undertaking (vadimonium) involving sums of 1 sestertius and 5,000 sesterces, made in Puteoli before the Hordionian altar of Augustus, with Caius Sulpicius Faustus as stipulator and Quintus Baebius as promisor.

EDCS-08000859.text_edition: \n Vadimonium factum Quinto Acilio Leon in pridie Idus Septembres primas Puteolis in foro ante aram Augusti Hordionianam HS MMMMMquinquaginta milia sic dari stipulatus est Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus spopondit Quintus Acilius Leon \n
EDCS-08000859.translation: A legal undertaking was made with Quintus Acilius Leon for the first day before the Ides of September in Puteoli, in the forum, before the Hordionian altar of Augustus, that Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus would pay 50,000 sesterces; Quintus Acilius Leon pledged his word.
EDCS-08000859.summary: This inscription records a financial undertaking (vadimonium) involving Quintus Acilius Leon and Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus, likely from the late Republican or early Imperial period, concerning a sum of money.

EDCS-08000861.text_edition: \n Vadimonium factum Lucio Mario Floro in Idus Ianuarias primas Puteolis in foro ante aram Augusti Hordionianam hora tertia HS DCCCXL et HS DCLX dari stipulatus est Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus spopondit Lucius Marius Florus\n
EDCS-08000861.translation: A legal undertaking was made with Lucius Marius Florus for the first day of the Ides of January in Puteoli, in the forum, before the Hordionian altar of Augustus, at the third hour, that Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus would pay 840 and 660 sesterces; Lucius Marius Florus pledged his word.
EDCS-08000861.summary: This inscription details a financial undertaking (vadimonium) between Lucius Marius Florus and Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus, involving two sums of sesterces, dated to the early Imperial period.

EDCS-08000862.text_edition: \n Vadimonium factum Caio Vitrasio Muro in VI Kalendas Iunias primas Puteolis in foro ante aram Augusti Suettianam hora tertia HS stipulatus est Caius Sulpicius Onirus\n
EDCS-08000862.translation: A legal undertaking was made with Gaius Vitrasius Murus for the first day before the Kalends of June in Puteoli, in the forum, before the Suettian altar of Augustus, at the third hour, for a sum of sesterces; Caius Sulpicius Onirus pledged his word.
EDCS-08000862.summary: This inscription records a financial undertaking (vadimonium) involving Gaius Vitrasius Murus and Caius Sulpicius Onirus, concerning a sum of money in Puteoli, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-08000864.text_edition: \n Vadimonium factum Sexto Lucilio Caerulo in IIII Nonas Iunias primas Puteolis in foro ante aram Augusti \n
EDCS-08000864.translation: A legal undertaking was made with Sextus Lucilius Caerulus for the first day before the Nones of June in Puteoli, in the forum, before the altar of Augustus.
EDCS-08000864.summary: This inscription records a legal undertaking (vadimonium) involving Sextus Lucilius Caerulus in Puteoli, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-08000865.text_edition: \n A in basilica ante curiam hora prima HS MM dari stipulatus est Lucius Lucretius Firmus miles cohortis XIII |(cohortis) Marci Salvi Firmi spopondit Lucius Aelius Repentinus Actum Capuae IIII Kalendas Septembres Tito Axio Tito Mussidio Polliano consulibus\n
EDCS-08000865.translation: In the basilica, before the curia, at the first hour, Lucius Lucretius Firmus, a soldier of the 13th cohort of Marcus Salvius Firmus, pledged that Lucius Aelius Repentinus would pay 2,000 sesterces. Acted in Capua on the fourth day before the Kalends of September, when Titus Axious and Titus Mussidius Pollianus were consuls.
EDCS-08000865.summary: This is a legal pledge (sponsio) made by a soldier, Lucius Lucretius Firmus, concerning a sum of money to be paid by Lucius Aelius Repentinus, dated by consuls to the reign of Augustus or Tiberius.

EDCS-08000866.text_edition: \n Vadimonium factum Truphoni Potamonis filio Alexandrino in pridie Kalendas Octobres primas Romae in foro Augusto ante statuam Cnaei Senti Saturnini triumphalem hora tertia HS |(mille) dari fide rogavit Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus fide promisit Trupho Potamonis filio sic Alexandrinus actum consulibus\n
EDCS-08000866.translation: A legal undertaking was made with Trupho, son of Potamon, an Alexandrian, for the first day before the Kalends of October in the Forum of Augustus in Rome, before the triumphal statue of Gnaeus Sentius Saturninus, at the third hour, that Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus would pay 1,000 sesterces on behalf of Trupho, son of Potamon, the Alexandrian. Acted under the consulship of [consul names implied].
EDCS-08000866.summary: This inscription records a financial undertaking (vadimonium) in Rome, involving Trupho the Alexandrian and Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus, related to a sum of money and a statue, dated by consuls to the reign of Augustus or Tiberius.

EDCS-08000870.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Caesare Augusto V Tito Flavio Vespasiano consulibus Puteolis in foro ante aram Augusti Hordionianam Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus hora tertia stetit\n
EDCS-08000870.translation: In Puteoli, in the forum, before the Hordionian altar of Augustus, Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus stood at the third hour, during the consulship of Emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus V and Titus Flavius Vespasian.
EDCS-08000870.summary: This inscription records the appearance of Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus in Puteoli before the altar of Augustus, dated by consuls to the early Julio-Claudian period.

EDCS-08000871.text_edition: \n Puteolis in foro ante aram Augusti Suettianam Caius Sulpicius Onirus hora secunda stetit quod diceret se cum Tiberio Claudio Cadmo vadimonium habere actum\n
EDCS-08000871.translation: In Puteoli, in the forum, before the Suettian altar of Augustus, Caius Sulpicius Onirus appeared at the second hour, stating that he had a legal undertaking (vadimonium) with Tiberius Claudius Cadmus. Acted.
EDCS-08000871.summary: This inscription records Caius Sulpicius Onirus appearing in Puteoli before the Suettian altar of Augustus to declare a legal undertaking (vadimonium) with Tiberius Claudius Cadmus, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-08000874.text_edition: \n Stetit quod habere se vadimonium diceret cum Caio Stlaccio Curiato actum\n
EDCS-08000874.translation: He appeared, stating that he had a legal undertaking (vadimonium) with Gaius Stlaccius Curiatus. Acted.
EDCS-08000874.summary: This inscription records a person appearing to declare a legal undertaking (vadimonium) with Gaius Stlaccius Curiatus, date uncertain but likely Roman Republican or Imperial.

EDCS-08000876.text_edition: \n Heredem esse in iure aput sic Lucium Granium Probum IIvirum Caius Sulpicius Faustus interrogavit Aulum Castricium Onesimum essetne Aulo Castricio Isochryso heres et quota ex parte Aulus Castricius Onesimus respondit se ab Aulo Castricio Isochryso ex parte XII et ex parte XXIIII partis quintae heredem esse III Kalendas Maias Caio Cestio Marco Servilio Noniano consulibus Aulus Marci Aulus Duroni Amic Cnaeus A Felicis Aulus Felicis Caius Sulpicius Faustus \n
EDCS-08000876.translation: Caius Sulpicius Faustus asked Aulus Castricius Onesimus, while he was in court before the duovir Lucius Granius Probus, whether he was the heir of Aulus Castricius Isochrysus and in what part. Aulus Castricius Onesimus responded that he was the heir of Aulus Castricius Isochrysus by 1/12 and 24/24 parts of a fifth part. Acted on the third day before the Kalends of May, when Gaius Cestius and Marcus Servilius Nonianus were consuls. [Names of witnesses follow].
EDCS-08000876.summary: This inscription details a legal proceeding concerning inheritance, where Aulus Castricius Onesimus declares his heirship to Aulus Castricius Isochrysus, dated by consuls to the late Republican period.

EDCS-08000877.text_edition: \n Interrogavit Aulum Castricium ex <qu=C>ota parte heres esset Caius Sulpicius in iure aput sic Quintum Puteolanum Aquilam iudicem adiectum Caius Sulpicius Faustus interrogavit Aulum Castricium Hermerotem Hordionianum essetne heres aut bonorum possessor Auli Castrici Isocrusi sic et quota ex parte Aulus Castricius Hermeros Hordionianus respondit se Auli Castrici Isochrysi ex X parte heredem esse sor media actum Puteolis IIII Kalendas Maias Caio Cestio Marco Servilio Noniano consulibus \n
EDCS-08000877.translation: In court before the judge Quintus Puteolanus Aquila, Gaius Sulpicius Faustus questioned Aulus Castricius Hermerotes Hordionianus, asking if he was the heir or possessor of the goods of Aulus Castricius Isocrusus, and to what extent. Aulus Castricius Hermeros Hordionianus replied that he was the heir of Aulus Castricius Isocrusus to the extent of one-tenth. The proceedings were held in Puteoli on the 4th day before the Kalends of May, when Gaius Cestius and Marcus Servilius Nonianus were consuls.
EDCS-08000877.summary: This is a legal record from Puteoli detailing a procedural question regarding inheritance before a judge, dated to 47 AD.

EDCS-08000878.text_edition: \n In iure aput sic Lucium Clodium Rufum duumvirum Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus interrogavit Caium Iulium Prudentem essentne homines Hyginus et Hermes servi eius et in potestate eius Caius Iulius Prudens respondit homines Hyginum et Hermen quibus de ageretur suos suaque in potestate esse ibique Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus denuntiavit Caio Iulio Prudenti uti Hyginum et Hermen quibus de ageretur in potestate sua haberet eosque Romae exhiberet quod diceret ad cognitionem iudicis qui inter se et eum futurus esset de credito suo pertinere desideranti Caio Iulio Prudenti verba edita sunt actum Putiolis sic Nonis Februariis Nerone Claudio Caesare Augusto Germanico Lucio Antistio Vetere consulibus\n
EDCS-08000878.translation: In court before the duumvir Lucius Clodius Rufus, Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus questioned Gaius Julius Prudentes, asking if the men Hyginus and Hermes were his slaves and under his power. Gaius Julius Prudentes replied that Hyginus and Hermes, who were the subject of the action, were his and under his power. Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus then warned Gaius Julius Prudentes to keep Hyginus and Hermes, who were the subject of the action, under his power and to produce them in Rome, as he stated this pertained to the judgment of the judge who would be between them concerning his claim, and the words were issued to the requesting Gaius Julius Prudentes. The proceedings were held in Puteoli on the Nones of February, when Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus and Lucius Antistius Vetus were consuls.
EDCS-08000878.summary: This is a legal record from Puteoli concerning a dispute about the ownership and possession of two individuals, Hyginus and Hermes, as slaves, dated to 40 AD.

EDCS-08000879.text_edition: \n Lucius Lucretius Firmus denuntiavit Lucio Aelio Repentino uti puerum Felicem servum in potestate sua haberet actum Capuae VI Kalendas Septembres Tito Axio Tito Mussidio Polliano consulibus\n
EDCS-08000879.translation: Lucius Lucretius Firmus warned Lucius Aelius Repentinus to keep the boy Felix, his slave, under his power. The proceedings were held in Capua on the 6th day before the Kalends of September, when Titus Axies and Titus Mussidius Pollianus were consuls.
EDCS-08000879.summary: This is a legal record from Capua concerning a warning related to the possession of a slave, dated to 44 AD.

EDCS-08000880.text_edition: \n Lucio Vitellio filio Messala Vipstano Gallo consulibus pridie Nonas Septembres Lucius Faenius Eumenes scripsi mihi convenisse cum Caio Sulpicio Fausto finiendae controversiae caussa sic quae mihi cum eo est qua de re ultro citro cognitores ab nobis dati sunt me id vadimonium quod Sulpici cognitor cum cognitore meo Romae haberet remittere et quod is Caius Sulpicius Faustus eo nomine iudicatum solvi satis dedit acceptam stipulationem et facere ideo si Tiberius Iulius Sporus vadimonium quod ei cum Lucio Faenio Thallo cognitore meo Kalendis Novembribus primis Romae in foro Augusto ante statuam Cnaei Senti Saturnini triumphalem hkora tertia HS MMMMMquinque milia est deseruerit neminem ex ea caus cum Tiberio Iulio Sporo aut cum sponsore eius acturum aut a quo eorum petiturum exacturumque quid esse si adversus ea factum erit quanti ea res erit tantam pecuniam dare stipulatus est Caius Sulpicius Faustus spopondi Lucius Faenius Eumenes quodque Caius Sulpicius Faustus eo nomine quo de agitur iudicatum solvi mihi satis dedit interrogante eo me ei acceptam stipulationem facere actum Puteolis\n
EDCS-08000880.translation: During the consulship of Lucius Vitellius the son and Messala Vipstanus Gallus, on the day before the Nones of September, I, Lucius Faenius Eumenes, wrote that I had reached an agreement with Gaius Sulpicius Faustus for the purpose of settling a dispute that I have with him, concerning which arbitrators were appointed by us. I have agreed to remit the security bond that Sulpicius's arbitrator had with my arbitrator in Rome, and also that Gaius Sulpicius Faustus had provided security for the judgment in that name, and to accept the stipulation. Therefore, if Tiberius Julius Sporus should abandon the security bond which he has with my arbitrator Lucius Faenius Thallus on the Kalends of November next, in the Campus Martius, before the statue of Gnaeus Sentius Saturninus the triumphant general, at the third hour, for 5,000 sesterces, I shall pay a sum of money equivalent to the value of that matter, to be given if action is taken against Tiberius Julius Sporus or his guarantor. Gaius Sulpicius Faustus stipulated this, and I, Lucius Faenius Eumenes, pledged it. And since Gaius Sulpicius Faustus provided me with security for the judgment in that name, concerning which we are discussing, I agreed to accept the stipulation. The proceedings were held in Puteoli.
EDCS-08000880.summary: This is a legal document from Puteoli detailing a settlement of a dispute involving a security bond and a stipulation, dated to 52 AD.

EDCS-08000883.text_edition: \n Ea res agetur de sponsione Caius Blossius Celadus iudex esto si parret Caium Marcium Saturninum Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo HS MMMMMMsex milia nummum dare oportere qua de re agitur Caius Blossius Celadus iudex Caium Marcium Saturninum HS MMMMMMsex milia nummum Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo condemnato si non parret apsolvito sic Caius Blossius Celadus iudex esto si parret Caium Marcium Saturninum Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo HS MMMMMMMMocto milia nummum dare oportere qua de re agitur Caius Blossius Celadus iudex Caium Marcium Saturninum HS MMMMMMMMocto milia nummum Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo condemnato si non parret apsolvito sic iudicare iussit Publius Cossinius Priscus IIvir actum Puteolis Fausto Cornelio Sulla Felice Quinto Marcio Barea Sorano consulibus\n
EDCS-08000883.translation: Let Caius Blossius Celadus be the judge in this case concerning the wager. If it appears that Caius Marcius Saturninus ought to pay Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus 600,000 sesterces, concerning which the case is being heard, let Caius Blossius Celadus, the judge, condemn Caius Marcius Saturninus to pay 600,000 sesterces to Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus. If it does not appear, let him acquit him. Let Caius Blossius Celadus be the judge in this case. If it appears that Caius Marcius Saturninus ought to pay Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus 800,000 sesterces, concerning which the case is being heard, let Caius Blossius Celadus, the judge, condemn Caius Marcius Saturninus to pay 800,000 sesterces to Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus. If it does not appear, let him acquit him. Publius Cossinius Priscus, duumvir, ordered the judgment to be issued. The proceedings were held in Puteoli on the Kalends of April, when Faustus Cornelius Sulla Felix and Quintus Marcius Barea Soranus were consuls.
EDCS-08000883.summary: This is a legal judgment from Puteoli concerning two separate wager disputes between Caius Marcius Saturninus and Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus, with Caius Blossius Celadus as judge, dated to 52 AD.

EDCS-08000885.text_edition: \n Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus in tertium sumpsit cum Quinto Laberio Philippo quibus de rebus inter se et eum Quintus Laberius Cerdo Maior iudex esse diceretur atque ex die perendino iudicare\n
EDCS-08000885.translation: Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus took action for the third time with Quintus Laberius Philippus, concerning matters between them and him, when Quintus Laberius Cerdo Maior was to be the judge and was to judge from the day after tomorrow.
EDCS-08000885.summary: This is a legal record concerning a procedural matter involving a judge in a dispute between Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus and Quintus Laberius Philippus, dated to the early imperial period.

EDCS-08000886.text_edition: \n ssidentes patroni omnes testimonia deferre omnem Barbati Felix Carus curatores denaria sic quaedam sic\n
EDCS-08000886.translation: All the seated patrons should bring forward testimony concerning all the matters. Felix Carus, curators, concerning the denarii, thus some things thus.
EDCS-08000886.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, possibly related to legal or administrative proceedings, mentioning curators and testimony, with an uncertain date and location.

EDCS-08000888.text_edition: \n Marcus Barbatus Epaphroditus arbiter ex compromisso inter Caium Sulpicium Cinnamum et Caium Iulium Prudentem coram utroque praesente diem is ad audiendum dedit adesseque eos iussit Idibus Martiis primis Puteolis in chalcidico Hordioniano ab hora tertia in horam quartam actum Puteolis XVIIII Kalendas Ianuarias Caio Lentulo Gaetulico Tito Curtilio Mancia consulibus\n
EDCS-08000888.translation: Marcus Barbatus Epaphroditus, as arbitrator by agreement between Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus and Gaius Julius Prudentes, in the presence of both, set a day for hearing and ordered them to appear on the first Ides of March, in Puteoli, in the Hordionian portico, from the third to the fourth hour. The proceedings were held in Puteoli on the 19th day before the Kalends of January, when Gaius Lentulus Gaetulicus and Titus Curtilius Mancia were consuls.
EDCS-08000888.summary: This is a legal record from Puteoli establishing a date for arbitration between Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus and Gaius Julius Prudentes, dated to 42 AD.

EDCS-08000892.text_edition: \n Caius Sulpicius Faustus testatus est se mulierem Tychen qua de agitur quam apud se Pactumeia Prima et Aulus Attiolenus Atimetus deposuissent exhibuisse pridie Nonas Maias Puteolis in foro ante aram Augusti Hordionianam hora tertia utrisque praesentibus eamque mulierem iussu Aulus Attioleni Atimeti Pactumeiam Primam duxisse actum Fausto Cornelio Sulla Felice Lucio Salvio Othone Titiano consulibus Puteolis in foro ante aram Augusti Hordionianam hora tertia utrisque praesentibus eamque mulierem iussu Auli Attioleni Atimeti Pactumeiam Primam duxisse actum Fausto Cornelio Sulla Felice Lucio Salvio Othone Titiano consulibus\n
EDCS-08000892.translation: Caius Sulpicius Faustus testified that he had produced the woman Tyche, who is the subject of the action, whom Pactumeia Prima and Aulus Attiolenus Atimetus had entrusted to him, on the day before the Nones of May, in Puteoli, in the forum, before the Hordionian altar of Augustus, at the third hour, with both parties present. And that woman, by order of Aulus Attiolenus Atimetus, was taken by Pactumeia Prima. The proceedings were held in Puteoli, in the forum, before the Hordionian altar of Augustus, at the third hour, with both parties present. And that woman, by order of Aulus Attiolenus Atimetus, was taken by Pactumeia Prima. The proceedings were held in Puteoli, in the forum, before the Hordionian altar of Augustus, at the third hour, with both parties present. The proceedings were held in Puteoli when Faustus Cornelius Sulla Felix and Lucius Salvius Othon Titianus were consuls.
EDCS-08000892.summary: This is a legal record from Puteoli where Caius Sulpicius Faustus testifies to producing a woman named Tyche, who was entrusted to him, and her subsequent disposition, dated to 58 AD.

EDCS-08000895.text_edition: \n solutum esse fugitivom erronem non esse et cetera ex edicto aedilis curulis quae scripta conprehensaque sunt recte praestari et duplam pecuniam ex formula ita uti adsolet recte dari stipulatus est Titus Vestorius Arpocra minor spopondit Titus Vestorius Phoenix actum Puteolis XII Kalendas Septembres Servio Asinio Sexto Nonio consulibus Cai Iuli Cai filii Faleria Senecionis Cai Munni Cai filii Rufi Auli Fufici Donati Luci Ponti Philadelphi Titi Vestori Phoenicis Cai Pacci Felicis Cai Claudi Cai Matei Primogeni Cai Suetti Damae DOM\n
EDCS-08000895.translation: It has been paid, he is not a runaway slave, and other matters are properly presented from the decree of the curule aedile, and the double penalty is to be properly paid according to the formula, as is customary. Titus Vestorius Arpocra the younger stipulated, Titus Vestorius Phoenix pledged. The proceedings were held in Puteoli on the 12th day before the Kalends of September, when Servius Asinius Sextus and Nonius were consuls. Witnesses: Gaius Julius, son of Caius, of the Falernian tribe, Senecio; Caius Munnius, son of Caius, Rufus; Aulus Fuficius Donatus; Lucius Pontius Philadelphus; Titus Vestorius Phoenix; Gaius Paccius Felix; Gaius Claudius, son of Caius, Primogenes; Caius Suettius Dama. DOM.
EDCS-08000895.summary: This is a legal record from Puteoli concerning a payment and a stipulation regarding a runaway slave, dated to 40 AD.

EDCS-08000897.text_edition: \n Caio Caesare Germanico Augusto Tiberio Claudio Nerone Germanico consulibus VI Nonas Iulias Diognetus Cai Novi Cypaeri servus scripsi iussu Cypaeri domini mei coram ipsum me locasse Hesico Tiberi Iulii Augusti liberti PR Eueni servo horreum XII in horreis Bassianis publicis Puteolanorum medis in quo repositum est triticum Alexandrinium quod pignori accepit hodie ab Caio Novio Euno item in isdem horreis imis intercolumnia ube sic repositos habet saccos legumenum sic ducentos quos pignori accepit ab aeodem sic Eunum ex Kalendis Iulis in menses singulos sestertis singulis nummis actum Puteolis Cai Novii Cypaeri Acui Auli Fal Iuli Diogneti Cai Novii Cypaeri servi Cai Novii Cypaeri liberti i ni Cai Iuli Senecionis Cai Novii Cipaeri sic servi Caio Caesare Germanico Augusto Tiberio Claudio Nerone Germanico cons(ulibus) sextum Nonas Iulias Diognetus Cai Novi Cypaeri servus scripsi iussu Cypaeri domini mei coram ipso me locasse Hesycho Tiberi Iuli Augusti liberti Eueni servo horreum duodecimum in horreis Bassianis publicis Puteolanorum medis in quo repositum est triticum Alexandrinum quod pignori accepit hodie a Caio Novio Euno item in isdem horreis imis intercolumnia ubi repositos habet saccos leguminum ducentos quos pignori accepit ab eodem Euno ex Kalendis Iulis in menses singulos sestertiis singulis nummis actum Puteolis chirographum tritici locationis horrei XII in Bassianis a Caio Novio Euno\n
EDCS-08000897.translation: On the sixth day before the Nones of July, when Gaius Caesar Germanicus Augustus and Tiberius Claudius Nero Germanicus were consuls, I, Diognetus, slave of Gaius Novius Cypaerus, have written, by order of my master Cypaerus, that I myself have leased to Hesychus, slave of Tiberius Julius Augustus' freedman Euenus, the twelfth storehouse in the public Bassian warehouses of Puteoli, in the middle section, in which is stored Alexandrian wheat which he today took as pledge from Gaius Novius Euno, and likewise in the same lower warehouses, in the spaces between columns, where he has stored two hundred sacks of legumes, which he took as pledge from the same Euno from the Kalends of July for each month at one sestertius. Done at Puteoli. Attested by Gaius Novius Cypaerus, son of Acuius, son of Aulus, son of Gaius Julius Senecio, slave of Gaius Novius Cypaerus, freedman of Gaius Julius Senecio, slave of Gaius Novius Cypaerus. On the sixth day before the Nones of July, when Gaius Caesar Germanicus Augustus and Tiberius Claudius Nero Germanicus were consuls, I, Diognetus, slave of Gaius Novius Cypaerus, have written, by order of my master Cypaerus, that I myself have leased to Hesychus, slave of Tiberius Julius Augustus' freedman Euenus, the twelfth storehouse in the public Bassian warehouses of Puteoli, in the middle section, in which is stored Alexandrian wheat which he today took as pledge from Gaius Novius Euno, and likewise in the same lower warehouses, in the spaces between columns, where he has stored two hundred sacks of legumes, which he took as pledge from the same Euno from the Kalends of July for each month at one sestertius. This is a record of the lease of the twelfth storehouse in the Bassian warehouses by Gaius Novius Euno.
EDCS-08000897.summary: This is a record of a lease agreement for a storehouse in Puteoli, dated to 41 CE, detailing the storage of Alexandrian wheat and legumes as pledges.

EDCS-08000899.text_edition: \n actum Cumis parti Campi Duroni XXIIII\n
EDCS-08000899.translation: Done at Cumae, share of Campi Duroni, 24.
EDCS-08000899.summary: This is a brief record of a transaction or division occurring in Cumae, related to an area or property called Campi Duroni.

EDCS-08000900.text_edition: \n Aulo Vitellio Lucio Vipstano Poplicola consulibus Caius Iulius Prudens scripsi me rogasse Caium Sulpicium Cinnamum sive mandasse eis quantam pecuniam is aut Eros aut aut si quidam eius aut Martialis servus eius aut Caius Sulpicius Faustus aliusve quis iussu rogatu mandatuve cuius eorum semel saepiusve sua in iure meo aut Higini frater aliive cui iussu cuius eorum mandatum dedisset aut pro quo eorum promisisset spopondisset iure ut sua esse iussisset aliove quo nomine obligatus esset quantaque ea pecunia erit quae ita data credita et cuiusque pecuniae obligatio quoque nomine ita uti supra comprehensum est facta erit tantam pecuniam dari dolumque malum ei rei promissionique abesse afuturumque esse a me heredeque meo et aliis omnibus ad quos ea res qua de re agitur pertinet cui rei ita dolus malus non adest non aderit quanti ea res erit tantam pecuniam dari haec sic recte dari plurium stipulatus est Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus spopondit Caius Iulius Prudens actum Puteolis\n
EDCS-08000900.translation: When Aulus Vitellius and Lucius Vipstanus Poplicola were consuls, I, Gaius Julius Prudens, have written that I asked Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, or entrusted to him, what amount of money he, or Eros, or someone belonging to him, or his slave Martial, or Gaius Sulpicius Faustus, or anyone else at the command or request of any of them, once or more often, in my own court or that of Higinus my brother, or at the command of any of them, had given or promised for someone, or had promised on behalf of someone, by law, or by whatever other name he was obligated, and whatever that amount of money will be, and by whatever name the obligation of that money is made, as is set forth above, that amount of money will be given, and that I, and my heir, and all others to whom this matter pertains, will be free from fraud and deceit regarding this matter, and will be free from fraud and deceit regarding the promise, to the extent of the value of the matter. Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus stipulated for this to be correctly done, and Gaius Julius Prudens promised. Done at Puteoli.
EDCS-08000900.summary: This document, dated to 48 CE, records Gaius Julius Prudens' formal acknowledgment and assumption of financial obligations previously incurred by Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus or his associates, ensuring indemnification against fraud.

EDCS-08000903.text_edition: \n Cnaeo Acceronio sic Proculo Caio Petronio Pontio consulibus IV Kalendas Iulias Caius Novius Eunus scripssi sic me accepisse ob mutua ab Eueno Tiberi Caessaris sic Augusti luberto sic Primiano apssente sic per Hessucus sic servum eius et debere ei sestertia decem milia nummum quae ei reddam cum petiaerit sic et ea sestertia decem milia quae supra scripta sunt probe recte dari stipulatus est est Hessucus sic Eueni Tiberi Caessaris sic Augusti liberti Primiani servus sp<o=E>pondi ego Caius Novius Eunus pro quem iis sestertiis decem milibus nummum dedi pignoris arrabonis V nomine tridici Alexandrini modium septem milia plus minus et ciceris faris monocopi lentis in saccis ducentis modium quattuor milia plus minus quae ominia possita habeo penes me in horreis Bassianis puplicis sic Puteolanorum quae ab omini vi periculo meo est dico fateor actum Putiolis sic \n
EDCS-08000903.translation: On the fourth day before the Kalends of July, when Gnaeus Acceronius Proculus and Gaius Petronius Pontius were consuls, I, Gaius Novius Eunus, have written that I received and owe Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus' freedman Primianus, in the absence of his slave Hessuchus, ten thousand sesterces, which I will repay him when he asks, and these ten thousand sesterces, written above, are correctly and properly given. Hessuchus, slave of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus' freedman Primianus, stipulated. I, Gaius Novius Eunus, for whom I gave seven thousand measures, more or less, of Alexandrian wheat and four thousand measures, more or less, of chickpeas, spelt, and lentils in two hundred sacks as a pledge and down payment for these ten thousand sesterces, all of which I possess in my storehouses in the public Bassian warehouses of Puteoli, and which are at my own risk from all violence and danger, I admit. Done at Puteoli.
EDCS-08000903.summary: This inscription from 40 CE records a loan of 10,000 sesterces from Gaius Novius Eunus to Primianus, a freedman of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus, with a pledge of wheat and legumes.

EDCS-08000904.text_edition: \n Caio Caessasare Germanico Augusto Tiberio Claudio Germanico consulibus VI Nonas Iulias Caius Novius Eunus scripssi me accepisse mutua ab Hessco sic Eunni sic Tiberi Caessaris Augusti liberti Primiani servo mutua et debere ei sestertia tria milia nummum praeter HS X nummum quae alio chirographo meo eidem debo et ea sestertia tria milia numerata nummum quae supra scripta sunt probe recete dari stipulatus ets sic Hessucus sic Euni Tiberi Caessaris Augusti liberti Primiani servus sp<o=E>pondi ego Caius Novius Eunus in qua ominis summa dedi ei pignoris tridici Alexandrini modium septem milia quot sic est possitum in horreis Bassianis puplicis sic Puteolanorum mediis horre<is=SI> quod facit et saccos ducentos lentis ciceris farris monocoxpi et cados in quibus sunt modium quattuor milia qui sunt possiti in isdem horreis quae ominia ab omini vi periculo meo est fateor actum Puteolis chirographum Cai Novii Eueni HS MMMtria milia mutua praeter alia HS X ob pignus tritici \n
EDCS-08000904.translation: On the sixth day before the Nones of July, when Gaius Caesar Germanicus Augustus and Tiberius Claudius Germanicus were consuls, I, Gaius Novius Eunus, have written that I received from Hessuchus, slave of Tiberius Caesar Augustus' freedman Primianus, a loan of three thousand sesterces, in addition to 10 sesterces which I already owe him by another document, and these three thousand sesterces, written above, are correctly and properly given. Hessuchus, slave of Tiberius Caesar Augustus' freedman Primianus, stipulated. I, Gaius Novius Eunus, for which entire sum I gave him as a pledge seven thousand measures of Alexandrian wheat, which is stored in the middle section of the public Bassian warehouses of Puteoli, and two hundred sacks of lentils, chickpeas, and spelt, and jars containing four thousand measures, which are stored in the same warehouses, all of which is at my own risk from all violence and danger, I admit. Done at Puteoli. Document of Gaius Novius Eunus for a loan of 3000 sesterces, in addition to 10 sesterces for the pledge of wheat.
EDCS-08000904.summary: This record from 41 CE details a loan of 3000 sesterces to Gaius Novius Eunus from Hessuchus, slave of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus' freedman Primianus, secured by a pledge of wheat and legumes stored in the Puteoli warehouses.

EDCS-08000905.text_edition: \n Caio Laecanio Basso Quinto Terentio Cullione consulibus III Idus Martias Lucius Marius Didae libertus Iucundus scripsi me accepisse et debere Caio Sulpicio Fausto sestertia viginti millia nummum quae supra scripta sunt quae ab eo mutuo et numerata accepi eaque sestertia viginti millia nummum quae supra scripta sunt proba recte dari stipulatus est Caius Sulpicius Faustus spopondi ego Lucius Marius Iucundus\n
EDCS-08000905.translation: On the third day before the Ides of March, when Gaius Laecanius Bassus and Quintus Terentius Culleo were consuls, I, Lucius Marius Iucundus, freedman of Dida, have written that I have received and owe Gaius Sulpicius Faustus twenty thousand sesterces, which are written above, which I received from him as a loan and in cash, and these twenty thousand sesterces, written above, are to be given correctly and properly. Gaius Sulpicius Faustus stipulated. I, Lucius Marius Iucundus, promised.
EDCS-08000905.summary: This document from 34 CE records Lucius Marius Iucundus acknowledging a loan of 20,000 sesterces from Gaius Sulpicius Faustus.

EDCS-08000906.text_edition: \n Marco Pompeio Silvano Aulo Antonio Rufo consulibus V Nonas Octobres Marcus Lollius Philippus scripsi me accepisse mutuo et debere Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo HS MMviginti millia numQ viginti millia nummum quae supra scripta sunt probe recte dari stipulatus est Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus spopondit Marcus Lollius Philippus actum Puteolis isdem consulibus V Nonas Octobres Gaius Alius Cinnamus scripsi interrogante Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo ea HS MMviginti millia nummum quae supra scripta sunt fide et periculo meo esse iussi pro Marco Lollio Philippo Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo fateor autem et iuravi per Iovem et numen divi Augusti me hoc anno pro eadem nulli ali fide mea esse iussisse actum Puteolis\n
EDCS-08000906.translation: On the fifth day before the Nones of October, when Marcus Pompeius Silvanus and Aulus Antonius Rufus were consuls, I, Marcus Lollius Philippus, have written that I received a loan of and owe Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus 22,000 sesterces, which are written above, to be given correctly and properly. Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus stipulated. Marcus Lollius Philippus promised. Done at Puteoli. In the same consulship, on the fifth day before the Nones of October, I, Gaius Alius Cinnamus, have written, at the request of Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, that I have caused the 22,000 sesterces written above to be at my own responsibility and risk for Marcus Lollius Philippus. I also swear by Jupiter and the divine spirit of Augustus that this year I have not caused it to be at the responsibility of anyone else for the same amount. Done at Puteoli.
EDCS-08000906.summary: This inscription from 54 CE records Marcus Lollius Philippus borrowing 22,000 sesterces from Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, with Gaius Alius Cinnamus acting as a guarantor and swearing he has not guaranteed the same amount for anyone else that year.

EDCS-08000908.text_edition: \n Fausto Cornelio Sulla Felice Lucio Salvio Othone Titiano consulibus Nonis Martis Niceros colonorum coloniae Puteolanae servus arcarius scripsi me accepisse mutuos et debere Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo HS Mmille nummos eosque HS mille nummos qui supra scripti sunt pridie Kalendis Iulis primis probe recte dari fide rogavit Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus fide promisit Niceros colonorum coloniae servus arcarius actum Puteolis chirographum Nicerotis coloniae servi HS Mmille in Kalendis Iulis primis HS Mmille in Kalendas Iulias primas\n
EDCS-08000908.translation: On the Nones of March, when Faustus Cornelius Sulla Felix and Lucius Salvius Otho Titianus were consuls, I, Niceros, slave of the tenants of the colony of Puteoli, have written that I received a loan of and owe Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus 1,000 sesterces, and that these 1,000 sesterces written above are to be given correctly and properly on the day before the first Kalends of July. Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus requested security, Niceros, slave of the tenants of the colony, promised security. Done at Puteoli. Document of Niceros, slave of the colony, for 1,000 sesterces on the first Kalends of July, 1,000 sesterces on the first Kalends of July.
EDCS-08000908.summary: This document from 52 CE records Niceros, a slave representing the tenants of Puteoli, borrowing 1,000 sesterces from Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, with repayment due on July 1st.

EDCS-08000909.text_edition: \n XII Kalendas Maias Publius Urvinus Zosimus scripsi me accepisse mutuo et debere Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo sestertia duo milia nummum praeter sestertia decem duo milia nummum quae ei debeo per chirographum alis tabellis aeque HS duo milia nummum quae supra scripta sunt probe recte dari stipulatus est Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus spopondi Publius Urvinus Zosimus isdem consulibus XII Kalendas Maias Latinus Felix S interrogante Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo HS duo milia nummum quae supra scripta sunt fide et periculo meo esse iussi pro Publio Urvino Zosimo Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo\n
EDCS-08000909.translation: On the twelfth day before the Kalends of May, when [consuls not named] were consuls, I, Publius Urvinus Zosimus, have written that I have received a loan of and owe Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus two thousand sesterces, in addition to twelve thousand sesterces which I already owe him by another document, and these two thousand sesterces written above are to be given correctly and properly. Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus stipulated. I, Publius Urvinus Zosimus, promised. In the same consulship, on the twelfth day before the Kalends of May, I, Latinus Felix, have written, at the request of Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, that the two thousand sesterces written above are to be at my own responsibility and risk for Publius Urvinus Zosimus.
EDCS-08000909.summary: This inscription, dated to an unspecified year before a Kalends of May, records Publius Urvinus Zosimus acknowledging a debt of 2,000 sesterces to Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, in addition to a previous debt of 12,000 sesterces, with Latinus Felix acting as a guarantor.

EDCS-08000910.text_edition: \n praeter HS viginti millia sic nummum iurationem Priscillae Dominae meae eaque HS quattuor millia sic quae supra scripta sunt probe recte dari fide rogavit Caius Sulpicius Faustus probe promisit Pyramus Caesiae Priscillae servus actum Puteolis Priscillae nni sic dominae Caesiae nni sic Marci Valeri Euphemi Alexandri Pyrami Caesiae Priscillae servi\n
EDCS-08000910.translation: In addition to 20,000 sesterces from the oath of my mistress Priscilla, and these 4,000 sesterces which are written above, are to be given correctly and properly. Gaius Sulpicius Faustus requested security, and Pyramus, slave of Caesius Priscilla, promised security. Done at Puteoli. For Priscilla, my mistress, daughter of Caesius, daughter of Marcus Valerius Euphemius Alexander, slave of Pyramus, slave of Caesius Priscilla.
EDCS-08000910.summary: This record from an unspecified date indicates Gaius Sulpicius Faustus requested security for a total of 24,000 sesterces (20,000 from an oath and 4,000 written above), with Pyramus, slave of Priscilla, providing that security.

EDCS-08000911.text_edition: \n videri CE debere Caio Sulpicio Fausto sestertia quattuor millia nummum\n
EDCS-08000911.translation: It seems that Gaius Sulpicius Faustus ought to owe four million sesterces.
EDCS-08000911.summary: This inscription, likely a record of debt, concerns Gaius Sulpicius Faustus and an amount of money owed.

EDCS-08000913.text_edition: \n Solutum Arcae eos HS D nummos qui supra scripta sunt interroganti Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo fide sua esse iussit Epichares Aphrodisi filius Athenaeus pr Euplia Theodo(ri) filia Meliacae Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo est autem ea pecunia praeter alias summas quos Euplia et Epichares debent eidem Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo et Titiniae Antracidi actum Puteolis XIII Kalendas Augustas Sexto Palpellio Histro Lucio Pediano Secundo consulibus Luci Faeni La Cai Iuli Erotis B Cai Nummi Epaphroditi Cai Hosti Maronis Fortunati \n
EDCS-08000913.translation: Epichares, daughter of Aphrodisius of Athens, a freedwoman of Euplia, at the request of Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, ordered on her own responsibility that the sum of 500 sesterces, as written above, be paid. The sum is due to Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus and Titinia Anthracis, in addition to other sums which Euplia and Epichares owe to the same Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus. Done at Puteoli on the 19th of July in the consulship of Sextus Palpellius Hister and Lucius Pedianus Secundus.
EDCS-08000913.summary: This is a legal record from Puteoli, dated to 19 July under consuls Sextus Palpellius Hister and Lucius Pedianus Secundus, detailing a payment of 500 sesterces by Epichares to Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus.

EDCS-08000914.text_edition: \n Eos HS quos supra scripti sunt interrogante Caio Sulpicio Kinnamo Epichares Aphrodisi filius Atheniensis fide sua esse iussit pro Euplia Theodori ab Melo Caio Sulpicio Kinnamo actum Puteolis III Kalendas Apriles Caio Caecina Largo Caio Cestio consulibus\n
EDCS-08000914.translation: At the request of Gaius Sulpicius Kinnamus, Epichares, daughter of Aphrodisius of Athens, ordered on her own responsibility that the sum of money written above be paid for Euplia, daughter of Theodorus from Mela, to Gaius Sulpicius Kinnamus. Done at Puteoli on the 30th of March in the consulship of Gaius Caecina Largus and Gaius Cestius.
EDCS-08000914.summary: This inscription records a debt payment from Epichares to Gaius Sulpicius Kinnamus for Euplia, executed at Puteoli on 30 March during the consulship of Gaius Caecina Largus and Gaius Cestius.

EDCS-08000916.text_edition: \n Sunt interrogante Titinia Basilide fide sua esse iussit Numerius Castricius Agathopus pro Faecia prima actum Puteolis IIII Nonas Februarias Decimo Iunio Silano Torquato Quinto Haterio Antonino consulibus Quinti Entei Cnaei Pompei Celeris Cai Vestori Felicis Publi Vedi Hermerotis Cnaei Entei Blatti Marci Popidi Optati Quinti Atti Hilari Quinti Atti Primigeni Numeri Castrici Agathopedis sic\n
EDCS-08000916.translation: Numerius Castricius Agathopus, at the request of Titinia Basilides, ordered on his own responsibility that it be paid for Faecia Prima. Done at Puteoli on the 2nd of February in the consulship of Decimus Junius Silanus Torquatus and Quintus Haterius Antoninus.
EDCS-08000916.summary: This inscription details a transaction at Puteoli on 2 February during the consulship of Decimus Junius Silanus Torquatus and Quintus Haterius Antoninus, where Numerius Castricius Agathopus acts on behalf of Faecia Prima at the behest of Titinia Basilides.

EDCS-08000919.text_edition: \n Servio Assinio sic Celere Sexto Nonio consulibus IV Kalendas Septemberes sic Caius Novius Eunus scripssi sic me debere Hesuco sic Cai Caessaris sic Augusti Germanici servo Eueniano sestertios mille centum triginta nummos quos ab eo mutuo sumssi sic quos reddam ei ipssi sic aut Caio Sulpicio Fausto cum petiarit sic eosque sestertios mille centum triginta nummos quae supra sripta sunt proba recte dari stipulatus est Hesychus Cai Caessaris sic Augusti Germanici servus Euenianus spepondi sic ego Caius Novius Eunus actum Puteolis\n
EDCS-08000919.translation: Under the consulship of Servius Assinius Celer and Sextus Nonius, on the 29th of August, I, Gaius Novius Eunus, have written that I owe 1130 sesterces to Hesychus, slave of Gaius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, which I borrowed from him. I will pay them to him or to Gaius Sulpicius Faustus when he demands them. Hesychus, slave of Gaius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, has stipulated that the aforesaid 1130 sesterces be properly paid, and I, Gaius Novius Eunus, have guaranteed it. Done at Puteoli.
EDCS-08000919.summary: This is a debt acknowledgment dated 29 August, made at Puteoli under consuls Servius Assinius Celer and Sextus Nonius, where Gaius Novius Eunus owes 1130 sesterces to Hesychus, a slave of Emperor Nero.

EDCS-08000920.text_edition: \n Cnaeo Domitio Afro Aulo Didio Gallo consulibus XVII Kalendas Octobres Caius Novius Eunus scripsi me debere Hesicho Cai Caesaris Augusti Germanici servo sestertios mille ducentos quinquaginta nummos reliquos ratione omni putata quos ab eo mutuos accepi quam summam iuratus promisi me aut ipsi Hesicho aut Caio Sulpicio Fausto redditurum pridie Kalendis Novembribus primis per Iovem Optumum Maxumum et numen divi Augusti et genium Cai Caesaris Augusti quod si ea die non solvero me non solum peiuri teneri sed etiam poeneae sic nomine in dies singulos HS XX nummos obligatem sic iri et eos HS MCCL qui supra scripti sunt probe recte di sic stipulatus est Hesichus Cai Caesaris servus spopondi Caius Novius Eunus actum <Puteolis>\n
