EDCS-08000920.translation: Under the consulship of Gnaeus Domitius Afer and Aulus Didius Gallus, on the 15th of October, I, Gaius Novius Eunus, have written that I owe Hesychus, slave of Gaius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, 1250 sesterces, the remainder after all accounts are settled, which I borrowed from him. I have sworn that I will repay this sum either to Hesychus himself or to Gaius Sulpicius Faustus on the 31st of October, by Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the divine will of Augustus, and the genius of Gaius Caesar Augustus. If I do not pay on that day, I shall be held liable not only for perjury but also for a penalty of 20 sesterces per day. Hesychus, slave of Gaius Caesar, has properly stipulated the aforesaid 1250 sesterces, and I, Gaius Novius Eunus, have guaranteed it. Done at Puteoli.
EDCS-08000920.summary: This record from Puteoli, dated 15 October under consuls Gnaeus Domitius Afer and Aulus Didius Gallus, details a debt of 1250 sesterces owed by Gaius Novius Eunus to Hesychus, a slave of Nero, with penalties for default.

EDCS-08000921.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Caesare Augusto Germanico Quinto Servilio Cornelio Orfito consulibus V Nonas Maias Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus scripsi me debere Phosphoro Tiberi Claudi Caesaris Augusti servo Elpidiano sestertia nonaginta quattuor millia nummum eaque sestertia quattuor nonaginta millia nummum quae supra scripta sunt Idibus Iunis primis proba sic recte dari stipulatus est Phosphorus Tiberi Claudi Caesaris \n
EDCS-08000921.translation: Under the consulship of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus and Quintus Servilius Cornelius Orfitus, on the 3rd of May, I, Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, have written that I owe Phosphoro, slave of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus, 94,000 sesterces. The aforesaid 94,000 sesterces are to be properly paid on the 13th of June. Phosphorus, slave of Tiberius Claudius Caesar, has stipulated.
EDCS-08000921.summary: This inscription from 3 May, during the consulship of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus and Quintus Servilius Cornelius Orfitus, records Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus owing 94,000 sesterces to Phosphorus, a slave of the emperor.

EDCS-08000922.text_edition: \n Quinto Futio Lusio Saturnino Marco Seio Varano consulibus XVI Kalendas Augustas Cnaeus Pompeius Epiri libertus Blastus scripsi me accepisse ab Sexto Granio Numenio sestertia ses\n
EDCS-08000922.translation: Under the consulship of Quintus Futius Lusius Saturninus and Marcus Seius Varana, on the 19th of July, I, Cnaeus Pompeius Epiri, freedman, have written that I received from Sextus Granius Numenius sesterces...
EDCS-08000922.summary: This fragment from 19 July, during the consulship of Quintus Futius Lusius Saturninus and Marcus Seius Varana, records Cnaeus Pompeius Epiri receiving an unspecified amount of sesterces from Sextus Granius Numenius.

EDCS-08000923.text_edition: \n Marco Silano Sulpicio Camerino consulibus X Kalendas Apriles Caius Iulius Amarantus scripsi accepisse ab F Priscilla serva sestertia tria millia sestertia tria millia nummum ex epistula\n
EDCS-08000923.translation: Under the consulship of Marcus Silanus Sulpicius Camerinus, on the 29th of March, I, Gaius Julius Amarantus, have written that I received from Priscilla, a slave, three thousand sesterces, three thousand sesterces, according to a letter.
EDCS-08000923.summary: This inscription from 29 March, during the consulship of Marcus Silanus Sulpicius Camerinus, records Gaius Julius Amarantus receiving 3000 sesterces from Priscilla, a slave, as per a letter.

EDCS-08000924.text_edition: \n Cnaeo Hosidio Geta Caio Volasenna Severo consulibus pridie Kalendas Ianuarias Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus scripsi me accepisse ab Alcimo Cai Epri Valgi servo HS XXX in debitum Fausti patroni mei ex HS MMMMMMM ex qua summa accepi pridie Nonas Decembres HS XXIIX et hac die HS II actum Puteolis\n
EDCS-08000924.translation: Under the consulship of Gnaeus Hosidius Geta and Gaius Volasenna Severus, on the 31st of December, I, Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, have written that I received from Alcimus, slave of Gaius Eprius Valgus, 30 sesterces on account of my patron Faustus, from a total of 30,000 sesterces. From which sum I received on the 24th of December 28 sesterces and on this day 2 sesterces. Done at Puteoli.
EDCS-08000924.summary: This record from Puteoli, dated 31 December under consuls Gnaeus Hosidius Geta and Gaius Volasenna Severus, details Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus receiving partial payments totaling 30 sesterces from Alcimus, a slave, related to a larger debt of 30,000 sesterces.

EDCS-08000925.text_edition: \n Lucio Vitellio filio Messalla Vipstano Gallo consulibus XII Kalendas Septembres Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus scripsi me accepisse ab Gnosto Lolliae Saturninae servo nomine Marci Lolli Philippi sestertia \n
EDCS-08000925.translation: During the consulship of Lucius Vitellius and Messalla Vipstanus Gallus, on the 12th day before the Kalends of September, I, Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, write that I have received from Gnostus, the slave of Lollia Saturnina, in the name of Marcus Lollius Philippus, sesterces [amount missing].
EDCS-08000925.summary: This is a receipt of payment, dated to 69 AD, from Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus to Gnostus, slave of Lollia Saturnina.

EDCS-08000929.text_edition: \n Aulo Vitellio Lucio Vipstano Poblicola consulibus pridie Idus Ianuarias Caius Caesius Quartus scripsi me habere ab Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo sestertia quattuor millia sic trecentos nummos ex redact<a=E> venditum quam summam accepi protinus a Publio Acilio Primo \n
EDCS-08000929.translation: During the consulship of Aulus Vitellius and Lucius Vipstanus Poblicola, on the day before the Ides of January, I, Gaius Caesius Quartus, write that I have from Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus four thousand three hundred sesterces and [unknown number of] nummi, received immediately from the sale of [property], the full sum of which I received straightaway from Publius Acilius Primus.
EDCS-08000929.summary: This document records Gaius Caesius Quartus acknowledging receipt of money from Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, relating to a sale, dated to 69 AD.

EDCS-08000931.text_edition: \n Caio Laecanio Basso Quinto Terentio Cullione consulibus Idibus Mais in praediis Lepidianis primis Barbatianis superioribus libellus adfixus fuit medianus in quo chirographum scripsi me iturum Romam si Idibus Mais primis ea HS MD quae supra scripta sunt non satisve fecere tum deceat sub praecone de condicione quo magis quod superest quo minoris venierit triticum utique id triticum quo de agitur ab omni periculo esset meo heredisve mei haec mihi tecum ita convenerunt pactusque sum actum Puteolis\n
EDCS-08000931.translation: During the consulship of Gaius Laecanius Bassus and Quintus Terentius Cullio, on the Ides of May, on the first Barbatian upper fields of the Lepidian estates, a notice was affixed in the middle stating that I write that I will go to Rome if by the first Ides of May the 1500 sesterces mentioned above are not sufficiently paid, then it would be fitting, under the auctioneer, for the sake of the condition that the remaining wheat sells for less, and that this wheat in question is free from all risk for me or my heir. These terms have been agreed between us and I have made a pact. Done in Puteoli.
EDCS-08000931.summary: This is a private agreement posted in Puteoli concerning the sale of wheat, with terms for a potential auction if payment is not made by a certain date, dated to 68 AD.

EDCS-08000932.text_edition: \n Theophilus Aphrodisio fratri salutem accipies de nave Octa amphoras vini X sematas VI aceti LXXVII crocina Sicula XVI EVESditi amphoras suis\n
EDCS-08000932.translation: Theophilus sends greetings to his brother Aphrodisius. You will receive from the ship Octa ten amphorae of wine, six of semata, seventy-seven of vinegar, and sixteen of Sicilian crocina. [List continues with unspecified items].
EDCS-08000932.summary: A letter from Theophilus to his brother Aphrodisius, listing goods to be received, likely concerning trade or shipment.

EDCS-08000933.text_edition: \n Tiberio Plautio Aeliano Tauro Statilio Corvino consulibus XIII Kalendas Iulias Aulus Castricius Onesimus scripsi me promisisse Caio Sulpicio Fausto quanta pecunia ex auctione Publi Servili Narcissi in stipulatum meum meorumque veniet venerit diducta mercedem in representatum in HS Mdecem milia nummum\n
EDCS-08000933.translation: During the consulship of Tiberius Plautius Aelianus Victor and Statilius Corvinus, on the 13th day before the Kalends of July, I, Aulus Castricius Onesimus, write that I have promised to Gaius Sulpicius Faustus that whatever sum of money comes from the auction of Publius Servilius Narcissus into my stipulation and that of my associates, after deducting the commission, will be paid to me in representation to the amount of 10,100 sesterces.
EDCS-08000933.summary: This is a financial promise made by Aulus Castricius Onesimus to Gaius Sulpicius Faustus concerning proceeds from an auction, dated to 86 AD.

EDCS-08000934.text_edition: \n Publio Fabio Firmiano Publio Ampio Flaviano consulibus Nonis Augustis Lucius Patulcius Epaphroditus scripsi rogatu et mandatu Patulciae Erotidis libertaes meae coram ipsa eam accepisse ab Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo sestertia ducenta milia et quingenta\n
EDCS-08000934.translation: During the consulship of Publius Fabius Firmianus and Publius Ampius Flavianus, on the Nones of August, I, Lucius Patulcius Epaphroditus, write that I have received in my own name and by the order and mandate of Patulcia Erotis, my freedwoman, in her presence, from Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, two hundred and fifty thousand sesterces.
EDCS-08000934.summary: Lucius Patulcius Epaphroditus attests to receiving a large sum of money on behalf of his freedwoman from Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, dated to 85 AD.

EDCS-08000935.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio V Lucio Calventio Carminio Vetere consulibus VIII Idus Septembres Putiolis sic in porticu Augusti Sextiana in parastatica libellus fixus fuit in quo scriptum erat id quod infra scriptum est purpuras Laconicas reliquas quas Lucius Agathemeros Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo pignori dedisse dicitur venibunt V Idus Septembres primas \n
EDCS-08000935.translation: During the consulship of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus V and Lucius Calventius Carminus Vetus, on the 8th day before the Ides of September in Puteoli, in the portico of Sextus Augustus, on the parastatic, a notice was affixed containing what is written below: the remaining Laconian purple dyes, which Lucius Agathemeros is said to have pledged to Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, will be sold on the first 8th day before the Ides of September.
EDCS-08000935.summary: This is a public notice posted in Puteoli, dated to 54 AD, announcing the sale of pledged Laconian purple dyes.

EDCS-08000936.text_edition: \n In parastatica libellus adfixus est in quo scriptum erat id quod est infra purpuras Laconicas reliquas quas Lucius Marius Agathemer Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo pignori dedisse dicitur\n
EDCS-08000936.translation: On the parastatic, a notice was affixed containing what is written below: the remaining Laconian purple dyes, which Lucius Marius Agathemer is said to have pledged to Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus.
EDCS-08000936.summary: This is a partial inscription, likely a public notice, referencing Laconian purple dyes pledged to Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus.

EDCS-08000937.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Caesare Augusto V Lucio Calventio Vetere consulibus III Nonas Octobres Puteolis in foro in porticum Augusti Sextiana in parastatica libellus adfixus fuit in quo scriptum erat id quod infra scriptum est homo Felix homo Carus homo Ianuarius mulier Primigenia mulier Primigenia iunior puer Ampliatus quae mancipia Marcus Egnatius Suavis Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo fidei fiduciae causa HS nummum I pro HS XXVII mancipio dedisse dupcebatur venibunt pridie Idus Octobres primas Putiolis sic in foro ante chalcidicum Caesonianum fiducia proscribi coepta est ex XVII Kalendas Octobres Tiberio Claudio Caesare Augusto V Lucio Calventio Vetere Carminio consulibus actum Puteolis\n
EDCS-08000937.translation: During the consulship of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus V and Lucius Calventius Vetus Carminius, on the 3rd day before the Nones of October in Puteoli, in the forum, in the portico of Sextus Augustus, on the parastatic, a notice was affixed stating what is written below: the slaves Felix, Carus, Ianuarius, the women Primigenia and Primigenia iunior, and the boy Ampliatus, which were pledged by Marcus Egnatius Suavis to Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus for the sum of 1 sestertius for a value of 27 sesterces, will be sold on the first day before the Ides of October. In Puteoli, in the forum, before the Caesonian chalcidicum, the pledging for sale began to be proclaimed from the 17th day before the Kalends of October, during the consulship of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus V and Lucius Calventius Carminius Vetus. Done in Puteoli.
EDCS-08000937.summary: A public notice from Puteoli, dated to 54 AD, announcing the sale of several slaves who were pledged as security for a debt to Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus.

EDCS-08000938.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Caesare Augusto V Lucio Calventio Vetere consulibus X Kalendas Novembres Puteolis in foro in porticum sic Augusti Sextiana in parastatica libellus adfixus fuit in quo scriptum erat id quod est infra sponsores et creditores Marci Egnati Suavis ex colonia Puteolis cuius heres non extat\n
EDCS-08000938.translation: During the consulship of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus V and Lucius Calventius Vetus, on the 10th day before the Kalends of November in Puteoli, in the forum, in the portico of Sextus Augustus, on the parastatic, a notice was affixed stating what is written below: the guarantors and creditors of Marcus Egnatius Suavis from the colony of Puteoli, whose heir does not exist.
EDCS-08000938.summary: A public notice from Puteoli, dated to 54 AD, regarding the guarantors and creditors of Marcus Egnatius Suavis, whose heir is absent.

EDCS-08000939.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Caesare Augusto V Lucio Calventio Vetere consulibus III Kalendas Novembres Puteolis in foro ante chalcidicum Caesonianum Caius Sulpicius Euthychus qui se procuratorem esse dicebat Cai Sulpici Cinnami testatus est mancipia hominem Felicem hominem Carum hominem Ianuarium mulierem Primigeniam mulierem Primigeniam iuniorem puerum Ampliatum quae mancipia Marcus Egnatius Suavis Caio Sulpicio Cinnami fidei \n
EDCS-08000939.translation: During the consulship of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus and Lucius Calventius Vetus, on the third day before the Kalends of November, at Puteoli in the forum before the Caesonian chalcidicum, Gaius Sulpicius Euthychus, who said he was the procurator, testified that he had transferred as property the slaves Felix, Carus, Ianuarius, the women Primigenia and the younger Primigenia, and the boy Ampliatus, to Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, on trust.
EDCS-08000939.summary: This inscription records a property transfer (mancipatio) of slaves, witnessed by a procurator, at Puteoli.

EDCS-08000940.text_edition: \n Decimo Iunio Silano Torquato Quinto Haterio Antonino consulibus Idus Maias Puteolis in porticu Augusta Sextiana in parastatica libellus adfixus erat in quo scriptum erat id quod infra scriptum est Valerianus Pompeianus sub Iulias IVBIdibus Maiis\n
EDCS-08000940.translation: During the consulship of Decimus Iunius Silanus Torquatus and Quintus Haterius Antoninus, on the Ides of May, at Puteoli, in the Augusta Sextiana portico, on a side support, a notice was affixed containing what is written below: Valerianus Pompeianus, under the Ides of May.
EDCS-08000940.summary: This inscription records the affixing of a notice in the Augusta Sextiana portico at Puteoli, with a date during the consulship of Silanus Torquatus and Haterius Antoninus.

EDCS-08000942.text_edition: \n Cai Suetti Damasceni Luci Patulci Fortunati Titi Aufidi Felicis Marci Amulli Isochrysi Publi Laeli Donati Titi li Alcesi Fci Felicis Publio Petronio Turpiliano Lucio Iunio Caesennio Paeto consulibus XIV Kalendas Martias Puteolis signatum de fiducia mulieris Fortunatae\n
EDCS-08000942.translation: During the consulship of Gaius Suettius Damascenus, Lucius Patulcius Fortunatus, Titus Aufidius Felix, Marcus Amullius Isochrysus, Publius Laelius Donatus, Titus Alcesi Felix, during the consulship of Gaius Suettius Damascenus, Lucius Patulcius Fortunatus, Titus Aufidius Felix, Marcus Amullius Isochrysus, Publius Laelius Donatus, Titus Alcesi Felix, during the consulship of Publius Petronius Turpilianus and Lucius Iunius Caesennius Paetus, on the fourteenth day before the Kalends of March, at Puteoli, it was signed in trust by the woman Fortunata.
EDCS-08000942.summary: This inscription records a financial transaction, possibly a trust agreement, at Puteoli during the consulship of Petronius Turpilianus and Iunius Caesennius Paetus.

EDCS-08000943.text_edition: \n Publio Petronio Turpiliano Lucio Iunio consulibus Caesennio Paeto XIII Kalendas Martias Puteolis in porticu Augusti Sextiana in parastatica libellus adfixus fuit in quo scriptum erat id quod est infra mulier Fortunata quam Marcia Aucta Caio Sulpicio Oniro fidei fiduciae causa HS nummo I mancipasse diceretur venibit III Nonas Martias primas Puteolis in foro ante chalcidicum Caesonianum sub praecone pecunia presenti fiducia qua de agitur proscribi coepta est ex ante diem III Nonas Martias Publio Petronio Turpiliano Lucio consulibus Iunio Caesennio Paeto libellus adfixus fuit XIII\n
EDCS-08000943.translation: During the consulship of Publius Petronius Turpilianus and Lucius Iunius Caesennius Paetus, on the thirteenth day before the Kalends of March, at Puteoli, in the Augustan Sextian portico, on a side support, a notice was affixed stating what is below: The woman Fortunata, who was being said to have transferred in trust for one sestertius to Gaius Sulpicius Onirus, will be sold on the third day before the Nones of March, the first, at Puteoli in the forum before the Caesonian chalcidicum, under a crier, with present payment; the trust concerning which this is being announced began to be proclaimed on the day before the third Nones of March, during the consulship of Publius Petronius Turpilianus and Lucius Iunius Caesennius Paetus, a notice was affixed on the thirteenth.
EDCS-08000943.summary: This inscription records the public proclamation and impending sale of property under trust at Puteoli, dating to the consulship of Petronius Turpilianus and Iunius Caesennius Paetus.

EDCS-08000946.text_edition: \n comitarem Caio Sulpicio Cinnamo ut supra scriptum est Caio Caecina Largo Caio Cestio Gallo consulibus III Nonas Iunias Sulpicio Amaranti Tiberi Claudii Caesaris Augusti Germanici servi Hycinthiani sic vicario HS D\n
EDCS-08000946.translation: As written above, with Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, during the consulship of Gaius Caecina Largus and Gaius Cestius Gallus, on the third Nones of June, Sulpicius Amaranthus, a slave of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, paid 500 sesterces through his vicarius.
EDCS-08000946.summary: This inscription records a payment of 500 sesterces made by a slave of Emperor Claudius, during the consulship of Caecina Largus and Cestius Gallus.

EDCS-08000947.text_edition: \n Sulpicio Amaranthi vicario HS D Caio Caesare Augusto Germanico II consule VII Idus Iulias Phallophori vicario HS MMDduo milia quingenta nummum\n
EDCS-08000947.translation: Sulpicius Amaranthus, vicarius, 500 sesterces. During the second consulship of Gaius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, on the seventh Ides of July, Phallophori, vicarius, 2500 sesterces.
EDCS-08000947.summary: This inscription records two payments, one to a vicarius of Sulpicius Amaranthus and another to a vicarius of Phallophori, during the second consulship of Gaius Caesar Augustus Germanicus.

EDCS-08000949.text_edition: \n Vadimonium cui curis suis addendum nummum XIII milibus actum Puteolis Sexto Palpellio Histro Lucio Pedanio Secundo\n
EDCS-08000949.translation: A summons for 13,000 sesterces, handled by his curators, enacted at Puteoli before Sextus Palpellius Histro and Lucius Pedanius Secundus.
EDCS-08000949.summary: This inscription records a summons for a debt of 13,000 sesterces at Puteoli, during the consulship of Sextus Palpellius Histro and Lucius Pedanius Secundus.

EDCS-08000957.text_edition: \n S S Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus qui se procuratorem Primigeni esse dicebat spopondit Caius Iulius Atimetus actum VII Idus Martias Cornelio consulibus\n
EDCS-08000957.translation: Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, who said he was the procurator of Primigenius, guaranteed [the debt]. Gaius Iulius Atimetus enacted [the agreement] on the seventh Ides of March, during the consulship of Cornelius.
EDCS-08000957.summary: This inscription records a guarantee provided by Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus, acting as procurator, during the consulship of Cornelius.

EDCS-08000958.text_edition: \n Theodori Prisci diacrisis apud Nilum Sidonium et Rhodium XVI mil dedisse Sidonio Africano eis diadosin Nilum quod a Flacci vadimoni Sidoni Africani esse hariolos aut xenos Armenios adiisset actum Puteolis X Kalendas Apriles Nerone Claudio Caesare Augusto II Lucio Calpurnio Pisone consulibus\n
EDCS-08000958.translation: Theodorus Priscus reported that he gave 16,000 [sesterces] to Sidonius Africanus at the Nile, as a succession (diadosis) to the Nile; the report was that the divination of Flaccus concerning Sidonius Africanus and his associates or foreign Armenians had been summoned. Enacted at Puteoli on the tenth day before the Kalends of April, during the second consulship of Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus and Lucius Calpurnius Piso.
EDCS-08000958.summary: This inscription records a financial transaction and a legal dispute involving divination and foreign individuals, enacted at Puteoli during the consulship of Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus and Lucius Calpurnius Piso.

EDCS-08000966.text_edition: \n Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus Niceroti colonorum coloniae Puteolanae servo arcario HS per chirographum hac die dedit credidit \n
EDCS-08000966.translation: Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus gave and entrusted 100 sesterces to Nicerotes, the steward (arcarius) of the slaves of the colony of Puteoli, by written document on this day.
EDCS-08000966.summary: This inscription records a financial transaction where Gaius Sulpicius Cinnamus gave 100 sesterces to the steward of the slaves of Puteoli.

EDCS-08000971.text_edition: \n ettieni Crescentis Cnaei Cossuti Atimeti Sexti Cervi Celsi Luci Laroni Comminii Felicis nnini\n
EDCS-08000971.translation: To Ettienus Crescentis, Cnaeius Cossutius Atimetus, Sextus Cervius Celsus, Lucius Laronius Comminius Felix, and nninius.
EDCS-08000971.summary: This inscription lists the names of several individuals, likely patrons or members of a collegium, from an unspecified date.

EDCS-08000973.text_edition: \n Cai Iuli Ruphi Cai Iuli Faucilli Cai Stlacci Primogenis Cai Cipei Pitti Auli Sueti Ephebi\n
EDCS-08000973.translation: To Caius Julius Rufus, Caius Julius Faucillus, Caius Stlacius Primogenis, Caius Cipeius Pittus, and Aulus Suetius Ephebus.
EDCS-08000973.summary: This inscription lists the names of several individuals, likely patrons or members of a collegium, from an unspecified date.

EDCS-08300108.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Herma pater et Pyrallis mater efcerunt sic S DV Ursillae filiae bonae memoriae dulicissimae vixit annum I menses VIII dies XXIX haec maledixit nulli nocuit nemini dolorem dedit parentibus suis de hoc caudere sic potest nemo haec non pomum acerbum gustavit non cucumerem non uvam non ficum acervam sic misit orcus\n
EDCS-08300108.translation: To the departed spirits. Herma the father and Pyrallis the mother set this up for their daughter Ursilla, of good memory and most sweet. She lived 1 year, 8 months, 29 days. May she curse no one, harm no one, give pain to no one. No one can be saddened by this. No one tasted a sour apple, no cucumber, no grape, no sweet fig. Thus Orcus has sent her away.
EDCS-08300108.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young girl named Ursilla, set up by her parents, detailing her age at death and praising her gentle nature.

EDCS-08300109.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that appears to record the name of a woman named Aelia Galla, possibly on a tomb or other monument.

EDCS-08300110.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Alexandriae vixit annos XV menses VI Titus Flavius Fortunatus et Valeria Cliopatra sic vernae carissimae bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-08300110.translation: To the departed spirits. Alexandria lived 15 years and 6 months. Titus Flavius Fortunatus and Valeria Cleopatra set this up for their beloved freedwoman, who deserved well.
EDCS-08300110.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young woman named Alexandria, set up by her owners or patrons, Titus Flavius Fortunatus and Valeria Cleopatra.

EDCS-08300111.text_edition: \n Publius Anicius Publi filius Anicia Publi filia Quieta ollas II fecer(unt)\n
EDCS-08300111.translation: Publius Anicius, son of Publius, and Anicia, daughter of Publius Quietus, made 2 pots.
EDCS-08300111.summary: This inscription records the creation of two pots by Publius Anicius and Anicia, possibly for their own use or as a dedication.

EDCS-08300113.summary: This very brief inscription possibly refers to silver objects or currency, its exact context is unknown.

EDCS-08300115.summary: This inscription simply records the name and profession of Atticus, likely a teacher or tutor.

EDCS-08300116.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aufidiae Parhesiae Lucio Aufidio Crispo Lucio Aufidio Luci filio Stellatina Tranquillo filiis Lucius Aufidius Corymbus coniugi erga se bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08300116.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius Aufidius Corymbus made this for his wife Aufidia Parhesia, who deserved well from him, and for his sons Lucius Aufidius Crispus and Lucius Aufidius, son of Lucius, of the Stellatina tribe Tranquillus.
EDCS-08300116.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Lucius Aufidius Corymbus for his wife Aufidia Parhesia and their sons.

EDCS-08300117.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Gelasinus Augusti libertus coniux fecit coniugi incomparabili\n
EDCS-08300117.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Aurelius Gelasinus, freedman of the Emperor, made this for his incomparable wife.
EDCS-08300117.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Marcus Aurelius Gelasinus for his beloved wife.

EDCS-08300118.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Benedicta, who lived [------].
EDCS-08300118.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Benedicta, whose age at death is missing.

EDCS-08300119.text_edition: \n Vivit Casos @(obiit) Amphio fullo nnis LII @(obiit) Euterpe vestispica nnis XXVI @(obiit) Musa filia eius nnis III vivit Callipoleis mater vivit Nicomedes frater vivit Lesbius mulio\n
EDCS-08300119.translation: Lived. Casos (died) Amphio, fuller, 52 years. (died) Euterpe, cloth-seller, 26 years. (died) Musa, his daughter, 3 years. Lived. Callipoleis, mother. Lived. Nicomedes, brother. Lived. Lesbius, coachman.
EDCS-08300119.summary: This inscription lists the ages at death for several individuals, including Amphio, Euterpe, and Musa, and notes that Callipoleis, Nicomedes, and Lesbius were still living.

EDCS-08300120.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Caecilio qui vixit annos VIII Quintus Caecilius Damo pater\n
EDCS-08300120.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Quintus Caecilius, who lived 8 years. Quintus Caecilius Damo, father.
EDCS-08300120.summary: A funerary inscription by Quintus Caecilius Damo for his son, Quintus Caecilius, who died at the age of 8.

EDCS-08300122.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Caelio Gallicano vixit annos XXXIIII Flavia Agathetyche coniugi bene merenti et sibi et libertis\n
EDCS-08300122.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Gaius Caelio Gallicanus, who lived 34 years. Flavia Agathetyche, to her well-deserving wife, and to herself and her freedmen.
EDCS-08300122.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated by Flavia Agathetyche to her husband, Gaius Caelio Gallicanus, who died at 34, and also for herself and their freedmen.

EDCS-08300123.text_edition: \n Cal Lucius Cal cara q vixit annos\n
EDCS-08300123.translation: Cal. Lucius Cal[---], who lived [---] years.
EDCS-08300123.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription for Lucius Cal[---], with his age at death missing.

EDCS-08300125.text_edition: \n Diis Manibus Ceiae Orphne vixit nnos XXVII\n
EDCS-08300125.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Ceia Orphne, who lived 27 years.
EDCS-08300125.summary: A funerary inscription for Ceia Orphne, who died at the age of 27.

EDCS-08300126.text_edition: \n et Claud Dona Iulius Ap optimo itum posterisque\n
EDCS-08300126.translation: and Claud[ia?]. For Julius Ap[---], to the best [---] and descendants.
EDCS-08300126.summary: A fragmentary inscription, possibly funerary, with the dedicant Julius Ap[---] honoring someone named Claud[ia?] and their descendants.

EDCS-08300127.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Castor vixit nnos LXX Tiberius Claudius Vitalis filius eius vixit annos XV\n
EDCS-08300127.translation: Tiberius Claudius Castor lived 70 years. Tiberius Claudius Vitalis, his son, lived 15 years.
EDCS-08300127.summary: A funerary inscription commemorating Tiberius Claudius Castor, who died at 70, and his son Tiberius Claudius Vitalis, who died at 15.

EDCS-08300129.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Augusti liberto Felici actario Caesaris vernae divi Augusti ex trigeminis Tiberius Claudius Successus patrono suo bene merenti fecit et Anniae Secundae dedicatus ab Tiberio Claudio filio suo\n
EDCS-08300129.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Felix, freedman of Augustus, an actor of Caesar, a slave of the deified Augustus from the triplets. Tiberius Claudius Successus made this for his deserving patron and for Annia Secunda, dedicated by Tiberius Claudius, his son.
EDCS-08300129.summary: A funerary monument erected by Tiberius Claudius Successus for his patron Tiberius Claudius Felix, an actor and freedman of Augustus, and for Annia Secunda, and also dedicated by Successus's son.

EDCS-08300130.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Claud[---] Mess [---] years.
EDCS-08300130.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription for someone named Claud[---] Mess[---], with the age at death missing.

EDCS-08300133.summary: This is a funerary epitaph identifying the deceased as Publius Cornelius Philargyrus.

EDCS-08300135.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Dionysius Ummidiae Quadratillae actoris sic sibi et Primae coniugi suae posterisque suis\n
EDCS-08300135.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Dionysius, steward of Ummidia Quadratilla, thus for himself and his wife Prima and their descendants.
EDCS-08300135.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication to the Divine Spirits made by Dionysius for himself, his wife, and his descendants, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08300136.summary: This is a fragmented funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Lucius Dom.

EDCS-08300137.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Epaphroditus Gabinianus Aeliae Marianae coniugi suae et Cunerius Cresces coniugi suae bene merenti sua inpensa dono dederunt\n
EDCS-08300137.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Epaphroditus Gabinianus and Cresces, husband of Aelia Mariana, gave this at their own expense for their deserving wife/wives.
EDCS-08300137.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication by Epaphroditus Gabinianus and Cresces for their wives, Aelia Mariana and Cunerius, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08300138.translation: Of Euhemerus, son of Cossutius Hilarius, of the Pupia tribe.
EDCS-08300138.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording the name and tribal affiliation of a Roman citizen, possibly on a tombstone or other dedication.

EDCS-08300140.text_edition: \n Ti Eutyche coniugi et Caius Cornelius Menophilus patri bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08300140.translation: Gaius Cornelius Menophilus made this for his deserving wife, Eutyche, and his deserving father.
EDCS-08300140.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication made by Gaius Cornelius Menophilus for his wife Eutyche and his father.

EDCS-08300142.text_edition: \n ellius Felix fecit et libertis libertabusque p<o=Q>sterisqu<e=F> eius \n
EDCS-08300142.translation: Aelius Felix made this for himself and for his freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-08300142.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication made by Aelius Felix for himself, his freedmen and freedwomen, and their descendants.

EDCS-08300143.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus idio Firmo retiario fratres bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-08300143.translation: To the Divine Spirits. The brothers made this for their deserving retiarius brother, Firminus.
EDCS-08300143.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication made by brothers for their deserving retiarius brother, Firminus.

EDCS-08300144.text_edition: \n Flaviae quae vixit annos X coniu[gi ------\n
EDCS-08300144.translation: For Flavia, who lived 10 years, [his] deserving wife ------.
EDCS-08300144.summary: This is a fragmented funerary epitaph commemorating a young girl named Flavia who died at the age of 10.

EDCS-08300145.text_edition: \n Tito Flavio Augusti liberto Carpo et Claudiae Fortunatae Tito Flavio Augusti liberto Carpo patri et Claudiae Fortunatae matri Flavia Augusti liberta Auge filia bene merentibus fecit et libertis libertabus posterisque eorum et Tiberio Claudio Zosimo patri\n
EDCS-08300145.translation: Augusti liberta Auge, daughter of Titus Flavius Augusti libertus Carpus and Claudia Fortunata, made this for her deserving parents, Titus Flavius Augusti libertus Carpus and Claudia Fortunata, and for her father Tiberius Claudius Zosimus, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and descendants.
EDCS-08300145.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication by Flavia Auge, daughter of Titus Flavius Carpus and Claudia Fortunata, for her parents and other family members, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08300146.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Flavio Euhemero bene merenti\n
EDCS-08300146.translation: To the departed spirits of Titus Flavius Euhemerus, deserving of good.
EDCS-08300146.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Titus Flavius Euhemerus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300147.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fortunata se viva fecit tum FSIM \n
EDCS-08300147.translation: To the departed spirits. Fortunata made this for herself while alive, then FSIM.
EDCS-08300147.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Fortunata for herself, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300152.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Ioniche sic vixit nnos XII menses III dies XI Caius Iulius Acutus et Iulia Moschis parentes filiae piissimae fecerunt\n
EDCS-08300152.translation: To the departed spirits. For Julia Ionica, who lived 12 years, 3 months, and 11 days. Gaius Julius Acutus and Iulia Moschis, parents, made this for their most devoted daughter.
EDCS-08300152.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Julia Ionica, detailing her age at death and erected by her parents, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300154.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Leucadiae filiae pientissimae Nemesis\n
EDCS-08300154.translation: To the departed spirits. For Leucadia, most devoted daughter. Nemesis [erected this].
EDCS-08300154.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Leucadia, set up by Nemesis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300156.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Licinio Iuveni Licinia Thallusa filio bene merenti mater fecit sibi et suis posterisque eorum vixit annis III mensibus IIII diebus IIII\n
EDCS-08300156.translation: To the departed spirits. For Publius Licinius Iuvenis. Licinia Thallusa made this for her son, deserving of good, for herself and her descendants and their posterity. He lived 3 years, 4 months, and 4 days.
EDCS-08300156.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Licinius Iuvenis, detailing his age at death and erected by his mother, Licinia Thallusa, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300157.text_edition: \n Lucius Lollius Libani Psaltis libertus Eros\n
EDCS-08300157.translation: Lucius Lollius Libanius, the singer, freedman Eros.
EDCS-08300157.summary: This inscription records the name of Eros, a freedman and singer, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300158.text_edition: \n Mac Cinc in fronte pedes X in agro pedes \n
EDCS-08300158.translation: In the front, 10 feet; in the field, [space for more measurements].
EDCS-08300158.summary: This inscription appears to record land measurements, likely related to property or a boundary, date uncertain.

EDCS-08300159.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Marius Fortunatus Graniae Ethale coniugi bene merenti fecit et sibi et suis\n
EDCS-08300159.translation: To the departed spirits, sacred. Quintus Marius Fortunatus made this for his deserving wife Grania Ethale, and for himself and his household.
EDCS-08300159.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Quintus Marius Fortunatus for his wife Grania Ethale and for his family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300160.summary: This inscription records two names, Maria and Ilara, likely a funerary epitaph or a simple inscription of ownership, date uncertain.

EDCS-08300163.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Manio Muttio filio Fabia Saturn[ino ------\n
EDCS-08300163.translation: To the departed spirits. For Manius Muttius, son of Fabia Saturnina [...].
EDCS-08300163.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Manius Muttius, son of Fabia Saturnina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300164.translation: To Publius Naevius, his freedmen and freedwomen [---].
EDCS-08300164.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication made by or for Publius Naevius and his freed dependents, possibly on a tomb or public building.

EDCS-08300165.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus ae sorori piissimae quae vixit annos V Quintus Nonius Nundinarius frater fecit\n
EDCS-08300165.translation: To the Divine Manes. Quintus Nonius Nundinarius made this for his most devoted sister, who lived 5 years.
EDCS-08300165.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Quintus Nonius Nundinarius for his sister, who died at the age of five.

EDCS-08300166.translation: To the Divine Manes. Eros, sweet (son/slave) of Niphas and Dionysermus.
EDCS-08300166.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Eros, likely a young boy or slave, set up by his owner(s) Niphas and Dionysermus.

EDCS-08300167.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus pio Onesimo magistro OIC\n
EDCS-08300167.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Onesimus, the worthy master.
EDCS-08300167.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Onesimus, identified as a master ('magister'), likely by a freedman or someone under his charge.

EDCS-08300168.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Onesimo coniugi Iulia Festa bene merenti fecit quae vixit cum eo annos X menses VII\n
EDCS-08300168.translation: To the Divine Manes. Iulia Festa made this for her well-deserving husband Onesimus, who lived with her 10 years and 7 months.
EDCS-08300168.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Iulia Festa for her husband Onesimus, commemorating their 10-year marriage.

EDCS-08300169.text_edition: \n SO Rattius Diomedes sibi suis prospexit\n
EDCS-08300169.translation: Rattius Diomedes provided for himself and his family.
EDCS-08300169.summary: This inscription records that Rattius Diomedes made provision for himself and his kin, likely for burial arrangements.

EDCS-08300170.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Pan vixit annos XII fra\n
EDCS-08300170.translation: To the Divine Manes. Pan lived 12 years. [His] brother/father made [this].
EDCS-08300170.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a boy named Pan, who died at age 12, with the monument erected by his brother or father.

EDCS-08300171.translation: For Philip, my son. Mother Stibas made [this].
EDCS-08300171.summary: This is a funerary dedication set up by a mother named Stibas for her son Philip.

EDCS-08300172.text_edition: \n Philodamus Casianus servus publicus fecit Fufia curavit\n
EDCS-08300172.translation: Philodamus Casianus, a public slave, made this. Fufia supervised.
EDCS-08300172.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument erected by Philodamus Casianus, a public slave, with Fufia overseeing the work.

EDCS-08300174.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Plaetoriae Tertiae et Titiae Felicitati et Terentiae Primitivae sorori bonae carissimae et Publio Aelio Daphno patri et Aeliae Sophe matri carissimae et piissimae Quintus Cornelius Euander et sibi libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum\n
EDCS-08300174.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Plaetoria Tertia and Titia Felicitas, and for Terentia Primitiva, good and dearest sister, and for Publius Aelius Daphnus, father, and Aelia Sophia, most dear and most devoted mother, Quintus Cornelius Euander, and for himself, his freedmen and freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-08300174.summary: This extensive funerary inscription by Quintus Cornelius Euander honors his father, mother, sister, and wife, and also provides for his own burial and that of his dependents.

EDCS-08300175.text_edition: \n Plautus Caesaris Augusti liberti libertus liae collibertae\n
EDCS-08300175.translation: Plautus, freedman of a freedman of Caesar Augustus, to his fellow freedwoman.
EDCS-08300175.summary: A funerary dedication by Plautus to a fellow freedwoman, dating to the Augustan period.

EDCS-08300176.summary: An incomplete inscription, likely a funerary dedication, with partially legible names and uncertain dating.

EDCS-08300179.translation: To Ogenicus, deserving of these honors.
EDCS-08300179.summary: A dedication to Ogenicus, likely funerary, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-08300181.text_edition: \n Pyramus vixit menses VI Paphus pater et Pyrallis filio \n
EDCS-08300181.translation: Pyramus lived 6 months. Paphus (his father) and Pyrallis (his mother) (dedicated this) to their son.
EDCS-08300181.summary: A funerary epitaph for a child named Pyramus, erected by his parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300182.summary: A very fragmented inscription, possibly related to funerary or commemorative purposes, with an uncertain date and content.

EDCS-08300184.text_edition: \n Restuta patrono suo bene merenti fecit cum quo vixit annos XXXVI\n
EDCS-08300184.translation: Restuta made (this) to her deserving patron, with whom she lived 36 years.
EDCS-08300184.summary: A funerary dedication by Restuta to her patron, indicating a relationship of 36 years, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300186.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Secundae coniugi bene merenti Caius Iulius Nigrinus fecit et sibi\n
EDCS-08300186.translation: To the Divine Manes of Secunda, his deserving wife. Caius Iulius Nigrinus made (this) for her and for himself.
EDCS-08300186.summary: A funerary epitaph for Secunda, erected by her husband Caius Iulius Nigrinus, who also included himself, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300187.text_edition: \n Diis Manibus Secundae Flaviae Thallusae servae vixit annos XX Hermes conservae suae bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08300187.translation: To the Divine Manes of Secunda Flavia Thallusa, a slave. Hermes made (this) for his deserving fellow slave, who lived 20 years.
EDCS-08300187.summary: A funerary epitaph for the slave Secunda Flavia Thallusa, erected by a fellow slave named Hermes, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300188.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Sentius Verecundus Primitivo vernae suo carissimo bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08300188.translation: To the Divine Manes of Marcus Sentius Verecundus. (This was) made by Primitivus for his dearest vernus (slave born in the household), deserving of these honors.
EDCS-08300188.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Sentius Verecundus, erected by his vernus Primitivus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300189.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Themistocleti vixit nnos V menses VII dies XVIII horas VI Trophimus et Eufraenusa sic parentes filio dulcissimo et Titus Claudius Faustus invitus fecerunt\n
EDCS-08300189.translation: To the Divine Manes of Themistocletus, who lived 5 years, 7 months, 18 days, and 6 hours. Trophimus and Eufraenusa, his parents, and Titus Claudius Faustus, against his will, made (this) for their dearest son.
EDCS-08300189.summary: A funerary epitaph for a child, Themistocletus, detailing his short life, erected by his parents and Titus Claudius Faustus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300191.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Tyrannus Hypatus, identified as a procurator, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-08300194.text_edition: \n Lucius Vetilius Luci filius Marianus vixit annos VII\n
EDCS-08300194.translation: Lucius Vetilius Marianus, son of Lucius, lived 7 years.
EDCS-08300194.summary: This is a tombstone recording the short life of a young boy named Lucius Vetilius Marianus.

EDCS-08300195.text_edition: \n Vettia Rufa si fortuna animo certaret fecissem tibi plura merenti vivus Lucius Vettius Luci libertus Dionisius sic viva Vettia Luci liberta Acris vivus Eros Cai Atili Auli filii Pomptina Lucius Pomponius Luci filius Pomptina Lucius Egnatius Sexti filius Aemilia\n
EDCS-08300195.translation: If Vettia Rufa had contended with fortune by her spirit, I would have done more for you, who deserved it. While alive: Lucius Vettius Dionisius, freedman of Lucius. While alive: Vettia Acris, freedwoman of Lucius. Lucius Pomponius, son of Lucius, from the Pomptina tribe. Lucius Egnatius, son of Sextus, from the Aemilia tribe.
EDCS-08300195.summary: This complex epitaph lists several individuals, possibly family members or patrons and freedmen, with the primary dedication seemingly from Lucius Vettius Dionisius and Vettia Acris to Vettia Rufa.

EDCS-08300196.summary: This is a simple ownership or dedication inscription, likely for a tomb or a building, belonging to Marcus Vipsanius Dama.

EDCS-08300197.summary: This inscription likely records the names of a couple, Lucius and Julia, possibly on a tomb or monument.

EDCS-08300198.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vitalis Corneliani publici pedisequi praefecti aerari militaris fecit <F=E>ortunatus Severianus publicus XV virum sacris faciundis \n
EDCS-08300198.translation: To the departed spirits. Fortunatus Severianus, a public attendant (pedisequus) of the prefect of the military treasury, made this for Vitalis Cornelianus, a public attendant (pedisequus) of the XV viri sacris faciundis.
EDCS-08300198.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication made by Fortunatus Severianus, a public attendant in the military treasury, to Vitalis Cornelianus, also a public attendant serving the College of Fifteen for Public Rites.

EDCS-08300200.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpiae Mel vixit nnos V menses parentes filiae bene merenti fecerunt et Ulpiae Helpidi Marcus Ulpius Sporus suis et sibi\n
EDCS-08300200.translation: To the departed spirits. For Ulpia Mel, who lived 5 years and some months, her parents made this for their well-deserving daughter, and for Ulpia Helpidi. Marcus Ulpius Sporus, for himself and his own [family].
EDCS-08300200.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Ulpia Mel, set up by her parents, and also including Ulpia Helpidi and Marcus Ulpius Sporus, likely family members or patrons.

EDCS-08300201.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Ummidius Anicetus fecit sibi et Ummidiae te libertae idem coniugi et libertis libertabusque suis posterisque eorum\n
EDCS-08300201.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Ummidius Anicetus made this for himself and for Ummidia, his freedwoman and wife, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-08300201.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Gaius Ummidius Anicetus for himself, his freedwoman and wife Ummidia, and their descendants.

EDCS-08300202.text_edition: \n Caius Volcacius Felix Volcacia Cai liberta Helle Varia Titi liberta Ambrosia Maiania Rufa\n
EDCS-08300202.translation: Gaius Volcacius Felix; Helle, freedwoman of Gaius; Ambrosia, freedwoman of Titus; Maiania Rufa.
EDCS-08300202.summary: This inscription lists several names, possibly indicating a group of individuals associated together, perhaps freedmen and patrons, or owners of a property.

EDCS-08300203.text_edition: \n Quinto Volusio Crescenti fratri optimo Tiberius Claudius Fortunatus fecit\n
EDCS-08300203.translation: Tiberius Claudius Fortunatus made this for his most excellent brother, Quintus Volusius Crescens.
EDCS-08300203.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument dedicated by Tiberius Claudius Fortunatus to his brother, Quintus Volusius Crescens.

EDCS-08300204.text_edition: \n Quinto Voreno Vorena Vestalis filio dulcissimo et Max\n
EDCS-08300204.translation: To Quintus Vorenus, from his most beloved Vorena Vestalis, his son.
EDCS-08300204.summary: This inscription is a dedication from a parent to their son.

EDCS-08300208.text_edition: \n Vivit Marcus Marcius Marci libertus Agrippa\n
EDCS-08300208.translation: Marcus Marcius Agrippa, freedman of Marcus, is alive.
EDCS-08300208.summary: This inscription seems to be a testament to someone's survival or existence.

EDCS-08300209.text_edition: \n Sextus Curfidius Cai filius Marcus Caesius Luci filius Minervae pondo XXS de senatus sententia faciendum curaverunt IIIviri\n
EDCS-08300209.translation: Sextus Curfidius, son of Caius, and Marcus Caesius, son of Lucius, saw to it that this be made at a cost of 20 pounds of Minerva, by decree of the Senate, as a board of three men.
EDCS-08300209.summary: This inscription records the construction of a public work by two individuals, commissioned by the Senate and overseen by a board of three, likely from the Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-08300210.text_edition: \n Marci Mini Marci fili Teretina Rufi fili annorum XXI\n
EDCS-08300210.translation: Marcus Mini Rufi, son of Marcus, of the Teretina tribe, aged 21.
EDCS-08300210.summary: This inscription appears to be a brief record of a person's name, parentage, tribal affiliation, and age, possibly on a tombstone.

EDCS-08300211.text_edition: \n a Marci filia Firm optim ponendam fecit sibi\n
EDCS-08300211.translation: His daughter Firm, daughter of Marcus, optimus, had this made for herself.
EDCS-08300211.summary: This inscription records that a daughter named Firm commissioned a monument for herself.

EDCS-08300213.text_edition: \n Betutiae Sertorianae Vitrasi Aequi Thabraceni locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-08300213.translation: A plot of land was granted to Betutia Sertoriana, daughter of Vitrasius Aequus, from Thabracens, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-08300213.summary: This inscription records the granting of land by local town councilors to a woman named Betutia Sertoriana.

EDCS-08300214.text_edition: \n Munus Nolae de quadriduo Marci Comini Heredis\n
EDCS-08300214.translation: A public show of Nola for four days, by Marcus Cominius Heres.
EDCS-08300214.summary: This inscription records the sponsorship of a public spectacle in Nola by Marcus Cominius Heres.

EDCS-08300225.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Gaius Julius Polybius, who held the office of duumvir.

EDCS-08400053.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Bathyllidi vixit annis V mensibus III diebus XXII Bathyllus filiae karissimae\n
EDCS-08400053.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Bathylli lived 5 years, 3 months, and 22 days. Bathyllus, to his dearest daughter.
EDCS-08400053.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young girl named Bathylli, set up by her father Bathyllus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08400056.text_edition: \n Publio Sulpicio Luci filio Oufentina Peregrino Mediolaniensi equiti speculatori vixit annis XXVIII militavit annis VIIII Lucius Sulpicius Cai filius Oufentina Messor pater et frater\n
EDCS-08400056.translation: To Publius Sulpicius Peregrinus, son of Lucius, of the Oufentina tribe, from Milan, a cavalryman and scout. He lived 28 years and served 9 years. Lucius Sulpicius Messor, son of Caius, of the Oufentina tribe, his father and brother, [set this up].
EDCS-08400056.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Sulpicius Peregrinus, detailing his life and military service, set up by his father and brother, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08400103.text_edition: \n Ddomini nnostri Flavius Valerius Constantinus Maximus et Valerius Licinianus Licinius Pii Felices Invicti semper Augusti thermasi temporis deformatas Laurentibus suis addito cultu restituerunt curante Camilio Aspro viro clarissimo curatore\n
EDCS-08400103.translation: Our Lords Flavius Valerius Constantinus Maximus and Valerius Licinianus Licinius, the Pious, Fortunate, Invincible, Ever-August Emperors, restored their baths for the people of Laurentum, which had been disfigured by time, with added decoration, under the supervision of Camilius Asprius, a most distinguished man, the curator.
EDCS-08400103.summary: This inscription records the restoration of public baths by the emperors Constantine and Licinius, likely in the early 4th century CE, under the supervision of a curator.

EDCS-08400106.text_edition: \n V Auli libertus Philemo in fronte\n
EDCS-08400106.translation: Aulus' freedman Philemo, on the front.
EDCS-08400106.summary: This is a very short inscription likely marking a property or a tomb, identifying a freedman named Philemo.

EDCS-08400129.text_edition: \n Decimus Pontius Decimi filius Palatina Sabinus ex colonia Catinense\n
EDCS-08400129.translation: Decimus Pontius Sabinus, son of Decimus, of the Palatine tribe, from the colony of Catina.
EDCS-08400129.summary: This inscription simply records the name and tribal affiliation of an individual named Decimus Pontius Sabinus, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08400131.text_edition: \n Popillius Cai filius Vitalius IIvir Decimiae Venustae et Publio Aelio Turpilio Vero coniugi eius Decimiae Chiae Decimio Celeri filio et Valeriae Fortunatae et suis locus datus decreto decurionum in fronte pedes XXV in agro pedes X\n
EDCS-08400131.translation: By decree of the decurions, a plot was granted to Popillius Vitalius, son of Caius, twice *duovir*, to his wife Decimia Venusta and her husband Publius Aelius Turpilius Verus, to their son Decimius Celer, to Decimia Chia, to Valeria Fortunata, and to their families; 25 feet in front and 10 feet in depth in the land.
EDCS-08400131.summary: This inscription details a land grant for burial plots decreed by local officials, specifying dimensions and beneficiaries, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08400132.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Valerio Calocaero Valeria Onesias marito bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08400132.translation: To the departed spirits. Valeria Onesias erected this for her well-deserving husband, Quintus Valerius Calocaerus.
EDCS-08400132.summary: This is a funerary dedication from a wife to her deceased husband, Quintus Valerius Calocaerus, typical of the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08400133.text_edition: \n Porphyrus Augusti libertus procurator regionum Falernae et Statanae Aug(usti) nostri Genio votum solvit\n
EDCS-08400133.translation: Porphyrus, freedman of the Emperor, procurator of the Falernian and Statanae regions, paid a vow to the Genius of our Emperor.
EDCS-08400133.summary: This inscription records a vow paid to the emperor's genius by Porphyrus, an imperial freedman and procurator, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08400135.text_edition: \n Caio Messio Cai filio Quinti nepoti Scaevae IIviro tertium cui lege Flavia datum est primus sententiam sui ordinis interrogaretur cuique post mortem publice funus locusque sepulturae decretus est Scaeva filius\n
EDCS-08400135.translation: To Gaius Messius Scaeva, son of Gaius, grandson of Quintus, twice *duovir*, to whom by the Flavian law was granted the right to be consulted first on matters of his order, and to whom after death a funeral and burial place were publicly decreed; his son Scaeva [erected this].
EDCS-08400135.summary: This inscription honors Gaius Messius Scaeva, a prominent local official, detailing his honors including the right to speak first in council and a public burial, likely from the late 1st century CE or later.

EDCS-08400136.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Serviliae Adauctae quae vixit nnos XXVIIII menses V Aulus Statius Vitalis coniugi incomparabili bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08400136.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aulus Statius Vitalis erected this for his incomparable wife, Servilia Adaucta, who lived 27 years and 5 months.
EDCS-08400136.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Servilia Adaucta, who died at the age of 27 years and 5 months, erected by her husband Aulus Statius Vitalis, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08400138.summary: This is a simple funerary or ownership inscription identifying Gnaeus Pompeius Epirus, possibly from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08400139.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelii Onesimus et Florentina vivi sibi et suis fecerunt libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum\n
EDCS-08400139.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Onesimus and Florentina, alive, made this for themselves, their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-08400139.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a couple, Aurelius Onesimus and Florentina, for themselves and their descendants and freedpersons, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08400140.text_edition: \n Quintus Calavius Quinti libertus Anoptes Calavia Quinti liberta Salvia Marcus Avilius Marci filius Latinus\n
EDCS-08400140.translation: Quintus Calavius, freedman of Quintus; Calavia Salvia, freedwoman of Quintus; Marcus Avilius Latinus, son of Marcus.
EDCS-08400140.summary: This inscription lists the names of three individuals, likely freedmen and their patrons or family members, without specifying the monument type.

EDCS-08400141.text_edition: \n Quinto Granio Pollioni Q(uintus) Granius Libanus sibi et patrono monumentum fecit de suo\n
EDCS-08400141.translation: Quintus Granius Libanus made this monument for Quintus Granius Pollio and for himself at his own expense.
EDCS-08400141.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Quintus Granius Libanus for Quintus Granius Pollio and himself, indicating a relationship of patronage.

EDCS-08400142.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Heuresi fecit Valerius Alypus quae vixit annos II menses VIII\n
EDCS-08400142.translation: Valerius Alypus made this for the departed spirits of Heuresis, who lived 2 years and 8 months.
EDCS-08400142.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Heuresis, erected by Valerius Alypus and specifying her lifespan.

EDCS-08400143.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flaviae Benustae sic coniugi quae vixit annos LV menses VIIII dies \n
EDCS-08400143.translation: For the departed spirits of Flavia Benusta, wife, who lived 55 years, 9 months, and [number] days.
EDCS-08400143.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Flavia Benusta, specifying her relationship (wife) and lifespan.

EDCS-08400145.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Pomponiae Luci filiae Saturninae et Blaesiano filio eius et Plocamo marito libertis libertabusque et eis quos a Plocamo manumitti voluit\n
EDCS-08400145.translation: For the departed spirits of Pomponia Saturnina, daughter of Lucius, and her son Blaesianus, and her husband Plocamus, and their freedmen and freedwomen, and those whom Plocamus wished to manumit.
EDCS-08400145.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Pomponia Saturnina, her son, her husband, and their freedpersons, indicating family relationships and manumission intentions.

EDCS-08400153.text_edition: \n ------ su]nt Themistae Valerii Hestia sic sunt Erentae sic sunt sunt Hestiae sunt Eumeni Afri sunt sumptu \n
EDCS-08400153.translation: ... belong to Themista of Valerius; Hestia belongs; Erenta belongs; Hestia belongs; Eumenes the African belongs, at the expense of...
EDCS-08400153.summary: This fragmented inscription lists ownership or belonging for several individuals, possibly related to property or inheritance, with a mention of expense.

EDCS-08400154.text_edition: \n Crescentis Novi Claudi Veneriani Enni Crescentis Publi Enni Crescentis Primigeni Euni ante diem Kalendas Februarias Primigeni frater Primigeni mater\n
EDCS-08400154.translation: Of Crescentis Novi, son of Claudius Venerianus; of Ennius Crescentis; of Publius Ennius Crescentis; of Primigenius Euni; before the Kalends of February; for Primigenius, his brother; for Primigenius, his mother.
EDCS-08400154.summary: This inscription lists several names with patronymic and family relationships, possibly related to a funerary monument or property ownership, with a date reference.

EDCS-08400155.text_edition: \n Marcus Barbatius Epaphroditus arbiter ex compromisso diem dedit ad audiendum pactas summas in chalcidico Octaviano ab hora tertia in horam quintam actum Puteolis XII Dolabella \n
EDCS-08400155.translation: Marcus Barbatius Epaphroditus, acting as arbiter by compromise, set a date for hearing the agreed sums in the Octavian portico, from the third hour until the fifth hour. Acted in Puteoli on the 12th [day] of Dolabella.
EDCS-08400155.summary: This inscription records a legal proceeding or arbitration agreement involving Marcus Barbatius Epaphroditus, setting a time and place for a hearing in Puteoli.

EDCS-08400157.text_edition: \n Lucio Pedanio Secundo Histro Publio Palpellio consulibus Kalendas Martias Caius Sulpicius Faustus scripsit me debere Caio Statilio de Statilia Maiore Kalendas Martias arcarius Hordonianam actum Putiolis sic\n
EDCS-08400157.translation: On the Kalends of March, with Lucius Pedanius Secundus and Publius Palpellius as consuls, Caius Sulpicius Faustus wrote that I owe Caius Statius on behalf of Statilia Maior on the Kalends of March. Acted as cashier for Hordoniana in Puteoli. Thus.
EDCS-08400157.summary: This inscription details a debt owed by the writer to Caius Statius on behalf of Statilia Maior, recorded during the consulship of Pedanius Secundus and Palpellius, with reference to a cashier and location in Puteoli.

EDCS-08400158.text_edition: \n Vadimonium factum Zenoni Zenobi liberto Tyrio in III Idus Iunias primas Puteolis in foro ante aram Augusti Hordionianam hora tertia HS M CCmille ducentos nummum dari fide rogavit Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus fide promisit Zenon Zenobi libertus Tyrius actum Puteolis V Idus Iunias Fausto Cornelio Sulla Felice Quinto Marcio Barea Sorano consulibus\n
EDCS-08400158.translation: A summons was made to Zenon, freedman of Zenobius the Tyrian, for the 3rd day before the Ides of June, first, in Puteoli in the forum before the altar of Augustus. Hordioniana, third hour. Caius Sulpicius Cinnamus requested, as surety, that 1200 sesterces be given. Zenon, freedman of Zenobius the Tyrian, promised as surety. Acted in Puteoli on the 5th day before the Ides of June, with Faustus Cornelius Sulla Felix and Quintus Marcius Barea Soranus as consuls.
EDCS-08400158.summary: This inscription records a legal summons and surety agreement involving Zenon, a freedman, for a debt of 1200 sesterces, specifying the date, location (Puteoli), and consuls.

EDCS-08400159.text_edition: \n adesseque eos pridie Nonas s primas Puteolis in chalcidico ab hora tertia in horam am actum Puteolis XI Kalendas Marco Trebellio Lucio Annaeo Seneca consulibus\n
EDCS-08400159.translation: And that they be present at Puteoli in the chalcidicum on the day before the first Nones of March from the third hour until the eighth hour; at Puteoli, on the eleventh day before the Kalends of April, in the consulship of Marcus Trebellius and Lucius Annaeus Seneca.
EDCS-08400159.summary: This inscription records a decree or announcement concerning the presence of certain individuals at an event in Puteoli during the consulship of M. Trebellius and L. Annaeus Seneca (likely AD 51).

EDCS-08400161.text_edition: \n Titus Fundanius Optatus saenator sic populi Romani regionis possessor domino Herculi templum restituit recio sic Auffeeiana sic felix et tu\n
EDCS-08400161.translation: Titus Fundanius Optatus, senator and owner of the land in this district of the Roman people, restored the temple to the master Hercules, the temple of Auffia, and may it be fortunate, and you too.
EDCS-08400161.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple to Hercules and the Auffia temple by Titus Fundanius Optatus, a senator and landowner, likely in the imperial period.

EDCS-08500053.text_edition: \n Aulus Titinius Titinia Aulus Titinius Titini \n
EDCS-08500053.translation: Aulus Titinius Titinia, Aulus Titinius Titini.
EDCS-08500053.summary: This inscription appears to be a very brief record of names, possibly related to a family or ownership, the date of which is uncertain.

EDCS-08500055.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Gi Cariano Caecilio, likely a personal dedication or memorial, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-08500059.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Caeliuss libertus Caeli Rufiiae Proculae Redemptaia et Felicitas libertae ipsius Marco Pomponio o et Semproniae Sabinaeinae conus et liberis libertis libertabusque eorum locum donaberunt sic concesseruntque\n
EDCS-08500059.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Caelius, freedman of Caelius Rufus, and Procula Redemptaia and Felicitas, his freedwomen, and their freedmen and freedwomen, granted and conceded the place to them and their freedmen and freedwomen.
EDCS-08500059.summary: This funerary inscription records the granting of a burial plot by Caius Caelius and his freedwomen to themselves and their descendants, with a date that is not precisely determinable but likely imperial.

EDCS-08500060.text_edition: \n V consul II proconsul pater patriae collegi fabrum cur(ante) Marco Opilio \n
EDCS-08500060.translation: Consul for the second time, proconsul, father of the fatherland, collegium of the artisans, under the care of Marcus Opilius.
EDCS-08500060.summary: This inscription mentions official titles (Consul II, proconsul, pater patriae) and the involvement of a collegium of artisans under Marcus Opilius, suggesting a public work or monument from the imperial period.

EDCS-08500062.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aemilius Iuus fecit et \n
EDCS-08500062.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aemilius Iuus made this and...
EDCS-08500062.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording that Aemilius Iuus made something, likely a tomb or monument, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-08500063.text_edition: \n s Caius Cum us Lucius Cornelius lio Caius Geminius nus Aulus Livi us Marcus Mal Marcus Claudius Lucius Balerius sic Mamius Titus Annius \n
EDCS-08500063.translation: Caius Cumus, Lucius Cornelius, Caius Geminius, Aulus Livius, Marcus Mal..., Marcus Claudius, Lucius Valerius, Mammius, Titus Annius.
EDCS-08500063.summary: This inscription lists several names, possibly of members of a group or beneficiaries, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-08500064.text_edition: \n sibi et Aureliae Victoriae et incomparabilissimis onos Feli construibimus sic\n
EDCS-08500064.translation: We will build for ourselves and for Aurelia Victoria and for our incomparable [---].
EDCS-08500064.summary: This inscription records a promise or intention to build something for the authors and Aurelia Victoria, likely a private dedication or memorial from the imperial period.

EDCS-08500066.text_edition: \n ermius Valerius Aurelius Iunius V Tullius Flo\n
EDCS-08500066.translation: Germius Valerius Aurelius Junius V Tullius Flo...
EDCS-08500066.summary: This inscription appears to list names, likely of individuals involved in a project or commemoration, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-08500067.translation: To Adauctus, freedman of the Emperor, for Scribonianus.
EDCS-08500067.summary: This inscription is a dedication, possibly a monument or offering, made to Scribonianus by Adauctus, a freedman of the emperor.

EDCS-08500068.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus locum concessum ad sic Caio Iulio Sucesso Publius Ponponio sic Rogatianus fecit Publio Ponponio sic Marco fratri vene sic merenti qui vixit annis XX menses sic VII diebus XV\n
EDCS-08500068.translation: To the Divine Shades. Publius Ponponius Rogatianus granted this plot of land to Gaius Iulius Sucesso, his deserving brother, who lived for 20 years, 7 months, and 15 days.
EDCS-08500068.summary: This is a funerary inscription granting burial land to Gaius Iulius Sucesso, set up by his brother Publius Ponponius Rogatianus.

EDCS-08500075.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Salbiae sic Impestratae coniugi piae bene merenti quae vixit annos XXXXII menses dies XVIII Lollius Herodianus fecit\n
EDCS-08500075.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lollius Herodianus made this for his deserving wife, Salbia Impestrata, a pious woman, who lived for 42 years, 18 days.
EDCS-08500075.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Lollius Herodianus for his deceased wife, Salbia Impestrata.

EDCS-08500076.text_edition: \n Terentius Eunus libens concessit locum e Crinitae bene merenti quae vixit annis XVIIII mensibus VIIII diebus VIII\n
EDCS-08500076.translation: Terentius Eunus willingly granted this plot to Crinitus, deserving, who lived for 17 years, 9 months, and 8 days.
EDCS-08500076.summary: This funerary inscription records the voluntary granting of burial land by Terentius Eunus to Crinitus.

EDCS-08500079.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Paeteio Zosimo alumno vixit annos III menses III horas III\n
EDCS-08500079.translation: To the Divine Shades. Gaius Paeteius Zosimus, his ward, lived for 3 years, 3 months, and 3 hours.
EDCS-08500079.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Gaius Paeteius Zosimus, a ward, who died at a very young age.

EDCS-08500080.text_edition: \n Quinto Licinio Iusto Licinia Luci filia Prima merenti fecit et sibi liberis libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum Publio Adio Maximo |(centurioni) legionis XV Apollinaris Publius Adius Publi filius Arnensis Ferox patri fecit et sibi liberis libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum in fronte pedes XXV in agro pedes XX hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-08500080.translation: To the deserving Quinto Licinio Iusto. Licinia Prima, daughter of Lucius, made this for him, and for herself, their children, freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants. Publius Adius Ferox, son of Publius, from the Arnensis tribe, legionary centurion of the XV Apollinaris legion, made this for his father and for himself, their children, freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants. In the front 25 feet, in the land 20 feet. This monument will not follow an heir.
EDCS-08500080.summary: This is a complex funerary inscription detailing the erection of a tomb by Publius Adius Ferox for his father, Quinto Licinio Iusto, and for himself and his family, specifying dimensions and legal conditions.

EDCS-08500081.text_edition: \n Aurelio Fortunio qui vixit nnos plus minus XL in pace depositus VI\n
EDCS-08500081.translation: Aurelius Fortunius, who lived more or less 40 years, was laid to rest in peace on the 6th.
EDCS-08500081.summary: This is a funerary inscription noting the death and burial of Aurelius Fortunius, who lived approximately 40 years.

EDCS-08500082.translation: Fortunula, on the 7th day before the Kalends of November.
EDCS-08500082.summary: This inscription records a date, likely related to a funerary event or commemoration, for Fortunula.

EDCS-08500084.text_edition: \n Mindi Aemi abe sic salbus sic sis cum subiai \n
EDCS-08500084.translation: Mindi, son of Aemus, may you be healthy with your wife.
EDCS-08500084.summary: This is a personal greeting or wish for good health addressed to Mindi and his wife.

EDCS-08500085.text_edition: \n Lettae militis aureficis! filius eius Laurentius \n
EDCS-08500085.translation: To Letta, soldier, goldsmith! His son Laurentius [made this].
EDCS-08500085.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Laurentius to his father, Letta, who was a soldier and a goldsmith.

EDCS-08500086.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Tannonius Donatus in hoc munumento loco concesso sibi sarcophagum posuit si quis autem in hoc sarcofago sic aliut sic corpus intulerit imferet sic aerario populi Romani SS Lquinquaginta milia nummum\n
EDCS-08500086.translation: To the Divine Manes. Julius Tannonius Donatus, having been granted this place in this monument, erected a sarcophagus for himself. If anyone else should bring a body into this sarcophagus, they will pay a fine of 50,000 sesterces to the treasury of the Roman people.
EDCS-08500086.summary: A funerary inscription from Rome recording a man's self-provided sarcophagus and the penalty for unauthorized burials.

EDCS-08500087.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Antherianae alumnae Synforus Augusti libertus\n
EDCS-08500087.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Aelia Antheriana, his foster-daughter. Synforus, freedman of Augustus.
EDCS-08500087.summary: A funerary epitaph from Rome for Aelia Antheriana, dedicated by Synforus, a freedman of Augustus.

EDCS-08500088.text_edition: \n o Cai filio Stellatina Pollioni tribuno militum legionis II \n
EDCS-08500088.translation: To Gaius Pollio, son of Caius, of the Stellatina tribe, military tribune of the II Legion.
EDCS-08500088.summary: An inscription honouring Gaius Pollio, son of Caius, of the Stellatina tribe, who served as a military tribune in the II Legion.

EDCS-08500090.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aveieno Re Herculaneo coactori argentario et libertis libertabusque suis\n
EDCS-08500090.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Marcus Aveienus Re Herculaneus, tax collector, banker, and for his freedmen and freedwomen.
EDCS-08500090.summary: A funerary dedication from Rome for Marcus Aveienus Re Herculaneus, who held roles as a tax collector and banker.

EDCS-08500091.text_edition: \n Ex visu deo Epapho Aureliano Lucius Plotius Sabinus praetor designatus sodalis Titialis et Florentia Domitilla\n
EDCS-08500091.translation: In accordance with a vision of the god Epaphos. Lucius Plotius Sabinus, praetor-designate, and Florentia Domitilla, member of the Sodales Titiales.
EDCS-08500091.summary: An inscription recording a votive offering to the god Epaphos by Lucius Plotius Sabinus, a praetor-designate, and Florentia Domitilla, a member of the Sodales Titiales.

EDCS-08500093.summary: A fragment of a funerary inscription from Rome, initiated by Servilius Silanus for his wife.

EDCS-08500095.text_edition: \n Decimi Foli Sabiniani Foliae Decimi libertae Hilarae Clodiae |(mulieris) libertae Thalassae\n
EDCS-08500095.translation: For Hilara, freedwoman of Decimus Folius Sabinianus, and for Thalassa, freedwoman of the wife.
EDCS-08500095.summary: A funerary inscription honouring two freedwomen, Hilara and Thalassa.

EDCS-08500097.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Herenniae Marci filiae tillae matri optimae Atilius Pius filius\n
EDCS-08500097.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Herennia Tilla, daughter of Marcus, his most excellent mother. Atilius Pius, his son.
EDCS-08500097.summary: A funerary epitaph from Rome for Herennia Tilla, dedicated by her son Atilius Pius.

EDCS-08500099.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Calpurnio Aquilae Aelia Prisca et Antistius Ingenus filio karissimo bene merenti parentes fecerunt qui vixit nnis XVIII mensibus II diebus VIIII\n
EDCS-08500099.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Marcus Calpurnius Aquila. Aelia Prisca and Antistius Ingenus made this for their dearest son, who deserved well of them; he lived 18 years, 2 months, and 9 days.
EDCS-08500099.summary: A funerary monument from Rome for Marcus Calpurnius Aquila, detailing his age at death and dedicated by his parents.

EDCS-08500103.text_edition: \n Titus Flavius Augusti libertus Valens colliberto dignissimo \n
EDCS-08500103.translation: Titus Flavius Valens, freedman of Augustus, for his most worthy fellow freedman.
EDCS-08500103.summary: A funerary inscription from Rome dedicated by Titus Flavius Valens, a freedman of Augustus, to a fellow freedman.

EDCS-08500104.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Papiriae riae vixit annos III menses II dies \n
EDCS-08500104.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Papiria lived for 3 years, 2 months, and [ ] days.
EDCS-08500104.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young child named Papiria, indicating her lifespan.

EDCS-08500107.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudiae Calyces Claudia Spuri filia Merope matri piissimae et Tiberio Claudio Hermeroti patri indulgentissimo fecit et sibi et libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum orum \n
EDCS-08500107.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Claudia Calyces, daughter of Claudia, made this for her most dutiful mother Merope and her most indulgent father Tiberius Claudius Hermerotes, and for herself and for her freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-08500107.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Claudia Calyces for her parents, Merope and Tiberius Claudius Hermerotes, and also for herself and her descendants, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08500108.text_edition: \n Lucio Cornelio Crispio sic Lucius Fabius Luci libertus Rufus\n
EDCS-08500108.translation: To Lucius Cornelius Crispus, thus Lucius Fabius Rufus, freedman of Lucius.
EDCS-08500108.summary: This inscription records a dedication or marking of property, possibly funerary, for Lucius Cornelius Crispus, by his freedman Lucius Fabius Rufus.

EDCS-08500109.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Iulio Phileto haryspici sic regionis huius patrono bene merenti fecerunt vixit annis LXXX Lucius Iulius Agatario et Iulia Arescusa\n
EDCS-08500109.translation: To the spirits of the departed: To Lucius Julius Philetus, haruspex of this region, a deserving patron, they made this. He lived 80 years. Lucius Julius Agatarius and Iulia Arescusa [made this].
EDCS-08500109.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Julius Philetus, a haruspex and patron, set up by Lucius Julius Agatarius and Iulia Arescusa, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-08500110.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luci Mindi Damalei Matidiae Augustae filiae liberti procuratoris summarum\n
EDCS-08500110.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Of Lucius Mindius Damaleas, freedman and procurator of the highest [offices], to Matidia Augusta, daughter [of Augustus].
EDCS-08500110.summary: This is a funerary inscription possibly related to Matidia Augusta and her freedman Lucius Mindius Damaleas, who held administrative positions, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08500114.text_edition: \n Albulae ex voto suscepto Hermes Marci Armeni libertus\n
EDCS-08500114.translation: From Albula, according to a vow undertaken: Hermes, freedman of Marcus Armenius.
EDCS-08500114.summary: This inscription records a votive offering or dedication made by Hermes, freedman of Marcus Armenius, likely to a deity.

EDCS-08500116.text_edition: \n Pallanti vixit nnis XXII mensibus VI diebus VII Cosmus Augusti libertus et Tadia Publi filia Creperulla filio piissimo\n
EDCS-08500116.translation: To Pallanti, who lived 22 years, 6 months, and 7 days: Cosmus, freedman of Augustus, and Tadia, daughter of Publius Creperulla, [made this] for their most dutiful son.
EDCS-08500116.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Pallanti, set up by his parents Cosmus (a freedman of Augustus) and Tadia, indicating a date in the early Imperial period.

EDCS-08500117.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus coniugi have Epiteuxi secura et vale et ameris\n
EDCS-08500117.translation: To the spirits of the departed: To Marcus, to his wife Epiteuchis, [may you be] safe and farewell and loved.
EDCS-08500117.summary: This is a funerary epitaph expressing sentiments of love and farewell to Marcus and his wife Epiteuchis.
