EDCS-08500121.text_edition: \n Cnaeus Orchivius |(mulieris) libertus Dio in fronte pedes XIIX in agro XX\n
EDCS-08500121.translation: Cnaeus Orchivius, freedman of a woman, Dio, [in area] 18 feet in front and 20 in the field.
EDCS-08500121.summary: This inscription seems to record land boundaries or dimensions associated with Cnaeus Orchivius Dio, a freedman, possibly related to property or a burial plot.

EDCS-08500122.translation: Of Publius Silius, son of Marcus Criton, praetor.
EDCS-08500122.summary: This inscription likely records the name and office of Publius Silius, son of Marcus Criton, who held the position of praetor.

EDCS-08500124.text_edition: \n rilius simae fuit sine ulla querella AE M \n
EDCS-08500124.translation: C(aius) Julius Simae, her slave, lived without any complaint. A(ulus) M(artius?)
EDCS-08500124.summary: A funerary epitaph mentioning a slave named Simae, set up by C. Julius and A. Martius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08500126.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sentiae Victorinae Sentius s \n
EDCS-08500126.translation: To the Divine Manes of Sentia Victorina. Sentius [---]
EDCS-08500126.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Sentia Victorina by Sentius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08500127.translation: To the Divine Manes of Iulia Fortunata [---]
EDCS-08500127.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulia Fortunata, with the dedicant and further details lost, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08500131.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Aplanio Severino qui vixit annis VIII mensibus VI parentes bene merenti filio\n
EDCS-08500131.translation: To the Divine Manes of Iulius Aplanus Severinus, who lived 8 years and 6 months, his parents set this up for their deserving son.
EDCS-08500131.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young boy, Iulius Aplanus Severinus, set up by his parents, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08500133.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Naevia Myrtis vixit menses III dies VII Naevius Maximus trierarchus filiae infelicissimae fecit\n
EDCS-08500133.translation: To the Divine Manes. Naevia Myrtis lived 3 months and 7 days. Naevius Maximus, trierarch, made this for his most unhappy daughter.
EDCS-08500133.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young child, Naevia Myrtis, set up by her father, Naevius Maximus, a trierarch, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08500134.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Munio Puteolano qui vixit annis XXVII mensibus XI diebus XXII amatores huius fecerunt merenti\n
EDCS-08500134.translation: To the Divine Manes. Munius Puteolanus, who lived 27 years, 11 months, and 22 days. His lovers set this up for him, a deserving man.
EDCS-08500134.summary: A funerary epitaph for Munius Puteolanus, set up by his lovers, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08500135.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Iuli Petroniani manipularis ex III Silvano natione Alexandrino militavit nnos XXII vixit nnos XXXX Tiberius Claudius Iullus heres bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08500135.translation: To the Divine Manes. Caius Julius Petronianus, a recruit from the Third Cohort, Silvanus by nation, from Alexandria, served for 22 years and lived 40 years. Tiberius Claudius Iullus, his heir, made this for the deserving man.
EDCS-08500135.summary: A military funerary epitaph for Caius Julius Petronianus, an Alexandrian recruit, set up by his heir, Tiberius Claudius Iullus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08500136.summary: A simple funerary epitaph recording the age of death for Antigonus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08500137.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Crescentiano patri fili fecerunt\n
EDCS-08500137.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Iulius Crescens, their father. His sons set this up.
EDCS-08500137.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulius Crescens, set up by his sons, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08500138.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Noniae Publi filiae Optatae et Caio Iulio Artemoni parentibus libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum Caius Iulius Cai filius Optatus filius\n
EDCS-08500138.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Nonia Optata, daughter of Publius, and for Gaius Julius Artemon, their parents, freedmen and freedwomen, and for their descendants. Gaius Julius Optatus, son of Gaius.
EDCS-08500138.summary: A complex funerary epitaph dedicated to Nonia Optata and Gaius Julius Artemon, likely indicating a family including freedpersons, set up by Gaius Julius Optatus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08500140.text_edition: \n Lucio Aurelio Dorotheo viro egregio patrono coloniae omnibus honoribus oneribus muneribusque honeste perfuncto ordo splendidissimus studioso sui et patriae\n
EDCS-08500140.translation: To Lucius Aurelius Dorotheus, an outstanding man, patron of the colony, who honestly discharged all honors, burdens, and duties, the most distinguished council, devoted to itself and the fatherland.
EDCS-08500140.summary: This is a dedication from the council of a colony to their patron, Lucius Aurelius Dorotheus.

EDCS-08500142.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tertia, a freedwoman.

EDCS-08500144.text_edition: \n Thesprotus libertus vixit annos IV e<t=E> menses V \n
EDCS-08500144.translation: Thesprotus, freedman, lived 4 years and 5 months.
EDCS-08500144.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of a freedman named Thesprotus.

EDCS-08500147.summary: This inscription likely records the name of the owner or dedicator of a monument.

EDCS-08500225.text_edition: \n Sextus Verginius Anteros locum emit in fronte pedes XXIIII in agro pedes XII\n
EDCS-08500225.translation: Sextus Verginius Anteros bought a plot measuring 24 feet in front and 12 feet in depth.
EDCS-08500225.summary: This record details a property transaction by Sextus Verginius Anteros.

EDCS-08600067.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XIIII consuli IIII patri patriae corpus urinatorum Ostiensium sua pecunia posuit\n
EDCS-08600067.translation: The body of divers of Ostia placed this at their own expense for the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, chief priest, in his 14th year of tribunician power, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-08600067.summary: This is a dedication by the divers of Ostia to Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely dating to his 14th year of tribunician power.

EDCS-08600070.text_edition: \n Secundenius sic Iulianus DECON inlivato amore I L NF N titulum iure donationis posuit sibi ipso et Pupeniae Zoe coniugi\n
EDCS-08600070.translation: Secundenius, also known as Iulianus, erected this monument, inspired by strong love, by right of gift, for himself and for his wife Pupenia Zoe.
EDCS-08600070.summary: This is a dedication by Secundenius Iulianus for himself and his wife Pupenia Zoe.

EDCS-08600081.text_edition: \n Lucius Lallius Luci libertus Licinus testamento fieri iussit sibi et Lallio Luci liberto Alexandro et Lucio Lallio Luci liberto Licino et Luciae Lalliae Luci libertae Salviae et Acestiae Spuri filiae Gemellae et Lalliae Luci libertae Soterini et Lucio Acestio Luci liberto Salvittoni heredes familiae exterae sequantur\n
EDCS-08600081.translation: Lucius Lallius Licinus, freedman of Lucius, ordered by his will that this be made for himself, and for Lucius Lallius Alexander, freedman of Lucius, and for Lucius Lallius Licinus, freedman of Lucius, and for Lucia Lallia Salvia, freedwoman of Lucius, and for Acestia Gemella, daughter of Spurius, and for Lallia Soterini, freedwoman of Lucius, and for Lucius Acestius Salvittone, freedman of Lucius; let the heirs of the family outside follow.
EDCS-08600081.summary: This is a funerary inscription detailing who the monument was for and naming the heirs of the family, established by Lucius Lallius Licinus.

EDCS-08600082.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Caesar, son of Severus Pius Augustus...
EDCS-08600082.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Caesar, son of Severus.

EDCS-08600085.text_edition: \n Deivo Iulio iussu populi Romani e lege Rufrena\n
EDCS-08600085.translation: To the deified Julius, by order of the Roman people, according to the law of Rufrenus.
EDCS-08600085.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the deified Julius Caesar, enacted by popular order and law.

EDCS-08600086.translation: To Hadrian Augustus, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-08600086.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Hadrian, likely from the second century CE.

EDCS-08600091.text_edition: \n Pro salute Augusti et reditu et victoria Hermes Augustalis signum cum statera et ponderibus coloniae Minturnensium dono dedit\n
EDCS-08600091.translation: For the health of Augustus and for his return and victory, Hermes Augustalis dedicated a statue with scales and weights to the colony of Minturnae as a gift.
EDCS-08600091.summary: This inscription records a dedication of a statue, scales, and weights by Hermes Augustalis to the colony of Minturnae, likely in the 1st or 2nd century CE, for the emperor's well-being and success.

EDCS-08600095.text_edition: \n Pompeio Appio Faustino viro clarissimo correctori Campaniae\n
EDCS-08600095.translation: To Pompeius Appius Faustinus, most distinguished man, corrector of Campania.
EDCS-08600095.summary: This inscription honors Pompeius Appius Faustinus, who held the office of corrector in Campania, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08600096.text_edition: \n Sexti Manli Sexti filii Fabia Quintani ex testamento arbitratu Publi Acuti Sexti filii Fabia\n
EDCS-08600096.translation: From the will of Sextus Manlius, son of Sextus, of the Fabia tribe, according to the arbitration of Publius Acutius, son of Sextus, of the Fabia tribe.
EDCS-08600096.summary: This inscription records the execution of a will based on the arbitration of Publius Acutius, concerning Sextus Manlius, likely from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-08600097.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sextia Gorge Primitivo filio dolcissimo sic fecit\n
EDCS-08600097.translation: To the departed spirits. Sextia Gorge made this for her most sweet son Primitivus.
EDCS-08600097.summary: This is a tombstone inscription set up by Sextia Gorge for her son Primitivus, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08600098.text_edition: \n Lucio Magio Sexti filio Aemilia Urgulaniano p |(centurioni) speculatorum primo pilo legionis praefecto vexillariorum legionum trium VIIII V IIII tribuno cohortis vigilum tribuno cohortis urbanae Sexto Urgulanio Sexti filio IIviro auguri Rauricae Menturnis sic praefecto fabrum Corneliae Publi filiae Corneliae Cai filiae\n
EDCS-08600098.translation: To Lucius Magius, son of Sextus, of the Aemilia tribe, Urgulanius, centurion of the praetorian guard, first-rank centurion of the legion, prefect of detachments of three legions (VII, VIIII, V, IIII), tribune of a cohort of the Vigiles, tribune of the Urban cohort, Sextus Urgulanius, son of Sextus, duovir, augur of Raurica, of Minturnae, executor of the engineers for Cornelia, daughter of Publius, and Cornelia, daughter of Caius.
EDCS-08600098.summary: This complex inscription lists the career and family connections of Lucius Magius and Sextus Urgulanius, holding numerous military and civic offices, likely from the late 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08600099.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberio Claudio Lenaeo Augustali Tiberius Claudius Privatus liberto bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08600099.translation: To the departed spirits. Tiberius Claudius Privatus made this for his well-deserving freedman Tiberius Claudius Lenaeus, Augustalis.
EDCS-08600099.summary: This is a tombstone inscription set up by Tiberius Claudius Privatus for his freedman Tiberius Claudius Lenaeus, who held the position of Augustalis, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08600100.text_edition: \n Marco Maio mulieris lib(erto) Graphico Augustali Claudia Pia coniux Marcus Maius Pinna Auctus Cai Caerelli Vitalis Marcus Maius Vitalis pa<t=I><e=L>r Maia Zosime ma<t=H>er nnorum XXVII \n
EDCS-08600100.translation: To Marcus Maius, freedman of a woman, Graphicus, Augustalis. Claudia Pia, his wife, Marcus Maius Pinna Auctus, son of Caius Caerellus Vitalis, Marcus Maius Vitalis, his father, Maia mother, (and) the sons, 27 years old.
EDCS-08600100.summary: This inscription commemorates Marcus Maius Graphicus, an Augustalis and freedman, and lists his family members, including his wife, son, father, mother, and siblings, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08600103.translation: Aulus Opinius, son of Caius, Sulla.
EDCS-08600103.summary: This is a simple inscription identifying Aulus Opinius Sulla, son of Caius, likely a personal marker or ownership inscription from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-08600104.text_edition: \n Thymeli habe sic Dis Manibus sacrum Tito Flavio Proculo homini innocentissimo qui vixit annos XXXVIII menses V dies XXV Fortunula coniugi bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08600104.translation: To the departed spirits. Sacred. Fortunula made this for her most innocent husband Titus Flavius Proculus, who lived 38 years, 5 months, and 25 days, a deserving wife.
EDCS-08600104.summary: This tombstone inscription is dedicated by Fortunula to her husband Titus Flavius Proculus, providing his age at death and her affectionate description of him, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08600107.text_edition: \n Publius Confuleius Publi et Marci libertus Sabbio sagarius domum hanc ab solo usque ad summum fecit architecto Tito Safinio Titi filio Falerna Pollione\n
EDCS-08600107.translation: Publius Confuleius Sabbio, freedman of Publius and Marcus, a sagarius (seller of garlands or similar), built this house from the ground up, with Titus Safinius Pollio, son of Titus, of the Falerna tribe, as architect.
EDCS-08600107.summary: This inscription records the construction of a house by a freedman, naming the architect and his tribe, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08600109.text_edition: \n Publi Confulei Publi et Marci liberti Pampili ossa heic sic sita sunt Publi Confulei Publi liberti Philerotis ossa heic sic sita sunt Sexti Atili Sexti liberti oni ossa heic sic sita sunt\n
EDCS-08600109.translation: The remains of Publius Confuleius Pampilus, freedman of Publius and Marcus, lie here. The remains of Publius Confuleius Phileros, freedman of Publius, lie here. The remains of [---]oni, freedman of Sextus Atilius, lie here.
EDCS-08600109.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of several individuals, identified by their patrons and freedman status, likely a family or household.

EDCS-08600110.text_edition: \n Numerius Cannutius Auctus Statoniae Liberalini concubinae et suis posterisque\n
EDCS-08600110.translation: For Numerius Cannutius Auctus, for Statonia Libera, his concubine, and for his own descendants.
EDCS-08600110.summary: This funerary inscription dedicates a burial place to a concubine and descendants by Numerius Cannutius Auctus.

EDCS-08600112.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marci Mari Fortunati Maria Procula uxor bene merenti\n
EDCS-08600112.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Marcus Marius Fortunatus. By Maria Procula, his wife, for one who deserved well.
EDCS-08600112.summary: This is a dedication to the Divine Spirits (Manes) by a wife for her deceased husband, Marcus Marius Fortunatus.

EDCS-08600113.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Proiae filiae Proius Epici filius Augustalis\n
EDCS-08600113.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Proia, daughter of Proius. Augustalis.
EDCS-08600113.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Proia, daughter of Proius, by Proius Epici.

EDCS-08600114.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucio Procilio Luci filio Primigenio qui vixit annos XVIIII dies XXIII Procilio Luci filio Severo Secundae matri Ceriali liberto Procilius Severus sibi suisque\n
EDCS-08600114.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. For Lucius Procilius Primigenius, son of Lucius, who lived 19 years and 23 days. For Lucius Procilius Severus, son of Lucius. For their mother Cerialis. For Procilius Severus, his freedman, for himself and his own.
EDCS-08600114.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Lucius Procilius Primigenius and his family, including his parents and a freedman, providing his age at death.

EDCS-08600119.text_edition: \n Vadimonium factum Cassio Sereno in V Idus Martias hora nona ante Hordionianam actum Puteolis\n
EDCS-08600119.translation: A summons was made for Cassius Serenus for the 5th day before the Ides of March, at the ninth hour, before the fixed time, enacted at Puteoli.
EDCS-08600119.summary: This inscription records a legal summons (vadimonium) issued in Puteoli on a specific date and time.

EDCS-08600121.text_edition: \n Silano Caio Sulpicio Galba consulibus X Kalendas Septembres Caius Sulpicius supra scripta sunt s Cinnamus esse HS IIII HS IIIII in X Kalendis Septembribus\n
EDCS-08600121.translation: Under the consulship of Silanus and Gaius Sulpicius Galba, on the 10th day before the Kalends of September. Gaius Sulpicius. The above are written. Cinnamus. For 4 sesterces. For 5 sesterces. On the 10th day before the Kalends of September.
EDCS-08600121.summary: This inscription, likely a record of a financial transaction or debt, is dated to the consulship of Silanus and Galba and specifies amounts in sesterces.

EDCS-08600123.text_edition: \n Cai Nestori Eueni Cai Nestori Felicis Eueni Thesprotis sic Numeri Eueni Fenti Numenii Fenti Quintiani Quintiani Numeri Nenciani Thesproti\n
EDCS-08600123.translation: For Caius Nestor Euenus. For Caius Nestor Felix. For Euenus Thesprotis. For Numerius Euenus. For Fentius Numenius. For Fentius Quintianus. For Quintianus. For Numerius. For Nencianus. For Thesprotis.
EDCS-08600123.summary: This inscription appears to be a list of names, possibly related to inheritance, property, or a shared funerary monument, tracing lineage through multiple generations.

EDCS-08600195.text_edition: \n Et deis et deabus sacrum Resia Titi filia Rufa\n
EDCS-08600195.translation: Sacred to both gods and goddesses. By Resia Rufa, daughter of Titus.
EDCS-08600195.summary: This is a dedication to both male and female deities by a woman named Resia Rufa.

EDCS-08600197.text_edition: \n ola Quintus Lucretius Numerianus IIIIviri iure dicundo senatum consuluerunt Ferentini in curia Augusta basilicae Bar April scribendo adfuerunt Titus Pompeius Potamon Publius LuSecundus Caius Safinius Regillus quod Aulus Caecilius Quirinalis filius nomine Sexti Alli Seadsistente ipso et libellum desideri sui proponente verba fecit usum eum et ceterorum civium precibus et reverentia quam memoriae patris habere debeat velle pecuniae ab Allio Augustale oblatae adicere in ius avitarium fundum SM cum casa et taberna \n
EDCS-08600197.translation: Quintus Lucretius Numerianus, IIIIvir for administering justice, consulted the senate in Ferentini in the curia Augusta of the basilica. Titus Pompeius Potamon, Publius LuSecundus, and Caius Safinius Regillus were present for the recording. Because Aulus Caecilius Quirinalis, at the request of Sextus Alli, with Sextus Alli himself attending and presenting a petition for his desires, spoke regarding the use that he and other citizens should have of the money offered by Allius, wishing to add to it, and the ancestral farm with house and shop from Allius.
EDCS-08600197.summary: This inscription records a senatorial decree from Ferentini, where Quintus Lucretius Numerianus, a local magistrate, consulted the senate regarding a donation from Allius to fund public use and an ancestral farm.

EDCS-08600205.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Axilio Grato Caius Callaeus Cordus pater et Axilia Grata mater filio vixit annis II mensibus VIIII diebus XXVI sibi et suis fecit\n
EDCS-08600205.translation: To the departed spirits. Caius Callaeus Cordus and Axilia Grata, his parents, made this for their son Lucius Axilius Gratus, who lived 2 years, 9 months, and 26 days. They made it for themselves and their family.
EDCS-08600205.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Axilius Gratus, set up by his parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08600206.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudiae Hispanillae Flavius Hermes uxori\n
EDCS-08600206.translation: To the departed spirits. Flavius Hermes for his wife Claudia Hispanilla.
EDCS-08600206.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Claudia Hispanilla, dedicated by her husband Flavius Hermes.

EDCS-08600208.text_edition: \n Hateria Eucharis liberta Caio Rufio Cai liberto Herm\n
EDCS-08600208.translation: Hateria Eucharis, freedwoman of Caius Rufius, for Herm[---], freedman of Caius Rufius.
EDCS-08600208.summary: This inscription records Hateria Eucharis, a freedwoman, setting up an inscription potentially for another freedman, Herm[---], indicating a relationship or shared benefaction.

EDCS-08600209.text_edition: \n Herenni Quinti filii Publilia Nasonis testamento fieri iussit\n
EDCS-08600209.translation: Publilia Nasonis ordered this to be made in her will for Herennius Quinti filius.
EDCS-08600209.summary: This inscription records a posthumous commission by Publilia Nasonis in her will for Herennius Quinti filius.

EDCS-08600211.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Popiliae Priscae Popilius Eutychianus heres\n
EDCS-08600211.translation: To the departed spirits, sacred to Popilia Prisca. Popilius Eutychianus, the heir.
EDCS-08600211.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Popilia Prisca, set up by her heir Popilius Eutychianus.

EDCS-08600212.text_edition: \n Marco Ulpio Phoibo sic marito bene merenti Cussia Sabina coniunx\n
EDCS-08600212.translation: For Marcus Ulpius Phoebus, her deserving husband. Cussia Sabina, his wife.
EDCS-08600212.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Ulpius Phoebus, dedicated by his wife Cussia Sabina.

EDCS-08600214.text_edition: \n ------ quae basilicam saevis]sima persecutione derutam de suis propriis refecit vixit annos XXXIIII menses X Valerius Gaius maritus coniugi dulcissimae feci\n
EDCS-08600214.translation: [---] who rebuilt the basilica, destroyed during a most savage persecution, from her own private means. She lived 34 years and 10 months. Valerius Gaius, her husband, made this for his most sweet wife.
EDCS-08600214.summary: This funerary epitaph records Valerius Gaius dedicating a monument to his wife, who rebuilt a basilica from her own funds after persecution, and notes her lifespan.

EDCS-08700828.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flaviae Priscae Marcus Ulpius Augusti libertus Abascantus bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08700828.translation: To the departed spirits, sacred to Flavia Prisca. Marcus Ulpius Augusti libertus Abascantus made this for her, deserving.
EDCS-08700828.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavia Prisca, dedicated by Marcus Ulpius Abascantus, a freedman of Augustus.

EDCS-08900144.text_edition: \n obitus Lucius Vettius |(mulieris) libertus Alexander @(obita) Vettia Luci filia Polla Vettia Luci liberta Eleutheris Vettia |(mulieris) liberta Hospita\n
EDCS-08900144.translation: Died: Lucius Vettius Alexander, freedman of the woman. Died: Vettia Polla, daughter of Lucius Vettius. Vettia Eleutheris, freedwoman of Lucius Vettius. Vettia Hospita, freedwoman of the woman.
EDCS-08900144.summary: This inscription lists the deaths of several individuals associated with the Vettius family, including freedmen and a daughter, likely marking a family burial plot.

EDCS-08900145.text_edition: \n Salvideni liberti HS MMviginti milia arbitratu Philadelphi\n
EDCS-08900145.translation: From Salvidenus, freedman, 20,000 sesterces by the decision of Philadelphus.
EDCS-08900145.summary: This inscription records a financial transaction or bequest of 20,000 sesterces from the freedman Salvidenus, as decided by Philadelphus.

EDCS-08900146.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Fuficuleno Icelo Iuniori et Fuficulenae Antiochidi matri eius Quintus Fuficulenus Icelus Senior coniugi et filio eius\n
EDCS-08900146.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Quintus Fuficulenus Senior made this for his wife Quintus Fuficulenus Iunior and his mother Fuficulena Antiochidis, and for his son.
EDCS-08900146.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Quintus Fuficulenus Senior to his deceased wife, son, and mother.

EDCS-08900150.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fuficia Prosoce Fuficiae Hedoniae patronae su(a)e fecit\n
EDCS-08900150.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Fuficia Prosoce made this for her patroness Fuficia Hedonia.
EDCS-08900150.summary: This funerary inscription records Fuficia Prosoce dedicating a monument to her patroness Fuficia Hedonia.

EDCS-08900152.text_edition: \n Marcus Pupullius Marci Hermia hoc monumentum\n
EDCS-08900152.translation: Marcus Pupullius Hermia, son of Marcus, made this monument.
EDCS-08900152.summary: This inscription records Marcus Pupullius Hermia, son of Marcus, as the maker of this monument.

EDCS-08900153.text_edition: \n Marc-- Pet Marci libert-- D Petti\n
EDCS-08900153.translation: Marcus [---] Pet, freedman of Marcus [---], D. Petti [---]
EDCS-08900153.summary: This is a fragmented inscription likely recording a freedman of Marcus and possibly a person named Petti.

EDCS-08900157.text_edition: \n Cocceia Nymphe fecit sibi et Cocceio Eroti patrono et ilio Eroti viro suo et iae Speculae collibertae et libertis libertabusque nostris ta est Sempronia Publi liberta Adiecta quae vixit annis XVIII\n
EDCS-08900157.translation: Cocceia Nymphe made this for herself, her patron Cocceius Eros, her husband Eros, her freedwoman colleague Iae Specula, and for our freedmen and freedwomen. Adiecta, freedwoman of Sempronius Publius, lived 18 years.
EDCS-08900157.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Cocceia Nymphe for herself and her family, patrons, and freedpersons, also recording the death of Adiecta at age 18.

EDCS-08900158.text_edition: \n Lucius Marcius Princeps monumentum fecit sibi et libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum hoc monumentum sive sepulchrum est heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-08900158.translation: Lucius Marcius Princeps made this monument for himself, and for his freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants; this is a monument or tomb, it will not be subject to inheritance claims.
EDCS-08900158.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Lucius Marcius Princeps establishing a monument for himself, his freedpersons, and their descendants, with a clause against inheritance claims.

EDCS-08900160.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caesiae Secundillae dulcissimae piissimae quae decessit CRANIATILEA mater bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08900160.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld and to the memory of Caesias Secundilla, most sweet, most dutiful, who died [---]. Her mother made this for a deserving person.
EDCS-08900160.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by the mother to her deceased daughter, Caesias Secundilla, praising her virtues.

EDCS-08900162.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription recording the burial of Coponius, who died at the age of 12.

EDCS-08900164.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et memoriae sacrum Pompeiae Maximae vixit annis XXX mensibus VII Nemonius Irenio coniugi bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08900164.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld and to the memory: Nemonius Irenio made this for his deserving wife, Pompeia Maxima, who lived 30 years and 7 months.
EDCS-08900164.summary: This funerary inscription records Nemonius Irenio dedicating a monument to his wife, Pompeia Maxima, who lived 30 years and 7 months.

EDCS-08900165.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aciliae Primigeniae uxori optimae Marcus Circius Servaeus\n
EDCS-08900165.translation: To the Divine Shades. Marcus Circius Servaeus [dedicated this] to his most excellent wife, Acilia Primigenia.
EDCS-08900165.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a husband to his wife, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08900166.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Faustillae coniugi incomparabili quae vixit nnis XX mensibus X diebus XXV horis VI sine ulla discordia Marcus Valerius Felicissimus veteranus Augusti cum qua vixit annis VIII animae dulci bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08900166.translation: To the Divine Shades. Marcus Valerius Felicissimus, a veteran of the Emperor, made this for his incomparable wife, Julia Faustilla, who lived 20 years, 10 months, 25 days, and 6 hours, without any discord. He lived with her for 8 years; to his sweet soul, who deserved well, he dedicated this.
EDCS-08900166.summary: A funerary epitaph by a veteran to his wife, emphasizing her virtue and their happy marriage, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08900168.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Luci Vetti Threpti Augustalis Capuae\n
EDCS-08900168.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Lucius Vettius Threptus, Augustalis of Capua.
EDCS-08900168.summary: A funerary dedication by Lucius Vettius Threptus, identified as an Augustalis, for unknown recipients, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08900169.text_edition: \n Sexti Allidi Sexti fili Fabia Rufi (ex) cohortibus praetoriis missus ossa hic sita sunt\n
EDCS-08900169.translation: Sextus Allidius, son of Sextus, descendant of Rufus, who was discharged from the Praetorian Guard, his bones lie here.
EDCS-08900169.summary: An epitaph marking the burial place of Sextus Allidius, a former member of the Praetorian Guard, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08900172.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus bene merente sic C<o=U>rnutius Maximus ex III Marte fecit Lappio Secundo fratri suo ex III Athen<o=U>neice sic \n
EDCS-08900172.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Cornutius Maximus from the Third Cohort of Mars made this for his brother Lappius Secundus, also from the Third Cohort, and for Athenoice. So it is.
EDCS-08900172.summary: A funerary dedication by Cornutius Maximus to his brother Lappius Secundus and Athenoice, both apparently members of the Third Cohort of Mars, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08900173.text_edition: \n Titus Heterenus Titi filius C quinqueIAVVIES PRIM\n
EDCS-08900173.translation: Titus Heterenus, son of Titus, of the tribe Camilia...
EDCS-08900173.summary: The beginning of an inscription identifying Titus Heterenus, son of Titus, and his tribal affiliation, with the text cut off, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08900174.text_edition: \n Caius Vibius Cai filius Macer Caius Trebatios Rufio faciundum curaverunt\n
EDCS-08900174.translation: Gaius Vibius Macer, son of Gaius, and Gaius Trebatius Rufus arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-08900174.summary: A funerary dedication commissioned by Gaius Vibius Macer and Gaius Trebatius Rufus for unknown recipients, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08900175.text_edition: \n Brittius Marci libertus Alexander vilicus et Brittia Marci liberta Apamia\n
EDCS-08900175.translation: Brittius Alexander, freedman of Marcius, the farm manager, and Brittia Apamia, freedwoman of Marcius.
EDCS-08900175.summary: An inscription naming two freedmen, Alexander and Apamia, associated with Marcius, likely from the Imperial period and possibly a funerary dedication or property marker.

EDCS-08900176.translation: Marcus Ennius Elpidius, farm manager of anatocismus.
EDCS-08900176.summary: An inscription identifying Marcus Ennius Elpidius as a farm manager, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08900177.text_edition: \n Amplifice ut dolui monumentum hoc coniugi dono maestus lubenti animo praemia digna bonae haud data pro bonitate mea sunt sed data pro re Monniae |(mulieris) libertae Arbusculae ossa\n
EDCS-08900177.translation: Amplification: as much as I grieved, I have given this monument to my wife, Monnia, freedwoman of Arbuscula, with a willing spirit. Worthy rewards for her goodness have not been given by me, but have been given according to my means. Her bones.
EDCS-08900177.summary: A funerary dedication by a grieving husband to his wife, Monnia, a freedwoman, expressing his love and the limits of his ability to honor her, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08900179.text_edition: \n Lucio Cassio Luci filio Palatina Cereali praefecto fabrum auguri quaestori curatori operum publicorum et locorum primo facto IIviro quinquiens quinquennali curatori aquae Augustae huic universa pleps cum gladiatorium munus [Neroni] Caesari Augusto in amphitheatro edidit Cassia Cale Cereali filio piissimo\n
EDCS-08900179.translation: To Lucius Cassius Cerealis, son of Lucius, of the Palatine tribe, prefect of the engineers, augur, quaestor, curator of public works and lands, first appointed IIvir quinquennalis, curator of the Augustan aqueduct; to him the entire populace presented a gladiatorial show in the amphitheater in honor of Nero Caesar Augustus; from Cassia Cale, to her most devoted son.
EDCS-08900179.summary: This inscription honors Lucius Cassius Cerealis, detailing his extensive public career and a gladiatorial show given in his honor by the populace, likely in the late 1st century CE.

EDCS-08900180.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marci Laecani Achilliani vixit annis XXIX mensibus IIII diebus XV Polycarpus libertus patrono merenti\n
EDCS-08900180.translation: To the departed spirits of Marcus Laecanius Achillianus, who lived 29 years, 4 months, and 15 days. Polycarpus, his freedman, for a deserving patron.
EDCS-08900180.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Marcus Laecanius Achillianus, detailing his age at death and set up by his freedman, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900181.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Haeuremoni sic vixit annis IIII mensibus VIIII Publius Aelius Haeuremo sic pater filio bene merenti\n
EDCS-08900181.translation: To the departed spirits of Publius Aelius Haeuremo, who lived 4 years and 9 months. Publius Aelius Haeuremo, his father, for his well-deserving son.
EDCS-08900181.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for a young boy, Publius Aelius Haeuremo, detailing his age at death and set up by his father, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900182.summary: This is a brief funerary inscription identifying Marcus Blossius Miccus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900183.text_edition: \n Marco vixit annos V Clunia Ianuaria filio dulcissimo\n
EDCS-08900183.translation: To Marcus, who lived 5 years. Clunia Ianuaria, for her most beloved son.
EDCS-08900183.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for a child named Marcus, detailing his age at death and set up by his mother, Clunia Ianuaria, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900184.translation: Marcus Messius Heraclida, freedman of Marcus.
EDCS-08900184.summary: This is a funerary or dedicatory inscription identifying Marcus Messius Heraclida, a freedman, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900185.text_edition: \n Lucio Acilio Strabonis liberto Nicephoro Lucius Acilius Glyptus et Acilia Hagne patrono bene merenti\n
EDCS-08900185.translation: To Nicephorus, freedman of Lucius Acilius Strabo. Lucius Acilius Glyptus and Acilia Hagne, for a deserving patron.
EDCS-08900185.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by patrons for their freedman Nicephorus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900186.text_edition: \n Diomedes dispensator magister Laribus et familiae de suo fecit\n

EDCS-08900190.text_edition: \n Maria Mari Marionis Veneri votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-08900190.translation: Diomedes, the administrator, made this at his own expense for the Lares and his household.
EDCS-08900190.summary: This is a dedication of a private religious offering made by Diomedes, an administrator, for his household gods and family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900191.summary: This is a simple ownership or funerary inscription identifying Marcus Mussidius Felix, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900192.summary: This inscription records the name Lucius Autronius Ethymus, likely a dedication or ownership mark.

EDCS-08900193.text_edition: \n Sexti Patulci Felicis Sexti Patulci Felicis\n
EDCS-08900193.translation: Sextus Patulcius Felix, [son of] Sextus Patulcius Felix.
EDCS-08900193.summary: This inscription records the name Sextus Patulcius Felix twice, possibly indicating a family name or a very strong personal identifier.

EDCS-08900194.summary: This inscription records the name Quintus Iunius Philadespotes, likely a dedication or ownership mark.

EDCS-08900195.summary: This inscription records the name Marcus Helvius Erotis, likely a dedication or ownership mark.

EDCS-08900196.translation: Of Marcus, son of Co[---], Fru[---].
EDCS-08900196.summary: This inscription records the abbreviated names of Marcus and possibly his father and another individual, likely a dedication or ownership mark.

EDCS-08900800.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Anniae Abitae sic Admetus uxori \n
EDCS-08900800.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Admetus, husband to Annia Abita, made this.
EDCS-08900800.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording that Admetus set up a monument to his wife Annia Abita.

EDCS-08900801.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Potamoni Augusti liberto a pacticiis Festus Augusti libertus tabularius rationis chartariae heres et coherhedhes sic\n
EDCS-08900801.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Festus, freedman of Augustus and tabularius of the paper accounts office, his heir and co-heir, made this.
EDCS-08900801.summary: This funerary inscription records that Festus, a freedman of Augustus and an official in the accounts office, set up a monument to himself and designated his heir(s).

EDCS-08900802.text_edition: \n Statilia Titi liberta Ploce uxor Felicis Tauri nomenclatoris vixit annis XVII\n
EDCS-08900802.translation: Ploce, freedwoman of Statilia, wife of Felix Taurus, a nomenclator, lived for 17 years.
EDCS-08900802.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the life and death of Ploce, freedwoman of Statilia and wife of the nomenclator Felix Taurus.

EDCS-08900804.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpiae Beronices fecit Claudia Tiberi filia Ulpia Marc\n
EDCS-08900804.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Claudia Ulpia Marc, daughter of Tiberius, made this for Ulpia Beronice.
EDCS-08900804.summary: This funerary inscription records that Claudia Ulpia Marc, daughter of Tiberius, set up a monument for Ulpia Beronice.

EDCS-08900809.text_edition: \n us Cai libertus Athenio ci thenim s Cai libertus cius ssi Celer |(mulieris) liberta Amoena \n
EDCS-08900809.translation: Caius, freedman of [---]us, Athenio; Caius, freedman of [---]us; his slave Amoena, a freedwoman.
EDCS-08900809.summary: This inscription records the names of three individuals: Athenio, a freedman; another freedman; and Amoena, a freedwoman, possibly indicating a family or household connection.

EDCS-08900818.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sariolenae Luci libertae Atalante Daphnus conlibertus et Auricula filia fecerunt\n
EDCS-08900818.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Daphnus, freedman of Sariolena, and her daughter Auricula made this for Atalante, freedwoman of Lucius.
EDCS-08900818.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Atalante by her freedman Daphnus and her daughter Auricula.

EDCS-08900824.translation: For Ulpius Bassus, from Ulpius Marcellus.
EDCS-08900824.summary: A dedication from Ulpius Marcellus to his freedman Ulpius Bassus, likely a funerary monument.

EDCS-08900827.text_edition: \n Lucio Faenio Polybio thurario Lucio Faenio Celado thurario Lucio Faenio Iucundo Faeniae Moschidi Faenia Luci filia Procula fecit et Marco Livio Maximo parentibus et fratri et filio et sibi et libertis libertabusque suis posterisque eorum omnium\n
EDCS-08900827.translation: Procula, daughter of Lucius and Moschis, made this for Lucius Faenius Polybius, incense merchant; Lucius Faenius Celadus, incense merchant; Lucius Faenius Iucundus; Faenia Moschis; her daughter Faenia Procula; and for Marcus Livius Maximus, her parents, brother, son, and for herself, and for their freedmen and freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-08900827.summary: A complex funerary epitaph set up by Procula for her family, including relatives and freedpersons, with multiple incense merchants mentioned.

EDCS-08900829.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavia Cnaei filia vixit annis diebus V\n
EDCS-08900829.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Flavia, daughter of Cnaeus, lived for V days.
EDCS-08900829.summary: A funerary epitaph for Flavia, daughter of Cnaeus, recording her short lifespan.

EDCS-08900832.text_edition: \n Publius Maneilius |(mulieris) libertus Pamphilus Publius Maneilius Publi libertus Memno Publius Maneilius Publi libertus Sabbio Publius Maneilius Publi libertus Salvius structores paveimentari\n
EDCS-08900832.translation: Publius Maneilius Pamphilus, freedman of a woman; Publius Maneilius Memno, freedman of Publius; Publius Maneilius Sabbio, freedman of Publius; Publius Maneilius Salvius, freedmen of Publius; pavement makers.
EDCS-08900832.summary: A list of three freedmen named Publius Maneilius and one slave named Salvius, all pavement makers, likely related to a burial or a collegium.

EDCS-08900836.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Csidi coniugi rarissimae Fidelis et Primitiva mater bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-08900836.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Fidelis and Primitiva made this for Csida, a most rare wife, a well-deserving woman.
EDCS-08900836.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by Fidelis and Primitiva to their wife and mother, Csida.

EDCS-08900837.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Quirina Fado Iulia Procula marito optumo\n
EDCS-08900837.translation: For Tiberius Claudius Fado, of the Quirina tribe, from Iulia Procula, his most excellent husband.
EDCS-08900837.summary: A dedication from Iulia Procula to her husband, Tiberius Claudius Fado, honoring him as an excellent husband.

EDCS-08900840.text_edition: \n Lucio Alfio Luci filio Rufo praefecto municipii Caius Alfius Cnaei filius Surus Lucius Vibullius Cai filius amici de suo\n
EDCS-08900840.translation: For Lucius Alfius Rufus, son of Lucius, prefect of the town. Caius Alfius Surus, son of Cnaeus, and Lucius Vibullius, son of Caius, friends, at their own expense.
EDCS-08900840.summary: A monument erected at the expense of friends Caius Alfius Surus and Lucius Vibullius for Lucius Alfius Rufus, prefect of the town.

EDCS-08900841.text_edition: \n T(ito) Flavio Titi filio Tromentina IIviro municipi Fabrateriae veteris omnibus honoribus perfuncto sodales iuvenumvenumsium patrono dignissimo ob merita eius aere collato statuam posuerunt ei ob cuius dedicationem singulis discunbentibus! dedit et donavit eis HS VI milia nummum die natalis sic eius IIII Nonas Ianuarias ut omnibus annis sodalibus supra scriptis sportulae dividantur quod si diae sic suo divisae non fuerint tunc testamento meo dividentur sub ea condicione cura agente Caio Trebio Proculo IIviro Aemiliano et Basso consulibus\n
EDCS-08900841.translation: For Titus Flavius, son of Titus, of the Tromentina tribe, twice a magistrate of the ancient municipality of Fabrateria, who has held all offices, the associates of the young men of Venusium, for their most worthy patron, because of his merits, erected this statue with a contribution of money; and for its dedication, he gave to each reclining guest 6,000 sesterces, and by his will it is to be divided every year to the aforementioned associates on his birthday, the 4th day before the Nones of January, with the condition that if it is not divided by his own hand, it will be divided according to my will, under the supervision of Caius Trebius Proculus, twice a magistrate, when Aemilianus and Bassus were consuls.
EDCS-08900841.summary: A statue erected by a youth association for their patron Titus Flavius, a former magistrate, with funds for annual distributions on his birthday, to be supervised by officials including the donor.

EDCS-08900842.text_edition: \n Caio Mollio Cai filio Tromentina Secundino IIviro municipi Fabrateriae veteris curatori pecuniae legendae et nnonae sodales Herculei patrono dignissimo ob merita aere collato merenti posuerunt ob cuius dedicationem singillatim nummos divisit et donavit his HS XX milia nummum ut ex usuris eorum diae sic VI Nonas Maias collegio supra scripto sportulae dividantur\n
EDCS-08900842.translation: For Caius Mollins Secundinus, son of Caius, of the Tromentina tribe, twice a magistrate of the ancient municipality of Fabrateria, curator of the money for leasing and of the annona, the associates of Hercules, for their most worthy patron, because of his merits, erected this with a contribution of money; and for its dedication, he distributed and gave to each person 20,000 sesterces, so that from the interest, on the 6th day before the Nones of May, distributions are to be made to the aforementioned college.
EDCS-08900842.summary: A monument erected by the associates of Hercules for their patron Caius Mollins Secundinus, a magistrate and curator, with funds for annual distributions to the college on a specific date.

EDCS-08900845.translation: The love of the old Roman women.
EDCS-08900845.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription by the old Roman women, likely to a deity or a local patron, with an approximate date in antiquity.

EDCS-08900846.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Variae Proculae Proculus filiae dulcissimae quae vixit nnos VIII merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08900846.translation: To the Divine Manes. Proculus made this for his most beloved daughter, Varia Procula, who lived 8 years, deserving.
EDCS-08900846.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Proculus for his daughter Varia Procula, who died at the age of 8, dating to Roman Imperial times.

EDCS-08900848.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Caesare Augusto Germanico III Lucio Vitellio iterum consulibus Publius Crusius Germanus donavit decurionibus pagi Sarclani HS MMduo milia uti ex usuris eius VI Kalendas Apriles natali suo quodannis sic in pago vescantur quae summa publica est Caesare et Sentio consulibus Idibus Ianuaris decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-08900848.translation: While Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus was consul for the third time and Lucius Vitellius for the second time, Publius Crusius Germanus donated 2,000 sesterces to the decurions of the Sarclani pagus. He stipulated that from the interest on this sum, on his birthday, the 27th of March, they should feast in the pagus every year. This public sum was established with the decree of the decurions, when Caesar and Sentius were consuls, on the Ides of January.
EDCS-08900848.summary: This inscription records a generous donation made by Publius Crusius Germanus to the local council (decurions) of the Sarclani pagus for an annual feast on his birthday, dated to 40 CE.

EDCS-08900849.text_edition: \n CXII domino nostro Lucio Valerio Constantino Pio Felici Invicto Augusto divi Constanti Pii filio bono rei publicae nato\n
EDCS-08900849.translation: To our lord, Lucius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, son of the divine Constantius the Pious, born for the good of the state.
EDCS-08900849.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Constantine I, emphasizing his titles and lineage, dating to his reign in the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-08900850.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Tito Aemilio Finito veterano Augusti nostri probato nnis XX militavit nnis XXVII vixit nnis IL mensibus X diebus XXIIII Aemilius Potentinus et Aelius Laetinus veterani Augusti nostri bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-08900850.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Tito Aemilius Finitus, a veteran of our Augustus, who served for 20 years, lived 27 years, 49 years, 10 months, and 24 days. Aemilius Potentinus and Aelius Laetinus, veterans of our Augustus, made this for the deserving one.
EDCS-08900850.summary: A funerary monument for Tito Aemilius Finitus, a veteran who served 20 years and lived 49 years, erected by fellow veterans, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900851.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Sexto Iulio Eutycho veterano Auggustorum nnostrorum qui vixit annis LXXVIII mensibus VI diebus X Sextus Iulius Felicianus alumnus patrono bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08900851.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. For Sextus Julius Eutyches, veteran of our Augusti, who lived 78 years, 6 months, and 10 days. Sextus Julius Felicianus, his ward, made this for his deserving patron.
EDCS-08900851.summary: A funerary inscription for Sextus Julius Eutyches, a veteran who lived 78 years, erected by his ward, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900852.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Gnaeus Curvius Lithus and possibly a place name, likely a funerary or dedicatory monument from antiquity.

EDCS-08900853.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a name on a monument, dating to Roman times.

EDCS-08900855.text_edition: \n Marco Aurelio Libero domino et agitatori factionis prasinae Kapitolionicae natione Afer sic Teanenses ob merita eius decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-08900855.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Liber, master and charioteer of the Green faction of the Capitol, of African nationality, the people of Teanum, on account of his merits, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-08900855.summary: This inscription honors Marcus Aurelius Liber, a charioteer of the Green faction, with a dedication by the town of Teanum, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900857.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cornelio ni qui vixit annis LV bene merenti fecit Oc Severina coniugi suo\n
EDCS-08900857.translation: To the Divine Manes. Oc Severina made this for her husband Cornelius, who lived 55 years, deserving.
EDCS-08900857.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by Oc Severina for her husband Cornelius, who died at the age of 55, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900858.summary: This inscription records a freedman of the Imperial cult, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900860.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Nonia Antistia, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900861.text_edition: \n Lucius Oculatius Celer |(centurio) ex classe praetoria Misenensi de liburna Salute natione Dalmata militavit annis XXXV vixit nnis LV sibi et Barbiae Helpidi coniugi libertis suis et libertabus posterisque eorum testamento fieri iussit\n
EDCS-08900861.translation: Lucius Oculatius Celer, a centurion from the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, served for 35 years and lived for 55 years. He ordered this to be made in his will for himself and his wife, Baria Helpidi, and for his freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-08900861.summary: This is a funerary monument for Lucius Oculatius Celer, a centurion in the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, who served for 35 years and lived for 55 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900862.text_edition: \n Flavio Valerio Constantino Pio Felici Invicto Augusto divi Constanti Pii filio\n
EDCS-08900862.translation: To Flavius Valerius Constantinus, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Augustus, son of the deified Constantius.
EDCS-08900862.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantine I, son of Constantius, dating to the Tetrarchy or later in the 4th century CE.

EDCS-08900863.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luci Aponi Proculi vixit annis XVIII mensibus IIII militavit nnis II Aponius et Mettia parentes filio incomparabili\n
EDCS-08900863.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aponius and Mettia made this for their incomparable son, Lucius Aponius Proculus, who lived 18 years and 4 months and served for 2 years.
EDCS-08900863.summary: This is a funerary monument for Lucius Aponius Proculus, who died at the age of 18 years and 4 months after serving for 2 years, set up by his parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900865.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Olympias filio qui vixit nnos XVIII bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08900865.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. [She] made this for her son, Olympias, who lived 18 years, a well-deserving [son].
EDCS-08900865.summary: This is a funerary monument for Olympias, who died at the age of 18, set up by his mother, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900866.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Sabidius Marcellus miles ex classe praetoria Misenensi Marcello filio qui vixit nnos VIII\n
EDCS-08900866.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Gaius Sabidius Marcellus, soldier from the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, [made this] for his son, Marcello, who lived 8 years.
EDCS-08900866.summary: This is a funerary monument for Marcello, son of Gaius Sabidius Marcellus, a soldier in the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, who died at the age of 8, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900867.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cassius Albanus III Mercurio natione Corsus vixit nnos XXX menses II nnos sic II Marcus Urbinius Rufus IIII Dacico bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08900867.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Marcus Urbinius Rufus made this for Cassius Albanus, III, a Corsican, who lived 30 years and 2 months and served for 2 years, a well-deserving [soldier].
EDCS-08900867.summary: This is a funerary monument for Cassius Albanus, a soldier who died at the age of 30 years and 2 months after serving for 2 years, set up by Marcus Urbinius Rufus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900868.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Aurelio Secundo manipulari liburnae Iunonis natione Aegypto militavit nnos XVIII vixit nnos L Flavia Fundana uxor bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08900868.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Flavia Fundana, his wife, made this for Titus Aurelius Secundus, a maniple-member of the liburna of Juno, from Egypt, who served for 18 years and lived for 50 years, a well-deserving [soldier].
EDCS-08900868.summary: This is a funerary monument for Titus Aurelius Secundus, a soldier from the liburna of Juno, who served for 18 years and lived for 50 years, set up by his wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900871.text_edition: \n Pro splendore et dignitate civitatis Flavius Lucretius Publianus curator rei publicae Nolanorum stateriam cum ponderibus iustis deferendam curavit\n
EDCS-08900871.translation: For the splendor and dignity of the city, Flavius Lucretius Publianus, curator of the public affairs of the Nolans, arranged for a steelyard with just weights to be provided.
EDCS-08900871.summary: This inscription records the provision of a steelyard with accurate weights for the city of Nola by Flavius Lucretius Publianus, curator of public affairs, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900872.text_edition: \n Augusto sacrum AAuli Lucii Auli filii Menenia Proculus et Iulianus pecunia sua dedicatione decurionibus et Augustalibus cenam dederunt\n
EDCS-08900872.translation: Sacred to Augustus. Aulus Lucius Proculus and his son Iulianus gave a dinner, at their own expense, to the decurions and Augustales on the occasion of the dedication.
EDCS-08900872.summary: An inscription recording a dinner given by Aulus Lucius Proculus and his son Iulianus to local officials, likely in the late 1st century BCE or early 1st century CE.

EDCS-08900880.text_edition: \n Iuliae Augustae Titi Caesaris filiae decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-08900880.translation: To Iulia Augusta, daughter of Caesar, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-08900880.summary: An inscription honoring Iulia Augusta, daughter of Emperor Titus, likely erected by public decree on a monument or statue base, dating to the late 1st century CE.

EDCS-08900882.summary: A funerary inscription or dedicatory base for a son named Nonius Balbus, likely from the 1st century BCE or early 1st century CE.

EDCS-08900884.text_edition: \n Marco Nonio Balbi liberto Eutycho Marciano decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-08900884.translation: To Marcus Nonius Balbus, by decree of the decurions, for his freedman Eutychus Marcianus.
EDCS-08900884.summary: A dedication by decree of the decurions, honoring Marcus Nonius Balbus by commemorating his freedman Eutychus Marcianus, likely from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-08900886.text_edition: \n Hirpini Augusti liberti a rationibus de statione Augusta Mediolanensi portorii inaria\n
EDCS-08900886.translation: Hirpinus, freedman of Augustus, from the accounts department, from the Augusta Mediolanensis customs station for the harbour tax.
EDCS-08900886.summary: A record of service or duty for Hirpinus, a freedman of Augustus employed in fiscal administration at the Augusta Mediolanensis customs post, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09001169.text_edition: \n annos XIII menses V permissu Semproniae Dignitatis optimae feminae huic tumulo dolus malus abesto\n
EDCS-09001169.translation: For 13 years and 5 months, with the permission of Sempronia Dignitas, a most excellent woman, let malice be absent from this tomb.
EDCS-09001169.summary: A funerary inscription specifying the age of the deceased and invoking protection against malicious interference with the tomb, possibly from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09001195.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ponpeiae sic Caralitanae parentes fecerunt vixit annis XI filiae bene merenti\n
EDCS-09001195.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Pompeia Caralitana, who lived 11 years. Her parents made this for their well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-09001195.summary: A funerary monument for Pompeia Caralitana, erected by her parents, dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09001196.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valentino qui vixit annos II menses VII dies XIV parentes filio dulcissimo loco dato faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-09001196.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Valerius, who lived 2 years, 7 months, and 14 days. His parents arranged for this to be made for their dearest son, dedicating a place.
EDCS-09001196.summary: A funerary monument for Valerius, erected by his parents, specifying his age at death and the dedication of a burial plot, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09001197.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Flavius Augusti libertus Nicostratus Titianus fecit sibi et libertis libertabusque suis posterisque eorum vixit annis LXXX hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-09001197.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Titus Flavius Nicostratus, freedman of Augustus, [dedicated this] for himself and for his freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. He lived 80 years. This monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-09001197.summary: A funerary monument self-dedicated by Titus Flavius Nicostratus, a freedman of Augustus, for himself and his descendants, dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09001198.text_edition: \n Publi progenies Appi cognomine Pulchri occubuit letum\n
EDCS-09001198.translation: Offspring of Publius, surnamed Pulcher, son of Appius, succumbed to death.
EDCS-09001198.summary: An epitaph recording the death of a man whose father was Appius and whose grandfather or ancestor was named Publius, likely from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-09001201.text_edition: \n Luci nni Luci liberti Chresimi VIviri Umbeiae |(mulieris) libertae Primigeniae\n
EDCS-09001201.translation: To Chresimus, freedman of Lucius, freedman of Umbeia, freedwoman, and Primigenia, freedwoman of Umbeia, six men.
EDCS-09001201.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Chresimus and Primigenia, freedpersons associated with Umbeia, to six men.

EDCS-09001202.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Pacciae Mu Donatae Marcus Tossius Gallus heres\n
EDCS-09001202.translation: To the Divine Manes. Marcus Tossius Gallus, his heir, [erected this] for Paccia Mu Donata.
EDCS-09001202.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Marcus Tossius Gallus for Paccia Mu Donata.

EDCS-09001203.text_edition: \n Vivit Marcus Septumius Marci libertus Felix patrono sibi et suis\n
EDCS-09001203.translation: Marcus Septumius Felix, freedman of Marcus, lives for his patron, himself, and his own.
EDCS-09001203.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Septumius Felix, a freedman, who established it for his patron and himself.

EDCS-09001204.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Virio Fortunato Viria Fortunata et Natalis parentes filio pientissimo\n
EDCS-09001204.translation: To the Divine Manes. Viria Fortunata and Natalis, parents, [erected this] for their most dutiful son, Lucius Virius Fortunatus.
EDCS-09001204.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Viria Fortunata and Natalis for their son, Lucius Virius Fortunatus.

EDCS-09001205.text_edition: \n ai Cai liberti roni in fronte pedes XII in agro pedes \n
EDCS-09001205.translation: [---] Cai, freedman of [---]roni, 12 feet in front, [---] feet in the field.
EDCS-09001205.summary: This inscription seems to record land measurements related to a freedman named Cai.

EDCS-09001206.text_edition: \n A s Cai Luci libertus Hilarus \n
EDCS-09001206.translation: Hilarus, freedman of Lucius, son of Cai.
EDCS-09001206.summary: This is a short epitaph identifying Hilarus, son of Cai and freedman of Lucius.

EDCS-09001207.text_edition: \n Publius Vertuleius Filodamus sic Quintus Proculeius Quinti libertus Hilarus sibi et suis\n
EDCS-09001207.translation: Quintus Proculeius Hilarus, freedman of Quintus, [erected this] for himself and his own.
EDCS-09001207.summary: This is an epitaph by Quintus Proculeius Hilarus, a freedman, for himself and his family.

EDCS-09001210.text_edition: \n Caio Furio Cai liberto Chresimo VIviro Avia Luci liberta Iusta et Lucius Aviu[s ------\n
EDCS-09001210.translation: To Gaius Furius Chresimus, freedman of Gaius, a six-man; Avia Iusta, freedwoman of Lucius, and Lucius Avius [---].
EDCS-09001210.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Avia Iusta and Lucius Avius for Gaius Furius Chresimus, a freedman and six-man.

EDCS-09001211.text_edition: \n Cnaei Nasenni Cnaei filii Teretina vivi Marcia Cai filia uxor\n
EDCS-09001211.translation: Marcia, daughter of Cai and wife [of], lives; Gnaeus Nasennius, son of Gnaeus, [---].
EDCS-09001211.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or epitaph for Marcia and her husband Gnaeus Nasennius.

EDCS-09001213.summary: This is a dedication to Germanicus Caesar.

EDCS-09001214.text_edition: \n Caio Timinio Cai filio Teretina Gallo quaestori Timiniae Cai O\n
EDCS-09001214.translation: To Gaius Timinius Gallus, son of Gaius, of the Teretina tribe, quaestor; [dedicated by] Timinia.
EDCS-09001214.summary: This is a dedication to Gaius Timinius Gallus, a quaestor, likely set up by a family member or associate named Timinia.

EDCS-09001215.text_edition: \n ius Marci libertus Ero curator sevirum SCR CRE\n
EDCS-09001215.translation: Eros, freedman of Marcius, curator, [member of the body of] seviri.
EDCS-09001215.summary: This inscription records Eros, a freedman and curator, who was a member of the seviri, a group of prominent local citizens.

EDCS-09001216.text_edition: \n Marci Hei Marci liberti Stabilionis Marci Hei Marci liberti Philarguri Laniae Cai libertae \n
EDCS-09001216.translation: Of Marcius Helius, freedman of Marcius; Of Marcius Helius, freedman of Marcius; Of Lania, freedwoman of Philargyrus, freedman of Marcius.
EDCS-09001216.summary: This text lists the names of several individuals, all connected through manumission, likely for funerary or property records.

EDCS-09001217.text_edition: \n Luci Pomponi Luci liberti Pilarcuri Tiliae Cai libertae Florae Pomponia Luci liberta Prima Pomponia Luci liberta Hilara\n
EDCS-09001217.translation: Of Lucius Pomponius Pilarcurus, freedman of Lucius; Of Flora, freedwoman of Gaius; Pomponia Prima, freedwoman of Lucius; Pomponia Hilara, freedwoman of Lucius.
EDCS-09001217.summary: This inscription lists several individuals, including freedmen and freedwomen, likely for funerary purposes, with connections to Lucius and Gaius.

EDCS-09001218.text_edition: \n Caius Rullius Spuri filius Rufus Lucius Bietius Luci filius Labeo\n
EDCS-09001218.translation: Gaius Rullius Rufus, son of Spurius; Lucius Bietius Labeo, son of Lucius.
EDCS-09001218.summary: This inscription simply records the names and patronymics of two individuals, Gaius Rullius Rufus and Lucius Bietius Labeo.

EDCS-09001219.text_edition: \n Capria |(mulieris) liberta Amphelis Caio Danio |(mulieris) liberto Eroti Quinto Caprio |(mulieris) liberto Festo \n
EDCS-09001219.translation: From Amphelis, freedwoman of Capria; To Eros, freedman of Gaius Danus; To Festus, freedman of Quintus Caprio.
EDCS-09001219.summary: This inscription records a dedication or bequest from Amphelis to Eros and Festus, who are also freedmen, indicating familial or social connections.

EDCS-09001220.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Plotillae Titus Ennius Callistus pater sibi fecit\n
EDCS-09001220.translation: To the Divine Shades. Titus Ennius Callistus made this for himself for Plotilla.
EDCS-09001220.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Plotilla, made by Titus Ennius Callistus for himself, possibly indicating a shared tomb or a later addition.

EDCS-09001222.text_edition: \n Plancia Titi liberta? Titus Plancius Titi libertus? Plancia Titi liberta? Titus Plancius Titi libertus? vivit Plancia Titi liberta? vivit Plancia Titi liberta? vivit Marcus Marriusieis et posteris\n
EDCS-09001222.translation: To the Divine Shades. Plancia, freedwoman of Titus? Titus Plancius, freedman of Titus? Plancia, freedwoman of Titus? Titus Plancius, freedman of Titus? lives. Plancia, freedwoman of Titus? lives. Plancia, freedwoman of Titus? lives. Marcus Marrius [et al.?] for them and their descendants.
EDCS-09001222.summary: This inscription appears to be a funerary text for members of the Plancius family, mentioning freedmen and freedwomen, and possibly intended for future generations.

EDCS-09001223.text_edition: \n Cnaei Pomponi Cnaei liberti Metropanis Pomponiae Cnaei libertae Nicenis\n
EDCS-09001223.translation: Of Cnaeus Pomponius Metropanis, freedman of Cnaeus; Of Pomponia Nice, freedwoman of Cnaeus.
EDCS-09001223.summary: This inscription lists two individuals, both freedmen connected to Cnaeus, likely for funerary or ownership records.

EDCS-09001225.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Mariae Dignillae Marius Callistus coniugi dulcissimae castae univiriae incomparabili cum qua vixit nnos XXV pius\n
EDCS-09001225.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Marius Callistus [made this] for his wife Dignilla, most sweet, chaste, married only once, incomparable, with whom he lived 25 years; pious.
EDCS-09001225.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Marius Callistus to his wife Maria Dignilla, praising her virtues and stating the duration of their marriage.

EDCS-09001226.text_edition: \n Marcus Valerius Falernus Fanniae mulieris lib(ertae) Crinusae\n
EDCS-09001226.translation: Marcus Valerius Falernus, freedman of the woman Fannis, Crinus, [erected this].
EDCS-09001226.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Valerius Falernus, identified as a freedman.

EDCS-09001227.text_edition: \n Diis Manibus Aemiliae Episteme Lucius Calpurnius Soter coniugi carissimae et sibi\n
EDCS-09001227.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lucius Calpurnius Soter [erected this] for Aemilia Episteme, his dearest wife, and for himself.
EDCS-09001227.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Shades by Lucius Calpurnius Soter for his wife Aemilia Episteme and himself.

EDCS-09001228.translation: [Dedicated] by Aelius Mamertinus in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-09001228.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated by Aelius Mamertinus.

EDCS-09001229.text_edition: \n Baebiae Sexti filiae Numeri Avilli uxori ex decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-09001229.translation: For Baebia, daughter of Sextus, wife of Numerius Avillus, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-09001229.summary: This inscription records an honorific dedication for Baebia, wife of Numerius Avillus, by local councillors.

EDCS-09001230.text_edition: \n s Marci libertus Eros ae libertae concubinae suae\n
EDCS-09001230.translation: To Eros, freedman of Marcus, and his concubine, freedwoman of [---].
EDCS-09001230.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Eros, a freedman, and his concubine.

EDCS-09001231.text_edition: \n Publio Catieno Publi filio Sergia Sabino quaestori tribuno plebis praetori proconsuli praefecto aerarii militaris legato Caesaris Augusti decreto decurionum patrono\n
EDCS-09001231.translation: For Publius Catienus, son of Publius, of the Sergia tribe, Sabinus, quaestor, tribune of the plebs, praetor, proconsul, prefect of the military treasury, legate of Caesar Augustus, patron, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-09001231.summary: This is an honorific inscription detailing the distinguished career of Publius Catienus, likely dating to the early imperial period.

EDCS-09001232.text_edition: \n Staia Secundilla sibi et Anniae Tertullae filiae et suis et Avillio Antho humandi corporis sui (causa)\n
EDCS-09001232.translation: Staia Secundilla for herself and for her daughter Annia Tertulla, and for her own family, and for Avillius Anthus, for the burial of their bodies.
EDCS-09001232.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Staia Secundilla, her daughter Annia Tertulla, and Avillius Anthus, establishing burial arrangements.

EDCS-09200056.text_edition: \n Caius Valerius Cai filius Sabatina Vindemitori sic Decia Prisca fratri carissimo\n
EDCS-09200056.translation: To Caius Valerius Vindemitor, son of Caius, of the Sabatina tribe. Thus, Decia Prisca to her dearest brother.
EDCS-09200056.summary: This is a funerary inscription from Decia Prisca to her brother, Caius Valerius Vindemitor.

EDCS-09200059.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Decimo Valerio As Decimus Valerius Litus et Sextia Ide fratri carissimo\n
EDCS-09200059.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Decimus Valerius Litus and Sextia Ide, for their dearest brother.
EDCS-09200059.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to the Divine Shades by Decimus Valerius Litus and Sextia Ide for their brother.

EDCS-09200060.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aeliae Marci filiae Thetidi vixit annis XI mensibus IIII Marcus Aelius Eutychus et Iulia Tertulla parentes\n
EDCS-09200060.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. To Aelia Thetis, daughter of Marcus, who lived 11 years and 4 months. From Marcus Aelius Eutychus and Iulia Tertulla, her parents.
EDCS-09200060.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aelia Thetis, who died at the age of 11 years and 4 months, dedicated by her parents.

EDCS-09200062.text_edition: \n Caius Maecilius Dionysius Attia Cai liberta Musa Caius Maecilius Celer\n
EDCS-09200062.translation: Musa, freedwoman of Gaius Maecilius Dionysius, [and] Gaius Maecilius Celer.
EDCS-09200062.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Musa, freedwoman of Gaius Maecilius Dionysius, and Gaius Maecilius Celer.

EDCS-09200063.text_edition: \n Caiustinius |(mulieris) libertus Priamus tinius Cai filius Rufus filius tinius Cai filius Proculus filius sibi et liberis libertisque eorum\n
EDCS-09200063.translation: Priamus, freedman of Caiustinius, son of Caius, Rufus, son of Caius, Proculus, son of Caius, for himself and his children and their freedmen.
EDCS-09200063.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Priamus, his children, and their freedmen, set up by Priamus, a freedman of Caiustinius.

EDCS-09200064.summary: This is a partial inscription recording a dedication by Gaius Va.

EDCS-09200065.text_edition: \n Caius Trebellius fecit Caio Trebellio T vixit [annos ------\n
EDCS-09200065.translation: Gaius Trebellius made [this] for Gaius Trebellius, [who] lived [---] years.
EDCS-09200065.summary: This inscription records the maker, Gaius Trebellius, and the deceased, Gaius Trebellius, likely a funerary monument.

EDCS-09200068.text_edition: \n Gegania Luci liberta Sopatra fecit sibi et Diodoro vilico Vipsani\n
EDCS-09200068.translation: Sopatra, freedwoman of Lucius Geganius, made [this] for herself and for Diodorus, the farm manager of Vipsanius.
EDCS-09200068.summary: This funerary dedication was made by Sopatra, freedwoman of Lucius Geganius, for herself and Diodorus, a farm manager.

EDCS-09200069.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription mentioning Alfidia.

EDCS-09200071.text_edition: \n Aufidiae Luci liberta Salu Nicef fecit in fronte pedes XII in agro IX\n
EDCS-09200071.translation: Salvia, freedwoman of Lucius Aufidius, [set this up] in front 12 feet, in the field 9 [feet].
EDCS-09200071.summary: This inscription records land dimensions (12 feet front, 9 feet field) associated with Salvia, freedwoman of Lucius Aufidius.

EDCS-09200072.text_edition: \n pantomimo hieronico clarissimae memoriae viri A populi pecunia s \n
EDCS-09200072.translation: To Hieronicus, the pantomime, man of most distinguished memory, from the people, with funds...
EDCS-09200072.summary: This inscription honors Hieronicus, a pantomime, funded by the public.

EDCS-09200074.translation: Aufidius M. [set this up] for Aufidia, freedwoman of a woman.
EDCS-09200074.summary: This inscription records Aufidius M. dedicating something to Aufidia, a freedwoman.

EDCS-09200075.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Thaelypis Graecus annorum XX\n
EDCS-09200075.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Thaelypis the Greek, aged 20.
EDCS-09200075.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Thaelypis the Greek, aged 20.

EDCS-09200081.text_edition: \n Luci Brutti Heracleonis maceria privata ex agro suo ab fundamentis alta pedes VIII longa usque ad angulum superiorem pedes CCCLXXV\n
EDCS-09200081.translation: Lucius Bruttius Heracleo: a private wall, from his own land, built from the foundations, 8 feet high, 375 feet long up to the upper corner.
EDCS-09200081.summary: This inscription records the dimensions and materials of a private wall built by Lucius Bruttius Heracleo.

EDCS-09200083.text_edition: \n Arriae Cai libertae Primae Arriae Tigridi et Caio Arrio Cerintho libertis libertabus\n
EDCS-09200083.translation: To Prima, freedwoman of Arria, for Arria Tigridis and her freedmen and freedwomen Gaius Arrius Cerinthus.
EDCS-09200083.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Prima, freedwoman of Arria, for her patrons and their freedmen and freedwomen.

EDCS-09200084.text_edition: \n Obscaecata tempora luctuosaque grande relictum mihi Maximo a Gaudentia coniuge cara cum quem sine vile sic vixi annis VIIII menses sex dies XXIII larga obsequia erga conparem faciens MERETE iam fines aeternae domus tumuloque requiescit sic\n
EDCS-09200084.translation: I, Gaudentia, your loving wife, lived with you, Maximu, for 9 years, six months, and twenty-three days, making generous provisions for my companion, now you rest in the eternal home and tomb.
EDCS-09200084.summary: This is a deeply personal epitaph from a wife, Gaudentia, to her deceased husband, Maximu, detailing their years together.

EDCS-09200085.summary: This very brief inscription simply marks a boundary or limit, identifying Rufinus.

EDCS-09200087.text_edition: \n Publius Nunnius Publi filius Cornelia Nunnia Publi liberta Salvia Publius Nunnius Publi libertus rtus\n
EDCS-09200087.translation: Publius Nunnius, son of Publius; Cornelia, freedwoman of Publius; Salvia; Publius Nunnius, freedman of Publius.
EDCS-09200087.summary: This inscription appears to be a list of names, possibly a family or group, with their parentage or status indicated.

EDCS-09200088.text_edition: \n Firidia Veneria Calvisi Secundi magistra Dianae Karenae dono dedit loco dato decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-09200088.translation: Firidia Veneria, as magistra, gave this as a gift to Diana Karena, on land granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-09200088.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Diana Karena by Firidia Veneria, acting as magistra, with land provided by local officials.

EDCS-09200089.translation: Of Retius Voltius, freedman of Stabilio.
EDCS-09200089.summary: This is a simple ownership or dedication marker, identifying Retius Voltius as the freedman of Stabilio.

EDCS-09200090.text_edition: \n Tettia Marci liberta Myrtale Cu<p=D>ania Numeri liberta magistrae Dianae basim dono dederunt \n
EDCS-09200090.translation: Tettia, freedwoman of Marcus, and Myrtale, freedwoman of Cu(p)anius Numerius, gave this base as a gift to their magistra of Diana.
EDCS-09200090.summary: This inscription records a dedication of a base to Diana by two freedwomen, Tettia and Myrtale, who served as magistrae.

EDCS-09200091.text_edition: \n Fadiae Marci filiae publice decreto decurionum quod Sextus Titinius IIIIvir iure dicundo bis tertio quinquennalis Palpo IIIIvir iure dicundo praefectus quinquennalis lege Petronia verba fecerunt Fadiam Marci filiam diem suum obisse itaque in honorem domus eius oportere quos videretur honores ei decerni quid de ea re fieri placeret de ea re ita censuerunt cum Marcus Fadius Crispus pater qua advixit eum animum in re publica nostra semper exhibuerit uti plenissime quicquid rei publicae opus fuisset ultro semper et pollicitus sit et praestiterit cupidissime neque cessaverit unquam cumque tales adulescentes Fadii Lucius et Gaius suboles patriae nostrae speive maxumae secundum vota nostra surgentes bonis auspiciis qui propter quos et singuli et universi ita et dicere et optare debemus itaque in honorem eorum placere nobis Fadiam Marci filiam ex loco publico efferri et hoc amplius statuam poni ex pecunia publica loco publico dum taxat HS MMMMMMMMocto milibus ut et meritis oneribus Fadi patris subscripsisse videamur et non defuisse officio supremo die funeris Fadiae Marci filiae omnes censuere\n
EDCS-09200091.translation: For Fadia, daughter of Marcus. By public decree of the decurions, because Sextus Titinius, the duovir for pronouncing judgment, twice and a third time quinquennial, Palpus, the duovir for pronouncing judgment, prefect, quinquennial, made the following pronouncements: That Fadia, daughter of Marcus, had died, and therefore, in honor of her house, whatever honors seemed appropriate should be decreed to her. What was pleasing to be done about this matter, they resolved as follows: Since Marcus Fadius Crispus, her father, during his lifetime, always showed such spirit in our state, that he most eagerly not only promised but also provided whatever the state needed, and never ceased; and since his sons, Lucius and Gaius Fadius, rising as the hope of our fatherland and of our greatest expectations, according to our wishes, and with good auspices, because of whom we both individually and collectively must speak and wish well. Therefore, for their honor, it is pleasing to us that Fadia, daughter of Marcus, be carried out from a public place, and furthermore, that a statue be erected at public expense in a public place, to the limit of 800,000 sesterces, so that we may seem to have subscribed to the merits and burdens of father Fadius and not have failed in our duty on the supreme day of the funeral of Fadia, daughter of Marcus. All agreed.
EDCS-09200091.summary: This extensive inscription details a decree by the decurions honoring Fadia, daughter of Marcus, and her father Marcus Fadius Crispus, for their public service, including the erection of a statue and a significant public expenditure.

EDCS-09200093.text_edition: \n Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo tribunicia potestate proconsuli Severi Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi filio \n
EDCS-09200093.translation: To the conqueror of Arabia, of Adiabene, of Parthia, the Greatest, holder of tribunician power, proconsul, son of Severus Augustus, conqueror of Arabia, of Adiabene, of Parthia, the Greatest.
EDCS-09200093.summary: This inscription is an honorific dedication to a high-ranking official, likely a son of Emperor Severus, listing his military victories and titles.

EDCS-09200096.translation: Gnaeus Decumius Coxsa, son of Caius, and Septumia Statia, daughter of Lucius, saw to the construction of this for her husband.
EDCS-09200096.summary: This epitaph records a funerary monument commissioned by a wife for her husband.
