EDCS-09200097.text_edition: \n Diis Manibus sacrum Lucius Vitorius Ianuarius sevir iterum Augustalis Rubriae uxsori et Ampliae quae vixit annis X et Vitoriae matri et nniae hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-09200097.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Lucius Vitorius Ianuarius, a second-time *sevir Augustalis*, erected this monument for his wife Rubria, for Ampia who lived ten years, for his mother Vitorius, and for Nnia; it will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-09200097.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Lucius Vitorius Ianuarius and his family members, specifying that the monument is not inheritable.

EDCS-09200100.text_edition: \n i et Aulo Dentrio Auli liberto Philarguro et Dentrio amico suo et Aulo Dentrio AAulorum liberto Proxsumo sic \n
EDCS-09200100.translation: and to Aulus Dentrius Philargyrus, freedman of Aulus, and to his friend Dentrius, and to Aulus Dentrius Proxsumus, freedman of the Auli...
EDCS-09200100.summary: This incomplete inscription seems to be a dedication or funerary text honoring several individuals, including freedmen.

EDCS-09200102.translation: To Publius Servilius Pancrates, freedman of Publius.
EDCS-09200102.summary: This is a dedication to Publius Servilius Pancrates, a freedman.

EDCS-09200106.text_edition: \n Virius Turbo vir clarissimus consularis Campaniae curavit\n
EDCS-09200106.translation: Virius Turbo, most distinguished man, consular governor of Campania, oversaw this.
EDCS-09200106.summary: This inscription records that Virius Turbo, a former governor of Campania, supervised a project or monument.

EDCS-09200107.summary: This fragment appears to be a dedication or ownership marker for Quintus Aufustius Pamphilus.

EDCS-09200114.text_edition: \n na Tiberius Claudius Gauphicus libertus bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09200114.translation: Tiberius Claudius Gauphicus, his freedman, made this for a deserving person.
EDCS-09200114.summary: This inscription indicates that a freedman named Tiberius Claudius Gauphicus commissioned a monument for someone deserving.

EDCS-09200115.text_edition: \n Cnaeo Pomponio Hylite Pomponia Cnaei liberta Vitalis sibi et o\n
EDCS-09200115.translation: Pomponia Vitalis, freedwoman of Gnaeus Pomponius Hylites, [erected this] for herself and...
EDCS-09200115.summary: This funerary epitaph was commissioned by Pomponia Vitalis for herself and possibly another person.

EDCS-09200116.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription, possibly naming a person named Nympha.

EDCS-09300131.text_edition: \n Cnaeus Vettius Globulus dissignator Caesarum et magister Herculis Victoris et sevir Augustalis \n
EDCS-09300131.translation: Gnaeus Vettius Globulus, designer for the Caesars, master of Hercules Victor, and *sevir Augustalis*...
EDCS-09300131.summary: This inscription lists the titles and offices held by Gnaeus Vettius Globulus, including his roles in imperial administration and religious collegia.

EDCS-09300132.text_edition: \n Titus Livius Cerialis Lucius Munatius Trophimus Caius Fundanius Saturninus Publius Lollius Bucolus Titus Flavius Demetrius Marcus Marcius Hermes Publius Plautius Laetus Tiberius Bussenus Eutychius Aemilius Crescens icanus Philetus Pompeius Tyllus Pompeius Ponticus dius Paris us Geminus Caius Acilius Hyacinthus Publius Argentarius Lucius Antonius Titus Albius P Caius Annius P Lucius Antistius Marcus Aurelius Marcus Antonius Marcus Aurelius Marcus Aufidius Marcus Aurelius Lucius Al Marcus A \n
EDCS-09300132.translation: Titus Livius Cerialis, Lucius Munatius Trophimus, Caius Fundanius Saturninus, Publius Lollius Bucolus, Titus Flavius Demetrius, Marcus Marcius Hermes, Publius Plautius Laetus, Tiberius Bussenus Eutychius, Aemilius Crescens Icanus, Philetus, Pompeius Tyllus, Pompeius Ponticus, Dius Paris, Geminus, Caius Acilius Hyacinthus, Publius Argentarius, Lucius Antonius, Titus Albius, Caius Annius, Lucius Antistius, Marcus Aurelius, Marcus Antonius, Marcus Aurelius, Marcus Aufidius, Marcus A[---]
EDCS-09300132.summary: This inscription lists numerous names, likely those of officials or members of a collegium, possibly related to a public building or endowment.

EDCS-09300134.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marci Aureli Secundi Sabina coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-09300134.translation: To the divine spirits of Marcus Aurelius Secundus. Sabina, to her deserving wife.
EDCS-09300134.summary: A funerary dedication by Sabina to her husband, Marcus Aurelius Secundus, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09300135.text_edition: \n Titus Precilius Titi libertus Papia Titus Precilius Titi libertus Suavis in fronte pedes XII in agro pedes XVI\n
EDCS-09300135.translation: Titus Precilius Papia, freedman of Titus Precilius, and Titus Precilius Suavis, freedman of Titus Precilius. In the front, 12 feet; in the field, 16 feet.
EDCS-09300135.summary: This inscription records the dimensions of a property, likely a tomb or plot of land, belonging to Titus Precilius and his freedmen Papia and Suavis.

EDCS-09300137.translation: Petilius Callistus arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-09300137.summary: A dedication indicating that Petilius Callistus commissioned the construction or erection of a monument or object.

EDCS-09300140.text_edition: \n Fulvio Cai filio Sergia S procons(uli) III praetori ex auctoritate Tiberi? Caesaris Augusti T\n
EDCS-09300140.translation: To Fulvius, son of Caius, of the Sergia tribe, thrice consul, praetor, by the authority of Tiberius Caesar Augustus [---]
EDCS-09300140.summary: An honorific inscription for Fulvius, son of Caius, detailing his high offices and mentioning the authority of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, likely dating to the early Imperial period.

EDCS-09300141.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Mummio Exspectato bene merenti fecit nus Crescens cum Crispina ta matre sua\n
EDCS-09300141.translation: To the divine spirits. Crescens and his mother Crispina made this for the deserving Mummio Exspectatus.
EDCS-09300141.summary: A funerary dedication by Crescens and his mother Crispina for Mummio Exspectatus, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09300142.translation: Sabinia made this for her deserving Valentis.
EDCS-09300142.summary: A funerary dedication by Sabinia for her deserving Valentis, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09300145.text_edition: \n bertus bene merenti et sibi Scantiae Athenaidi coniugi suae Publius Scantius Pri Heraclida vixit nnos menses \n
EDCS-09300145.translation: [---] freedman, for the deserving and for himself, and for his wife Scantiae Athenaidis. Publius Scantius Pri[---] Heraclida lived [---] years [---] months [---]
EDCS-09300145.summary: A funerary inscription for Publius Scantius Heraclida, mentioning his wife Scantiae Athenaidis and possibly another freedman, with incomplete details about his lifespan.

EDCS-09300146.text_edition: \n tia Marci a Scaevinia Herculi sacrum\n
EDCS-09300146.translation: [Sca]evinia, daughter of Marcus, sacred to Hercules.
EDCS-09300146.summary: A religious dedication of a sacred object or site to the god Hercules by Scaevinia, daughter of Marcus.

EDCS-09300147.summary: An incomplete inscription beginning with a man's name, Raecius, son of Aulus, possibly the start of a dedication or epitaph.

EDCS-09300151.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Cassius Saturnino qui vixit anno uno mensibus VI diebus VIIII fecit pater\n
EDCS-09300151.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Caius Cassius Saturninus, who lived for one year, six months, and nine days. His father made this.
EDCS-09300151.summary: A tombstone for a young child, Caius Cassius Saturninus, erected by his father.

EDCS-09300153.text_edition: \n Cai Volumni Cai liberti Salvl sic Poenica liberta de suo fecit\n
EDCS-09300153.translation: Cai Volumnius Salvius, freedman of Caius, and Poenica, freedwoman, made this from her own funds.
EDCS-09300153.summary: A dedication made by two freedpersons, Cai Volumnius Salvius and Poenica.

EDCS-09300820.text_edition: \n Sicut coram praecepit vir perfectissimus Messius Extricatus praefectus nnonae titulus ponetur qui demonstret ex quo loci in quem locum saborrariis saborram tollere liceat factum autem opus est ut idem titulo retro omnium praefectorum litterae instruantur quibus de podismo est statutum quibusque suam auctoritatem idem vir perfectissimus manere praecipit titulus scriptus per Iulium Maternum |(centurionem) frumentarium XV Kalendas Octobres Faustino et Rufino consulibus curam agente Marco Vargunteio Victore\n
EDCS-09300820.translation: As the most distinguished man Messius Extricatus, prefect of the Annona, previously ordered, this notice shall be placed to show from which place to which place it is permitted to remove grain from the granary. The work was done so that the letters of all former prefects, concerning the granary floor, which this same distinguished man ordered to remain in force, are also instructed on this notice. The notice was written by Julius Maternus, a centurion of the frumentarii, on the 15th day before the Kalends of October, during the consulship of Faustinus and Rufinus, with Marcus Vargunteius Victor overseeing the work.
EDCS-09300820.summary: An inscription detailing the regulation of grain storage and removal within the Annona, commissioned by Prefect Messius Extricatus and inscribed in 214 AD.

EDCS-09300823.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Tiberi filio Pluto Tiberius Claudius Tiberi filius Apollinaris una cum Claudia Philete matre fratri Tiberio Claudio Pluto Claudia Tiberi filia Taposiris patri fecerunt et sibi liberis libertis posterisque eorum hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur in fronte pedes XX in agro pedes XXX et Petelliae Priscae nepoti\n
EDCS-09300823.translation: Tiberius Claudius Apollinaris, together with his mother Claudia Philete, made this for his brother Tiberius Claudius Pluto and his sister Claudia Taposiris, and for their father, and for themselves, their children, freedmen, and their descendants. This monument will not pass to an heir. It measures 20 feet in front and 30 feet in the field, and also for Petellia Prisca, his granddaughter.
EDCS-09300823.summary: A tomb inscription for Tiberius Claudius Pluto and Claudia Taposiris, made by their brother and mother, which also provides for future descendants and a granddaughter.

EDCS-09300827.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gnaei Fresidi Marsi veterano Augusti quaestori alumentorum sic aedili Ficolensium foederatorum magistratu sic designato Ostorius Aprilis pater infelicissimus\n
EDCS-09300827.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Gnaeus Fresidius Marsus, a veteran of the Augustan cohort, formerly quaestor of the alumni, and subsequently aedile of the Ficolenses Foederati, and a magistrate elect. His most unhappy father Ostorius Aprilis.
EDCS-09300827.summary: A tombstone for Gnaeus Fresidius Marsus, a veteran and former official, erected by his father.

EDCS-09300829.text_edition: \n Caius Sallustius Cai filius Sergia Hostianus praefectus fabrum vixit nnos XXVI Caius Sallustius Primus Hostianus pater\n
EDCS-09300829.translation: Caius Sallustius Hostianus, son of Caius Sallustius, of the Sergia tribe, prefect of engineers, lived 26 years. Caius Sallustius Primus Hostianus, his father.
EDCS-09300829.summary: A tombstone for Caius Sallustius Hostianus, a prefect of engineers, erected by his father.

EDCS-09300830.text_edition: \n Quinto Aullio Publi filio Cornelia Camerti | (centurioni) cohortis III vigilum |(centurioni) cohortis XIII urbanae Egloge liberta patrono bene merenti sibi et suis fecit\n
EDCS-09300830.translation: For Quintus Aullius, son of Publius, a centurion of the 3rd Cohort of Vigiles and a centurion of the 13th Urban Cohort. Egloge, his freedwoman, made this for her well-deserving patron and for herself and her own.
EDCS-09300830.summary: A dedication to Quintus Aullius, a centurion in two cohorts, made by his freedwoman.

EDCS-09300833.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Florus Mercurium collegio d(ono)? d(edit)\n
EDCS-09300833.translation: Tiberius Claudius Florus gave (this) to the college of Mercury as a gift.
EDCS-09300833.summary: A dedication of a gift to the college of Mercury by Tiberius Claudius Florus.

EDCS-09300834.text_edition: \n Spurius Blossius Spuri filius Teretina duovir vivus Spurius Blossius Spuri libertus Nicefor\n
EDCS-09300834.translation: Spurius Blossius, son of Spurius, of the Teretina tribe, duovir for life. Spurius Blossius Nicefor, his freedman.
EDCS-09300834.summary: An inscription recording Spurius Blossius, a duovir, and his freedman Nicefor.

EDCS-09300835.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Quinto Salluvio Iaso qui vicxit sic nnos XXXII dies XLIII Salluvia Vitalis fratri bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09300835.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Iaso, who lived 32 years and 43 days. His brother Salluvius Vitalis made this for his well-deserving brother.
EDCS-09300835.summary: A tombstone for Quintus Salluvius Iaso, erected by his brother.

EDCS-09300836.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tyche filia matri bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09300836.translation: To Tyche, daughter, for her well-deserving mother, erected this.
EDCS-09300836.summary: A funerary dedication by a daughter to her mother, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-09300837.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Mindius Thelymorphus Lucio Mindio Thelymorphano alumno karissimo fecit\n
EDCS-09300837.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Mindius Thelymorphus erected this to his dearest foster-son, Lucius Mindius Thelymorphanus.
EDCS-09300837.summary: A funerary dedication by a foster-father to his foster-son, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-09300839.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marco Annio Ianuario coniugi Ennia Ianuaria cum qua vixit annis XII bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09300839.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Ennia Ianuaria erected this to her husband, Marcus Annius Ianuarius, to whom she was married for 12 years, for his deservingness.
EDCS-09300839.summary: A funerary dedication by a wife to her husband, mentioning their 12 years of marriage, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-09300840.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Silviae Luci filiae Apriculae uxori inconparabili sic quae sibi et marito suo testamento fieri iussit Aulus Tatius Auli filius Ampliatus maritus\n
EDCS-09300840.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Aulus Tatius Ampliatus, son of Aulus, her husband, erected this to his incomparable wife, Silvia Apicula, daughter of Lucius, as she had ordered in her will to be made for herself and her husband.
EDCS-09300840.summary: A funerary dedication by a husband for his wife, based on her will, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-09300843.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-09300843.text_edition: \n Mavortii Quinto Flavio Maesio Egnatio Lolliano clarissimo viro quaestori kandidato praetori urbano auguri publico populi Romani Quiritium consulari albei sic Tiberis et cloacarum consulari operum publicorum consulari aquarum consulari Campaniae comiti Flaviali comiti Orientis comiti primi ordinis et proconsuli provinciae Africae regio arae Lucullianae patrono dignissimo\n
EDCS-09300843.translation: To Mavortius, Quintus Flavius Maesius Egnatius Lollianus, most distinguished man, quaestor designate, urban praetor, public augur of the Roman People of Quirites, consul for the Tiber riverbed and sewers, consul for public works, consul for aqueducts, consul for Campania, count of the Flavians, count of the East, count of the first rank, and proconsul of the province of Africa, patron of the region of the Lucullian altar, most worthy patron.
EDCS-09300843.summary: An honorific inscription praising Quintus Flavius Maesius Egnatius Lollianus for his numerous high offices and patronage, likely from the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-09300844.text_edition: \n Mavortii Quinto Flavio Maesio Egnatio Lolliano clarissimo viro quaestori kandidato praetori urbano auguri publico populi Romani Quiritium consulari albei sic Tiberis et cloacarum consulari operum publicum sic consulari aquarum consulari Campaniae comiti Flaviali comiti Orientis comiti primi ordinis et proconsuli provinciae Africae regio clivi vitriari sive vici turari patrono dignissimo\n
EDCS-09300844.translation: To Mavortius, Quintus Flavius Maesius Egnatius Lollianus, most distinguished man, quaestor designate, urban praetor, public augur of the Roman People of Quirites, consul for the Tiber riverbed and sewers, consul for public works, consul for aqueducts, consul for Campania, count of the Flavians, count of the East, count of the first rank, and proconsul of the province of Africa, patron of the region of the glassmakers' slope or the potters' street, most worthy patron.
EDCS-09300844.summary: An honorific inscription praising Quintus Flavius Maesius Egnatius Lollianus for his numerous high offices and patronage, likely from the late Roman Empire, similar to EDCS-09300843 but specifying different patronages.

EDCS-09300846.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate V consuli III inquilini vici ani\n
EDCS-09300846.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Traianus Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 3rd time, [by the] residents of the Anius quarter.
EDCS-09300846.summary: A dedication to Emperor Hadrian by the residents of a specific city quarter (vici ani), likely from the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-09300847.text_edition: \n Auctori salutis publicae genitori principum divo Caro Rutilius Crispinus vir clarissimus curator rei publicae Puteolanorum\n
EDCS-09300847.translation: To the Author of Public Health, Father of Princes, the deified Carus. Rutilius Crispinus, most distinguished man, curator of the Puteolan Republic.
EDCS-09300847.summary: An inscription honoring the deified Emperor Carus by Rutilius Crispinus, a curator in Puteoli, likely from the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09300860.summary: A very brief inscription possibly recording names and a location, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-09300862.text_edition: \n Cernite Thebaides modo tales sed Bromios regia Menas\n
EDCS-09300862.translation: Behold the Theban women, such as they were, but Bromius [is] king of Mena.
EDCS-09300862.summary: A poetic fragment, possibly a riddle or a quotation, referencing figures from mythology and Bacchus (Bromius), likely from antiquity.

EDCS-09300863.text_edition: \n Munera Poppaea misit Veneri sanctissimae berullum helencumque unio mixtus erat\n
EDCS-09300863.translation: Poppaea sent gifts to the most holy Venus: a beryl and a pearl mixed with an emerald.
EDCS-09300863.summary: This is a dedication to Venus, likely from the empress Poppaea Sabina, listing valuable offerings.

EDCS-09300864.text_edition: \n Caesar ut ad Venerem venet sanctissimam ut tui te vexere pedes caelestes Auguste millia milliorum ponderis auri fuit\n
EDCS-09300864.translation: May Caesar come to the most holy Venus, may your celestial feet carry you, Augustus, a weight of thousands of myriads of gold.
EDCS-09300864.summary: This is a complex dedication to Venus and Augustus, possibly a vow or a poetic invocation, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-09300865.text_edition: \n Pro salutem sic reditum sic et victoria Cai Iuli Philippi votum hic fecit Laribus Publius Cornelius Felix et Vitalis Cuspi\n
EDCS-09300865.translation: For the safety and return and also victory, Publius Cornelius Felix and Vitalis Cuspi made a vow here to the household gods for Caius Julius Philippus.
EDCS-09300865.summary: This is a dedication recording a vow made by Publius Cornelius Felix and Vitalis Cuspi for the well-being and success of Caius Julius Philippus, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09300866.summary: This is a simple ownership or dedication marking the name of Caius Julius Philippus, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09300867.summary: This inscription is a memorial or dedication to Cassius Cascellius.

EDCS-09300868.summary: This is a brief acclamation or dedication, possibly to a gladiator or athlete named Paris, and celebrating victory.

EDCS-09300869.summary: This inscription records an honor or dedication to Caius Julius in his role as aedile.

EDCS-09300870.text_edition: \n Caium Iulium Polybium duumvirum dignum rei publicae oro vos faciatis\n
EDCS-09300870.translation: I beg you, make Caius Julius Polybius a worthy duumvir for the republic.
EDCS-09300870.summary: This is a public plea or petition urging the citizens to elect Caius Julius Polybius as a duumvir, likely from a Roman colony.

EDCS-09300871.summary: This inscription records an event or meeting between Habitus and Ysasal, the meaning of 'habitus' here is unclear and could refer to a person or a state.

EDCS-09400115.text_edition: \n Iuliae Augustae matri castrorum Imperatoris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici patris patriae quinquennalitate Cai Vibi Felicis IIviratu Cai Volcei Magni Quinti Egrili Ingenui cura agentibus Caio Lucceio Felice Marco Mettio Modesto Dedicata Idibus Septembribus Dextro II (et) Prisco consulibus\n
EDCS-09400115.translation: Dedicated in the consulship of Dextrus II and Priscus, on the Ides of September, during the quinquennial festival and the duumvirate of Caius Vibus Felix and Caius Volcius Magnus, with Quintus Egilius Ingenuus overseeing, for Julia Augusta, mother of the camp of the emperor Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-09400115.summary: This is a dedication stone commemorating public works or a festival during the reign of Septimius Severus, overseen by local officials and magistrates, dated to September 13, 202 CE.

EDCS-09400117.text_edition: \n Aram Volcano Publius Pacilius Publi filius Palatina Zenon Laetus dictator aedilis praefectus iure dicundo et sacris faciundis loco dato decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-09400117.translation: To Vulcan. Publius Pacilius Zenon Laetus, son of Publius, of the Palatina tribe, dictator, aedile, prefect for dispensing justice and performing sacred rites, on land granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-09400117.summary: This is a dedication to Vulcan by Publius Pacilius Zenon Laetus, who held several civic offices, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09400118.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Aelio Euticheti et Tito Aelio Victorino fecerunt patroni libertis bene merentibus\n
EDCS-09400118.translation: To the spirits of the departed. The patrons made this for their well-deserving freedmen, Titus Aelius Eutycherus and Titus Aelius Victorinus.
EDCS-09400118.summary: A funerary dedication made by patrons for their freedmen, Titus Aelius Eutycherus and Titus Aelius Victorinus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09400120.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Telesphorus Hyciae coniugi karissimae bene merenti et Athymnius filius \n
EDCS-09400120.translation: Telesphorus made this for Hycia, his dearest, well-deserving wife, and for his son Athymnius.
EDCS-09400120.summary: A funerary dedication by Telesphorus for his wife Hycia and son Athymnius, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09400121.summary: A dedication to Jupiter by Ecestus, the date of which is uncertain but likely Roman imperial.

EDCS-09400123.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Genucia Valentina quae vixit nnos XXVIII Caesia Valentina mater filiae bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09400123.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caesia Valentina made this for her well-deserving daughter, Genucia Valentina, who lived 28 years.
EDCS-09400123.summary: A funerary dedication by Caesia Valentina for her daughter Genucia Valentina, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09400124.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Gaianus veteranus miles Auggustorum Caesia Unenlia coniux vene sic merenti coniugi fecit cum filia militavit annos XXVIII\n
EDCS-09400124.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caesia Unenlia made this for her well-deserving husband, Aelius Gaianus, a veteran soldier of the Emperors, with whom she served for 28 years, along with their daughter.
EDCS-09400124.summary: A funerary dedication by Caesia Unenlia for her husband Aelius Gaianus, a veteran who served 28 years, and their daughter, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09400126.text_edition: \n Tito Aelio Augusti liberto Felici procuratori Publius Aelius Victor frater et Aelius Victorinus filius\n
EDCS-09400126.translation: For Titus Aelius Felix, freedman of Augustus and procurator. Publius Aelius Victor, his brother, and Aelius Victorinus, his son.
EDCS-09400126.summary: An inscription honoring Titus Aelius Felix, a freedman of Augustus and a procurator, with his brother and son mentioned, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09400127.text_edition: \n Marco Ceionio Flavino Marcus Ceionius Silanus et Vallia parentes\n
EDCS-09400127.translation: For Marcus Ceionius Flavius. Marcus Ceionius Silanus and Vallia, his parents.
EDCS-09400127.summary: An inscription by parents Marcus Ceionius Silanus and Vallia for their son Marcus Ceionius Flavius, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09400128.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aquilinus made this for Aurelia Hierophila, his sweetest, well-deserving wife.
EDCS-09400128.summary: A funerary dedication by Aquilinus for his wife Aurelia Hierophila, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09400129.translation: Of Marcus Aurelius Caricus, freedman of Augustus.
EDCS-09400129.summary: An inscription mentioning Marcus Aurelius Caricus, a freedman of Augustus, the purpose of which is not specified, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09400130.text_edition: \n o Clementino qui vixit annis sic XXIII menses VII dies XVI Aurelius Seneca miles cohortis III praetoriae piae vindicis Severianae et Aelia Maxima mater et Aurelia Valeria heredes fecerunt\n
EDCS-09400130.translation: To Clementinus, who lived 23 years, 7 months, and 16 days. Aurelius Seneca, soldier of the 3rd Praetorian Cohort, Pious Vindex Severiana, and Aelia Maxima, mother, and Aurelia Valeria, heirs, made this.
EDCS-09400130.summary: A tombstone set up by heirs for Aurelius Seneca, a soldier of the Praetorian Guard, and his relative Clementinus, who died young.

EDCS-09400131.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Marcus inaco man qui vixit annos XX bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09400131.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aurelius Marcus, who lived 20 years, made this for a deserving person.
EDCS-09400131.summary: A tombstone set up by Aurelius Marcus for an unnamed deserving individual, likely a relative.

EDCS-09400133.text_edition: \n Aurel(ius) Congn legionis II Parthicae qui vixit annos XXXIII Cisonia Primitiva coniux cum filiis suis bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09400133.translation: Cisonia Primitiva, wife, with her sons, made this for her deserving husband Aurelius Congn, of the 2nd Parthian Legion, who lived 33 years.
EDCS-09400133.summary: A tombstone dedicated by a wife and sons to a soldier of the 2nd Parthian Legion who died at 33.

EDCS-09400134.text_edition: \n imani vixit annos XXX Valerius Gaius coniugi pientissimae fecit\n
EDCS-09400134.translation: Valerius Gaius made this for his most devoted wife, who lived 30 years.
EDCS-09400134.summary: A tombstone erected by a husband for his wife, who died at the age of 30.

EDCS-09400135.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Melixsae sic momeriae sic vixit nnos III mensem I et dies decem TAPE Aurelius Taurus pater filiae bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09400135.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aurelia Melixsa, who lived 3 years, 1 month, and 10 days. Aurelius Taurus, father, made this for his deserving daughter.
EDCS-09400135.summary: A tombstone erected by Aurelius Taurus for his daughter Aurelia Melixsa, who died in early childhood.

EDCS-09400136.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Septimio qui vixit nnum I et menses XI et dies XIII Aurelius Iulius miles legionis II Parthicae memoriam fecit bene merenti\n
EDCS-09400136.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aurelius Iulius, soldier of the 2nd Parthian Legion, who lived 1 year, 11 months, and 13 days, made this memorial for a deserving person.
EDCS-09400136.summary: A tombstone set up by Aurelius Iulius, a soldier of the 2nd Parthian Legion, for an unnamed deserving individual, likely a relative.

EDCS-09400137.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Avistus vixit avus imius memoriae G Valerius Iraneus sic miles legionis II Parthicae ans curator e manu\n
EDCS-09400137.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aurelius Avistus, grandfather, for his deserving grandson. G. Valerius Iraneus, soldier of the 2nd Parthian Legion, freedman, curator of a scribe. Lived [---] years.
EDCS-09400137.summary: A tombstone set up by a grandfather and a soldier of the 2nd Parthian Legion, possibly a freedman, for the deceased Aurelius Avistus.

EDCS-09400138.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus menses XI dies a emes pater Aurelia mater bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-09400138.translation: To the Divine Manes. [---] father and Aurelia mother, made this for a deserving person. [---] 11 months.
EDCS-09400138.summary: A tombstone set up by a father and mother for an unnamed deserving individual, likely their child.

EDCS-09400139.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurean qui vixit annos XIX menses XII dies XIII Aurel(ia) Decor mater bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09400139.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aurean, who lived 19 years, 12 months, and 13 days. Aurelia Decor, mother, made this for a deserving person.
EDCS-09400139.summary: A tombstone erected by Aurelia Decor for her daughter Aurean, who died at 19.

EDCS-09400140.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marci Aureli Iuliani militis legionis II Parthicae Severianae Antoninianae qui vixit annos XXXII (militavit) annos XI Septimius Breucus et Aurelius Herculanus heredes bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-09400140.translation: To the Divine Manes. Marcus Aurelius Iulianus, soldier of the 2nd Parthian Legion Severiana Antoniniana, who lived 32 years and served 11 years. Septimius Breucus and Aurelius Herculanus, heirs, set up this memorial for a deserving person.
EDCS-09400140.summary: A tombstone for Marcus Aurelius Iulianus, a soldier of the 2nd Parthian Legion, who died at 32 after 11 years of service, set up by his heirs.

EDCS-09400141.text_edition: \n Aurelius Iulianus miles in legione Parthica Aur militavit nnos XXXIII memoriae pi amantissim bene merenti\n
EDCS-09400141.translation: Aurelius Iulianus served as a soldier in the Parthica Legion for 33 years; in memory of a most loving and deserving man.
EDCS-09400141.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Aurelius Iulianus, a soldier who served for 33 years in the Parthica Legion.

EDCS-09400142.text_edition: \n Memoriae perpetuae Pudentilla dulcissimo qui vixit nnos XVIII bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09400142.translation: For the perpetual memory of Pudentilla, who lived 18 years; made by a deserving person.
EDCS-09400142.summary: A funerary inscription for Pudentilla, who died at the age of 18, set up by a loving individual.

EDCS-09400143.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vernacla vixit annos V menses VI Septimius Doles vernae suae fecit\n
EDCS-09400143.translation: To the departed spirits. Vernacla lived 5 years and 6 months. Septimius Doles made this for his own slave.
EDCS-09400143.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Vernacla, a slave child who died at the age of 5 years and 6 months, dedicated by her owner Septimius Doles.

EDCS-09400144.text_edition: \n Alexander tubicen legionis II Parthicae et Aurelia Amanda dulcissima \n
EDCS-09400144.translation: Alexander, trumpeter of the II Parthica Legion, and Aurelia Amanda, his most beloved.
EDCS-09400144.summary: An epitaph commemorating Alexander, a trumpeter in the II Parthica Legion, and Aurelia Amanda.

EDCS-09400145.text_edition: \n Valeriae filiae piissimae quae vixit annis III mensibus X diebus XX posuit Valerius Ursinus et Aurelia Quarta parentes bene merent(i)\n
EDCS-09400145.translation: Valerius Ursinus and Aurelia Quarta, parents, set this up for their most dutiful daughter Valeria, who lived 3 years, 10 months, and 20 days, for her well-deserving memory.
EDCS-09400145.summary: A funerary epitaph from parents Valerius Ursinus and Aurelia Quarta for their daughter Valeria, who died at a young age.

EDCS-09400146.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vi Partiano militavit annos qui vixit annos XXIX He \n
EDCS-09400146.translation: To the departed spirits. Vi Partiano served in the army for 29 years; he lived 29 years.
EDCS-09400146.summary: A funerary inscription for Vi Partiano, who served in the army for 29 years and died at the age of 29.

EDCS-09400147.text_edition: \n Aurelius Bithus venator n Aurelius Mulu bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09400147.translation: Aurelius Bithus, hunter, and Aurelius Mulu, made this for a deserving person.
EDCS-09400147.summary: An inscription dedicating something to Aurelius Bithus, a hunter, by Aurelius Mulu.

EDCS-09400148.text_edition: \n Aurelius Axu qui vixit annos X menses VIII dies VII bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09400148.translation: Aurelius Axu, who lived 10 years, 8 months, and 7 days, made this for a deserving person.
EDCS-09400148.summary: A funerary inscription for Aurelius Axu, who died at the age of 10 years, 8 months, and 7 days.

EDCS-09400149.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Novio Sabino margaritario fecit Novia Tyche liberta et uxor coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-09400149.translation: To the departed spirits. For Lucio Novio Sabino, the pearl merchant. Novia Tyche, freedwoman and wife, made this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-09400149.summary: A funerary inscription for Lucio Novio Sabino, a pearl merchant, dedicated by his freedwoman wife, Novia Tyche.

EDCS-09400150.text_edition: \n Diis Manibus Cai Lusii Clementis Eulalus libertus\n
EDCS-09400150.translation: To the departed spirits. For Caius Lusius Clementis. Eulalus, his freedman.
EDCS-09400150.summary: A funerary inscription honoring Caius Lusius Clementis, dedicated by his freedman Eulalus.

EDCS-09400152.summary: This inscription records a shrine dedicated to the goddess Laverna.

EDCS-09400153.text_edition: \n Sexto Atilio Sexti et |(mulieris) liberto Italico Sextus Oppius Sexti libertus Felix fecit\n
EDCS-09400153.translation: Sextus Oppius Felix, freedman of Sextus, made this for Sextus Atilius Italicus, freedman of Sextus and a woman, and for his freedwoman.
EDCS-09400153.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the maker of a monument for freedmen and a freedwoman, likely from the early imperial period.

EDCS-09400154.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Praeciliae Luci filiae Moschidi coniugi carissimae et sanctissimae quae vixit annis XLIII mecum annis XXXV Publius Tettius Aspecus et sibi fecit\n
EDCS-09400154.translation: For Praecilia Moschis, daughter of Lucius, his dearest and most sacred wife, who lived 43 years with me for 35 years. Publius Tettius Aspecus also made this for himself.
EDCS-09400154.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a husband for his deceased wife and for himself, dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-09400155.text_edition: \n Trophimus filiae piissimae et Cnaeus Cornelius Epaphroditus coniugi carissimae fecerunt\n
EDCS-09400155.translation: Trophimus and Gnaeus Cornelius Epaphroditus made this for their most devoted daughter and dearest wife.
EDCS-09400155.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication of a monument by a husband and son for a wife and mother, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-09400163.text_edition: \n Flacceia Auli liberta Lais Orbia |(mulieris) liberta Lais Cominia Marci liberta Philocaris Venturia Quinti liberta Thais culinam Veneri de suo fecerunt loco precario\n
EDCS-09400163.translation: Flacceia Lais, freedwoman of Aulus, Orbia Lais, freedwoman of a woman, Cominia Philocaris, freedwoman of Marcus, and Venturia Thais, freedwoman of Quintus, made the kitchen for Venus from their own property by precarious tenure.
EDCS-09400163.summary: This inscription records the joint construction of a kitchen for Venus by four freedwomen, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-09400175.text_edition: \n Caio Ligustio Cai filio Falerna et Caio Ligustio Cai filio Falerna Prisco et Ligustiae Cai filiae Priscae et Caio Ligustio Cai liberto Nemori et Ligustiae Cai libertae Musae arbitratu Ligustiae Cai libertae Musae\n
EDCS-09400175.translation: For Gaius Ligustius Falerna, son of Gaius, and for Gaius Ligustius Falerna Priscus, son of Gaius, and for Ligustia Prisca, daughter of Gaius, and for Gaius Ligustius Nemori, freedman of Gaius, and for Ligustia Musa, freedwoman of Gaius, by the arbitration of Ligustia Musa, freedwoman of Gaius.
EDCS-09400175.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to several members of the Ligustii family, with a specific mention of arbitration, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-09400176.text_edition: \n Augusto sacrum Genio Augustalium Sextus Gellius Georgus curator Augustalium anni sui pequnia sic sua\n
EDCS-09400176.translation: Sacred to Augustus. Sextus Gellius Georgus, curator of the Augustales for his year, [made this] with his own money.
EDCS-09400176.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the genius of the Augustales by a curator, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-09400177.translation: For the deified Vespasian. Gaius Volusius Menecles.
EDCS-09400177.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the deified emperor Vespasian by Gaius Volusius Menecles, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09400178.text_edition: \n Lucio Plaetorio pellioni et Plaetoriae Quartae conlibertae suae et Floro filio vixit nnos XVI\n
EDCS-09400178.translation: For Lucius Plaetorius Pellio and his fellow freedwoman Plaetoria Quarta and for Florus their son, who lived 16 years.
EDCS-09400178.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to a freedman, his fellow freedwoman, and their son, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-09400179.translation: For Soterichus of the highest honors of Plaetorius.
EDCS-09400179.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Soterichus, possibly a freedman honored by the Plaetorius family, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-09400180.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gnaeus Helvius Neo.

EDCS-09400181.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Prisca, a freedwoman.

EDCS-09400878.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luci Volussi Primitivi vixit annis IX mensibus VII diebus XX Pomponius Fortunatus pater filio dulcissimo\n
EDCS-09400878.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Volussius Primitivus lived for 9 years, 7 months, and 20 days. Pomponius Fortunatus, his father, [dedicated this] to his most beloved son.
EDCS-09400878.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Volussius Primitivus, who died young, dedicated by his father.

EDCS-09400879.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luci P Euuhodi qui bisit sic annis VII diebus XXXIII fecc(it) Secundus pater filio dulcissimo\n
EDCS-09400879.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lucius P. Euuhodus lived for 7 years and 33 days. Secundus, his father, made [this] for his most beloved son.
EDCS-09400879.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius P. Euuhodus, who died young, dedicated by his father.

EDCS-09400882.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus M C A Thaidi vernae carissimae quae bixit sic annis III mensibus VIII dies sic XVIII\n
EDCS-09400882.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Thaïs, the most beloved slave girl, who lived for 3 years, 8 months, and 18 days.
EDCS-09400882.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Thaïs, a young slave girl.

EDCS-09400883.text_edition: \n Quinto Pomponio Munatiano Clodiano consuli XVviro sacris faciundis praesidi provinciae Baeticae sodali Titiali praetori curatori viae Latinae tribuno plebis quaestori provinciae Macedoniae Pomponii Marcellinus et Aprilis liberti patrono praestantissimo\n
EDCS-09400883.translation: To Quintus Pomponius Munatianus Clodianus, consul, member of the XVvir sacris faciundis, governor of Baetica, member of the sodales Titii, praetor, curator of the Via Latina, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of Macedonia. His freedmen Pomponii Marcellinus and Aprilis [dedicated this] to their most distinguished patron.
EDCS-09400883.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Quintus Pomponius Munatianus Clodianus, listing his numerous high-ranking political and religious offices, and dedicated by his freedmen.

EDCS-09400884.text_edition: \n Publio Coelio Luci liberto Sileno viro suo et sibi agri iugera XXV |(sextans) |(sesuncia) |(sicilicus) ex fundo Soraniano monumento dedit\n
EDCS-09400884.translation: Publius Coelius gave to his husband Silenus, freedman of Lucius, and to himself, 25 jugera of land, one-sixth of a jugerum, one-sixth of a sextans, and one sicilicus, from the Soranian estate, as a monument.
EDCS-09400884.summary: This is a dedication of land by Publius Coelius to his husband Silenus and himself, likely for funerary or commemorative purposes.

EDCS-09400886.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Crispino filio equiti Romano vixit annis XXIIII Aelia Aphrodite mater et sibi\n
EDCS-09400886.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Marcus Aurelius Crispinus, son, Roman knight, who lived for 24 years. Aelia Aphrodite, his mother, [dedicated this] for him and for herself.
EDCS-09400886.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Aurelius Crispinus, a young Roman knight, dedicated by his mother.

EDCS-09400889.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Henante sic hic sita Auriolus contubernali bene merenti fecit vixit annos numero XXV\n
EDCS-09400889.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Auriolus dedicated this here, well-deserved, to his comrade, who lived for 25 years.
EDCS-09400889.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Auriolus to his well-deserving comrade.

EDCS-09400892.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Claudia Dionysiades, patron.
EDCS-09400892.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Claudia Dionysiades.

EDCS-09400898.text_edition: \n Tito Sextio Cai filio Papiria Alexandro Quintus Sextius Martialis frater Vettia Cai filia Tranquilla \n
EDCS-09400898.translation: To Titus Sextius Alexander, son of Caius, of the Papiria tribe. Quintus Sextius Martialis, his brother, and Vettia Tranquilla, daughter of Caius, made this.
EDCS-09400898.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a brother and sister for their deceased relative, Titus Sextius Alexander.

EDCS-09400905.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titi Flavi Felicis Successus Augusti libertus tabularius rationis marmorum Lunessium sic liberto karissimo\n
EDCS-09400905.translation: To the Divine Spirits of Successus, freedman of the emperor, administrator of the marble accounts of Lunessium, a most beloved freedman, for his freedman.
EDCS-09400905.summary: This is a funerary dedication set up for Successus, an imperial freedman managing marble accounts, by his own freedman.

EDCS-09400911.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Artemisiae filiae Callistianus Auggustorum libertus et Aurelia Euthenia parentes\n
EDCS-09400911.translation: To the Divine Spirits of Aelia Artemisia, daughter of Aelius. Callistianus, freedman of the emperors, and Aurelia Euthenia, her parents, made this.
EDCS-09400911.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by parents for their deceased daughter, Aelia Artemisia.

EDCS-09400929.text_edition: \n Eucarpo filio Eucarpus Luci Claudi Pollionis servus\n
EDCS-09400929.translation: To Eucarpo, my son. Eucarpus, slave of Lucius Claudius Pollio, made this.
EDCS-09400929.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a slave named Eucarpus for his son, also named Eucarpo.

EDCS-09400947.text_edition: \n Titus Fadius Titi libertus Teucer Titus Fadius Teucri [libertus ------\n
EDCS-09400947.translation: Titus Fadius Teucer, freedman of Titus, [son of] Titus Fadius Teucer, [freedman ------
EDCS-09400947.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription likely for a freedman, possibly recording a complex family relationship involving multiple freedmen.

EDCS-09400948.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that likely begins a dedication to someone named Tiberius Claudius.

EDCS-09400949.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titiae filiae piissimae et dulcissimae quae vixit an nus pater bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09400949.translation: To the Divine Spirits of Titia, most dutiful and dearest daughter, who lived [X] years. Her father made this for a well-deserving [daughter].
EDCS-09400949.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a father for his beloved and dutiful daughter, Titia.

EDCS-09400954.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely the name of the owner or the subject of a monument.

EDCS-09400956.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely the name of the owner or the subject of a monument.

EDCS-09400957.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luci Lusi Martialis vixit annis VII mensibus VI diebus VIIII incisus frumento publico Stloga et Hoenio Severo consulibus accepit V Kalendas Iulias Flacco et Herode consulibus Lucius Lusius Phoebion et Lusia Callityche dulcissimo fecerunt\n
EDCS-09400957.translation: To the Divine Spirits of Lucius Lusius Martialis, who lived 7 years, 6 months, and 9 days. He was buried at public expense. Under the consulship of Stloga and Hoenius Severus, he received [his burial on?] the day before the Kalends of July, under the consulship of Flaccus and Herodes. Lucius Lusius Phoebion and Lusia Callityche made this for their dearest [son].
EDCS-09400957.summary: This funerary inscription details the life and burial of a young boy, Lucius Lusius Martialis, and records the consuls of two different periods, with his parents dedicating the monument.

EDCS-09400960.translation: To the departed spirits of Cornelia Amanda.
EDCS-09400960.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cornelia Amanda.

EDCS-09400976.text_edition: \n Tito Flavio Acindyni filio Quirina Scopelliano duorum equitum Romanorum patri adlecto in decuriis praetori IIviro iterum praetori IIviro quinquennali patrono coloniae huic Privernates cenam Idibus Martiis dandam et statuam ponendam fecerunt MIRI quinnnalis ob merita eius quod ob honorem quinquennalitatis ludos scaenicos diebus quinque ediderit locus datus decreto decurionum XIII Kalendas Septembres Lucio Aelio Caesare II Publio Coelio Balbino consulibus ob dedicationem crustum et mulsum populo dedit\n
EDCS-09400976.translation: The people of Privernum, for Titus Flavius Acindynus, son of Titus, of the Quirina tribe, Scopellianus, father of two Roman equestrians, who was promoted to the praetorian order, twice a praetorian duovir, quinquennial duovir, patron of the colony, had a dinner given and a statue erected in his honor on the Ides of March. They voted this at the expense of the quinquennalia because he provided five days of theatrical games for the office of quinquennial duovir. The place was granted by decree of the decurions on the 13th day before the Kalends of September, during the consulship of Lucius Aelius Caesar II and Publius Coelius Balbinus. For the dedication, he gave honeyed wine and cake to the populace.
EDCS-09400976.summary: This is a public dedication honoring Titus Flavius Acindynus for his services as quinquennial duovir in Privernum, likely dating to the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-09400977.text_edition: \n Bono Eventui sacrum Aemilius Maximus praetor IIvir cultoribus donum dedit\n
EDCS-09400977.translation: Sacred to Good Fortune. Aemilius Maximus, praetorian duovir, gave this gift to the cultores.
EDCS-09400977.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Bono Eventus (Good Fortune) by Aemilius Maximus, a praetorian duovir.

EDCS-09400980.text_edition: \n ------ ma]tri Appii Caecinae Suetri Sabini clarissimi viri Suetrius Tertius libertus eius et Septimus servus cum suis patronae praestantissimae\n
EDCS-09400980.translation: To their most distinguished mother, Appia Caecina. Suetrius Tertius, his freedman, and Septimus, his slave, together with their own, [dedicated this] to their most excellent patroness.
EDCS-09400980.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Appia Caecina, set up by her freedman and slave.

EDCS-09400982.text_edition: \n Octavius Luci libertus Alexander magister Graecus Marcus Alfius Marci libertus Philemon\n
EDCS-09400982.translation: Octavius Alexander, freedman of Lucius, Greek master; Marcus Alfius Philemon, freedman of Marcus.
EDCS-09400982.summary: This inscription lists the names and roles of two individuals, likely freedmen, one a Greek teacher.

EDCS-09400994.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Heriae Phoebe libertae bene merenti Heria Celerina patrona fecit\n
EDCS-09400994.translation: To the departed spirits of Heria Phoebe, a deserving freedwoman. Heria Celerina, her patroness, made this.
EDCS-09400994.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Heria Phoebe, a freedwoman, erected by her patroness.

EDCS-09400995.text_edition: \n Trebellia Marci liberta Veneria sibi et suis Trebelliae Marci libertae Hilarae matri Marco Trebellio Marci liberto Priamo Marco Trebellio Marci liberto Amphioni fratribus\n
EDCS-09400995.translation: Trebellia Veneria, freedwoman of Marcus, for herself and her family: to her mother Trebellia Hilara, freedwoman of Marcus; to her brother Marcus Trebellius Priamus, freedman of Marcus; and to her brother Marcus Trebellius Amphion, freedman of Marcus.
EDCS-09400995.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Trebellia Veneria and her relatives, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09400996.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Lucius miles classis praetoriae Misenensium natione Surus militavit annos XXVIIII ex III Salvia Drusius Aelius Lucianus patri bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09400996.translation: To the departed spirits of Publius Aelius Lucius, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet of Misenum, a Syrian by birth. He served for 29 years. Salvia Drusia and Aelius Lucianus made this for their deserving father.
EDCS-09400996.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Aelius Lucius, a soldier in the Praetorian fleet at Misenum.

EDCS-09400997.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberi Claudi Tiberi filii Arriani veterani natione Alexandrini Claudius Arrianus pientissimo patri et bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09400997.translation: To the departed spirits of Tiberius Claudius Arrianus, son of Tiberius, a veteran, an Alexandrian by birth. Claudius Arrianus made this for his most dutiful and deserving father.
EDCS-09400997.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tiberius Claudius Arrianus, a veteran of Alexandrian origin, erected by his son.

EDCS-09400998.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberius Claudius Puteolanus Claudiae Philumene coniugi bene merenti fecit quae vixit nnos XXI\n
EDCS-09400998.translation: To the departed spirits of Tiberius Claudius Puteolanus. He made this for his deserving wife, Claudia Philumene, who lived 21 years.
EDCS-09400998.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Claudia Philumene, erected by her husband Tiberius Claudius Puteolanus.

EDCS-09400999.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Herennius Epaphroditus sibi et Herenniae Clade et Herenniae Marcellae patronabus sic et Herennio Fideli et Herennio Crescenti et Herenniae Tyche coniugi suae et libertis meis libertabusque posterisque eorum et Volussio Lamyro nepoti et Herennio Synergo et Herennio Africano et Herenniae sic Meniadi\n
EDCS-09400999.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Lucius Herennius Epaphroditus for himself, and for his patrons Herennia Clade and Herennia Marcella, and also for Herennius Fidelis, and Herennius Crescens, and his wife Herennia Tyche, and for his freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants, and for his grandson Volussius Lamyros, and for Herennius Synergos, and Herennius Africanus, and Herennia Meniadis.
EDCS-09400999.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Herennius Epaphroditus, who set it up for himself and many family members and freedpersons.

EDCS-09401000.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Eria Veneria sibi et Aegiali sic Caesaris servo coniugi bene merenti hoc monumentum sive sepulcrum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-09401000.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Eria Veneria for herself and for Aegialis, slave of Caesar, her well-deserving husband. This monument or tomb shall not pass to an heir.
EDCS-09401000.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Eria Veneria and her husband Aegialis, specifying that the monument is not inheritable.

EDCS-09401001.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Antonino veterano ex classe praetoria Misenense\n
EDCS-09401001.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Gaius Julius Antoninus, a veteran from the Praetorian fleet at Misenum.
EDCS-09401001.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Julius Antoninus, a veteran of the Praetorian fleet.

EDCS-09401002.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iunio Serapioni qui vixit nnos IIII menses VIII Iunius Severus alumno dulcissimo bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09401002.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Iunius Serapio, who lived 4 years and 8 months. Iunius Severus made this for his most cherished adopted son, who deserved well.
EDCS-09401002.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Iunius Serapio, set up by his adopted father Iunius Severus.

EDCS-09401003.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Patulcio Calidiano vixit nno I mensibus X diebus XXVII Sextus Patulcius Trophimion\n
EDCS-09401003.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Patulcius Calidianus, who lived 1 year, 10 months, and 27 days. Sextus Patulcius Trophimion [made this].
EDCS-09401003.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Patulcius Calidianus, set up by Sextus Patulcius Trophimion.

EDCS-09401005.text_edition: \n Manius Sirtius Mani filius Valeriae Callityche et Epaphrae delicieis sic leiberteis sic leibertabusque sic quos ex testamento inferre iussit\n
EDCS-09401005.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Manius Sirtius for his freedmen Epaphra and Valeria Callityche, his darlings, and for the freedmen and freedwomen whom he ordered to be buried by his will.
EDCS-09401005.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Manius Sirtius, concerning his freedpersons whom he wished to be buried.

EDCS-09401006.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Tantiliae Megaleni coniugi incomparabili Vinuleius Fortunatus fecit\n
EDCS-09401006.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Vinuleius Fortunatus made this for his incomparable wife Tantilia Megalene.
EDCS-09401006.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tantilia Megalene, set up by her husband Vinuleius Fortunatus.

EDCS-09401007.text_edition: \n Auli Titini Narcissi Auli Titini Agathopod[is ------ Auli Titini Hisochrysi sic Titiniae Urban[ae ------\n
EDCS-09401007.translation: For Aulus Titinius Narcissus, for Aulus Titinius Agathop[odis ---], for Aulus Titinius Hisochrysus, for Titinia Urbana [---]
EDCS-09401007.summary: This inscription lists several names, likely members of the Auli Titinii family, possibly on a shared monument or epitaph.

EDCS-09401008.text_edition: \n Trebia Cai filia Saturnina vixit annis XIII mutatum officium est alium speraverat usum fax infelicis virginis heu superi quae thalamis aetas fuerat iam nubilis apta destituit sponsum flebilis et soceros\n
EDCS-09401008.translation: Trebia Saturnina, daughter of Caius, lived 13 years. Her situation changed; she had hoped for another outcome. Alas, the gods! What age for the bridal chamber had been fitting for this destined bride, who has now perished, leaving behind her betrothed and her parents-in-law.
EDCS-09401008.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Trebia Saturnina, a young girl who died before marriage, lamenting her lost future.

EDCS-09401009.text_edition: \n us Dabali filius o de liburna Margarita sibi et Quinto Valerio Apro et Valeriae ae contubernali suae et libertis libertabusque suis posterisque omnibus testamento \n
EDCS-09401009.translation: For Dabalius, son of Dabalis, from the ship Margarita, for himself and for Quintus Valerius Aper, and for his companion Valeria, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and all their descendants by will.
EDCS-09401009.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Dabalius, set up for himself and others, including his companion Valeria and their freedpersons.

EDCS-09401010.text_edition: \n nti liberto et Zosimo liberto et contubernali suae lciae Proculae libertae nuleio Dionysio posterisque eorum\n
EDCS-09401010.translation: To Antius, freedman, and Zosimus, freedman and comrade to his partner Lucia Procula, freedwoman, and to their descendants.
EDCS-09401010.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to freedmen and their partner, likely set up by Antius.

EDCS-09401011.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Iuli Seneciani principalis IIII Venere natione Raetus vixit annos XLV militavit annos XV Caius Iulius Iuvenis filius et heres bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09401011.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Caius Iulius Senecianus, principalis of the fourth cohort, a Raetian by nation, lived 45 years, served 15 years. Caius Iulius Iuvenis, his son and heir, made this for the deserving man.
EDCS-09401011.summary: This is a tombstone for Caius Iulius Senecianus, a soldier who served in the legions, erected by his son.

EDCS-09401017.translation: Pontia Agele: Bassus, at his own expense.
EDCS-09401017.summary: This is a dedication by Bassus for Pontia Agele, likely a tombstone or memorial.

EDCS-09401018.text_edition: \n Lucius Paccius Atimetus et Gerulana Euphrosyne sibi et suis\n
EDCS-09401018.translation: Lucius Paccius Atimetus and Gerulana Euphrosyne, for themselves and their family.
EDCS-09401018.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a space for Lucius Paccius Atimetus, Gerulana Euphrosyne, and their relatives.

EDCS-09401019.summary: This is a tombstone for a child named Sinquilus who died at the age of 14.

EDCS-09401020.text_edition: \n Marco Virtio Marci filio avoncolo sic\n
EDCS-09401020.translation: To Marcus Virtius, son of Marcus, his maternal uncle: Thus (he was placed).
EDCS-09401020.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Marcus Virtius, likely erected by a nephew.

EDCS-09401021.text_edition: \n Marco Virtio Luci filio Menenia patri veterano legionis XIX\n
EDCS-09401021.translation: To Marcus Virtius, son of Lucius, of the Menenia tribe, a veteran of the 19th legion.
EDCS-09401021.summary: This is a tombstone for Marcus Virtius, a veteran soldier of the 19th legion.

EDCS-09401347.text_edition: \n Dedicata IIII Idus Maias Imperatore Commodo II Publio Martio Vero II locus acceptus ex auctoritate Flavi Pisonis praefecti nnonae adsignante Valerio Fusco procuratore Auggustorum\n
EDCS-09401347.translation: Dedicated on the 12th day before the Kalends of June, during the second consulship of the Emperor Commodus and the second consulship of Publius Martius Verus, the site having been accepted by authority of Flavius Pison, prefect of the grain supply, and assigned by Valerius Fuscus, procurator of the Emperors.
EDCS-09401347.summary: This is a dedication stone for a property, noting the date, imperial officials, and administrative personnel involved in its assignment during the reign of Commodus.

EDCS-09401348.text_edition: \n Octavia Marci filia Gamalae porticum poliendam et sedeilia faciunda et culinam tegendam Deae Bonae curavit\n
EDCS-09401348.translation: Octavia Gamala, daughter of Marcus, arranged for the portico to be repaired, seats to be made, and the kitchen to be roofed, for the goddess Bona Dea.
EDCS-09401348.summary: This inscription records Octavia Gamala's commission of repairs and construction for a sanctuary of the goddess Bona Dea.

EDCS-09401352.text_edition: \n Invicto Deo Crescens actor Alfi Seberi sic dono posuit\n
EDCS-09401352.translation: To the unconquered god: Crescens, agent of Albius Seberus, placed this gift.
EDCS-09401352.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to an unconquered god by Crescens, an agent of Albius Seberus.

EDCS-09401355.text_edition: \n Lucius Minutius Luci filius Pica Titus Tutorius Titi filius Paulus IIIIviri praetores quinquennales iterum de senatus sententia faciundum curaverunt\n
EDCS-09401355.translation: Lucius Minutius Pica, son of Lucius, and Titus Paulus, son of Titus, the four men who were praetors, holding office for the fifth year, a second time, had this made by decree of the Senate.
EDCS-09401355.summary: This inscription records that the four leading magistrates (IIIIviri praetores quinquennales) commissioned the construction of a public work by senatorial decree, likely in the 1st century BCE.

EDCS-09401356.text_edition: \n arii Egnatio Maeciano Antistio Lucerino viro clarissimo quaestori q praetori consuli curatori sacrae urbis curatori rei publicae \n
EDCS-09401356.translation: To Egnatius Maecianus Antistius Lucerinus, a most distinguished man, quaestor, propraetor, consul, governor of the sacred city, and governor of the republic.
EDCS-09401356.summary: This inscription honors Egnatius Maecianus Antistius Lucerinus, listing his distinguished career and public offices, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-09401365.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucius Cerrinius Luci filius Niger fieri fecit Decimiae filiae libertabusque in fronte pedes L in agro \n
EDCS-09401365.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Cerrinius Niger, son of Lucius, had this made for his daughter Decimia and her freedpersons, fifty feet in front and on the land.
EDCS-09401365.summary: This is a funerary dedication set up by Lucius Cerrinius Niger for his daughter Decimia and her freedpersons, indicating the dimensions of the plot.

EDCS-09401366.text_edition: \n Servius Ennius Teretina quaestor sibi et hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-09401366.translation: Servius Ennius, quaestor, for himself; this monument will not pass to his heir.
EDCS-09401366.summary: This inscription records that Servius Ennius, a quaestor, built this monument for himself and stipulated that it should not be inherited.

EDCS-09401368.summary: This very short inscription is a dedication, possibly to Juno Populona.

EDCS-09401370.text_edition: \n Lucius Calceius Luci filius augur quinquennalis\n
EDCS-09401370.translation: Lucius Calceius, son of Lucius, augur, holding office for the fifth year.
EDCS-09401370.summary: This inscription identifies Lucius Calceius, son of Lucius, as an augur who held office for the fifth year (quinquennalis), likely a civic or religious role.

EDCS-09401371.text_edition: \n Marco Clodio Auli filio patri Marcus Clodius Marci filius fieri iussit\n
EDCS-09401371.translation: Marcus Clodius, son of Marcus, ordered this to be made for his father Marcus Clodius, son of Aulus.
EDCS-09401371.summary: This inscription records that Marcus Clodius, son of Marcus, commissioned a monument for his father, Marcus Clodius, son of Aulus.

EDCS-09401374.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caesiae Severae filiae dulcissimae fecit\n
EDCS-09401374.translation: To the spirits of the departed. [He/She/I] made [this] for Caesiae Severae, my most sweet daughter.
EDCS-09401374.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by a parent for their beloved daughter, Caesiae Severae.

EDCS-09401376.text_edition: \n Marcia Decimi liberta Erotis sibi et Marciae Decimi libertae Primae filiae hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-09401376.translation: Marcia Erotis, freedwoman of Decimus Marcia, for herself and for Marcia Prima, daughter of the freedwoman Marcia, freedwoman of Decimus; this monument will not pass to her heir.
EDCS-09401376.summary: This inscription records that Marcia Erotis, a freedwoman, established a monument for herself and Marcia Prima, also a freedwoman, stipulating it would not pass to heirs.

EDCS-09401378.text_edition: \n Lucio Paccio Em liberto Protemo monumentum sibi et suos\n
EDCS-09401378.translation: To Lucius Paccio Protemus, freedman of Em, a monument for himself and his own people.
EDCS-09401378.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication of a monument by Lucius Paccio Protemus, a freedman, for himself and his family.

EDCS-09401379.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Obidio Marci filio Teretina Quintino Aedi Asiatici vixit annis sic III menses VI dies X Obidius Celer\n
EDCS-09401379.translation: To the departed spirits. To Marcus Obidius Quintinus, son of Marcus, of the Teretina tribe, freedman of Aedius Asiaticus. He lived for 3 years, 6 months, and 10 days. Obidius Celer [made this].
EDCS-09401379.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Obidius Quintinus, recording his age at death and dedication by Obidius Celer, likely a relative or patron, possibly from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09401380.text_edition: \n Caius Mussius Luci filius de senatus sententia faciundum coeravit eidemque probavit\n
EDCS-09401380.translation: Gaius Mussius, son of Lucius, arranged for this to be made by decree of the senate and approved it for the same person.
EDCS-09401380.summary: This is a dedication or commission for a public work, initiated by Gaius Mussius based on a senatorial decree, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09401381.text_edition: \n lio o Marci filio Rufo aedili auguri quinquennali\n
EDCS-09401381.translation: to... Rufus, son of Marcus, aedile, augur for the quinquennial, ...
EDCS-09401381.summary: This is a fragmented inscription honoring an individual named Rufus, who held several important religious and administrative offices (aedile, augur), likely from the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-09401383.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Mettiae Marci filiae Harmoniae animae simplicissimae quae vixit annis XXII mensibus XI diebus XVII Dmus pater\n
EDCS-09401383.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To Mettia Harmonia, daughter of Marcus, a soul most gentle. She lived 22 years, 11 months, and 17 days. Her father, Dmus, [made this].
EDCS-09401383.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Mettia Harmonia, detailing her age at death and dedicated by her father, Dmus, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09401384.text_edition: \n Lucio Pomponio Luci Bassi consulis et Torquatae filio Horatia Basso Casco Scriboniano consuli auguri fetiali sodali Titiali praefecto urbis feriarum Latinarum\n
EDCS-09401384.translation: To Lucius Pomponius Bassus Cascus Scribonianus, son of Lucius, of the Horatia tribe, consul and augur, fetialis, member of the Titian sodality, prefect of the city for the Latin Games, son of Lucius Pomponius Bassus, consul and Torquata.
EDCS-09401384.summary: This inscription lists the extensive public career and familial connections of Lucius Pomponius Bassus Cascus Scribonianus, a prominent figure likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-09700053.text_edition: \n Publius Murrius PPubliorum libertus Zetus Placentinus mercator purpurarius hic situs est viator consiste et casus hominum cogita annorum natus XXXV arbitror fuissem quom plurimi sic fui et florebam maxume et cecidi longe ab domo et meis amantibus Publius Murrius PPubliorum libertus Eros conlibertus et socius vivus hoc monumentum fecit ossaque transtulit Placentiam\n
EDCS-09700053.translation: Publius Murrius Zetus, freedman of Publius Murrius, a purple-dyer from Placentia, lies here. Traveler, stop and consider the fortunes of men. Born 35 years ago, I suppose I would have been most vigorous and flourishing, but I fell, far from home and my loved ones. Publius Murrius Eros, freedman and partner of Publius Murrius, erected this monument during his lifetime and moved the remains to Placentia.
EDCS-09700053.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Murrius Zetus, a purple-dyer who died at 35, lamenting his fate and detailing the monument's erection by his freedman and partner, Eros, likely from the 1st century CE.

EDCS-09700057.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-09700057.text_edition: \n Successi mirae prosapiae adque sic nimiae integritatis Iulio Sulpicio Successo viro egregio patrono coloniae procuratori portus Puteolanorum ob sic meritis et adfectione amoreque eius erga cives et patriam ut ordo splendidissimus populusque Puteolanus comprobavit regio decatriae cultores dei patri vexillari statuam ponendam sollicite adcuraverunt dedicata VI Idus Augustas Tito Vitrasio Pollione II Marco Flavio Apro II consulibus\n
EDCS-09700057.translation: To Successus of wondrous lineage and excessive integrity, an excellent man, patron of the colony, procurator of the port of Puteoli. Because of his merits and his affection and love for the citizens and his homeland, the most distinguished council and the people of Puteoli approved that a statue be erected. The inhabitants of the tenth district, devoted to the fatherland, and the standard-bearers carefully arranged for its erection. Dedicated on the 6th day before the Ides of August, when Titus Vitrasius Pollio and Marcus Flavius Aper were consuls for the second time.
EDCS-09700057.summary: This is a dedication of a statue to the esteemed official Julius Sulpicius Successus by the city council and people of Puteoli, honoring his service as patron and procurator, dated to 62 CE.

EDCS-09700058.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Pollioni gubernatori ex III Parthico natione Cilix Corycaeus militavit annis XVII Tatias coniugi suo bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09700058.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To Julius Pollio, navigator of the Third Parthica legion, a Cilician from Corycus. He served for 17 years. Tatias erected this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-09700058.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Julius Pollio, a soldier and navigator from Cilicia who served in the Third Parthica legion, erected by his wife Tatias, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09700060.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Sempronia Prisca pia vixit annos XXVI menses VI dies V Haero fecit filiae dulcissimae et sibi et suis posterisque eorum hic sita est\n
EDCS-09700060.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Sempronia Prisca, pious, lived 26 years, 6 months, and 5 days. Haero made this for her dearest daughter, and for herself, and for their descendants.
EDCS-09700060.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sempronia Prisca, who died at age 26, dedicated by her mother Haero, who also included herself and their descendants, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09700061.text_edition: \n Iulia Sophia Isidori Tiberi Caesaris Augusti liberti liberta medici sic vixit annos XXII\n
EDCS-09700061.translation: Iulia Sophia, freedwoman of Isidorus, freedman of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, a physician. She lived 22 years.
EDCS-09700061.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Iulia Sophia, a physician and freedwoman of an imperial freedman, recording her age at death, likely from the 1st century CE.

EDCS-09700534.text_edition: \n Cassiae Corneliae Cai filiae Priscae clarissimae feminae Aufidi Frontonis consulis pontificis proconsulis Asiae patroni coloniae uxori sacerdoti Augustae et patriae Formiani publice pro splendore munificentiae eius Aginatii iunioris\n
EDCS-09700534.translation: To Cassia Cornelia Prisca, daughter of Caius, a most illustrious woman, wife of Aufidius Frontonis, consul, pontiff, proconsul of Asia, patron of the colony, priestess of Augusta and of the fatherland, the people of Formiae [erected this] publicly for the splendor of her munificence, [by the agency of] Aginatius the younger.
EDCS-09700534.summary: A public honorary inscription from Formiae for Cassia Cornelia Prisca, honoring her munificence and public service.

EDCS-09700538.text_edition: \n Decimo Septumuleio Decimi liberto Athenioni magistro Augustali Septumuleiae Decimi libertae Daphne Decimus Septumuleius Decimi libertus Atticus magister Augustalis vivos sibi et patrono et conlibertae fecit\n
EDCS-09700538.translation: Decimus Septumuleius Atticus, freedman of Decimus, a master of the Augustales, made this for himself and for his patron and his freedwoman Daphne, while alive.
EDCS-09700538.summary: A funerary or commemorative dedication made by a freedman, Decimus Septumuleius Atticus, for himself, his patron, and a freedwoman, Daphne.

EDCS-09700539.text_edition: \n Marco Minio Marci et Miniae uxori ex testamento Marci Mini Marci liberti arbitratu\n
EDCS-09700539.translation: To Marcus Minius, son of Marcus, and to Minia [his wife], according to the will of Marcus Minis, son of Marcus, by the arbitration of his freedman.
EDCS-09700539.summary: A dedication referencing a will, likely for the deceased Marcus Minius and his wife, Minia.

EDCS-09700541.translation: To the Invincible Divinity, Plutius Zoillus, [dedicated this].
EDCS-09700541.summary: A dedication to the god Invictus (Unconquered) by Plutius Zoillus.

EDCS-09700544.text_edition: \n Augustae sacrum Matri Deum Marcus Antonius Faustus Augustalis Cumis dendrophoris Baianis aram pecunia sua\n
EDCS-09700544.translation: Sacred to Augusta, the Mother of the Gods; Marcus Antonius Faustus, a master of the Augustales of Cumae, the dendrophori of Baiae, [erected] this altar with his own money.
EDCS-09700544.summary: A dedication to the Mother of the Gods (Cybele) by Marcus Antonius Faustus, a priest of Cumae, with the dendrophori of Baiae.

EDCS-09700546.text_edition: \n Lucio Stenio Silvini liberto Cytiso seviro Augustali et Rubriae Arurae\n
EDCS-09700546.translation: To Lucius Sthenius Cytisus, freedman of Silvinus, a seviri Augustalis, and to Rubria Arura.
EDCS-09700546.summary: A dedication to Lucius Sthenius Cytisus, a freedman and Augustalis, and to Rubria Arura.

EDCS-09700548.text_edition: \n Quinto Pedio Marci filio proconsuli patrono\n
EDCS-09700548.translation: To Quintus Pedius, son of Marcus, proconsul, patron.
EDCS-09700548.summary: An honorary dedication to Quintus Pedius, son of Marcus, who held the office of proconsul and was a patron.

EDCS-09700550.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Casiniae Primitivae dulcissimae coniugi incomparabili\n
EDCS-09700550.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed; to Casinia Primitiva, most beloved and incomparable wife.
EDCS-09700550.summary: A funerary dedication to Casinia Primitiva, the wife of the dedicator.

EDCS-09700555.translation: Gaius Firmidius, son of Gaius, [from the tribe] Teretina.
EDCS-09700555.summary: An incomplete inscription identifying Gaius Firmidius, son of Gaius, and his tribal affiliation.

EDCS-09700559.text_edition: \n iae Marci filiae Mi Marcius Cai Teretina Capito filius fecit Lateranae Marci filiae Rufillae uxori lio Cnaei filio Teretina o fratri\n
EDCS-09700559.translation: [To his] wife, Laterana Rufilla, daughter of Marcus, [and to his] brother, [son of] Gnaeus, [erected by] Marcius Capito, son of Gaius, [from the tribe] Teretina.
EDCS-09700559.summary: A dedication by Marcius Capito to his wife Laterana Rufilla and his brother.

EDCS-09700560.text_edition: \n Petronia |(mulieris) liberta Salvia in agro pedes XII\n
EDCS-09700560.translation: Salvia, freedwoman of Petronia, [made this] in the fields for twelve feet.
EDCS-09700560.summary: This inscription seems to record a boundary marker or a land division, possibly related to a freedwoman named Salvia.

EDCS-09700563.text_edition: \n Seppius |(mulieris) libertus Daus vico Longi de sua pecunia libens populo sacravit\n
EDCS-09700563.translation: Seppius Daus, freedman of the woman, dedicated this willingly to the people from his own funds in the Longus street.
EDCS-09700563.summary: This inscription records a dedication of public works by Seppius Daus, a freedman, indicating a benefactor's contribution to the community.

EDCS-09700565.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Publio Tampio Verecundo Vettia Zmyrna et Tampilus Verecundinus patri bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-09700565.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Vettia Zmyrna and Tampilus Verecundinus made this for their well-deserving father Publius Tampius Verecundus.
EDCS-09700565.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Publius Tampius Verecundus, erected by his wife or daughter (Vettia Zmyrna) and son (Tampilus Verecundinus).

EDCS-09700568.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Imperatori Marco Aurelio Valerio Maxentio Pio Felici Invicto Augusto XV\n
EDCS-09700568.translation: To our lord, the fifteenth [consulship], Emperor Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maxentius, the Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Augustus.
EDCS-09700568.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the emperor Maxentius, likely commemorating his fifteenth consulship, dating it to his reign (306-312 AD).

EDCS-09700872.text_edition: \n Egriliae Marci filiae Plariae filiae Marci Acili Prisci Egrili Plariani patroni coloniae et iuvenum publice posuerunt\n
EDCS-09700872.translation: Egrilia Plaria, daughter of Marcus, daughter of the daughter of Marcus Acilius Priscus, patron of the colony and of the youth, and Egrilius Plarianus, patron of the colony and of the youth, had this publicly set up.
EDCS-09700872.summary: This inscription records a public monument erected in honor of Egrilia Plaria, daughter of Marcus, by Egrilius Plarianus, patron of the colony, likely in the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-09700873.text_edition: \n Caius Calvisius Cai filius Sabinus consul imperator LXXIX\n
EDCS-09700873.translation: Gaius Calvisius Sabinus, son of Gaius, consul, commander, seventy-ninth [consulship].
EDCS-09700873.summary: This inscription records the consular authority of Gaius Calvisius Sabinus, likely dating to the late Republic or early Empire, possibly referring to his seventy-ninth year of office or a specific honor.

EDCS-09700876.text_edition: \n Marcus Clodius Marci libertus Anoptes testamento poni statuique iussit\n
EDCS-09700876.translation: Marcus Clodius Anoptes, freedman of Marcus, ordered this to be set up and established by his will.
EDCS-09700876.summary: This inscription records a posthumous commission by Marcus Clodius Anoptes, a freedman, for the erection of a monument or structure.

EDCS-09700878.text_edition: \n Varia Pansina Luci Corelli Celeris Fisi Rufini patroni coloniae Luci Varii Ambibuli procuratoris Augusti filia Varii Ambibuli proconsulis legati Augusti patroni coloniae soror porticum cum statuis et viridibus numini Veneris Ioviae et coloniae solo et pecunia suo sic fecit cuius operis dedicatione decurionibus HS XXX Augustalibus HS XX ministris HS XII populo et mulieribus HS III dedit\n
EDCS-09700878.translation: Daughter of Lucius Varius Ambibulus, procurator of the Emperor, sister of Varius Ambibulus, proconsul, legate of the Emperor, patron of the colony, Varia Pansina, daughter of Lucius Corellius Celer, Fisi Rufinus, patron of the colony, patron of the Emperor, patron of the colony, with her own resources and funds, made the portico with statues and gardens for the divine power of Venus Iovia and of the colony, [and she] gave to the decurions for the dedication of this work 30,000 sesterces, to the Augustales 20,000 sesterces, to the ministers 12,000 sesterces, and to the people and women 3,000 sesterces.
EDCS-09700878.summary: This extensive inscription details the construction of a portico with statues and gardens dedicated to Venus Iovia and the colony by Varia Pansina, a woman of high social standing, and outlines her generous donations for its inauguration, likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09700879.text_edition: \n Propagatori orbis sui Romani nominis conditori Flavio Valerio Constantino Pio Felici Victori Augusto Lucius Aelius Proculus vir perfectissimus corrector Campaniae devotus numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-09700879.translation: To Flavius Valerius Constantinus, the Pious, Fortunate, Victorious Augustus, propagator of his empire, founder of the name of Rome, Lucius Aelius Proculus, most perfect man, corrector of Campania, devoted to his divine power and majesty.
