EDCS-09700879.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Lucius Aelius Proculus, an official of high rank, to Emperor Constantine the Great, praising his achievements and devotion to the imperial cult, likely from the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-09700881.text_edition: \n Dardanius carui avaritia metu sollicitudine hominum Lucio Valerio Valeriano viro perfectissimo praefecto Mesopotamiae et Hosroenae\n
EDCS-09700881.translation: Dardanius, free from avarice, fear, and anxiety for men, [dedicated this] to Lucius Valerius Valerianus, most perfect man, prefect of Mesopotamia and Hosroena.
EDCS-09700881.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Dardanius to Lucius Valerius Valerianus, a prefect of Roman provinces, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09700882.text_edition: \n Sacratissimo die natali divi Augusti prosperis felicibusque auspiciis dedicata est per pontifices et augures pecunia Quinti Murrasi Glyconis Luci Dentri Communis VIIII Kalendas Octobres Lucio sic Iunio Silano Caio Vellaeo Tutore consulibus\n
EDCS-09700882.translation: On the most sacred birthday of the divine Augustus, with prosperous and fortunate omens, it was dedicated by the pontiffs and augurs with money from Quintus Murrasius Glyco, from the public treasury, on the 23rd of September, when Lucius Junius Silanus and Gaius Vellaeus Tutor were consuls.
EDCS-09700882.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument or building on the birthday of Augustus, funded by Quintus Murrasius Glyco, and consecrated by pontiffs and augurs during the consulship of Silanus and Tutor.

EDCS-09700887.text_edition: \n Aureliani Iulio Aureliano viro clarissimo XVviro sacris faciundis pontifici dei Herculis pontifici dei Solis consulari Bithyniae consulari Campaniae\n
EDCS-09700887.translation: To Aurelianus, to Julius Aurelianus, a most distinguished man, member of the college of fifteen priests for performing sacred rites, pontiff of the god Hercules, pontiff of the god Sol, consular governor of Bithynia, consular governor of Campania.
EDCS-09700887.summary: This inscription is a dedication or honorific text for Julius Aurelianus, listing his numerous high priestly and administrative offices, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09700888.text_edition: \n Marcus Drusius Caius Iulius triclas et semina de su[o ------\n
EDCS-09700888.translation: Marcus Drusius Gaius Iulius [dedicated] a triclinium and seeds from his own [property].
EDCS-09700888.summary: This fragmentary inscription records Marcus Drusius Gaius Iulius dedicating a triclinium and seeds, likely as a private benefaction.

EDCS-09700889.translation: To Livia Pulchra, daughter of Caius.
EDCS-09700889.summary: This is a simple dedication to Livia Pulchra, daughter of Caius, possibly for a tomb or a statue.

EDCS-09700890.text_edition: \n Dominis nostris Constantino Augusto III et Maximino Augusto III consulibus XI Kalendas Februarias Cornelius Gelasius sacerdos XVviralis Matris Magnae Idaeae Frygiae sic taurobolium movit feliciter\n
EDCS-09700890.translation: On the 19th of January, during the third consulship of our lords Constantine Augustus and Maximius Augustus, Cornelius Gelasius, priest of the fifteen, of the Phrygian Mother Goddess of the Mountain, successfully performed the taurobolium.
EDCS-09700890.summary: This inscription records the performance of a taurobolium ritual by Cornelius Gelasius, a priest of the Great Mother, during the triple consulship of Constantine and Maximius.

EDCS-09700891.text_edition: \n Quintus Oppius Frontinus sacerdos XVviralis I\n
EDCS-09700891.translation: Quintus Oppius Frontinus, priest of the fifteen.
EDCS-09700891.summary: This inscription identifies Quintus Oppius Frontinus as a priest of the college of fifteen, possibly a funerary or honorific dedication.

EDCS-09700892.text_edition: \n Publius Umidius Publi libertus Strato fide qum sic magna vixit bonus atque benignus copo sic rem quaesivit ea re usus est qum sic hospitibus atque et amicis tamen et qum sic mors obiet sic rem quesitam sic reliquit ille homo fecit quam ob rem hospites et amici benedicere possint\n
EDCS-09700892.translation: Publius Umidius Strato, freedman of Publius, lived honorably, was good and kind, acquired wealth through his trade, and used it for guests and friends; however, when death came, he left his acquired wealth behind. This man did this so that guests and friends could speak well of him.
EDCS-09700892.summary: This epitaph praises the deceased freedman Publius Umidius Strato for his honorable life, kindness, and the wealth he acquired and left for his guests and friends, intended to elicit positive remembrance.

EDCS-09700893.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerio Marci filio Gemello vixit annis VIIII Valeriae Athenaidi matri vixit nnis XXX diebus XXIX Valerius Menestratus fecit libertis libertabus posterisque eorum in fronte pedes VIIII in agro comprensa maceria pedes XXVIIII\n
EDCS-09700893.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Valerius Gemellus, son of Marcius, lived 9 years. Valeria Athenaïs, mother, lived 30 years and 29 days. Valerius Menestratus made this for freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants. In front, 9 feet; in the enclosed field, 29 feet.
EDCS-09700893.summary: This funerary inscription records the deaths of Valerius Gemellus and his mother Valeria Athenaïs, and was erected by Valerius Menestratus for his freedpeople and their descendants, specifying the dimensions of the plot.

EDCS-09701368.text_edition: \n Marcus Maecilius Marci filius urr aedem Bonae Deae ex sua idemque pro\n
EDCS-09701368.translation: Marcus Maecilius, son of Marcius, [dedicated] the temple of the Good Goddess from his own [funds] and also for [his own...]
EDCS-09701368.summary: This fragmentary inscription records Marcus Maecilius, son of Marcius, dedicating a temple to the Good Goddess, likely using his own funds.

EDCS-09701370.text_edition: \n Caius Cartilius Cai filius Palatina Sabinus patronus coloniae omnibus honoribus functus sua pecunia fecit \n
EDCS-09701370.translation: Caius Cartilius Sabinus, son of Caius, patron of the colony, having discharged all the honors, made this at his own expense.
EDCS-09701370.summary: This inscription records Caius Cartilius Sabinus, a patron of the colony who had held all offices, dedicating a work at his own expense.

EDCS-09701372.text_edition: \n Imperatore Antonino III et Comazonte II consulibus VIII Idus Augustas\n
EDCS-09701372.translation: During the third consulship of Emperor Antoninus and the second of Comazontus, on the 6th day before the Ides of August.
EDCS-09701372.summary: This inscription records a date specified by consuls and a date in the Roman calendar.

EDCS-09701374.text_edition: \n coniux et heres et Aelius Firminus filius bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-09701374.translation: His wife and heir and his son Aelius Firminus made this for one who deserved well.
EDCS-09701374.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by a wife, son, and heir for a deceased individual.

EDCS-09701375.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Marcus qui vixit nnos VIII Iulia Valeria filio memoria fecit IIIX Kalenda(s) Iunias\n
EDCS-09701375.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aelius Marcus, who lived for 8 years, his mother Iulia Valeria made this memorial for her son, on the 19th day before the Kalends of June.
EDCS-09701375.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a child named Aelius Marcus, set up by his mother, with the date of dedication.

EDCS-09701376.text_edition: \n qui vixit nnos Antonii PrM et Romanus parentes bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-09701376.translation: His parents, Antonii PrM and Romanus, made this for one who deserved well.
EDCS-09701376.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by parents for a deceased individual.

EDCS-09701378.text_edition: \n Aurelio vixit annis XXVII militavit annis VIII coniugi bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09701378.translation: For Aurelius, who lived 27 years and served 8 years, his wife made this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-09701378.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Aurelius, detailing his age at death and military service, set up by his wife.

EDCS-09701379.text_edition: \n Aurelio Alexandro militi legionis II Parthicae qui vixit nnos XL militavit annos lia Afrodisia uxor et Aurelius Masus filius heredes bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-09701379.translation: For Aurelius Alexander, a soldier of the II Parthica legion, who lived 40 years and served for lia years. His wife Afrodisia and son Aurelius Masus, his heirs, made this for one who deserved well.
EDCS-09701379.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a soldier of the II Parthica legion, recording his age and service, set up by his wife and son.

EDCS-09701380.text_edition: \n nimus miles legionis IIue Severianae qui militavit nnos XXV vixit nnos XLA CENVN et com dies est ANSEAD LEG MONCO Aureli Grani fecerunt\n
EDCS-09701380.translation: To the spirits of the departed. [Nim]us, a soldier of the II Severianae legion, who served for 25 years and lived 40 years. CENVN and com [---] ANSEAD LEG MONCO, Aureli Grani, made this.
EDCS-09701380.summary: This inscription is a funerary epitaph for a soldier of the II Severianae legion, providing his service and lifespan, with dedicants.

EDCS-09701381.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Martinianus vixit nnos XVII dies XI Aurelio filio qui SET VM\n
EDCS-09701381.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Martinianus, who lived 17 years and 11 days. For Aurelius his son, who [---] VM.
EDCS-09701381.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Aurelius Martinianus and his son Aurelius, with ages and lifespans.

EDCS-09701382.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Muciano qui vixit nnos XXX militavit X Aurelius Quintanus et Aurelius Heraclida heredes bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-09701382.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aurelius Mucianus, who lived 30 years and served 10 years. Aurelius Quintanus and Aurelius Heraclida, his heirs, made this for one who deserved well.
EDCS-09701382.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan and military service of Aurelius Mucianus, erected by his heirs.

EDCS-09701383.text_edition: \n Licinio Valentino militi legionis II Parthicae immuni venatori |(centuriae) II principis prioris qui vixit nnos XXX militavit annos VIII Aurelia Calanitana uxor et heres coniugi dulcissimo\n
EDCS-09701383.translation: For Licinius Valens, soldier of the II Parthica legion, immune, hunter, of the 2nd century, first principal. Who lived 30 years and served 8 years. Aurelia Calanitana, his wife, and his heir, to their dearest husband.
EDCS-09701383.summary: This funerary epitaph is for Licinius Valens, a soldier and hunter in the II Parthica legion, detailing his age and service, set up by his wife and heir.

EDCS-09701384.translation: Cesius Secundus made this for Faesonius.
EDCS-09701384.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Cesius Secundus for Faesonius.

EDCS-09701385.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cuspi Paramoni qui vixit nnos XC Cuspius Pius Profuturus patri bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09701385.translation: To the Divine Shades. Cuspius Pius Profuturus made this for his well-deserving father, Cuspi Paramon, who lived 90 years.
EDCS-09701385.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Cuspius Pius Profuturus for his father, Cuspi Paramon, who lived 90 years.

EDCS-09701386.text_edition: \n Iulio Bitt Aelia Gaia mater filio fecit bene merenti\n
EDCS-09701386.translation: Aelia Gaia, mother, made this for her well-deserving son, Iulio Bitt.
EDCS-09701386.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Aelia Gaia for her son, Iulio Bitt.

EDCS-09701387.text_edition: \n Iuliae Tertullinae vixit annos XLVII menses V dies I Aurelia Publia amica feminae incomparabili bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09701387.translation: Aurelia Publia made this for her incomparable female friend, Iulia Tertullina, who lived 47 years, 5 months, and 1 day, for her well-deserving companion.
EDCS-09701387.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Aurelia Publia for her friend Iulia Tertullina, who lived 47 years, 5 months, and 1 day.

EDCS-09701389.text_edition: \n vixit nnos LXXX rius Maximinus filius et heres T Africanus\n
EDCS-09701389.translation: His son and heir, T. Africanus, [made this] for rius Maximinus, who lived 80 years.
EDCS-09701389.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by the son and heir, T. Africanus, for rius Maximinus, who lived 80 years.

EDCS-09701391.text_edition: \n N qui vixit nnos L militavit nnos XXII Valerius Martinianus et Valeria Flavia filii et heredes et Ulpia coniux et Valerius Sabinianus secundus heres bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-09701391.translation: Valerius Martinianus and Valeria Flavia, sons and heirs, and Ulpia, wife, and Valerius Sabinianus Secundus, second heir, made this for [N.], who lived 50 years and served as a soldier for 22 years, for their well-deserving [relative].
EDCS-09701391.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by relatives and heirs for N., who lived 50 years and served as a soldier for 22 years.

EDCS-09701395.text_edition: \n PAR Pannonia O hi N S E VIMVios patrono PIG piissimo fecit\n
EDCS-09701395.translation: Pannonia Ohi Nse Vimvios made this for their most pious patron PIG.
EDCS-09701395.summary: This is a dedication of a monument by Pannonia Ohi Nse Vimvios to their patron PIG.

EDCS-09701396.text_edition: \n Lucio Caesonio Luci filio Ovinio Malio Basso clarissimo viro curatori albei sic Tiberis et Benebentanorum sic pontifici maiori consuli praetori quaestori kandidato triumviro prones tracto sic Piceno curatori Laviniensium legato Carthaginensium sic curatori eiusdem dioecesis Carthaginensium domino patrono praestantissimo actys libertus actor cum suis\n
EDCS-09701396.translation: Actys, freedman and agent, with his people, made this for Lucius Caesonianus Lucius f. Ovinus Malis Bassus, a most distinguished man, curator of the Tiber and Beneventan aqueducts, pontiff, chief priest, consul, praetor, quaestor designate, triumvir for the allocation of land, curator of Picenum, curator of the people of Lavinium, legate of the Carthaginians, curator of the same diocese of Carthage, their lord and most outstanding patron.
EDCS-09701396.summary: This is a dedication by the freedman and agent Actys for the distinguished official Lucius Caesonianus Lucius f. Ovinus Malis Bassus, listing his numerous important public offices.

EDCS-09701397.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Priscae coniugi bene merenti Zosimus maritus vilicus fecit\n
EDCS-09701397.translation: To the Divine Shades. Zosimus, husband and steward, made this for his well-deserving wife, Iulia Prisca.
EDCS-09701397.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Zosimus for his wife Iulia Prisca.

EDCS-09701398.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberio Claudio Stasimo vixit annos III menses III dies XVII Tiberio Claudio Euangeliano vixit menses X dies X Tiberio Claudio Aureliano vixit annos III dies X Terentia Auxesis senior mater fecit et sibi posterisque suis\n
EDCS-09701398.translation: To the Divine Shades. Terentia Auxesis the elder, mother, made this for herself and her descendants, for Tiberius Claudius Stasimus, who lived 3 years, 3 months, and 17 days, for Tiberius Claudius Euangelianus, who lived 10 months and 10 days, and for Tiberius Claudius Aurelianus, who lived 3 years and 10 days.
EDCS-09701398.summary: This is a family funerary dedication by Terentia Auxesis for her three young children, and also for herself and her descendants.

EDCS-09701402.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Domnae quae vixit nnos XVI menses IIII Aurelius Bitus miles legionis II Parthicae coniugi bene merenti de suo memoriam fecit\n
EDCS-09701402.translation: To Aurelia Donna, who lived 16 years and 4 months. Aurelius Bitus, soldier of the Second Parthian Legion, made this memorial at his own expense for his deserving wife.
EDCS-09701402.summary: A tombstone erected by a soldier for his wife, dating to the period when the Second Parthian Legion was active in the region.

EDCS-09701721.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Verrio Threpto homini incomparabili qui vixit annis LXIII mensibus V diebus XVI Claudia Afrodisia marito bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09701721.translation: To Marcus Verrius Threptus, an incomparable man, who lived 63 years, 5 months, and 16 days. Claudia Afrodisia made this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-09701721.summary: A tombstone commemorating Marcus Verrius Threptus, erected by his wife Claudia Afrodisia, likely dating to the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-09800035.text_edition: \n Quinto Caecilio Quinti filio Aniensi tribuno militum XXVIvirum Quinto Caecilio Quinti filio Aniensi praefecto fabrum\n
EDCS-09800035.translation: To Quintus Caecilius, son of Quintus, of the Aniensis tribe, military tribune, member of the vigintiviri, to Quintus Caecilius, son of Quintus, of the Aniensis tribe, prefect of engineers.
EDCS-09800035.summary: This inscription records the cursus honorum (sequence of offices) of a father and son, both named Quintus Caecilius, highlighting their military and administrative careers.

EDCS-09800036.text_edition: \n Corneliae Servi filiae Cethegillae Cethegilla et Orfitus et Piso nepotes\n
EDCS-09800036.translation: For Cornelia Cethegilla, daughter of Servius. Cethegilla, Orfitus, and Piso, grandsons.
EDCS-09800036.summary: A dedication by grandsons (Cethegilla, Orfitus, and Piso) to their grandmother Cornelia Cethegilla.

EDCS-09800037.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aebutiae Philete fecerunt Doryphorus et Philete parentes et suis posterisque omnibus vixit nnis VI diebus XXVII\n
EDCS-09800037.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Doryphorus and Philete, parents, made this for Aebutia Philete and for themselves and all their descendants. She lived 6 years and 27 days.
EDCS-09800037.summary: A family tomb inscription dedicated by parents to their young daughter and for future descendants, indicating the child's death at a very young age.

EDCS-09800038.translation: For Quintus Annius Pallans, a contribution.
EDCS-09800038.summary: This brief inscription records a contribution made for Quintus Annius Pallans.

EDCS-09800039.text_edition: \n Caecilia Caio Iulio Cai filio viro suo VIvir(o) Augustali\n
EDCS-09800039.translation: To Caecilia, for Gaius Julius, son of Gaius, her husband, a member of the vigintiviri Augustales.
EDCS-09800039.summary: A dedication by Caecilia to her husband, Gaius Julius, who held the position of VIvir Augustalis.

EDCS-09800040.text_edition: \n Marco Claudio Olympiano Caius Claudius Olympianus ex testamento arbitratu\n
EDCS-09800040.translation: For Marcus Claudius Olympianus, by Gaius Claudius Olympianus, in accordance with the will, by the arbitrator.
EDCS-09800040.summary: This inscription records a posthumous dedication or endowment for Marcus Claudius Olympianus, executed by Gaius Claudius Olympianus as per a will.

EDCS-09800041.text_edition: \n Claudiae Iustinae Heracles August amantissimae bene merenti quae mecum vixit annos XXXIIII menses III\n
EDCS-09800041.translation: To Claudia Iustina, beloved by Heracles Augustus, for her deserving service. She lived with me for 34 years and 3 months.
EDCS-09800041.summary: A tombstone for Claudia Iustina, erected by her admirer Heracles Augustus, who notes their shared life of over 34 years.

EDCS-09800042.text_edition: \n Honoris et sanctitatis causa Claudiae Ide uxori optimae Publius Sextilius Primigenius\n
EDCS-09800042.translation: In honor and reverence, for Claudia Idea, his excellent wife. Publius Sextilius Primigenius.
EDCS-09800042.summary: A monument dedicated by Publius Sextilius Primigenius to his wife, Claudia Idea, emphasizing her virtues.

EDCS-09800043.text_edition: \n Aulus Iunius Auli et |(mulieris) libertus Felicissimus Iuniae Auli libertae Moscharioni in fronte pedes XX in agro pedes XX\n
EDCS-09800043.translation: Aulus Iunius Felicissimus, freedman of Aulus and (a) woman, to Moscharion, freedwoman of Aulus, for the front 20 feet and for the land 20 feet.
EDCS-09800043.summary: This is a boundary inscription from the Roman period, defining the extent of a plot of land.

EDCS-09800045.text_edition: \n io Luci liberto Nan us Luci libertus Diogenes patrono et sibi et Luci liberta Arguta\n
EDCS-09800045.translation: Diogenes, freedman of Lucius, to his patron and to himself and to Arguta, freedwoman of Lucius.
EDCS-09800045.summary: This inscription records a dedication by a freedman to his patron and other family members.

EDCS-09800046.text_edition: \n Cnaeo Pompeio Paulo Caius Memmius Regulus amico bene merenti\n
EDCS-09800046.translation: To Gnaeus Pompeius Paulus, from Caius Memmius Regulus, for a well-deserving friend.
EDCS-09800046.summary: This is a dedication from one friend to another, likely a tombstone or honorific marker.

EDCS-09800047.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sexto Pompeio Martiali Caesenia Felicula coniugi karissimo cum quo vixit annis XXVII item Pompeius Martialis filius patri indulgentissimo\n
EDCS-09800047.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Sextus Pompeius Martialis, my dearest husband, with whom I lived 27 years; also to Pompeius Martialis, my most indulgent son, [from] his father.
EDCS-09800047.summary: This funerary epitaph commemorates a husband and son, with the wife and father as the dedicants.

EDCS-09800049.text_edition: \n marito nnos XXV pridie Kalendas Septembres Ausus maritus vi inconparavili sic \n
EDCS-09800049.translation: husband, 25 years, the day before the Kalends of September. Ausus, my husband, [left me] because of incomparable love.
EDCS-09800049.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription, likely detailing the duration of a marriage and expressing grief.

EDCS-09800051.summary: This is a very short inscription, likely an ownership mark or a dedication to Publius Lurius Hedylus.

EDCS-09800053.text_edition: \n Titus Flavius Epigonus honoratus collegio hastoforum sic Ostiensium signum Matris Deum dedicatum VIII Idus Aelio Antonino Augusto Aurelio Caesare consulibus\n
EDCS-09800053.translation: Titus Flavius Epigonus, honored, to the college of the hastofori of Ostia, dedicated the statue of the Mother of the Gods, in the consulship of Aelius Antoninus Augustus and Aurelius Caesar, on the 6th day before the Ides of August.
EDCS-09800053.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue of the Mother of the Gods by Titus Flavius Epigonus to a guild, during the consulship of Antoninus Pius and Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-09800060.translation: I ask you to make Felix IIvir.
EDCS-09800060.summary: This is a plea or request, likely from an election context, asking for Felix to be appointed as a IIvir (a type of magistrate).

EDCS-09800063.text_edition: \n Auli Oppei Auli liberti Glauciae purpurarei ossa heic sita sunt\n
EDCS-09800063.translation: The bones of Glaucia, freedman of Aulus Oppius, a purple-dyer, are placed here.
EDCS-09800063.summary: This is a funerary inscription stating the burial place of Glaucia, a freedman and dyer.

EDCS-10201131.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Gaviae Donatae filiae dulcissimae quae vixit nnis mensibus diebus XI Gavius Quintilianus et Elbia Donata parentes\n
EDCS-10201131.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. To Gavia Donata, my sweetest daughter, who lived 11 years, months, and days. From Gavia Quintilianus and Elbia Donata, parents.
EDCS-10201131.summary: This funerary epitaph commemorates a daughter, Gavia Donata, and was set up by her parents.

EDCS-10400044.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolich... Aurelius Theotec... will assign. Claudius Herennianus.
EDCS-10400044.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Claudius Herennianus, with Aurelius Theotecnus making an assignment, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-10400052.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, grandson of the deified Julius, with tribunician power for the 38th time.
EDCS-10400052.summary: This inscription records Tiberius Caesar Augustus holding tribunician power for the 38th time, dating to 18 CE.

EDCS-10400053.translation: Drusus Caesar, son of Tiberius Augustus, grandson of the deified Augustus, great-grandson of the deified Julius, with tribunician power for the 2nd time.
EDCS-10400053.summary: This inscription records Drusus Caesar holding tribunician power for the 2nd time, likely dating to the early 1st century CE.

EDCS-10400056.text_edition: \n Pater Silvane Silvane ruris incola et Nemus silens Hechoque loquax Dryades et Silvae meae Antonius me Balbus hac natus casa equestri genere nunc proconsul Africae posuit prais hoc via pes\n
EDCS-10400056.translation: Father Silvanus, Silvanus, inhabitant of the woods, and Silent Nemus, and talkative Dryads, and my Woods, Antonius Balbus, born in this house, of equestrian rank, now proconsul of Africa, erected this beside this road.
EDCS-10400056.summary: This dedication by Antonius Balbus, an equestrian and former proconsul of Africa, honors various sylvan deities, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10400057.text_edition: \n Lucio Valerio Luci filio Publicolae Messalae H<e>lbi<di>o sic Thraseae Prisco Minicio Natali consuli VIIviro epulonum salio collino IIIviro aere auro argento flando feriundo tribuno mili<t>um legionis secundae adiutricis quaestori Auggustorum atlecto sic inter praetorios seviro equitum Romanorum legatorum a<d> census provinciae Belgicae et curatori aquarum item et at sic fraudis Miniciae patrono et curatori rei publicae Laurentium Lavinatium munic(ipes) statuam publice ponendam statuerunt\n
EDCS-10400057.translation: The municipes of Laurentum Lavinium, by public decree, decided that a statue be erected to Lucius Valerius Publicola Messala Helbidius, consul for the 7th time, member of the college of epulones, Salian priest of the Colline tribe, IIIvir monetalis, military tribune of the II Adjutrix legion, quaestor of the Augusti, selected among the praetorian prefects, prefect of the Roman equestrians, legate for the census of the province of Belgica, curator of the waters, also patron and curator of the Republic of Laurentium Lavinium, and also of the Minicia fraud, and of Thrasea Priscus Minicius Natalis.
EDCS-10400057.summary: This decree by the citizens of Laurentum Lavinium records the public decision to erect a statue to Lucius Valerius Publicola Messala Helbidius, a distinguished senator and administrator, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-10400059.text_edition: \n Caio Servilio Quirina Diodoro viro egregio procuratori CCducenario provinciarum Hispaniarum citerioris et superioris item procuratori Ccentenario Moesiae inferioris et regni Norici item procuratori LXsexagenario rationis privatae praefecto alae I Tungrorum Frontonianae tribuno legionis XIIII geminae praefecto cohortis II Aureliae novae |(miliariae) equitatae [] Laurenti Lavinati domo Girba ex Africa coniugi incomparabili Egnatia Salviana eius Caius Servilius Diodorus Pontio Fusco Pontiano suo salutem Egnatia Salviana mater familias meae pro sua pietate erga me statuam mihi in civitate Laurentum Lavinatium ubi sacerdotalis summsuit eius obsequia amplianda crededi sic domine ut collegio dendrophorum quod est in eadem civitate dem SS XXviginti milia nummum ut ea quantitas conlocetur et eius summae usurae quicunces id est SS |(mille) eidem collegio quodannis praestentur ut die pridie Iduum Novembrium natalis mei sportulas accipere et epulari publice eodem loco cum libertis meis possint quam rem credo et ad splendorem ipsius loci pertinere peto igitur domine pro tua insigni aequitate suscipi eam a Magio Iusto et Vibusio Severino praetoribus eiusdem civitatis iubeas eaque observari quae his litteris meis comprehendi ut beneficio humanitatis tuae quodannis celebrari dies natalis mei simul cum libertis meis possit datum VI Nonas Octobres Nummio Albino et Laelio Maximo consulibus Pontius Fuscus Pontianus Magio Iusto et Vibusio Severino suis salutem legite in subiectis exemplum litterarum quas ad me fecit Se<r>vilius Diodorus vir egregius sacerdotalis splendidissimae civitatis vestrae ut secundum haec quae litteris suis complexus est voluntatis eius satis fiat Magius Iustus et Vibusius Severinus Pontio Fusco Pontiano suo salutem secundum praeceptum litterarum tuarum domine et voluntatem Servili Diodori viri egregii suscepta esse IIII Nonas Octobres Albino et Maximo consulibus ab eodem Diodoro viro egregio SS XXviginti milia nummum quam summam suscepit Asclepiades rei publicae Laurentium Lavinatium servus arkarius ut scires notum amplitudini tuae facimus Dedicatum VII Idus Septembres Nummio Albino et Laelio Maximo consulibus Quinto Aiacio Modesto (iterum) et Marco Maecio Probo consulibus XV Kalendas Septembres in Caesareum quod est in foro cum ordo collegi dendrophororum Laurentium Lavinatium convenisset ibi Cornelius Trophimus et Varenius Legitimus quinquennales verba fecerunt ante omnia gratias agendas Servilio Diodoro viro egregio quod dignatus sit aput sic rem publicam Laurentium Lavinatium splendidissimae civitatis nostrae collegio nostro SS XXviginti milia nummum nomine nostro collocare cuius summae usuram quod idest SS |(milia) nummum a re publica Laurentium Lavinatium quodannis praestari ut die pridie Idus Novembres natalis eius quodannis sportulas habeamus secundum ea quae in tabula aenea quam schola in quam convenimus perscribtum sic posuit idque ordini nostro maxime placere tam bono viro bene merenti gratias agere cum sciamus quantam laetitiam in perpetuum ab eo perceperimus placet itaque universis patronum eum Egnatiam Salvianam eius matrem cooptemus et petamus ab eo ut tabulam aeneam patronati suscipere et libens rescribere dignetur et ut commendatos habeat singulos universos qui nos et in clientela sua recipere dignatur et quo notius sit ista voluptas hoc decretum nostrum in tabula aenea perscribtum sic ei offeratur per Iulium Sabinum Sextium Fidelem seniorem Ulpium Crescentem Servilius Diodorus collegio dendrophororum oblatam mihi tabulam aeream nomine vestro per Iulium Sabinum Sextium Fidelem seniorem Ulpium Crescentem collegis sic vestris sic libens accepi et gratos vos muneris quod vobis obtuli et observaturos ea quae desideravi intellexi maxime cum ea res exemplo sit ceteris consacerdotalibus meis optantibus a vobis honorem patronatus\n
EDCS-10400059.translation: To Gaius Servilius Diodorus, an excellent man, procurator of the Spanish provinces, procurator of Lower Moesia and the Norican kingdom, procurator of the private treasury, prefect of the I Fronto Alae of Tungrians, tribune of the XIIII Gemina legion, prefect of the II Aurelia Nova equitata (miliaria) cohort, from Girba in Africa, to my incomparable husband, Egnatia Salviana, his wife, sends greetings. Gaius Servilius Diodorus Pontius Fuscus Pontianus to his [father?] greeting. Egnatia Salviana, the materfamilias of my family, out of her piety towards me, established a statue for me in the splendid city of Laurentum Lavinatium, where I am a priest. I believed her requests should be amplified, Lord, and so that the collegium of dendrophori in the same city receives 20,000 sesterces annually, to be paid as interest from this sum, which is 1,000 sesterces, to the same collegium every year, so that on the day before the Ides of November, my birthday, I may receive sportulae and feast publicly in the same place with my freedmen. I believe this matter also pertains to the splendor of this place. Therefore, Lord, I ask you, because of your outstanding equity, to undertake this through Magius Justus and Vibius Severinus, praetors of the same city, and to ensure that my letters are observed, so that by your benefaction, my birthday may be celebrated every year together with my freedmen. Given on the 6th day before the Nones of October, during the consulship of Nummio Albinus and Laelius Maximus. Pontius Fuscus Pontianus to his Magius Justus and Vibius Severinus, greeting. We have read, Lord, the exemplar of the letters which Servilius Diodorus, an excellent man and priest of your most splendid city, sent to me, so that in accordance with these things which he set forth in his letters, his wishes may be fulfilled. Magius Justus and Vibius Severinus to their Pontius Fuscus Pontianus, greeting. In accordance with your command, Lord, and the will of Servilius Diodorus, an excellent man, on the 4th day before the Nones of October, during the consulship of Albinus and Maximus, we received 20,000 sesterces from the same Diodorus, an excellent man. This sum was received by Asclepiades, a slave of the Republic of Laurentium Lavinatium, the treasurer. So that you may know, we make known to your magnificence. Dedicated on the 7th day before the Ides of September, during the consulship of Nummio Albinus and Laelio Maximo. On the 15th day before the Kalends of September, in the Caesareum, which is in the forum. When the order of the collegium of dendrophori of Laurentium Lavinatium had convened, Cornelius Trophimus and Varenius Legitimus, quinquennales, spoke. First of all, thanks are to be given to Servilius Diodorus, an excellent man, because he deigned to place 20,000 sesterces in the name of our collegium in the Republic of Laurentium Lavinatium, our most splendid city. The annual interest of this sum, which is 1,000 sesterces, is to be provided by the Republic of Laurentium Lavinatium, so that on the day before the Ides of November, his birthday, we may have sportulae every year according to what he inscribed on the bronze tablet in the schola where we convene. And since this most pleases our order, and to thank such a good man who has benefited us greatly, we resolve unanimously to elect him patron, and to ask him to undertake the bronze tablet of patronage and willingly to write back, and to hold in his care all individuals whom he deigns to receive into his patronage. And so that this wish may be more widely known, this our decree, inscribed on a bronze tablet, is to be offered to him. Given by Iulius Sabinus Sextius Fidelis the elder, Ulpius Crescens. Servilius Diodorus to the collegium of dendrophori: Having received the bronze tablet offered to me in your name by Iulius Sabinus Sextius Fidelis the elder, Ulpius Crescens, your colleagues, I have gladly accepted it, and I understand that you are grateful for the gift I offered you and that you will observe what I have requested, especially since this is an example to my other fellow priests who desire the honor of patronage from you.
EDCS-10400059.summary: This lengthy inscription details the dedication of a statue to Gaius Servilius Diodorus by his wife Egnatia Salviana, followed by Diodorus' endowment to the dendrophori collegium of Laurentum Lavinatium for the celebration of his birthday and the election of himself and his mother as patrons, dating to the consulship of Albinus and Maximus (likely 201 or 207 CE).

EDCS-10400062.translation: To Lucius Arellius Petronius Karus, son of Lucius of the Fabia tribe, a distinguished Roman equestrian, flamen of the deified Augustus, duovir, aedile, quaestor of the colony. The cultores of Jupiter Arkanus of the market district, as their most worthy patron. A place was granted by decree of the decurions. Dedicated on the 5th day before the Ides of April, during the consulship of Arrianus and Papus. Under the care of Tiberius Claudius Victor, IIIIvir Augustalis.
EDCS-10400062.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a place to Lucius Arellius Petronius Karus, a distinguished local official and equestrian, by the cultores of Jupiter Arkanus in the market district, dating to the consulship of Arrianus and Papus (likely 147 or 155 CE).

EDCS-10400066.translation: To the Indigitamenta deities: a male lamb from Fucinus, a flock from Summanus, a flock from Fiscello. [---] to the Tempestates. [---] to Jupiter.
EDCS-10400066.summary: This inscription is a list of sacrificial animals offered to various deities, including the Indigitamenta, Summanus, Fiscello, Tempestates, and Jupiter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10400077.translation: To Titus Cassius (?) in the field (?) feet.
EDCS-10400077.summary: This fragmented inscription likely marks a land boundary or measurement related to a person named Titus Cassius, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10400078.translation: Sacred to the gods of the underworld. To Cassius Silvanus, freedman of Caius.
EDCS-10400078.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Cassius Silvanus, a freedman, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10400082.translation: Lucius Spurius Tertius, freedman of Lucius Spurius; Lucius Spurius Augctus, freedman of Lucius Spurius; Tuccia Salvia, freedwoman of a woman; Spuria Calliste, freedwoman of Lucius Spurius: 13 feet on the ground.
EDCS-10400082.summary: This inscription appears to record property boundaries or divisions, listing names possibly related to landowners or previous occupants.

EDCS-10400083.translation: Lucius Spurius Tertius, freedman of Lucius Spurius; Lucius Spurius Augctus, freedman of Lucius Spurius; Tuccia Salvia, freedwoman of a woman; Spuria Calliste, freedwoman of Lucius Spurius: 18 feet in front.
EDCS-10400083.summary: This inscription seems to define a property boundary or frontage, listing names possibly associated with the property.

EDCS-10400085.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Gnaeus Umbrio Clementi, son of Gnaeus, of the Aniensis tribe. Gnaeus Umbrius Rufinus and Quintilianus made this for [their] father.
EDCS-10400085.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by two sons, Gnaeus Umbrius Rufinus and Quintilianus, for their deceased father, Gnaeus Umbrio Clementi.

EDCS-10400099.translation: Lucius Titius Neptunalis, a plumber, made this with his son.
EDCS-10400099.summary: This inscription records a lead worker, Lucius Titius Neptunalis, and indicates he made something with his son, likely a commission or a shared work.

EDCS-10400101.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Suetria Isocrisis [made this] for her husband C(aius) Suetrius Eutyches, with whom she lived for 18 years and 3 months. Two sons [Valeria...].
EDCS-10400101.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a wife, Suetria Isocrisis, to her husband C(aius) Suetrius Eutyches, noting their years of marriage and mentioning two sons.

EDCS-10400102.translation: Demetrius, minister of Spei, sacred to Serrana Antonia.
EDCS-10400102.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Demetrius, a minister (attendant or servant), to Serrana Antonia, possibly a deity or a deceased person honored as such.

EDCS-10400103.translation: Gaius Aurelius Cotta, son of Gaius.
EDCS-10400103.summary: This is a simple name inscription, likely identifying an individual, possibly on a tomb or a public marker.

EDCS-10400106.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Cerdo made this for his well-deserving wife, Cassia Damalis.
EDCS-10400106.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Aurelius Cerdo for his deserving wife, Cassia Damalis.

EDCS-10400107.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. [Someone] took care to make this for Aristo, husband of Antiochis, who deserved well.
EDCS-10400107.summary: This funerary inscription records that someone (faciendum curavit) arranged for the erection of this monument for Aristo, the deserving husband of Antiochis.

EDCS-10400108.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Epigone [for] Phoebus, a fellow slave.
EDCS-10400108.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Epigone for Phoebus, identifying Phoebus as a fellow slave (conservo).

EDCS-10700761.text_edition: \n Marco Mindio Marci filio Marcello praefecto classis quei militant Caesari navarchi et trierarchi patrono\n
EDCS-10700761.translation: To Marcus Mindius Marcellus, son of Marcus, prefect of the fleet, the boatswains and captains serving Caesar, his patrons.
EDCS-10700761.summary: This inscription is a dedication by naval officers to their patron, Marcus Mindius Marcellus, prefect of the fleet serving Caesar.

EDCS-10700764.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Secundo sacerdoti Matris Deum coniugi et patrono optimo et incomparabili qui vixit annis LXXXIIII AVCT <u=I>xsor sic et fili et Aurelius Quintus convictor fecerunt bene merenti cum quo vixit annis viginti septem \n
EDCS-10700764.translation: To the spirits of the departed. His wife, AVCTuxsor, and his son Aurelius Quintus, his associate, made this for Gaius Julius Secundus, priest of the Mother of the Gods, an excellent and incomparable husband and patron, who lived 84 years, with whom he lived twenty-seven years.
EDCS-10700764.summary: This is a tombstone for Gaius Julius Secundus, a priest of the Mother of the Gods, set up by his wife and son, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-10700765.text_edition: \n Lucius Octavius Onesimus viam Mactorinam longa vetustate rescissam pecunia sua restituit acceptis ab re publica invectui silicis HS XIIII milibus ob quod opus ex decreto decurionum honorem decurionatus consecutus est item Luci Octavi Lucius Octavianus filius Lucius Annianus filius eius gratis inter decuriones adlecti sunt quorum alter qui aedilitate IIII PI \n
EDCS-10700765.translation: Lucius Octavius Onesimus restored the Mactorian road, which had fallen into ruin through long neglect, at his own expense, having received 14,000 sesterces from the state for this work; in return for which service, by decree of the town council, he obtained the honor of membership in the council. Likewise, his son Lucius Octavius and his grandson Lucius Annianus were admitted among the councilors without charge, of whom the former, when he held the office of aedile for the fourth time...
EDCS-10700765.summary: This inscription records Lucius Octavius Onesimus restoring a road at his own expense and being honored for it with a position on the town council, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-10700767.text_edition: \n Auli Brutti Asclepiani Tiberi Claudi Eutropi\n
EDCS-10700767.translation: Of Aulus Bruttius Asclepius and Tiberius Claudius Eutropus.
EDCS-10700767.summary: This is a simple dedication or funerary marker for two individuals, Aulus Bruttius Asclepius and Tiberius Claudius Eutropus.

EDCS-10700769.summary: This inscription is a dedication or funerary marker for Quartilla Egnatia Taurina.

EDCS-10700778.text_edition: \n Caius Baebius CCaiorum libertus P idem danista hoc commodum est factum ex testamento arbitratu Pamphili\n
EDCS-10700778.translation: Gaius Baebius, freedman of Gaius, a moneylender, this loan was made according to the will and at the discretion of Pamphilus.
EDCS-10700778.summary: This inscription records a loan made according to a will, established by Gaius Baebius, a freedman and moneylender.

EDCS-10700782.text_edition: \n Anicia Musas sibi et contibernali sic suo Quinto Cornelio Cupito et filio Quinto Anicio Concordino qui vixit annos X carissimis posterisque suis omnibus\n
EDCS-10700782.translation: Anicia Musas for herself and her companion and legal partner Quintus Cornelius Cupitus, and for her son Quintus Anicius Concordinus, who lived 10 years, and for all their descendants.
EDCS-10700782.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Anicia Musas for herself, her partner Quintus Cornelius Cupitus, their son Quintus Anicius Concordinus, and their future descendants.

EDCS-10700785.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caeciliae Albinae coniu<g>(i) incomparabili quae vixit nnos <XX=M>VI menses V dies XIII Lucius Herennius Dionysius et Caecilia Vitalis fecerunt \n
EDCS-10700785.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Herennius Dionysius and Caecilia Vitalis made this for Caecilia Albina, their incomparable wife, who lived 26 years, 5 months, and 13 days.
EDCS-10700785.summary: This is a tombstone for Caecilia Albina, erected by her husband Lucius Herennius Dionysius and Caecilia Vitalis, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-10700786.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caeciliae Bitali sic Caecilia Bitalis sic filia bene merenti matri fecit\n
EDCS-10700786.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caecilia Bitalis made this for her well-deserving mother, Caecilia Bitalis.
EDCS-10700786.summary: This is a tombstone for Caecilia Bitalis, erected by her daughter, also named Caecilia Bitalis.

EDCS-10700787.text_edition: \n Lucius Careius Cai filius Stellatina Lucius Careius Cai filius\n
EDCS-10700787.translation: Lucius Careius, son of Gaius, from the Stellatina tribe. Lucius Careius, son of Gaius.
EDCS-10700787.summary: This inscription appears to be a declaration of parentage and tribal affiliation for two individuals named Lucius Careius, son of Gaius.

EDCS-10700789.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberius Claudius Ario Antoniae coniu<g>i bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-10700789.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Tiberius Claudius Ario made this for his deserving wife Antonia.
EDCS-10700789.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Tiberius Claudius Ario to his wife Antonia.

EDCS-10700792.text_edition: \n Deciriae Luci filiae Tertiae Quinto Trebonio Quinto filio Candido filio\n
EDCS-10700792.translation: To Deciria, daughter of Lucius, the Third; and to Quintus Trebonius Candidus, son of Quintus.
EDCS-10700792.summary: This funerary monument honors Deciria and her son Quintus Trebonius Candidus.

EDCS-10700793.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Gaius Iul[ius? ...]. FO[...]
EDCS-10700793.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription potentially for Gaius Iulius.

EDCS-10700794.text_edition: \n Memoriae bonae Iulio Silbano sic Aurelia Thiofila sic coniugi incomparabili con sic quo vixit mensem I bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-10700794.translation: To the good memory of Silbanus Iulius. Aurelia Thiofila thus to her incomparable husband, with whom she lived one month, made this for him, who deserved well.
EDCS-10700794.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Aurelia Thiofila to her husband Iulius Silbanus, emphasizing their brief but happy marriage.

EDCS-10700797.text_edition: \n Lucio Marcio Aniceto Marcia Helpis patrono eiusdem coniugi bene merenti haec anima benedicta hoc loco secura requiescit cum quo vixit annos XXX sene sic ulla iniuria fecit et sibi et suis libertis libertabusque suis posterisque eorum ita uti macerie clusum est a piscina usque at sic puteal quoquo versus iugerum plus minus dimidium et Tito Flavio Augusti liberto Hermeti medico et libertis libertabus posterisque eorum Iovis Iuno\n
EDCS-10700797.translation: To Lucius Marcius Anicetus. Marcia Helpis, his patron, and to her deserving husband, to whom she lived with 30 years without any wrong, made this for herself and her freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants, and for Titus Flavius Hermetes, freedman of Augustus, a doctor, and his freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants, within the boundary wall from the pool to the well, about half a jugerum in all directions. Jupiter, Juno.
EDCS-10700797.summary: A complex funerary inscription detailing property arrangements and dedications to Lucius Marcius Anicetus, Marcia Helpis, and Titus Flavius Hermetes, with significant land allocation.

EDCS-10700798.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Naeviae Paratae Phaenia Blaste filia et Flavius Faenianus nepos matri bene merenti\n
EDCS-10700798.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Naevia Parata: from her daughter Phaenia Blaste and her grandson Flavius Faenianus.
EDCS-10700798.summary: A funerary dedication from Phaenia Blaste and Flavius Faenianus to Naevia Parata.

EDCS-10700799.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus L(ucio) Roscio Catathymio et Domitiae Secundae eius L(ucius) Roscius Aelianus Paculus patronus\n
EDCS-10700799.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Lucius Roscius Catathymius and his wife Domitia Secunda: from their patron Lucius Roscius Paculus.
EDCS-10700799.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Lucius Roscius Paculus to his freedmen Lucius Roscius Catathymius and Domitia Secunda.

EDCS-10700800.text_edition: \n Saturninus sibi et Serapioni patri suo et u sic matri et ate Proculo Primigenio fratribus\n
EDCS-10700800.translation: Saturninus for himself and for his father Serapio and his mother Ate [---] and his brothers Proculus and Primigenius.
EDCS-10700800.summary: A funerary inscription by Saturninus dedicating the monument to himself and his family members, including his parents and brothers.

EDCS-10700801.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Saturnio Hermeti patri Lucius Saturnius Hermes bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-10700801.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Saturnius Hermes, his father. Lucius Saturnius Hermes made this for him, who deserved well.
EDCS-10700801.summary: A funerary dedication by Lucius Saturnius Hermes to his father, Saturnius Hermes.

EDCS-10700802.text_edition: \n Titedia Apicula invisa nulli mater Colpi et matertera Themidis\n
EDCS-10700802.translation: Titedia Apicula, unloved by none, mother of Colpius and aunt of Themis.
EDCS-10700802.summary: An epitaph for Titedia Apicula, described as a beloved mother and aunt.

EDCS-10700819.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-10700819.text_edition: \n Faltoniae Hilaritati dominae filiae carissimae quae hoc coemeterium a solo sua pecunia fecit et huhic religioni donavit\n
EDCS-10700819.translation: To Faltonia Hilaritas, dearest mistress and daughter, who made this cemetery from the ground up with her own money and dedicated it to the gods.
EDCS-10700819.summary: This inscription is a dedication of a cemetery by Faltonia Hilaritas, likely a wealthy patron, possibly dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-10700820.translation: To good memory. Geneso, who lived 72 years, 4 months, and 11 days. Buried on the 6th day before the Kalends of August, in peace.
EDCS-10700820.summary: This epitaph records the life span and burial date of Geneso, a person of unknown status, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-10700822.translation: Leo, who lived 27 years and 10 months. Buried on the 10th day before the Kalends of October.
EDCS-10700822.summary: This epitaph records the life span and burial date of Leo, a young man, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-10701676.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Amatiae Adauctae Caius Amatius Polybius coniugi bene merenti vixit annos XXX menses III dies VI\n
EDCS-10701676.translation: To the Divine Shades. Caius Amatius Polybius to his well-deserving wife, Amatia Adaucta, who lived 30 years, 3 months, and 6 days.
EDCS-10701676.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Caius Amatius Polybius to his deceased wife, Amatia Adaucta, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-10701677.text_edition: \n Floronius binet ac miles legionis VII hic fuit neque mulieres scierunt nisi paucae et ses erunt\n
EDCS-10701677.translation: Floronius, a soldier of the Seventh Legion, was here and only a few women knew him and they will be his.
EDCS-10701677.summary: This inscription, likely related to a soldier, has an ambiguous and potentially suggestive message, possibly from the Imperial period.

EDCS-10900132.translation: The Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 10th time, acclaimed imperator 5 times, consul 5 times, father of the fatherland, saw to its restoration. 43.
EDCS-10900132.summary: This is an inscription recording the restoration of a building or monument by Emperor Trajan, likely during his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-10900135.text_edition: \n Caio Barbio Cleonti Medico seviro Augustali qui vixit nnos LXXVII Ulpia Chreste coniugi et Barbius Valens patri fecerunt\n
EDCS-10900135.translation: To Gaius Barbius Cleontus, a freedman of Augustalis, who lived 77 years. His wife, Ulpia Chreste, and his son, Barbius Valens, made this.
EDCS-10900135.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a wife and son for Gaius Barbius Cleontus, a freedman and Augustalis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400528.translation: Sacred to Silvanus. In fulfillment of a vow for the health of our Lord Domitianus Augustus, Lucius Domitius Phaon assigned his farms Junianum, Lolliamum, Percennianum, and Statuleianum with their respective villages and boundaries to the worship, protection, and sacrifices for all future time to those who were today in the college of Silvanus and to those who would later join it. He decreed that from the income of the aforesaid farms, on the Kalends of January, the birthday of our Domitia Augusta, on the Ides of February, on the dedication of Silvanus, and on the 12th day before the Kalends of July, on the rose festival, and on the 9th day before the Kalends of November, the birthday of our Domitianus Augustus, sacrifices should be made in the presence of the priests. He also decreed that those in the college should gather for a banquet, with the magistrates of each year being responsible. It is clear that anyone who would bring harm or deceit to prevent these things from happening is acting against the consecration of the farm below for the health of the Optime Princeps and Lord, and the days of sacrifice are established. Furthermore, the place or part of the land and woods in the park, which is marked by boundary stones around Silvanus, will be ceded to Silvanus, and the road and access to Silvanus through the Quaesicianum farm will be open to all. Wood from the Gallicianum farm and water from the park may also be freely used for sacrifice. Lucius Domitius Phaon, whose was the entire place, ordered and permitted these things to be given, done, and provided without deceit.
EDCS-11400528.summary: This extensive inscription records the endowment of several farms by Lucius Domitius Phaon to the cult of Silvanus for perpetual sacrifices and banquets, with specific dates and provisions for the members of his college, likely dating to the late 1st or early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-11400592.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Crescentina, dearest daughter, well-deserving, who lived 12 years, 11 months, and 1 day. Mother and father made this.
EDCS-11400592.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Crescentina, a young girl, erected by her parents, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400593.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Felix, who lived 3 years, 11 months, and 18 days. Diadumenus had this made for his well-deserving son.
EDCS-11400593.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Felix, a young child, erected by his father, Diadumenus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400596.translation: To the deified Julius, by the community of the people of Smyrna.
EDCS-11400596.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the deified Julius by the city of Smyrna, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400597.translation: To Germanicus Caesar, son of Tiberius Augustus, grandson of the deified Augustus, great-grandson of the deified Julius, pontiff, Augustalis, augur, twice consul, twice imperator.
EDCS-11400597.summary: This inscription is an honorific dedication to Germanicus Caesar, listing his titles and lineage, dating to the early Roman Empire.

EDCS-11400599.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan the Parthian, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, chief pontiff, with tribunician power for the 5th time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, founder of the sacred Iselastic contest, by the associated popular lictors and Puteolan heralds.
EDCS-11400599.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Antoninus Pius by the lictors and heralds of Puteoli, praising him as the founder of a sacred contest and listing his numerous titles and powers, likely dating to the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-11400601.translation: Alpinius Magnus, a most distinguished man, corrector of Lucania and the Bruttii, dedicated this to our lady Flavia Helena Augusta, most chaste wife of the deified Constantine, mother of our lord Constantine the Greatest, Most Pious, and Victorious Augustus, and grandmother of our lords [[Crispus]] and Constantine and Constantius, most blessed and fortunate Caesars, devoted to her excellence and piety.
EDCS-11400601.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Alpinius Magnus to Empress Flavia Helena Augusta, wife of Constantine the Great and mother of Constantine II, likely dating to the 4th century AD.

EDCS-11400605.translation: Dedicated on the sixteenth day before the Kalends of June, during the consulship of our Lord Emperor Gordian Pius Felix Augustus for the second time and Pompeianus. Geminius Tuticius Aemilianus, a most distinguished man, most worthy and incomparable patron, of admirable munificence, [dedicated this in the] Hortensian region.
EDCS-11400605.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument or area on a specific date in 241 AD, honoring the patron Geminius Tuticius Aemilianus and mentioning Emperor Gordian III and his co-consul.

EDCS-11400606.translation: To the departed spirits. For Marcus Insteius Bithynicus, consul.
EDCS-11400606.summary: This is a tomb inscription (dis manibus) for Marcus Insteius Bithynicus, who held the office of consul, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400607.translation: To the departed spirits. For Lucilia Aprulla, a woman of most distinguished memory, by her grandson through the degrees of inheritance, the heir.
EDCS-11400607.summary: This tomb inscription is for Lucilia Aprulla, set up by her heir who is also her grandson, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400608.translation: [Erected] by the Augustal decurions and colonists, at their own expense, to Servilius Marsus, son of Gaius, a most distinguished youth, patron of the colony, because of his exceptional honorific regard towards them individually and collectively.
EDCS-11400608.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by the local council and colonists of a Roman colony to honor their patron, Servilius Marsus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400611.translation: Tiberius Claudius Erastus, freedman of the deified Claudius, secretary for correspondence, made this for himself and for his wife Valeria Chione, and for their freedmen and freedwomen, and for their descendants.
EDCS-11400611.summary: This is a private funerary or dedication inscription made by Tiberius Claudius Erastus, a freedman of Emperor Claudius, for himself, his wife, and their dependents, likely dating to the mid-1st century AD.

EDCS-11400612.translation: To the departed spirits. For Hermerotes, a personal attendant of our Caesar, [his] colleagues arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-11400612.summary: This tomb inscription is for Hermerotes, a servant of the emperor, likely from the Roman Imperial period, erected by his colleagues.

EDCS-11400617.translation: To Marcus Caninius Priscus, son of Marcus, of the Polentia tribe, a soldier of the 6th Praetorian cohort, Centuria Satrius, who lived 35 years and served 15 years. Titus Attulius Severus, his tent-mate, and Valeria Helpis, his dearest wife, set this up for him, deserving well.
EDCS-11400617.summary: This is a funerary monument for a Praetorian guardsman named Marcus Caninius Priscus, set up by his tent-mate and wife.

EDCS-11400618.translation: To Decimus Decuminus, a soldier of the 2nd Praetorian cohort, the Pious, Victorious, Severian.
EDCS-11400618.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a soldier of the Praetorian guard, likely dating to the Severan period.

EDCS-11400619.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Titus Flavius Tallio, son of Titus, an urban soldier of the 12th Urban cohort, who served 11 years and lived 33 years. Flavia Elpis, his mother, set this up.
EDCS-11400619.summary: This is a funerary monument for Titus Flavius Tallio, an urban soldier, erected by his mother.

EDCS-11400620.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Rutilius Vibianus, a soldier who lived 21 years and 11 days. Marcus Rutilius Maximus and Fabia Veratia, his parents, for him deserving well.
EDCS-11400620.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Rutilius Vibianus, a soldier, set up by his parents.

EDCS-11400621.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Titinius Pater, his father, made this for his most excellent son, Titinius Pulcher, an evocatus Augusti.
EDCS-11400621.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by a father to his son, Titinius Pulcher, who held the honorary military rank of evocatus Augusti.

EDCS-11400623.translation: They consecrated this to Acerius Firmus Leontius, a primary man and editor of his own munus, with the Libyan beasts, who lived 30 years, most worthy son.
EDCS-11400623.summary: This is a funerary monument for Acerius Firmus Leontius, who died at 30 and was involved in staging gladiatorial games.

EDCS-11400624.translation: To Gnaeus Haius Doryphorus, a purpurarius Augustalis, dupliciarius, who lived 47 years, 6 months, and 29 days.
EDCS-11400624.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gnaeus Haius Doryphorus, a merchant dealing in purple dyes and holding a higher rank within the Augustal association.

EDCS-11400629.translation: Lucius Visellius Felix, freedman of Lucius and [Libertas?], a wine merchant, who lived 55 years. Visellia Quarta, his mother, made this for her devoted son and for herself.
EDCS-11400629.summary: This is a funerary monument for Lucius Visellius Felix, a wine merchant, set up by his mother, who also reserved a place for herself.

EDCS-11400630.translation: To Acte Caesaris, who lived 36 years.
EDCS-11400630.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Acte Caesaris, who died at the age of 36.

EDCS-11400632.translation: Flavia Musias made this for her dearest husband, Publius Aelius Agathemerus.
EDCS-11400632.summary: This is a funerary monument for Publius Aelius Agathemerus, set up by his wife.

EDCS-11400633.translation: Lucius Aelius Blandus lived for 24 years.
EDCS-11400633.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Lucius Aelius Blandus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400634.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Callistus, a freedman and brother, made this for Publius Aelius Pistus, a freedman of the emperor.
EDCS-11400634.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication made by a freedman and brother to a fellow freedman of the emperor, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400635.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Septimius Saturninus, and his brother Lucius Septimius Achilles, and his granddaughters Antiochis and Victoria Colenda, and their heirs, made this for their most beloved wife and mother, Aelia Antiochis, who lived for 51 years and 9 months.
EDCS-11400635.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication by a husband, his brother, and grandchildren to a deceased woman, Aelia Antiochis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400636.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Herus, with his Inulus, made this for his well-deserving grandmother Aemilia Victorina.
EDCS-11400636.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication by Herus and Inulus to their grandmother Aemilia Victorina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400637.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Alfenatia Floscula made this for her most devoted mother Halei.
EDCS-11400637.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication by Alfenatia Floscula to her mother Halei, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400638.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Helvicia Paulina made this for her well-deserving husband Gaius Ampedius Disco.
EDCS-11400638.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication by Helvicia Paulina to her husband Gaius Ampedius Disco, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400640.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Her parents Philochorus and Arescusa, and her brother Albanus, made this for their sweetest daughter Anthusa, well-deserving, a virgin, who lived for 16 years, 3 months, and 18 days.
EDCS-11400640.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Anthusa, a virgin, and includes her lifespan and ages, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400641.translation: Marcus Antonius Felix lived for 50 years. Antonia Prepis made this for her freedman.
EDCS-11400641.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Marcus Antonius Felix and a dedication by Antonia Prepis to her freedman, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400643.translation: To Aprius and Flavia Eutychia, for their dearest son.
EDCS-11400643.summary: This is a dedication to a son from his parents, Aprius and Flavia Eutychia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400644.translation: Lucius Asinius Philocalus and Asinia Helene, parents, and for themselves and their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants, made this for their sweetest daughter Asinia Ascia Venusta, who lived for 14 years, 10 months, and 8 days.
EDCS-11400644.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Asinia Ascia Venusta and includes her lifespan and ages, with dedication from her parents and provision for descendants, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400645.translation: To the departed spirits. To Gaius Atlius Destrus.
EDCS-11400645.summary: A funerary dedication to Gaius Atlius Destrus.

EDCS-11400646.translation: To the departed spirits. For Atilia Quartilla, daughter of Gaius, who lived 2 years, 5 months, and 23 days. Iulius Felicissimus made this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-11400646.summary: A funerary epitaph for Atilia Quartilla, detailing her age and erected by her husband, Iulius Felicissimus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11400647.translation: To Gaius Avedius [...] Avedia [...] to her most beloved husband made [...] who lived [years]...
EDCS-11400647.summary: A funerary inscription for a husband, Gaius Avedius, and wife, Avedia, with significant portions missing, indicating the deceased's lifespan.

EDCS-11400648.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Dionysius, freedman of the Emperor, and Maestria Aelia, his wife, made and completed this tomb for themselves while alive, at their own expense, for themselves, their sons, likewise their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants, and also for my brother Aurelius Nicon, I granted one place for the departed of Decentianum.
EDCS-11400648.summary: A self-funded funerary monument erected by Aurelius Dionysius and his wife Maestria Aelia for themselves, their descendants, and his brother Aurelius Nicon, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-11400650.translation: To the departed spirits. For Lucius Aurelius Severus, dearest son, aged 25. Lucius Aurelius Primus.
EDCS-11400650.summary: A funerary inscription for Lucius Aurelius Severus, aged 25, dedicated by his father, Lucius Aurelius Primus.

EDCS-11400651.summary: A very brief funerary inscription recording the lifespan of a person named Blastus.

EDCS-11400652.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gaius Caesorius Hagus. Titus Annianus Hymnus, father, made this for his most devoted son.
EDCS-11400652.summary: A funerary dedication for Gaius Caesorius Hagus, made by his father Titus Annianus Hymnus.

EDCS-11400654.translation: Gaius Calpurnius Sermius, son of Marcus, of the tribe Quirina, a student of law, lived 39 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-11400654.summary: An epitaph for Gaius Calpurnius Sermius, a law student who died at 39, stating his burial location.

EDCS-11400655.translation: For Cannutia Decilma, daughter of Gaius, an excellent and deserving woman. Gaius Curvius Successus made [this]. She lived [X] years [Y] months [Z]...
EDCS-11400655.summary: A funerary inscription for Cannutia Decilma, erected by her husband Gaius Curvius Successus, with her lifespan partially preserved.

EDCS-11400657.translation: For Cicereia Zenonis. Aurelius Victor, son, made this for his deserving mother.
EDCS-11400657.summary: A funerary dedication for Cicereia Zenonis, made by her son Aurelius Victor.

EDCS-11400658.translation: Tiberius Claudius Gordus made this for Claudia Fusca, his freedwoman, and for her, his dearest and most holy wife.
EDCS-11400658.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Tiberius Claudius Gordus commissioned this monument for his freedwoman and wife, Claudia Fusca.

EDCS-11400660.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Of Tiberius Claudius Calenus.
EDCS-11400660.summary: This is a dedication to the departed spirits (Dis Manibus), likely from the tombstone or monument of Tiberius Claudius Calenus.

EDCS-11400661.translation: To the departed spirits. Tiberius Claudius Marcellus lived 18 years and 3 months. Aurelia Procla, his most dutiful daughter, made this.
EDCS-11400661.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Aurelia Procla commissioned this monument for her son, Tiberius Claudius Marcellus, who died at the age of 18 years and 3 months.

EDCS-11400662.translation: To the departed spirits. Claudia Eutychia lived 71 years. Servius Anicius Turannus made this for his most excellent wife and for himself.
EDCS-11400662.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Servius Anicius Turannus commissioned this monument for his wife, Claudia Eutychia, who died at age 71, and also for himself.

EDCS-11400663.translation: Here lies a boy deprived of his eighth year, whom the cruel ruin of fate presses down. His name was Clemens, one who should least have been harmed, and hope rested in the boy; now he is ashes and a groan.
EDCS-11400663.summary: This funerary epigram mourns the death of a young boy named Clemens, who died before reaching the age of eight, lamenting the tragic loss of his life and future.

EDCS-11400666.translation: Cornelia Hygia, freedwoman of Lucius, lived 40 years. Tiberius Claudius Inopus, freedman of Augustus, made this for his wife.
EDCS-11400666.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Tiberius Claudius Inopus commissioned this monument for his wife, Cornelia Hygia, who lived 40 years.

EDCS-11400668.translation: For Eucharis, my sweet daughter, who lived 10 years and 4 hours.
EDCS-11400668.summary: This funerary epitaph is a loving dedication for Eucharis, a daughter who died at the age of 10 years and 4 hours.

EDCS-11400669.translation: To the departed spirits. For Fadia Primigenia, my dearest wife. Marcus Antonius Patrocles.
EDCS-11400669.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Marcus Antonius Patrocles dedicated this to his dearest wife, Fadia Primigenia.

EDCS-11400670.translation: For Lucius Faenius Euanthes, freedman of Lucius, my sweetest son. Euanthes, the father [made this].
EDCS-11400670.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Euanthes the father commissioned this monument for his son, Lucius Faenius Euanthes, his freedman.

EDCS-11400672.translation: To the departed spirits. For Flaminia Sympherusa. Publius Aurelius Abascantus, the patron, made this for his well-deserving freedwoman.
EDCS-11400672.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Publius Aurelius Abascantus, the patron, commissioned this monument for his deserving freedwoman, Flaminia Sympherusa.

EDCS-11400674.translation: Flavia Chrysis made this for her well-deserving adopted son, who lived 16 years and 1 month.
EDCS-11400674.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavia Chrysis' adopted son, dated by the style and content.

EDCS-11400675.translation: Flavia Prisca, daughter of Quintus, lived 35 years. Flavia Prima, freedwoman of Quintus Quintus.
EDCS-11400675.summary: This epitaph records the death of Flavia Prisca and also mentions Flavia Prima, likely related, possibly from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11400678.translation: Publius Galerius Maximus lived 4 years, 6 months, and 3 days; he is buried here.
EDCS-11400678.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young child, Publius Galerius Maximus.

EDCS-11400679.translation: To the gods of the underworld. His most deserving heirs provided this for Aulus Geminius Demetrius at his own expense.
EDCS-11400679.summary: This funerary inscription records the provision of a burial by the heirs for Aulus Geminius Demetrius, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11400681.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Helmonia Pyrallis, who died at age 18.

EDCS-11400682.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Helmonia Sympherusa, who died at age 31.

EDCS-11400683.translation: Sacred to the gods of the underworld. Helphenuleia Helphis, freedwoman, made this for her patron Aulus Herennuleius Ampiatus, who deserved well.
EDCS-11400683.summary: This funerary inscription was dedicated by a freedwoman to her well-deserving patron.

EDCS-11400684.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Hermes, who died at age 16.

EDCS-11400685.translation: Iulia, who lived 23 years, one month, and 29 days, rests here. Marcus Annaeus Agatho for his sweetest wife.
EDCS-11400685.summary: This funerary epitaph was erected by Marcus Annaeus Agatho for his wife Iulia.

EDCS-11400686.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Julius Aurelius Festus the younger lived 6 years, 11 months, and 21 days. Festus, his father, and Matrona, his mother.
EDCS-11400686.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Julius Aurelius Festus the younger and was set up by his parents.

EDCS-11400688.translation: To the departed spirits. Calocaerus, freedman of the emperor, made this for his most holy wife, Julia Agrippina, most rare and incomparable.
EDCS-11400688.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by a freedman to his wife, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11400690.translation: Iulia Pascusa, freedwoman of (her patron), lived 23 years.
EDCS-11400690.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespan of a freedwoman named Iulia Pascusa, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11400691.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Flavius Espectatus, son, placed this for his mother, Iulia Paulina.
EDCS-11400691.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a son for his mother, Iulia Paulina, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11400692.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulia Primitiva made this for her most beloved husband, Iulius Crescens, who deserved well.
EDCS-11400692.summary: This is a funerary inscription made by a wife for her well-deserving husband, Iulius Crescens, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11400693.translation: To the departed spirits. Publius Iunius Avitus, whom his parents and his most sweet and pious (son) made (this) for, well-deserving, lived 23 years, 7 months, and 29 days.
EDCS-11400693.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Iunius Avitus, set up by his parents and his son, recording his age at death, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11400694.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Iuventius Asiaticus lived 39 years. His mother, Iuventia Secunda, placed this.
EDCS-11400694.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by a mother for her son, Lucius Iuventius Asiaticus, recording his age, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11400695.translation: To the departed spirits. Lartia Chresime made this for her well-deserving freedman, Lucius Lartius Trophimus.
EDCS-11400695.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by a patroness to her freedman, Lucius Lartius Trophimus, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11400696.translation: To the departed spirits. Mallia Pantheris, daughter of Titus, lived 18 years, 10 months, and 24 days.
EDCS-11400696.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the age at death of a young woman named Mallia Pantheris, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11400697.translation: To Manilia Primigenia, freedwoman of Caius, most holy woman, lived 25 years.
EDCS-11400697.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Manilia Primigenia, a freedwoman, recording her age and commending her character, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11400698.translation: To the departed spirits. Caius Maeclonius Parthinus lived 2 years, 5 months. His mother, Iuventia Ephesia, made this.
EDCS-11400698.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by a mother for her young son, Caius Maeclonius Parthinus, recording his age, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11400699.translation: To the departed spirits. Minicius Uttedianus made this for his father, Minicius Justus, who deserved well.
EDCS-11400699.summary: A funerary epitaph for a father, set up by his son Minicius Uttedianus.

EDCS-11400700.translation: To Neratia [---], most holy woman of exemplary character, farewell.
EDCS-11400700.summary: A funerary epitaph for Neratia, praising her character.

EDCS-11400701.translation: To the departed spirits. Nonius Faustianus made this for his most unhappy sister, Nonia Larinatia, who lived 23 years, 7 months, and 8 days.
EDCS-11400701.summary: A funerary epitaph for Nonia Larinatia, set up by her brother Nonius Faustianus.

EDCS-11400703.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Perperna Eutyches made this for himself and for his dearest daughter, Perperna Fortunata, who lived 11 years, 6 months, and 16 days.
EDCS-11400703.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Perperna Eutyches, his daughter, and himself.

EDCS-11400704.translation: Caius Petronius Marcellinus made this for his son, Caius Petronius, who lived two years, four months, and 21 days.
EDCS-11400704.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Petronius, set up by his father Caius Petronius Marcellinus.

EDCS-11400705.translation: Decimus Petronius Mercurius lived 18 years, 8 months, and 11 days; he rests here. Decimus Petronius Epictetus, the father, made this for his most dutiful son.
EDCS-11400705.summary: A funerary epitaph for Decimus Petronius Mercurius, set up by his father Decimus Petronius Epictetus.

EDCS-11400708.translation: To the departed spirits. Publius Popilius Apollonius, Popilia Eutychis, and Sergius Anicius Triumphalis (called?) made this for their patron who deserved well.
EDCS-11400708.summary: A funerary epitaph for a patron, set up by his freedmen and freedwomen.

EDCS-11400709.translation: To the departed spirits. [---] Proculeius Magnus, son of Caius, from the Falerna tribe, made this for his father, Caius Proculeius Alexander, freedman of Proculeius, and for his mother, Proculeia, freedwoman of Proculeius.
EDCS-11400709.summary: A funerary epitaph for parents, set up by their son, who records their freedman status.

EDCS-11400710.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Quirinius Hermes made this for his patrons, Quirinia Ianuaria, who lived 45 years, and Sextus Vestilius Lycus, who deserved well.
EDCS-11400710.summary: A funerary epitaph for two patrons, set up by their freedman Marcus Quirinius Hermes.

EDCS-11400711.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Rennius Crispinus made this for himself while alive, and for his freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants thereafter.
EDCS-11400711.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Rennius Crispinus, made by himself for himself, his freedpersons and their descendants.

EDCS-11400713.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Sallustius Quartio, who lived 40 years, 5 months, 1 day. Marcus Sallustius Crescens, his freedman, erected (this) to one deserving well.
EDCS-11400713.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Sallustius Quartio, set up by his freedman, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400715.translation: Hail Septima, may the earth lie lightly upon you. Whoever placed (this) on this tomb, may golden earth cover her ashes while she burns.
EDCS-11400715.summary: This is a poetic epitaph, possibly for a woman named Septima, expressing a wish for a light burial and future peace, likely from the Roman Imperial period.
