EDCS-11400716.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aulus Sergius Agatho. Aulus Sergius Amoenus, (his?) freedman, made (this) to one deserving well.
EDCS-11400716.summary: This funerary epitaph records the dedication of a tomb to Aulus Sergius Agatho by his freedman, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400718.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Sertorius [---], for Sertorius Prosdus [---], for Sertoria Tyche, to (her) son.
EDCS-11400718.summary: This is a fragmented funerary epitaph dedicating a monument to multiple individuals, likely family members, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400719.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Servilius [---] C D. Cinadus [---].
EDCS-11400719.summary: This is a severely damaged funerary epitaph, possibly for an individual named Servilius, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400721.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Servilia Artemonis, freedwoman of Aulus, dearest wife. // To the spirits of the departed. For Aulus Servilius Apella, in memory.
EDCS-11400721.summary: This epitaph records the dedication of two funerary monuments, one for Servilia Artemonis by her husband and another for Aulus Servilius Apella, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400724.translation: For Taietia Restituta, fellow freedwoman and deserving wife. Lucius Taietius Zosimus, with whom he lived for 20 years. She lived for 35 years.
EDCS-11400724.summary: This funerary epitaph records the relationship between Lucius Taietius Zosimus and his fellow freedwoman and wife, Taietia Restituta, detailing their lifespans and marriage duration, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400725.translation: Gaius Terentius Rarus lived for 3 years and 18 days. Gaius Terentius Carpoforus and Terentia Fortunata (erected this) to their most dutiful son.
EDCS-11400725.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for the young child Gaius Terentius Rarus, set up by his parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400726.translation: The bones of Marcus Titicenus Maximus, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, who lived 32 years.
EDCS-11400726.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age at death of Marcus Titicenus Maximus, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400727.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Valerius Aemilianus, who lived 9 years, 4 months, 18 days. Valerius Dragontianus and Attia Zenonis, unhappy parents, (erected this) to their sweetest son.
EDCS-11400727.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of the young Marcus Valerius Aemilianus, set up by his grieving parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400728.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication for Valeria, daughter of Gaius.

EDCS-11400729.translation: Lucius Veratius Nicomachus made this for Veratia Regina, his dearest foster-child, who lived 5 years, 9 months, and 23 days. Posphorus and Rustica, her parents, made this.
EDCS-11400729.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by parents for their foster-child, Veratia Regina, who died at a young age.

EDCS-11400730.translation: To Publius Vetilius Acutus, freedman of Publius Vetilius. Publius Vetilius Amiantus lived 1 year and 6 months.
EDCS-11400730.summary: This inscription records two individuals, Publius Vetilius Acutus and Publius Vetilius Amiantus, likely related through slavery or freedman status and possibly a father-son relationship, with the latter dying in infancy.

EDCS-11400731.translation: To the most sacred and dearest Caia Vettia Grata, a most distinguished young girl, who lived two years, two months, and 8 days. Aurelius Gentianus, her nurse, placed this.
EDCS-11400731.summary: This is a funerary dedication for a young girl named Caia Vettia Grata, placed by her nurse.

EDCS-11400732.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Vettiena Dicaeosyne made this for Gaius Vettienus Lupercus and herself, with the condition that after her death no one else's bones are to be added here.
EDCS-11400732.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Vettienus Lupercus, set up by Vettiena Dicaeosyne, who also designated a burial place for herself with specific instructions.

EDCS-11400734.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caius Vibius Eusebes made this for his well-deserving mother, Vibis Collylyche.
EDCS-11400734.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by a son, Caius Vibius Eusebes, to his mother, Vibia Collylyche.

EDCS-11400737.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Ulpius Euphorbus, freedman of Augustus, who lived 39 years and 8 days.
EDCS-11400737.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Ulpius Euphorbus, a freedman of Augustus, who died at the age of 39.

EDCS-11400738.translation: [---]cissimus, who lived [---] years. [---]lidromus and Paterus, who died.
EDCS-11400738.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to be a funerary dedication for multiple individuals, recording their names and lifespans.

EDCS-11400745.summary: This inscription is a dedication to an animal named Delicius, described as most innocent, possibly a pet.

EDCS-11400749.translation: Leo made this for himself while alive.
EDCS-11400749.summary: This is a dedication made by Leo for himself during his lifetime, possibly for a tomb or a memorial.

EDCS-11400750.summary: This is a short funerary inscription for Lucilla, likely from a tomb.

EDCS-11400753.translation: To Pelagius, a young boy, innocent, who lived 28 years and 10 months. [Dedicated by] parents to a son.
EDCS-11400753.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young boy named Pelagius, dedicated by his parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400761.translation: To our most pious and venerable lady, Faustina Augusta, wife of our lord Maximius Victor Augustus, mother of our lords Constantinus, Crispus Constantinus, and Constantius, most blessed Caesars. The public community of the Surrentines [erected this].
EDCS-11400761.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription from the public community of the Surrentines honoring Faustina Augusta, wife of Maxentius, and their children, dating to the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-11400763.translation: [To...]ae, daughter of Lucius, public priestess of Venus and Ceres. To Marcus Sittius Caius filius Falerna Frontonius Saufeius Proculus, he lived for one year and three months. The decurions decreed for him a place of burial and 5,000 sesterces for his funeral. To Titus Clodius Caius filius Proculus, prefect of the engineers, tribune of the IV Scythica legion, sent by the Emperor Caesar Augustus as proconsul to the Lusitanians.
EDCS-11400763.summary: This is a funerary inscription and honorific decree honoring several individuals, including a priestess and prominent local officials, likely from the late 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11400767.translation: Titus Claudius Longinus of the 5th Praetorian Cohort, a scout, made [this] for himself and his family while alive. To the spirits of the departed. He lived 26 years.
EDCS-11400767.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titus Claudius Longinus, a scout in the Praetorian Guard, who set it up for himself and his family, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400768.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caesius Ne[---], a soldier from the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, aged 30, of the NIX nationality.
EDCS-11400768.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caesius Ne[---], a soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400769.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Of Marcus Stabi Verus, centurion of the 2nd Praetorian Cohort, a trecenarius.
EDCS-11400769.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Stabi Verus, a centurion and trecenarius in the 2nd Praetorian Cohort, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400770.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Tarullius Tatentius, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, of Sardinian origin. His heirs made [this] for him as one who deserved well.
EDCS-11400770.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tarullius Tatentius, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum of Sardinian origin, erected by his heirs, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400772.translation: To Lucius Arruntius Rufus Teiedius Proculus, son of Publius, grandson of Lucius, of the Menenia tribe. He lived 24 years. The decurions decreed that two statues be erected for him, one at public expense and the other from bronze contributed by the people, in the forum.
EDCS-11400772.summary: This is a funerary inscription and decree honoring Lucius Arruntius Rufus Teiedius Proculus, detailing public honors and statues erected by the decurions, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400773.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Quintus Valerius Salutaris, Augustalis of Puteoli and Cumae, and for Valeria Tryphena. [Erected by] her heirs.
EDCS-11400773.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus Valerius Salutaris, an Augustalis of Puteoli and Cumae, and Valeria Tryphena, erected by her heirs, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400774.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Acorus, a slave born in the imperial household, lived for 27 years, 11 months, and 10 days.
EDCS-11400774.summary: This is a tombstone for Acorus, an imperial slave, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11400777.translation: Cerdo, a slave born in the imperial household, lived for 70 years.
EDCS-11400777.summary: This is a tombstone for Cerdo, an imperial slave, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11400778.translation: To Cerdon Lupercus, freedman of the emperor, who served as chamberlain; he lived 54 years. Ampliata, his faithful fellow servant, [dedicated this] from her own property.
EDCS-11400778.summary: This is a tombstone for Cerdon Lupercus, an imperial freedman and chamberlain, dedicated by his fellow servant Ampliata, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11400780.translation: Chorinbus, freedman of the emperor, lived for 65 years.
EDCS-11400780.summary: This is a tombstone for Chorinbus, an imperial freedman, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11400782.translation: [Claudia] Sepi, possibly a freedwoman of the emperor, lived for [an unknown number of] years.
EDCS-11400782.summary: This is a tombstone for Claudia Sepi, possibly an imperial freedwoman, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11400783.translation: Tiberius Claudius Optatus, freedman of the emperor, lived for 75 years.
EDCS-11400783.summary: This is a tombstone for Tiberius Claudius Optatus, an imperial freedman, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11400785.translation: Eutychus, a slave of the emperor, died from illness and lived for 40 years.
EDCS-11400785.summary: This is a tombstone for Eutychus, an imperial slave who died from illness, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11400786.translation: Faustilla, a slave born in the imperial household, lived for 15 years.
EDCS-11400786.summary: This is a tombstone for Faustilla, an imperial slave, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11400787.translation: Felix Tharsi, born in the imperial household, lived for 18 years.
EDCS-11400787.summary: This is a tombstone for Felix Tharsi, an imperial slave, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11400788.translation: [---] Flavius Tharsus, [---] tax collector, lived for 60 years.
EDCS-11400788.summary: This is a tombstone for Flavius Tharsus, identified as a tax collector, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11400790.translation: To Fronton, servant of Augustus, builder.
EDCS-11400790.summary: This is a dedication to Fronton, identified as a builder and a servant of the emperor, likely from a tombstone.

EDCS-11400791.translation: Hedymele, a servant of Augustus, named Veneria, lived 30 years.
EDCS-11400791.summary: This epitaph records the lifespan of Hedymele, a servant of Augustus, and possibly a freedwoman given her name.

EDCS-11400794.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Oriens, a slave of Augustus, lived 47 years.
EDCS-11400794.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Oriens, a slave of Augustus, who lived to be 47 years old.

EDCS-11400795.translation: Zizes of the Augustan atrium, a Lycaean by nationality, lived 50 years; placed by his cohabitant Moeris.
EDCS-11400795.summary: This tombstone records the life of Zizes, an atrium attendant of Augustus and of Lycaean origin, placed by his partner Moeris.

EDCS-11400796.summary: This is a brief epitaph recording the lifespan of Aeitales, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400797.translation: Albinia Honorata lived 22 years, 10 months, and 27 days.
EDCS-11400797.summary: This epitaph details the age and lifespan of Albinia Honorata.

EDCS-11400798.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Marcus Antonius Olympicus.
EDCS-11400798.summary: A funerary dedication to Marcus Antonius Olympicus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400799.summary: This tombstone records the lifespan of Atilia Lycinice, who lived to be 80 years old.

EDCS-11400801.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Avillus Nymphus, who lived 80 years. By Nymphianus, his son, and Successus, his freedman.
EDCS-11400801.summary: This funerary inscription honors Marcus Avillus Nymphus, who lived to 80, with dedications from his son and freedman.

EDCS-11400802.summary: This incomplete epitaph records that Avita Ampliatae lived for 30 years, with the rest of the inscription missing.

EDCS-11400806.translation: To the Divine Shades. Thamyris, freedman of Aulus Caesennius, lived 60 years.
EDCS-11400806.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Thamyris, a freedman, recording his age at death.

EDCS-11400807.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caesennia Graphice, indicating her burial place.

EDCS-11400808.translation: To the Divine Shades. Claudia Ianuaria lived 24 years and 23 days.
EDCS-11400808.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Claudia Ianuaria, recording her age at death.

EDCS-11400810.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lascuccia Felicitas made this for her husband Quintus Cominius Elpidianus, who lived 35 years.
EDCS-11400810.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by Lascuccia Felicitas to her husband Quintus Cominius Elpidianus, who lived 35 years.

EDCS-11400812.translation: To the Divine Shades. Dolutius October made this for his well-deserving patron Marcus Dolutius Justus.
EDCS-11400812.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Dolutius October for his patron Marcus Dolutius Justus.

EDCS-11400813.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Publius Equitius Auctus lived 40 years.
EDCS-11400813.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Equitius Auctus, recording his age at death.

EDCS-11400815.translation: To the Divine Shades. Faenia Nice made this for herself. To the Divine Shades(?) and for her dearest Faenia Caes[---], her daughter.
EDCS-11400815.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by Faenia Nice for herself and her deceased daughter, Faenia Caes[---].

EDCS-11400816.translation: To the Divine Shades. Titius Flavius Felix lived 31 years, 8 months, and 21 days. Caesenna Lasciva made this for her son.
EDCS-11400816.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Caesenna Lasciva for her son Titius Flavius Felix, recording his age at death.

EDCS-11400817.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavia Cleonica, recording her age at death.

EDCS-11400818.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gavia Iucunda, recording her age at death.

EDCS-11400819.translation: To the divine shades. Sacred to Gellius Myrtales, who lived 25 years. Lucius Gellius Primus to his dearest, well-deserving wife.
EDCS-11400819.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gellius Myrtales, erected by her husband.

EDCS-11400821.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Julius Charito, stating his age at death.

EDCS-11400822.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Julius Successus, stating his age at death.

EDCS-11400823.translation: To the divine shades. Iulia Callityche lived 36 years and 7 months.
EDCS-11400823.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulia Callityche, stating her age at death.

EDCS-11400824.translation: To the divine shades. Freedmen of both, Licinia Daphne, freedwoman of Lucius, and Marcus Livius Achelous, her husband, have set this up for their well-deserving patrons.
EDCS-11400824.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by freedmen for their patrons, Licinia Daphne and Marcus Livius Achelous.

EDCS-11400827.translation: To the divine shades. Sacred to Livia Lucinia, who lived 80 years. Livia Primitiva made this for her well-deserving mother.
EDCS-11400827.summary: A funerary epitaph for Livia Lucinia, erected by her daughter.

EDCS-11400829.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Octavius Priscus, stating his age at death, with an incomplete name following.

EDCS-11400830.translation: To the divine shades. Quintus Otincius Ionicus lived 14 years and 36 days.
EDCS-11400830.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Otincius Ionicus, stating his age at death.

EDCS-11400832.translation: For the well-deserving Plautia Ampliata, who lived 30 years.
EDCS-11400832.summary: A funerary epitaph for Plautia Ampliata, stating her age at death.

EDCS-11400833.translation: To the divine shades. Sacred to Plotia Rhodope, freedwoman of Lucius. Euchrestus to his most devoted, well-deserving wife.
EDCS-11400833.summary: A funerary epitaph for Plotia Rhodope, erected by her husband.

EDCS-11400834.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the lifespan of Pompeia Ampelis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400835.translation: Caius Fufius Diogenes, freedman and father, [erected this for] Proba Syntyca, daughter of Syntyca; he lived 76 years.
EDCS-11400835.summary: This tombstone inscription records the death of Proba Syntyca and the lifespan of her freedman and father, Caius Fufius Diogenes, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400836.summary: This tombstone inscription records the lifespan of Tertia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400837.translation: To the departed spirits of Valerius Erotis.
EDCS-11400837.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Valerius Erotis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400838.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulia Ispes made this for her well-deserving husband, Marcus Valerius Florentiniano, who lived 60 years, 2 months, and 11 days.
EDCS-11400838.summary: This tombstone inscription records a dedication by Iulia Ispes to her husband, Marcus Valerius Florentiniano, and his lifespan, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400839.summary: This tombstone inscription records the lifespan of Venustus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400848.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Curatius Gluco made this for himself, his most holy wife Curatia Elpis, and his son Gaius Curatius Gluco, for himself and his family, freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants, on a plot of 15 feet, facing 14 feet.
EDCS-11400848.summary: This tombstone inscription is a dedication by Gaius Curatius Gluco for his family and descendants, specifying plot dimensions, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400849.translation: To the departed spirits. Father and Mother made this for their sweetest, well-deserving son Hymnus; he lived 27 years, 3 months, and 16 days.
EDCS-11400849.summary: This tombstone inscription records a dedication by parents for their son Hymnus and his lifespan, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400851.translation: To the departed spirits. Sabidius the slave lived 40 years; Conserva [made this] for her well-deserving wife.
EDCS-11400851.summary: This tombstone inscription records the lifespan of Sabidius, a slave, and a dedication by his wife Conserva, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400852.translation: To the departed spirits of Lucius Sergius Lysias.
EDCS-11400852.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Lucius Sergius Lysias, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11400856.translation: By decree of the decurions, [3]aesius Daphnus, Augustalis of Nuceria and [3] restored the temple of the Genius of Stabiae, which had been damaged by fallen marble.
EDCS-11400856.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple by an Augustalis of Nuceria, likely in the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11400858.translation: Of Publius Sabidius Pollio, urban praetor.
EDCS-11400858.summary: This inscription likely records the ownership or dedication of an object or monument by Publius Sabidius Pollio, an urban praetor, probably from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-11400859.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Iulius Probus, heir, made this for Publius Aelius Audax, a cavalryman of the emperor, of the turma of Marcus Ulpius Iustus, as deserving well. [Greek text follows: 'I desire a cascade of fortune or a cask of sense'].
EDCS-11400859.summary: This funerary inscription records the erection of a tomb by an heir for a deserving imperial cavalryman, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11400864.translation: To the departed spirits. Miniaria Prisca lived for 3 years, 2 months, 8 days. Gaius Miniarius Viator placed (this).
EDCS-11400864.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of a young girl, Miniaria Prisca, and was set up by Gaius Miniarius Viator, likely her father, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11400865.summary: This inscription records the short life of a child named Nepele, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11400866.summary: This inscription records the early death of a boy named Peregrinus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11400867.translation: To the departed spirits. For Marcus Perperna Charito.
EDCS-11400867.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication to Marcus Perperna Charito, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11400868.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gaius Pomponius Trofimus, to his deserving wife Plautia Tertulla.
EDCS-11400868.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication by a husband for his deserving wife, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11400869.translation: To the departed spirits. Sircius Victorinus, who lived 25 years, 3 months, 26 days. Sircia Fundas made this for her deserving brother. [Possibly more text follows].
EDCS-11400869.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Sircius Victorinus and was erected by his sister, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11400871.translation: Marcus Holconius Rufus, twice duovir for judging, Gaius Egnatius Postumus, once duovir for judging, by decree of the decurions, bought for 3000 sesterces the right to block lights, and undertook to have a private wall built up to the tiles for the colony Veneria Cornelia.
EDCS-11400871.summary: This inscription records a public works project in the colony of Corduba, concerning building rights and the construction of a wall, commissioned by two duovirs, likely in the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11400876.translation: To Quintus Sallustius, son of Publius, duovir for administering justice, quinquennial magistrate, by decree of the decurions, our patron.
EDCS-11400876.summary: This is a funerary or honorary inscription dedicated to Quintus Sallustius, who held significant civic offices.

EDCS-11400877.translation: Aulus Clodius Flaccus, son of Aulus, and Numerius Arcaeus Arellianus Caledus, son of Numerius, as duovirs for administering justice, by decree of the decurions, for equalizing measures.
EDCS-11400877.summary: This inscription records the action of two duovirs in charge of standardizing weights and measures, commissioned by a decree of the decurions.

EDCS-11400879.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified [Augustus]
EDCS-11400879.summary: This is an inscription dedicated to an emperor, likely Augustus himself or a divinized successor, honoring him.

EDCS-11400881.translation: To Spurius Turranius Proculus Gellianus, son of Lucius, grandson of Spurius, great-grandson of Lucius (tribe Fabia), prefect of the engineers, prefect of the Tiber River caretakers, prefect with praetorian power for administering justice in the city of Lavinium, father-leader of the people of Laurentum for making treaties with the Roman people, responsible for the sacred rites and origins of the Roman people and the Latin name, which are honored at Lavinium, priest of Jupiter, priest of Mars, Salian priest, augur, pontiff, prefect of a cohort of Gaetulians, military tribune of the 10th legion. The site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11400881.summary: This is a lengthy inscription detailing the extensive career of Spurius Turranius Proculus Gellianus, a prominent official in Lavinium, highlighting his religious and military roles, likely dating to the early Empire.

EDCS-11400883.translation: To Augusta Julia, daughter of Drusus, of deified Augustus, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11400883.summary: This inscription honors Augusta Julia, daughter of Drusus and likely related to the imperial family, by decree of the local council.

EDCS-11400885.translation: To the goddess Trivia, Marcus Fabius Secundus fulfilled his vow, with the permission of the aediles Aulus Hordionius Proculus and Tiberius Iulius Rufus.
EDCS-11400885.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to the goddess Trivia by Marcus Fabius Secundus, with the approval of local magistrates.

EDCS-11400886.translation: Lucius Sepunius Sandilianus, son of Lucius, and Marcus Herennius Epidianus, son of Aulus, as duovirs for administering justice, have taken care to have this made at their own expense.
EDCS-11400886.summary: This is an inscription indicating that two duovirs funded and commissioned the construction or repair of a public work.

EDCS-11400887.translation: Quintus Tullius, son of Quintus, and Marcus Cinnius, son of Marcus, as duovirs for administering justice; Gaius Naevius, son of Marcus, and Gaius Mammius, son of Lucius, as duovirs for overseeing public roads, buildings, and sacred matters, by decree of the decurions. It costs 672.5 sesterces.
EDCS-11400887.summary: This inscription records the expenditure of 672.5 sesterces for the maintenance of public infrastructure and sacred sites by two sets of duovirs, as ordered by the decurions.

EDCS-11400888.translation: Marcus Cinnius, son of Marcus, and Quintus Tullius, son of Quintus, as duovirs for administering justice; Gaius Naevius, son of Marcus, and Gaius Mammius, son of Lucius, as duovirs for overseeing public roads, buildings, and sacred matters, by decree of the decurions. It costs 672.[5] sesterces.
EDCS-11400888.summary: This inscription, similar to EDCS-11400887, details the cost of public works overseen by two pairs of duovirs, under the authority of the decurions.

EDCS-11400893.translation: Romulus, son of Mars, founded the city of Rome and reigned for thirty-eight years. He was the first leader, after killing Acron, king of the Caeninenses, and defeating their army, he consecrated the 'spolia opima' to Jupiter Feretrius. Having been received into the number of the gods, he was called Quirinus.
EDCS-11400893.summary: This inscription is a narrative account of the legendary founding of Rome by Romulus, detailing his reign, his first military victory, and his deification as Quirinus.

EDCS-11400901.translation: Gnaeus Melissaeus, son of Gnaeus, and Marcus Staio Rufus, son of Marcus, as IIviri for the second time, judging by right, had a basin made at public expense, which cost 5,250 HS.
EDCS-11400901.summary: This inscription records the provision of a public basin by two magistrates, identified as IIviri for the second time.

EDCS-11400902.translation: Marcus Nigidius Vaccula, at his own expense.
EDCS-11400902.summary: This is a very short inscription, likely recording a dedication or construction made at the expense of Marcus Nigidius Vaccula.

EDCS-11400904.translation: Marcus Tullius, son of Marcus, as duumvir for the third time, quinquennalis, augur, and tribune of the soldiers by the people, [dedicated] the temple of Fortuna Augusta at his own expense, with land and money.
EDCS-11400904.summary: This inscription records the dedication of the temple of Fortuna Augusta by Marcus Tullius, who held multiple civic and military offices.

EDCS-11400905.translation: Private area of Marcus Tullius, son of Marcus.
EDCS-11400905.summary: This inscription simply denotes a private property belonging to Marcus Tullius.

EDCS-11400906.translation: [Marcus Tullius], son of Marcus, duumvir for the third time, [quinquennalis], augur, tribune of the soldiers by the people.
EDCS-11400906.summary: This fragmentary inscription lists the titles and offices held by Marcus Tullius, likely relating to a public monument.

EDCS-11400908.translation: Agathemerus, Vettius Suavis, Caesius Primus, Pothus, Numitorius Anteros, [and] Lacutanus, first attendants of Fortuna Augusta, by order of Marcus Staius Rufus and Gnaeus Melissaeus, duumvirs judging by right, during the consulship of Publius Silicus and Lucius Volusius Saturninus.
EDCS-11400908.summary: This inscription records the action taken by attendants of Fortuna Augusta, by order of the duumvirs, during a specific consulship.

EDCS-11400909.translation: During the consulship of Taurus Statilius and Tiberius Plautius Aelianus, Lucius Statius Faustus, on behalf of the statue which he was supposed to erect according to the law of the attendants of Fortuna Augusta, on the recommendation of Quintus Pompeius Amethystus, quaestor, decreed two marble bases to be placed as a statue.
EDCS-11400909.summary: This inscription records the decree for two marble bases to be set up as a statue, initiated by Lucius Statius Faustus and approved by a quaestor, during a specific consulship.

EDCS-11400910.translation: Martialis, son of Caius Oli Primus; Manius Salarius Crocus; Primigenius, son of Caius Oli Primus, attendants of Fortuna Augusta, by order of Quintus Postumius Modestus and Caius Vibius Secundus, duumvirs judging by right, and of Caius Memmius Junius and Quintus Bruttius Balbus, aediles, during the consulship of Lucius Duviius and Publius Clodius.
EDCS-11400910.summary: This inscription lists attendants of Fortuna Augusta and the magistrates who ordered their actions during a specific consulship and aedilship.

EDCS-11400911.translation: Lucius Numisius Primus, Lucius Numisius Optatus, Lucius Melissaeus, Plocamus, attendants of Fortuna Augusta, by decree of the decurions and by order of Lucius Julius Ponticus and Publius Gavis Pastor, duumvirs judging by right, and of Quintus Poppaeus and Caius Vibius, aediles, during the consulship of Quintus Futius and Publius Calvisius.
EDCS-11400911.summary: This inscription details the actions of attendants of Fortuna Augusta, sanctioned by local council decree and specific magistrates, during a particular consulship and aedilship.

EDCS-11400912.text_edition: \n Maccio Auli filio Pupinia patrono suo et sibi Maccia\n
EDCS-11400912.translation: To Maccio, son of Aulus, his patron, and to himself.
EDCS-11400912.summary: This is a dedication to a patron and the dedicator himself, possibly on a tombstone or a dedication tablet.

EDCS-11400915.translation: Lucius Sepunius Sandilianus, son of Lucius, and Marcus Herennius Epidianus, son of Aulus, served as duoviri with judicial authority and saw to the construction of a school and a public clock at their own expense.
EDCS-11400915.summary: This inscription records the construction of a school and a public clock by two duoviri with judicial authority at their own expense, likely in the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-11400916.translation: To Marcus Claudius Marcellus, son of Caius, patron.
EDCS-11400916.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Claudius Marcellus, honoring him as a patron.

EDCS-11400917.translation: Marcus Holconius Rufus and Marcus Holconius Celer built the crypt, the tribunal, and the theatre at their own expense.
EDCS-11400917.summary: This inscription records the construction of a crypt, tribunal, and theatre by Marcus Holconius Rufus and Marcus Holconius Celer at their own expense, likely in Pompeii.

EDCS-11400930.translation: Numerius Popidius Celsinus, son of Numerius, rebuilt the temple of Isis, which had collapsed from an earthquake, from its foundations at his own expense; the decurions, on account of his liberality, enrolled him free of charge into their order when he was six years old.
EDCS-11400930.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding of the temple of Isis by Numerius Popidius Celsinus at his own expense and his subsequent honorary enrollment into the town council due to his generosity, dating to the period before the destruction of Pompeii in 79 AD.

EDCS-11400931.translation: Numerius Popidius Ampliatus, father, at his own expense.
EDCS-11400931.summary: This inscription indicates a construction or dedication undertaken at the expense of Numerius Popidius Ampliatus, referred to as 'father'.

EDCS-11400932.translation: From Numerius Popidius Ampliatus, son of Numerius; Numerius Popidius Celsinus; and Corelia Celsa.
EDCS-11400932.summary: This inscription lists three individuals, likely donors or beneficiaries associated with a monument or building, possibly related to the Popidius family.

EDCS-11400935.translation: Marcus Lucretius Decidianus Rufus, twice duovir, quinquennial duovir, pontifex, military tribune, chosen by the people, prefect of the arsenal, restored this; Marcus Decidius Pilonius Rufus restored it.
EDCS-11400935.summary: This inscription records the restoration of something by Marcus Lucretius Decidianus Rufus, who held several high civic and religious offices, and Marcus Decidius Pilonius Rufus.

EDCS-11400945.translation: Caius Cuspius Pansa, son of Caius, father, duovir with judicial authority, quinquennial duovir for the fourth time, prefect with judicial authority, by decree of the decurions, according to the law of Petronius.
EDCS-11400945.summary: This inscription records the achievements and offices held by Caius Cuspius Pansa, including his roles as duovir and quinquennial duovir, mandated by a decree of the decurions.

EDCS-11400976.translation: Publius Stallius Agatho, minister, by decree of the decurions, when Emperor Caesar was consul for the ninth time and Marcus Silanus was consul; it was renewed when Publius Alfienus and Publius Vinicius were consuls, by order of Marcus Pomponius Marcellus and Lucius Valerius Flaccus, duoviri with judicial authority, and Lucius Obellius Lucretianus and Aulus Perennius Merulīnus, duoviri for the care of public roads, buildings, and sacred places.
EDCS-11400976.summary: This inscription details the renewal of a public work by the duoviri responsible for roads, buildings, and sacred places, mentioning consuls and imperial dating.

EDCS-11400979.translation: [---] Sitti, slave of Marcus; [---]s, slave of Sornius Titus; [---]a, slave of Volusius Titus; ministers of Mercury and Maia; sacred [rite] by order of [---] Celeris.
EDCS-11400979.summary: This inscription lists several slaves acting as ministers for the deities Mercury and Maia and records a sacred rite ordered by someone named Celeris.

EDCS-11400980.translation: Gratus, son of Arrius Messius, the freedman Inventus, servant of Istacidius Memor, acting on the order of the council of decurions, by decree of Marcel(li?).
EDCS-11400980.summary: This inscription records the action taken by a servant of the emperor, possibly ordered by a local magistrate, based on a council decree.

EDCS-11400983.translation: Lucius Caecilius Felix, Quintus Lollius Felix, Quintus Arrius Hieronicus, servants of the emperor, by order of the council of decurions, under the duumvirs with judicial power Marcus Pomponius Marcellus and Lucius Valerius Flaccus, and the duumvirs responsible for public works Aulus Perennius Merulinus and Lucius Obellius Lucretianus, during the consulship of Gaius Caesar and Lucius Paulus.
EDCS-11400983.summary: This inscription records a public works project or dedication undertaken by municipal officials during the consulship of Gaius Caesar and Lucius Paulus, with servants of the emperor involved.

EDCS-11400985.translation: In the consulship of Sextus Aelius and Gaius Sentius, by order of Marcus Staius Rufus and Gnaeus Melissus Aprius, duumvirs with judicial power for the second time, this was renewed.
EDCS-11400985.summary: This inscription records the renewal of something, possibly a building or a contract, under the duumvirs with judicial power during the consulship of Sextus Aelius and Gaius Sentius.

EDCS-11401000.translation: Narcissus, freedman of Popidius Moschus; Nymphodotus, freedman of Iucundus, servants of the emperor, by order of the council of decurions, by the order of Publius Vettius Celer and Decimus Alfidius.
EDCS-11401000.summary: This inscription records an action taken by servants of the emperor, under the authority of the council of decurions and local magistrates, Publius Vettius Celer and Decimus Alfidius.

EDCS-11401018.translation: Quintus Lollius Scylax and his mother Calidia Antiochis, and Marcus Calidius Nasta, have willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow to Jupiter.
EDCS-11401018.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicating a fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter by members of the Lollius and Calidius families.

EDCS-11401022.summary: This is a short inscription indicating that Pacuvius Erasistratus dedicated something in response to a vision.

EDCS-11401037.translation: To Marcus Holconius Celer, son of Marcus, priest of the deified Augustus.
EDCS-11401037.summary: This is an honorary inscription dedicated to Marcus Holconius Celer for his role as a priest of the deified Augustus.

EDCS-11401038.translation: ... Celer, ... priest of the deified Augustus, ... duumvir with judicial power, quinquennial.
EDCS-11401038.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription honoring an individual named Celer who served as a priest of the deified Augustus and a quinquennial duumvir with judicial power.

EDCS-11401043.translation: Marcus Lucretius Decidianus Rufus, son of Lucius, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11401043.summary: This inscription records a decision made by the council of decurions concerning Marcus Lucretius Decidianus Rufus.

EDCS-11401051.translation: To Titus Sornius Velox, son of Titus, of the tribe Velina, prefect of the artisans. Aulus Clodius Flaccus, duumvir with judicial power twice and quinquennial, gave this place as a gift, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11401051.summary: This inscription records Aulus Clodius Flaccus, a prominent local official, dedicating a place by decree of the decurions, honoring Titus Sornius Velox.

EDCS-11401084.translation: Marcus Cerrinius Restitutus Augustalis, the place was given by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11401084.summary: This inscription records a plot of land granted by decree of the decurions to Marcus Cerrinius Restitutus Augustalis.

EDCS-11401086.translation: To Aulus Veius, son of Marcus, twice chief magistrate with judicial power, twice quinquennial censor, tribune of the soldiers appointed by the people, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11401086.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Aulus Veius, honoring his various civic and military offices, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-11401087.translation: From the decree of the decurions, for Marcus Porcius, son of Marcus: 25 feet in front, 25 feet into the land.
EDCS-11401087.summary: This inscription details a land division or grant of 25 feet in front and 25 feet deep, originating from a decree of the decurions for Marcus Porcius.

EDCS-11401088.translation: To Mammia, daughter of Publius, public priestess, a place for burial was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11401088.summary: This inscription records the grant of a burial plot to the public priestess Mammia, daughter of Publius, by decree of the decurions.

EDCS-11401090.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Aulus Bucius Victor.

EDCS-11401092.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the incomplete lifespan of Apta Buccia.

EDCS-11401097.translation: To Numerius, son of Numerius, from Istacidius, son of Numerius, Menophilus.
EDCS-11401097.summary: This inscription is a dedication or identifies individuals, possibly related to a tomb or monument, for Numerius, son of Numerius, and others.

EDCS-11401101.translation: Melissea, daughter of Spurius, known as Asiatice, lived 17 years.
EDCS-11401101.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Melissea Asiatice.

EDCS-11401102.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the short lifespan of Fortunatus.

EDCS-11401108.translation: By the authority of the Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, Titus Suedius Clemens, tribune, after hearing the cases and making the measurements, restored to the public property of the Pompeians lands possessed by private individuals.
EDCS-11401108.summary: This inscription records the restoration of public lands to the people of Pompeii by Titus Suedius Clemens, a tribune, under the authority of Emperor Vespasian, after legal proceedings and measurements.

EDCS-11401109.translation: To Titus Terentius Felix Maior, son of Titus, of the Menenia tribe, aedile; a public plot of land was granted to him, and 2000 sesterces, by his wife Fabia Sabina, daughter of Probus.
EDCS-11401109.summary: This is a funerary monument commemorating Titus Terentius Felix Maior, an aedile, with a plot of land and money granted by his wife.

EDCS-11401112.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription identifying Lucceia Ianuaria.

EDCS-11401116.translation: To Gaius Calventius Quietus, an Augustalis; the honor of a bisellium was granted to him by decree of the decurions and with the consent of the people, on account of his generosity.
EDCS-11401116.summary: This inscription records the posthumous honor of a bisellium granted to Gaius Calventius Quietus for his generosity.

EDCS-11401117.translation: To Numerius Istacidius Helenus, pagan of the pagus Augusti; to Numerius Istacidius Ianuarius, and Mesonia Satulla; 15 feet in front, 15 feet.
EDCS-11401117.summary: This inscription appears to mark a plot of land granted to several individuals, possibly for funerary use.

EDCS-11401120.translation: Naevoleia Tyche, freedwoman of Lucius, made this monument for herself and for Gaius Munatius Faustus, an Augustalis and pagan, to whom the decurions by consent of the people decreed a bisellium for his merits, and for her freedmen and freedwomen, and for Gaius Munatius Faustus, during her lifetime.
EDCS-11401120.summary: This is a funerary monument built by Naevoleia Tyche for herself, her freedmen, and for Gaius Munatius Faustus, who was honored with a bisellium.

EDCS-11401121.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the name and age of Gaius Munatius Atimetus.

EDCS-11401122.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the name and age of Salvius, a young boy.

EDCS-11401123.translation: To Gnaeus Vibrius Saturninus, son of Quintus, of the Falerna tribe, by his freedman Callistus.
EDCS-11401123.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a monument to Gnaeus Vibrius Saturninus by his freedman Callistus.

EDCS-11401126.translation: To Marcus Alleius Luccius, father of Libella, aedile, duovir, quinquennalis prefect, and to Marcus Alleius Libella, his son, decurion, who lived 17 years; a plot for the monument was publicly granted; Alleia Decimilla, daughter of Marcus, public priestess of Ceres, saw to its construction for her husband and son. // To Marcus Alleius Luccius, father of Libella, aedile, duovir, quinquennalis prefect, and to Marcus Alleius Libella, his son, decurion, who lived 17 years; a plot for the monument was publicly granted; Alleia Decimilla, daughter of Marcus, public priestess of Ceres, saw to its construction for her husband and son.
EDCS-11401126.summary: This is a funerary monument commemorating Marcus Alleius Luccius, an official, and his son Marcus Alleius Libella, a decurion who died young, built by his wife and mother Alleia Decimilla.

EDCS-11401131.translation: To Numerius Velasius Gratus, who lived 12 years.
EDCS-11401131.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the name and age of Numerius Velasius Gratus.

EDCS-11401137.translation: Marcus Afillius Primogenes, freedman of Marcus, lived 20 years.
EDCS-11401137.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age of Marcus Afillius Primogenes, who lived for 20 years.

EDCS-11401140.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age of Caius Cornelius Lotus, who lived for 50 years.

EDCS-11401143.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age of Daphine, who lived for 22 years.

EDCS-11401145.translation: Caius Novellius Natalis, magistrate of the Augustales.
EDCS-11401145.summary: This inscription records Caius Novellius Natalis, who held the office of magister Augusti, likely a local official associated with the imperial cult.

EDCS-11401148.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age of Primigenius, who lived for 7 years.

EDCS-11401149.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age of Saturninus, who lived for 1 year and 7 months.

EDCS-11401151.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age of Terentia Prisca, who lived for 10 years.

EDCS-11401152.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age of Tettia Prepusa, who lived for 20 years.

EDCS-11401153.translation: The Baths of Marcus Crassus Frugi, with sea water and baths with fresh water, Ianuarius the freedman.
EDCS-11401153.summary: This inscription names the baths of Marcus Crassus Frugi and specifies their water sources (sea and fresh water), with Ianuarius the freedman associated with their operation.

EDCS-11401154.translation: Lucius Avianius Flaccus Pontianus, son of Lucius, of the Menenia tribe, and Quintus Spedius Firmus, son of Quintus, of the Menenia tribe, as dual magistrates with judicial authority, paved the road from the milestone to the wagon drivers' area, where the territory of the Pompeiani is, at their own expense.
EDCS-11401154.summary: This inscription records the paving of a road by two high-ranking local magistrates, Lucius Avianius Flaccus Pontianus and Quintus Spedius Firmus, at their own expense.

EDCS-11401157.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Caesia Egloge.

EDCS-11401161.translation: To Tintiria, daughter of Publius, for herself and her mother Istacidia, daughter of Marcus.
EDCS-11401161.summary: A funerary dedication by Tintiria for herself and her mother.

EDCS-11401167.translation: Aulus Clodius Flaccus, son of Aulus, of the Menenia tribe, twice chief magistrate, twice quinquennial, military tribune appointed by the people. In his first duumvirate, for the Apollinaris games in the forum, he provided a procession, bulls, bull-catchers, assistants, pontoon-workers, 3 pairs of gladiators, boxers and wrestling teams for all, pantomimes and actors for all, and Pylades, for 10,000 sesterces in cash for public use. In his second duumvirate, for the Apollinaris games in the forum, he provided a procession, bulls, bull-catchers, assistants, boxers, wrestling teams, on the following day alone in the spectacles, 30 pairs of athletes, 5 pairs of gladiators, and 35 pairs of gladiators, and hunts with bulls, bull-catchers, boars, bears, and other varied hunts with his colleague. In his third duumvirate, games with the first faction, with added actors, with his colleague. Clodia, daughter of Aulus, erected this monument at her own expense for herself and her family.
EDCS-11401167.summary: A lengthy inscription detailing the extensive public games and spectacles provided by Aulus Clodius Flaccus during his three terms as duumvir, with a dedication by his wife Clodia.

EDCS-11401171.translation: Primigenia lived for 16 years and 3 months.
EDCS-11401171.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Primigenia.

EDCS-11401172.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Traebia Fortunata.

EDCS-11401173.translation: Lucius Vottonius Saturninus Drips, son of Lucius, of the Collina tribe, lived for 26 years and served for 5 years; he ordered this to be set up by his will.
EDCS-11401173.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and military service of Lucius Vottonius Saturninus Drips, who requested it be placed by his will.

EDCS-11500010.translation: To the Shades Below. To Titus Trebius Priscus, veteran signifer of the Praetorian fleet from Misenum, who served for 26 years and lived for 60 years. Valeria Festa, his most devoted wife, and Titus Trebius Priscus, his son, set this up for their dearest and well-deserving father. He was by nation a Bessian. For himself and his family.
EDCS-11500010.summary: A funerary inscription for Titus Trebius Priscus, a veteran signifer, set up by his wife and son, noting his ethnicity.

EDCS-11500011.text_edition: \n Marco Virtio Marci filio Menenia Cerauno aedili IIviro iure dicundo praefecto fabrum Vviro cui decuriones ob munificentiam eius quod equom sic magnum posuerat et denarios populo dedicatione eius dederat duumviratum gratuitum dederunt Nuceriae\n
EDCS-11500011.translation: To Marcus Virtius Ceraunus, son of Marcus, of the Menenia tribe, aedile, duumvir with judicial authority, prefect of the artisans, quinquennial magistrate, to whom the town councillors, because of his munificence, for he had provided a great horse and had given denarii to the people at its dedication, granted the duumvirate without payment. In Nuceria.
EDCS-11500011.summary: An honorary inscription from Nuceria honoring Marcus Virtius Ceraunus for his munificence, specifically for providing a horse and distributing money, which led to his appointment as duumvir without charge.

EDCS-11500014.translation: Marcus Genicius Menecrates, possibly an Augustalis, for himself and his wife Accia Maxima, and his family.
EDCS-11500014.summary: A funerary dedication by Marcus Genicius Menecrates for himself, his wife, and his family.

EDCS-11500015.translation: To the Shades Below. To Eutychus the Dancer, who lived for approximately 85 years. Myro made this for the deserving one.
EDCS-11500015.summary: A funerary epitaph for Eutychus the Dancer, set up by Myro, and noting his approximate age.

EDCS-11500016.translation: To the spirits of the departed. The sodales (members of a collegium) made this for Aelius Cosmus.
EDCS-11500016.summary: A funerary dedication by a collegium to one of its members, Aelius Cosmus.

EDCS-11500017.translation: To Publius Aius, son of Publius, of the Menenia tribe, in accordance with his will.
EDCS-11500017.summary: A funerary dedication to Publius Aius, likely funded by his will.

EDCS-11500019.translation: Allia Blesilla, a woman of rare example, who lived 26 years, 2 months, and 12 days, lies here; her husband made this for his most sweet wife.
EDCS-11500019.summary: A funerary epitaph for Allia Blesilla, recording her age and erected by her husband.

EDCS-11500020.translation: Marcus Calavius Flaccus, son of Marcus, of the Menenia tribe; Avillia Betua, mother, daughter of Lucius; [---]; Phialus, freedman of the patrons; [---] and his family. Marcus Calavius [---], son of Aulus; Avillia [---]
EDCS-11500020.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription for members of the Calavius and Avillia families, possibly related to a collegium given the mention of 'col(onorum)'.

EDCS-11500021.translation: For Calpurnia Severa, daughter of Lucius, a most rare woman, who lived 23 years and 4 months. Viria Prima, her most unhappy mother, who survived her, made this for her incomparable daughter, whose fame and piety live forever.
EDCS-11500021.summary: A funerary epitaph for Calpurnia Severa, recording her age and erected by her grieving mother.

EDCS-11500022.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Constantius Threptus lived 72 years. His freedman erected this for him, deserving well.
EDCS-11500022.summary: A funerary dedication by a freedman for his former master, Quintus Constantius Threptus.

EDCS-11500023.text_edition: \n Lucio Fulvio Marci filio Menenia Cano Paquia uxsor sic arbitratu Luci Renni Quinti filii Flavia Fausti coniugi fecit\n
EDCS-11500023.translation: To Lucius Fulvius Cano, son of Marcus, of the Menenia tribe; Paquia, his wife, has made this by the judgment of Lucius Rennius, son of Quintus; for his conjugal partner.
EDCS-11500023.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Fulvius Cano, erected by his wife Paquia.

EDCS-11500024.translation: Herma lived 45 years. His companion made this.
EDCS-11500024.summary: A funerary epitaph for Herma, recording his age and erected by his companion.

EDCS-11500025.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iucundus lived 8 years, 8 months, and 12 days. Quintus Paccius Iucundus made this for his son.
EDCS-11500025.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young boy named Iucundus, erected by his father, Quintus Paccius Iucundus.

EDCS-11500026.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Mincullius Felix lived 35 years and 3 months.
EDCS-11500026.summary: A funerary dedication for Mincullius Felix, stating his age.

EDCS-11500027.translation: To Gaius Novellius Rufus, son of Gaius.
EDCS-11500027.summary: This is an epitaph for Gaius Novellius Rufus.

EDCS-11500028.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Pontius Callinicus lived 8 years, 11 months, and 9 days. Lucius Pontius Fortunatus and Coelia Secundilla, his most unhappy parents, made this.
EDCS-11500028.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Pontius Callinicus, who died at a young age, set up by his parents.

EDCS-11500029.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Maecius Prosdoxus lived 60 years.
EDCS-11500029.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Maecius Prosdoxus, who lived to be 60 years old.

EDCS-11500030.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Gaius Sestullius Felix made this for his well-deserving wife, Rubria Felicissima, who lived 32 years.
EDCS-11500030.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Rubria Felicissima, who lived 32 years, set up by her husband.

EDCS-11500031.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Baria Parasceve made this for her well-deserving husband, Publius Senecio Maximus.
EDCS-11500031.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Senecio Maximus, set up by his wife.

EDCS-11500032.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Antistius Donatus made this for his well-deserving wife, Sergia Vibias Maxima, who lived 47 years and 11 months.
EDCS-11500032.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sergia Vibias Maxima, who lived 47 years and 11 months, set up by her husband.

EDCS-11500034.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Cornelia Festa made this for her good husband, Marcus Ulpius Felix, who lived 48 years, 1 month, and 23 days, a holy and incomparable husband.
EDCS-11500034.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Ulpius Felix, who lived 48 years, 1 month, and 23 days, set up by his wife.

EDCS-11500042.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-11500042.translation: Her most devoted husband made this for his well-deserving wife, Felix Orestilia, who happily married Crispinus Euodius, but died unhappily in childbirth. Against (our) wishes.
EDCS-11500042.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Felix Orestilia, who died in childbirth, set up by her husband.

EDCS-11500044.translation: Sacred to Silvanus. Marcus Vicirius Rufus fulfilled his vow, and I have placed this marble statue for you, as I was permitted to restore the household gods of the Iuniani.
EDCS-11500044.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Silvanus, by Marcus Vicirius Rufus.

EDCS-11500045.translation: To Gaius Caesar, son of the deified [Augustus], nephew of the deified [Augustus], pontifex, consul, prince of youth [---]
EDCS-11500045.summary: This is a dedication to Gaius Caesar, son of Augustus, likely from a public monument.

EDCS-11500046.translation: To the deified Commodus, Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul, by public decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11500046.summary: This inscription is a posthumous dedication to the deified emperor Commodus, likely erected by the local senatorial body.

EDCS-11500047.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul, father of the country, the best and most valiant prince, from the colony of Veneria Livia Augusta Alexandriana Abellinatium.
EDCS-11500047.summary: This is a dedication from the colony of Abellinum to the emperor Gordian III, celebrating his titles and virtues.

EDCS-11500050.translation: To Marcus Antonius Rufinus, a most distinguished man, for the merit of his labors and studies, who built our city's basilica from its foundation with his own money, to our best patron, the most splendid order of the colony of Abellinum.
EDCS-11500050.summary: This is a dedication by the local council of Abellinum to Marcus Antonius Rufinus for funding the construction of the city basilica.

EDCS-11500055.translation: To Tatianus, Gaius Iulius Rufinianus Ablabius Tatianus, a most distinguished man, son of Rufinianus the orator, patron of the treasury, admitted among the consulars by the judgment of the deified Constantine, governor of the province of Asia, corrector of Tuscia and Umbria, consular of Aemilia and Liguria, pontiff of Vesta Mater and chief priest in the college of pontiffs, consular of Campania; the most splendid order and the people of Abellinum decreed that a statue be erected to him for his outstanding benevolence, religious devotion, and integrity towards them.
EDCS-11500055.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription for a statue erected by the council and people of Abellinum to Gaius Iulius Rufinianus Ablabius Tatianus, honoring his distinguished career and public service.

EDCS-11500059.translation: To Lucius Pinarius Natta, son of Lucius, of the Galeria tribe, who served as aedile, duumvir, quaestor, tribune of soldiers, prefect of Berenicis. Marcus Bivellius, son of Caius, of the Galeria tribe.
EDCS-11500059.summary: This is a dedication by Marcus Bivellius to Lucius Pinarius Natta, listing his various magistracies and military service.

EDCS-11500061.translation: To Septimia Silva(?), daughter of Lucius, and to Marcus Allius Rufus, son of Marcus, of the Menenia tribe, who served as prefect of the armed forces, censor, quaestor, tribune of soldiers, from the people, an equestrian Roman. The decurions admitted him to their order free of charge and requested that their voluntary action be recorded by decree.
EDCS-11500061.summary: This inscription records the free admission of Marcus Allius Rufus into the local council by the decurions, noting his prior public service.

EDCS-11500062.translation: To Ulpia Antigonidis, who lived for two years and twenty-one days. Iulius Antigonus, a veteran of our Augustus, made this for his most sweet daughter.
EDCS-11500062.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Ulpia Antigonidis, set up by her father, a veteran of the emperor.

EDCS-11500064.translation: Decimus Cottius Flaccus, son of Decimus, of the Galeria tribe, aedile, quaestor, praetor, duumvir, censor. Manlia, his wife.
EDCS-11500064.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Decimus Cottius Flaccus, who held multiple civic offices, set up by his wife.

EDCS-11500067.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gaius Mammercius Ianuarius, son of Spurius, quaestor, aedile, praetor, duumvir, quaestor of the alimentary fund, and for Paccia Lucretiana. Publius Paccius Ianuarius, for his natural son, and Mamercia Grapte, his mother, made this for their most devoted son and relative.
EDCS-11500067.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Mammercius Ianuarius and Paccia Lucretiana, set up by the former's son and mother.

EDCS-11500077.translation: [... free man?] Faustus, Augustalis, for himself and his wife Vettia, freedwoman, and Vettia Secunda, freedwoman of Publius, his sister.
EDCS-11500077.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription likely set up by Faustus for himself and his wife and sister.

EDCS-11500079.translation: To Marcus Ofillius Salvius Augustalis, freedman of Marcus, and Fabia Egloge, freedwoman of Marcus.
EDCS-11500079.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication to a freedman and his freedwoman partner, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500080.translation: Marcus Paccius Narcissus, freedman of Marcus, freedman of Claudius, of the Augustalis tribe, made this for himself, for his well-deserving wife Paccia Fortunata, and for his family; the space was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11500080.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by a freedman for himself, his wife, and family, with the land provided by a local government decision, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500081.translation: Egnatius Mercurialis, son of Gaius, of the Galeria tribe, master, made this monument for himself and for his wife Cottia Secunda, daughter of Decimus; it will not pass to an heir; for Egnatia Glaphyra, freedwoman of Gaius, and for a certain Nol[---] traveler, may it not be burdensome for Ten[---].
EDCS-11500081.summary: This funerary inscription, though partially reconstructed, records a man setting up a monument for himself, his wife, and a freedwoman, with a clause preventing it from passing to heirs, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500084.translation: Aulus Ahius Salvius, freedman of Aulus, for himself and for Atilia Salvia, freedwoman of Gaius, with a frontage of 16 feet and a depth of 18 feet in the field.
EDCS-11500084.summary: This is a property boundary inscription for a burial plot measuring 16 by 18 feet, set up by a freedman for himself and his freedwoman partner, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500087.translation: Marcus Kadius Celer, son of Gaius, of the Galeria tribe, made this alive for his father Gaius Kadion Cimbros, freedman of Gaius, for his brother Gaius Kadion Rufus, son of Gaius, of the Galeria tribe, and for himself.
EDCS-11500087.summary: This inscription records a man setting up a burial monument for his father, brother, and himself while still alive, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500092.translation: Messia, daughter of Gaius, made this monument for herself and her family, for Gaius Oviedius Tumulus, son of Titus, and for Gaius Oviedius Lupercus, son of Gaius, and for Titus Oviedius Tumulus, son of Gaius.
EDCS-11500092.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by a woman for herself and her family, listing three male relatives who are recipients of the monument, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500094.translation: Ofilia Firmilla, freedwoman of Lucius, made this for herself and her family, with a frontage of 6 feet and a depth of 18 feet.
EDCS-11500094.summary: This is a property boundary inscription for a burial plot measuring 6 by 18 feet, set up by a freedwoman for herself and her family, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500096.translation: For Pompeia, daughter of Publius, wife of Gaius Furius [---]polia. The mother made this while alive, which is the most miserable thing.
EDCS-11500096.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication made by a mother for her daughter, Pompeia, while the mother was still alive, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500097.translation: Poppaea Procca, daughter of Gaius, lived for 9 years and 4 months.
EDCS-11500097.summary: This is a brief funerary epitaph recording the short lifespan of a young girl named Poppaea Procca, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500102.translation: To the departed spirits. Statilia Prota, for herself and her family, with a frontage of 16 feet and a depth of 18 feet.
EDCS-11500102.summary: This is a funerary monument inscription with a property boundary of 16 by 18 feet, set up by Statilia Prota for herself and her family, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500131.translation: To Numerius Marcius Plaetorius Celer, son of Numerius, of the Galeria tribe, quaestor, twice duovir, centurion of the VII Gemina legion, centurion of the XVI Flavia Firma legion, honored with gifts by the deified Trajan in the Parthian War with a mural crown, neck torcs, armlets, and phalerae, centurion of the III Gallica legion, centurion of the XIIII Gemina Martia Victrix legion, centurion of the VII Claudia Pia Fidelis legion, centurion of the I Adiutrix Pia Fidelis legion, primus pilus of the same legion, commander of the companies serving on the Pontus, tribune of the 3rd cohort of the vigiles, patron of the colony, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11500131.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Numerius Marcius Plaetorius Celer, detailing his extensive military career and civic honors, likely set up by the decurions of a colony.

EDCS-11500137.translation: Caius Julius Severus and Marcus Junius Rufinus Sabinianus serving as consuls, on the third day before the Nones of December, in Abella, in the basilica. Marcus Munatius Priscianus, son of Marcus, of the Galeria tribe, Titus Egnatius Rufus, son of Titus, of the Palatina tribe, Clemens Herennius, son of Titus, of the Galeria tribe, and Numerius Rufus, son of Numerius, of the Galeria tribe, Aulus Fuficius Priscus, son of Aulus, of the Galeria tribe, acted as scribes. By decree of the decurions, in view of the requests of all the men of the order, that the funeral of this most excellent youth should be attended to with the greatest solicitude, it was resolved as follows: [---]
EDCS-11500137.summary: This inscription records a decree by the decurions of Abella concerning funeral arrangements for a distinguished young man, with details about the scribes present, likely dating to the consulship mentioned.

EDCS-11500138.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Calidius Epaphroditus, master of the Augustales, quaestor, while living, for himself and for his wife Critonia Amaryllis, this monument or tomb does not follow the heir.
EDCS-11500138.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus Calidius Epaphroditus and his wife Critonia Amaryllis, indicating it was built during his lifetime and will not pass to an heir.

EDCS-11500139.translation: To ... Cilo, son of Titus, of the Sergia tribe, formerly prefect, [he] took care of the construction of the swimming pool and served as duovir a second time, as quinquennalis, with his own money. [---] the statue of the colonist and inhabitant.
EDCS-11500139.summary: This inscription honors Cilo, son of Titus, for his public works, specifically the construction of a swimming pool, and for his service as duovir and quinquennalis, likely set up in a public space.

EDCS-11500145.translation: To Numerius Pettius Rufus, son of Numerius, of the Galeria tribe, twice duovir, quaestor of the alimenta, quaestor of the public treasury, curator of the grain supply. The devotees of Jupiter, in recognition of his merits, have been granted this place by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11500145.summary: This inscription honors Numerius Pettius Rufus for his public service, particularly in financial and supply roles, with the place granted by the decurions likely referring to a burial plot.

EDCS-11500146.translation: To Numerius Plaetorius Onirus, Augustalis, bisellarius, honored with the insignia of a decurion. The people of Abella, by a contribution of bronze, because he increased from his own funds the money for the grain supply by 10,000 sesterces and provided curtains for the theatre with all its decoration, have granted this place by decree of the decurions, at his own expense.
EDCS-11500146.summary: This inscription honors Numerius Plaetorius Onirus for his generosity in augmenting the grain supply funds and providing theater decorations, with the place granted by the decurions from public contributions.

EDCS-11500171.translation: To Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 13th time. [---]
EDCS-11500171.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Hadrian, listing his imperial titles and consulships, likely part of a public monument.

EDCS-11500183.translation: To Gnaeus Petronius Strabo Probus Iustus Clarissimus Vir, legate of the two legions XIII Gemina and VIII Augusta Severiana Alexandriana, proconsul of the province of Crete, legate of the province of Achaia, praetor, commissioner of trusts, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of the province of Africa, curator of the public affairs of the Ardeates, quattuorvir for the care of roads. Marcus Terentius Aelianus, centurion of the VIII Augusta legion, his most just president.
EDCS-11500183.summary: This inscription honors Gnaeus Petronius Strabo Probus Iustus Clarissimus Vir, detailing his extensive senatorial and military career, likely commissioned by Marcus Terentius Aelianus.

EDCS-11500187.translation: To Titus Rutilius Varus, military tribune of the V Macedonica legion, prefect of the ala of the Bosporani, quaestor of the deified Vespasian, curule aedile, companion of the emperor, patron, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11500187.summary: This inscription honors Titus Rutilius Varus for his military and administrative career, including service as a tribune, prefect, quaestor, and aedile, and declares him a patron of the community.

EDCS-11500190.translation: To Titus Flavius, freedman of the Emperor, procurator of the deified Vespasian and the deified Titus. The Augustales, by decree of the decurions, have granted this place.
EDCS-11500190.summary: This inscription honors Titus Flavius, a freedman of the emperors Vespasian and Titus who served as a procurator, with the place granted by the Augustales and decurions.

EDCS-11500191.translation: To Lucius Curiatius, son of Lucius, flamen of the deified Augustus, first-rank centurion, military tribune of the II legion, prefect of the camp, prefect of craftsmen, by the arbitration of Hyacinthus, his freedman.
EDCS-11500191.summary: This inscription records the career of Lucius Curiatius, a military officer and priest, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500192.translation: To the gods of the departed. To Gaius Germanis Valentinus, son of Gaius, soldier of the XV Apollinaris legion, soldier of the 11th urban cohort, century of Albius (?), veteran of the same cohort, settled by the deified Nerva. Gaius Germanis Florenus, his freedman and also son, and Germania [his wife/mother?].
EDCS-11500192.summary: This funerary inscription honors Gaius Germanis Valentinus, a soldier and veteran, likely dating to the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500194.translation: To the gods of the departed. For Quintus Antistius Comus, Comianus, aedile of the colony, and Julia Eut[--], his wife.
EDCS-11500194.summary: This funerary inscription is for Quintus Antistius Comus, an aedile of a colony, and his wife, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500195.translation: To Quintus Caesius Fistulanus, son of Quintus, of the Falernian tribe, curator of public works appointed by the deified Vespasian Augustus, aedile, quaestor, duovir, prefect of craftsmen. Cisionia Firmilla, daughter of Lucius, his wife, at her own expense, and Quintus Caesius Optandus, her son, and Cisionia Firmilla. The space was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11500195.summary: This inscription records the honors and burial plot granted to Quintus Caesius Fistulanus, a prominent local official, and his family, by his wife and son, likely dating to the late 1st century CE.

EDCS-11500197.translation: To Lucius Calvidius Clemens, son of Lucius, aged 20, designated duovir. To Lucius Calvidius Felix, freedman of Lucius, Augustalis. The space was granted to both by decree of the decurions, 130 feet in front and 25 feet in depth, which the son should have made for his father, the father made for his son.
EDCS-11500197.summary: This inscription records a burial plot granted by the decurions for Lucius Calvidius Clemens and his freedman Lucius Calvidius Felix, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500202.translation: To the gods of the departed. For Publius Sextilius Rufus, son of Publius, of the Falernian tribe, twice aedile, quinquennial duovir, decurion, enrolled from the veterans of Nola. For Aufidia Maxima, daughter of Statius, his mother. To Lucius Petronius Vitricus, son of Lucius, of the Falernian tribe, decurion of Nola. By his will, 4,000 sesterces, by the arbitration of Fidus, his freedman.
EDCS-11500202.summary: This funerary inscription honors Publius Sextilius Rufus, a local official in Pompeii and Nola, and his mother and stepfather, funded by his will and likely dating to the 1st century CE.

EDCS-11500203.translation: To Lucius Sitrius Modestus, son of Marcus, of the Falernian tribe, aedile, prefect of craftsmen, by decree of the decurions. The space was granted to him, his father, and his mother, 50 feet in front.
EDCS-11500203.summary: This inscription records a burial plot granted by decree of the decurions for Lucius Sitrius Modestus, his father, and mother, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500204.translation: To Marcus Statius Fisus Serenus, son of Marcus, of the Palatine tribe, Rutilius Caesianus, duovir, augur, lived 31 years, 11 months, 18 days. You see this marble of a noble youth, a sad monument. The father consecrated it for the supreme rites, and the brother, overcome with wondrous devotion, lamenting his lost brother, whom the whole crowd mourned with new laments, because he perished, a flower of beauty, modesty, and simplicity. Traveler, whoever reads this poem, and as he deserves, lend a tear to his soul. His most wretched father.
EDCS-11500204.summary: This funerary inscription, composed in elegiac verse, commemorates Marcus Statius Fisus Serenus, a young man of noble character, by his father and brother, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500213.translation: Nardus, the modest poet, is covered by this tomb.
EDCS-11500213.summary: This brief epitaph identifies Nardus as a poet and states he is buried here, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500215.translation: To the gods of the departed. To Aelia Festa, a deserving wife. Quintus Lutatius Ianuarius, for himself and his family, 8 feet in front, 13 feet in depth.
EDCS-11500215.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Quintus Lutatius Ianuarius to his well-deserving wife, Aelia Festa, and for his family, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500216.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius(?) Annius Bassianus made this for his most holy and most reverent wife.
EDCS-11500216.summary: A funerary dedication by Lucius(?) Annius Bassianus to his wife, dating likely to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500218.translation: To the departed spirits. Urbanus made this for his dearest mother Aurelia Amplicata, well deserving.
EDCS-11500218.summary: A funerary dedication by Urbanus to his mother Aurelia Amplicata, dating likely to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500220.translation: For Titilia Prota, daughter of Lucius, to the departed spirits. For Gaius Bruttius Varus Protus, son of Gaius, who lived 6 years, 11 months, and 20 days. Gaius Varius Fortunatus, an unhappy father, [made this] for his most dutiful son.
EDCS-11500220.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaius Bruttius Varus Protus, who died at a young age, dedicated by his father, Gaius Varius Fortunatus, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500222.translation: For Gaius Cavilius Apollonius, son of Gaius, freedman, as patron, in accordance with his will.
EDCS-11500222.summary: A funerary dedication for Gaius Cavilius Apollonius, made posthumously by testament, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500223.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Clo(dia) Verecunda made this for her most excellent husband Decimus Clodius Herculanius, well deserving.
EDCS-11500223.summary: A funerary dedication by Clo(dia) Verecunda to her husband Decimus Clodius Herculanius, dating likely to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500225.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Dasumius Germanus lived 55 years. His sons, the heirs, [made this] for their most sweet father.
EDCS-11500225.summary: A funerary dedication by the sons and heirs of Lucius Dasumius Germanus, who lived 55 years, dating likely to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500226.translation: To the departed spirits. Epaphrys and Lyca, parents, for their son Epaphrytus.
EDCS-11500226.summary: A funerary dedication by parents Epaphrys and Lyca for their son Epaphrytus, dating likely to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500227.translation: To the departed spirits. For Felicissimus, who lived 9 years, 10 months. Publius Titius Priscus, for his son and for Titia Aristarche, his sister.
EDCS-11500227.summary: A funerary inscription by Publius Titius Priscus for his son Felicissimus and his sister Titia Aristarche, dating likely to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500230.translation: To the departed spirits. For Titus Flavius Artemidorus, by his parents.
EDCS-11500230.summary: A funerary dedication by the parents of Titus Flavius Artemidorus, dating likely to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500233.translation: To the departed spirits. For Flora, who lived 20 years, 8 months, and 25 days. Her stepmother, Silvani, [dedicated this] for her daughter of good hope.
EDCS-11500233.summary: A funerary dedication by Silvani to her stepdaughter Flora, who died young, dating likely to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500234.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Quintus Fulvius Secundus, son of Quintus, freedman, [dedicated this].
EDCS-11500234.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Quintus Fulvius Secundus, mentioning his patronymic and status as a freedman.

EDCS-11500235.translation: Marcus Heius, son of Gaius, of the Galeria tribe, rests here, and Heia Polla, daughter of Marcus.
EDCS-11500235.summary: This epitaph records the burial of Marcus Heius and Heia Polla, noting the former's tribal affiliation.

EDCS-11500237.translation: To Atimetus, a most excellent man, who lived 12 years, 4 months, and 24 days. Lucius Apusius Euhemer and Iulia Rodhope, most unhappy parents, [erected this] monument or sepulchre, which will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-11500237.summary: This epitaph commemorates Atimetus, a child who died at the age of 12 years, 4 months, and 24 days, and was set up by his grieving parents.

EDCS-11500238.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Iulia Artemis, daughter of Publius, who lived 34 years, 9 months, and 4 days.
EDCS-11500238.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulia Artemis, stating her age at death and dedicating it to the Divine Spirits.

EDCS-11500239.translation: To Iulia Rhodines. Castus, her father, [dedicated this] to his most excellent wife, and Castus, her son, to his most excellent mother. You fulfilled your pious vows, having completed the time of life; you deservedly linger as a light shade in the Elysian Fields. Your grandsons will restore your name and fame.
EDCS-11500239.summary: This funerary epitaph honors Iulia Rhodines, dedicated by her husband and son, and includes a poetic lament and hope for remembrance by future generations.

EDCS-11500240.translation: Iulius and Trebia lived well together for many years in eternal marriage; here too he now remains. This monument or sepulchre is [his], and it will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-11500240.summary: This inscription commemorates a couple, Iulius and Trebia, who lived together for many years, and states that the monument is theirs and will not be inherited.
