EDCS-11500241.translation: Lasciva, freedwoman of Augustus, lived 25 years. Titus Flavius, freedman of Augustus, [her brother], Flavia Carinia, freedwoman of Augustus, mother, Tiberius Claudius Primigenius, father. This monument or sepulchre will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-11500241.summary: This epitaph for Lasciva, who died at 25, lists her parents and brother, all freedmen of Augustus, and states the monument is non-hereditary.

EDCS-11500242.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Lucius Licinius Olcanus made this for himself while alive.
EDCS-11500242.summary: This is a self-funded funerary monument set up by Lucius Licinius Olcanus for himself, dedicated to the Divine Spirits.

EDCS-11500244.translation: Gnaeus Marius, son of Gaius, of the Galeria tribe, rests here, in front 12 feet long and 10 feet wide into the field.
EDCS-11500244.summary: This epitaph records the burial of Gnaeus Marius, son of Gaius, and specifies the dimensions of his burial plot.

EDCS-11500245.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. To Lucius Naevius [---] deceived [---] I left only [---] and tears to parents of [---] age.
EDCS-11500245.summary: This fragmentary funerary inscription, dedicated to Lucius Naevius and the Divine Spirits, laments a loss and the suffering of the parents.

EDCS-11500246.translation: To the Divine Spirits and to Marcus Nonius Quartio, son of Marcus Nonius, and to Quartinus, freedwomen Nonia Dac[---] and Nonia Sabina.
EDCS-11500246.summary: This is a funerary monument likely set up by freedwomen for their patron and his son, dating to the early Imperial period.

EDCS-11500248.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Veionia Vitalia(?) made this for her deserving husband, Marcus Nunnullius Priscus.
EDCS-11500248.summary: This funerary epitaph records a wife dedicating a monument to her deserving husband, Marcus Nunnullius Priscus, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500249.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Quintus Ovus Modestus, son of Quintus, of the Galeria tribe, who lived 20 years, and for his sister Gavia Dorcada. Gavia Lyris, their mother, made this for herself and them. 21 feet in front, 21 feet in the field.
EDCS-11500249.summary: This funerary monument, including land dimensions, was set up by a mother for her son and daughter, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500251.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Numerius Pisurius Ianuarius. 30 feet in front, 20 feet in the field.
EDCS-11500251.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Numerius Pisurius Ianuarius, including plot dimensions, probably from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500253.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Quaelia Sabina, who lived 25 years and 6 months. Her parents made this for their well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-11500253.summary: This funerary epitaph records parents dedicating a monument to their well-deserving daughter, Quaelia Sabina, who died young, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500254.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Quintius Cornelianus, who lived 11 years, 11 months, and 6 days. Antonia Vitalis made this for her most dutiful son.
EDCS-11500254.summary: This funerary epitaph records a mother dedicating a monument to her exceptionally dutiful son, Quintius Cornelianus, who died in childhood, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500256.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Sabidia Leda.
EDCS-11500256.summary: A very brief funerary dedication to Sabidia Leda, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500258.translation: For [---]iciae Felicitati, who lived 48 years, 5 months, and 8 days. Publius Sercius Felicio made this for his incomparable and well-deserving wife.
EDCS-11500258.summary: This funerary monument records a husband dedicating a monument to his incomparable wife, Felicitas, who lived to middle age, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500261.translation: For Quintus Sulpicius Eutactus and [---] Ianuarius F[---]. 3[---] feet in front, [---] feet in the field.
EDCS-11500261.summary: This fragmented funerary inscription appears to commemorate two individuals, Quintus Sulpicius Eutactus and another man, with land plot dimensions, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500262.translation: For Marcus Valerius Stephanus, son of Marcus Valerius, his most dutiful son, who lived 23 years and 10 months. Marcus Valerius Daphnicus, his most wretched father, made this for himself, his descendants, 25 feet in front, 25 feet in the field.
EDCS-11500262.summary: This funerary monument was erected by a grieving father for his son, Marcus Valerius Stephanus, and also for himself and his descendants, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500265.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Vibius Hermaiscus made this for his well-deserving freedmen Victorina and Epagathus and for his own descendants.
EDCS-11500265.summary: A funerary monument erected by Lucius Vibius Hermaiscus for his freedmen Victorina and Epagathus and his descendants.

EDCS-11500340.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII imperator XVII pater patriae consul VII designatus VIII templum Matris Deum terrae motu conlapsum restituit\n
EDCS-11500340.translation: Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, chief priest, in his seventh year of tribunician power, commander for the seventeenth time, father of the country, consul for the seventh time, designated consul for the eighth, rebuilt the temple of the Mother of the Gods, which had collapsed from an earthquake.
EDCS-11500340.summary: An inscription recording Emperor Vespasian's restoration of the temple of the Mother of the Gods, likely from his reign (69-79 AD).

EDCS-11500342.translation: Sacred to Nemesis the Holy. Quintus Pontius Euschemus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11500342.summary: A votive inscription to Nemesis by Quintus Pontius Euschemus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500349.translation: For Germanicus Caesar, son of Tiberius, grandson of the deified Augustus, great-grandson of the deified Julius, augur, Augustal priest, twice consul, twice commander. Lucius Mammius Maximus at his own expense.
EDCS-11500349.summary: A dedication to Germanicus Caesar, likely on a public building or monument, funded by Lucius Mammius Maximus during the early Roman Empire.

EDCS-11500357.translation: Appius Pulcher, son of Gaius, consul, commander, member of the board of seven priests.
EDCS-11500357.summary: A fragmentary inscription recording the titles and offices of Appius Pulcher, likely from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-11500359.translation: Marcus Nonius Balbus, son of Marcus, proconsul, [built] the basilica, gates, and wall at his own expense.
EDCS-11500359.summary: An inscription commemorating Marcus Nonius Balbus, a proconsul, who funded the construction of a basilica, gates, and wall, likely in the late Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-11500360.translation: To Marcus Nonius Balbus, son of Marcus, praetor, proconsul. The people of Herculaneum.
EDCS-11500360.summary: A dedication by the people of Herculaneum to Marcus Nonius Balbus, who served as praetor and proconsul, likely from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-11500361.translation: To Marcus Nonius Balbus, son of Marcus, praetor, proconsul. The people of Herculaneum.
EDCS-11500361.summary: A dedication by the people of Herculaneum to Marcus Nonius Balbus, who served as praetor and proconsul, likely from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-11500376.translation: To Lucius Annius, son of Lucius, of the Menenia tribe, son of a consul...
EDCS-11500376.summary: A fragmentary dedication to Lucius Annius, son of Lucius, specifying his tribal affiliation and potentially his father's consular status, likely from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-11500377.translation: Lucius Annius Mammianus Rufus, twice magistrate of the quinquennial board, [built] the theatre and orchestra at his own expense. Publius Numisius, son of Publius, architect.
EDCS-11500377.summary: An inscription recording Lucius Annius Mammianus Rufus's funding of a theatre and orchestra, with the architect Publius Numisius credited, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500386.translation: To Lucius Mammius Maximus, Augustalis, by the townsfolk and residents, with money collected.
EDCS-11500386.summary: This is a dedication to Lucius Mammius Maximus, an Augustalis, funded by the local community, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11500387.translation: On the day before the Kalends of March, all were present in the curia for the recording of minutes. Because the words spoken were that Marcus Remmius Rufus, father and son, as duovirs for the second time, have made from their own money the weights, the chalcidicum, and the school, in accordance with the city's splendor, and which it would be fitting to maintain publicly, it was resolved by this order. It is pleasing to this council, since Marcus Remmius, father and son, duovirs for the second time, have been so generous in performing their duties that their monuments are an ornament to the municipality, and so diligent that they have corrected the faults of the weights and have provided for this in perpetuity, it is pleasing to the decurions that Marcus Remmius, father and son, be given charge of those weights, school, and chalcidicum which they themselves made, for as long as they live, and that slaves bought for this purpose be put in charge of the business and not be withdrawn without a decree of the decurions and public thanks be given to Marcus Remmius, father and son, because they have dedicated their iteration of honor not to ambition or their own boasting, but have contributed to the upkeep and adornment of the municipality.
EDCS-11500387.summary: This inscription records a municipal decree from a Roman town, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, granting the care of public works (weights, chalcidicum, school) to the decurions Marcus Remmius, father and son, who had funded and built them, in recognition of their generosity and public service.

EDCS-11500391.translation: Marcus Spurius Rufus, son of Marcus, duovir for judging, had a market built from his own money and approved it.
EDCS-11500391.summary: This is a building dedication inscription from a Roman municipality, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE, recording that Marcus Spurius Rufus, a duovir, funded and approved the construction of a market.

EDCS-11500411.translation: To the Divine Manes. Titus Flavius Domitus to his well-deserving wife, Caerina Restituta, who lived 44 years.
EDCS-11500411.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Caerina Restituta, a Roman woman who died at the age of 44, set up by her husband Titus Flavius Domitus, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11500413.translation: To the all-powerful god Mithras. Appius Claudius Tarronius Dexter, a man of consular rank, dedicates.
EDCS-11500413.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mithras by Appius Claudius Tarronius Dexter, a prominent Roman official, likely from the 2nd-4th century CE.

EDCS-11500417.translation: To our most pious and most merciful lady Augusta Helena, mother of our lord Victor, always Augustus Constantine, and grandmother of our lords, the blessed Caesars, wife of the deified Constantius, the council and people of Neapolis.
EDCS-11500417.summary: This inscription is a dedication from the council and people of Neapolis, likely in the 4th century CE, to Augusta Helena, mother of Constantine and grandmother of his sons, honoring her and her lineage.

EDCS-11500421.translation: For Priscianus, master of studies, patron, priest of..., agonothetes of...
EDCS-11500421.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription honoring Priscianus, who held multiple significant roles including master of studies, patron, priest, and agonothetes, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-11500423.translation: (Decree) On the 1st day before the Kalends of April, under the scribes, were present Fulvius Probus, Lucius Pudes Neapolitanus, Pulcher, concerning whom Cornelius Cerealis, the archon, brought to the attention of those in the advisory council. Regarding this matter, it pleased Licinius Pollio, a man of our rank, an exemplary and worthy councilor of the city, that a burial place be given, which his relatives may choose, outside of sacred or rented places, and 20 liters of incense for the consolation of the parents. Caius Licinius Proclus and Meclonia Secundilla, parents, [place] granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11500423.summary: This is a bilingual inscription (Greek decree and Latin record) from Neapolis, likely from the 3rd century CE, granting a burial plot and incense for the family of Licinius Pollio, a deceased councilor, and recording the parents' acceptance.

EDCS-11500425.translation: To Gaius Herbicius Romanus, demarchisantes, duovir of food supply, quaestor, curator of sacred funds, curator of the purchase of grain, made (this) for himself while alive, who, because of a promised hunt, distributed 5,000 sesterces.
EDCS-11500425.summary: This is a dedication made by Gaius Herbicius Romanus for himself during his lifetime, likely from a Roman municipality in the 1st-3rd century CE, listing his various public offices and a significant expenditure for games.

EDCS-11500428.translation: Cornelianus, scribe, dedicated to his incomparable wife, against his will.
EDCS-11500428.summary: This is a funerary epitaph from a Roman context, likely from the 2nd-4th century CE, where a scribe named Cornelianus dedicates a monument to his wife, expressing his grief and possibly indicating an untimely death.

EDCS-11500432.translation: To the departed spirits. To Acutia Iustina, my incomparable wife, who deserved well.
EDCS-11500432.summary: A funerary dedication to a wife named Acutia Iustina.

EDCS-11500434.translation: To the departed spirits. To Calidia Nominata, my excellent wife. Lucius Vettius Sabinus [erected this].
EDCS-11500434.summary: A funerary dedication by Lucius Vettius Sabinus to his wife Calidia Nominata.

EDCS-11500435.translation: To Marcus Caninius Severus, son of Marcus, of the Mae(cia) tribe, who lived 8 years, 11 months. Marcus Caninius Botryo and Caninia Paezusa [erected this] to their son and their descendants.
EDCS-11500435.summary: A tomb inscription for a child, Marcus Caninius Severus, erected by his parents.

EDCS-11500436.translation: To the departed spirits. To Caninia Libera, my well-deserving wife. Marcus Dirius Claudianus and Caninia Liberalis [erected this] to their most dutiful daughter, who lived 25 years.
EDCS-11500436.summary: A funerary dedication by parents Marcus Dirius Claudianus and Caninia Liberalis to their deceased daughter Caninia Libera.

EDCS-11500437.translation: To the departed spirits. To Meteia Victorina, who lived 41 years. Marcus Dirius Claudianus erected this to his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-11500437.summary: A funerary dedication by Marcus Dirius Claudianus to his wife Meteia Victorina.

EDCS-11500440.translation: To the departed spirits. To Lucius Furio Furiano, who lived 27 years, 7 months. Fulvia Arete [erected this] to her most dutiful son.
EDCS-11500440.summary: A funerary dedication by Fulvia Arete to her son Lucius Furio Furiano.

EDCS-11500441.translation: Marcus Geminius Philumenus [erected this] for himself and for his female tent-mate Salaria Phillis, and for their freedmen of both sexes, to the departed spirits.
EDCS-11500441.summary: A funerary dedication by Marcus Geminius Philumenus for himself, his partner Salaria Phillis, and their freedpersons.

EDCS-11500442.translation: To the departed spirits. To Hordionia Moschis.
EDCS-11500442.summary: A funerary dedication to Hordionia Moschis.

EDCS-11500444.translation: To the departed spirits. To Lucius Licinius Pius, for himself and his family.
EDCS-11500444.summary: A funerary dedication by Lucius Licinius Pius for himself and his relatives.

EDCS-11500445.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Luxilius [erected this]. Luxilia Nice to her well-deserving wife [erected this].
EDCS-11500445.summary: A funerary dedication, possibly by Luxilia Nice to her wife, or a joint dedication.

EDCS-11500446.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Severa, mother, for her most dutiful son Lysius Severus, who lived 24 years.
EDCS-11500446.summary: This is a tombstone for a young man named Lysius Severus, set up by his mother Severa, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500447.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. [Someone] made [this] for Macrobius, son of Amator.
EDCS-11500447.summary: This tombstone is for a certain Macrobius, set up by his family, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500450.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valeria Lesbia lived 30 years and 5 days.
EDCS-11500450.summary: This tombstone commemorates Valeria Lesbia, who lived 30 years and 5 days, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500451.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Farewell Vettia Sabina, and good health to you whoever you are. Marcus Tullius Dionysius made [this] for his dearest wife, who lived 24 years, 3 months, and 22 days.
EDCS-11500451.summary: This tombstone is for Vettia Sabina, set up by her husband Marcus Tullius Dionysius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500456.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a woman named Edone, indicating she died in peace, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500463.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-11500463.translation: Eternal home. Rufinus rests here, who lived more or less 30 years, the sweetest, well-deserving, for Maias.
EDCS-11500463.summary: This tombstone records the death of Rufinus, who lived approximately 30 years, and was likely set up by his wife Maias, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500474.translation: Acilia Rogata made [this] for Romano Nolano, well-deserving, who lived 26 years, 9 months, and 23 days; he departed on the 7th day before the Kalends of January.
EDCS-11500474.summary: This tombstone commemorates Romano Nolano, who lived 26 years, 9 months, and 23 days, and died on December 26th, set up by Acilia Rogata, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500475.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-11500475.translation: In this holy place rests Annianus, who lived 13 years, 5 months, and 18 days; the incomparable, well-deserving husband made [this] for [his wife], may you rest in peace.
EDCS-11500475.summary: This is a tombstone for Annianus, who lived 13 years, 5 months, and 18 days, and was likely set up by his wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500476.translation: Servulus the boy rests here in peace, who lived seven years, 2 months, and a half; his father Paulus made [this] for [his son] of good memory.
EDCS-11500476.summary: This tombstone records the death of a young boy named Servulus, who lived seven years and two and a half months, set up by his father Paulus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500478.translation: Sacred to Apollo. Quintus Trebellius Restitutus made [this].
EDCS-11500478.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Apollo by Quintus Trebellius Restitutus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500479.translation: Sacred to Apollo, Gaius Ratinius Firmus [dedicated this].
EDCS-11500479.summary: This is a dedication to Apollo by Gaius Ratinius Firmus.

EDCS-11500480.translation: Aelia Nice and Callistus the doctor set up this gift to Asclepius and Hygieia.
EDCS-11500480.summary: Aelia Nice and Callistus dedicated a gift to Asclepius and Hygieia.

EDCS-11500481.translation: Callistus gave this as a sacred offering in fulfillment of a vow to Asclepius and Salus.
EDCS-11500481.summary: Callistus fulfilled a vow with a sacred offering to Asclepius and Salus.

EDCS-11500483.translation: Gaius Avillius December, a marble contractor, with Vellia Cinnamide, his associate, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the Bona Dea. This was placed by Philadespotus, the freedman of Claudius, imperial freedman, as priest. It was dedicated on the sixth day before the Kalends of November, when Quintus Junius Marullus was consul.
EDCS-11500483.summary: This is a dedication to the Bona Dea by Gaius Avillius December and Vellia Cinnamide, set up by the priest Philadespotus during the consulship of Quintus Junius Marullus.

EDCS-11500485.translation: Sacred to Concordia. The Augustales Publius Avinius Gallinatus (?) and Celer [dedicated this].
EDCS-11500485.summary: Publius Avinius Gallinatus and Celer, members of the Augustales, made a sacred dedication to Concordia.

EDCS-11500486.translation: Sacred to all the immortal gods and goddesses, Titus Aurelius Protogenes [dedicated this].
EDCS-11500486.summary: Titus Aurelius Protogenes made a sacred dedication to all the gods and goddesses.

EDCS-11500487.translation: Julius Secundus Faonius completed this sacred offering to the most holy god Patrius in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11500487.summary: Julius Secundus Faonius completed a votive offering to the god Patrius.

EDCS-11500493.translation: Marcus Annius Macer willingly fulfilled his vow to the Genius.
EDCS-11500493.summary: Marcus Annius Macer willingly fulfilled a vow to the Genius.

EDCS-11500494.translation: By order of the Genius of Alotiana, Euaristus, servant of the gods, willingly and with good heart fulfilled [this] from a vision.
EDCS-11500494.summary: Euaristus, a servant of the gods, fulfilled a votive offering inspired by a vision and the Genius of Alotiana.

EDCS-11500496.translation: For the safety of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, and of our Caesar Marcus Aurelius Caesar, [this was dedicated to] the Genius of the colony of Puteoli. Chrysanthus, imperial steward in charge of grain, [made this offering] at Puteoli and Ostia. The place was given by permission of the decurions.
EDCS-11500496.summary: Chrysanthus, an imperial steward, made a dedication to the Genius of the colony of Puteoli for the health of Emperors Antoninus Pius and Marcus Aurelius, with the approval of the local council.

EDCS-11500497.translation: To the most holy god, the Genius of the Colony of Puteoli. Nemonia Calliste, daughter of Nemonius Eutyches, along with her two sons Nemonius Eutychianus and Nemonius Gemellianus, her brothers, and her daughter Nemonia Ianuaria, give this as a gift during the consulship of Lucius Venuleius Apronianus and Lucius Sergius Paullus.
EDCS-11500497.summary: This is a dedication to the Genius of the colony of Puteoli by a family, likely dating to the consulship of Apronianus and Paullus (indicated by the consuls' names).

EDCS-11500498.translation: To the most holy god, the Genius of the Colony of Puteoli. Marcus Nemonius Tugurinus, son of Marcus, and Sabinus Felix give this as a gift.
EDCS-11500498.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Genius of the colony of Puteoli by two individuals, Marcus Nemonius Tugurinus and Sabinus Felix.

EDCS-11500501.translation: For the safety and victory of the Augusti, Quintus Aurelius Hermadion, a sevir Augustalis and curator for them, constructed and gives this gift to the Great God, the Genius of the Colony of Puteoli and his fatherland.
EDCS-11500501.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of a gift to the Genius of the colony of Puteoli by Quintus Aurelius Hermadion, a sevir Augustalis and curator, for the well-being of the emperors.

EDCS-11500502.translation: Sacred to the Genius of the Colony of Puteoli. Ptolemais, daughter of Issa, has dedicated Fortun(a?) with her mother Galeria Cuprogenia overseeing it.
EDCS-11500502.summary: This is a dedication of a shrine or offering to the Genius of the colony of Puteoli by Ptolemais, overseen by her mother Galeria Cuprogenia.

EDCS-11500505.translation: Titus Flavius Antipater, along with his wife Flavia Artemisia and his freedwoman Alcide, willingly fulfilled a vow to Asclepius and Hygieia, and to Iovius Flazzus.
EDCS-11500505.summary: This inscription records Titus Flavius Antipater, along with his wife and freedwoman, fulfilling a vow to Asclepius, Hygieia, and Iovius Flazzus.

EDCS-11500506.translation: Numerius Cluvius, son of Manius, who was a IIIIvir, Caudius IIvir, and IIIIvir quinquennalis in Nola, saw to its construction at his own expense and restored it; sacred to Iovius Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-11500506.summary: This inscription details the public works and restorations undertaken by Numerius Cluvius in Nola, including a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-11500507.text_edition: \n Numerius Cluvius Mani filius duumvir Nolae IIIIvir quinquennalis duumvir Kapuae macellum VII\n
EDCS-11500507.translation: Numerius Cluvius, son of Manius, duumvir in Nola, IIIIvir quinquennalis, duumvir in Capua, [oversaw?] the market for seven [months?].
EDCS-11500507.summary: This inscription likely records the official positions held by Numerius Cluvius in Nola and Capua, possibly related to a market building project.

EDCS-11500508.translation: For Quintus Volusius Saturninus and Publius Cornelius Scipio, consuls. The Augustales, who made the games for Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus and Agrippina Augusta, and for Iovius Optimus Maximus and the Genius of the colony, on the 12th day before the Kalends of March, with Gaius Tantilius Hyla, freedman of Gaius, and Gnaeus Pollius Victor, freedman of Gnaeus, and Gaius Glaphyr, freedman of Gaius, as curators.
EDCS-11500508.summary: This inscription records the Augustales of a colony who organized games in honor of the emperor Nero, his mother Agrippina, Jupiter, and the Genius of the colony, during a specific consulship and date.

EDCS-11500509.translation: By order of Iovius Optimus Maximus Damascenus, Publius Cossutius, grandson of Publius, of the Amat[i?], [priest?] of Hierotus, priest.
EDCS-11500509.summary: This inscription, though fragmentary, indicates an order from Jupiter Optimus Maximus Damascenus and mentions a priest and potentially a devotee.

EDCS-11500510.translation: By order of Iovius Optimus Maximus Damascenus, the priests honoring Marcus Nemonius Eutychianus, son of Marcus, of the Palatina tribe, who was honored with a public horse by Emperor Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, and admitted into the order of decurions of Puteoli; Marcus Nemonius Callistus, priest, his father?, remitted the collection.
EDCS-11500510.summary: This inscription honors Marcus Nemonius Eutychianus, a Puteolan decurion recognized for his military service and public honors, with his father, a priest, remitting a collection.

EDCS-11500511.text_edition: \n Cilix poni iussit argentum dono dedit\n
EDCS-11500511.translation: Cilix ordered and gave the silver as a gift.
EDCS-11500511.summary: This inscription records a private donation of silver by Cilix, likely a freedman given the name.

EDCS-11500512.translation: By order of Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Aurelius Theodorus, priest of the Heloiopolitani, son of the priest, curator of the temple of the Geremellensians, with the priests and the iugophori, by adding to the gifts the torques and the veil, placed these things at his own expense, with Acilius Secundus Troto[---] overseeing.
EDCS-11500512.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by priests and temple officials to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, during the curatorship of Aurelius Theodorus.

EDCS-11500516.translation: In the consulship of Gaius Caesar Augustus' son, Lucius Paullus, Quintus Numisius, son of Quintus, freedman Legio, and Lucius Safinius, son of Lucius, freedman Hilarus, a sodalis of Gaius Modius Cimbri, and Aeschinus, slave of Octavius Marcus, the magistri, arranged for this to be made at their own expense.
EDCS-11500516.summary: This inscription records the construction of a monument or building, financed by magistri (officials) and sodales (members of a society) during the consulship of Gaius Caesar and Lucius Paullus, likely in the early 1st century CE.

EDCS-11500517.translation: Sacred to Liber Pater. T(itus) Flavius Eclectianus and Olympius, his sons, priests and orgiophantae.
EDCS-11500517.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Liber Pater by Titus Flavius Eclectianus and his sons, who were priests and orgiophantae, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500519.translation: To Pertinax Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus and Publius Septimius Geta Nobilissimus Caesar and Julia Domna, mother of the Augusti and of the camp, Stlaccia [---], priestess of Ceres, donated the entrance for the Thiasus Placidianus, with Flavius Eclectianus, son of Titus, as priest, and Titis Stlacciis Sotere and Repar[---].
EDCS-11500519.summary: This inscription records a dedication of an entrance to Ceres by a priestess, Stlaccia, to the Thiasus Placidianus, during the reigns of several emperors including Pertinax, Septimius Severus, and Caracalla, and empresses Julia Domna.

EDCS-11500522.translation: To the departed spirits. Thleseus and Sy[---], from the collegium salutare of the Valerian family.
EDCS-11500522.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the departed spirits (Dis Manibus) by members of the collegium salutare of the Valerian family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500525.translation: To Sol Invictus. Claudius Aurelius Rufinus, with his wife and son, gave this as a gift to the Genius of the colony.
EDCS-11500525.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Sol Invictus and the Genius of the colony by Claudius Aurelius Rufinus, his wife, and son, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11500526.translation: To the Nymphs. Ducenia Tyche, daughter of Aulus, gave this as a gift.
EDCS-11500526.summary: This inscription records a private dedication to the Nymphs by Ducenia Tyche, daughter of Aulus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500527.translation: To the Lord Sarapis, for the vow undertaken, Gaius Herennius Priscus, son of Marcus, of the Claudian tribe, primipilaris of the Legio II Traiana Fortis, patron of the municipality, gave this as a gift.
EDCS-11500527.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to the god Sarapis by Gaius Herennius Priscus, a high-ranking military officer and patron of a municipality, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11500528.summary: This inscription is a dedication or memorial to Marcus Virius Fructus.

EDCS-11500529.translation: Vibius Comananus, son of Ovius, dedicated this gift to Venus.
EDCS-11500529.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Venus by Vibius Comananus.

EDCS-11500530.translation: During the consulship of Lucius Iulius Ursus Servianus for the third time, on the 6th of October, this taurobolium for Venus Caelestis and the sanctuary were performed by Herennia Fortunata through the priest Tiberius Claudius Felix, by decree of the goddess; it was repeated.
EDCS-11500530.summary: This inscription records a taurobolium (bull sacrifice) and the construction of a sanctuary for Venus Caelestis, performed by Herennia Fortunata through her priest, and it was a repeated ceremony.

EDCS-11500532.translation: Sacred to Augustus. ... [with] gold ... a gold palm ... silver lion heads ... with silver depictions ... of Aelesia ... in the name of Marcus Aelius ... gem enclosed in gold ... after his death his sons erected ... a gilded statue of the mistress, 1 pound ... two silver-plated bowls ... two silver-plated frontlets ... a silver mantle, 3 ounces ... Heliodorus.
EDCS-11500532.summary: This is a fragmented inscription likely detailing precious offerings, including gold and silver items, dedicated to Augustus and perhaps a female deity, possibly erected by sons after a father's death.

EDCS-11500538.translation: The name registered is Naevia Secunda, freedwoman of Lucius, or if she is under another name.
EDCS-11500538.summary: This is a legal declaration regarding the name of a freedwoman, Naevia Secunda.

EDCS-11500542.summary: This is a simple epitaph or dedication for Arellia Fortona, identified as a mother.

EDCS-11500546.translation: Caius Velleius Cerialis willingly fulfilled his vow for his parent.
EDCS-11500546.summary: This inscription records Caius Velleius Cerialis fulfilling a vow made for his parent.

EDCS-11500548.translation: Lucius Cocceius Auctus, freedman of Lucius Cocceius and Caius Postumius, architect.
EDCS-11500548.summary: This is an epitaph identifying Lucius Cocceius Auctus as an architect and freedman.

EDCS-11500555.translation: To Lucius Caesar, prince of the youth, consul-designate...
EDCS-11500555.summary: This is a fragmented dedication to Lucius Caesar, honoring his youth and consulship.

EDCS-11500557.translation: The Republic restored [this] to Tiberius Caesar, son of the deified Augustus, grandson of the deified Julius, Augustus, pontifex maximus, consul for the fourth time, imperator for the eighth time, with tribunician power for the thirty-second time. Augustales. Sardes, Ouloron, Magnesia, Philadelphia, Tmolus, Cyme, Temnos, Cibyra, Myrina, Ephesus, Apollonidea, Hyrcania, Mostene, Aege, Hierocaesarea.
EDCS-11500557.summary: This inscription commemorates the restoration of something by the Republic to Tiberius Caesar (who held numerous titles) and lists various cities, likely indicating a network of restored territories or a significant public works project.

EDCS-11500561.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII imperator XVII pater patriae censor consul VII designatus VIII\n
EDCS-11500561.translation: Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the seventh time, imperator for the seventeenth time, father of the fatherland, censor, consul for the seventh time, designated consul for the eighth time.
EDCS-11500561.summary: This is a titulature inscription for Emperor Vespasian, listing his various titles and powers, likely from a public monument.

EDCS-11500563.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Domitian Augustus, son of the deified Vespasian, conqueror of Germany, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 13th time, hailed as imperator for the 22nd time, consul for the 17th time, perpetual censor, father of the country, by the Vestorian and Calpurnian districts.
EDCS-11500563.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Domitian by the inhabitants of two districts in Rome.

EDCS-11500565.translation: To Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 15th time, hailed as imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 6th time, father of the country, the best of princes. Lucius Plutius Phoebus, son of Lucius, of the Palatine tribe.
EDCS-11500565.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Trajan by Lucius Plutius Phoebus, likely a freedman or a member of a prominent family.

EDCS-11500566.translation: To Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Optimus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus Parthicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 20th time, hailed as imperator for the 12th time, consul for the 6th time, father of the country, by the worshippers of Jupiter Heliopolitanus from Berytus who reside in Puteoli.
EDCS-11500566.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Trajan by a religious association of Berytans residing in Puteoli.

EDCS-11500567.translation: [...] to Trajan [...] Augustus [...] with tribunician power [...] Gnaeus Pompeius Euphem[us(?)].
EDCS-11500567.summary: This is a fragmented inscription likely recording a dedication to Emperor Trajan by Gnaeus Pompeius Euphem[us(?)].

EDCS-11500572.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II consul II designatus III pater patriae opus pilarum vi maris conlapsum a divo patre suo promissum restituit\n
EDCS-11500572.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul for the 2nd time, designated consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, restored the pier of the sea that had collapsed, a work promised by his deified father.
EDCS-11500572.summary: This inscription records Emperor Antoninus Pius restoring a collapsed sea pier, a project initiated by his adoptive father, Hadrian.

EDCS-11500574.translation: To Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, father of the country, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul for the 2nd time, by the college of scabillarii (shoe-scrapers), who are permitted to form a corporation by decree of the Senate. The place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11500574.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Antoninus Pius by the college of scabillarii, a guild permitted to organize by the Senate, with the location granted by local officials.

EDCS-11500579.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 15th time, consul for the 3rd time. Gaius Julius Fortunatus, quinquennial magistrate, in the name of the associates of the Puteolan scabillarii, who are permitted to form a corporation by decree of the Senate, gives this gift with his own money. The place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11500579.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Marcus Aurelius by Gaius Julius Fortunatus on behalf of the Puteolan scabillarii guild.

EDCS-11500582.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus, father of the country, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 4th time, hailed as imperator for the 8th time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the deified Commodus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, by the colony Flavia Augusta Puteoli.
EDCS-11500582.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Septimius Severus by the colony of Puteoli, listing his extensive titles and lineage.

EDCS-11500585.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurellius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, grandson of the deified Septimius Severus, [[son]] of the deified Antoninus Magnus Pius, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, by the colony Flavia Augusta Puteoli.
EDCS-11500585.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Severus Alexander by the colony of Puteoli.

EDCS-11500587.translation: To their most strong and most pious prince, Marcus Aurelius Carinus. Rufius Volusianus, a man of consular rank, by their judgment, most blessed again, corrector.
EDCS-11500587.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Carinus by Rufius Volusianus, a prominent official.

EDCS-11500619.translation: To Publius Aelius Agrippinus, son of Publius, cornicularius (aide) to the procurator of the province of Belgica, my dearest brother, and to Aelia Amabilis, my most dutiful mother, Victorinus, freedman of the emperor, made this.
EDCS-11500619.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Victorinus, a freedman of the emperor, for his brother, a cornicularius, and his mother.

EDCS-11500620.translation: To Alfius Licinius, son of Licinius, a most excellent man, patron of the colony, from the counts, truly a patron of the regio of the Thermensians.
EDCS-11500620.summary: This is a dedication honoring Alfius Licinius, a distinguished patron of the colony and the regio of the Thermensians.

EDCS-11500624.translation: To Marcus Bennius Rufus, son of Marcus, procurator for the Emperor Caesar Augustus, [from] the Oe(e)nses from the province of Africa.
EDCS-11500624.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Bennius Rufus, a procurator for the emperor, set up by people from Africa.

EDCS-11500625.translation: Lucius Bovius Celer, son of Lucius, grandson of Lucius, of the Falerna tribe, twice chief magistrate, quaestor, augur, prefect of the engineers, military tribune of the III Cyrenaicae legion, procurator of the imperial gladiatorial school at Alexandria in Egypt, selected from the elite by the Emperor Caesar Augustus, [dedicated this] for himself and for his most excellent wife, Sextia Nerula, daughter of Lucius, of the Nerula tribe, with whom he lived from childhood for 31 years without offense.
EDCS-11500625.summary: This is a funerary and autobiographical inscription detailing the extensive career of Lucius Bovius Celer, a high-ranking official, and commemorating his marriage.

EDCS-11500626.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Bovius and Sextia Nerula, parents, made [this] for their most sweet daughter Lucilla. She lived 8 years, 3 months, 23 days.
EDCS-11500626.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucilla, set up by her parents, recording her age at death.

EDCS-11500628.text_edition: \n Qui dedit Aeneadum fastis ex ordine consul nomina progenies Claudius Appiadum alta Sabinillae dat dulci moenia natae Iulianus genitor de rude coepta solo sospes ubi annoso mea Claudia lucis in aevo prospiciat lepidam pulchra anus in subolem\n
EDCS-11500628.translation: Here, from the order of the Aeneadae, Julianus, the father, whose ancestors were consuls, gives the walls to his sweet-born daughter, Sabinilla; from a rough beginning on the ground, may she, safe, look upon her lovely descendant in her old age.
EDCS-11500628.summary: This is a dedicatory poem or epitaph, likely for a daughter named Claudia, written by her father Julianus, celebrating family lineage and future descendants.

EDCS-11500634.translation: To Quintus Flavius Maesius Egnatius Lollianus, a most distinguished man, quaestor, candidate for consul, urban praetor, augur of the public augurs of the Roman people of Quirites, consular for the Tiber river and sewers, consular for public works, consular for aqueducts, consular for Campania, count of the Flaviales, count of the East, count of the first order, and proconsul of the province of Africa, most worthy patron of the regio of the Triumphal Gate.
EDCS-11500634.summary: This is an honorific inscription for Quintus Flavius Maesius Egnatius Lollianus, listing his extensive career and acknowledging him as a patron.

EDCS-11500635.translation: To Quintus Flavius Maesius Egnatius Lollianus, a most distinguished man, quaestor, candidate for consul, urban praetor, public augur of the Roman people of Quirites, consular for the Tiber river and sewers, consular for public works, consular for aqueducts, consular for Campania, count of the Flaviales, count of the East, count of the first order, and proconsul of the province of Africa, most worthy patron of the decastria, his colleagues placed this.
EDCS-11500635.summary: This is an honorific inscription for Quintus Flavius Maesius Egnatius Lollianus, detailing his high offices and acknowledging him as a patron, set up by his colleagues.

EDCS-11500636.translation: To Quintus Flavius Maesius Cornelius Severus Lollianus, a most distinguished boy, quaestor, candidate for consul, from the junior Mavortii. The Decatrenses, his clients, placed this for their most excellent patron.
EDCS-11500636.summary: This is an honorific inscription for Quintus Flavius Maesius Cornelius Severus Lollianus, a young man of high status, dedicated by his clients.

EDCS-11500637.translation: [...]ius Fronto, curule aedile, made [this] at his own expense. He brought [water], paved the road from the forum to the boundaries. [...] made [...], paved from the top to the emporium.
EDCS-11500637.summary: This inscription records public works undertaken by aedile Fronto, including water supply and road construction.

EDCS-11500638.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Proculinae uxori pudicissimae et opsequentissimae sic Aquillius Proculus consul proconsul XVvir et sibi\n
EDCS-11500638.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Julia Proculina, his most chaste and obedient wife. Thus Aquillius Proculus, consul, proconsul, and member of the XVvir, and for himself.
EDCS-11500638.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Aquillius Proculus for his wife, Julia Proculina, and himself, indicating his high status as consul and proconsul.

EDCS-11500639.translation: To Marcus Maecius Memmius Furius Baburio Caecilianus Placidus, a most illustrious man, chief pontiff, public augur of the Roman people, quindecemvir for performing sacred rites, corrector of Venice and Istria, prefect of the sacred grain supply of the city with the right of the sword, count of the first order, count of the East, Egypt, and Mesopotamia, judge of sacred cases for the third time, judge again by sacred delegations, ordinary consul, a most outstanding patron. The Palatine region erected this.
EDCS-11500639.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring Marcus Maecius Memmius Furius Baburio Caecilianus Placidus, listing his numerous prestigious religious, administrative, and judicial offices, and indicating he was a patron honored by the Palatine region.

EDCS-11500645.translation: To Septimius Rusticus, a most illustrious man, consular governor of Campania, restorer of the order of the baths. For his outstanding affection, the most splendid order and the most honorable people erected this to their most outstanding patron.
EDCS-11500645.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to Septimius Rusticus, a former consular governor of Campania, honoring him as a patron for his significant efforts in restoring the baths, erected by the local order and people.

EDCS-11500646.text_edition: \n Calvisio Publi filio Falerna Vero tribuno militum legionis XI geminae quaestori \n
EDCS-11500646.translation: To Calvisius Verus, son of Publius, of the Falernian tribe, tribune of the soldiers of the eleventh legion Gemini, quaestor.
EDCS-11500646.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription honoring Calvisius Verus, mentioning his military and financial offices, likely part of a larger monument.

EDCS-11500663.translation: To Gaius Quinctius, freedman of Trogius, kalator of the pontiffs; to Gaius Quinctius Receptus, son of Gaius, son of Quinctia Urbana; to Gaius Quinctius Auctus; to Publius Numisius Marion.
EDCS-11500663.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating several individuals, including a freedman who served as a kalator of the pontiffs, suggesting family and possibly social connections.

EDCS-11500664.translation: To Aurelius Symphorus, freedman of Augustus, a veteran official in charge of records and diplomas, honored with decurional ornaments. The most splendid order of the city, for his love and diligence towards the fatherland and its citizens.
EDCS-11500664.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription from the city council to Aurelius Symphorus, a freedman of Augustus and a veteran official, recognizing his service and affection for the community.

EDCS-11500665.translation: The bones of Tiberius Claudius Philoterus, freedman of Augustus, the aedituus, are placed here.
EDCS-11500665.summary: This is a funerary inscription marking the burial place of Tiberius Claudius Philoterus, a freedman of Augustus who served as an aedituus (temple attendant).

EDCS-11500666.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gregorio Marci Ulpi Nicephori Augusti liberti proximi commentariorum nnonae qui vixit nnis XVIII mensibus VIII diebus XI Nicephorus et Ulpia Profutura parentes miseri filio piissimo\n
EDCS-11500666.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Gregorius, son of Marcius Ulpius Nicephorus, freedman of Augustus, his closest associate in the commentaries on the grain supply, who lived 18 years, 8 months, and 11 days. Nicephorus and Ulpia Profutura, grieving parents, [set this up] for their most devoted son.
EDCS-11500666.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gregorius, a young boy who died at 18, son of a freedman of Augustus and an associate in the grain supply administration, set up by his parents.

EDCS-11500667.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Aelia Concordia, wife of Nicephorianus, freedman of Augustus, a dispensator, his dearest, well-deserving wife.
EDCS-11500667.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aelia Concordia, wife of Nicephorianus, a freedman of Augustus and a dispensator (financial officer), set up by her husband.

EDCS-11500669.translation: To Titacia Procula, daughter of Gaius, who lived 25 years. Secundus Vitalianus, freedman of Secundus, freedman of Augustus, dispensator, gave [this] to his most indulgent and dearest wife. Tiberius Claudius Hermas, quaestor, donated [this].
EDCS-11500669.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titacia Procula, who died at 25, set up by her husband Secundus Vitalianus, a dispensator and freedman of Augustus, and possibly a donation from Tiberius Claudius Hermas.

EDCS-11500671.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Tauros, Caesar's courier, lived 24 years. Calliste, his wife, made this for him, a well-deserving husband.
EDCS-11500671.summary: A funerary epitaph for Tauros, Caesar's courier, set up by his wife Calliste.

EDCS-11500673.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Octavia Censorina made this for her well-deserving husband, Felix, a freedman of the emperor and a plumber.
EDCS-11500673.summary: A funerary epitaph for Felix, a freedman of the emperor and plumber, set up by his wife Octavia Censorina.

EDCS-11500674.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aelia Hediste made this for her well-deserving husband, Agathemeros, a freedman of our emperor.
EDCS-11500674.summary: A funerary epitaph for Agathemeros, a freedman of the emperor, set up by his wife Aelia Hediste.

EDCS-11500675.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gnaeus Domitius Chrysanthus, procurator, [made this for?] Domitia, daughter of Domitianus Cibus.
EDCS-11500675.summary: A funerary epitaph fragment, possibly for Domitia, daughter of Domitianus Cibus, set up by Gnaeus Domitius Chrysanthus, a procurator.

EDCS-11500677.translation: Alexander, a most devoted son and procurator, made this for Oronte, freedman and procurator of the patrimony, who lived 96 years.
EDCS-11500677.summary: A funerary epitaph for Oronte, a freedman and procurator of the patrimony, set up by his devoted son Alexander.

EDCS-11500678.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Successus, Augustus's courier, aged 35, a footman in his lifetime.
EDCS-11500678.summary: A funerary epitaph for Successus, a courier and footman for the emperors, who died at 35.

EDCS-11500679.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aelius Ianuarius. Secundus, a freedman of Augustus and a scribe, [made this for his?] well-deserving freedman.
EDCS-11500679.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aelius Ianuarius, a scribe and freedman of Augustus, set up by his freedman.

EDCS-11500682.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Flavia Primigenia, freedwoman, made this for her well-deserving patron, T. Flavius Primigenus, freedman of the emperor, who served above the velarii.
EDCS-11500682.summary: A funerary epitaph for T. Flavius Primigenus, a freedman of the emperor and patron, set up by his freedwoman Flavia Primigenia.

EDCS-11500683.translation: Herodes, son of Aphrodisius, of Ascalon, lived 42 years. Demetrius, the manager, bought the place from the association of baulani.
EDCS-11500683.summary: An inscription recording the purchase of a plot of land by Demetrius, manager, from the association of baulani, for Herodes, son of Ascalon, who lived 42 years.

EDCS-11500684.translation: Eunea lived 20 years. Artichnus made this, with the permission of Corinthus, the procurator, from the association of baulani.
EDCS-11500684.summary: An inscription recording the funerary monument for Eunea, aged 20, made by Artichnus with permission from the procurator Corinthus, funded by the association of baulani.

EDCS-11500686.translation: To the departed spirits. Hermias, slave of Augustus and overseer, made this for his deserving wife Gemina and for himself, his descendants, and their descendants. He lived 32 years, 3 months, and 6 days.
EDCS-11500686.summary: A tombstone for Gemina, erected by her husband Hermias, a slave and overseer, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500687.translation: For Iulia Erotis. Mystis, overseer of Caesar, and the household under him, [made this] for her because of her merits.
EDCS-11500687.summary: A dedication to Iulia Erotis by Mystis, an overseer in the imperial service, and his household, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500688.translation: To the departed spirits. Circenia Ianuaria made this for her deserving husband Martialis, overseer of Caesar, and for herself, her freedmen, freedwomen, and all their descendants.
EDCS-11500688.summary: A tombstone for Martialis, an imperial overseer, set up by his wife Circenia Ianuaria, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500689.translation: To the departed spirits. Statilia Cy[---] had this made for her dearest husband Apollonius [---], overseer of the villa [---].
EDCS-11500689.summary: A partially preserved tombstone for Apollonius, an overseer of a villa, dedicated by his wife Statilia Cy[---], likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500694.translation: To the departed spirits. Patulcia Trophime [made this] for her deserving husband Quintus Cissonius Aprilis, son of Quintus, of the Horatia tribe, a veteran of the II Praetorian cohort and architect of the emperors.
EDCS-11500694.summary: A tombstone for Quintus Cissonius Aprilis, a veteran of the Praetorian guard and imperial architect, dedicated by his wife Patulcia Trophime, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500695.translation: [---] Claudia Eutychis Ma[---] [made this] for [---] Voturia Cornelius [---], a Roman knight of the V cohort, who lived 25 years [---].
EDCS-11500695.summary: A fragmentary tombstone mentioning a soldier of the V cohort and a Roman knight, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500696.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Caecilius Felix and Nonia Heraclia [made this] for themselves and their families, for Gaius Julius Quartus, a veteran from the praetorium, a Gaul by birth.
EDCS-11500696.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Julius Quartus, a Gaulish veteran, likely from the Imperial period, with the inscription set up by Marcus Caecilius Felix and Nonia Heraclia.

EDCS-11500697.translation: To the departed spirits. His father made this for Quintus Valerius Felix, a veteran of the X Praetorian cohort, deserving. But he should have made it for me, leaving me in my old age.
EDCS-11500697.summary: A tombstone for Quintus Valerius Felix, a veteran of the X Praetorian cohort, set up by his father who laments that he had to erect it himself, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500698.translation: To Lucius Valerius Fronton, son of Marcus, of the Claudia tribe, a soldier of the X Praetorian cohort, who lived 20 years and 3 months and [---] days, served for [---] year. To Marcus Valerius Fronton, son of Marcus, of the Claudia tribe, a soldier of the XIIII Urban cohort, [who lived] 24 years and 6 months, [served] 7 years. Mettia Lucilia, mother, made this.
EDCS-11500698.summary: A tombstone commemorating two brothers, both soldiers in Praetorian or Urban cohorts, erected by their mother Mettia Lucilia, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500699.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Avianiae Syntyche, sister of Crispina, and Lucius Antonius Proculus, standard-bearer of the IX Praetorian cohort, for her husband, [made this] for Valeria Artima, freedwoman of Crispinus, and her daughter Antonina, who lived 13 years, 9 months, and 28 days. Artema wished that these people, whom she held dearest, be consecrated together with herself to the departed spirits.
EDCS-11500699.summary: A tombstone for Valeria Artima and her daughter Antonina, and also for her sister and brother-in-law (a soldier), dedicated by Artema who wished to be buried with them, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11500702.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Quintus Iunius Iullinus, son of Quintus, a soldier of the 15th urban cohort, lived for 34 years and served for 16 years. Lucius Iustius Valerianus, out of duty and affection, made this.
EDCS-11500702.summary: This is a tombstone for Quintus Iunius Iullinus, a soldier in the Praetorian Guard, set up by his heir.

EDCS-11500703.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Silvanus Martinus, a fellow soldier and heir, made this for Aelius Fortunatus, a soldier of the numerus statorum of the Praetorian Guard Antoninianorum, who deserved well.
EDCS-11500703.summary: This is a tombstone for Aelius Fortunatus, a soldier in the Praetorian Guard, erected by his fellow soldier and heir.

EDCS-11500704.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Marcus Munatius Primus, son of Marcus of the Fabia tribe, a standard-bearer of the 5th Cohort Victrix Severiana, who lived for 28 years and 29 days. His father Munatius Zethus made this.
EDCS-11500704.summary: This is a tombstone for Marcus Munatius Primus, a standard-bearer in the Praetorian Guard, erected by his father.

EDCS-11500705.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Verticia Seni, freedwoman. Lucius Laelius Geminus, a soldier of the 7th cohort of vigiles, 2nd centuria of Marius, made this for his most excellent friend, along with Seratus, her patron.
EDCS-11500705.summary: This tombstone commemorates Verticia Seni, a freedwoman, and was erected by her lover, Lucius Laelius Geminus, a soldier of the vigiles, and her patron.

EDCS-11500706.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Aelius Asclepiades, a soldier of the 9th legion, from Cilicia, who lived 42 years and served 8 years. Aelia Seleria made this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-11500706.summary: This is a tombstone for Aelius Asclepiades, a soldier of the 9th legion, erected by Aelia Seleria.

EDCS-11500708.translation: [This tombstone was erected] for a soldier serving in the grain dole of the 7th Legion Gemina Felix. Aemilius Fortunatus, his heir, took care of its erection.
EDCS-11500708.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument for a frumentarius of the 7th Legion, by his heir.

EDCS-11500709.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Quintus Cornelius Victor, a veteran of the 2nd Traiana Legion, from Africa, who lived 78 years. His most devoted son, Lucius Cornelius Victor, made this for the well-deserving father.
EDCS-11500709.summary: This is a tombstone for Quintus Cornelius Victor, a veteran of the 2nd Traiana Legion, erected by his son.

EDCS-11500711.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Valerius Secundinus, a soldier of the 2nd Adiutrix Legion, from Pannonia, who lived 40 years and served 15 years. His fellow soldiers made this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-11500711.summary: This tombstone commemorates Valerius Secundinus, a soldier of the 2nd Adiutrix Legion, erected by his fellow soldiers.

EDCS-11500714.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Arria Geminia, a good woman, foster-sister of Arrius Germanus, junior primipilaris(?). Iulius Agrippa made this for his wife, who deserved well.
EDCS-11500714.summary: This is a tombstone for Arria Geminia, wife of Iulius Agrippa and foster-sister of Arrius Germanus, erected by her husband.

EDCS-11500715.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Titus Flavius Antoninus, grandson of Flavius Antoninus, a primipilaris, educated in Greek and Latin, who lived 19 years, 2 months, and 22 days. His unfortunate parents made this.
EDCS-11500715.summary: This tombstone records the death of Titus Flavius Antoninus, grandson of a primipilaris, erected by his parents.

EDCS-11500716.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcia Romana made this place for herself while alive, for her son Marcia Basso Romano, unequalled, who died on an expedition.
EDCS-11500716.summary: This is a funerary dedication for a son who died on an expedition, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500718.translation: For Lucius Annius Modestus, son of Lucius, of the Collina tribe, honored with the public equestrian rank, who on the Kalends of June was present in the curia of the Annian Basilica for the writing of records. Titus Oppius Severus Viguetius Liberalis and Iulius Capretanus Granius Longinus, as IIviri, spoke regarding the confirmation of authority for honoring his memory and for placing a statue of Annius Modestus, son of the distinguished man Annius Numisianus, and they decreed what should be done concerning it. Since Annius Numisianus, a distinguished man, bears witness to each individual with his integrity of character and quiet life, which is the sole support in grief, they deserved the consolation of our affections and, through the candor of our order, a good citizen and dutiful father in so grave an injustice of fortune, to divert from such great sadness. It is pleasing to this order to confirm the authority previously exercised in this matter and to permit Annius Adiectus, his freedman, to erect a statue to Annius Modestus in a location assigned by our IIviri, so that the affection of our republic may be more evident in praising him for what he achieved in life. Adiectus, freedman, granted the place by decree of the curia.
EDCS-11500718.summary: This inscription records a decree by the IIviri (duumvirs) of a town to confirm the authority for honoring Annius Modestus and to permit his freedman to erect a statue in his memory, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500719.translation: On the fourth day before the Nones of September, Quintus Granius Atticus, Marcus Stlaccius Albinus, Aulus Clodius Maximus, Marcus Amullius Lupus, and Marcus Fabius Firmus were present in the curia of the Annian Basilica for the writing of records. Since Titus Aufidius Thrasea and Tiberius Claudius Quartinus, as IIviri, spoke regarding the request of Laelius Atimetus, an excellent citizen, and decreed what should be done concerning it: since Marcus Laelius Atimetus, a most upright man, dear to individuals and the community, requested from our order that the roof terrace of the building he is constructing in the passageway be remitted to him under the condition that the use and enjoyment and possession of his building would belong to him during his lifetime, and afterwards would belong to our republic, it is pleasing to this order that the benevolent intention of so excellent a citizen be accepted and that the roof terrace be remitted to him, because our republic will later gain more from his piety than promised. There were 92 members in the curia.
EDCS-11500719.summary: This inscription records a decree by the IIviri (duumvirs) of a town to grant a request from Marcus Laelius Atimetus for a remission on a roof terrace he is constructing, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500720.inscription_type: tituli operum, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-11500720.translation: To Gavia Marciana, daughter of Marcus, a matron of honorable and incomparable character, wife of Gavia Puteolanus, decurion honored with all offices, and daughter-in-law of Curtius Crispinus, a distinguished Roman equestrian honored with all offices; sister of Gavia Iustus, a distinguished Roman equestrian. Since she was taken from life by an untimely and bitter death on account of her exceptional modesty and admirable chastity, the republic decreed a public funeral for her, as well as a public grant of money for oil and three statues. Marcus Gavius Puteolanus, her honored father, personally financed this, content with his own money. The place was granted by decree of the curia. On the fifth day before the Kalends of November, during the consulship of Lucius Bruttius Crispinus and Lucius Roscio Aelianus, Caepius Proculus and Cossutius Rufinus, and Claudius Priscus and Calpurnius Pistus were present in the temple of the divine Pius for the writing of records. Because Annianus Proculus, an honored man, requested the decree of a public funeral for Gavia Marciana, daughter of Marcus, a woman of good memory, and also the granting of ten pounds of oil and places for three statues which they themselves would choose, in which statues of the same Marciana should be placed according to the same Proculus's request, Publius Manlius Egnatius Laurinus, as duovir, spoke. What seemed fitting to be done in this matter, they decreed as follows: Although each and all of us wished to bestow honor during the lifetime of Curtius Crispinus, our chief magistrate, and likewise of Gavia Puteolanus, his father-in-law, an equally distinguished man, and Gavia Marciana, a most reverent woman, rather than to rush into such a decree, so that we might seek solace for the living, it is also for the purpose of honoring the memory of the girl herself that it is pleasing to this order to decree a public funeral for her and to grant ten pounds of oil, and to permit, according to the request of Annianus, an excellent man, that the places which they choose for setting up three statues should be obtained with our consent.
EDCS-11500720.summary: This lengthy inscription records a decree by the local government to grant a public funeral, oil, and statues for Gavia Marciana, a woman praised for her modesty and chastity, likely in the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500721.translation: To Gavia Fabia Rufina, daughter of Marcus, most honorable and rare woman, wife of Marcus Aurelius [[C[3]]], procurator of the imperial treasury, son of Marcus Gavius Puteolanus, duovir, aedile, curator of the gladiatorial games for four days, and fulfilled of all offices and duties; sister of Marcus Gavius Fabius Iustus, most distinguished Roman equestrian, augur, duovir twice, quaestor twice, curator of the gladiatorial games, and fulfilled of all offices and duties. The republic, with its own money offered...
EDCS-11500721.summary: This is an incomplete funerary dedication for Gavia Fabia Rufina, listing her distinguished family connections and mentioning that the republic offered money for her burial, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500726.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Quintus Aemilius Helpidephorus, decurion, dendrophorus, and Augustalis duplicarius, who lived 3 years, 2 months, and 25 days. [3 Aemilia [3]ina, to her well-deserving husband, made this.
EDCS-11500726.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Quintus Aemilius Helpidephorus, who held several local official positions, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11500727.translation: Gaius Avianius Flaccus, son of Gaius, grandson of Gaius, rebuilt this.
EDCS-11500727.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding of a structure by Gaius Avianius Flaccus, who held the office of duovir, likely in the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500732.translation: For Lucius Calpurnius Capolinus, son of Lucius. For Gaius Calpurnius [6], son of Lucius. Merchants who trade in Alexandria, Asia, Syria.
EDCS-11500732.summary: This inscription lists individuals identified as merchants trading in the eastern Mediterranean, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500733.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Faltonia Procula, priestess, who placed this for herself while alive.
EDCS-11500733.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Faltonia Procula, a priestess who commissioned her own tomb, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500734.translation: To Gaius Fictorius Firmus, son of Gaius, of the Falernian tribe, aedile, quaestor, duovir, curator of public works, curator [---]
EDCS-11500734.summary: This is an incomplete dedication honoring Gaius Fictorius Firmus for his various civic and administrative roles, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11500737.translation: To Heria Polla, daughter of Marcus, the place was given by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11500737.summary: This inscription records the granting of a burial place to Heria Polla by local officials.

EDCS-11500738.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Heria Victorina, the vine-bearer. Marcus Herius Valerianus to his most beloved daughter.
EDCS-11500738.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Marcus Herius Valerianus to his deceased daughter, Heria Victorina.

EDCS-11500739.translation: To Gaius Julius Puteolanus, son of Gaius, who was enrolled in the order of decurions, who lived 17 years, 6 months, and 27 days. Gaius Julius Maximus, his father, consecrated the eternal home of his most beloved son to the spirits of the underworld. If anyone sells or alienates this tomb or should give it away, he will owe the Puteolan public treasury 20,000 sesterces as a penalty.
EDCS-11500739.summary: This is a funerary monument and penal clause inscription for Gaius Julius Puteolanus, detailing his age at death and penalties for disturbing his tomb.

EDCS-11500741.translation: To [---]onio Musculus, the Falernian, priest, flamen of the deified Augustus, prefect of the artisans, quaestor, twice aedile, three times quinquennial aedile. The place was given by decree of the decurions. Bruttia gave it to her husband and his father at her own expense.
EDCS-11500741.summary: This inscription records the granting of a burial place to a prominent local magistrate named Musculus by his wife.

EDCS-11500742.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Numerius Naevius Vitulus, son of Numerius, decurion of Puteoli, and to his most dutiful mother Naevia Saturnina. Numerius Naevius Moschus, Augustalis of Puteoli, consecrated this to his most beloved son Numerius Naevius Vitulus and to his dearest wife.
EDCS-11500742.summary: This funerary inscription from Puteoli is a dedication by Numerius Naevius Moschus to his son, daughter-in-law, and wife.

EDCS-11500745.translation: To Aulus Paccius Rufus, aedile, quinquennial aedile. Scirtus, freedman of Augustus, made this for his comrade, a most upright man.
EDCS-11500745.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Scirtus, a freedman of Augustus, for his friend Aulus Paccius Rufus.

EDCS-11500747.translation: Sabina, priestess of the public Ceres, made this monument for herself while alive.
EDCS-11500747.summary: This is a self-funded funerary monument erected by Sabina, a priestess of Ceres.

EDCS-11500749.translation: A place was given with the authority of Flavius Longinus, a most distinguished man, curator of the public affairs, assigned by Marcus Valerius Pudens, duovir, with the curator, on the 10th day before the Kalends of April, when Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus was consul for the third time and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus was consul for the second time.
EDCS-11500749.summary: This inscription records the granting of a public space, assigned by a duovir and overseen by a curator, during the consulship of Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Verus.

EDCS-11500751.translation: [---] gave this gift with the permission of Gaius Valerius Iustus, son of Publius Nonius Herm[---].
EDCS-11500751.summary: This inscription records a gift given with specific permission from named individuals.

EDCS-11500752.translation: To Marcus Vinicius, son of Publius, after his death his fellow citizens erected this by contributing bronze out of piety.
EDCS-11500752.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument erected by fellow citizens for Marcus Vinicius out of piety after his death.

EDCS-11500758.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-11500758.translation: To Fabia(?) Rufina, mother, curator of the calendaries of the major, and of Clodianus and Minucius, [she?] administered the gilded equestrian statue of the deified Antoninus, and for this, the most distinguished council decreed that he administer the games alone at public expense.
EDCS-11500758.summary: This inscription is a dedication by an unnamed individual to his mother, Fabia Rufina, who held financial administrative roles, and mentions the erection of a gilded statue and games funded by public expense, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-11500803.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Betubia Euresis granted this sepulcher, by way of donation, to Publius Aemilius Conon, a man of the Augustales corporation, a decent man, except for one room; this monument Conon granted to himself and to his freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-11500803.summary: This is a tomb inscription recording the donation of a burial plot by Betubia Euresis to Publius Aemilius Conon, a member of the Augustales, who then reserved it for himself and his descendants, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11500806.translation: Aulus Arrius Chrysanthus, marble worker, Augustalis of Puteoli, dupliciarius, [member of] the century of Petronius, while living, for himself.
EDCS-11500806.summary: This is an epitaph for Aulus Arrius Chrysanthus, a marble worker and Augustalis of Puteoli who held the rank of dupliciarius, likely a self-funded tomb from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11500807.translation: To Gaius Caesanius Eudiaconus, father, cultor of the century of Cornelius, for his merits.
EDCS-11500807.summary: This is an inscription honoring Gaius Caesanius Eudiaconus, referred to as 'father' and 'cultor' of a century, likely a dedication for his services or merits, probably from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11500808.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Antonia Lentybiane made this for her husband, Gnaeus Cornelius Verna, a cherished Augustalis, dupliciarius, who lived 45 years and 2 months. To a most excellent husband, deserving well.
EDCS-11500808.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Antonia Lentybiane for her husband, Gnaeus Cornelius Verna, a dupliciarius and Augustalis, who died at age 45 years and 2 months, dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11500810.translation: He died on the 6th day before the Ides of June, when Pollius and Apro were consuls for the second time. To the spirits of the underworld. Quintus Insteius Diadumenus, an Augustalis, served for 45 years, lived 84 years, 6 months, and 15 days. His heirs.
EDCS-11500810.summary: This epitaph records the death date and lifespan of Quintus Insteius Diadumenus, an Augustalis who lived 84 years and served for 45 years, and was erected by his heirs, dating to the consulship of Pollius and Apro, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11500812.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Gaius Julius Victor, an Augustalis, who lived 30 years.
EDCS-11500812.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Gaius Julius Victor, an Augustalis, who died at the age of 30, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11500813.translation: On the estates of the Augustales corporati, which Lucius Laecanius Primitivus, their perpetual curator, gave to them, so that from their income, a distribution and a banquet should occur annually on his birthday, the eleventh day before the Kalends of January.
EDCS-11500813.summary: This inscription records a donation of estates by Lucius Laecanius Primitivus to the Augustales corporati, with the condition that an annual distribution and banquet be held on his birthday, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11500814.translation: Dedicated on the 3rd day before the Kalends of August, when Pudens and Orfitus were consuls. To Lucius Licinius Primitivus, honored with the dignities of a decurion, curator of the Augustales in perpetuity, by the Augustales corporati, for his perpetual and manifold generosity, and because he administers their affairs and business with integrity. On whose dedication, he gave 12,000 sesterces to individual decurions, 8,000 to Augustales, 6,000 to freedmen and corporati veterans, and 4,000 sesterces to municipals, and a banquet for decurions and Augustales. Likewise, on the day before the Ides of February, on the eve of the ancestral gods' festival, he gave double that amount. The place was granted by decree of the decurions. Under the care of Lucius Laecanius Primitivus.
EDCS-11500814.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to Lucius Licinius Primitivus, a distinguished official and perpetual curator of the Augustales, by the Augustales corporati, detailing his generous donations and banquets funded by him and overseen by Lucius Laecanius Primitivus, dating to the consulship of Pudens and Orfitus (likely 166 CE).

EDCS-11500815.translation: Eubulus, his freedman, placed this in his own fine spot for Lucius Lollius Zotion, Augustalis, dupliciarius.
EDCS-11500815.summary: This epitaph is a dedication by the freedman Eubulus for his patron, Lucius Lollius Zotion, an Augustalis and possibly a dupliciarius, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11500816.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Manlius Epaphroditus, a member of the Augustales, who also served as quinquennalis (a quinquennial official).
EDCS-11500816.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Marcus Manlius Epaphroditus, who held prestigious positions within the Augustales, likely in Puteoli, with the date inferable from his offices.

EDCS-11500817.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Marcio Tauro Augustali fecit se vivo sibi et Corneliae Abascantillae uxori suae et Corneliae Agathonidi bene meritae uxori\n
EDCS-11500817.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Tito Marcius Taurus, a member of the Augustales, made this for himself while alive, and for his wife Cornelia Abascantilla, and for his wife Cornelia Agathonis, who deserved well.
EDCS-11500817.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Tito Marcius Taurus, a member of the Augustales, for himself and his wives, indicating a self-funded burial arrangement.

EDCS-11500819.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Sextus Patulcius Apollonustus, an Augustalis duplarius of Puteoli, and for his wife Pomponia Chrysis. Sextus Patulcius Eunus, his adopted son, (dedicated this) to (them) who deserved well.
EDCS-11500819.summary: This is a funerary dedication by an adopted son for his deceased adoptive parents, Sextus Patulcius Apollonustus (an Augustalis duplarius of Puteoli) and his wife, suggesting a late 1st or early 2nd century AD date.

EDCS-11500820.translation: [Son of Marcus' freedman] Phileros, [an] Augustalis, [dedicated] the temple of Victoria Augusta with his colleagues to the people and protected [it]. [He] also built it with his own money and gave honeyed wine to the people. [He also gave] For you this is not enough to satisfy your will; be well in return. [Son of Marcus' freedman] Atticus, an Augustalis.
EDCS-11500820.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a temple to Victoria Augusta, its construction and decoration, and offerings made by an Augustalis named Phileros and potentially another Augustalis, Atticus, suggesting a date in the imperial period.

EDCS-11500821.translation: Lucius Plutius Eutychio [---], joined with the schools of the century of Petronius.
EDCS-11500821.summary: This inscription possibly records a building or property boundary associated with Lucius Plutius Eutychio and the school or century of Petronius, with the date being indeterminate without further context.
