EDCS-11900086.summary: A dedication to Marcus Aurelius Caesar, son and heir of Antoninus Pius, detailing his imperial lineage and consulship, likely erected during his time as Caesar.

EDCS-11900088.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Antoninus, grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the divine Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 16th time, consul for the 3rd time, for the seven cohorts of vigiles.
EDCS-11900088.summary: An inscription dedicated to Marcus Aurelius by the seven cohorts of vigiles, likely dating to the period he held tribunician power for the 16th time and consulship for the 3rd time.

EDCS-11900096.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Antoninus, grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the divine Nerva, Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul for the 2nd time, for the seven cohorts of vigiles.
EDCS-11900096.summary: An inscription dedicated to Lucius Verus by the seven cohorts of vigiles, likely dating to the period he held tribunician power for the 2nd time and consulship for the 2nd time.

EDCS-11900098.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Commodi Antonini Augusti Iulio Iulian[o] praefecto praetorio Aelio Iuliano praefecto vigilum Opcatiano sub praefecto Rustio Rufino tribuno cohortis VI vigilum Valerio Proculino cohortis IIII vigilum egerunt IIACOH Primi Caius Asinius Victorti Palatina Lucius Antonius LeoRoma Marcus Valerius estus Fabia Roma Idus Iulias Commodo Augusto VI Marco Petronio Septimiano consulibus adiutore cohortis V Hippone regio\n
EDCS-11900098.translation: For the safety of the Emperor Commodus Antoninus Augustus, executed by Iulio Iuliano, praetorian prefect; Aelio Iuliano, prefect of the vigiles; Opcatiano, sub-prefect; Rustio Rufino, tribune of the 6th cohort of vigiles; Valerio Proculino, of the 4th cohort of vigiles, the IIACOH Primi, Gaius Asinius Victorti Palatina, Lucius Antonius Leo Roma, Marcus Valerius estus Fabia Roma, during the consulship of Commodus Augustus VI and Marcus Petronius Septimianus, with the assistance of the 5th cohort of vigiles from Hippone regio.
EDCS-11900098.summary: A dedication for the safety of Emperor Commodus, listing various officials including the prefect of the vigiles and tribunes, and mentioning the 5th cohort of vigiles, dating to the consuls Commodus Augustus VI and Marcus Petronius Septimianus.

EDCS-11900100.text_edition: \n cohortes VII vigilum Fulvio Plautiano praefecto vigilum Cassio Ligure tribuno praeposito vexillationis\n
EDCS-11900100.translation: The seven cohorts of vigiles, to Fulvio Plautiano, prefect of the vigiles, and Cassio Liguro, tribune in charge of a detachment.
EDCS-11900100.summary: An inscription by the seven cohorts of vigiles dedicated to their prefect Fulvio Plautiano and a tribune.

EDCS-11900101.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, happy, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 15th time, Imperator for the 12th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, son of the divine Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the divine Commodus, nephew of the divine Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the divine Nerva, restorer of the camps, for the people of Ostia, under Gnaeus Marcius Rustius Rufinus, prefect of the vigiles, an excellent man, while C(aius) Laecanius Novatillianus, subprefect, and M(arcus) Flavius Raesianus, tribune of the 2nd cohort of vigiles, in charge of a detachment, were managing affairs.
EDCS-11900101.summary: An inscription dedicated to Emperor Septimius Severus by the people of Ostia, mentioning various officials including the prefect of the vigiles and subprefect, and celebrating his military victories and titles.

EDCS-11900106.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, happy, designated consul for the 3rd time, with tribunician power for the 10th time, consul for the 2nd time, son of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, father of the country, nephew of the divine Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the divine Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus and the divine Nerva, restorer of the camps for the people of Ostia, under Gn(aeus) Marcius Rustius Rufinus, prefect of the vigiles, most excellent man, with C(aius) Laecanius Novatilliano, subprefect, and M(arcus) Flavius Raesianus, tribune of the 2nd cohort of vigiles, in charge of a detachment, overseeing.
EDCS-11900106.summary: An inscription dedicated to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (likely Caracalla) by the people of Ostia, referencing his father Septimius Severus and other imperial ancestors, and mentioning the prefect of the vigiles and subprefect.

EDCS-11900107.translation: Dedicated on the day before the Nones of April, in the consulship of Gentianus and Bassus. To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus Britannicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, father of the country, with tribunician power for the 14th time, Imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 3rd time, proconsul, the most courageous and fortunate prince above all, son of the divine Septimius Severus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Britannicus Maximus, nephew of the divine Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the divine Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus and the divine Nerva, under C(aius) Iulius Quintillianus, prefect of the vigiles, an excellent man, while M(arcus) Firmus Amyntianus, subprefect, and M(arcus) Antonius Proculus, tribune of the 6th cohort of vigiles, in charge of a detachment, were managing affairs.
EDCS-11900107.summary: A dedication from April 4th, during the consulship of Gentianus and Bassus, to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (likely Caracalla) by the vigiles, mentioning various imperial titles and officials.

EDCS-11900112.translation: To Marcus Antoninus Diadumenianus, most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, son of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Felix Augustus, with tribunician power, designated consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, proconsul, by Valerius Titanianus, prefect of the vigiles, most eminent man, with Flavius Lupus, subprefect, overseeing.
EDCS-11900112.summary: An inscription dedicated to Caesar Marcus Antoninus Diadumenianus, son of Emperor Macrinus, by Valerius Titanianus, prefect of the vigiles.

EDCS-11900118.summary: A fragmentary inscription that appears to be a dedication to Furia Sabinia Tranquillina.

EDCS-11900120.translation: The people of Ostia, devoted to Publius Licinius Cornelius Valerianus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-11900120.summary: A dedication from the people of Ostia to Caesar Valerian (likely Gallienus or Saloninus).

EDCS-11900164.text_edition: \n Aulus Egrilius Plarianus praefectus aerarii militaris patronus coloniae pontifex Volkani \n
EDCS-11900164.translation: Aulus Egrilius Plarianus, prefect of the military treasury, patron of the colony, pontifex of Vulcan.
EDCS-11900164.summary: This is an honorific inscription for Aulus Egrilius Plarianus, listing his titles and offices.

EDCS-11900166.text_edition: \n Quinto Asinio Quinti filio Tromentina Marcello consuli praetori quaestori Augusti curioni salio Palatino tribuno militum legionis III Augustae Xviro patrono coloniae decurionum decreto publice\n
EDCS-11900166.translation: To Quintus Asinius Tromentinus Marcellus, son of Quintus, consul, praetor, quaestor of Augustus, curio, Salian priest of the Palatine, military tribune of the Legio III Augusta, duovir, patron of the colony, by decree of the decurions, publicly.
EDCS-11900166.summary: This is a public dedication to Quintus Asinius Tromentinus Marcellus, listing his numerous civic and military offices and recognizing him as patron of the colony.

EDCS-11900168.text_edition: \n Fidae sic exercitationem sic bonitati sic pollenti Lucio Crepereio Madaliano viro clarissimo praefecto nnonae cum iure gladii comiti Flaviali correctori Flaminiae et Piceni legato pro praetore provinciae Asiae legato provinciae Africae consulari aedium sacrarum consulari molium fari atque purgaturae quaestori candidato praetori consuli ob multa in se eius testimonia ordo et popu<l=I>us F<l=I>(aviae) Constantinianae Portuenses sic statuam publicae sic ponendam censuerunt \n
EDCS-11900168.translation: In recognition of his integrity, his diligence, his excellence, and his power, the Senate and People of Flavia Constantiana Portuenses resolved to set up this public statue to Lucius Crepereius Madalianus, a most distinguished man, prefect of the grain supply with the power of life and death, count of the Flavians, corrector of Flaminia and Picenum, proconsular governor of the province of Asia, consular governor of the province of Africa, in charge of sacred buildings, in charge of the moles and purification facilities, quaestor designate, praetor, and consul, because of the many testimonies he gave in his own behalf.
EDCS-11900168.summary: This is a decree by the city of Flavia Constantiana Portuenses to erect a statue honoring Lucius Crepereius Madalianus for his distinguished service in various high administrative and military roles.

EDCS-11900169.text_edition: \n Marco Flavio Marci filio Marciano Iliso procuratori monetae Augustorum procuratori aquarum urbis procuratori annonae \n
EDCS-11900169.translation: To Marcus Flavinus Marcianus, son of Marcus, procurator of the mint of the Emperors, procurator of the city's water supply, procurator of the grain supply.
EDCS-11900169.summary: This is an honorific inscription for Marcus Flavinus Marcianus, listing his procuratorial appointments.

EDCS-11900170.text_edition: \n Publio Flavio Publi filio Palatina Prisco egregio viro equestris ordinis religiosa disciplina ad centena provecto pontifici et dictatori Albano primo annos viginti octo agenti quinquennali collegii pistorum patrono coloniae Ostiensium sacerdoti Genii coloniae patrono corporis pistorum corpus mensorum frumentariorum Ostiensium patrono dedicata Kalendis Martis Aemiliano iterum et Aquilino consulibus\n
EDCS-11900170.translation: To Publius Flavius Priscus, son of Publius, of the Palatine tribe, an outstanding man of equestrian rank, promoted to the rank of centurion through religious discipline, pontifex and dictator of Alba, in his first year serving as quinquennial magistrate for twenty-eight years, patron of the colony of Ostia, priest of the Genius of the colony, patron of the guild of bakers, patron of the body of grain measurers of Ostia; dedicated on the Kalends of March, when Aemilianus was consul for the second time and Aquilinus was consul.
EDCS-11900170.summary: This is an honorific inscription for Publius Flavius Priscus, detailing his extensive career in religious, civic, and guild leadership roles in Ostia, dedicated during his consulship.

EDCS-11900173.translation: Manilius Rusticianus, a most distinguished man, prefect of the grain supply, acting in place of the most eminent prefects of the praetorium, curator and patron of the most splendid colony of Ostia, for his loyalty and merits towards the state, the Senate and People of Ostia decreed and established that the city might be made more illustrious by titles of his administration.
EDCS-11900173.summary: The decurions and people of Ostia decreed to honor Manilius Rusticianus, a former prefect of the grain supply, with titles that would bring greater prestige to the city, in recognition of his service.

EDCS-11900196.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ingenuo Augusti liberto tabulario portus Augusti vixit annis XXVIIII mensibus X diebus XIIII Flavia Crispina marito optime de se merito et Flavius Faustus fratri piissimo\n
EDCS-11900196.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Ingenuus, freedman of Augustus, registrar of the port of Augustus. He lived 27 years, 10 months, and 14 days. Flavia Crispina to her husband, who deserved supremely well of her, and Flavius Faustus to his most dutiful brother.
EDCS-11900196.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Ingenuus, a registrar of the port of Augustus, erected by his wife and brother.

EDCS-11900203.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Gargilius Quinti filius Iulianus qui et Semelius miles vixit nnos XXIII menses III dies XIII hunc Karthago lubens puerum et suo nomine Parcae caelesti monstrante dea per caerula ponti Tyrrhenum per iter placida labante karina Italiam misere huic suo numine laeto castrorum decus ac virtutum nobile munus Roma aeterna potens tribuit post talia dona IRE \n
EDCS-11900203.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Quintus Gargilius Iulianus, son of Quintus, also known as Semelius, a soldier. He lived 23 years, 3 months, and 13 days. Carthage willingly sent this boy, and on behalf of his own name, the Fates, showing a celestial goddess, brought him through the blue of the Tyrrhenian sea with a smoothly gliding keel to Italy; to him, gladdened by his own divine favor, eternal and powerful Rome bestowed the glory of the camp and a noble gift of virtues.
EDCS-11900203.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Quintus Gargilius Iulianus, a soldier who died young, with a mythological narrative about his journey to Italy and his posthumous honors from Rome.

EDCS-11900210.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Marius Nepos miles classis praetoriae Ravennatis natione Corsus annos vixit XXVI militavit annos VIII faciendum curavit Titus Monius Crispus secundus heres\n
EDCS-11900210.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Marcus Marius Nepos, a soldier of the praetorian fleet of Ravenna, a Corsican by birth. He lived 26 years and served 8 years. Titus Monius Crispus, the second heir, saw to its construction.
EDCS-11900210.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Marcus Marius Nepos, a soldier in the Ravenna fleet, erected by his second heir.

EDCS-11900214.text_edition: \n Lucio Venuleio Aproniano II Lucio Sergio Paullo II consulibus Marco Bassaeo Rufo praefecto Lucio Aemilio Rufo tribuno cohortis tribuno cohortis VII ||(centurione) Quintio V Martiale cohortis VII Rufrio cohortis II Iuventio Rixion qui descenderunt Idibus Decembribus Caio Aemilio CIIN Lucius Herennius Luci libertus Scaptia Vindex Florentia frumentum publicum accipit die IX tabula LV kapite centesimo Lucius Pomponius Luci filius Falerna Urbanus Capua frumentum publicum accipit die VII tabula LXIX kapite centesimo Numerius Aerius Numeri filius Iulius Communis Ferentio frumentum publicum accipit die VII tabula LXIX kapite centesimo Quintus Aufidenus Quinti filius Crustumina Severianus Tudere frumentum publicum accipit die XXII <t=O>(abula) XLIV kapite centesimo Tiberius Claudius Tiberi libertus Camilia Stephanus Ravenna frumentum publicum accipit die Marcus Tannius Marci libertus Iulius Feculiaris Aemilia frumentum publicum accipit die Quintus Antonius Quinti libertus Iulius Coriscus Luco Feroniae frumentum publicum accipit die titulus dedicatus VI Idus Martias isdem consulibus \n
EDCS-11900214.translation: In the consulship of Lucius Venuleius Apronianus II and Lucius Sergius Paullus II, when Marcus Bassaeus Rufus was prefect, Lucius Aemilius Rufus was tribune of the cohort, Quintus Martialis was centurion of the VII cohort, Rufrius was tribune of the II cohort, [and] Iuventius Rixion descended on the Ides of December. Caius Aemilius [---]. Lucius Herennius Vindex, freedman of Lucius, of the Scaptian tribe, from Florence, received public grain on the 9th day, tablet 55, per head the hundredth. Lucius Pomponius Urbanus, son of Lucius, of the Falernian tribe, from Capua, received public grain on the 7th day, tablet 69, per head the hundredth. Numerius Aerius Communis, son of Numerius, of the Iulian tribe, from Ferentino, received public grain on the 7th day, tablet 69, per head the hundredth. Quintus Aufidenus Severianus, son of Quintus, of the Crustuminian tribe, from Tudera, received public grain on the 22nd day, tablet 44, per head the hundredth. Tiberius Claudius Stephanus, freedman of Tiberius, of the Camilian tribe, from Ravenna, received public grain on the [---] day. Marcus Tannius Feculiaris, freedman of Marcus, of the Aemilian tribe, received public grain on the [---] day. Quintus Iulius Coriscus, freedman of Quintus Antonius, of the Iulian tribe, from Luco Feroniae, received public grain on the [---] day. The inscription was dedicated on the 6th day before the Ides of March, in the same consulship.
EDCS-11900214.summary: This is a lengthy record of grain distribution in various locations under specific consuls and prefects, also listing officials and dedicating the record on a specific date.

EDCS-11900215.text_edition: \n Cohors III centuria Prisci Prisco et Apollinare consulibus hi desciderunt Ostis Idibus Augustis io Pomponiano tribuno cohortis VI ntano |(centurione) Albanio Aphro tione IEM FR\n
EDCS-11900215.translation: Cohort III, century of Priscus. With Priscus and Apollinaris as consuls, these men perished in Ostia on the Ides of August, under Tribune of Cohort VI Pomponianus, [centurion] Albanius Afro.
EDCS-11900215.summary: This inscription records the deaths of soldiers from Cohort III, century of Priscus, with specific consuls and tribunes mentioned, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11900216.text_edition: \n Cohors V centuria Respecti Imperatore Commodo III et Byrro sic consulibus Sempronio Laeto praefecto Sempronio Urbano subpraefecto I qui desciderunt suc sic cura Iuni Reburri tribuni cohortis III Tetti Paulini |(centurionis) cohortis V et optione Cominio Fausto cohortis easdem sic ex Idus sic Augustas sic in Idus Decembres Sextus Iulius Sexti filius a Lucius Domitius Luci libertus Seianus Caius Refidius Cai filius Sabinus Marcus Lucius Marci filius Vales Marcus Aurelius Marci libertus Aelianus Quintus Annius Quinti filius Verus Tiberius Claudius Tiberi libertus He dedicat-- Kalend-- \n
EDCS-11900216.translation: Cohort V, century of Respectus. With Emperor Commodus (consul for the 3rd time) and Byrrus as consuls, under Prefect Sempronius Laetus, Subprefect Sempronius Urbanus. These men perished, from the care of Tribune of Cohort III Iunius Reburrus, centurion of Cohort V Tettius Paulinus, and Optio of the same cohort Cominius Faustus, from the Ides of August to the Ides of December. Sextus Iulius Sexti son, Lucius Domitius Lucius' freedman Seianus, Caius Refidius Cai's son Sabinus, Marcus Lucius Marcus' son Vales, Marcus Aurelius Marcus' freedman Aelianus, Quintus Annius Quintus' son Verus, Tiberius Claudius Tiberius' freedman He dedicates... Kalends...
EDCS-11900216.summary: This inscription details the deaths of several soldiers from Cohort V, century of Respectus, during the consulship of Emperor Commodus and Byrrus, and lists their patrons and the period of their service from August to December.

EDCS-11900218.text_edition: \n Cohors III centuria Carpiani Felix sic Mamertino et Rufo consulibus i qui desci<v=D>erunt Ostiis in vexillatione ex Idibus Decembribus in Idus Apriles tabula CXXVII Lucius Critonius Luci filius Trophimus frumentum publicum accepit die VIII ostio X Lucius Tullius Luci filius Felicissimus incisus frumento publico die IIII ostio X Marcus Aemilius Marci filius Hermes incisus frumento publico die IIII ostio X Quintus Faeronius Quinti filius Castor Publius Brittius Publi filius Felixs sic Lucius Fannius Luci filius Saturnalis [] Quintus Vibidius Quinti filius Timocrates Imperatore Commodo IIII et Victorino II consulibus dedicata VII Idus Apriles \n
EDCS-11900218.translation: Cohort III, century of Carpianus. With Felix Mamertinus and Rufus as consuls, who perished in Ostia in the vexillation from the Ides of December to the Ides of April. Tablet 127. Lucius Critonius Lucius' son Trophimus received the public grain on the 8th day of Ostia X. Lucius Tullius Lucius' son Felicissimus inscribed for public grain on the 4th day of Ostia X. Marcus Aemilius Marcus' son Hermes inscribed for public grain on the 4th day of Ostia X. Quintus Faeronius Quintus' son Castor, Publius Brittius Publi son Felix, Lucius Fannius Lucius' son Saturnalis. Quintus Vibidius Quintus' son Timocrates. Dedicated under Emperor Commodus (consul for the 4th time) and Victorinus (consul for the 2nd time) on the 7th day before the Kalends of April.
EDCS-11900218.summary: This inscription from Ostia records soldiers from Cohort III, century of Carpianus, who perished while serving in a vexillation, and also lists individuals who received public grain, with a dedication date in the consulship of Commodus IV and Victorinus II.

EDCS-11900243.translation: Cohort VI, century of Ostia. Under Emperor Antoninus, with Laetus and Cerialis as consuls. Sebacarius Calpurnius X (inscribed) for decennalia vows.
EDCS-11900243.summary: This inscription from Ostia records a soldier from Cohort VI, century of Ostia, making decennalia vows during the consulship of Laetus and Cerialis under Emperor Antoninus.

EDCS-11900287.text_edition: \n Clodius Primigenius Claudius Crescens quinquennales stuppatores restiones\n
EDCS-11900287.translation: Clodius Primigenius, Claudius Crescens, quinquennales, stuppatores, restiones.
EDCS-11900287.summary: This inscription lists two individuals, Clodius Primigenius and Claudius Crescens, identified as quinquennales and stuppatores (tar-makers/caulkers), possibly related to a guild or public service.

EDCS-11900313.translation: Caius Bibius Felicianus gave as a gift to the youth.
EDCS-11900313.summary: This is a dedication by Caius Bibius Felicianus, offering something to the youth, likely a religious or public benefaction.

EDCS-11900314.text_edition: \n Lucio Aelio Aurelio Commodo Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii patris patriae filio Marcus Marius Marci filius Palatina Primitivus decurionum decreto aedilis II sacris Volkani faciundis corpori traiectus Rusticeli sua pecunia dono dedit dedicata XIII Kalendas Octobres Imperatore Caesare Antonino IIII Marco Aurelio Caesare II consulibus\n
EDCS-11900314.translation: Marcus Marius Primitivus, son of Marcus, of the Palatina tribe, by decree of the decurions, as aedile for the second time for the sacred rites of Vulcan, to be performed by the body of Rusticelius, gave as a gift from his own money. Dedicated on the 13th day before the Kalends of October, with Emperor Caesar Antoninus (consul for the 4th time) and Marcus Aurelius Caesar (consul for the 2nd time) as consuls.
EDCS-11900314.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Marcus Marius Primitivus, an aedile, of a sacrifice to Vulcan for the body of Rusticelius, funded from his own resources, during the consulship of Antoninus IV and Marcus Aurelius Caesar II.

EDCS-11900320.text_edition: \n Dedicata VII Idus Septembres Attico et Praetextato consulibus per qqaestores Caio Tannonio Sabbatio rogatione e Iulio curantibus Aulo Egrilio Leontio Caio Po Caio Mundio Felice Caio E\n
EDCS-11900320.translation: Dedicated on the 7th day before the Ides of September, with Atticus and Praetextatus as consuls, by the quaestors Caius Tannonius Sabbatius and Julius, under the supervision of Aulus Egrilius Leontius, Caius Po, Caius Mundius Felix, Caius E.
EDCS-11900320.summary: This inscription records a dedication made on the 7th day before the Ides of September during the consulship of Atticus and Praetextatus, overseen by quaestors and other officials.

EDCS-11900346.text_edition: \n Cnaeo Claudio Severo II Tiberio Claudio Pompeiano II ordo corporatorum us Papa Publius Anteius us Fructus Marcus Campunnius s Felicissimus Titus Pompeius C us Victor Publius Arruntius us Servilio Quintus Vennius Marcus Campunnius Agathopus Marcus Sulpicius Maior us Bradua Lucius Laecanius Alexander us Deuter Marcus Marius Primitivos sic us Victor Cnaeus Rullius Felix us Hilarus Quintus Cerellius Marinus s Fortunatus Aulus Caedicius Callistianus s Corinthianus Caius Aninius Primus us Crispinus Quintus Tadius Cerialis Callistio Marcus Ulpius Fortunatus IIS Marcus nnius Primigenius Marcus nnius Restitutus RI Septimio PO annonae \n
EDCS-11900346.translation: Under Gnaeus Claudius Severus (consul for the 2nd time) and Tiberius Claudius Pompeianus (consul for the 2nd time), the order of corporators, Papa Publius Anteius, Fructus, Marcus Campunnius Felicissimus, Titus Pompeius Victor, Publius Arruntius Servilius, Quintus Vennius, Marcus Campunnius Agathopus, Marcus Sulpicius Maior, Bradua, Lucius Laecanius Alexander, Deuter, Marcus Marius Primitivos, Victor, Gnaeus Rullius Felix, Hilarus, Quintus Cerellius Marinus, Fortunatus, Aulus Caedicius Callistianus, Corinthianus, Caius Aninius Primus, Crispinus, Quintus Tadius Cerialis, Callistio, Marcus Ulpius Fortunatus, Marcus Annius Primigenius, Marcus Annius Restitutus, [officials of the] annona.
EDCS-11900346.summary: This inscription lists numerous members of the 'ordo corporatorum' and officials related to the annona, serving during the second consulship of Gnaeus Claudius Severus and Tiberius Claudius Pompeianus.

EDCS-11900351.text_edition: \n Locus adsignatus a Callisto Augusti liberto procuratore cultoribus Larum et imaginum dominorum nostrorum invictissimorum Augustorum praediorum Rusticelianorum ad sollemnes dies confrequentandos curante Maximiano Augusti nostri verna vilico eorundem praediorum sicut litteris ab eodem Callisto emissis continentur dedicatus Kalendis Iunis Imperatore Antonino Pio Felice Augusto II consule exemplum libelli Callistus Maximiano libellum datum mihi a cultoribus Larum Augustorum ad te misi opportuerat te in tam religiosam rem ipse sic etiam omne sollicitudine adhibuisse ut locus olim consacratus confrequentetur pro salute dominorum nnostrorum Auggustorum quod vel nunc etiam volentibus cultoribus facere intervenire cura ut sine recrastione mundetur\n
EDCS-11900351.translation: A place was assigned by Callistus, freedman of Augustus, procurator for the cultors of the Lares and images of our invincible Lords Augusti of the Rusticelian estates, for gathering on solemn days, under the care of Maximianus, overseer of our Emperor's Rusticelian estates, as is contained in the letters issued by the same Callistus. Dedicated on the Kalends of June, with Emperor Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus (consul for the 2nd time) as consul. Callistus to Maximianus: I have sent to you the libellus given to me by the cultors of the Augusti's Lares, as it was fitting that you yourself should take care in so religious a matter and apply all diligence so that the place, once consecrated, may be frequented for the health of our Lords Augusti, which even now the cultors are willing to do; ensure that it is cleaned without delay.
EDCS-11900351.summary: This inscription details the assignment of a sacred space by the procurator Callistus for the worship of household gods and imperial images at the Rusticelian estates, dedicated on the Kalends of June during the second consulship of Antoninus Pius, with instructions for its maintenance and use.

EDCS-11900353.text_edition: \n Probo consule ordo quibus senatus consulto ord corporatorum Saturninus Panfilus sic Gaius Rothus Cucumer Larcidius Magnus Aemilius Saturninus Quietius Feli quaestor Plotius Petru Aurelius Iustin Varius \n
EDCS-11900353.translation: Under the consulship of Probus, the council, by decree of the senate, of the guild of corporators: Saturninus Panfilus, Gaius Rothus Cucumer, Larcidius Magnus, Aemilius Saturninus, Quietius Feli, quaestor Plotius Petru, Aurelius Iustin, Varius.
EDCS-11900353.summary: This inscription lists members of a guild (corporatores) under the consulship of Probus, likely for administrative or commemorative purposes.

EDCS-11900354.text_edition: \n Lucio Virio Lupo et Lucio Mario Maximo consulibus corpus Fontanorum quod ex senatus consulto coire licet PAIISII Publius Antonius Chariton obitus ius CEM bius Grumentinus onius Myrismus Tiberius Claudius Epagathus EBS Titus Iulius Felix Marcus CIius Dionysius s Thalamus Volusius Saturninus IVS Antin Petronius O Lucius Petronius Myrismus Lucius Aelius Athenodorus Publius Iulius Geminus Publius Iulius Grumentinus - Petronius Myrismus iunior ulius Satyrus afius Dalis lius Hilarus iccius Mithres vius Cognitus ius Hesper s Liberalis Grumentinus iunior nus cissimus Martialis Pagatus mus VI Publius Petron<i=L>us Felix Lucius Pompeius Quintinus Gaius Iulius Saturus Naevius Catullinus Quintus Aurelius Vitalio Caecilius Sotericus Publius Sextillus Seberianus sic Lucius Flavius Onesimianus \n
EDCS-11900354.translation: Under the consulship of Lucius Virius Lupus and Lucius Marius Maximus, the body of water managers, which is permitted to assemble by decree of the senate: Publius Antonius Chariton, Marius Grumentinus, Onius Myrismus, Tiberius Claudius Epagathus, Titus Iulius Felix, Marcus Clius Dionysius, Thalamus Volusius Saturninus, Antius Petronius O, Lucius Petronius Myrismus, Lucius Aelius Athenodorus, Publius Iulius Geminus, Publius Iulius Grumentinus, Petronius Myrismus the younger, Julius Satyrus, Afius Dalis, Helius Hilarus, Iccius Mithres, Vius Cognitus, Hesper, Liberalis Grumentinus the younger, Martialis Pagatus, VI Publius Petronius Felix, Lucius Pompeius Quintinus, Gaius Iulius Saturus, Naevius Catullinus, Quintus Aurelius Vitalio, Caecilius Sotericus, Publius Sextillus Seberianus, Lucius Flavius Onesimianus.
EDCS-11900354.summary: This inscription lists members of a body responsible for managing water resources (corpus Fontanorum) under specific consuls, indicating an organized administrative function.

EDCS-11900396.text_edition: \n osaHostilinianus IIvir quaestor aerarii Ostiensium flamen decreto decurionum curator lusus iuvenalis qui primus omnium ab urbe condita ludos cum or et mulieres ad ferrum dedit una cum Sabina uxore fecit sibi et nio agonio corporis togat um qui et \n
EDCS-11900396.translation: Hostilinianus, duovir, quaestor of the treasury of Ostia, flamen, by decree of the decurions, curator of the youth games, who for the first time of all since the founding of the city gave games with men and women for the arena, made for himself and ... together with his wife Sabina ... for the body of toga-clad ... who also ...
EDCS-11900396.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a games festival, including both men and women, by a local magistrate (duovir) and curator of youth games in Ostia, highlighting civic entertainment and social participation.

EDCS-11900399.text_edition: \n Publio Aufidio Publi filio Quirina Forti IIviro quaestori aerarii Ostiensis V patrono coloniae Faustianus Epictetus Eufrosynus sic Ianuarius patrono indulgentissimo\n
EDCS-11900399.translation: To Publius Aufidius Fortis, son of Publius, of the Quirina tribe, duovir, quaestor of the Ostian treasury, patron of the colony, Faustianus Epictetus, Eufrosynus, Ianuarius, to a most indulgent patron.
EDCS-11900399.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Publius Aufidius Fortis, a patron of the colony of Ostia, by freedmen and associates, recognizing his benefaction and public service.

EDCS-11900400.text_edition: \n Publio Aufidio Publi filio Quirina Forti Aufidi Fortis patroni coloniae filio IIviro quaestori aedili flamini Romae et Augusti flamini divi Titi Faustianus Epictetus Euphrosynus Ianuarius liberti\n
EDCS-11900400.translation: To Publius Aufidius Fortis, son of Publius, son of Aufidius Fortis, patron of the colony, duovir, quaestor, aedile, flamen of Rome and Augustus, flamen of the deified Titus, Faustianus Epictetus, Euphrosynus, Ianuarius, his freedmen.
EDCS-11900400.summary: This inscription records the dedication to Publius Aufidius Fortis, a prominent local official in Ostia who held multiple religious and civic roles, made by his freedmen.

EDCS-11900404.text_edition: \n Decimo Iunio Decimi filio Palatina Bubalo Impetrato equiti Romano decurioni coloniae Ostiensis decurioni Laurentium vicanorum Arulensi praetori primo sacris Volkani faciundis \n
EDCS-11900404.translation: To Decimus Iunius Bubalus, son of Decimus, of the Palatina tribe, who obtained Roman equestrian rank, decurion of the colony of Ostia, decurion of the villagers of Laurentum Arulensis, praetor, first in performing the rites of Vulcan.
EDCS-11900404.summary: This inscription honors Decimus Iunius Bubalus, a Roman equestrian and local official in Ostia and Laurentum, recognizing his civic and religious duties.

EDCS-11900422.text_edition: \n Caio Domitio Luci filio Fabio sic Hermogeni\n
EDCS-11900422.translation: To Gaius Domitius Hermogenes, son of Lucius, of the Fabio tribe.
EDCS-11900422.summary: A simple dedication to Gaius Domitius Hermogenes, son of Lucius, of the Fabio tribe, likely part of a tomb or public monument.

EDCS-11900443.text_edition: \n decurioni adlecto aedili designato quaestori alimentorum flamini divi Vespasiani Titus Rubrius Eupator filio piissimo locus datus decreto decurionum publice\n
EDCS-11900443.translation: To Titus Rubrius Eupator, son of Titus, most dutiful son, decurion, having been added to the council, designated aedile, quaestor of the alimenta, flamen of the deified Vespasian, a place was granted by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-11900443.summary: This inscription records the granting of a public burial place to Titus Rubrius Eupator, a local Ostian official who held various civic and religious positions, by decree of the decurions.

EDCS-11900457.translation: Sacred. Marcianus, son of Gaius, of the Palatina tribe, Roman equestrian, decurion of the colony of Ostia, having fulfilled offices, of the people of Laurentum Lavinas. In fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11900457.summary: This is a votive inscription for Marcianus, a Roman equestrian and decurion of Ostia who also served in Laurentum, indicating a personal dedication made in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-11900481.text_edition: \n Caius Antistius Cai filius Cai nepos Vetus Caius Valerius Luci filius Flaccus Tanur Publius Vergilius Marci filius Pontianus Publius Catienus Publi filius Sabinus Tiberius Vergilius Tiberi filius Rufus curatores riparum et alvei Tiberis ex senatus consulto terminaverunt recto rigore longum pedes \n
EDCS-11900481.translation: Gaius Antistius Vetus, son of Gaius, grandson of Gaius, Gaius Valerius Flaccus Tanur, son of Lucius, Publius Vergilius Pontianus, son of Marcus, Publius Catienus Sabinus, son of Publius, Tiberius Vergilius Rufus, son of Tiberius, curators of the banks and channel of the Tiber, by decree of the senate, marked the boundary with straight rigor, a length of feet.
EDCS-11900481.summary: This inscription records the work of Tiber, river curators appointed by senatorial decree, who demarcated the river's banks and channel, likely for flood control or navigation.

EDCS-11900490.text_edition: \n <G=C>laucen vectat equus transmittens aequora nando quippe vehi remis copia nulla datur Flavius Octabius sic vir clarissimus praefectus annonae curavit Flavius Octavius vir clarissimus praefectus annonae curavit \n
EDCS-11900490.translation: Glaucus carries the horse, swimming across the waters, since there is no possibility of being carried by oars. Flavius Octabius, a most distinguished man, prefect of the annona, took care of this. Flavius Octabius, a most distinguished man, prefect of the annona, took care of this.
EDCS-11900490.summary: This inscription records a prefect of the annona commissioning a work, likely related to transport or infrastructure, as indicated by the imagery of a horse swimming across water and the mention of oars.

EDCS-11900519.text_edition: \n locus datus decreto decurionum ob dedicationem ebus singulis |(denarios III VI Kalendas Iulias Iuventio Celso II Neratio Marcello II consulibus\n
EDCS-11900519.translation: A space was granted by decree of the decurions for the dedication of individual objects, for 3 denarii, on the 6th day before the Kalends of July, in the consulship of Iuventius Celsus II and Neratius Marcellus II.
EDCS-11900519.summary: This is a record from municipal administration, granting a space for dedications of objects and specifying a price and date, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11900543.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Felixs sic Augusti libertus fecit Aeliae Niceni coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-11900543.translation: To the departed spirits. Publius Aelius Felix, freedman of Augustus, made this for Aelia Nicene, his deserving wife.
EDCS-11900543.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a freedman for his wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11900544.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Maximus fecit sibi et Veiania Iotape et libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum item Lucio Genucio Aepaphrodito sic et Mariae Deuterae et posterisque eorum cubiculum hypogeum eis donatum concessoque itum ambitum transitum per porticum aeis sic a Publio Aelio Maximo\n
EDCS-11900544.translation: To the departed spirits. Publius Aelius Maximus made this for himself and for Veiania Iotape and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants, and also for Lucius Genucius Aepaphroditus and for Maria Deutera and for their descendants, a bedroom and an underground chamber donated to them, with granted right of way, passage, and access through the portico, by Publius Aelius Maximus.
EDCS-11900544.summary: A funerary monument inscription detailing the dedication of burial spaces and associated rights of way by Publius Aelius Maximus for himself, his family, freedmen, and others, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11900567.text_edition: \n Marco Antistio Melitoni filio dulcissimo qui vixit anno I mensibus X diebus XIII Marcus Antistius Epictetus fecit sibi et Arriae Melitine uxori sanctissimae et liberis suis et Iuniae Epictesi filiae suae naturali sarcofagum unum in pavimento et Flaviae Erotidi coniugi sanctissimae feminae et libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum\n
EDCS-11900567.translation: For Marcus Antistius Melito, my dearest son, who lived for 1 year, 10 months, and 13 days. Marcus Antistius Epictetus made this for himself and for Arria Melitine, his most sacred wife, and for his children, and for his natural daughter Iunia Epictesi, one sarcophagus in the floor, and for Flavia Erotis, his most sacred wife, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-11900567.summary: A complex funerary epitaph and testament detailing the burial arrangements made by Marcus Antistius Epictetus for his family, including a deceased son, wife, other children, and freedmen, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11900571.text_edition: \n I S io Artemidoro vixit annos XVII menses XI dies XXI us Calogaerus sic o fecit et rae coniugi libertis libertabusque posterisque e<o=D>rum hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur \n
EDCS-11900571.translation: To Isidorus Artemidorus, who lived 17 years, 11 months, and 21 days. Calogaerus made this for himself and for his wife, freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants. This monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-11900571.summary: A funerary epitaph for Isidorus Artemidorus, erected by Calogaerus for his family and freedmen, with a clause stating the monument is not inheritable, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11900585.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelia Che[---] for Volusius Secular, her husband, and for Ralvenna, her relative [---].
EDCS-11900585.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph dedicated to a husband and a relative by Aurelia Che[---], dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11900597.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caesellia Luci filia Macrina fecit sibi et Marco Valerio <F=E>elici et <F=E>lavae Primitivae filis libertis libertabus posterisque eorum hoc monumentum heredem exterum non sequetur si qui post obitum Macrinae corpus exterum intulerit inferet aerario populi Romani HS Mdecem milia nummum in fronte pedes XI in agro pedes XI \n
EDCS-11900597.translation: To the departed spirits. Caesellia Macrina, daughter of Lucius, made this for herself and for Marcus Valerius Felicus and for Flava Primitiva, her sons, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants. This monument will not pass to an external heir; if anyone after Macrina's death brings an external body into the tomb, they will pay a fine of 10,000 sesterces to the treasury of the Roman people. 11 feet in front, 11 feet in the field.
EDCS-11900597.summary: A detailed funerary epitaph with inheritance restrictions and a penalty clause, set up by Caesellia Macrina for her family and descendants, indicating her status and concerns about her burial plot.

EDCS-11900603.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Calpenius Hermes fecit sibi et suis et libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum et Antistiae Coetonidi coniugi suae hoc monumentum heredem externum non sequetur cubiculum intrantibus ad dextram et foras in pavimento sarcophagae sic et contra et laeva parietibus duobus aediculas cum ollis et sarcophagis fecit\n
EDCS-11900603.translation: To the departed spirits. Caius Calpenius Hermes made this for himself and for his family and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants, and for Antistia Coetonis, his wife. This monument will not pass to an external heir. He made a bedroom to the right upon entering, and outside in the floor a sarcophagus, and opposite and on the left, on two walls, chapels with urns and sarcophagi.
EDCS-11900603.summary: A funerary monument inscription by Caius Calpenius Hermes detailing the construction and allocation of burial spaces and features for his family, freedmen, and himself, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11900632.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberius Claudius Tiberi filius Iulianus fecit sibi et Claudiae Donatae matri carissimae et Tiberio Claudio Demetrio fratri carissimo qui vixit annis XXI mensibus VIII diebus XIIII et Claudiae Iulianae filiae dulcissimae et libertis libertabus posterisque eorum in fronte pedes XIII in agro\n
EDCS-11900632.translation: To the departed spirits. Tiberius Claudius Iulianus, son of Tiberius Claudius, made this for himself and for Claudia Donata, his dearest mother, and for Tiberius Claudius Demetrius, his dearest brother, who lived 21 years, 8 months, and 14 days, and for Claudia Iuliana, his sweetest daughter, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants. 13 feet in front, [---] in the field.
EDCS-11900632.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by Tiberius Claudius Iulianus for his mother, brother, daughter, and their freedmen, providing details about the dimensions of the burial plot and the lifespan of his brother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11900692.text_edition: \n Lucius Fabius Aquilinus et Hermione fecerunt suae et coniugi suae quae vix(it) annos XXVIII menses VI dies XVII \n
EDCS-11900692.translation: Lucius Fabius Aquilinus and Hermione made this for themselves and for his wife, who lived 28 years, 6 months, and 17 days.
EDCS-11900692.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication made by Lucius Fabius Aquilinus and Hermione for themselves and for Hermione, specifying her lifespan.

EDCS-11900708.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Flavius Carpus [made this] for Geminia Euchariste, his well-deserving wife, and for Geminia Pantheris, his most dutiful daughter.
EDCS-11900708.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by Titus Flavius Carpus to his wife Geminia Euchariste and his daughter Geminia Pantheris.

EDCS-11900709.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Flavius Hermes fecit sibi et suis libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum\n
EDCS-11900709.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Flavius Hermes made this for himself and for his freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-11900709.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication of a monument by Lucius Flavius Hermes for himself, his freedpeople, and their descendants.

EDCS-11900716.text_edition: \n Decimus Folius Decimi libertus Mela in fronte pedes XXV in agro pedes XX\n
EDCS-11900716.translation: Mela, freedman of Decimus Follius, [dedicated this monument with a frontage of] 25 feet and a depth in the field of 20 feet.
EDCS-11900716.summary: This inscription records the dimensions of a plot of land acquired by Mela, freedman of Decimus Follius, likely for a tomb.

EDCS-11900748.text_edition: \n Iulius Artemidorus Mrus locum sibi concessum a fronte L Iulio Aristobuloae bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-11900748.translation: Iulius Artemidorus Mrus made [this monument on] the plot granted to him, with a frontage of 50 feet, for Iulius Aristobuloa, who deserved well.
EDCS-11900748.summary: This inscription records Iulius Artemidorus Mrus dedicating a monument on a plot of land granted to him, for Iulius Aristobuloa.

EDCS-11900751.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Euplo vixit nnos XL horas II A Q Pistori Ianuario\n
EDCS-11900751.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Iulius Euplus lived 40 years and 2 hours. [This was made by] A. Q. Pistorius Ianvarius.
EDCS-11900751.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Gaius Iulius Euplus and is dedicated by A. Q. Pistorius Ianvarius.

EDCS-11900757.text_edition: \n Iulia Tiberi filia Dionysia vixit annos XII menses VIIII dies XIII\n
EDCS-11900757.translation: Iulia Dionysia, daughter of Tiberius, lived 12 years, 9 months, and 13 days.
EDCS-11900757.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Iulia Dionysia, daughter of Tiberius.

EDCS-11900802.text_edition: \n Lucius Mindius Dius fecit sibi et Genuciae Tryphaenae coniugi incomparabili cum qua vixit annis XXIIII mensibus III et Lucceiae Ianuariae maritae et Anniae Laveriae contuvernali sic suae sanctissimae et libertis libertabusque suis posterisque eorum hoc monumentum externum heredem non sequetur in fronte pedes XXX in agro pedes XXXI\n
EDCS-11900802.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Mindius Dius made this for himself and for his incomparable wife Genucia Tryphaena, with whom he lived 24 years, 3 months, and for his wife Luccia Ianuaria, and for his most holy companion Annia Laveria, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. This external monument will not pass to an heir; frontage 30 feet, depth in the field 31 feet.
EDCS-11900802.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Lucius Mindius Dius dedicating a monument for himself, his wives Genucia Tryphaena and Luccia Ianuaria, his companion Annia Laveria, and their freedpeople and descendants, with specified dimensions and a clause about inheritance.

EDCS-11900812.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Neratius Antas et Clodia Eusebia fecerunt sibi et Lucio Neratio Nicosstrato sic filio qui vixit nnos XXIII et Neratiae Eusebiae filiae dulcissimae quae vixit nnos XIII menses III et libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum si quis AVTEMIHORVM boluerit sic locum vel donare vel vendere extero hunc iubeo inferre aerario populi Romani poenae nomine sestertia milia nummum\n
EDCS-11900812.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Neratius Antas and Clodia Eusebia made this for themselves and for their son Lucius Neratius Nicosstratus, who lived 23 years, and for their sweetest daughter Neratia Eusebia, who lived 13 years and 3 months, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. If any of them wishes to donate or sell the plot to an outsider, I order that the sum of 10,000 sesterces be paid to the treasury of the Roman people as a penalty.
EDCS-11900812.summary: This funerary inscription by Lucius Neratius Antas and Clodia Eusebia dedicates a monument for themselves, their children, and freedpeople, including a strict clause concerning the sale or donation of the plot to outsiders.

EDCS-11900816.translation: To the departed spirits and ashes of Nonia Pontica.
EDCS-11900816.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph dedicated to Nonia Pontica.

EDCS-11900825.translation: To the departed spirits. Quintus Ostorius Ostorianus, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, lived [3] years, XI (?) months, [3] days.
EDCS-11900825.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Quintus Ostorius Ostorianus, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11900839.translation: Po<m=N>peia made [this] and for [her] daughter who lived [3] years.
EDCS-11900839.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning a woman named Pompeia and her daughter, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11900860.translation: To the departed spirits. Publicius Glaucus made [this] for his dearest brother, Publicius Florus, who lived 28 years, 18 days. The burial plots were granted by Flavius Crescentianus, patron, son, and brother.
EDCS-11900860.summary: This funerary inscription records the erection of a monument by Publicius Glaucus for his brother Publicius Florus, noting plot acquisition from a patron, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11900883.text_edition: \n Scantia Cai filia Silvina fecit sibi et Lucio Scantio Luci filio Eparchico filio suo et Sulpiciae Marci filiae Iulittae nepoti suae et libertis suis libertabusque posterisque eorum hoc monumentum heredem externum non sequetur in fronte pedes XII in agro pedes XIII\n
EDCS-11900883.translation: Scantia Silvina, daughter of Caius, made [this] for herself, for her son Lucius Scantius Eparchicus, son of Lucius, and for her granddaughter Sulpicia Iulitta, daughter of Marcus, and for her freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. This monument will not pass to an external heir. 12 feet in front, 13 feet in depth.
EDCS-11900883.summary: This is a private funerary monument inscription set up by Scantia Silvina for herself and her descendants, including specific dimensions and a clause preventing external inheritance, likely from the late 1st century CE to the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11900907.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Suavius Geta et Marcus Suavius Fortunatus fecerunt Marco Suavio Getae et Aristiae Fortunatae parentibus bene merentibus et Suaviae Eutaliae et Suaviae Fortunatae et Suaviae Urbanae sororibus et libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum hoc monumentum heredem externum non sequetur\n
EDCS-11900907.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Suavius Geta and Marcus Suavius Fortunatus made [this] for their well-deserving parents Marcus Suavius Geta and Aristia Fortunata, and for their sisters Suavia Eutalia, Suavia Fortunata, and Suavia Urbana, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. This monument will not pass to an external heir.
EDCS-11900907.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument erected by Marcus Suavius Geta and Marcus Suavius Fortunatus for their parents, sisters, and descendants, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11900940.text_edition: \n Caius Beianius sic Philippus Beianae sic Iotapes sic libertus sarcofagii sibe sic Tacliae Corinthiadi coniugi\n
EDCS-11900940.translation: Gaius Beianius, called Philippus, for Beiana, called Iotapes, his freedman, for himself and for Taclia Corinthias, his wife, [and] his freedman.
EDCS-11900940.summary: This funerary inscription records the erection of a sarcophagus by Gaius Beianius (Philippus) for himself, his wife Taclia Corinthias, and his freedmen, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11900950.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Ulpius Agathangelus Augusti libertus et Consilia Tyche fecerunt sibi et suis liberis libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum itum ambitum hoc monimentum sic heredem non sequetur in fronte pedes XVIII in agro pedes XI\n
EDCS-11900950.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Ulpius Agathangelus, freedman of the Emperor, and Consilia Tyche made [this] for themselves, their children, freedmen and freedwomen, and their descendants. This monument, with its passage and access, will not pass to an external heir. 18 feet in front, 11 feet in depth.
EDCS-11900950.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Ulpius Agathangelus, an imperial freedman, and Consilia Tyche, detailing a monument for their family and descendants, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11900951.text_edition: \n Marcus Ulpius Artemidorus fecit Marco Ulpio Hilaro patri et Atriae Fortunatae coniuigi sic libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum Atria Fortunata Publio Afranio Callisto coniugi concessit ex portione sua parte dimidia libertis libertabusque eius Lucius Domitius Callistion et Domitia Eutychia pro parte dimidia recepti in soccietate sic ab Ulpio Artemidoro fecerunt sibi et Domitis Callisto Plotinae Baeticae filis et libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum\n
EDCS-11900951.translation: Marcus Ulpius Artemidorus made [this] for his father Marcus Ulpius Hilarus and his wife Atria Fortunata. Atria Fortunata granted to Publius Afranius Callistus, husband, part of her share, namely the half, for his freedmen and freedwomen. Lucius Domitius Callistion and Domitia Eutychia received into partnership for the other half by Ulpius Artemidorus, they made [this] for themselves and for Domitia Callisto Plotina Baetica, their daughters, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-11900951.summary: This complex funerary inscription records a monument established by Marcus Ulpius Artemidorus and later expanded upon by Atria Fortunata and others, concerning inheritance and shared ownership, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12000034.text_edition: \n Orfito et Prisco consulibus ex ratione Lucili Hermetis CTRI CI\n
EDCS-12000034.translation: In the consulship of Orfitus and Priscus, from the account of Lucilius Hermes. CTRI CI
EDCS-12000034.summary: This inscription records a date based on the consulship of Orfitus and Priscus, and references an account by Lucilius Hermes, likely administrative or financial, probably from the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-12000205.text_edition: \n Quintus Vergilius TIIVVS Imperatore Domitiano X consule\n
EDCS-12000205.translation: Quintus Vergilius TIIVVS, under Emperor Domitian, in his 10th consulship.
EDCS-12000205.summary: This inscription records the name Quintus Vergilius TIIVVS and a date associated with Emperor Domitian's tenth consulship, likely from the late 1st century CE.

EDCS-12000207.text_edition: \n Colonorum coloniae Ostiensium Quintus Vergilius Eupsychus factus\n
EDCS-12000207.translation: Quintus Vergilius Eupsychus was made a colonist of the colony of Ostia.
EDCS-12000207.summary: This inscription records an individual, Quintus Vergilius Eupsychus, being granted colonial status in Ostia, likely during the Roman period.

EDCS-12000212.translation: Gaius Nasennius Musaeus made [this] for our Emperor Hadrian Augustus.
EDCS-12000212.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Gaius Nasennius Musaeus to Emperor Hadrian, likely part of a public work or monument.

EDCS-12000216.summary: This inscription records a craftsman, Gaius Porcius Agatho, as the maker of an object or structure.

EDCS-12000218.translation: [This is for] our Emperor Hadrian Augustus.
EDCS-12000218.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription dedicating something to Emperor Hadrian, likely part of a monument or public building.

EDCS-12000219.translation: [This is for] Hadrian Augustus our Emperor.
EDCS-12000219.summary: This fragmentary inscription records a dedication to Emperor Hadrian, possibly on a monument or public edifice.

EDCS-12000220.translation: Of Emperor Titus Aelius Antoninus Pius Augustus
EDCS-12000220.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription referring to Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely part of a larger dedication or title.

EDCS-12000221.translation: Under the supervision of Flegontis, freedman of the emperor, procurator of the Ostian workshop, for our Emperor Antoninus Augustus. [---] VIII
EDCS-12000221.summary: This inscription records administrative oversight by an imperial freedman and procurator, Flegontis, for a workshop in Ostia, related to Emperor Antoninus Augustus.

EDCS-12000222.translation: Of our Emperors Severus and Antoninus.
EDCS-12000222.summary: This fragmentary inscription refers to the co-reigning emperors Severus and Antoninus, likely part of a dedication or honorific text.

EDCS-12000225.text_edition: \n Titi Aeli Augusti liberti Proculi a rationibus\n
EDCS-12000225.translation: Of Titus Aelius Augustus, Proculus, freedman of the emperor, in charge of accounts.
EDCS-12000225.summary: This inscription records an official, Proculus, freedman and accountant for Titus Aelius Augustus, likely on a building or document.

EDCS-12000226.translation: Of our Lord Severus Alexander. From the workshop of Nasennius Fortunatus.
EDCS-12000226.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Severus Alexander and indicates it originated from the workshop of Nasennius Fortunatus.

EDCS-12000228.text_edition: \n Imperatoris Caesaris Cai Vibi Treboniani Galli Pii Felicis Augusti nost(ri) sub cura rationalium vvirorum pperfectissimorum procuratoris Hesriano ex officina\n
EDCS-12000228.translation: Under the supervision of the most perfect officials, the procurator Hesrianus, from the workshop...
EDCS-12000228.summary: This inscription records an official act or construction project under the authority of Emperor Trebonianus Gallus, managed by his procurator.

EDCS-12000231.summary: This inscription is a simple ownership or dedication by Lucius Apuleius Marcellus.

EDCS-12000235.summary: This inscription records a dedication or commission made by Marcus Quintilius Ortphus.

EDCS-12000236.translation: Of Sextus Valerius Zosimus. From the workshop of Aurelius Valerius.
EDCS-12000236.summary: This inscription indicates ownership or a commission by Sextus Valerius Zosimus, associated with the workshop of Aurelius Valerius.

EDCS-12000237.summary: This inscription is a simple ownership or dedication by Sextus Valerius Zosimus.

EDCS-12000242.translation: From the workshop of Nasennius Fortunatus.
EDCS-12000242.summary: This inscription indicates the origin of an item or work from the workshop of Nasennius Fortunatus.

EDCS-12000244.summary: This inscription records a dedication or commission made by Gaius Nasennius Musaeus.

EDCS-12000245.summary: This inscription indicates the origin of an item or work from the workshop of Marcus Ostiensis Asclepiadis.

EDCS-12000315.translation: To Drusus Caesar, son of Emperor Tiberius, grandson of Emperor Augustus, great-grandson of the deified Julius, pontifex. Pothus, freedman of Nymphodotus, a viator, who held tribunician and Augustal authority, set this up.
EDCS-12000315.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Drusus Caesar, likely set up by Pothus, a freedman holding public office, possibly during the Julio-Claudian period.

EDCS-12000319.translation: By decree of the decurions. To Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, and to the deified Faustina, for their outstanding harmony, so that virgins who are married in the colony of Ostia and their husbands may also supplicate at the altar.
EDCS-12000319.summary: This inscription records a decree by the local council (decurions) to honor Emperor Antoninus Pius and Empress Faustina for their harmony, establishing a ritual practice for newly married couples in Ostia.

EDCS-12000337.translation: To Lollianus, egregius vir, procurator of the grain supply of our Augusti, from Ostia; procurator of the 20th tax on freedom; procurator of the 20th tax on inheritances beyond the Po and throughout Aemilia; procurator of the property of condemned persons; tribune of the cohort of Germans; prefect of cohort II [---]; *duumvir* (?) of Bononia and *duumvir* (?) [---]; honored member of the college of carpenters of Ostia for his exceptional love for them, with the quinquennial magistrates of the 30th lustrum, Epagathus and Doryphorianus, acting as officials.
EDCS-12000337.summary: This inscription honors Lollianus, a procurator with various fiscal and military responsibilities, with a dedication from the college of carpenters of Ostia.

EDCS-12000338.translation: To Quintus Petronius Melior, son of Quintus, procurator of the grain supply, assistant to the curator of the Tiber river and its sewers, curator of the public affairs of Saena, praetor of Etruria of the 15 peoples, twice tribune of soldiers of the 3rd Gallic legion, quaestorian scribe, first principial praetor of the Laurentes Lavinates, quinquennial *duumvir* of Faesulae, pontiff of Faesulae and Florence, by the college of carpenters of Ostia (?), for his many benefactions, with the quinquennial magistrates of the 25th lustrum, Germanianus and Apolaustus and Peculiaris, acting as officials.
EDCS-12000338.summary: This is a dedication by the college of carpenters of Ostia to Quintus Petronius Melior, a distinguished official with extensive administrative and financial responsibilities, acknowledging his benefactions.

EDCS-12000340.translation: To Lucius Volusius Maecianus, son of Lucius, prefect of Egypt, prefect of the grain supply, minor pontiff, *a libellis* and for census matters to Emperor Antoninus Augustus Pius, *a studiis* and procurator of the libraries, prefect of the transport service, *a libellis* to Antoninus Augustus Pius, under the deified Hadrian, assistant for public works, prefect of the 1st Aelian fleet, prefect of the carpenters, patron of the colony, by decree of the decurions publicly.
EDCS-12000340.summary: This inscription honors Lucius Volusius Maecianus, a high-ranking imperial official with responsibilities in Egypt, grain supply, imperial administration, libraries, and transportation, as patron of the colony by decree of the decurions.

EDCS-12000344.translation: To Annius Postumus, son of Caius, procurator of Pannonia inferior, procurator of the grain supply, procurator for the magistracy of the 20th tax on inheritances, procurator of the libraries of the deified Trajan, by the college of carpenters.
EDCS-12000344.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the college of carpenters to Annius Postumus, a procurator with responsibilities in Pannonia, the grain supply, inheritances, and imperial libraries.

EDCS-12000366.translation: (List of officials and members, including patrons and quinquennial magistrates, with a mention of a dedication in the consulship of Pontianus and Atilanus) to the college of corporators (?).
EDCS-12000366.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to be a list of members and officials, possibly patrons and quinquennial magistrates, related to a collegium, with a date reference to the consulship of Pontianus and Atilanus.

EDCS-12000370.translation: For the departed spirits. Lucius Julius Romulus, Roman equestrian, *decurion* (?), and Iulia Iuliana made this for Iulia Quartilla, their mother, and for Iulius Nicephorus, their father, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-12000370.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Lucius Julius Romulus and Iulia Iuliana for their parents, Lucius Julius Romulus and Iulia Quartilla.

EDCS-12000382.translation: For the departed spirits. For [---]ae, daughter of Aulus, called Birronilla, who lived 20 years, 2 months, and 10 days.
EDCS-12000382.summary: A tombstone inscription for Birronilla, daughter of Aulus, recording her age at death.

EDCS-12101503.translation: For the departed spirits. Hygiae made this for herself and for Alexander, her husband, so that after Hygiae's death, only she and her husband may be placed in this sarcophagus; but if anyone places another, they shall pay a penalty of 10,000 sesterces to the college of pontiffs.
EDCS-12101503.summary: This is a funerary inscription establishing rules for burial in a sarcophagus, with a penalty for violation, made by Hygiae for herself and her husband Alexander.

EDCS-12300206.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 12th time, imperator for the 27th time, father of the country, censor, consul for the 5th time, granted citizenship and the right of marriage to the trierarchs and rowers who served in the fleet at Misenum under Tiberius Iulius Optatus, freedman of Augustus, and who were dismissed with an honorable discharge, and to their children and descendants, and also to those who were unmarried when citizenship was granted to them, with the women they would subsequently marry, but only one each. On the third day before the Ides of December, during the consulship of Faustus Cornelius Sulla Felix and Lucius Salvidienus Rufus Salvinus. For the common soldier Sparticus, son of Diuzenus, from Dibpscurtus, a Besso. Described and recognized from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, in the temple of Fides, on the right side. Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 12th time, imperator for the 27th time, father of the country, censor, consul for the 5th time, granted citizenship and the right of marriage to the trierarchs and rowers who served in the fleet at Misenum under Tiberius Iulius Optatus, freedman of Augustus, and who were dismissed with an honorable discharge, and to their children and descendants, and also to those who were unmarried when citizenship was granted to them, with the women they would subsequently marry, but only one each. On the third day before the Ides of December, during the consulship of Faustus Cornelius Sulla Felix and Lucius Salvidienus Rufus Salvinus. For the common soldier Sparticus, son of Diuzenus, from Dibpscurtus, a Besso. Described and recognized from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, in the temple of Fides, on the right side. For Lucius Messius Priscus, son of Lucius, of the Aemilian tribe, from Dyrrhachium. For Lucius Nutrius Venustus, of Dyrrhachium. For Caius Durrachinus Anthus, of Dyrrhachium. For Caius Sabinus Nedymus, of Dyrrhachium. For Caius Cornelius Ampliatus, of Dyrrhachium. For Titus Pomponius Epaphroditus, of Dyrrhachium. For Numerius Minianus Hylas, of Thessalonica.
EDCS-12300206.summary: This is a copy of an imperial decree by Emperor Claudius granting Roman citizenship and marriage rights to sailors from the fleet at Misenum who were honorably discharged, with a list of recipients and those associated with them.

EDCS-12300212.translation: Servius Galba Imperator Caesar Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, designated consul for the second time, granted an honorable discharge and citizenship to the veterans who served in the 1st Adjutrix legion, and to their children and descendants, and the right of marriage with the women they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with the women they would subsequently marry, but only one each. On the eleventh day before the Kalends of January, during the consulship of Caius Bellicus Natale and Publius Cornelius Scipio. For Diomedes, son of Artimon, a Phrygian. Described and recognized from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, in the altar of the Julian family. Servius Galba Imperator Caesar Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, designated consul for the second time, granted an honorable discharge and citizenship to the veterans who served in the 1st Adjutrix legion, and to their children and descendants, and the right of marriage with the women they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with the women they would subsequently marry, but only one each. On the eleventh day before the Kalends of January, during the consulship of Caius Bellicus Natale and Publius Cornelius Scipio. For Diomedes, son of Artimon, a Phrygian, from Laodicea. Described and recognized from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, in the altar of the Julian family. For Tiberius Iulius Pardala, a Sardian. For Caius Iulius Charmus, a Sardian. For Tiberius Claudius, known as Fidinus Maonianus. For Caius Iulius Libo, son of Caius, of the Collina tribe, a Sardian. For Tiberius Fonteius Cerialis, a Sardian. For Publius Graltius Provincialus, son of Publius, of the Aemilian tribe, and his own (family). For Marcus Arrius Rufus, a Sardian.
EDCS-12300212.summary: This is a copy of an imperial decree by Emperor Galba granting Roman citizenship and marriage rights to veterans of the 1st Adjutrix legion who were honorably discharged, with a list of recipients and associated individuals.

EDCS-12300215.translation: Emperor Vespasian Caesar Augustus, holding the tribunician power, consul for the second time. To the veterans who served in the Second Legion Adiutrix, Loyal and Faithful, who had earned twenty or more years of service and were honorably discharged, whose names are listed below, I have granted citizenship and the right to marry to them, their children, and their descendants, with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with the wives they subsequently married, allowing each man only one wife. Dated the Nones of March, with Emperor Vespasian Caesar Augustus (serving) as consul for the second time and Caesar Augustus' son Vespasian as consul. Tablet I, page V, location XXXXVI. Recorded and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, on the podium of the altar of the Julian family. Emperor Vespasian Caesar Augustus, holding the tribunician power, consul for the second time. To the veterans who served in the Second Legion Adiutrix, Loyal and Faithful, who had earned twenty or more years of service and were honorably discharged, whose names are listed below, I have granted citizenship and the right to marry to them, their children, and their descendants, with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with the wives they subsequently married, allowing each man only one wife. Dated the Nones of March, with Emperor Vespasian Caesar Augustus (serving) as consul for the second time and Caesar Augustus' son Vespasian as consul. Tablet I, page V, location XXXXVI. Recorded and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, on the podium of the altar of the Julian family, on the right side, in front of the statue of Liber Pater. From Gaius Helvius Lepidus of Salona. From Quintus Petronius Musaeus of Iader. From Lucius Valerius Acutus of Salona. From Marcus Nassius Phoebus of Salona. From Lucius Publicius Germullus. From Quintus Publicius Macedo of Nedina. From Quintus Publicius Crescens.
EDCS-12300215.summary: This is a bronze tablet recording Emperor Vespasian's grant of Roman citizenship and marriage rights to veterans of the Second Legion Adiutrix, dated to 70 AD.

EDCS-12300219.translation: Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the second time, imperator for the sixth time, father of the fatherland, consul for the third time, designated consul for the fourth time. To the veterans who served in the Misenum fleet under Sextus Lucilius Bassus, who had earned eighteen and twenty years of service or more and were transferred to Paestum, whose names are listed below, he granted citizenship and the right to marry to them, their children, and their descendants, with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with the wives they subsequently married, allowing each man only one wife. Dated the Nones of April, with Caesar Augustus' son Domitian and Gnaeus Pedius Cascus as consuls. To a common soldier, Marcus Damas, son of Sura Garasenus. Recorded and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, on the podium of the Julian family altar, on the exterior part. Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the second time, imperator for the sixth time, father of the fatherland, consul for the third time, designated consul for the fourth time. To the veterans who served in the Misenum fleet under Sextus Lucilius Bassus, who had earned eighteen and twenty years of service or more and were transferred to Paestum, whose names are listed below, he granted citizenship and the right to marry to them, their children, and their descendants, with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with the wives they subsequently married, allowing each man only one wife. Dated the Nones of April, with Caesar Augustus' son Domitian and Gnaeus Pedius Cascus as consuls. To a common soldier, Marcus Damas, son of Sura Garasenus. Recorded and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, on the podium of the Julian family altar, on the exterior part. From Appius Didius Praxias of Laodicea, Roman Equite. From Gaius Julius Agathocles of Laodicea. From Gnaeus Cestius, son of Gnaeus, of the Collina tribe, Cestius of Antioch. From Lucius Cornelius Simon of Caesarea Stratonis. From Tiberius Claudius Epaphroditus of Antioch. From Gaius Julius Theopompus of Antioch. From Tiberius Claudius Demosthenes of Laodicea.
EDCS-12300219.summary: This bronze tablet, dated to 70 AD, records Emperor Vespasian's grant of citizenship and marriage rights to veterans of the Misenum fleet.

EDCS-12300356.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300356.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the fourth time, consul for the third time, designated consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the fourth time, designated consul, father of the fatherland. To those who served in the praetorian fleet Philippiana Pia Vindice Misenensis, which is under Aelius Aemilianus, prefect, having completed eighteen and twenty years of service and honorably discharged, whose names are listed below, to them and their sons whom they have fathered by women with whom they have proven to have lived according to granted custom, I have granted Roman citizenship and the right to marry with the same women whom they had with them when citizenship was granted to them, or if they did not have them then, with the wives they subsequently married, allowing each man only one wife. Dated the fifth day before the Kalends of January, with Emperor Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus as designated consul for the third time and Emperor Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus as designated consul for the second time as consuls. By selection: Titus Flavius Alexander, son of Titus, of Italian origin, from Misenum, and Marcia Procula, his wife, and Marcius Alexander, and Flavius Marcus, and Ulpius Sabinus, and Aurelia Fasta, his children. Recorded and verified from a bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the deified Augustus, near the Minerva.
EDCS-12300356.summary: This bronze tablet, dated to 363 AD, records the grant of Roman citizenship and marriage rights by Emperor Philip the Arab and his son to sailors of the praetorian fleet.

EDCS-12401832.translation: Aemilia Chreste, freedwoman of Gaius, sacred to Ceres.
EDCS-12401832.summary: A dedication to Ceres by a freedwoman named Aemilia Chreste.

EDCS-12401833.translation: Gaius Aemilius Eleuther, freedman of Gaius, has fulfilled his vow to the Gallic Hercules, as is deserved.
EDCS-12401833.summary: A votive dedication to Hercules Gallicus by Gaius Aemilius Eleuther.

EDCS-12401834.translation: Salluvia (?), daughter of Marcus Postuma (?), has given the statue and base as a gift, sacred to Juno Regina.
EDCS-12401834.summary: A dedication to Juno Regina, likely a gift of a statue and base, by a woman named Salluvia.

EDCS-12401835.translation: Gaius Popillius Philodespotus has given Jupiter a table and a sundial as a gift.
EDCS-12401835.summary: A dedication to Jupiter by Gaius Popillius Philodespotus, including a table and a sundial.

EDCS-12401836.translation: Lucius Cominius Faustus, freedman of Lucius, sacred to the Lares. Lucius Cominius Faustus, freedman of Lucius, sacred to the Lares.
EDCS-12401836.summary: A dedication to the Lares by Lucius Cominius Faustus, a freedman.

EDCS-12401838.translation: To Ulpia Severina Augusta, wife of our lord, the unconquered Emperor Aurelian Augustus.
EDCS-12401838.summary: An inscription honoring Ulpia Severina, wife of Emperor Aurelian.

EDCS-12401839.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus, the Pious, Fortunate, Augustus.
EDCS-12401839.summary: An inscription honoring Emperor Marcus Claudius Tacitus.

EDCS-12401840.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Probus, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered, Augustus, Consul, our Lord.
EDCS-12401840.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Probus, likely from an official context given the titles used, dating to his reign (276-282 AD).

EDCS-12401844.translation: To Acilia Maniola, daughter of Manius, a woman of the highest distinction, great-granddaughter of Manius Acilius Glabrio, twice consul, granddaughter of Manius Acilius Glabrio, consul, and daughter of Manius Acilius Faustus, consul. By the order of the decurions.
EDCS-12401844.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Acilia Maniola, a woman of senatorial family, likely commissioned by the local town council (ordo decurionum).

EDCS-12401845.translation: To Acilia Gavinia Frestana, a girl of the highest distinction, daughter of Lucius Claudius Acilius Cleobolus, granddaughter of Manius Acilius Faustus, consul, great-granddaughter of Acilius Glabrio, twice consul, duumvir for five years, and great-great-granddaughter of Tiberius Claudius Cleobulus, senior consul.
EDCS-12401845.summary: This is a funerary inscription for the young Acilia Gavinia Frestana, detailing her distinguished ancestry from senatorial families.

EDCS-12401847.translation: To Cominia Vipsania Dignitas, daughter of Lucius, a woman of the highest distinction. By the college of capulator priests of Diana.
EDCS-12401847.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the college of capulator priests of Diana to Cominia Vipsania Dignitas, a distinguished woman.

EDCS-12401850.translation: To Quintus Tarronius Felix Dexter, son of Quintus, a man of the highest distinction. By the college of fabrum tignuariorum (carpenters and timber workers) to their patron.
EDCS-12401850.summary: This inscription records the appointment of Quintus Tarronius Felix Dexter as patron by the college of carpenters and timber workers, as he was designated aedile.

EDCS-12401851.translation: To Quintus Egnatius Gallienus Lucillus Lolliamus Tarronius Piso, a man of the highest distinction, born of a noble family, educated in Greek and Latin letters. Quintus Egnatius Gallienus, his father, a most distinguished man, made this. He lived 18 years, 10 months, and 22 days.
EDCS-12401851.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a father for his son, Quintus Egnatius Gallienus Lucillus Lolliamus Tarronius Piso, who died at the age of 18.

EDCS-12401856.translation: Lucius Appuleius Niger, son of Gaius, of the Aniensis tribe, as duumvir and curator of the road-paving, made a road ten thousand paces long with his own money.
EDCS-12401856.summary: This inscription records a public works project, the construction of a road, funded by Lucius Appuleius Niger in his capacity as duumvir and road curator.

EDCS-12401857.translation: To Aquillius Rufus, son of Lucius, of the Teretina tribe, aedile, duumvir, praefect with judicial power; for himself, and for his son Gaius Aquillius Florus, and for his daughter Maria Pieris, and for his concubine Maria Stacte, by his decision and testament.
