EDCS-16200547.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Aurelius [---] made this for his brother Marcus Aurelius Ingenuus(?), a soldier, who lived for [---] years.
EDCS-16200547.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Aurelius Ingenuus, a soldier, set up by his brother Marcus Aurelius, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16200548.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Aurelius [---] made this for his Lucius Aurelius Maximus, who lived for 30 years, 3 months, and 10 days, and served for 7 years in the praetorian fleet at Misenum.
EDCS-16200548.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Aurelius Maximus, a sailor in the Misenum fleet, set up by Marcus Aurelius, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16200549.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Pettia Crinusa made this for her well-deserving husband Marcus Aurelius Maximus, a veteran of our Emperor, who lived for 47 years and with whom she lived for 18 years.
EDCS-16200549.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Aurelius Maximus, an imperial veteran, set up by his wife Pettia Crinusa, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16200550.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Julius Demetrius made this for his well-deserving Aurelius Varus, boatswain of the Trireme Victoria, a Dalmatian by birth.
EDCS-16200550.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Varus, a naval officer, set up by Julius Demetrius, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16200551.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Ulpius Marinus, boatswain of the Trireme Ceres, and Claudius Marinus, boatswain of the Trireme Danuvius, are the heirs of Publius Babbio Maturus, soldier of the praetorian fleet at Misenum in the Quadrireme Fides, a Syrian by birth, who served for 4 years.
EDCS-16200551.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Babbio Maturus, a soldier in the Misenum fleet, with his heirs named, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16200552.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caius Bebius Hermes, a soldier of the praetorian fleet at Misenum, and Aurelia Proba, for their dearest son Sextus Bebius Stolo, who lived for 4 days and 10 hours.
EDCS-16200552.summary: A funerary epitaph for Sextus Bebius Stolo, an infant, set up by his parents, a soldier and his wife, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16200687.text_edition: \n Lucium Popidium Luci filium Ampliatum aedilem de re publica et coloniae Pompeianae oro vos faciatis infanticulus cum sodalibus rogat\n
EDCS-16200687.translation: I beg you, Pompeians, to make Lucius Popidius Ampliatus, son of Lucius, aedile for the republic and for the colony of Pompeii; the group of young children asks for it.
EDCS-16200687.summary: A proposal or request for the election of Lucius Popidius Ampliatus as aedile in Pompeii, likely from the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-16200688.text_edition: \n Lollium Fuscum aedilem cliens Surge facit\n
EDCS-16200688.translation: His client Surge erects this for Lollius Fuscus as aedile.
EDCS-16200688.summary: This inscription records a client dedicating a monument to his patron who served as an aedile.

EDCS-16200689.text_edition: \n Paria XLIX familia Capiniana muneribus Augustorum pugnabunt Puteolis ante diem IV Idus Maias pridie Idus Maias et XVII XV Kalendas Iunias vela erunt Magus\n
EDCS-16200689.translation: The forty-nine slaves of the Capinian family will fight in the games of the Augusti at Puteoli on the fourth day before the Ides of May, the day before the Ides of May, and the seventeenth day before the Kalends of June; there will be races. Magus.
EDCS-16200689.summary: This inscription details the schedule and participants for gladiatorial games sponsored by the Capinian family at Puteoli.

EDCS-16200780.text_edition: \n Caio Clodio Hilaro biselliario cui ordo conscriptorum ornamenta decurionalia dedit quod is ob honorem biselli HS XXVviginti quinque milia nummum rei publicae obtulerit ex quibus familia gladiatoria ex postulatu universorum per ipsum edita est ad cuius impensas insuper universa plebs ad ampliandam muneris eius famam optulit sic insuper HS XXVviginti quinque milia nummum ordo Augustalium pecunia sua ob cuius dedicationem pavit in Capitolio pane et vino promiscue posito et dedit sportulas decurionibus Augustalibus regalibus singulis |(denarios) quinos locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-16200780.translation: To Gaius Clodius Hilarus, a stage performer, to whom the order of scribes granted decurional honors because he offered 25,000 sesterces to the state upon taking office as a stage performer, from which a gladiatorial troupe was presented by him at the request of all; furthermore, the entire populace offered on his behalf to enhance the fame of his munificence, and moreover the order of Augustales contributed 25,000 sesterces from their own funds for its dedication, and feasted in the Capitol with bread and wine freely distributed, and gave five denarii each to the decurions and Augustales. A place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-16200780.summary: This is a dedication to Gaius Clodius Hilarus, a public benefactor who provided significant funds for games and public feasts, for which he received honors and a place granted by decree.

EDCS-16200781.text_edition: \n Publio Acilio Publi filio Palatina Restitutiano IIviro quaestori alimentorum plebs universa consentiente splendidissimo ordine decurionum aere conlato locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-16200781.translation: A place was granted by decree of the decurions to Publius Acilius Restitutianus, son of Publius, of the Palatine tribe, twice-chiliarch, quaestor of food supplies, with the consent of the entire populace and the distinguished order of decurions, with funds collected.
EDCS-16200781.summary: This inscription records the granting of a place by decree to Publius Acilius Restitutianus, an official involved in public works and food supply, honored by the populace and decurions.

EDCS-16200782.text_edition: \n Tito Flavio Lysipono Augustales viro ordinis sui patrono ob merita eius locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-16200782.translation: A place was granted by decree of the decurions to Titus Flavius Lysiponus, an Augustalis and patron of his order, for his merits.
EDCS-16200782.summary: This inscription records the granting of a place by decree to Titus Flavius Lysiponus, an Augustalis and patron, in recognition of his services.

EDCS-16200783.text_edition: \n Lucio Varronio Luci filio Palatina Capitoni scribae aedilicio accenso velato IIviro quinquennali patrono coloniae curatori aquarum ordo decurionum pecunia sua\n
EDCS-16200783.translation: The order of decurions erected this at their own expense for Lucius Varronius Capito, son of Lucius, of the Palatine tribe, scribe, elected official, veiled official, twice-chiliarch for five years, patron of the colony, curator of the waters.
EDCS-16200783.summary: This is a dedication by the order of decurions, funded by them, to Lucius Varronius Capito, who held multiple important civic and administrative positions in the colony.

EDCS-16200784.text_edition: \n Lucio Bruttio Luci filio Palatina Saturnino equo publico IIviro quinquennali auguri patrono coloniae quaestori alimentorum ordo decurionum sua pecunia posuit\n
EDCS-16200784.translation: The order of decurions erected this with their own funds for Lucius Bruttius Saturninus, son of Lucius, of the Palatine tribe, with public horse, twice-chiliarch for five years, augur, patron of the colony, quaestor of food supplies.
EDCS-16200784.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by the order of decurions, funded by them, for Lucius Bruttius Saturninus, who served in various important roles including patron of the colony and augur.

EDCS-16200885.text_edition: \n Publio Clodio Publi filio Publi nepoti Falerna Lucio Arleno Basso patrono et curatori coloniae universus ordo municipum ob merita eius\n
EDCS-16200885.translation: The entire order of townspeople erected this for Publius Clodius, son of Publius, grandson of Publius, of the Falernian tribe, and for Lucius Arlenus Bassus, patron and curator of the colony, for his merits.
EDCS-16200885.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by the townspeople to their patron and curator, Lucius Arlenus Bassus, in recognition of his services.

EDCS-16200886.text_edition: \n Caio Clodio Cai filio Quirina Adiutori praetori tribuno plebei sic quaestori publice quot sic HS CCC milia nummum rei publicae legaverint sic ex qua pecunia templum exstructum et forum stratum est\n
EDCS-16200886.translation: To Gaius Clodius Adiutor, son of Gaius, of the Quirine tribe, praetor, tribune of the plebs, quaestor; thus he bequeathed 300,000 sesterces to the state, from which money a temple was built and the forum paved.
EDCS-16200886.summary: This inscription records the bequest of 300,000 sesterces by Gaius Clodius Adiutor for the construction of a temple and the paving of the forum, detailing his public offices.

EDCS-16200887.text_edition: \n Isidi Restitutrici Lucius Terentius Stephanus Augustalis aras et dromum pecunia sua dono dedit et Anxorates ita posuerunt ex decreto decurionum et posticum\n
EDCS-16200887.translation: Lucius Terentius Stephanus, an Augustalis, dedicated the altars and the racecourse to Isis the Restorer with his own money, and the Anxorates placed these and the back entrance by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-16200887.summary: This inscription records a dedication by an Augustalis to the goddess Isis, including altars and a racecourse, with additional structures funded and placed by the Anxorates and the decurions.

EDCS-16201025.text_edition: \n Cai Flavidi Marci filii Teretina haruspicis\n
EDCS-16201025.translation: Caius Flavius, son of Marcus, of the Teretina tribe, diviner.
EDCS-16201025.summary: This is an epitaph for Caius Flavius, identifying his name, filiation, tribe, and profession as a diviner.

EDCS-16201026.text_edition: \n Vetedius Iustus patronus coloniae signum Ganimedes sic civitati et civibus donum dedit\n
EDCS-16201026.translation: Vetedius Iustus, patron of the colony, dedicated this statue of Ganymede to the community and its citizens as a gift.
EDCS-16201026.summary: This is a dedication of a statue of Ganymede by Vetedius Iustus, patron of the colony, to the community.

EDCS-16201173.text_edition: \n Nonio Asprenate Marco Aquila Iuliano consulibus Baebio Gallo IIIIviro iure dicundo Aulo Sextio Paullo praetore Lichastus populi servus Aegisus Cai Afri Fausti servus Chrysanthus Marci Bibuloris servus Saturio sic Scaevini servus Phileros Tadi Albae servus Zefyrus Gallae servus\n
EDCS-16201173.translation: During the consulship of Nonius Asprenas and Marcus Aquila Julianus, and with Baebius Gallus as quaestor, Lichastus, slave of the people, Aegisus, slave of Caius Africanus Faustus, Chrysanthus, slave of Marcus Bibulorius, Saturio, slave of Scaevinius, and Phileros, slave of Tadius Albus, and Zefyrus, slave of Gallus.
EDCS-16201173.summary: This inscription lists several individuals, likely freedmen or slaves, serving under specific magistrates during a particular consular period, possibly related to a legal or administrative proceeding.

EDCS-16201176.text_edition: \n Numerius Popidius Florus Iovi Optimo Maximo Numerius Popidius Florus Veneri Libero Herculi\n
EDCS-16201176.translation: Numerius Popidius Florus [dedicated this] to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Numerius Popidius Florus [dedicated this] to Venus, Liber, and Hercules.
EDCS-16201176.summary: This is a dedication of a monument or altar to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Venus, Liber, and Hercules by Numerius Popidius Florus.

EDCS-16201178.text_edition: \n Graphico Augusti liberto Domitianiano procuratori hereditatium tractus Campaniae et Flaviae Stacte matri eius libertis libertabusque suis et alumnis suis fecit Publius Aelius Athenodorus\n
EDCS-16201178.translation: Publius Aelius Athenodorus made this for Graphicus, freedman of Augustus, procurator of inheritances in the district of Campania and Flavia, and for his mother Stacte, and for his freedmen and freedwomen and his foster children.
EDCS-16201178.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Publius Aelius Athenodorus for Graphicus, an imperial freedman and procurator, and his family members.

EDCS-16201179.text_edition: \n Lucius Cominius Luci libertus Apollonius Cominia Luci liberta Dionysia Lucius Cominius Luci libertus Dionysius colonus in dolostribus Antiaunis Lucius Cominius Luci libertus Eros fabarius vivite liberteisque eorum Lucius Cominius Luci libertus M Luci Comini Luci libertus Teri\n
EDCS-16201179.translation: Lucius Cominius Apollonius, freedman of Lucius, and Cominia Dionysia, freedwoman of Lucius, and Lucius Cominius Eros, freedman of Lucius, a cloth merchant, and his freedmen and their freedmen, and Lucius Cominius Teri, freedman of Lucius.
EDCS-16201179.summary: This is a tombstone inscription commemorating multiple freedmen and freedwomen of the Cominii family, including a cloth merchant, with a general exhortation to live well.

EDCS-16201221.text_edition: \n Aram Nymphis sanctis Amnion Auggustorum nnostrorum servus liberatus numine earum gravi infirmitate VOVETCTA fecit dicavitque Idus I Anullino II et Frontone consulibus\n
EDCS-16201221.translation: Aram to the holy Nymphs. Amnion, freedman of our Emperors, made and dedicated this after being freed from a serious illness by their divine power. On the Ides of January, during the second consulship of Anullinus and Frontinus.
EDCS-16201221.summary: This is a dedication of an altar to the Nymphs by Amnion, a freedman of the emperors, for his recovery from illness, dated to the consulship of Anullinus and Frontinus.

EDCS-16201545.text_edition: \n Asiaticus praefectus cohortis I Asturum praetor quaestor curator pecuniae publicae Cumis filiae\n
EDCS-16201545.translation: Asiaticus, prefect of the I Cohort of Astures, praetor, quaestor, curator of public funds in Cumae, [and] his daughter.
EDCS-16201545.summary: This inscription lists the offices held by Asiaticus, including prefect, praetor, quaestor, and curator, and mentions his daughter.

EDCS-16201610.translation: Vibius Marcellus, soldier of the II Parthica Legion, in the 4th century, posterior cohort, lived 27 years, served 3 years. Nummius Mercurius and Saturnius Hilarus, his heirs, saw to the construction of this monument for him as deserving.
EDCS-16201610.summary: This is a military tombstone for Vibius Marcellus, a soldier in the II Parthica Legion, who lived 27 years and served for 3 years, with his heirs responsible for the monument.

EDCS-16201611.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius sic Paibae equiti legionis II Parthicae qui vixit nnos XXVIII militavit nnos VIII Aurelius Zyphyr sic et Aurelius Mestrius heredes et contubernales bene fecerunt merito\n
EDCS-16201611.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Paibae, equestrian of the II Parthica Legion, who lived 28 years and served 8 years. Aurelius Zyphyr and Aurelius Mestrius, heirs and tent-mates, made this monument for him deservedly.
EDCS-16201611.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Paibae, an equestrian soldier in the II Parthica Legion, noting his age and service length, with his heirs and tent-mates erecting the monument.

EDCS-16201612.text_edition: \n Sodalicius sic iuubentutis sic Fabio Sodali bene merenti faciundum curavit\n
EDCS-16201612.translation: The association of youth ordered this to be made for their well-deserving comrade Fabius.
EDCS-16201612.summary: This inscription is a dedication by a youth association to their comrade Fabius, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-16202008.text_edition: \n Popidium Luci filium Ampliatum aedilem Valens faciatis et ille te fecit Infantio scripsit\n
EDCS-16202008.translation: Make Ampliatus, son of Lucius, aedile; Valens made you, and Infantio wrote it.
EDCS-16202008.summary: This is a graffiti or informal inscription, possibly related to a local election or public office, from the 1st century BCE to 1st century CE.

EDCS-16202009.text_edition: \n Trebi surge faciatis aedilem Lollium Fuscum adulescentem probum\n
EDCS-16202009.translation: Trebius, get up and make Lollius Fuscus, a good young man, aedile.
EDCS-16202009.summary: This inscription appears to be a piece of graffiti urging an election or appointment for a young man named Lollius Fuscus to the office of aedile.

EDCS-16300127.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caio Sulpicio Firmo Augustali Fabrateriae marito optumo\n
EDCS-16300127.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. To Gaius Sulpicius Firmus, Augustalis, my best husband, from Fabrateriae.
EDCS-16300127.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Gaius Sulpicius Firmus, who was an Augustalis, by his wife Fabrateriae, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16300129.text_edition: \n Hic locus maceria clusus cum eo quidquid in eo est cum hac maceria sanctus religiosus est neque venire potest neque donari neque mancipari ius autem morandi in eo loco is erit quicumque ex domo Domitiae Luci filiae Lepidae erunt Domitive aut Domitiaeve vocabuntur praeterea huic loco via via libera datur ex publica datur usque ad introitum in eum locum item aqua promiscue licebit uti ex hoc fundo villaque iis qui in eo loco morabuntur haec sic praestari sine dolo malo iussit permisitque Lucius Domitius Phaon cuius qui sic locus fuit in omne tempus posterum cippis octo positis XII Kalendas Iulias Lucio Aurelio Prisco Lucio Iulio Rufo consulibus\n
EDCS-16300129.translation: This place, enclosed by a wall, with whatever is in it, and with this wall, is sacred and inviolable, and it cannot be sold or transferred. The right to dwell in this place will belong to whoever is from the household of Domitia, daughter of Lucius Lepida, and will be called Domitius or Domitia. Furthermore, a free right of way is given to this place from the public road, up to the entrance to this place. Likewise, those who will dwell in this place will be permitted to use the water from this property and villa in common. Lucius Domitius Phaon, to whom this place belonged, ordered and permitted these things to be guaranteed without bad faith for all future time, with eight boundary stones set up on the 21st of June, under the consulship of Lucius Aurelius Priscus and Lucius Iulius Rufus.
EDCS-16300129.summary: This is a property deed and grant of rights detailing land use, access, and water rights, established by Lucius Domitius Phaon and recorded with consular dating to 57 CE.

EDCS-16300147.translation: From the workshop of Marcus, son of Ostius Eutyches.
EDCS-16300147.summary: This inscription indicates the origin of a product or work from the workshop of Marcus Ostius Eutyches, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16300257.text_edition: \n Imperatoris Marci Aureli Antonini sub cura rationalis Hermippi Augusti liberti procuratoris ex officina Hierocletisi liberti\n
EDCS-16300257.translation: From the workshop of Hierocles, freedman of Augustus, under the supervision of the rationalis Hermippus, freedman of Augustus, procurator of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus.
EDCS-16300257.summary: This inscription marks a product from the workshop of Hierocles, a freedman, under the administration of the rationalis Hermippus, during the reign of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (161-180 CE).

EDCS-16300258.text_edition: \n Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Severi Alexandri Augusti sub cura rationalium vvirorum pperfectissimorum procuratoris Agricolae Augusti liberti ex officina Hierocletis Augusti liberti\n
EDCS-16300258.translation: From the workshop of Hierocles, freedman of Augustus, under the supervision of the most perfect rationalis Agricola, freedman of Augustus, procurator of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Augustus.
EDCS-16300258.summary: This inscription denotes a product from the workshop of Hierocles, a freedman, under the administration of the rationalis Agricola, during the reign of Emperor Severus Alexander (222-235 CE).

EDCS-16300270.text_edition: \n Rutediae Publi filiae Ursiae huic ordo decurionum in honorem Rutedi Nigri patris eius principis coloniae nostrae funus publicum faciundum locumque sepulturae dandum statuas duas pecunia publice ponendas censuit\n
EDCS-16300270.translation: The town council decreed that a public funeral and a burial plot should be provided for Rutedia, daughter of Publius, Ursa, in honor of her father Rutedius Niger, a leading citizen of our colony, and that two statues should be publicly erected with public funds.
EDCS-16300270.summary: This inscription records a decree by the town council to honor Rutedia Ursa with a public funeral and burial, and to erect statues, in recognition of her father's status, likely from a Roman colony in the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-16300271.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Decrio Luci filio Sergia Longino praefecto fabrum |(centurioni) legionis II Augustae et VII geminae bis et XXII Deiotarianae primo pilo legionis eiusdem praefecto castrorum legionis VIIII Hispanae Lucius Decrius Luci filius Sergia Iulianus qui et Numisianus princeps legionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis patri optimo testamento fieri iussit\n
EDCS-16300271.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Lucius Decrius Iulianus, also called Numisianus, primus pilus of the 11th Claudian Pious Faithful Legion and commander of the camp of the 9th Hispana Legion, whose father was Lucius Decrius Longinus Sergia, a prefect of the engineers of the Legio II Augusta and the VII Gemina bis, and centurion of the 22nd Deiotariana, ordered this to be made for his most excellent father in his will.
EDCS-16300271.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Lucius Decrius Iulianus for his father, Lucius Decrius Longinus Sergia, detailing the father's distinguished military career, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16300272.text_edition: \n legio II Parthica pia felix fidelis aeterna devota numini maiestatique eorum dedicante Claudio Silvano praefecto legionis supra scriptae Idus Februarias Fulvio Aemiliano II et Naevio Aquilino conssulibus\n
EDCS-16300272.translation: The Second Parthian Legion, dutiful, fortunate, loyal, faithful, eternal, devoted to the divinity and majesty of them, dedicates, with Claudius Silvanus, prefect of the aforementioned legion, on the Ides of February, with Fulvius Aemilianus II and Naevius Aquilinus as consuls.
EDCS-16300272.summary: A dedication by the Second Parthian Legion, led by its prefect Claudius Silvanus, during the consulship of Fulvius Aemilianus II and Naevius Aquilinus.

EDCS-16300285.translation: Amandus, the priest, for Fuscum, the aedile.
EDCS-16300285.summary: A dedication by a priest named Amandus for Fuscum, who was an aedile.

EDCS-16300295.text_edition: \n Lucium Popidium Luci filium Ampliatum aedilem Montanus cliens rogat cum latruncaris\n
EDCS-16300295.translation: Montanus, the client, requests Lucius Popidius Ampliatus, son of Lucius, who was an aedile, to be appointed, together with the brigands.
EDCS-16300295.summary: A request from a client named Montanus regarding Lucius Popidius Ampliatus, an aedile, in a context involving 'latrones' (brigands or perhaps actors in a play).

EDCS-16300299.text_edition: \n Cnaeum Helvium Sabinum aedilem pistores rogant et cupiunt cum vicinis\n
EDCS-16300299.translation: The bakers, and wish, together with their neighbors, ask Cnaeus Helvius Sabinus to be the aedile.
EDCS-16300299.summary: A request from bakers and their neighbors for Cnaeus Helvius Sabinus to become an aedile.

EDCS-16300305.text_edition: \n Septumiae sic Luci filiae decreto decurionum locus sepulturae publice datus et in funere HS MMduo milia Antistia Publi filia Prima filia fecit\n
EDCS-16300305.translation: By decree of the decurions, a burial plot was publicly granted to Septumia Prima, daughter of Lucius, and on the funeral, her daughter Antistia Prima, daughter of Publius, made a donation of 2000 sesterces.
EDCS-16300305.summary: A public burial plot granted by decree of the town council to Septumia Prima, with her daughter Antistia Prima making a funerary donation.

EDCS-16300445.text_edition: \n Corneliae Saloninae Augustae Fabraterni veteres publice decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-16300445.translation: Cornelia Salonia, Augusta, [honored by] the veterans of Fabraterni, by public decree of the decurions.
EDCS-16300445.summary: A monument honoring Cornelia Salonia, Augusta, erected by veterans by decree of the decurions.

EDCS-16300449.text_edition: \n augur r August-- f bis Vaccula\n
EDCS-16300449.translation: augur, son of August-- , twice [consul?], Vaccula.
EDCS-16300449.summary: A fragmentary inscription mentioning an augur named Vaccula, who may have held the consulship twice.

EDCS-16300450.text_edition: \n renium dat-- III Nonas Augustas Feliciano et Titiano conssulibus Asclepiadi actori\n
EDCS-16300450.translation: on the third day before the Nones of August, with Feliciano and Titiano as consuls, for Asclepiades, the actor.
EDCS-16300450.summary: A dedication to Asclepiades, an actor, during the consulship of Feliciano and Titiano.

EDCS-16300457.summary: A funerary inscription for Sextus Valerius Zosimus.

EDCS-16300459.inscription_type: tituli fabricationis, tituli operum, tituli possessionis
EDCS-16300459.text_edition: \n Squilla et Titiano consulibus ex fundo Brutiano Statili Maximi Severi Hadriani\n
EDCS-16300459.translation: Under the consulship of Squilla and Titianus, from the estate of Brutius, of Severus Hadrianus.
EDCS-16300459.summary: An indication of origin or ownership related to the estate of Brutius, belonging to Severus Hadrianus, during the consulship of Squilla and Titianus.

EDCS-16300468.text_edition: \n Caium Iulium Polybium IIvirum iure dicundo Zmyrina rogat\n
EDCS-16300468.translation: The city of Smyrna requests Gaius Julius Polybius, duumvir with judicial power.
EDCS-16300468.summary: This is a request from the city of Smyrna to a local magistrate, Gaius Julius Polybius, dated to the Roman period.

EDCS-16300470.translation: On the occasion of the dedication of the arena, the games of Gnaeus Alleius Nigidius Maius will include a procession, a hunt, athletes, and distributions of entertainment. Long live Maius, prince of the colony!
EDCS-16300470.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an arena and lists the entertainments provided by Gnaeus Alleius Nigidius Maius, likely a magistrate or patron of the colony, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16300501.translation: First games: Marcus Meso[...]. May 3rd. Thracians, Murmillones. [...] Neronianus won II bouts. Tigris Julianus won I bout. [...]cius Neronianus won III bouts. Speculator won LXIX bouts. The charioteer [...] the Retiarius. Crysantus won II bouts. Marcus Artorius [...]. Hoplomachus, Murmillones. [...]peacius Julianus [...]. [...] Julianus won LV bouts. [...]ius [...]. Thracian? [...]B[...] Neronianus [...]. Games [...] V IV III the day before the Ides, on the Ides of May. Dimachaeri(?), Hoplomachi(?). [...]ciens Neronianus won XX[...]. Nobilior Julianus won II bouts. Thraex, Murmillo. Lucius Sempronius [...] won. Platanus Julianus [...]. Thraex, Murmillo. Pugnax Neronianus won III bouts. Murranus Neronianus died in III bouts. Hoplomachus, Thraex. Cycnus Julianus won VIIII bouts. Atticus Julianus won XIV bouts. Thraex, Murmillo. Herma Julianus won IV bouts. Quintus Petillius [...]. Charioteers. Publius Ostorius won LI bouts. Scylax Julianus won XXVI bouts. Thraex, Murmillo. Nodu[...]. Julianus won VII bouts. Lucius Petronius won XIV bouts. Thraex, Murmillo. Lucius Fabius died in VIIII bouts. Astus Julianus won XIV bouts. [...] shall be praised [...] XIV bouts. [...] N[...] bouts.
EDCS-16300501.summary: This is a detailed record of gladiatorial combats and their outcomes, including types of gladiators, bouts won or lost, and some casualties, likely from a public festival or games in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16400054.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Caius Interamnius Crescentio libertus et tabularius rei publicae aram iussu numinis restituit\n
EDCS-16400054.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest. Gaius Interamnius Crescentio, freedman and public scribe, restored this altar by order of the divinity.
EDCS-16400054.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter, recording the restoration of an altar by a freedman and public scribe named Gaius Interamnius Crescentio, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16400055.text_edition: \n Amandus Titi filius Eiolai Hep servus fecit\n
EDCS-16400055.translation: Amandus, son of Titus Eiolai Hep, his slave, made this.
EDCS-16400055.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the maker of a monument, Amandus, a slave, for someone named Titus Eiolai Hep, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16400061.text_edition: \n Ex officina A Rufo et Materno consulibus\n
EDCS-16400061.translation: From the workshop of Rufus and Materno, in the consulship of.
EDCS-16400061.summary: This inscription indicates the origin of a product or work from the workshop of Rufus and Materno, dated by the consuls mentioned, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16400080.text_edition: \n Lucio Volu Caio Iulio Clem Cnaeo Valerio I Caio Vallio Proculo Lucio Caesio Clemente curatore magistri Herculanii Aulus Terentius Anthus Marcus Albius Phos Marcus Petronius Aulus Iustuleius Lucius Virius Caius Ve Caius \n
EDCS-16400080.translation: To Lucius Volu, Caius Julius Clem, Gnaeus Valerius I, Caius Vallius Proculus, Lucius Caesius Clemens, curator of the magistrate of the Herculanii, Aulus Terentius Anthus, Marcus Albius Phos, Marcus Petronius, Aulus Iustuleius, Lucius Virius, Caius Ve, Caius [...].
EDCS-16400080.summary: This is a list of names, possibly officials or members of a collegium, associated with the Herculanii, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16400081.text_edition: \n Imperatori domino nostro Maxentio semper Augusto milia XIIIIII\n
EDCS-16400081.translation: To our Emperor and lord Maxentius, always Augustus, 14 miles.
EDCS-16400081.summary: This is an inscription likely related to a milestone or a boundary marker, indicating a distance of 14 miles to or from a location associated with Emperor Maxentius, dated to his reign (306-312 AD).

EDCS-16400084.text_edition: \n Numerius Herennius Numeri filius Menenia Celsus duumvir iure dicundo iterum praefectus fabrum Aesquilliae Cai filiae Pollae uxori vixit annos XXII locus sepulturae publice datus decreto decurionum Felicio cum fructo hac Dianensis\n
EDCS-16400084.translation: Numerius Herennius Celsus, son of Numerius, of the Menenia tribe, duumvir with judicial power for the second time, prefect of engineers, to Polla, daughter of Caius, wife of Aesquillian, who lived 22 years, with the right to burial in this plot granted publicly by decree of the decurions and Felicio with profit, this plot in Dianensis.
EDCS-16400084.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Polla, wife of Aesquillian, recording her age, her husband's and father-in-law's official positions, and details about the burial plot granted by the local council, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16400085.text_edition: \n Caio Vestorio Prisco aedili vixit annis XXII locus sepulturae datus et in funere HS MMduo milia decreto decurionum Mulvia Prisca mater pecunia sua\n
EDCS-16400085.translation: To Caius Vestorius Priscus, aedile, who lived 22 years. The burial plot was granted, and at the funeral, 2,000 sesterces were given by decree of the decurions by his mother Mulvia Prisca with her own money.
EDCS-16400085.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caius Vestorius Priscus, an aedile, recording his age, the cost of the burial plot and funeral expenses provided by his mother, and the decree of the decurions, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16400113.text_edition: \n Sexti Publici Faustinae Augustae Consortis opus doliare ex figlinis Ponticesibus\n
EDCS-16400113.translation: The tile-work of Sextus Publicius Faustinus, made from the Ponticesian kilns.
EDCS-16400113.summary: This inscription records ownership of tile-work from the Ponticesian kilns, likely indicating a manufacturing or ownership mark.

EDCS-16400125.text_edition: \n Agathemeris Manliae serva Achulea Fabiae serva ornatrix Caletuche Vergiliae serva ornatrix Hilara Liciniae serva ornatrix Crheste sic Corneliae serva ornatrix Hilara Seiae serva ornatrix Moschis ornatrix Rufa Apeiliae sic serva ornatrix Chila ornatrix\n
EDCS-16400125.translation: Achulea, slave of Agathemeris, beautician of Manlia; Caletuche, slave of Fabia, beautician; Hilara, slave of Vergilia, beautician; Crheste, slave of Licinia, beautician; Hilara, slave of Cornelia, beautician; Moschis, beautician; Rufa, beautician; Chila, beautician of Apeilia.
EDCS-16400125.summary: This inscription lists the names of various women, many identified as slaves and beauticians, possibly from a workshop or household, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-16400127.text_edition: \n Rei publicae Tarricinensis sic curator Valerius Genialis\n
EDCS-16400127.translation: Valerius Genialis, curator of the republic of Tarricina.
EDCS-16400127.summary: This inscription records Valerius Genialis as the curator of the public affairs of the town of Tarricina, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-16400133.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Publi filio Palatina Bassiano IIIIviro marito optimo de me bene mereto sic Allia Luperca\n
EDCS-16400133.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Allia Luperca, to Publius Aelius Bassianus, son of Publius, of the Palatine tribe, her most excellent husband, who deserved well of me.
EDCS-16400133.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Allia Luperca for her husband, Publius Aelius Bassianus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-16400135.translation: Niger, slave of Publius Volumnius the Barber.
EDCS-16400135.summary: This inscription records Niger, a slave belonging to Publius Volumnius, a barber, likely from a funerary context or ownership mark.

EDCS-16400323.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio Cai filio Claudia Alexandro stolarcho classis praetoriae Misenensis piae vindicis Philippianae adampliato iudicis divi Alexandri Augusti ceterorumque principum praeposito reliquationi classium praettoriarum Misenensis et nnatis ppiarum vvindicium expeditioni orientali adlecto in ordinem decurionum splendidissimae civitatis Misenensium itemque splendidissimae coloniae Antiochiensis et splendidissimae coloniae Mallotarum patrono quoque civitati Chilmanensium homini verecundissimo Caius Iulius Aprilis veteranus Auggustorum locus datus ab Aurelio Serapione tribuno praeposito Dedicata Idibus Martiis Caio Praesente et Albino consulibus\n
EDCS-16400323.translation: To Gaius Julius Alexander, son of Gaius, of the Claudia tribe, commander of the praetorian fleet of Misenum, pious defender of Philippi, promoter of justice of the deified Augustus Alexander and other emperors, supervisor of the remaining praetorian fleets of Misenum and (illegible) and of the vindicatory expeditions in the East, admitted into the order of decurions of the most splendid city of Misenum, and likewise of the most splendid colony of Antioch, and of the most splendid colony of Mallota, and also patron of the community of the Chilmanensians, a most worthy man. Gaius Julius Aprilis, veteran of the emperors, has been granted a place by Aurelius Serapion, tribune and prefect. Dedicated on the Ides of March in the consulship of Gaius and Albinus.
EDCS-16400323.summary: This lengthy inscription honors Gaius Julius Alexander, a high-ranking naval officer and civic official, detailing his career and honors bestowed by various cities and communities, dedicated by a veteran in the consulship of Gaius and Albinus (likely late 2nd or early 3rd century CE).

EDCS-16400375.text_edition: \n ob praecipuam liberalitatem ac reformatam in meliorem statum civitatem recreata --- quoque annonae cura quam temporibus tyranni caruit ordo Laurentium Lavinatium devotus numini maiestatique eius devotissimus\n
EDCS-16400375.translation: On account of outstanding generosity and for having restored the city to a better state, and also the grain supply, which it lacked in the times of the tyrant, the order of the Laurentes Lavinates, devoted to his divinity and majesty, is most devoted.
EDCS-16400375.summary: This inscription praises an unnamed individual for their exceptional generosity and for restoring the city and its grain supply, likely dating to a period of political instability or tyranny in the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-16400392.text_edition: \n Ex praediis Titi Statili Severi ex figlinis Brutianis fecit Flavius Onesimus Apro et Catullino consulibus\n
EDCS-16400392.translation: From the estates of Titus Statilius Severus, made by Flavius Onesimus from the Brutian kilns, in the consulship of Apro and Catullinus.
EDCS-16400392.summary: This inscription records the maker, Flavius Onesimus, and the source of materials (estates of Titus Statilius Severus and Brutian kilns) for a product, made during the consulship of Apro and Catullinus (likely 197 CE).

EDCS-16400519.text_edition: \n Fortunae Primigeniae Tiberius Claudius Asinius donum dedi[t ------\n
EDCS-16400519.translation: Tiberius Claudius Asinius gave this gift to Fortuna Primigenia [---].
EDCS-16400519.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Fortuna Primigenia, with Tiberius Claudius Asinius as the donor, but the full details of the gift and recipient are lost.

EDCS-16400520.text_edition: \n Flaminia Nais Fortunae Primigeniae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-16400520.translation: Flaminia Nais willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow to Fortuna Primigenia.
EDCS-16400520.summary: This inscription records Flaminia Nais fulfilling a vow to the goddess Fortuna Primigenia, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-16400528.text_edition: \n Herculi Sancto et Iunoni Sispiti sic Sextus Valerius Clau Caesa miles cohortis VII vigilum M\n
EDCS-16400528.translation: To the Holy Hercules and Juno Sispita, thus Sextus Valerius Clau Caesa, soldier of the 7th cohort of Vigiles.
EDCS-16400528.summary: This is a military dedication to the gods Hercules and Juno Sispita by a soldier of the Vigiles, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-16400569.text_edition: \n Tito Caesio Titi filio Titi nepoti Luci abnepoti Palatina Anthiano viro egregio procuratori XX hereditatium tractus Etruriae Umbriae Piceni partis Campaniae procuratori familiae gladiatoriae per Aemiliam Transpadanam DVIAS Delmatiam sic procuratori alimentorum per tribuno legionis II Parthicae piae fidelis felicis aeternae tribuno legionis praefecto cohortis II Augustae Thracum equitatae patrono et curatori Abellinatium Puteolani publice civi indigenae\n
EDCS-16400569.translation: To Titus Cassius Anthianus, son of Titus, grandson of Titus, great-grandson of Lucius, of the Palatine tribe, an outstanding man, procurator of 20 inheritances for the district of Etruria, Umbria, Picenum, and part of Campania, procurator of the gladiatorial household through the Via Emilia, DVIAS, Dalmatia, thus procurator of the alimenta, tribune of the 2nd Parthica legion, pious, faithful, fortunate, eternal, tribune of the legion, prefect of the 2nd cohort of Thracian equites Augusta, patron and curator of the Abellinati and Puteolani, publicly, a native citizen.
EDCS-16400569.summary: This is a complex funerary or honorary inscription for Titus Cassius Anthianus, detailing his extensive career in various procuratorial and military offices, likely dating to the high imperial period.

EDCS-16400575.text_edition: \n Satrius Numeri filius Teretina Rufus tribunus militum a populo IIIIvir iure dicundo quinq(uennalis) Marcus? Fufidius Luci filius Luci nepos Luci pronepos Teretina Proculus vixit IIvirum hunc colonia Iadestinorum ex provincia Hillyrico sic funere publico efferundum sic curavit Martia Fufidia Marci filia Mar Fufidius Marci filius Luci nepos (Luci) pronepos Max\n
EDCS-16400575.translation: To Ruf(us), son of Numerius, of the Teretina tribe, military tribune chosen by the people, twice duovir for dispensing justice, twice quinquennial duovir. Marcus? Fufidius Proculus, son of Lucius, grandson of Lucius, great-grandson of Lucius, of the Teretina tribe, lived. The colony of Jadestini from the province of Illyricum arranged for him to be carried in public funeral procession. Martia Fufidia, daughter of Marcus, and Mar(cus) Fufidius, son of Marcus, grandson of Lucius, great-grandson of Lucius, Max, arranged for this.
EDCS-16400575.summary: This inscription records the public funeral granted by the colony of Jadestini to Ruf(us), son of Numerius, a former military tribune and local magistrate, and possibly also to Marcus Fufidius Proculus, with arrangements made by their relatives, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16400624.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate senatus populique Tusculani Marcus Lorentius Atticus aedilis aediculam Larum Augustorum vici Angusculani vetustate dilapsam pecunia publica a solo restituit <anno> Publi Clodi Pauliniani Luci Comini Secundi aedilium quinqennalium Caius Plotius Bassus Sabinianus praefectus\n
EDCS-16400624.translation: By the authority of the Senate and People of Tusculum, Marcus Lorentius Atticus, aedile, restored from the ground up with public funds a small shrine of the Augustan Lares in the vici Angusculani, which had fallen into disrepair due to age, in the year of the quinquennial aediles Publius Clodius Paulinianus and Lucius Cominius Secundus; Caius Plotius Bassus Sabinianus, prefect.
EDCS-16400624.summary: This public works inscription documents the restoration of a shrine to the Augustan Lares by the aedile Marcus Lorentius Atticus at Tusculum, funded by the state and completed during the tenure of specific quinquennial aediles, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16500122.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Attio Cai liberto Blasto lictori III decuriarum qui Imperatori et consulibus et praetoribus apparuit et Attiae Faustillae parentibus piissimis Caius Attius Plocamianus\n
EDCS-16500122.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Caius Attius Blastus, freedman of Caius Attius, a lictor of three decuries, who attended the Emperor and consuls and praetors, and to Attia Faustilla, his most devoted parents. Caius Attius Plocamianus [erected this].
EDCS-16500122.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Caius Attius Blastus, a lictor who served various high officials, and his parents, set up by his son Caius Attius Plocamianus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16500156.summary: This is a dedication or ownership marker for Lucius Aurunculeius Secundio, possibly an individual or related to a building or property, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16500172.translation: For Aufillia, wife of Publius Mamurrus.
EDCS-16500172.summary: This is a dedication or memorial for Aufillia, wife of Publius Mamurrus, likely a funerary or honorary inscription from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16500173.translation: Ampullus erected this, decorating the face [of the statue].
EDCS-16500173.summary: This inscription records Ampullus's action of decorating a statue or monument, likely a dedication or part of a larger commission, from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16500175.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Sarmaticus Germanicus maximus Britannicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XV imperator VIII consul VI pater patriae pontem arcendae inundationis gratia fecit dedicavitque\n
EDCS-16500175.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, victor of Sarmatia, greatest victor of Germany, greatest victor of Britain, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 15th time, imperator for the 8th time, consul for the 6th time, father of the fatherland, built and dedicated this bridge for the purpose of preventing floods.
EDCS-16500175.summary: This is a monumental dedication by Emperor Commodus for the construction and dedication of a bridge to prevent floods, listing his extensive imperial titles and powers, dating to his reign (177-192 AD).

EDCS-16500181.summary: This inscription likely indicates ownership or dedication by Marcus Nautius, who held the office of quaestor, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16500182.translation: Of Gaius, son of Cem, quaestor.
EDCS-16500182.summary: This inscription records the name and office of Gaius, son of Cem, as a quaestor, likely part of a funerary monument or a dedication.

EDCS-16500183.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marco Aurelio Felici rectori regionis Statanae Nicianus Augusti nostri verna patri Aurelia Hamilla coniugi bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-16500183.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Nicianus, a household slave of our Emperor, and Aurelia Hamilla, his wife, erected this for Marcus Aurelius Felix, prefect of the Statanian district, a husband well-deserving.
EDCS-16500183.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Nicianus and Aurelia Hamilla to Marcus Aurelius Felix, a prefect of a district, and is dated to the period of the emperor Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-16600224.summary: This inscription records the name of Caecilius, son of Marcus, likely part of a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-16600429.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare VI Marco Agrippa II consulibus Publius Decrius Statius Publius Decrius Saturus Gillio sic Lucili M servus Philargur Corneli Nicol servus ETRAS\n
EDCS-16600429.translation: During the consulship of Emperor Caesar VI, Marcus Agrippa II. Publius Decrius Statius, Publius Decrius Saturus Gillio, the slave Philargyrus belonging to Lucius, and the slave Etras belonging to Cornelius Nicoll...
EDCS-16600429.summary: This inscription lists officials during the consulship of Marcus Agrippa, mentioning several individuals and their slaves, possibly related to a public building or event.

EDCS-16600430.text_edition: \n Garum factum scombri Scauri ex officina Agathonis\n
EDCS-16600430.translation: Garum made from mackerel, from the workshop of Scaurus the fish-sauce maker, belonging to Agatho.
EDCS-16600430.summary: This inscription identifies a product, garum (fish sauce), made from mackerel and produced in the workshop of Agatho, originating from Scaurus.

EDCS-16600441.translation: To Plotilla, daughter of Lucius Plotius.
EDCS-16600441.summary: This is a dedication or funerary inscription to Plotilla, daughter of Lucius Plotius.

EDCS-16600442.summary: This is a dedication by Marcus Primigenius Anteros, likely for a statue or building.

EDCS-16700115.text_edition: \n ------ XV]viro sacris faciundis VIIviro epulonum sodali Augustali Lucius Nerianus Tertius praeco apparitor ipsius et Lucius Nerianus Venustus seviri Augustales fratres\n
EDCS-16700115.translation: ...fifteen years [member] of the college of Fifteen for Sacred Rites, seven years [member] of the college of Seven for Feasts, a sodalis Augustalis. Lucius Nerianus Tertius, herald and attendant to himself, and Lucius Nerianus Venustus, Augustan seviri, brothers...
EDCS-16700115.summary: This inscription lists the public offices and honors held by Lucius Nerianus Tertius and his brother Lucius Nerianus Venustus, including membership in various priestly colleges and the Augustales, dating to the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-16700116.text_edition: \n Publio Vei Pupinia Attiano consuli po[ntifici ------\n
EDCS-16700116.translation: To Publius Veius Attianus, son of Pupinia, consul, [pontiff]...
EDCS-16700116.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Publius Veius Attianus, who held the offices of consul and pontiff, with his tribal affiliation (Pupinia) mentioned.

EDCS-16700147.text_edition: \n ------ M]aximo IIviro aedili quaestori plebs aere conlato locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-16700147.translation: ...to Maximus, twice a magistrate, aedile, quaestor. A site was granted by the plebs with contributions of money, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-16700147.summary: This inscription records the granting of a public site by the plebs and decurions to Maximus, who had served as a twice-elected magistrate, aedile, and quaestor.

EDCS-16700148.text_edition: \n Aeliae Energiae Aeli Saturnini ordo decurionum et plebs locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-16700148.translation: To Aelia Energeia, a place was given by decree of the town council and the people, in honour of Aelius Saturninus.
EDCS-16700148.summary: This inscription records a grant of public space to Aelia Energeia, likely in recognition of her husband Aelius Saturninus, as determined by the town council and populace.

EDCS-16700149.text_edition: \n Lepidio Primigenio Augustali perpetuo accenso II consulis ornamentis decurionalibus publice decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-16700149.translation: To Lepidus Primigenius, perpetual Augustalis, accensus, with the ornaments of a II consul, publicly by decree of the town council.
EDCS-16700149.summary: This inscription honors Lepidus Primigenius, who held multiple significant civic and religious offices, as decreed by the town council.

EDCS-16700214.translation: I ask you to make Postumus IIvir.
EDCS-16700214.summary: This is a public appeal urging the voters to elect Postumus to the office of IIvir (a high civic magistrate).

EDCS-16700215.translation: We, the neighbours, ask [you to elect] Lucretius Fronto as aedile.
EDCS-16700215.summary: This inscription is a public plea from neighbors requesting the election of Lucretius Fronto to the position of aedile (an official responsible for public buildings and games).

EDCS-16700258.summary: This inscription is a fragment likely naming the emperor Gaius Caesar Augustus Germanicus (Caligula), possibly part of a larger dedication or monument.

EDCS-16700277.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely indicating ownership or a dedication belonging to Lucius Acilius Strabo.

EDCS-16700279.summary: This inscription is a dedication or memorial for Gaius Rufus Holconius Priscus, a prominent individual.

EDCS-16700280.text_edition: \n Cnaeum Helvium Sabinum aedilem dignum rei publicae oro vos faciatis\n
EDCS-16700280.translation: I ask you to make Gnaeus Helvius Sabinus, a worthy aedile, for the state.
EDCS-16700280.summary: This inscription is a public appeal urging the voters to elect Gnaeus Helvius Sabinus as aedile, highlighting his suitability for the office.

EDCS-16700344.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flaviae Sozusae Flavia Augusti liberta Oeogonomia vernae suae carissimae fecit vixit annis XIII mensibus X diebus II\n
EDCS-16700344.translation: To the divine shades. Flavia Augusti liberta Oeogonomia, her freedwoman and dearest verna, Flavia Sozusa, made this, having lived 13 years, 10 months, and 2 days.
EDCS-16700344.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavia Sozusa, set up by her freedwoman and slave Flavia Oeogonomia, recording her age at death.

EDCS-16800221.text_edition: \n Imperator Titus Caesar divi Vespasiani filius Vespasianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate IX imperator XV consul IIX censor pater patriae horologium cum suis ornamentis terrae motibus conlapsum restituit\n
EDCS-16800221.translation: Emperor Titus Caesar Vespasianus Augustus, son of the deified Vespasian, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 9th time, Imperator for the 15th time, consul for the 8th time, censor, father of the fatherland, restored the horologium with its furnishings, which had collapsed due to earthquakes.
EDCS-16800221.summary: This inscription records Emperor Vespasian's restoration of a horologium (sundial or clock) that had been damaged by earthquakes, detailing his numerous titles and magistracies.

EDCS-16800245.text_edition: \n Iunoni Reginae Titus Claudius Augusti libertus Capito Diodorianus Claudia mma Claudius Atticus Claudius Felix seviri Augustales dono dederunt\n
EDCS-16800245.translation: To Juno Regina, Titus Claudius Capito, freedman of Augustus Diodorianus, and Claudia Mma, Claudius Atticus, and Claudius Felix, the seviri Augustales, gave this offering.
EDCS-16800245.summary: This inscription records an offering to Juno Regina made by several seviri Augustales, including Titus Claudius Capito, a freedman of Augustus.

EDCS-16800281.text_edition: \n Aelius Augusti libertus Agylaeus procurator libens posuit dedicavit \n
EDCS-16800281.translation: Aelius Agylaeus, freedman of Augustus, procurator, willingly placed and dedicated this.
EDCS-16800281.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Aelius Agylaeus, a procurator and freedman of Augustus.

EDCS-16800288.text_edition: \n Diis Manibus Cai Laecani Philumeni Augustalis quaestoris Caesonia Tertulla uxor bene merenti\n
EDCS-16800288.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Caesonia Tertulla, wife of Caius Laecanius Philumenus, an Augustalis and quaestor, [erected this] for him, who deserved well.
EDCS-16800288.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Caius Laecanius Philumenus, an Augustalis and quaestor, set up by his wife Caesonia Tertulla.

EDCS-16800289.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luci Terenti Valentini primi pili legionis I adiutricis praefecti legionis I Italicae Iuliae Rufinae eius\n
EDCS-16800289.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Lucius Terentius Valentinus, primipilus of the I Adiutrix legion, prefect of the I Italica Iulia Rufina legion, [set up by] his...
EDCS-16800289.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Lucius Terentius Valentinus, who held high military ranks in two legions.

EDCS-16800360.text_edition: \n Marci Pomponi Marcelli Luci Valeri Flacci duumvirorum iure dicundo Luci Obelli Lucretiani Auli Perenni Merulini duumvirorum viis aedibus sacris publicis procurandis Caio Caesare Lucio Paullo consulibus\n
EDCS-16800360.translation: By Marcus Pomponius Marcellus, Lucius Valerius Flaccus, the duumvirs with jurisdiction, Lucius Obellius Lucretianus, Aulus Perennius Merulinus, the duumvirs responsible for public roads, buildings, and sacred matters, when Gaius Caesar and Lucius Paulus were consuls.
EDCS-16800360.summary: This inscription records the names of officials responsible for public works and jurisdiction, likely related to the maintenance or construction of infrastructure during the consulship of Caesar and Paulus.

EDCS-16800361.text_edition: \n Lucius Piricatius Luci filius Menenia duumvir iure dicundo iterum sibi et suis\n
EDCS-16800361.translation: Lucius Piricatius, son of Lucius, of the Menenia tribe, duumvir with jurisdiction, for the second time, for himself and his family.
EDCS-16800361.summary: This inscription records Lucius Piricatius, serving for a second term as duumvir with jurisdiction, dedicating it for himself and his family.

EDCS-16800410.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Corneli Hilariani vixit annis XIII et Cai Corneli Primitivi vixit annis VII et Cai Corneli Heliodori vixit annis V Cornelius Festus alumnis\n
EDCS-16800410.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Caius Cornelius Hilarianus lived 13 years, and Caius Cornelius Primitivus lived 7 years, and Caius Cornelius Heliodorus lived 5 years. Cornelius Festus [set this up] for his adopted sons.
EDCS-16800410.summary: This tomb inscription records the ages of death for three children, Caius Cornelius Hilarianus, Caius Cornelius Primitivus, and Caius Cornelius Heliodorus, with the inscription set up by Cornelius Festus.

EDCS-16800411.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marci Ulpi Proculi tabulari fisci Alexandrini Domitiani Caesaris verna<e et = III> Augustorum liberto sic qui vixit ann \n
EDCS-16800411.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Marcus Ulpius Proculus, clerk of the treasury of Emperor Domitian Caesar, and a freedman of the emperors, who lived...
EDCS-16800411.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Marcus Ulpius Proculus, a clerk for the imperial treasury and a freedman.

EDCS-16800412.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caio Belleo Soteri sic Bellea Soteria patri merenti fecit\n
EDCS-16800412.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Bellea Soteria made this for her deserving father, Gaius Belleus Soter.
EDCS-16800412.summary: This is a tomb inscription set up by Bellea Soteria for her father, Gaius Belleus Soter.

EDCS-16800419.text_edition: \n Titus Octavius Titi libertus Publius Fidustius poscavia si qui a<d=R>vorsarius sic aut a<d=R>vorsaria sic \n
EDCS-16800419.translation: Titus Octavius Publius, freedman of Titus, [erected this for] Fidustius, the pursuer, or a female pursuer.
EDCS-16800419.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication or marker related to a pursuer, possibly in a legal or athletic context, by Titus Octavius Publius.

EDCS-16900086.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titi Mari Titi filii Falerna Iuliani hic ante legitimam aetatem curam egit rei frumentariae quaestoris arkae sic publicae observavit pequniam sic mun(erariam) pro nitore coloniae edidit qui vixit nnos XXVIII menses V dies X Titus Marius Onesimianus et Maria Felicitas parentes miserrimi\n
EDCS-16900086.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Titus Iulianus, son of Titus, of the Falerna tribe, who died before reaching the proper age, served as quaestor of the public treasury, administering the grain supply and contributing munificently to the prosperity of the colony. He lived 28 years, 5 months, and 10 days. Titus Marius Onesimianus and Maria Felicitas, most miserable parents.
EDCS-16900086.summary: A tombstone erected by parents for their son who died young after serving in public office, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16900124.text_edition: \n Ex iussu Iovis Optimi Maximi Heliopolitani aedem dilapsam Marcus Ulpius Sabinus aedituuus magister \n
EDCS-16900124.translation: By order of Jupiter Optimus Maximus Heliopolitanus, Marcus Ulpius Sabinus, caretaker and magistrate, [repaired] the dilapidated temple.
EDCS-16900124.summary: An inscription recording the repair of a temple dedicated to Jupiter Heliopolitanus by its caretaker and magistrate, Marcus Ulpius Sabinus.

EDCS-16900125.summary: A fragmentary inscription likely indicating ownership or dedication by Lucius Acilius Strabo.

EDCS-16900126.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Theagene veterano ex classe <p=L>raetoria Misenatium militavit ergodota vixit annis LVIII mensibus XI Aurelia Synyche coniugi bene merenti fecit \n
EDCS-16900126.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Publius Aelius Theagenes, a veteran from the Praetorian fleet of Misenum, served as a skilled soldier. He lived 58 years and 11 months. Aurelia Synyche made this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-16900126.summary: A tombstone set up by a wife for her deceased husband, Publius Aelius Theagenes, a veteran of the Misenum fleet, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16900176.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Verus sacerdos Liberi Patris item Solis Invicti domum aeternam Baebiae Berae sic matri et Baebiae Trofimeni sic sorori et Pacciae SAL Sallustiae coniugi se vivo suis et sic posterisque aeorum sic fecit\n
EDCS-16900176.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Verus, priest of Liber Pater and also of Sol Invictus, erected this eternal home for his mother Baebia Bera, for his sister Baebia Trofimena, and for his wife Paccia Sallustia, during his own lifetime, and for their descendants. He dedicated this for himself.
EDCS-16900176.summary: A funerary inscription by Verus, a priest of Liber Pater and Sol Invictus, dedicating a tomb for his family and himself, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-17000088.text_edition: \n Marcus Lucretius Luci filius Decidianus Rufus IIvir iterum quinquennalis tribunus militum a populo praefectus fabrum\n
EDCS-17000088.translation: Marcus Lucretius Decidianus Rufus, son of Lucius, twice chief magistrate, quinquennial censor, military tribune elected by the people, and prefect of the engineers.
EDCS-17000088.summary: An inscription listing the various high-ranking political and military offices held by Marcus Lucretius Decidianus Rufus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-17100023.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Caecilio Dioscoro patrono et Caeciliae Marcianae patronae et Caeciliae Piste coniugi Lucius Caecilius Hermias bene merenti fecit Dis Manibus Lucio Caecilio Dioscoro curatori Augustalium Cumanorum perpetuo itemque Augustali dupliciario Puteolanorum et curatori perpetuo embaenitariorum IIItrierum pisciniensium vixit annis LXXIII menses VIII Caecilius Hermias patrono bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-17100023.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Lucius Caecilius Hermias made this for his well-deserving patron Lucius Caecilius Dioscorus, and for his patroness Caecilia Marciana, and for his wife Caecilia Piste. To the Divine Spirits. Lucius Caecilius Dioscorus, perpetual curator of the Augustales of Cumae, and also a double-salaried Augustalis of the Puteolani, and perpetual curator of the embatarii of the fish ponds, lived 73 years and 8 months. Caecilius Hermias made this for his well-deserving patron.
EDCS-17100023.summary: A tombstone for Lucius Caecilius Dioscorus, who held multiple important civic and religious positions in Cumae and Puteoli, erected by his freedman Caecilius Hermias, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-17100123.summary: A fragmentary inscription mentioning the emperor Gaius Caesar Augustus Germanicus (Caligula), likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-17100160.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Andronicus Archon et Iulia Euporia ex voto donum dederunt\n
EDCS-17100160.translation: Gaius Julius Andronicus, Archon, and Iulia Euporia made this dedication in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-17100160.summary: A dedication made by Gaius Julius Andronicus, identified as an Archon (a Greek civic title), and Iulia Euporia, likely from a Greek-speaking region of the Roman Empire.

EDCS-17100211.text_edition: \n Marcum Heium Marci filium Caledum --- Blossiam Cai filiam Publium Heium Marci filium Caledum Chilonem Hei Marci servum Marcum Heium M(arci)? l(ibertum)? --- C(aium) Blossium |(mulieris) libertum Bithum Attonem Hei Marci servum Blossiam Luci filiam homines omnes infereis deis deligo ita ut niquis eorum quem dum videt possit ni quidq possit id dedico --- ut ea ita faciant\n
EDCS-17100211.translation: I dedicate Marcus Heius Caledus, son of Marcus, and Blossia, daughter of Gaius Publius, and Marcus Heius Caledus, son of Marcus, and Chilo, slave of Heius Marcius, and Marcus Heius, freedman of Marcus(?), and Gaius Blossius, freedman of the woman [Blossia?], and Bithus, slave of Heius Marcius, and Blossia, daughter of Lucius, as persons entirely belonging to the infernal gods, so that no one who sees them can possess them, nor have any claim over them. I dedicate them so that they may remain so.
EDCS-17100211.summary: A complex dedication of multiple individuals, including slaves and freedmen, to the infernal gods, likely a funerary or manumission-related inscription from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-17100215.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Nervae filio Nervae Traiano Augusto Germanico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate V consuli III patri patriae decuriones populusque dedicata XIIII Kalendas Octobres <T=I>iberio Claudio Attalo Mamiliano Tito Salidio Sabino IIviris \n
EDCS-17100215.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland. The decurions and the people dedicated this on the 14th day before the Kalends of October, when Tiberius Claudius Attalus Mamillianus and Titus Salidius Sabinus were duovirs.
EDCS-17100215.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Trajan, likely on a public monument, by local officials.

EDCS-17100221.text_edition: \n Laturnia Ianuaria calcaria vixit nnos XXXXV\n
EDCS-17100221.translation: Laturnia Ianuaria, a quarry worker, lived 45 years.
EDCS-17100221.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for a woman named Laturnia Ianuaria, who worked as a quarry worker.

EDCS-17100230.text_edition: \n N D E C Idibus Iulis Celer finget bipedas VXXXI actum Casilino Modesto II et Probo consulibus\n
EDCS-17100230.translation: On the Ides of July, Celer fashioned 141 bricks. This was completed in the consulship of Modestus II and Probus.
EDCS-17100230.summary: This is a record of brick production, mentioning the maker Celer and the consuls of the year.

EDCS-17200019.text_edition: \n IISII Marcus Antonius Marci filius Verna frumentum Titus Verutius Titi libertus Glycon frumentum publicum accipit die Marcus Plenius Marci filius Cogitatus frumentum publicum accipit Decimus Terentius Decimi libertus Genialis frumentum publicum accipit Caius Claudius Cai filius Silvinus frumentum publicum accipit die dedicatus Kalendis Decembribus Pisone et Iulio consulibus PANE OPT frumentum publicum accipit die XXOXXX\n
EDCS-17200019.translation: [---]a Primigenia dedicated this gift to the Good Goddess.
EDCS-17200019.summary: This is a dedication of a gift to the Bona Dea by a woman named Primigenia.

EDCS-17200069.translation: [...]ius Maxi[---] / [priest of the Great Mother Idaea of the Laurentes Lavinates] and I[---] / [---] the priesthood of the taurobolium [---] / [---] vowed [---] with Aspro again / and Aspro as consuls.
EDCS-17200069.summary: This is a fragmented inscription mentioning a priest of the Great Mother of the Gods and a taurobolium ritual, dated by the consulship of Aspro.

EDCS-17200172.translation: Celer, scribe, slave of Priscus, freedman of the Emperor Tiberius Claudius, set this up for the Good Goddess of Castra Fontanorum.
EDCS-17200172.summary: This inscription records the setting up of a monument for the Good Goddess by a freedman slave and scribe.

EDCS-17200238.translation: Publius Cornelius Thallus, son of Publius Cornelius Architectus, master of the quinquennial guild of carpenters for the 27th lustrum, in the name of his son, Publius Cornelius Architectianus, elected to the order of decurions, dedicated this statue of Fides as a gift.
EDCS-17200238.summary: This inscription records a guild master dedicating a statue of Fides, likely in honor of his son's political advancement.

EDCS-17201666.translation: To Lucius Aemilius Nason Fabullinus, son of Lucius, of the Galeria tribe, a broad-striped tribune of the 20th Legion Valeria Victrix, a moneyer, by decree of the decurions, publicly.
EDCS-17201666.summary: This is a funerary dedication to a military officer, Lucius Aemilius Nason Fabullinus, honored by public decree.

EDCS-17201731.text_edition: \n Publice Caio Cartilio Caio filio Poplicolae duoviru sic III libereis sic posterisque eius decurionum decreto colonorumque consensu preimario sic viro pro eius meriteis sic hoc monumentum constitutum est eique merenti gratia rellata sic est isque octiens duomvir sic ter censor colonorum iudicio apsens sic praesensque factus est ob eius amorem in universos ab universieis sic Humaniae Marci filiae\n
EDCS-17201731.translation: By public decree and the consent of the colonists, this monument was established for Caius Cartilius Poplicola, son of Caius, duovir, and for his freeborn children and their descendants, and gratitude was shown to him for his merits, and he was made duovir eight times and censor three times by the judgment of the colonists, absent and present, because of his love for all, by all, [dedicated by] Humanias, daughter of Marcus.
EDCS-17201731.summary: This inscription honors Caius Cartilius Poplicola, a prominent local official who served multiple terms as duovir and censor, by public decree and the colonists.

EDCS-17300116.translation: Sacred to Minerva Augusta. Publius Aelius Charinus, soldier, provisioner of the Fourth Legion Flavia Felix, willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-17300116.summary: A soldier dedicates an offering to Minerva Augusta, likely a personal vow.

EDCS-17300919.translation: Claudia Claudiana Honorata, daughter of Tiberius, wife of Natalis, freedman of the emperor, a procurator, in memory of Flavius Aquilinus, dedicated a statue of Venus.
EDCS-17300919.summary: A woman dedicates a statue of Venus in memory of Flavius Aquilinus, possibly her deceased husband or a respected figure.

EDCS-17301028.translation: To Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, grandson of the deified Julius, pontifex maximus, consul for the 5th time, imperator for the 8th time, with tribunician power for the 38th time, augur, member of the college of fifteen for performing sacred rites, member of the college of seven for feasting, Lucius Scribonius Celer, son of Lucius, of the Voturia tribe, as aedile, by decree of the decurions, for the games.
EDCS-17301028.summary: An inscription honoring Emperor Tiberius, commissioned by an aedile for public games.

EDCS-17500065.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, son of the deified Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the deified Commodus, nephew of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-nephew of the deified Hadrian, grand-nephew of the deified Trajan Parthicus, and great-great-nephew of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 10th time, imperator for the 11th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, proconsul; and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, son of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, nephew of the deified Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-nephew of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-nephew of the deified Hadrian, and great-great-great-nephew of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva, with tribunician power for the 5th time, consul, proconsul; they constructed a new structure for the defense of the road, damaged by flooding from the sea.
EDCS-17500065.summary: An inscription detailing the construction of a new defensive structure by Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, likely to protect a road from coastal flooding, possibly dated to their co-reign.

EDCS-17500103.translation: Dedicated on the 11th day before the Kalends of July, during the consulship of Emperor Commodus Augustus for the 3rd time and Antistius Burrus. The site was assigned by Nasennius Marcellus, curator of public works, by the curators Valerius Felix and Avillius Pudentes.
EDCS-17500103.summary: A dedication marking the assignment of a site for public works during the consulship of Commodus, with specific officials named.

EDCS-17500105.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Flavia Epigone made this for her well-deserving husband, Bifonius Celestinus, an African by nation, who served for 18 years.
EDCS-17500105.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a wife for her well-deserving husband, noting his nationality and length of service.

EDCS-17500106.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Maia Donata made this for her well-deserving husband, Marcus Caecilius Letus, a veteran from the Praetorian fleet of Misenum, who lived for 63 years.
EDCS-17500106.summary: A tombstone for a veteran of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, erected by his wife.

EDCS-17500107.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Calpurnia Elpis for her well-deserving patron, Marcus Calpurnius [---]mo, a veteran from the Praetorian fleet of Misenum, who served for 28 years and lived for 60 years.
EDCS-17500107.summary: A funerary inscription for a veteran of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, honoring his patron.

EDCS-17500108.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Titus Suillus Albanus for his well-deserving [person], Marcus Calventius Priscus, a Bithynian, who lived 45 years and served 22 years on the galley Danuvius.
EDCS-17500108.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Calventius Priscus, a Bithynian who served in the navy and lived to 45, with Titus Suillus Albanus as the dedicator.

EDCS-17500109.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Octabia Arescusa, his wife, made this for her well-deserving Gaius Calbisius Secundus, a soldier from the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, who served 21 years on the galley Ceres III.
EDCS-17500109.summary: A funerary inscription for a sailor in the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, dedicated by his wife.

EDCS-17500110.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Lucius Carisius Blandus, soldier of the trireme Triumpho, a Bessian by nation, who lived 48 years and served 25 years. Carisia Fortunata to her well-deserving patron.
EDCS-17500110.summary: A funerary dedication by Carisia Fortunata to her deceased patron, Lucius Carisius Blandus, a Bessian soldier who served in the trireme Triumpho.

EDCS-17500111.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Gaius Cassius, veteran, a [---] by nation.
EDCS-17500111.summary: A fragmentary funerary dedication to a veteran named Gaius Cassius, whose ethnicity is partially preserved.

EDCS-17500112.translation: To Gaius Cassius Celer, soldier of the praetorian fleet of Misenum. Tyche, his freedwoman, to her well-deserving patron.
EDCS-17500112.summary: A funerary dedication by Tyche, a freedwoman, to her deceased patron, Gaius Cassius Celer, a soldier in the Misenum praetorian fleet.

EDCS-17500113.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Marcus Cassius Diogenes, veteran, a Cilician by nation, who lived 70 years. Marcus Cassius Bitalis and Cassia Ianuaria made this for their most beloved father, well-deserving.
EDCS-17500113.summary: A funerary monument erected by Marcus Cassius Bitalis and Cassia Ianuaria for their father, Marcus Cassius Diogenes, a Cilician veteran.

EDCS-17500115.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Marcus Licinius Valentis, soldier of the praetorian fleet of Misenum, serving in the trireme Augusto, who lived 32 years and served 12 years. Quintus Cassius Romanus, his heir, performed the rites for his well-deserving [---].
EDCS-17500115.summary: A funerary dedication by Quintus Cassius Romanus for Marcus Licinius Valentis, a soldier in the Misenum praetorian fleet who died at age 32 after 12 years of service.

EDCS-17500116.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, sacred. To Marcus Cassius Vitalis, soldier of the praetorian fleet of Misenum, who lived 25 years and 10 months. His heirs made this.
EDCS-17500116.summary: A funerary dedication by the heirs of Marcus Cassius Vitalis, a soldier of the Misenum praetorian fleet who died at age 25 years and 10 months.

EDCS-17500117.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Lucius Catius Viator, soldier of the trireme Aquila, a Corsican by nation, who lived 50 years and served 27 years. Tarquinius Valens, his heir from the same unit, made this for his well-deserving [---].
EDCS-17500117.summary: A funerary dedication by Tarquinius Valens for Lucius Catius Viator, a Corsican soldier of the trireme Aquila who died at age 50 after 27 years of service.

EDCS-17500118.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Claudia, daughter, who lived 1 year. Gaius Claudius Agricola and Flavia Agathoclea, her parents, made this [from the trireme] Libero Patre.
EDCS-17500118.summary: A funerary dedication by Gaius Claudius Agricola and Flavia Agathoclea for their daughter Claudia, who died at age 1.

EDCS-17500119.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Marcus Claudius Apollinaris, soldier of the trireme Aquila, an Alexandrian by nation, who served 7 years and lived 30 years. Claudia Auxesis, his heir, made this for her well-deserving [---].
EDCS-17500119.summary: A funerary dedication by Claudia Auxesis for her heir Marcus Claudius Apollinaris, an Alexandrian soldier of the trireme Aquila who died at age 30 after 7 years of service.

EDCS-17500120.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Gaius Claudius Isauricus, soldier of the trireme Concordia, a Phrygian by nation, who lived 38 years and served 19 years. Cn. Domitius Faustus, the fiduciary, and his heirs, Tertia for her husband and Secunda for her father, made this for the well-deserving [---].
EDCS-17500120.summary: A funerary dedication by the heirs, including his wife Tertia and daughter Secunda, for Gaius Claudius Isauricus, a Phrygian soldier of the trireme Concordia who died at age 38 after 19 years of service.

EDCS-17500121.translation: To the departed spirits. Tiberius Claudius Zeno, an Egyptian by birth, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, from the quinquereme Vesta. He lived for 32 years, served for 12 years. Petronius Ferox, of the quinquereme Fortuna, his heir, made this for him deserving of his merits.
EDCS-17500121.summary: A funerary epitaph for Tiberius Claudius Zeno, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet, set up by his heir.

EDCS-17500122.translation: [He served] for [3] years. He lived for [3] years. From [Al...] by birth. Clodia Piroclea, his wife and heir, had this made for him deserving of his merits.
EDCS-17500122.summary: A funerary epitaph, with significant portions missing, likely for a soldier, set up by his wife and heir.

EDCS-17500123.translation: To the departed spirits. To Quintus Cocceius Turbon, a centurion of the quinquereme Victoria. He served for 23 years, Egyptian by birth. He lived for 43 years. Callityche, his heir, made this for her husband deserving of his merits.
EDCS-17500123.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Cocceius Turbon, a centurion in the fleet, set up by his wife and heir.

EDCS-17500124.translation: To the departed spirits. To Gaius Cogitatius Valens, a centurion of the quinquereme Dacicus, a Pannonian by birth. He served for 2 years, lived for 25 years. Natalis Victor and Valerius Velox, his heirs from the same unit, [made this] for him deserving of his merits.
EDCS-17500124.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Cogitatius Valens, a soldier of the fleet, set up by his heirs.

EDCS-17500126.translation: To the departed spirits. Cyrilla, Cappadocian by birth, who lived for 30 years. Valerius Vitalio, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, made this for his sweetest wife deserving of her merits.
EDCS-17500126.summary: A funerary epitaph for Cyrilla, set up by her husband, Valerius Vitalio, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet.

EDCS-17500127.translation: To the departed spirits. For Titus Dinnius Celer, centurion of the trireme Vesta, Corsican by birth, with 22 years of service. Antonia Nereis made this for her husband deserving of his merits.
EDCS-17500127.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Dinnius Celer, a centurion in the fleet, set up by his wife.

EDCS-17500128.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gaius Dinnius Valens, a veteran of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, a Bessi by birth. Dinnius Calocaerus and Dinnia Valentina, his sons, and Afrodisia, his freedwoman, [made this] for their deserving patron.
EDCS-17500128.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Dinnius Valens, a veteran of the fleet, set up by his children and freedwoman.

EDCS-17500130.summary: A very brief inscription recording the name and veteran status of Gaius Domitius Priscus.

EDCS-17500131.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gaius Epidius Firmus, from the trireme Hercules, a Bessi by birth. He lived for 30 years, served for 11 years. Cominius [3]ius, his brother, [made this].
EDCS-17500131.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Epidius Firmus, a soldier, set up by his brother.

EDCS-17500132.translation: To the departed spirits. For Felicus, an excellent man, a German by birth. Gaius Iustus Gemellus made this for his slave, Felicus, of the trireme Victoria, deserving of his merits.
EDCS-17500132.summary: A funerary epitaph for Felicus, a slave, set up by his owner, Gaius Iustus Gemellus, a soldier.

EDCS-17500133.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caius Longinius Capito, a veteran, erected this for Lucius Flavius Valentis, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, serving on the trireme Nilus, his deserving heir.
