EDCS-17500133.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Lucius Flavius Valentis, a soldier of the Misenum fleet, set up by his veteran heir, Caius Longinius Capito.

EDCS-17500134.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Titus Iulius Numisianus, serving on the trireme Libero Patri, erected this for Caius Herennius Ptolemaeus, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, who served on the trireme Lucifero, an Egyptian by birth, who served for 25 years and lived for 44 years, his deserving heir.
EDCS-17500134.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caius Herennius Ptolemaeus, an Egyptian soldier of the Misenum fleet who served on the trireme Lucifero, erected by his heir.

EDCS-17500135.translation: To the spirits of the departed. The trireme [Salvia?] erected this for Caius? Julius? [---], with 25 years of service, [---] by nation, [---] heir V? pentere? Vict? [---] sonius Mar? [---] us, his heir Quintus Caesius? and Aelia Sot? [---] bene [merenti fecerunt].
EDCS-17500135.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription likely for a soldier of the trireme Salvia, with a restored dedication by heirs.

EDCS-17500137.translation: To the spirits of the departed. This monument is for Caius Iulius Alexandrus, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, serving on the trireme Castor, who lived for 53 years and served for 31 years, with Cassius Antiochus and Antonius Nigrinus attending.
EDCS-17500137.summary: This funerary inscription records the life and service of Caius Iulius Alexandrus, a soldier of the Misenum fleet, with caretakers named.

EDCS-17500138.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Caius Iulius Apollinaris, of Egyptian origin, serving on the trireme Hercules, who lived for 28 years and served for 9 years.
EDCS-17500138.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caius Iulius Apollinaris, an Egyptian soldier of the trireme Hercules, stating his age and service length.

EDCS-17500139.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Caius Iulius Apuleius, from the trireme Mars, who lived for 28 years and served for 10 years. Iulia Epiteuxis, his heir, erected this for him, as he deserved well.
EDCS-17500139.summary: This funerary inscription records the life and service of Caius Iulius Apuleius of the trireme Mars, erected by his heir Iulia Epiteuxis.

EDCS-17500140.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Caius Iulius Capiton, a manipularis from the trireme Vesta, who lived for 35 years and served for 16 years.
EDCS-17500140.summary: This funerary inscription records the life and service of Caius Iulius Capiton, a soldier of the trireme Vesta.

EDCS-17500141.text_edition: \n Lucio Valerio Tertullo veterano ex classe praetoria Misenensi Lucilia Fortunata coniunx bene merenti\n
EDCS-17500141.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucilia Fortunata, wife, for her deserving husband Lucius Valerius Tertullus, a veteran of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum.
EDCS-17500141.summary: This funerary dedication is for Lucius Valerius Tertullus, a veteran of the Misenum fleet, by his wife Lucilia Fortunata.

EDCS-17500142.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulia Maximilla, wife, erected this for her deserving husband Caius Iulius Didymus, a veteran.
EDCS-17500142.summary: This funerary inscription is for Caius Iulius Didymus, a veteran, by his wife Iulia Maximilla.

EDCS-17500143.translation: To the spirits of the departed. The heir erected this for his deserving [relative/friend?] Caius Iulius Gemellinus?, son of Caius?, of German origin, serving on the trireme Libertas? [---].
EDCS-17500143.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Caius Iulius Gemellinus?, of German origin, serving on the trireme Libertas, with the dedication by an heir.

EDCS-17500144.translation: To the shades of the departed. Caius Iulius Petronianus, from the warship *Armata*, of Egyptian nationality, lived 45 years, served 22 years. Caius Longinius Clemens the Third, heir, made this for the deserving one.
EDCS-17500144.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Iulius Petronianus, an Egyptian sailor who served in the navy, erected by his heir.

EDCS-17500145.translation: Of Caius Iulius Pudens, a centurion of the warship *Libertas*, who was of Bessian nationality, lived 40 years, served 23 years. Caius Antestius Rufus, heir, made this for the deserving one.
EDCS-17500145.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Iulius Pudens, a sailor of Bessian origin who served in the navy, erected by his heir.

EDCS-17500146.translation: To the shades of the departed. Quintus [---], from the trireme *Fides*, of Greek nationality, lived 32 years, served 13 years. The heirs Iulius Serenus and Domitius Sten[---] made this.
EDCS-17500146.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus, a Greek sailor from the trireme *Fides*, erected by his heirs.

EDCS-17500147.translation: To the shades of the departed. Marcus Iulius Valens, from the trireme *Cerere*, lived 23 years, served 2 years. Marcus Antonius Valens made this for his son, the deserving one.
EDCS-17500147.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Iulius Valens, a sailor on the trireme *Cerere*, erected by his father.

EDCS-17500148.translation: Caius Iulius Victor, also called Sola, son of Dinius, a soldier from the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, from the warship *Fides*, served 10 years, lived 30 years. He ordered this to be made by his will.
EDCS-17500148.summary: An epitaph for Caius Iulius Victor, a sailor in the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, who served on the warship *Fides*, his monument erected according to his will.

EDCS-17500149.translation: Iulia Benedicta made this for herself while alive, and for her husband Aelius Eutychianus, a veteran of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, and for her daughter Aelia Animequitati, and for her freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. If anyone wishes to place a stranger in this monument or burial plot or to sell the space, they will have to pay a penalty of 10,000 sesterces to the public treasury of the Puteolani.
EDCS-17500149.summary: A funerary monument erected by Iulia Benedicta for herself and her family, with strict conditions against unauthorized burial or sale of plots, establishing a penalty payable to the city of Puteoli.

EDCS-17500150.translation: To the shades of the departed. For Iulia Rufina, his most beloved wife. Iulius Apollinaris, a centurion of the trireme *Libero Patre*, made this for the deserving one.
EDCS-17500150.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulia Rufina, erected by her husband Iulius Apollinaris, a naval officer.

EDCS-17500151.translation: To the shades of the departed. Iulia Serapias lived 5 years, daughter of Iulius Apollinaris, from the trireme *Venera*.
EDCS-17500151.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulia Serapias, a young child and daughter of Iulius Apollinaris, a naval officer.

EDCS-17500152.translation: To the shades of the departed. For Titus Laelius Crispus, soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, on the trireme *Libertas*, of Bithynian nationality, lived 40 years, served 16 years. Lucius Sossius Dionysius, of the trireme *Capricorno*, heir, [made this] for the deserving one.
EDCS-17500152.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Laelius Crispus, a soldier from Bithynia serving on the trireme *Libertas* in the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, erected by his heir.

EDCS-17500153.translation: To the shades of the departed. Titus Licinius Memor, soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, from the trireme *Venera*, served 20 years, lived 40 years. Marcus Nonius Aquilinus, his brother, from the quinquereme *Libertas*, heir, made this for the deserving one.
EDCS-17500153.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Licinius Memor, a soldier from Sardinia serving on the trireme *Venera* in the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, erected by his brother who also served in the navy.

EDCS-17500154.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lollio Valens of Milan, a rower in the trireme Fides, who served as a rower for 17 days, lived 25 years. Annia Eutyches (?) made this for her son.
EDCS-17500154.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lollio Valens, a rower in the fleet, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17500155.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Maecius Rufus, a rower in the trireme, of the Vessus tribe, lived 30 years, served 11 years. Scedius Memor and Tarullius Rufus made this for him as deserving.
EDCS-17500155.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Maecius Rufus, a rower in the fleet, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17500156.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Caelestinus of Sardinia, who served 14 years, lived 32 years. His daughter made this for her deserving father.
EDCS-17500156.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Caelestinus, a sailor in the fleet, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17500157.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Marius Celsus, a rower in the trireme Athe, of the Bessus tribe, who lived 45 years, served 27 years. Lucius Valerius [---] made this for the deserving man.
EDCS-17500157.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Marius Celsus, a rower in the fleet, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17500158.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Marius Dexter, a rower in the trireme Sole, of Greek origin, who lived 25 years, served 5 years. Marcus Timinius Ponticus, his heir, made this for him as deserving.
EDCS-17500158.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Marius Dexter, a rower in the fleet, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17500160.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Gaius Marius Montanus, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, a rower in the quadrireme Veneris, of Cilician origin, who lived 40 years, served 20 years. Gaius Iu[---]
EDCS-17500160.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Marius Montanus, a soldier in the Praetorian fleet, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17500161.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lucius Marius Montanus, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, a rower in the quinquireme Victoria, who served 26 years. Iulia Helpis made this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-17500161.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Marius Montanus, a soldier in the Praetorian fleet, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17500162.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Marcius Martialis of Pannonia, who lived 50 years, served 26 years. Veturius Quintianus, his heir, made this for him as deserving.
EDCS-17500162.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Marcius Martialis, a sailor in the fleet, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17500163.translation: To the spirits of the departed, made for his deserving wife. For Memmia Fortunata of Picenum, who lived 45 years. Arrius Isidorus, a rower of Alexandrian origin, on whom he spent 300 denarii.
EDCS-17500163.summary: A funerary epitaph for Memmia Fortunata and Arrius Isidorus, possibly a couple, with a record of expenses, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17500164.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Gaius Munatius Rufus, a veteran. Iulia Rufina made this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-17500164.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Munatius Rufus, a veteran, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17500165.translation: To the departed spirits. To Gaius Mussidius Capito, soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, from the trireme Pietas. He lived 36 years, served 16 years. Terentius Valens, his heir, made this for him, deserving well.
EDCS-17500165.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Mussidius Capito, a soldier of the Misenum fleet, set up by his heir.

EDCS-17500166.translation: To the departed spirits. To Gaius Velonius Macer, a veteran centurion from the Praetorian fleet at Misenum. He served 26 years, lived 61 years, a Bessian by birth. Gaius Velonius Macer, a soldier from the same fleet, and Valeria Velonia, his children, made this for their most devoted father. To the departed spirits. To Naevius Sentianus, soldier of the VI(?) legion. And Carpine, his wife. Naevia Ammias, for her parents.
EDCS-17500166.summary: A double funerary epitaph for Gaius Velonius Macer, a veteran centurion and his son, and for Naevius Sentianus and his wife Carpine, likely all associated with the Misenum fleet.

EDCS-17500167.translation: To the departed spirits. To Gaius Numisius Heraclidas, a Greek by birth, manipularis on the trireme Victoria. He lived 55 years, served 29 years. Ammionius Ammonianus and Julia Celestina, his wife, his heirs, made this for him, deserving well.
EDCS-17500167.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Numisius Heraclidas, a Greek soldier of the Misenum fleet, set up by his heir and wife.

EDCS-17500168.translation: To the departed spirits. To Titus Flavius Calpurnius, from the trireme Pollux, a Sardinian by birth. He served 8 years, lived 25 years. Numisius Romanus from the same unit, and Julius Proclus from the trireme Pietas, his heirs, made this for him, deserving well.
EDCS-17500168.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Flavius Calpurnius, a soldier from the Misenum fleet, set up by his heirs.

EDCS-17500169.translation: To Lucius Papirius Ursio, veteran of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, an Egyptian by birth. He lived 55 years. Lucia Papiria, his freedwoman, made this for her patron, deserving well.
EDCS-17500169.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Papirius Ursio, a veteran of the Misenum fleet, set up by his freedwoman.

EDCS-17500170.translation: To the departed spirits. To Tiberius Petronius Celer, an Alexandrian by birth, from the trireme Isis. He lived 40 years, served 17 years. Titius Aquilinus, Epidius Pansa, from the trireme Isis, his heirs, made this for him, deserving well.
EDCS-17500170.summary: A funerary epitaph for Tiberius Petronius Celer, a soldier from the Misenum fleet, set up by his heirs.

EDCS-17500171.translation: To the departed spirits. To Marcus Petronius Valentis, veteran of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum. Petronia Prima, for her well-deserving patron.
EDCS-17500171.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Petronius Valentis, a veteran of the Misenum fleet, set up by Petronia Prima.

EDCS-17500172.translation: To the departed spirits. To Lucius Pompeius Asiaticus, of the trireme Sol, an Alexandrian by birth. He served 27 years, lived 54 years. And to Marcus Pompeius, his son, who lived 8 years, 5 months, and 10 days. Valeria Marcia made this for her most beloved husband and dearest son, deserving well.
EDCS-17500172.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Pompeius Asiaticus, a soldier of the Misenum fleet, and his young son Marcus Pompeius, set up by his wife.

EDCS-17500173.translation: To the departed spirits. To Gaius Ravonius Celer, also called Bato, a Dalmatian by birth, manipularis on the trireme Isis. He served 11 years, lived [---]. Lucius Aelius [---] Vener [---]
EDCS-17500173.summary: A partial funerary epitaph for Gaius Ravonius Celer, a Dalmatian soldier from the Misenum fleet, with incomplete information about his heirs and death.

EDCS-17500174.translation: To the departed spirits. To Gaius Rufius Cassianus, of the trireme Minerva, a Cilician by birth. He served 6 years, lived 30 years. Domitius Crispus made this for him, deserving well.
EDCS-17500174.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Rufius Cassianus, a soldier of the Misenum fleet, set up by Domitius Crispus.

EDCS-17500177.translation: Marcus Seius Longinus, also known as Menophilus Antonius Nicaensis, a veteran of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, lived 63 years, served 30 years, ordered this to be made by his will for himself and for his deserving wife Curtia Helpis, and for his freedmen, freedwomen, and descendants. Marcus Seius Hermes and Curtia Pietis.
EDCS-17500177.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Seius Longinus, a veteran of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, commissioned by his will and dedicated by his heirs.

EDCS-17500178.translation: Caius Sempronius Maximus, of the trireme Athenonice, of Cilician origin, lived 45 years, served 25 years. Iulia Marcia made this for her deserving wife.
EDCS-17500178.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Sempronius Maximus, a sailor of possibly mixed ethnicity, dedicated by his wife.

EDCS-17500180.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gaius Senius Severus, a subordinate sailor of the trireme Fides, of Bessian origin, lived 46 years, served 25 years. Lucius Aemilius Dolens, his heir, made this for the deserving deceased.
EDCS-17500180.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Senius Severus, a sailor of Bessian origin, dedicated by his heir.

EDCS-17500181.translation: To the departed spirits. For Lucius Sentius Zeno, a subordinate sailor of the trireme Minerva, of Syrian origin, lived 30 years, served 6 years. Murrius Rufus and Safe[nius?] Rufus, his heirs, made this for the deserving deceased.
EDCS-17500181.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Sentius Zeno, a sailor of Syrian origin, dedicated by his heirs.

EDCS-17500182.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gnaeus Silanus Pius, of the trireme Marte, of Sardinian origin, served 25 years, lived 45 years. Titia Nice and his son Genialis made this for the deserving deceased.
EDCS-17500182.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gnaeus Silanus Pius, a sailor of Sardinian origin, dedicated by his wife and son.

EDCS-17500183.translation: To the departed spirits. For Caius Silius Fortis, a veteran of Pannonian origin, lived 55 years. Silia Macaria and Silia Onesime, his freedwomen, made this for their deserving father.
EDCS-17500183.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Silius Fortis, a Pannonian veteran, dedicated by his freedwomen.

EDCS-17500184.translation: To the departed spirits. For Silicius Longus, a subordinate sailor of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, of the trireme Triumpho, served 21 years, lived 40 years. Claudius Longinus, his heir (?), made this for the deserving deceased.
EDCS-17500184.summary: A funerary epitaph for Silicius Longus, a sailor in the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, dedicated by his heir.

EDCS-17500185.translation: To the departed spirits. For Quintus Silicius Silbanus, of African origin, lived 60 years, a retired man of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum. Quintus Silbanus, his son, and Messea Ianuaria, his wife, made this for the deserving deceased.
EDCS-17500185.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Silicius Silbanus, an African veteran of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, dedicated by his son and wife.

EDCS-17500186.translation: To the departed spirits. For Silicius Victor, a soldier who lived 28 years, served 8 years, 7 months, and 5 days.
EDCS-17500186.summary: A brief funerary epitaph for Silicius Victor, recording his age and service duration.

EDCS-17500188.translation: To the departed spirits. For Marcus Stlabius Felicissimus, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, who lived 28 years, served 6 years. Aurelia Primigenia, his wife, made this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-17500188.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Stlabius Felicissimus, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, dedicated by his wife.

EDCS-17500190.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Taesis of Egypt, daughter of Apollonopolis from Coptos, lived 30 years; wife of Iulius Apollinaris, legionary of the trireme Venus.
EDCS-17500190.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Taesis, an Egyptian woman who lived 30 years and was married to a legionary, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-17500191.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Gaius Tamudius Cassianus, soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, a legionary of the trireme Providentia, from Sardinia, lived 28 years and served 8 years. Sextus Iulius Quirinus, legionary of the trireme Fortuna, his heir, made this for him as deserving.
EDCS-17500191.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Tamudius Cassianus, a Sardinian soldier in the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, who served 8 years and lived 28, with his heir erecting the monument, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-17500192.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Gaius Tamudius Priscus, legionary of the trireme Vict<o=C>ria, served 7 years and lived 35 years. Gaius Lucceius Priscus, his heir, [erected this].
EDCS-17500192.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Tamudius Priscus, a legionary of the trireme Vict<o=C>ria, who lived 35 years and served 7, with his heir erecting the monument, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-17500193.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Tesneus Serapias of Egypt, lived 32 years. His wife Annius Bassus, legionary of the trireme Iove, and his son Serapion made this for him as deserving.
EDCS-17500193.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tesneus Serapias of Egypt, who lived 32 years, erected by his wife and son, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-17500194.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius Terentius Sabinus, legionary of the trireme Salute, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, from Pannonia. Aelius Romanus made this for him as deserving.
EDCS-17500194.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Terentius Sabinus, a Pannonian soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, erected by Aelius Romanus, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-17500195.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Titus Tiberius Marcus, soldier of the Misenian fleet, from Moesia, lived 37 years and served 12 years. His wife, from the trireme Iside, made this for him as deserving.
EDCS-17500195.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titus Tiberius Marcus, a soldier from Moesia serving in the Misenian fleet, who lived 37 years and served 12, erected by his wife, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-17500196.translation: [Son]dius Valens, [member] of the liburna Aquila, [to his freedwoman] S<3>diae Sympha[erus]ae, for her deserving of this and for his descendants and their descendants.
EDCS-17500196.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a freedwoman named S<3>diae Sympha[erus]ae, set up by her patron or husband, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-17500197.translation: [To] Misa[u]cius Valens, soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, of the trireme Cupid(ine), from Dalmatia, served 21 years and lived 40 years. Cattius Vitalis, his heir, [erected this].
EDCS-17500197.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Misa[u]cius Valens, a Dalmatian soldier in the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, who lived 40 years and served 21, with his heir erecting the monument, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-17500198.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Valerius Bassus veteranus ex classe praetoria Misenensi natione Aegyptus vixit annos LVII menses militavit annos Doris liberta et heres bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-17500198.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius Valerius Bassus, veteran of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, from Egypt, lived 57 years and served [number of months illegible]. Doris, his freedwoman and heir, made this for him as deserving.
EDCS-17500198.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Valerius Bassus, an Egyptian veteran of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, who lived 57 years, erected by his freedwoman and heir, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-17500199.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Iulia Cyrila made this for her well-deserving husband, Valerius Aelianus, who lived 60 years and served 28 years.
EDCS-17500199.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valerius Aelianus, set up by his wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17500200.translation: Quintus Valerius Asclepiades made this for his well-deserving brother, who served 24 years and lived 48 years.
EDCS-17500200.summary: A funerary epitaph for an unnamed man, set up by his brother, indicating military service and likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17500201.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Basilius Germanus, a rower on the trireme Virtus of the Praetorian fleet of Ravenna, who served 15 years and lived 40 years, and Caius Iulius Constans, a rower on the trireme Virtus, his heir and sub-heir, set this up for the well-deserving Caius Valerius Bassus, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet of Ravenna, a rower on the trireme Triumpho.
EDCS-17500201.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Valerius Bassus, a soldier and rower in the Ravenna fleet, set up by his heir and sub-heir, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17500202.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Valerius Fortunalis and Valerius Secundus, sons, made this for their well-deserving father, Caius Valerius Clementis, a veteran, a native slave; and for their father's father and our mother, Hermae Tatani and Amynae Abiae. Also for freedmen and freedwomen.
EDCS-17500202.summary: A funerary epitaph for a veteran, Caius Valerius Clementis, and his family, set up by his sons, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17500203.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Caius Calbisius Cerealis, heir, made this for his well-deserving Marcus Valerius Dexter, a sailor on the liburna Neptuno.
EDCS-17500203.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Valerius Dexter, a sailor, set up by his heir, possibly dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17500204.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Mestria Euhodia, heir, made this for her well-deserving Caius Valerius Germanus, a soldier in the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, a rower on the trireme Tauro, who served 25 years, a native of Sardinia.
EDCS-17500204.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Valerius Germanus, a soldier in the Misenum fleet, set up by his heir, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17500205.translation: To the gods of the underworld. The heirs made this for their well-deserving Lucius Valerius Germanus, a soldier in the Praetorian fleet, a rower on the trireme Augusto, who lived 48 years and served 26 years.
EDCS-17500205.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Valerius Germanus, a soldier in the Praetorian fleet, set up by his heirs, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17500206.translation: Lucius Saturninus, heir from the same unit, made this for his well-deserving Sextus Valerius Ingenuus, a rower on the trireme Augusto, a native of Sardinia, who lived 30 years and served 7 years.
EDCS-17500206.summary: A funerary epitaph for Sextus Valerius Ingenuus, a rower, set up by his heir, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17500207.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Valerius Apollinaris, heir, made this for his well-deserving Caius Valerius Longinus, a sailor on the liburna Aesculapio, who served 15 years and lived 30 years, a native of Cilicia.
EDCS-17500207.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Valerius Longinus, a sailor, set up by his heir, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17500208.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Iulius Hermogenes, heir, made this for his well-deserving Marcus Valerius Marcinas, a rower on the trireme Venere, who served 16 years and lived 35 years, a native of Syria.
EDCS-17500208.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Valerius Marcinas, a rower, set up by his heir, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17500209.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Lucius Valerius Martial, soldier from the Praetorian fleet of Misenum, from the quadrire-me *Vesta*, a Bessian by birth. He served for 9 years, lived for 30 years. Marcus Valerius Asper made this for his well-deserving brother.
EDCS-17500209.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Valerius Martial, a soldier in the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, set up by his brother.

EDCS-17500210.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Quintus Valerius Maximo, a veteran, a slave from Ostia. He served for 26 years, lived for 47 years. Quintus Valerius Aquila, his son, made this for him, his well-deserving father.
EDCS-17500210.summary: This is a tombstone for Quintus Valerius Maximo, a veteran, erected by his son.

EDCS-17500211.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valerius Saturnius, soldier of the Praetorian fleet of Misenum, lived for 28 years. Ofania Ianuaria made this for her well-deserving friend.
EDCS-17500211.summary: This is a tombstone for Valerius Saturnius, a soldier in the Misenum fleet, dedicated by Ofania Ianuaria.

EDCS-17500212.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Marcus Valerius Similis, soldier from the Praetorian fleet of Misenum, a Bessian by birth. He served for 28 years, lived for 50 years. Lucius Valerius Macrinus, from the trireme *Neptune*, and Gaius Tarsinius Fuscus, his heirs, made this for the well-deserving man.
EDCS-17500212.summary: This is a tombstone for Marcus Valerius Similis, a Bessian soldier in the Misenum fleet, erected by his heirs.

EDCS-17500213.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Valerius Valens, a *manipularis* of the liburna *Iustitia*, a Bessian by birth. He served for 27 years, lived for 47 years. Lucius Valerius Priscus, his son and heir, made this for his well-deserving father.
EDCS-17500213.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Valerius Valens, a *manipularis* serving on the liburna *Iustitia*, erected by his son and heir.

EDCS-17500214.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Quintus Valerius Valentis, a *manipularis* of the trireme *Soles*, served for 21 years, lived for 40 years. Lucius Memmius Rufinus and Marcus Arruntius Longinus, his heirs, [made this] for his merits.
EDCS-17500214.summary: This is a tombstone for Quintus Valerius Valentis, a *manipularis* on the trireme *Soles*, erected by his heirs.

EDCS-17500216.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Valeria Nice, to Cornelius Crescens, her husband, most excellent, a Bessian by birth. She lived for 70 years, served for 26 years.
EDCS-17500216.summary: This is a tombstone for Valeria Nice, dedicated to her husband Cornelius Crescens, a Bessian by birth.

EDCS-17500218.translation: Veratio Umbro, a *manipularis* of the trireme *Athenonice*, a Cilician by birth, lived for 35 years, served for 13 years. Gaius Antonius Saturnius, heir of the trireme *Athenonice*, [made this] for the well-deserving man.
EDCS-17500218.summary: This is a tombstone for Veratio Umbro, a *manipularis* of the trireme *Athenonice*, erected by his heir.

EDCS-17500219.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Vetidius Rufus, soldier from the Praetorian fleet of Misenum, served for 16 years, [placed this] for the well-deserving man, his heir.
EDCS-17500219.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Vetidius Rufus, a soldier in the Misenum fleet, erected by his heir.

EDCS-17500220.text_edition: \n Marcus Vibius Lupus miles ex classe praetoria Misenensi natione Bessus militavit nnos XVIIII vixit annis XXXV heredes bene merenti fecerunt Lucius Cornelius Clemens ex III Iunone Marcus Maecenius Option ex III Venere Lucius Lucilius Calvenius IIItrireme Soli sic\n
EDCS-17500220.translation: Marcus Vibius Lupus, soldier from the Praetorian fleet of Misenum, a Bessian by birth, served for 19 years, lived for 35 years. Lucius Cornelius Clemens, from the III [ship] *Iuno*, Marcus Maecenius Option, from the III [ship] *Venus*, Lucius Lucilius Calvenius, from the III [ship] *Sol*, his heirs, made this for the well-deserving man.
EDCS-17500220.summary: This is a tombstone for Marcus Vibius Lupus, a Bessian soldier in the Misenum fleet, erected by his heirs.

EDCS-17500221.translation: To the divine shades. The heirs made [this] for Lucius Vibius Romulus, a veteran centurion, well-deserving.
EDCS-17500221.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Vibius Romulus, a centurion, set up by his heirs.

EDCS-17500222.translation: To the divine shades. Titus Cosconius Firmus, a trireme officer of Solis, set this up for his deserving heir for Lucius Virridius Celer, who was also called Temans, son of Delmatius, who lived 49 years and served 23 years.
EDCS-17500222.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Virridius Celer, mentioning his age, military service, and naming Titus Cosconius Firmus as his heir.

EDCS-17500225.translation: He lived 45 years, a soldier of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum. He made [this] for himself and his deserving wife. VE() postaei(!)
EDCS-17500225.summary: A funerary epitaph for a soldier of the Misenum fleet, set up by himself for himself and his wife.

EDCS-17500230.translation: For Lucius Aemilius Pertinax Acceianus, son of Lucius, Roman equestrian, born a Roman equestrian and decurion of the colony of Misenum, who lived 40 years and 3 months. Aemilia Agape and Sofia [set this up] for their dearest brother.
EDCS-17500230.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Aemilius Pertinax Acceianus, a Roman equestrian and decurion of Misenum, erected by his sisters.

EDCS-17500231.translation: To the divine shades. Marcus Antonius Ianuarius Honoratus, Augustalis of Misenum, lived 50 years and ordered this to be set up in his will. Marcus Antonius Alexander arranged for the completion for his most indulgent patron, for the freedmen and their freedwomen and children. This monument or tomb does not pass to an external heir.
EDCS-17500231.summary: A funerary monument for Marcus Antonius Ianuarius Honoratus, an Augustalis of Misenum, who left instructions in his will, with arrangement for its completion by his patron Marcus Antonius Alexander.

EDCS-17500232.translation: To the divine shades. Valeria Redempta, mother, [set this up] for Marcus Antoni Iulianus, Augustalis, immune of Misenum, also an Augustalis in Cumae, admitted to the tribunate of the Palatina tribe, who lived 25 years, 10 months, and 5 days.
EDCS-17500232.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Antoni Iulianus, an Augustalis in Misenum and Cumae, erected by his mother Valeria Redempta.

EDCS-17500233.translation: To the divine shades. Titus Aelius Aelianus [set this up] for his most dutiful mother, Valeria Redempta.
EDCS-17500233.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valeria Redempta, erected by her son Titus Aelius Aelianus.

EDCS-17500234.translation: The ordo and people of Misenum [erected this] to Titus Flavius Avitus Forensis, twice duovir, quinquennial, who had fulfilled all offices, for his son Titus Flavius Avitus, a man of distinction, patron of the colony, who also endowed the public baths in perpetuity with 400 nummi worth of durable wood for the vestibule; however, in such a way that the magistrates pass on the duty to their successors each year.
EDCS-17500234.summary: A public dedication by the town council and people of Misenum to Titus Flavius Avitus Forensis and his son, honoring the son's endowment of public baths.

EDCS-17500235.translation: To the divine shades. Caius Julius Hegemon and Cassia Urbana [set this up] for their sweetest son, Caius Julius Hegemonis, son of Caius, Claudia, admitted to the order of decurions in Misenum, who lived 5 years, 6 months, and 6 days.
EDCS-17500235.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Julius Hegemonis, a young boy admitted to the decurions' order in Misenum, erected by his parents.

EDCS-17500236.translation: To the divine shades. [Set up] for his most dutiful brother, Decimus Iunius Certus, son of Decimus, Claudia, priest and public haruspex, created from a priestly family.
EDCS-17500236.summary: A funerary epitaph for Decimus Iunius Certus, a priest and haruspex, erected by his brother.

EDCS-17500237.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Decimus Iunius Certus, son of Decimus, public priest and haruspex, from his most devoted grandsons.
EDCS-17500237.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a priest and haruspex, dedicated by his grandsons, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17500244.translation: For Polla and Tertulla, daughters of Gnaeus Luccius, with their own money.
EDCS-17500244.summary: This is a dedication made with private funds by two women, Polla and Tertulla, daughters of Gnaeus Luccius, likely to a deity.

EDCS-17500245.translation: For Maxima, daughter of Gnaeus Luccius, with her own money.
EDCS-17500245.summary: This is a dedication made with private funds by Maxima, daughter of Gnaeus Luccius, likely to a deity.

EDCS-17500249.translation: Sacred to Venus Proba Sanctissima. Hail, Tiberius Claudius Marcion, hail, brilliant one of a thousand souls, opus, hail, bearer of a beautiful burden, an unsurpassable gift, mistress of human and divine things, nourisher, protector, lover, sacrificer, hail, brilliant one of a thousand souls, opus, hail.
EDCS-17500249.summary: This is a dedication to Venus Proba Sanctissima, likely by Tiberius Claudius Marcion, praising her divine attributes and gifts.

EDCS-17500250.translation: Publius Avius Hedus gave this offering.
EDCS-17500250.summary: This is a simple dedication of a gift by Publius Avius Hedus.

EDCS-17500252.translation: During the reign of Emperor Caesar Antoninus Augustus, with Gaius Pomponius Xystus overseeing.
EDCS-17500252.summary: This inscription records a project overseen by Gaius Pomponius Xystus during the reign of Emperor Antoninus Augustus.

EDCS-17500253.translation: During the reign of Emperor Caesar Verus Augustus, with Gaius Pomponius Xystus overseeing.
EDCS-17500253.summary: This inscription records a project overseen by Gaius Pomponius Xystus during the reign of Emperor Verus Augustus.

EDCS-17500255.translation: Quintus Caecilius, Marcus Bennius S[---]. On the sixth day before the Ides of August, in the [---]. Because Gnaeus Luccius Fillus, while alive, made [---]. What it pleases to be done about it [---]. With Luccia, daughter of Gnaeus, whether in character [---]. Of the order and of the municipium [---]. His parent [---]. Added indeed [---]. Desiderius [---]. The end [---].
EDCS-17500255.summary: This fragmented inscription seems to concern a legal or familial matter involving the Luccius family, possibly a funerary monument or a dedication made during the lifetime of Gnaeus Luccius Fillus.

EDCS-17500256.translation: In the consulship of Marcus Magrius Bassus and Lucius Ragonius Quintianus, on the Kalends of June. In Cumae, in the temple of the deified Vespasian, in the order of decurions, which Marcus Mallonius Undanus and Quintus Claudius Acilianus, as praetors, had convened by drawing lots, Caelius Pannycus, Curtius Votivos, Considius Felicianus were present, as the praetors reported concerning the appointment of a priest of the Mother of the Gods of Baiae in place of the deceased priest Restitutus, it was decided by all that Licinius Secundus should be priest. To the XVviri sacris faciundis, praetors, and magistrates of Cumae, greetings. Since we learned from your letter that you appointed Licinius Secundus priest in place of the deceased Claudius Restitutus, according to your will, we have permitted him to perform his duties with a crown, only within the boundaries of your colony. As we wish you well, Pontius Gavius Maximus, as pro-magister, subscribed on the 16th day before the Kalends of September, in the consulship of Marcus Umbrius Primus and Titus Flavius Coelianus.
EDCS-17500256.summary: This inscription records the appointment of Licinius Secundus as a priest of the Mother of the Gods in Cumae, following a senatorial decree and with the approval of local magistrates, likely during the late Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-17500257.translation: By decree of the Senate, dendrophori were created, who are under the care of the XVviri sacris faciundis, clarissimi viri, patrons. Lucius Ampius Stephanus, quinquennial priest of the Mother of the Gods, the dendrophorus, gave bread, wine, and gifts for the dedication. // Gaius Valerius Picentinus, Gaius Iulius Herculanus, Longinius Iustinus, Aulus Firmius Polybius, Gaius Lisius Crescentinus, Lucius Decimius Felinus, Gaius Cupiennius Primitivus, Titus Minicius Sabinus, Marcus Iunnius Agrippinus, Aulus Camelius Protocensis, Aulus Agnanius Felicissimus, Gaius Litrius Fortunatus, Tiberius Iulius Callinicus, Quintus Curtius Scemanus, Lucius Oppius Lesiginus, Marcus Herennius Zerax, Gaius Lisius Pudentinus, Aulus Firmius Felicianus, Marcus Babbius Sodalis, Lucius Modestius Hilarus, Lucius Orfius Maximinus, Gaius Iulius Gauditurus, Lucius Lollius Viator, Gaius Iulius Cogitatus, Gaius Iulius Cerialis, Gaius Herennius Sabinus, Lucius Orfius Maximus, Numerius Pollius Primus Senior, Gaius Litrius Maior, Lucius Decimius Faustus, Gaius Iulius Severus, Gaius Nautius Spyntropus, Numerius Vibius Speratus, Lucius Pacius Maximinus, Quintus Granius Gemellus, Marcus Granius Marcianus, Quintus Servius Nicetianus, Gaius Lisius Secundinus, Gaius Publilius Genialis, Lucius Connius Castrensis, Quintus Granius Corint(h)us, Tiberius Iulius Atainopo, Quintus Granius Ianuarius, Gaius Turranius Priscus. // Lucius Plautius Victor, Aulus Firmius Severus, Gaius Fullonius Tertius, Titus Flavius Archilaus, Marcus Valerius Syntropus, Marcus Valerius Ianuarius, Numerius Lucius Cyricius, Gaius Iulius Carito, Marcus Curius Nianus, Gaius Martius Vitalis, Aerelius Lucius, Gaius Iulius Dianensis, Gaius Antonius Lucilianus, Gaius Magius Crescentianus, Gaius Cartilius Irenicus, Numerius Pollius Primus Junior, Gaius Titilius Privatus, Lucius Marcius Maruleius, Quintus Granius Gemellus, Gaius Clodius Mercurius, Numerius Vibius Super, Gaius Tuscenius Communio, Marcus Stennius Marcellinus, Marcus Valerius Eutyches, Gaius Rufus Seleucus, Marcus Mallonius Severianus, Lucius Gentius Nico, Lucius Pedanius Faustinus, Naevius Pollius Priscus, Iulius Decius Felicius, Marcus Sagarius Sedatus, Gaius Tuscentius Primitius, Marcus Samiantus Crescens, Publius Carsicius Florianus, Gaius Statrius Felicissimus, Titus Minicius Veratinus, Marcus Plautius Hilarus, Marcus Samilaris Fortunius, Gaius Iunius Mercurius, Gaius Iulius Crescens, Gaius Aurunculeius [---], Lucius Flavius Celer, Samiarius Silvanus. // Dedicated on the 7th day before the Ides of October, in the third consulship and once more consul.
EDCS-17500257.summary: This inscription records the creation of dendrophori (tree-bearers), religious officials under the supervision of the XVviri, and lists numerous individuals involved, along with details of a dedication on October 9th during a specific consular year.

EDCS-17500259.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gaius Avianus Epagathus, freedman of Augustus, from Cumae.
EDCS-17500259.summary: A funerary dedication for Gaius Avianus Epagathus, a freedman of Augustus, from Cumae.

EDCS-17500261.translation: A monument for Sextia Kana, daughter of Lucius, publicly erected by decree of the decurions of the colony of Iulium, because she was generous to the colony.
EDCS-17500261.summary: A public monument for Sextia Kana, funded by the town council, in recognition of her generosity to the colony of Iulium.

EDCS-17500262.translation: To Aulus Veratius Severianus, son of Aulus, of the Palatina tribe, a Roman knight, curator of the public affairs of Tegianum, admitted to the order of decurions, a most loving citizen, who, although protected by the privilege of the priesthood of Caeninum, could have easily been excused from offices and duties, but preferring love of country, laudably administered the office of aedile and celebrated a most joyous day, the 11th of January, the birthday of our city god, with a passive hunt of ten beasts, four toothed wild beasts, and four pairs fighting with swords, and other most honorable preparations, generously provided them. He also willingly undertook the office of duumvir to increase his patriotic contributions. To him, although the people continuously requested that a pair of chariots be erected in his honor at the games, and the distinguished order had rightly decreed it, he, content with the honor of one pair of chariots due to his innate modesty, remitted the expenses for the other pair to the public treasury. The site was granted by decree of the decurions, by order of the colonists.
EDCS-17500262.summary: An epitaph for Aulus Veratius Severianus, a Roman knight and local official, detailing his generous public service, particularly his supervision of games and his voluntary contributions to the colony of Tegianum.

EDCS-17500263.translation: Verrius Montanus, son of Marcus, priest.
EDCS-17500263.summary: An incomplete inscription identifying Verrius Montanus, son of Marcus, as a priest.

EDCS-17500264.translation: To the departed spirits. For Marcus Ulpius Securus, who capably served in all offices twice. From Cumae. Lucius Caesius Ampliatus, heir, made this.
EDCS-17500264.summary: A funerary dedication from Cumae for Marcus Ulpius Securus, who held all public offices twice, with the heir Lucius Caesius Ampliatus as dedicator.

EDCS-17500265.summary: An incomplete inscription identifying Lucius Cocceius as a contractor.

EDCS-17500268.translation: Primius, public servant, lived 9 years and 3 months.
EDCS-17500268.summary: A tombstone for Primius, who died at the age of nine years and three months, identified as a public servant.

EDCS-17500271.summary: An inscription presumably indicating ownership or dedication related to Marcus Benni Rufus.

EDCS-17500273.translation: To the departed spirits. For Quintus Saenius Spanus, a veteran, who lived 65 years and served 28 years in the army. Saenia Tyche made this for her well-deserving patron, and for his freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-17500273.summary: A funerary monument for Quintus Saenius Spanus, a veteran of 28 years service, erected by Saenia Tyche for him and his freedmen and their descendants.

EDCS-17500275.translation: This monument, 12 feet in front and 16 feet in the ground from the base and the dressed stone, Aelia Isigenia made for her most beloved husband Tiberius Claudius Zenati, and for herself, her freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-17500275.summary: A funerary monument for Tiberius Claudius Zenati, erected by his wife Aelia Isigenia, also for herself and their freedmen and descendants, with dimensions specified.

EDCS-17500276.translation: The bones of Avidia Faustus, freedwoman of Marcus and of the woman, are here; her husband Quintus Hortensius Alexander, freedman of Quintus, made this.
EDCS-17500276.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Avidia Faustus, set up by her husband Quintus Hortensius Alexander.

EDCS-17500280.translation: To Lucius Cestius Gallus Cerrenius Justus Lutatius Natalis, one of the four men in charge of roads, as military tribune with broad stripe of the 8th Augustan legion, urban quaestor, official for the senate records, curule aedile, praetor, as governor of the Augusti of the 20th Valeria Victrix legion, proconsul of the province Narbonensis, prefect of the treasury of Saturn, twice consul, patron of the colony, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-17500280.summary: This is a dedication for Lucius Cestius Gallus Cerrenius Justus, detailing his extensive political and military career, likely set up by the colony he patronized.

EDCS-17500281.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Tiberi filio Palatina MePrisco Rufino iuniori clarissimo viro tribuno militum legionis VII Claudiae allecto inter quaestorios praetori urbano legato provinciae Narbonensis legato provinciae Cretae praefecto Miniciae proconsuli provinciae Achaiae legato provinciae Africae patronus coloniae locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-17500281.translation: To Tiberius Claudius MePriscus Rufinus, junior, most brilliant man, son of Tiberius of the Palatine tribe, military tribune of the 7th Claudian legion, selected among the quaestorians, urban praetor, governor of the province Narbonensis, governor of the province Crete, prefect of the Minicia, proconsul of the province Achaia, governor of the province Africa, patron of the colony, his place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-17500281.summary: This is a dedication to Tiberius Claudius MePriscus Rufinus, outlining his distinguished career in military and provincial administration, with his honorary place in the colony granted by decree.

EDCS-17500282.translation: To Quintus Pompeius Senecio, son of Quintus of the Quirina tribe, Sosis Priscus, consul, praetor, pontifex, Hadrianic sodalis, Antonine sodalis, collino salian priest, quaestor, augur, moneyer triumvir for striking bronze, silver, and gold, governor of the province Asia, prefect of the city for the holidays, patron of the colony, publicly by decree of the decurions, whose honor contented him, he placed this at his own expense.
EDCS-17500282.summary: This is a dedication to Quintus Pompeius Senecio, listing his numerous high-ranking offices and priestly roles, who funded the monument himself to honor his patronage of the colony.

EDCS-17500283.translation: To Publius Aelius Archelaus, son of Publius of the Aelian tribe, Marcus, son of Marcus, a man of most distinguished origin, whose praiseworthy constancy in his age and recent dignity of patronage by foresight increased the strength of the state, Plutius Maximus, having completed his legation three times, [honors him] as patron for his merits.
EDCS-17500283.summary: This is an honorary inscription for Publius Aelius Archelaus, praising his character and patronage, and noting his merits recognized by Plutius Maximus.

EDCS-17500284.translation: Marcus Arrius, son of Marcus, and Marcus Sextius, son of Marcus, duoviri, by the decision of the council of scribes, saw to the making and repairing of the road.
EDCS-17500284.summary: This inscription records that Marcus Arrius and Marcus Sextius, serving as duoviri, were responsible for the construction and maintenance of a road, as ordered by the town council.

EDCS-17500285.translation: Publius Maenius, son of Publius, and [---]binius, son of Quintus, [---], duoviri, by the decision of the council of scribes, saw to the making of roads.
EDCS-17500285.summary: This inscription indicates that two duoviri, Publius Maenius and another unidentified individual, were responsible for road construction as decreed by the council.

EDCS-17500286.translation: Dedicated. During the consulship of Emperor Probus Augustus for the fourth time and Tiberianus, in the time of the IIIIviri, by Servilius Valerius Tertullinus junior and Petronius Satti, on the Nones of May.
EDCS-17500286.summary: This is a dedication of a monument, possibly public, dating to the consulship of Emperor Probus and Tiberianus, and specifying the officials who oversaw its dedication.

EDCS-17500287.translation: Anicia (mother). Publius Anicius Eros, freedman of Publius, a tiler. Anicia Creste, freedwoman of Publius. Domitia Fausta Aniciana, freedwoman of Gnaeus. Gnaeus Domitius Hermia, freedman of Gnaeus.
EDCS-17500287.summary: This is a list of names, likely a family or household, including a tiler and freedmen, possibly associated with a funerary monument or property.

EDCS-17500288.translation: To the Shades Below. To Sentia Pompeiana, daughter of Publius, who lived 32 years, 1 month, and 8 days. Lucius Licinius Liberianus (set this up) for his most pious and most chaste wife.
EDCS-17500288.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sentia Pompeiana, set up by her husband Lucius Licinius Liberianus, recording her lifespan.

EDCS-17500293.translation: To Gaius Caelio Censorino, a most distinguished man, candidate for praetor, consul, curator of the Via Latina, curator of Region VII, curator of splendid Carthage, count of our lord Constantine the Great Augustus, and collector of gold and silver of the three provinces, consular governor of Sicily, consular governor of Campania, who improved his city and reformed it, this place was granted by decree of the Senate.
EDCS-17500293.summary: This is a senatorial decree granting a public place to honor Gaius Caelio Censorinus for his distinguished public service in various high offices and provinces, likely dating to the Constantinian period.

EDCS-17500297.translation: Gnaeus Cele, son of Gnaeus, of the Tromentina tribe, lived 21 years, having been elected to the senate of Atella. [He held the office of] the fourth decurion (or similar office) and prefect. Monnia Rufa, his mother, [and] Gnaeus Faustus, freedman of Gnaeus Monnius.
EDCS-17500297.summary: This epitaph commemorates Gnaeus Cele, who died at age 21 and had been elected to the senate and served as prefect in Atella, set up by his mother and freedman.

EDCS-17500298.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Publius Terentius Nicephor [erected this] to his most excellent patron, Publius Terentius Felix, scribe and soldier.
EDCS-17500298.summary: This tombstone is dedicated to Publius Terentius Felix, a scribe and soldier, by his patron Publius Terentius Nicephor.

EDCS-17500299.translation: The bones of Ampelius, freedman, are placed here to his patron, Lucius Naevius Antiochus, a glassmaker.
EDCS-17500299.summary: This epitaph states that the bones of Ampelius, a freedman, are buried here, honoring his patron Lucius Naevius Antiochus, a glassmaker.

EDCS-17500300.translation: Hail! The bones of Arria Agathea, freedwoman, are placed here.
EDCS-17500300.summary: This inscription marks the burial place of Arria Agathea, a freedwoman.

EDCS-17500301.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Athenocora, who lived 21 years. Gaius Crassius Cossinius [erected this] to his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-17500301.summary: This tombstone is dedicated to Athenocora, who died at age 21, by her husband Gaius Crassius Cossinius.

EDCS-17500303.translation: The bones of Gaius Avianus Liccaeus are placed here. His mother made [this] with pious [care].
EDCS-17500303.summary: This epitaph states that the bones of Gaius Avianus Liccaeus are buried here, and the monument was erected by his mother.

EDCS-17600191.translation: Liberalis, to Matidia Augusta, from the procurator.
EDCS-17600191.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Matidia Augusta, likely by Liberalis, who served as a procurator.

EDCS-17600200.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely a funerary dedication, identifying Phadespotus, son of A. M.

EDCS-17700001.translation: Sacred to Isis Augusta. Gaius Novius Priscus, son of Gaius, [erected this] from the ground and with his own money.
EDCS-17700001.summary: This inscription records a sacred dedication to the goddess Isis Augusta, funded and erected by Gaius Novius Priscus.

EDCS-17700002.translation: The bones of Lucius Caestius, freedman of Lucius, are placed here.
EDCS-17700002.summary: An epitaph recording the burial place of Lucius Caestius, a freedman, likely from the late Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-17700003.translation: The bones of Cossutia Amata, freedwoman of Aulus, are placed here.
EDCS-17700003.summary: An epitaph recording the burial place of Cossutia Amata, a freedwoman, likely from the late Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-17700005.translation: To the divine shades. Eutychus to Cleopatra, his wife, well-deserving, who lived 24 years.
EDCS-17700005.summary: A funerary dedication by Eutychus to his wife Cleopatra, who died at age 24, dating likely to the 1st-2nd century CE.

EDCS-17700006.translation: To the divine shades. Felix lived 41 years, 10 months.
EDCS-17700006.summary: A funerary inscription for Felix, recording his age at death as 41 years and 10 months, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-17700007.translation: The bones of Quintus Hostius Eros, freedman of Quintus, are placed here; the patron made it.
EDCS-17700007.summary: An epitaph recording the burial place of Quintus Hostius Eros, a freedman, with the monument erected by his patron, likely from the late Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-17700008.translation: [Petronius Flaccus, son of Publius, of the Falerna tribe] according to the will, 10,000 sesterces to his son Secundus, son of Gnaeus.
EDCS-17700008.summary: A funerary bequest from the will of Petronius Flaccus, dedicating 10,000 sesterces to his son Secundus, likely from the 1st-2nd century CE.

EDCS-17700009.translation: Aulus Plautius Euhodus for himself and his children, for Aulus Plautius Daphnus and Plautia Primigenia and Plautia Laurilla and Plautia Festa and Plautia Successa and Aulus Plautius Asbestus, his freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants, and those who will be called Plauti; the Spurianus district with its properties and the apartment which is joined to this monument with its walls and foundations will belong to this monument; if any of those listed above wish to sell this monument or the Spurianus district or the apartment adjoining this monument, then it will belong to the public property of the colony of Puteoli.
EDCS-17700009.summary: A complex property endowment by Aulus Plautius Euhodus, establishing inheritance rights for his family, freedmen, and their descendants, with stipulations for public acquisition by the colony of Puteoli if sold, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17700010.translation: Sacred to the divine shades. To Priscus and Iusta, sons of Iustus. Agrippinus and Herennius, brothers, to their most dutiful brothers.
EDCS-17700010.summary: A funerary dedication by brothers Agrippinus and Herennius to their brothers Priscus and Iusta, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-17700011.translation: Caius Statius, freedman of Caius, made this for Pupia Salvia, freedwoman of Caius, his sister. The bones are placed here.
EDCS-17700011.summary: An epitaph recording the burial place of Pupia Salvia, a freedwoman, with the monument erected by her brother Caius Statius, also a freedman, likely from the late Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-17700012.translation: Titus Vettius, son of Titus, of the Quirina tribe. Lucius Marcius Dantius. Lucius Vettius, son of Titus, of the Falerna tribe. Saevia, daughter of Caius.
EDCS-17700012.summary: A funerary monument listing several individuals, possibly family members, with their names, patronymics, tribes, and possibly abbreviated cognomina or praenomina, likely from the 1st-2nd century CE.

EDCS-17700013.summary: This inscription records a bequest from Ubonius made in his will.

EDCS-17700016.translation: This temple, consecrated to the heroes, which bears the fortunate name of Augustus, may it remain for his descendants and may it rejoice in its growth. For when Caesar, your father, calls you to the gods in heaven, and you will take your place where you will rule the world, may your heirs who command and govern us on this earth with their fortunate vows. Lucius Aurelius Rufus, son of Lucius, of the Palatina tribe, a primipilaris soldier of the [Legion?] XVI, [erected a statue?] to the Emperor Caesar.
EDCS-17700016.summary: This inscription dedicates a temple to the heroes and honors the Emperor Caesar, with an elaborate preamble and a dedication by Lucius Aurelius Rufus, likely a high-ranking military officer.

EDCS-17700018.translation: To Heuresis, for Gnaeus Stennius Egnatius Primus, son of Gnaeus Stennius Egnatius Rufus, of the Falerna tribe, duovir for the second time, quinquennial duovir, who fulfilled all duties and honors, public priest of the goddesses Isis and Serapis, curator of public works, honorable freedman, and the Augustales, to their most worthy patron, for his infinite merits, by whose dedication he gave to each and all of them one hundred sesterces, and on that day he provided a full display of games; the site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-17700018.summary: This honorary inscription is dedicated to Gnaeus Stennius Egnatius Primus, recognizing his extensive public service, priesthoods, and generosity towards the community and its members, likely in a town in Italy.

EDCS-17700022.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. For Lucius Cocceius Marcellinus, censor for the second time, under the care of Crescentius of Suessula.
EDCS-17700022.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Cocceius Marcellinus, who served as censor twice, with the inscription arranged by Crescentius of Suessula.

EDCS-17700023.translation: To Lucius Pompeius Felicissimus, immunis dendrophorus of Suessula, and priest of the Mother of the Gods, XVvir, in the Novanensis street, for his father Lucius Pompeius Felicissimus, decurion, duovir, quaestor of supplies, and one who fulfilled all duties and offices. The cultores of Jupiter Optimus Maximus of Suessula, the Hortensenses, [erected this] to their patron who deserved well, for his singular liberality and excellence towards them; the site was granted by decree.
EDCS-17700023.summary: This dedication is from the cultores of Jupiter Optimus Maximus of Suessula and the Hortensenses to their patron Lucius Pompeius Felicissimus, recognizing his extensive public service and generosity.

EDCS-17700024.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. For Lucius Pompeius Verecundus, son of Spurius, duovir of Suessula. Lucius Pompeius Verecundus [erected this for his father].
EDCS-17700024.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Pompeius Verecundus, son of Spurius, who was a duovir of Suessula, with a likely addition by his son of the same name.

EDCS-17700025.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. For Abulene...
EDCS-17700025.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription, likely for an individual named Abulene.

EDCS-17700026.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. For Claudia Nymphia, daughter of Tiberius, who lived 32 years and [3] months. Gavia Felicitissima [erected this] for her most sweet daughter.
EDCS-17700026.summary: This funerary inscription is for Claudia Nymphia, who died at the age of 32 years and some months, and was erected by her mother, Gavia Felicitissima.

EDCS-17700027.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. For Culgius Candidianus, to his well-deserving wife, who lived with me for 14 years and bore [children] for 27 years and 9 months. Tiberius Claudius Felicissimus, her husband.
EDCS-17700027.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Tiberius Claudius Felicissimus to his wife, Culgius Candidiana, recording their marriage duration and the number of years she bore children.

EDCS-17700028.translation: To Publius Rutedius Philargyrus, freedman of [a woman].
EDCS-17700028.summary: This inscription records the name of Publius Rutedius Philargyrus, identified as a freedman of a woman.

EDCS-17700029.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Severia Zosime made this for her well-deserving husband, Marcus Severus Fuscus.
EDCS-17700029.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wife for her husband, dating likely to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-17700030.translation: Lucius Valerius Ilarcus [---]. For Valeria Ilarca, his freedwoman, who [---] of the accensi [---].
EDCS-17700030.summary: This is a fragmented funerary epitaph for a freedwoman named Valeria Ilarca, possibly set up by her former master or patron, Lucius Valerius Ilarcus.

EDCS-17700049.translation: During the consulship of Emperor Caesar and Titus Statilius. The attendants arranged for this to be made. Lucius Popillius, son of Spurius. Lucius Popillius Hilarius. Quintus Staedius Protus. Epicadus, slave of Lucius Popillius. Musicus, slave of Lucius Popillius. Felix, slave of Lucius Popillius. Glaucia, slave-woman of Lucius Popillius. Sosus, slave of Lucius Popillius. Eros the craftsman. Eros, son of Ingenuus. Atticus, slave of Lucius Popillius.
EDCS-17700049.summary: This inscription lists several individuals, likely freedmen and slaves, who were involved in the construction or dedication of a public work during the consulship of Emperor Caesar and Titus Statilius.

EDCS-17700052.translation: To the god Scholarius. Sextus Firmius Charito, with his patron's favor, has restored with bronze railings, dedicating this gift according to his vow to his divinity and majesty.
EDCS-17700052.summary: This is a votive dedication to the god Scholarius by Sextus Firmius Charito, who restored a sanctuary with bronze railings.

EDCS-17700055.translation: Latona, renowned virgin of the Tifata mountains, you have set up these miraculous offerings in your temples. Delmatius Laetus, known to all as a woodsman and protector of life by his immense praise, I believe indeed this gift, having no precedent in any hills or woods, unfolds its chief glory in the shadows. [---] which true [---].
EDCS-17700055.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Latona, likely by a local figure named Delmatius Laetus, celebrating her divine power and connection to nature.

EDCS-17700059.translation: To you, who are all things, goddess Isis. Arrius Balbinus, a man of consular rank, [dedicated this].
EDCS-17700059.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Isis by Arrius Balbinus, a prominent individual, indicating the presence of Isis worship in the region.

EDCS-17700623.translation: In Anio. Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the deified [Caesar], by decree of the Senate, 1200 Roman feet, 240 [feet].
EDCS-17700623.summary: This inscription records a land measurement of 1200 Roman feet and 240 feet, authorized by Emperor Augustus in the territory of Anio.

EDCS-17700624.translation: In Anio. Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the deified [Caesar], by decree of the Senate, 4932 [feet], 240 [feet].
EDCS-17700624.summary: This inscription records a land measurement of 4932 Roman feet and 240 feet, authorized by Emperor Augustus in the territory of Anio.

EDCS-17800147.translation: In Marcia. Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the deified [Caesar], by decree of the Senate, 1045 Roman feet, 240 [feet].
EDCS-17800147.summary: This inscription records a land measurement of 1045 Roman feet and 240 feet, authorized by Emperor Augustus in the territory of Marcia.

EDCS-17800150.translation: In Marcia. Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the deified [Caesar], by decree of the Senate, 1197 Roman feet, 240 feet.
EDCS-17800150.summary: This inscription records a land measurement of 1197 Roman feet and 240 feet, authorized by Emperor Augustus in the territory of Marcia.

EDCS-17800416.translation: During the consulship of Tiberius Claudius Nero and Publius Quinctilius Varus, and with Lucius Antistius, son of Lucius Campanus, and Tiberius Pomponius Quintus Bivellius as quaestors, Lucius Cocceius Papa, freedman of Caius Lucius Marcus, built a shrine to Jupiter and the household gods by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-17800416.summary: This inscription records the construction of a shrine to Jupiter and household gods by a freedman under the supervision of local magistrates in the consulship of Nero and Varus.

EDCS-17800417.translation: During the consulship of Publius Sulpicius Quirinius and Gaius Valgius, with Sextus Pontidius Bassus and Marcus Junius Celer as quaestors, and Sextus Helvius, son of Caius Publius, and Titus Falernus as aediles, Publius Rammius Chrestus, freedman of Publius, navigator, dedicated [this] to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-17800417.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus made by Publius Rammius Chrestus, a navigator, during the consulship of Quirinius and Valgius.

EDCS-17800418.translation: Maecius Probus, a man of consular rank and prefect of the grain supply, fulfilled his vow for being safe from a dangerous peril in this place and for recovering good health.
EDCS-17800418.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Maecius Probus, a high-ranking official, for surviving a dangerous situation and regaining health.

EDCS-17800423.text_edition: \n Lucius Viratius Ampliatus archigallus Matri Deum\n
EDCS-17800423.translation: Lucius Viratius Ampliatus, chief gallus (priest of Cybele), to the Mother of the Gods.
EDCS-17800423.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Lucius Viratius Ampliatus, a priest of Cybele, to the Mother of the Gods.

EDCS-17800425.translation: To the queen Nemesis and the associated gods. Arrianus dedicated this altar. To Justice Nemesis and the Fates, Cammarius set up this altar which he had vowed, revering the holy divinities.
EDCS-17800425.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an altar to Nemesis and associated deities by Arrianus and Cammarius.

EDCS-17800434.translation: To the Genius of the theater. Lucceius Peculiaris, contractor, made [this] from plans.
EDCS-17800434.summary: This inscription records the construction of something related to the theater by Lucceius Peculiaris, a contractor.

EDCS-17800435.translation: Servius Suettius Cimber, freedman of Servius, willingly fulfilled his vow, sacred to Mercury. Lucius Tampius, quaestor, and Lucius Acerratius, aedile, provided the place.
EDCS-17800435.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to Mercury by Servius Suettius Cimber and the provision of space by local magistrates.

EDCS-17800441.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus consul VIII fines locorum dicatorum Dianae Tifatinae a Cornelio Sulla ex forma divi Augusti restituit\n
EDCS-17800441.translation: Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, in his eighth consulship, restored the boundaries of the places dedicated to Diana Tifatina, according to a decree of Cornelius Sulla and the plan of the deified Augustus.
EDCS-17800441.summary: This inscription records Emperor Vespasian restoring boundary markers for lands dedicated to Diana Tifatina.

EDCS-17800447.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus, conqueror of Germany and Sarmatia, brother of the deified Commodus, nephew of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, descendant of the deified Nerva, holding tribunician power for the fourth time, acclaimed imperator for the eighth time, consul for the second time, proconsul, father of the country, pontifex maximus, by the colony of Capua.
EDCS-17800447.summary: This is a lengthy titulature of Emperor Septimius Severus, likely part of a dedication or honorary inscription from the colony of Capua.

EDCS-17900078.translation: Publius Pacilius Zenon Laetus, son of Publius, first centurion twice over, quaestor, praefect for dispensing justice and performing sacred rites, rebuilt and enclosed the temple of Fortuna and Victoria with his own money.
EDCS-17900078.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding and enclosure of a temple to Fortuna and Victoria by Publius Pacilius Zenon Laetus, a veteran military officer and local official.

EDCS-17900560.translation: To Gaius Clodius Adiutor, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, former praetor, tribune of the plebs, quaestor, who from an income of 100,000 sesterces, bequeathed by Clodius, provides for the upkeep of the road of the community of the Campanians.
EDCS-17900560.summary: This inscription records a bequest from Clodius to the community of the Campanians, designated for road maintenance, and honors Gaius Clodius Adiutor, who administered these funds.

EDCS-17900563.translation: To Lucius Cornelius Balbus, son of Lucius, consul, patron, by decree of the senators.
EDCS-17900563.summary: This is a dedication to Lucius Cornelius Balbus, who held the consulship and was a patron, authorized by a senatorial decree.

EDCS-17900564.translation: Gavia Procula, daughter of Claudius, a most loving and incomparable mother, placed [this] for Publius Flavius Menander Africanus, a most distinguished youth, a commissioner for the upkeep of roads, her dearest son.
EDCS-17900564.summary: This epitaph commemorates Publius Flavius Menander Africanus, a young official responsible for roads, erected by his grieving mother.

EDCS-17900568.translation: To Gaius Numitorius Callistratus, an excellent, most learned, and most rare man, by Flavius Aristus, a most distinguished man.
EDCS-17900568.summary: This is a dedication to Gaius Numitorius Callistratus, an exceptional individual, by Flavius Aristus.

EDCS-17900572.translation: To Marcus Valerius Homullus, consul, patron of the colony, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-17900572.summary: This inscription honors Marcus Valerius Homullus, a consul and patron of the colony, by decision of the local council.

EDCS-17900573.translation: To Varia Photine, his most sweet daughter, from her most unhappy father Photinus Ambibulus, freedman of a consul, and to Varia Elpis, his most chaste and most holy wife.
EDCS-17900573.summary: This epitaph is dedicated by Photinus, a freedman of a consul, to his deceased daughter Varia Photine and his wife Varia Elpis.

EDCS-17900574.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To Marcus Veserius Iucundianus, son of Marcus, of the Palatina tribe, chief of craftsmen, elevated censor, procurator for the food supply of the Flaminian Way, and designated duumvir. By his mother, Sacria Iucunda.
EDCS-17900574.summary: This funerary inscription honors Marcus Veserius Iucundianus, who held various administrative and military posts, erected by his mother.

EDCS-17900578.translation: Virius Vibius, of consular rank in Campania, restored [this].
EDCS-17900578.summary: This inscription records the restoration of something by Virius Vibius, a former consular governor of Campania.

EDCS-17900579.text_edition: \n Lucio Vitrasio Luci filio Poblilia Flaminino consuli proconsuli provinciae Africae legato pro praetore Italiae Transpadanae et provinciae Moesiae superioris et exercitus et provinciae Dalmatiae curatori alvei Tiberis et riparum et cloacarum urbis Lucius Vitrasius Ennius Aequus filius patri optimo\n
EDCS-17900579.translation: Lucius Vitrasius Ennius Aequus, son, to his most excellent father Lucius Vitrasius Flamininus, son of Lucius, of the Poblilia tribe, consul, proconsul of the province of Africa, legate with praetorian power of Italia Transpadana and the province of Moesia Superior and its army and the province of Dalmatia, curator of the Tiber River's channel, banks, and sewers.
EDCS-17900579.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Lucius Vitrasius Flamininus, a high-ranking official who served as consul and in various proconsular, legatine, and curatorial roles, by his son.

EDCS-17900584.translation: Barnaeus, of the associations of the twenty-percent tax on freedmen, made [this] for himself and his brothers while alive. // Salama, of the associations of the twenty-percent tax on freedmen, a slave, lived 25 years. // Sabbioni, of the associations of the twenty-percent tax on freedmen, a slave.
EDCS-17900584.summary: This inscription contains three separate funerary notices for Barnaeus, Salama, and Sabbioni, all associated with a tax collection guild, with Salama's age noted.

EDCS-17900585.translation: To the departed, from the instruments; she lived 33 years. Cluvia Dionysia to her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-17900585.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a woman named Cluvia Dionysia, who lived 33 years, placed by her husband.

EDCS-17900586.translation: Gaius Papius Salvius, freedman of Gaius. Gaius Papius Apelles, freedman of Gaius, accensus of the consul Publius Silius. The patron placed [this] for his deserving freedwoman, Papia Cleopatra.
EDCS-17900586.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument set up by a patron (likely a freedman himself) for his deserving freedwoman, Papia Cleopatra, mentioning another freedman, Gaius Papius Salvius.

EDCS-17900588.translation: To the departed gods. Lucius Varius Onesimus, decurialis, to his freedman Glycon.
EDCS-17900588.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Lucius Varius Onesimus, a decurialis, to his freedman Glycon.

EDCS-17900589.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Of Caius Aecius Similis, beneficiary of a tribune, optio of the cohort V of vigiles, soldier of cohort XI urbana, in the century of Bruttius. [---]
EDCS-17900589.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caius Aecius Similis, a soldier with various roles in different cohorts, with lacunae indicating missing information.

EDCS-17900590.translation: To Paulus Aemilius, son of Decimus, first-pike twice, prefect of cavalry, tribune of cohort IV praetoriae. Senecio, freedman.
EDCS-17900590.summary: This is an honorary inscription for Paulus Aemilius, detailing his military career, likely erected by his freedman Senecio.

EDCS-17900591.translation: In memory of Aurelius Dalmatianus, soldier, duplicius of the praetorian fleet of Misenum, who lived 38 years, 7 months, and 20 days. Castricia Prima to her husband, with whom she lived 20 years, made [this] for his deserving.
EDCS-17900591.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelius Dalmatianus, a soldier in the Misenum fleet, erected by his wife Castricia Prima after 20 years of marriage.

EDCS-17900592.translation: To Aulus Baebius, son of Aulus, [---] of legion V Macedonica, [---] armillae, [---] 45 years, Hilara, his freedwoman, [---] from herself [---] to Aulus Baebius, son of Aulus, freedman, M[---]
EDCS-17900592.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription for Aulus Baebius, a soldier in the V Macedonica legion, and his freedman, likely erected by the freedwoman Hilara.

EDCS-17900593.translation: Marcus Britius Philaros, freedman of Marcus, his father, made [this]. Britia Lais, freedwoman of Marcus, mother, lives. The bones of Marcus Britius, son of Spurius, soldier of legion VII Gemina, are placed here.
EDCS-17900593.summary: This funerary monument records Marcus Britius Philaros, who erected it for himself and his parents, and also marks the burial place of his son, Marcus Britius, a soldier in the VII Gemina legion.

EDCS-17900594.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. To Calpurnius Stabilis, soldier of cohort X praetoriae, century of Verus. Calpurnius Stabilis [---] to the well-deserving.
EDCS-17900594.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Calpurnius Stabilis, a soldier in the X praetorian cohort, likely erected by another Calpurnius Stabilis.

EDCS-17900595.translation: Gaius Canuleius, son of Quintus, of legion VII evocatus, died aged 35, having been presented with torques, armillae, and a phalera (and?) a crown. Quintus Canuleius, son of Quintus, of legion VII, killed in Gaul, aged 18. Their father made this monument for the two brothers.
EDCS-17900595.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a father for his two sons, Gaius and Quintus Canuleius, both soldiers in the VII legion, one dying in service and the other killed in Gaul.

EDCS-17900596.translation: Publius Felsinius, son of Lucius, from the Falernian tribe, and Quintus Felsinius, son of Lucius, from the Falernian tribe, standard-bearer of the 10th legion.
EDCS-17900596.summary: This inscription records the names and rank of two standard-bearers of the 10th legion, likely from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-17900598.translation: To Licinia Attiana, daughter of Lucius Licinius Licinianus, tribune of the first urban cohort. Publius Rapellius Kalendinus, prefect of the 2nd Gallic cohort, [erected this] to his most sacred wife.
EDCS-17900598.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a prefect of an urban cohort to his wife, Licinia Attiana, dating likely to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17900599.translation: To Lucius Magius, son of Marcus, from the Falernian tribe, a veteran of the 10th Fretensis legion. Marion, his freedman, [erected this to] his patron and himself.
EDCS-17900599.summary: This inscription records a freedman dedicating a monument to his patron, Lucius Magius, a veteran of the 10th Fretensis legion, probably from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17900600.translation: Lucius Norbanus Faustus, son of Lucius, from the Falernian tribe, a soldier of the 4th Praetorian cohort, century of Valerius. He lived for 34 years and served for 14 years. He ordered this to be made in his will.
EDCS-17900600.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Norbanus Faustus, a soldier in the Praetorian Guard, detailing his age at death and length of service, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17900602.translation: [---] Fal(erna) Restituto, served. [---] Kaiatia, served 5 years, lived 24 years. [---]ma, his mother, to her excellent son, [erected this] according to his will.
EDCS-17900602.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary epitaph from a mother to her son, indicating his military service and age at death, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.
