EDCS-21100690.translation: [3]ychus, freedman of(?) Augustus(?), and [3]onianus and his son dedicated [3]us as a gift from his own resources.
EDCS-21100690.summary: This fragmented inscription records a dedication made by a freedman of Augustus (?), named [3]ychus, along with others, possibly for a public building or religious site.

EDCS-21100702.summary: This very fragmentary inscription mentions 'Apstineas' and 'hands', possibly related to a legal context or a physical action described in the inscription.

EDCS-21100704.translation: Licinia Tertullinia lived in peace for 51 years. Amelius for his innocent wife, buried on the 6th day of the month of June, died in the consulship of Amantius and [Albinus].
EDCS-21100704.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Licinia Tertullinia, recording her age at death and her husband's dedication.

EDCS-21100706.translation: By the authority of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, the greatest conqueror of Parthia, the greatest conqueror of Britain, the high priest, and by decree of the college of the fifteen men who perform sacred rites, Servius Calpurnius Domitius Dexter, pro-magistrate, restored the altar to the most holy Circe. Dedicated on the 17th day before the Kalends of July, during the consulship of Emperor Antoninus Augustus for the fourth time and Balbinus for the second time.
EDCS-21100706.summary: This is a dedication of a restored altar to the goddess Circe, likely for a public sanctuary, commissioned by Servius Calpurnius Domitius Dexter under imperial authority and a senatorial college.

EDCS-21100712.translation: Lucius Faberius Murena, son of Caius, of the Pomptina tribe, augur, municipal commissioner, edile, arranged for the water that flowed from the lake to be collected and for the spring to be returned into the lake, and he had it done at his own expense.
EDCS-21100712.summary: This is a public works dedication by Lucius Faberius Murena, recording his funding of a project to manage water from a lake.

EDCS-21100713.translation: [...] Montanus, a municipal commissioner with judicial authority in the municipium of Circeii(?), built the wooden amphitheater at his own expense, and he dedicated it with a gladiatorial show and a hunt. The site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-21100713.summary: This is a dedication of a wooden amphitheater and games funded by Montanus, a local magistrate, in Circeii.

EDCS-21100718.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lamyrus made this for Marcella, his freedwoman, who lived more or less 18 years, for her deserving kindness.
EDCS-21100718.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a freedwoman named Marcella, set up by her former master.

EDCS-21100719.translation: Sacred to Liber Augustus. Alfius Protus, the priest, and Lucilius Martensis, the elder, placed the statue of the god Liber. Mollius Neoesius and Aurelius Sabinus gave the base as a gift.
EDCS-21100719.summary: This is a dedication of a statue of the god Liber, with contributions from a priest, another individual, and two donors for the base.

EDCS-21100720.translation: Gaius Munatius Felix willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Fortuna.
EDCS-21100720.summary: This is a votive inscription by Gaius Munatius Felix to the goddess Fortuna.

EDCS-21100721.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, father of his country, high priest, with tribunician power for the fourth time, acclaimed emperor for the seventh time, consul for the second time, proconsul, son of the deified Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva. The people of Privernum, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-21100721.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Septimius Severus by the people of Privernum, listing his titles and ancestry, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-21100728.translation: [...] Flaccilla [...] prefect of cavalry of the ala, tribune of the soldiers of the IV Macedonian legion, tribune of the soldiers of the [...] Macedonian legion and the XV Apollinaris legion, to her father, grandfather, grandmother, and mother.
EDCS-21100728.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Flaccilla, who held military and equestrian offices, to her ancestors.

EDCS-21100729.translation: Lucius Firvius Postumus, son of Lucius, of the Fabian tribe, from Luca, a soldier of the 15th Urban Cohort, in the centuria of Arruntius, standard-bearer, served for 18 years, lived for 36 years. He lies here.
EDCS-21100729.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Firvius Postumus, a standard-bearer in an urban cohort, recording his military service and age at death.

EDCS-21100730.translation: A site granted by decree of the decurions to Lucius [A]elius Hermeti[o]nus, *sevirus* Augustalis, priest (?).
EDCS-21100730.summary: This is a record of land allocation or designation of a public space, likely for a monument or building, to a prominent local official named Lucius Aelius Hermetionus.

EDCS-21100733.translation: Lucius Asinius Cudia, son of Lucius, of the Voting Tribe Oufentina, built (this) while alive for himself and for Suestidia, daughter of Marcus.
EDCS-21100733.summary: This epitaph records a self-funded funerary monument built by Lucius Asinius Cudia for himself and his wife, Suestidia.

EDCS-21100734.translation: The bones of Lucius Aninus Phosphorus.
EDCS-21100734.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription indicating the burial place of Lucius Aninus Phosphorus.

EDCS-21100736.translation: Quintus Castricius Potamo, freedman of Quintus, and Castricia Antiopa, freedwoman of Quintus; Caius Pacidius Pindarus, freedman of Caius, (left this) by will.
EDCS-21100736.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial arrangements made by two freedmen, Potamo and Pindarus, and a freedwoman, Antiopa, likely from the same household or family.

EDCS-21200026.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Cornelia Aglais (dedicated this) to Cornelia Fasta, her most dutiful mother, for herself and her descendants and their posterity.
EDCS-21200026.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Cornelia Aglais to her mother, Cornelia Fasta, intended for the family's continued use.

EDCS-21200027.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Flavia Faustina lived 18 years and 45 days. Titus Flavius Faustus, (her) patron, (dedicated this).
EDCS-21200027.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavia Faustina, recording her age at death and dedicated by her patron, Titus Flavius Faustus.

EDCS-21200028.translation: Epaphroditus and Bruttia (dedicated this) to Lucius Laelius Cercenus, son of Lucius.
EDCS-21200028.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Epaphroditus and Bruttia to Lucius Laelius Cercenus.

EDCS-21200030.translation: Marcus Privernius Cerialis and Lollia Prima (dedicated/placed) two jars.
EDCS-21200030.summary: This inscription likely records the dedication or placement of two funerary urns or containers by Marcus Privernius Cerialis and Lollia Prima.

EDCS-21200031.translation: Titus Rutilius Donatus (dedicated this) for himself and for his wife, the freedwoman of the family of Iulius, Clara, deserving wife, and for Rutilia Donata, freedwoman of Titus Rutilius, freedwoman.
EDCS-21200031.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Titus Rutilius Donatus for himself, his wife Clara, and another woman, Rutilia Donata.

EDCS-21200033.translation: [Marcus] son of Marcus, son of Marcus, of the Voting Tribe Oufentina; [Marcus] son of Marcus, son of Caius.
EDCS-21200033.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragmentary record of a family genealogy or memorial, possibly for father and son, with one individual identified by tribe.

EDCS-21200035.summary: This inscription is too fragmentary to determine its purpose, but it lists personal names and likely pertains to a funerary context.

EDCS-21200039.translation: Lucius Aninius Capra, son of Lucius, duovir for the second time, undertook the reconstruction of the temple of Apollo and the surrounding area and walls at his own expense.
EDCS-21200039.summary: This is a public works inscription from an unknown location, dating to the period when the office of duovir existed, recording a Lucius Aninius Capra funding repairs to the temple of Apollo.

EDCS-21200041.text_edition: \n Eros vivas Caio Oppio Cai filio Pon<t=I>(ina) Pomius plen collegium iubenum sic ob eximium amorem eius merenti patrono coloniae Setinae ob quem honorem quod in statuam contulerunt donavit SS IIII milia nummum quam summam ita donatam habebunt ut perpetuo annis singulis die natali ipsius ex usuris summae supra scriptae sportulas praesentibus dividant et decurionibus ob quam dedicationem omnibus divisit avi adlectis ac dedicatum XII Kalendas Albino II et Dextro consulibus \n
EDCS-21200041.translation: Eros, live! For Gaius Oppius Pontina, son of Gaius, the council of youths, because of his exceptional love for him, their deserving patron of the colony of Setia, for which honor they contributed to a statue, he donated 4000 sesterces. They will have this sum donated in such a way that perpetually, each year on his birthday, from the interest of the sum mentioned above, they will distribute sportulas to those present, and to the decurions. On account of this dedication, he distributed to all, having added the ancestors, and it was dedicated on the twelfth day before the Kalends of March in the consulship of Albinus and Dexter.
EDCS-21200041.summary: This is a donation inscription from Setia, dating to the consulship of Albinus and Dexter (likely 254 AD), recording Eros's endowment for the perpetual celebration of a patron's birthday.

EDCS-21200043.translation: Gaius Paconius Polio, son of Gaius, and Gaius Pomponius Polio, son of Lucius, as duovirs with judicial authority, paved the road with stone at their own expense.
EDCS-21200043.summary: This is a public works inscription from an unknown location, recording two duovirs paving a road at their own expense.

EDCS-21200044.translation: Lucius Pomponius Annianus, son of Lucius, undertook the paving of the road at his own expense.
EDCS-21200044.summary: This is a public works inscription from an unknown location, recording a Lucius Pomponius Annianus funding the paving of a road.

EDCS-21200047.translation: Gaius Licinius Asclepiades, physician. 10 feet in front, 10 feet in the land.
EDCS-21200047.summary: This is a boundary or property marker inscription, likely from a tomb or land plot, dating to the Roman Imperial period, specifying dimensions of a plot of land owned by a physician named Gaius Licinius Asclepiades.

EDCS-21200058.translation: Geminia Myrtis and Anicia Prisca, by order, made the temple of Bellona at their own expense for the health of the Emperor Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus.
EDCS-21200058.summary: This is a dedication inscription from an unknown location, dating to the reign of Trajan (98-117 AD), recording Geminia Myrtis and Anicia Prisca funding the construction of a temple to Bellona.

EDCS-21200063.translation: [...] of the Augusti Severus and Antoninus
EDCS-21200063.summary: This inscription is too fragmentary to determine its purpose, but it mentions the emperors Severus and Antoninus, suggesting a date within their reigns (likely 200s AD).

EDCS-21200065.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Marcus Petronius Montanus, son of Marcus, of the Collina tribe, veteran of the Praetorian Guard of Augustus, decurion, duovir of Ulubrae, quaestor of the public treasury.
EDCS-21200065.summary: This is a funerary inscription from Ulubrae, dating to the Roman Imperial period, for Marcus Petronius Montanus, listing his military and civic offices.

EDCS-21200067.translation: To the Spirits (or alive?) of the Underworld. Marcus Asidonius Atticus, son of Marcus, of the Pomptina tribe, from Ulubris. He lived 7 years, 11 months, 14 days. Gnaeus Utilius Ianuarius, his pupil.
EDCS-21200067.summary: This is a funerary inscription from Ulubris, dating to the Roman Imperial period, for a child named Marcus Asidonius Atticus, recording his age and dedicating him by his teacher.

EDCS-21200068.translation: Lucius Caecilius Plutus, freedman of Lucius, a pearl merchant from the Sacred Way, for himself and Cornelia Sosini, freedwoman of his woman, and his brother Lucius Caecilius Epaphroditus, freedman of Lucius. In accordance with the decision of Lucius Caecilius Athenio, freedman of Lucius.
EDCS-21200068.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Lucius Caecilius Plutus, a pearl merchant, set up by himself and others, including his brother and a freedwoman named Cornelia Sosini, with a reference to Lucius Caecilius Athenio.

EDCS-21200069.translation: Lucius Papius Phaselus, freedman of Lucius, wine merchant; Marcus Perperna Potitus, son of Marcus, of the Scaptia tribe; Marcus Alfidius Fortunatus, freedman of Marcus; Aulus Tettius Stlatta, freedman of Aulus, a money-changer; Lucius Flavius Alexander, freedman of Lucius; Marcus Thorius Auctus, freedman of Marcus, a butcher; Marcus Licinius Leonida, freedman of Marcus; Gaius Autronius Hilarus, freedman of Gaius; Appius Quintius Nicephor, freedman of Appius, a veterinarian for pack animals; Publius Nummius Pamphilus, freedman of Publius; Titus Heliades, freedman of Titus, in whom fear never slept; Marcus Alfidius Tertius, freedman of Marcus; Lucius Picidius Albanus, freedman of Lucius; Aulus Cervius Antilogus, freedman of Aulus; Gaius Rennius Laetus, freedman of Gaius; Aulus Sergius Acolutus, freedman of Aulus.
EDCS-21200069.summary: This inscription lists the names and occupations of numerous freedmen and possibly their patrons, suggesting a commercial or guild context, likely from the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-21200070.translation: Gaius Ruelius Bassus, son of Gaius, of the Tromentina tribe; Marcus Epaphra, freedman of Marcus; Aulus Hilarus, freedman of Aulus; Quintus [---]; Gaius Iucundus, freedman of Gaius; Marcus Auctus, freedman of Marcus; Gaius Ambactus, freedman of Lucius; Lucius Artemidorus, freedman of Gaius; Gnaeus Plut[---], freedman of Gnaeus; Marcus Felix [---], freedman of Marcus; Decimus Philargurus [---], freedman of Decimus; bakers(?).
EDCS-21200070.summary: This inscription lists names of individuals, possibly bakers, with their patrons or family connections, dating to the early Imperial period.

EDCS-21200072.translation: To the departed spirits. Abscintilla, the mother, made this for her most dutiful son, Aulus Calpurnius, son of Lucius, of the Menenia tribe, who deserved it.
EDCS-21200072.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a mother named Abscintilla for her son, Aulus Calpurnius.

EDCS-21200073.translation: To the departed spirits. Claudia Ias made this for her well-deserving husband, Tiberius Claudius Theopompus.
EDCS-21200073.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a wife, Claudia Ias, for her husband, Tiberius Claudius Theopompus.

EDCS-21200074.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Domitius Endymion made this for his dearest son, Lucius Domitius Rusticus, and for himself, his freedmen and freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-21200074.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Lucius Domitius Rusticus, set up by his father Lucius Domitius Endymion, who also made provisions for himself and his household.

EDCS-21200075.translation: Iulia Charmosyne, freedwoman of Augustus, made this for her freedmen and freedwomen and their families and their descendants. In front 48 feet, in the field 64 feet.
EDCS-21200075.summary: This inscription records a funerary dedication by Iulia Charmosyne, a freedwoman of Augustus, for her household, and also specifies the dimensions of the property.

EDCS-21200227.summary: This inscription records the name and office (duovir) of Publius Aquilius Gallus, likely a magistrate in a Roman colony.

EDCS-21200242.translation: Lucius Magulnius, son of Marcus, quaestor.
EDCS-21200242.summary: This inscription records the name and office of Lucius Magulnius, son of Marcus, who held the position of quaestor.

EDCS-21200244.summary: This inscription records the name and office of Marcus Nautius, who held the position of quaestor.

EDCS-21200413.translation: From the workshops of Avidius Quietus.
EDCS-21200413.summary: This inscription indicates that the object originated from the workshops of Avidius Quietus.

EDCS-21200502.translation: From the estates of Domitia Lucilla, daughter of Publius, wife of Pontius Clodianus, with Paetinus and Apronianus as consuls.
EDCS-21200502.summary: This inscription marks property belonging to Domitia Lucilla, daughter of Publius, wife of Pontius Clodianus, dating to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-21200524.translation: With Paetinus and Apronianus as consuls, from the estates of Aulus Pontius Clodianus belonging to Domitia Lucilla.
EDCS-21200524.summary: This inscription indicates property from Domitia Lucilla's estates, managed by Aulus Pontius Clodianus, during the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-21200533.translation: From the estates of Domitia Lucilla, daughter of Publius, wife of Pontius Clodianus, with Paetinus and Apronianus as consuls.
EDCS-21200533.summary: This inscription marks property belonging to Domitia Lucilla, daughter of Publius, wife of Pontius Clodianus, dating to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-21200535.translation: From the estates of Domitia Lucilla, daughter of Publius, wife of Pontius Clodianus, with Paetinus and Apronianus as consuls.
EDCS-21200535.summary: This inscription marks property belonging to Domitia Lucilla, daughter of Publius, wife of Pontius Clodianus, dating to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-21300030.translation: To the divine shades. Novia Victorina made this altar for her excellent husband, Marcus Poblicius Dionysus, lictor of the three decuriae, who served as attendant to Caesar and the magistrates.
EDCS-21300030.summary: This is a funerary altar dedicated by Novia Victorina to her husband, Marcus Poblicius Dionysus, an attendant official, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-21300031.translation: To the divine shades. For Gaius Egnatius Felix, a soldier of the 11th urban cohort, who served for 20 years. Titus Rabius Speratus and Marcus Arsius Felicianus, his heirs, [made this] for their excellent friend, well-deserving. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-21300031.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Gaius Egnatius Felix, a soldier in the 11th urban cohort, who served for 20 years, and was erected by his heirs.

EDCS-21300044.translation: To the shades of Aebutus Chresimus.
EDCS-21300044.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Aebutus Chresimus.

EDCS-21300045.translation: To the spirits of Aebutia Bella.
EDCS-21300045.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Aebutia Bella.

EDCS-21300050.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Sextus Granius Primus made this for his most dutiful daughter.
EDCS-21300050.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Sextus Granius Primus for his daughter.

EDCS-21300052.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Stertinius Philo made this for his well-deserving fellow student, who lived 24 years, 6 months, and 18 days.
EDCS-21300052.summary: This funerary dedication records the death of a student who lived 24 years, 6 months, and 18 days, and was made by a fellow student.

EDCS-21300055.translation: Of Lucius Lucretius Vitulus, son of Lucius, Papiria tribe, who lived 20 years.
EDCS-21300055.summary: This funerary dedication records the death of Lucius Lucretius Vitulus at the age of 20.

EDCS-21300057.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Petronia Synethia and Gnaeus Petronius Zosimus. Aper, patron.
EDCS-21300057.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Petronia Synethia and Gnaeus Petronius Zosimus by their patron Aper.

EDCS-21300062.translation: Geminus, praetor, quaestor, [dedicated?] the shrine of Fortuna to Antonius.
EDCS-21300062.summary: This inscription, possibly from the 1st century BC or AD, records a dedication by Geminus, who held the offices of praetor and quaestor, for an aedes Fortunae possibly related to Antonius.

EDCS-21300066.translation: Sacred to Silvanus. Publius Maianus Ianuarius made the little shrine from new [materials?].
EDCS-21300066.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a small shrine to Silvanus by Publius Maianus Ianuarius.

EDCS-21300067.translation: Quintus Fulvius Zoticus gave [as a gift?], dedicated [?].
EDCS-21300067.summary: This inscription records a dedication or gift by Quintus Fulvius Zoticus.

EDCS-21300069.text_edition: \n Medullinae Camilli filiae Tiberi Claudii Neronis Germanici sponsae Acratus libertus paedagogus\n
EDCS-21300069.translation: To Medullina, daughter of Camillus, wife of Tiberius Claudius Nero Germanicus. Acratus, the educator, [dedicated this].
EDCS-21300069.summary: This funerary dedication records Medullina, daughter of Camillus and wife of Tiberius Claudius Nero Germanicus, made by her educator Acratus.

EDCS-21300070.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Elvia Scepsis. Acratus, the educator.
EDCS-21300070.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Acratus, an educator, for Elvia Scepsis.

EDCS-21300079.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Alypus Augusti libertus procurator a sacris kastrensibus fecit Aureliae Sozomene coniugi bene merenti cun sic qua vixit nnos XXI menses VI et sibi et libertis superstitibus et alumnis suis et libertis libertabusquae sic item consummaverunt Aureli Alypus Auggustorum libertus Alypiane Alypianus fili\n
EDCS-21300079.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Aurelius Alypus, freedman of Augustus and procurator of the imperial household guard, made this for his deserving wife Aurelia Sozomene, with whom he lived for 21 years and 6 months, and for himself, his surviving freedmen and pupils, and those freedmen and freedwomen who likewise completed their lives here. Marcus Aurelius Alypus, freedman of the emperors, also his freedman Alypianus son.
EDCS-21300079.summary: A funerary monument set up by Marcus Aurelius Alypus, a procurator, for his wife and for himself and his household.

EDCS-21300080.translation: Claudia Elpis for herself and for Tiberius Claudius Scythopolitanus, her dearest freedman, a lictor of three decuries, and for Tiberius Claudius Carphorus.
EDCS-21300080.summary: A funerary epitaph for Claudia Elpis and her freedman, who held the office of lictor.

EDCS-21300081.translation: from the bedchamber, procurator, in charge of grain, accensus, for [his?] patron and for himself, Claudia A[---], Claudia Atticilla [---], and their descendants, erected in front.
EDCS-21300081.summary: A fragmented inscription likely concerning official duties and family members, possibly a funerary monument.

EDCS-21300085.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Caius Julius Malchus, a veteran of Augustus. His wife Aelia Iulia and his heirs made this for him, a deserving man.
EDCS-21300085.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by a wife and heirs to a veteran of the imperial service.

EDCS-21300086.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lucius Tridonius Marcellus, a soldier of the 8th Praetorian Cohort, in the centuria of Messianus. He served for 4 years, lived for 30 years. His heir Papiria Ticinus made this for him, a deserving man.
EDCS-21300086.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Tridonius Marcellus, a soldier in the Praetorian Guard.

EDCS-21300087.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lucius Vettius Optatus, son of Lucius, of the Tromentina tribe, from Aquis Statiellis, an evocatus of Augustus. Lucius Vettius Hermes and Lucius Vettius Chresimus, his freedmen, for their deserving patron.
EDCS-21300087.summary: A funerary monument erected by freedmen for their patron, Lucius Vettius Optatus, who was an evocatus.

EDCS-21300091.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Julius Hierax, a decurial [official], the best [of men]. Pomponius Musa, Iulia Magnilla, [his] heirs.
EDCS-21300091.summary: A fragmented funerary inscription for Julius Hierax, likely an official, with dedications from his heirs.

EDCS-21300092.translation: Varronius Nicia, master of the quinquennial office of the guild of carpenters, in the lustrum [period] of [---], lived 93 years.
EDCS-21300092.summary: An inscription recording the lifespan and official position of Varronius Nicia, a guild master.

EDCS-21300096.translation: For Claudia Actes, freedwoman of Augustus.
EDCS-21300096.summary: A dedication, likely funerary, for Claudia Actes, a freedwoman of an emperor.

EDCS-21300097.summary: A dedication, likely funerary, for Caius Julius Superstitis.

EDCS-21300099.translation: To the divine shades. Onomaste, his wife, made this for Iulianus, her most excellent husband, who deserved well of her. He lived 40 years and 7 days, an excellent actor and farmer.
EDCS-21300099.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a wife for her deceased husband, identifying his professions and praising his character.

EDCS-21300100.translation: Caius Iulius Philomus, freedman of Decimus, a builder. Marcia Chryse, freedwoman of a woman, his wife.
EDCS-21300100.summary: A funerary epitaph for a builder named Caius Iulius Philomus and his wife Marcia Chryse.

EDCS-21300101.translation: To the divine shades. For Aemilia Secunda.
EDCS-21300101.summary: A funerary dedication to Aemilia Secunda.

EDCS-21300102.translation: For Antonia Dea, dutiful daughter of Quintus.
EDCS-21300102.summary: A dedication to Antonia Dea, daughter of Quintus, emphasizing her filial piety.

EDCS-21300103.translation: Successus, freedman of Publius, made this for himself, for his wife Avillia Quincta, and for his freedmen and freedwomen, and for their descendants. In the front, 16 feet; in the field, 17 and a half feet.
EDCS-21300103.summary: A funerary monument set up by Successus for himself, his wife, and his freed dependents, with measurements of the plot.

EDCS-21300105.translation: Lucius Careius, son of Caius, from the Stellatina tribe. Lucius Careius Heracl, freedman of Lucius. For himself and his family. In the front, 12 feet; in the field, 24 feet.
EDCS-21300105.summary: A funerary monument set up by Lucius Careius and his freedman Lucius Careius Heracl for themselves and their relatives, with plot dimensions.

EDCS-21300106.translation: To the divine shades. For Claudia Actes.
EDCS-21300106.summary: A funerary dedication to Claudia Actes.

EDCS-21300891.translation: To the divine shades. For Claudia Victorina, deserving of the highest praise. Tiberius Claudius Philetus, her freedman.
EDCS-21300891.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a freedman for his deserving patroness, Claudia Victorina.

EDCS-21300895.translation: To the divine shades. Felix lived 5 years, 4 months. Felix and Ianuaria set this up for their son.
EDCS-21300895.summary: A funerary epitaph for a child named Felix, set up by his parents Felix and Ianuaria.

EDCS-21300896.translation: To the divine shades. Fidia Tryphera made this for Gaius Fudius Utilis, her husband and patron, who deserved well of her. He lived [---]
EDCS-21300896.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Fidia Tryphera for her husband and patron, Gaius Fudius Utilis, indicating a close relationship and his well-deserved respect.

EDCS-21300899.translation: To Flavia Ionice, who lived 15 years and 27 days. Epimachus, freedman of the emperor, and Domitia Eutychis, her most devoted parents, [set this up] for themselves and their descendants.
EDCS-21300899.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by parents for their deceased daughter, Flavia Ionice, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-21300900.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Elate, her most devoted mother, [set this up] for her daughter Fortunata, who lived 5 years and 9 months.
EDCS-21300900.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of a young girl, Fortunata, and was erected by her mother.

EDCS-21300902.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Sextus Lucanius Secundus set this up for his well-deserving wife, Lucilia Adiecta.
EDCS-21300902.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by a husband to his deserving wife, Lucilia Adiecta.

EDCS-21300903.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. [---]elius [---]trus, freedman of the emperor, set this up for his most sweet and incomparable, well-deserving wife, Seina Lucilla, who lived 35 years.
EDCS-21300903.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Seina Lucilla, who lived 35 years, and was set up by her husband, a freedman of the emperor.

EDCS-21300905.translation: Greetings, Manlia Anthusa. May it be well with you, reader, and with you, passerby. [May it be well] with me who made this monument in this place, and with my [family].
EDCS-21300905.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription on a monument that includes a greeting to passersby and a wish for well-being for the monument's maker and their family.

EDCS-21300907.translation: Octavia Urbana, freedwoman of a woman, [set this up] for her husband Quintus Ammius Optatus, freedman of Quintus; for her son Gaius Octavius Antiatus, son of Spurius; for Octavia Fortunata, freedwoman of a woman; for herself and her descendants.
EDCS-21300907.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Octavia Urbana for her husband, son, and herself, along with her descendants.

EDCS-21300912.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Publius Postumius Felix and Publius Postumius Abascantus set this up for their well-deserving father, Publius Postumius Farnaces, and for their well-deserving patron.
EDCS-21300912.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument erected by two sons for their father and their patron.

EDCS-21300914.translation: Marcus Quinctilius Phoebus ordered this to be made according to his will, for himself and for his wife Quinctilia Danae, who deserved well of him, and for his daughter Quinctilia Proba, who lived 10 years, and also for his descendants, permitting them to be of the legal right of this monument.
EDCS-21300914.summary: This inscription records a will-mandated funerary monument for Marcus Quinctilius Phoebus, his wife, his daughter, and his descendants.

EDCS-21300917.translation: Publius Rutilius Antiochus, freedman of Publius. Rutilia Aristonice, freedwoman of Publius. [The plot measures] 12 feet in front and 20 feet in the field.
EDCS-21300917.summary: This inscription records the dimensions of a burial plot and identifies the owners as freedmen, Publius Rutilius Antiochus and Rutilia Aristonice.

EDCS-21300919.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Gaius Mod[---] [set this up] for his most beloved, well-deserving wife, Ulpia Pompeia.
EDCS-21300919.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument dedicated by a husband, Gaius Mod[---], to his beloved and deserving wife, Ulpia Pompeia.

EDCS-21300937.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate V consul aedem vetustate corruptam restituit\n
EDCS-21300937.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrian Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, consul, restored this temple, corrupted by age.
EDCS-21300937.summary: Imperial dedication by Hadrian restoring a temple, dated to his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-21300938.translation: To Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, great-grandson of the deified Trajan the Parthian, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, consul for the second time, with tribunician power, by public decree of the Antiate people.
EDCS-21300938.summary: Public dedication by the people of Antium to Emperor Lucius Verus, likely commemorating his consulship (c. 161 AD).

EDCS-21300942.translation: To Marcus Aquilius Felix, son of Marcus, of the Fabian tribe, from the censuses of Roman equites, prefect of the Praetorian fleet at Ravenna, procurator of the patrimony twice, procurator of inheritances of the private patrimony, procurator of public works, commander of a detachment with the rank of primus pilus of the 11th Claudian Legion, centurion frumentarius, patron of the colony, for his merits, by public decree of the Antiate people.
EDCS-21300942.summary: Public dedication by the Antiate people to Marcus Aquilius Felix, honoring his extensive career in military and administrative posts, likely dating to the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-21300943.translation: To Gaius Julius Proculus, son of Marcus, of the Voltinian tribe, consul for the 15th time, XVvir sacris faciundis, fetial, curator of public works, legate of the Emperor with the rank of pro praetor for the province of Lugdunensis, legate of the Emperor with the rank of pro praetor for the Transpadane region, legate of the 6th Ferrata Legion, praetor, tribune of the plebs, from the acts of Emperor Trajan Augustus, tribune of the 4th Scythica Legion, quaestor of the Augusti for the third time, IIIvir monetalis, by public decree of the Antiate people, as patron.
EDCS-21300943.summary: Public dedication by the Antiate people to Gaius Julius Proculus, their patron, listing his extensive senatorial and military career, likely dating to the late 1st or early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-21300944.translation: To Aulus Larcius Lepidus, son of Aulus, former seviri of Roman equites, military tribune of the Adjutrix Legion, decemvir for judging lawsuits, quaestor with the rank of pro praetor of the province of Crete and Cyrene, legate of Emperor Vespasian Caesar Augustus of the 10th Fretensis Legion, recipient of military gifts from Emperor Vespasian Caesar Augustus and Titus Caesar, son of Augustus, during the Jewish War: a mural crown, a vallaris crown, a golden spear, two vexilla. Tribune of the plebs, legate of the province of Pontus and Bithynia. Caecinia Larga, daughter of Aulus, his wife, and Larcia Priscilla, daughter of Aulus, his daughter, made this.
EDCS-21300944.summary: Funerary monument set up by the wife and daughter for Aulus Larcius Lepidus, who had a distinguished military and political career under Vespasian and Titus, including service in the Jewish War (c. 70-80 AD).

EDCS-21300946.translation: Marcus Sextilius, son of Quintus, duovir twice, IIIvir monetalis. His wife Fabia, daughter of Publius.
EDCS-21300946.summary: Dedication by a duovir and his wife, likely from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-21300951.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Titus Flavius Evangellus, freedman of the Emperor, an accountant, former praetorian official of Antium. Marcus Ulpius Priscus, freedman of the Emperor, made this monument to a well-deserving parent. It will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-21300951.summary: Funerary dedication by Marcus Ulpius Priscus to his father Titus Flavius Evangellus, a freedman who served as an accountant and official in Antium, likely 2nd century AD.

EDCS-21300952.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Julia Demetrias. Priscus, freedman of the Emperor, procurator of the 4% tax and the 40th tax in Africa and Gaul, to his most chaste wife.
EDCS-21300952.summary: Funerary dedication by Priscus, an imperial freedman and procurator, to his wife Julia Demetrias, likely 2nd century AD.

EDCS-21300953.translation: To the Divine Shades. Marcus Antonius Fronto, a veteran, a Syrian by nationality, served for 35 years. He made this for himself and for his freedmen and freedwomen.
EDCS-21300953.summary: Funerary monument set up by Marcus Antonius Fronto, a Syrian veteran, for himself and his freed dependents, likely 2nd century AD.

EDCS-21300954.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Marcus Aurelius Dasios, soldier of the 4th Flavian Legion, with 15 years of service, lived 35 years. Marcus Aurelius Silbanus, his heir, [made this].
EDCS-21300954.summary: Funerary dedication for Marcus Aurelius Dasios, a soldier in the 4th Flavian Legion who served 15 years, likely late 1st or early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-21300958.translation: To Lucius Veratius Afro, son of Caius, of the tribe Quirina, a veteran of Forum Iulii, a decurion, quaestor. Lucius Munatius Sabinus, centurion of the speculators; Caius Mamillius Naus, centurion of the 7th Praetorian Cohort; Numerius Naevius Rufus, centurion of the 6th Urban Cohort; Lucius Veratius Certus, legionary centurion; his heirs. In the front 20 feet, in the rear 20 feet.
EDCS-21300958.summary: This funerary inscription honors Lucius Veratius Afro, a veteran of Forum Iulii and a former magistrate, with his burial plot financed by his heirs and military colleagues.

EDCS-21300959.translation: To the divine shades. Lucius Afinius Proc... [made this] for his most excellent father, Lucius Afinius, Augustalis, curator (?), and treasurer of the guild of craftsmen.
EDCS-21300959.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Lucius Afinius, who served as an Augustalis and guild treasurer, set up by his son, Lucius Afinius Proc...

EDCS-21300960.translation: To the divine shades. Sedata made this for Quintus Ci[---] Eutychetes, scribe of the public office, with whom she lived for 12 years and...
EDCS-21300960.summary: A funerary inscription by Sedata for her deceased partner, Quintus Ci[---] Eutychetes, a public scribe, whom she lived with for 12 years.

EDCS-21300961.translation: Caius Faustius Primitivos, curator of the Augustales' treasury, gave [---] nummi to the same body, [under Emperor Trajan?] in the 6th consulship.
EDCS-21300961.summary: This inscription records a donation of money by Caius Faustius Primitivos, curator of the Augustales' treasury, to his corporation, likely during Trajan's reign.

EDCS-21300964.translation: Quintus Rustius, son of Marcus, duovir, made this at his own expense.
EDCS-21300964.summary: A public works inscription by Quintus Rustius, a duovir, who funded a construction project at his own expense.

EDCS-21300975.summary: A simple dedication, likely funerary or for a public work, made by Sextus Egnatius Reditus.

EDCS-21300981.summary: A dedication or inscription on a monument, possibly honoring Lucius Cocceius Hadrianus Augustus.

EDCS-21300982.translation: To the divine shades. Publius Betilienus Pastor, the treasurer.
EDCS-21300982.summary: A funerary dedication to Publius Betilienus Pastor, identified as a treasurer (arcarius).

EDCS-21300983.translation: To the divine shades. Friends made this for Sura, the money-changer, the sub-aedile.
EDCS-21300983.summary: A funerary inscription by friends for Sura, a money-changer and former sub-aedile.

EDCS-21300984.translation: To the divine shades. Aemilia Asclepias made this for her well-deserving son [or daughter?], Asclepias, who lived 14 years, 5 months, and 10 days.
EDCS-21300984.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Aemilia Asclepias for her child, Asclepias, who died at the age of 14 years, 5 months, and 10 days.

EDCS-21400019.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Gnaeus Ambivius Maecianus, who lived 20 years. Fortunatius and Proculus made this for their most devoted brother.
EDCS-21400019.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gnaeus Ambivius Maecianus, who died at the age of 20, erected by his brothers Fortunatius and Proculus.

EDCS-21400021.translation: To Marcus Antonius Priscus, who lived 9 years and 8 months. Rustia Prisca, his mother, made this for her most devoted son.
EDCS-21400021.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Antonius Priscus, who died at the age of 9 years and 8 months, erected by his mother Rustia Prisca.

EDCS-21400023.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Arabio Lucianus, also called Sarga, who lived 10 years, 9 months, and 19 days. His father Lucianus made this for [him] undeserving [of such fate].
EDCS-21400023.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Arabio Lucianus (also known as Sarga), who died at the age of 10 years, 9 months, and 19 days, erected by his father Lucianus.

EDCS-21400024.inscription_type: tituli operum, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-21400024.translation: Sacred to Eternal Sleep. Antistia Magna, wife of Gaius Avidius Laco, freedman of the freedman of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, and his aunt, and Gaius Avidius Euphrosynus, son, made this. Beware, seller and buyer, for you will give 20,000 sesterces to the treasury of the Roman people, and the informer will receive a fourth. In the third consulship of the Emperor Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus and the third of Marcus Ummidius Quadratus, on the 3rd day before the Kalends of January. Avidius Laco bought a plot of land for an aria (or forge) of 18 feet from Tiberius Claudius Ph(aedrus?), from the land of Autolycus, freedman of Augustus, on the Via Latina, on the left side as one goes from the city, in front 6 feet, in the land 2 feet.
EDCS-21400024.summary: This is a complex legal inscription concerning the sale and dedication of land for a funerary aria or forge, dating to AD 166, involving prominent individuals and the imperial treasury.

EDCS-21400025.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Caius Aurelius Apollophanes made this for himself while alive, for his wife, brother, and freedmen, freedwomen, and their children, and their descendants, in such a way that his name shall not perish.
EDCS-21400025.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caius Aurelius Apollophanes, who made it for himself and his family during his lifetime, ensuring his name would be remembered.

EDCS-21400026.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Aurelius Fortunatus made this for Aurelia Blossia, his wife, for himself, and for his worthy wife.
EDCS-21400026.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelia Blossia, erected by her husband Aurelius Fortunatus for himself and for her.

EDCS-21400028.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Caius Bircius Epaphroditus made this for his well-deserving patroness Bircia Ampiata.
EDCS-21400028.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Bircia Ampiata, erected by her freedman Caius Bircius Epaphroditus.

EDCS-21400029.translation: To the shades of the underworld. For Cladus, a fellow slave, well-deserving. Gamus and Coris, fellow slaves.
EDCS-21400029.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cladus, a fellow slave, erected by his fellow slaves Gamus and Coris.

EDCS-21400030.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Claudia Gemela made this for Tiberius Claudius Carpus, who lived 25 years.
EDCS-21400030.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tiberius Claudius Carpus, who died at the age of 25, erected by his mother Claudia Gemela.

EDCS-21400032.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Fabia Rogata made this for her incomparable husband Marcus Cornelius Placidus, with whom she lived for 20 years, a well-deserving [husband].
EDCS-21400032.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Cornelius Placidus, who was married for 20 years, erected by his wife Fabia Rogata.

EDCS-21400033.translation: To the departed spirits. Cyrus made this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-21400033.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Cyrus to his wife.

EDCS-21400034.translation: The bones of Caius Decimus Agathemerus.
EDCS-21400034.summary: This inscription marks the burial place or commemorates the remains of Agathemerus.

EDCS-21400036.translation: To the departed spirits. Fabia Marcia made this for her deserving foster-son Anicetus, who lived four years and twenty days.
EDCS-21400036.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Fabia Marcia for her foster-son Anicetus, who died at a young age.

EDCS-21400037.translation: Marcus Fabius Hilarus, freedman of a woman; Caesonia Rufa, freedwoman of a woman; Caesonia Acome, freedwoman of a woman.
EDCS-21400037.summary: This inscription lists three freedpersons, possibly from the same household.

EDCS-21400041.translation: To the departed spirits. Fabia Omonia made this for her most beloved foster-daughter Valeria, who lived eight years and seven months.
EDCS-21400041.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Fabia Omonia for her foster-daughter Valeria, who died young.

EDCS-21400042.translation: To the departed spirits. For Flavia Athenaïs, incomparably beloved sister. Her father Flavius Fortunatus, and her brothers Flavius Fortunatus and Flavius Callistianus, made this for her, who deserved well.
EDCS-21400042.summary: This funerary inscription was made by the father and brothers for Flavia Athenaïs.

EDCS-21400043.translation: To the departed spirits. Erasinus made this for his deserving wife, Flavia Olimpias.
EDCS-21400043.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Erasinus for his wife, Flavia Olimpias.

EDCS-21400044.translation: To the departed spirits. For Flavia Victoria, who deserved well. Flavia Threpte, her most devoted sister, and Marcus Ulpius Saturninus made this for her, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-21400044.summary: This funerary inscription was made by a sister and Marcus Ulpius Saturninus for Flavia Victoria and also includes future descendants.

EDCS-21400045.translation: To the departed spirits. For Fortunatus, who lived fifteen years and six days. His mother Arscusa made this for her deserving son.
EDCS-21400045.summary: This funerary dedication by Arscusa for her son Fortunatus, who died at age fifteen.

EDCS-21400046.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gavenia Zoe. Marcus Ulpius Eutyches and Gavenia Civitas made this for their deserving spouse.
EDCS-21400046.summary: This funerary inscription was made by Marcus Ulpius Eutyches and Gavenia Civitas for their deserving spouse, Gavenia Zoe.

EDCS-21400047.translation: To Quintus Hortensius Alexander, freedman of Quintus. To Sextilia Moschis, freedwoman of Publius. Sextilia Alba, mother (of Sextilia Moschis and Sextilia?), freedwoman of Publius. Publius Sextilius Phestus. Publius Callus.
EDCS-21400047.summary: This inscription appears to be a funerary dedication, likely for a family or household, possibly related to the Hortensii, given the freedman's name.

EDCS-21400049.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius Spelius Hermes made (this) for his son Hymenaeus.
EDCS-21400049.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a father for his deceased son, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-21400050.translation: To the spirits of the departed. The freedwoman of Iulia Basilice made (this) for Iulia Procula, deserving (of it).
EDCS-21400050.summary: This funerary inscription records a freedwoman dedicating a monument to her former patroness, Iulia Procula.

EDCS-21400051.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Aemilius Saturninus made (this) for his deserving wife, Iunia Cales.
EDCS-21400051.summary: This is a funerary dedication from a husband to his well-deserving wife, Iunia Cales.

EDCS-21400052.translation: To the spirits of the departed. The heirs made (this) for Quintus Licinius Pirbocillea, deserving (of it).
EDCS-21400052.summary: This funerary dedication records heirs honoring Quintus Licinius Pirbocillea, likely a family member.

EDCS-21400053.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus made (this) for his sweetest son, Munio Felix. The parents made (this) for him, deserving (of it).
EDCS-21400053.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by parents to their beloved son, Munio Felix.

EDCS-21400054.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Mynucius Sabinus made (this) for his well-deserving wife, Mynucia Magna.
EDCS-21400054.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Lucius Mynucius Sabinus for his deserving wife, Mynucia Magna.

EDCS-21400055.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Nonia Caenis (dedicated this) to her most indulgent husband, Sextus Nonius Rhetoricus.
EDCS-21400055.summary: This funerary inscription records Nonia Caenis dedicating a monument to her highly indulgent husband, Sextus Nonius Rhetoricus.

EDCS-21400056.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Nunnius Fortunatus (dedicated this) to his most dutiful Nunnia Soteris.
EDCS-21400056.summary: This funerary dedication records a son, Lucius Nunnius Fortunatus, honoring his dutiful mother, Nunnia Soteris.

EDCS-21400057.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Of the slave of Octavius Plotianus.
EDCS-21400057.summary: This is a very brief funerary dedication for a slave belonging to Octavius Plotianus.

EDCS-21400058.translation: To the departed spirits. To Octavia Erotis, daughter of Octavia Fortun(), who lived 30 years and was taken away on her birthday.
EDCS-21400058.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Octavia Erotis by her mother, Octavia Fortun(), who died on her birthday at the age of 30.

EDCS-21400060.translation: To the departed spirits. To Gaius Petrusidius Fortunatus, son, who lived 6 years, 3 months, 2 days. Gaius Petrusidius Hermes and Valeria Deutera, most unhappy parents.
EDCS-21400060.summary: This tombstone commemorates Gaius Petrusidius Fortunatus, a child who died at age 6, erected by his grieving parents.

EDCS-21400061.translation: To the departed spirits. To Postumia Saturnina, daughter of Marcus, wife of Marcus Amelius Thalamus, most virtuous.
EDCS-21400061.summary: This funerary inscription honors Postumia Saturnina, daughter of Marcus, by her husband Marcus Amelius Thalamus.

EDCS-21400064.translation: To the departed spirits. Sextilia Helpis made (this) for her son [icom]o, well deserving, who lived 1 year, 10 months.
EDCS-21400064.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Sextilia Helpis for her young son, who died at the age of 1 year and 10 months.

EDCS-21400067.translation: To the departed spirits. Epaphroditus made (this) for Trofimio, his freedman.
EDCS-21400067.summary: This inscription records that Epaphroditus erected a monument for his freedman, Trofimio.

EDCS-21400068.translation: To the departed spirits. Turrania Attica lived 20 years, 31 days. Sulpicius Mar[--] (made this) for his most chaste beloved.
EDCS-21400068.summary: This funerary inscription honors Turrania Attica, who died at age 20 years and 31 days, erected by Sulpicius Mar[--] for his beloved.

EDCS-21400069.translation: To the departed spirits. To Lucius Valerius Charito, son of Domitius [---].
EDCS-21400069.summary: This incomplete funerary inscription is dedicated to Lucius Valerius Charito.

EDCS-21400070.translation: Vergilia Iucunda, daughter of Gaius, lived 25 years.
EDCS-21400070.summary: This epitaph records the life of Vergilia Iucunda, daughter of Gaius, who died at the age of 25.

EDCS-21400071.translation: To the departed spirits. Verus [---]lo made (this) for his brother, well deserving.
EDCS-21400071.summary: This funerary inscription was made by Verus for his deserving brother.

EDCS-21400072.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Licinius Eutycus made (this) for his dearest wife Vettia Primitiva and for himself.
EDCS-21400072.summary: This funerary inscription was erected by Gaius Licinius Eutyches for his beloved wife Vettia Primitiva and for himself.

EDCS-21400077.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Threpte made this for Urbicus Gaius, who lived 40 years, a well-deserving comrade.
EDCS-21400077.summary: This is a tombstone for Urbicus Gaius, set up by his friend Threpte.

EDCS-21400082.text_edition: \n Lucio Mario Maximo Perpetuo Aureliano clarissimo viro praefecto urbi procons(uli) provinciae Asiae II procons(uli) provinciae Africae consuli II fetiali patrono et curatori coloniae Ardeatium dignissimo Dedicatum V Idus Octobres Mario Maximo II Roscio Aeliano consulibus curam agentibus \n
EDCS-21400082.translation: Dedicated on the 11th day before the Ides of October, during the consulship of Lucius Marius Maximus Perpetuus Aurelianus, clarissimus vir, prefect of the city, proconsul of the province of Asia, twice proconsul of the province of Africa, twice consul, fetial, patron and most worthy curator of the colony of Ardea, while Marius Maximus and Roscus Aelianus were consuls and administering affairs.
EDCS-21400082.summary: This dedication records the dedication of a monument or building, possibly related to the administration of the colony of Ardea, during the consulship of Marius Maximus II and Roscus Aelianus.

EDCS-21400084.translation: Seianus, son of Publius, a priest and twice chief magistrate of the Augustales, made this for the fourth time.
EDCS-21400084.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by Seianus, son of Publius, who held priestly and civic offices in the Augustales.

EDCS-21400085.translation: Of Titus Flavius Claudianus, clarissimus vir.
EDCS-21400085.summary: This is a fragment, likely part of a dedication or funerary monument, honoring Titus Flavius Claudianus.

EDCS-21400086.summary: This is a simple dedication, likely for a tomb or a public monument, made by Titus Flavius Euhelpistus.

EDCS-21400087.summary: This is a fragment, likely part of a dedication or funerary monument, honoring Sallustius Paellinianus.

EDCS-21400088.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Aponia Delphis made this for her most cherished husband, Lucius Aponius Chryseros.
EDCS-21400088.summary: This is a tombstone set up by Aponia Delphis for her husband, Lucius Aponius Chryseros.

EDCS-21400089.translation: For Nicon, son of Hesperos, who lived 16 years and 4 months, well-deserving. Made in memory. [Greek text:] Be of good cheer, no one is immortal.
EDCS-21400089.summary: This is a tombstone for Nicon, son of Hesperos, who died at a young age, with an accompanying Greek epitaph reflecting on mortality.

EDCS-21400090.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Mani, father of Septicius.
EDCS-21400090.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Mani, father of Septicius.

EDCS-21400091.translation: Epaphroditus, freedman of Augustus, from his private chambers, made this for Marcus Ulpius Phaedimus, clarissimus vir.
EDCS-21400091.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument set up by Epaphroditus, a freedman of the emperor, for Marcus Ulpius Phaedimus.

EDCS-21400094.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Batusius made this for his well-deserving daughter, who lived 12 years and 8 months.
EDCS-21400094.summary: A funerary dedication by a father to his deceased daughter, providing her age at death.

EDCS-21400095.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Publius(?), Provincial, made this for his well-deserving fellow soldier Campanus, who lived 55 years.
EDCS-21400095.summary: A funerary dedication by a fellow soldier to Campanus, providing his age at death.

EDCS-21400096.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Megalo, the innocent, who lived...
EDCS-21400096.summary: A fragmentary funerary dedication to an individual named Megalo.

EDCS-21400099.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Felica made this for her well-deserving wife Chrysime, who lived 40 years.
EDCS-21400099.summary: A funerary dedication by a wife to her deceased spouse, specifying her age at death.

EDCS-21400100.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. LNVST made this for her well-deserving daughter Abia Cmunna, who lived 1 year, 4 months, and 10 days.
EDCS-21400100.summary: A funerary dedication by a mother to her deceased young daughter, providing her age at death.

EDCS-21400103.translation: The remains of ashes committed to this tomb rest. To the sacred Metrobie, freedwoman of Augustus, Clotho decreed that you should live for fifteen times ten years; Pandotira provided for you a long life and gave just laws to the people for the better; your mind was full of goodness, fair, not sharp of tongue; your loyalty was blameless and your modesty innocent; no malice could diminish your radiance; your final hour brought your brilliance to its end. Your devoted family mourns you, and the whole family, united in grief, laments. You would believe them all to be parents mourning a child, so equal was the sorrow on their faces. Your wife Iulia stands out above all in her grief, through whom, if it is permissible, to the departed in Elysium. She died on the 10th day before the Kalends of December, when Lucius Arruntius and Titus Flavius Bassus were consuls.
EDCS-21400103.summary: A lengthy epitaph for Metrobie, a freedwoman of Augustus, praising her virtues and lamenting her death, with a specific date provided by the consuls of 31 BCE.

EDCS-21400104.translation: To Apollo and the Nitrodian Nymphs. Gaius Metilius Alcimus willingly paid his vow. Menippos, a hypalpinal physician, dedicated his vow to the Nitrodian Nymphs and Apollo.
EDCS-21400104.summary: A bilingual dedication (Latin and Greek) of a vow to Apollo and the Nymphs, made by a Roman citizen and a Greek physician.

EDCS-21400105.translation: Argenne Poppaea, freedwoman of the deified Augustus, gave her vow willingly to Apollo and the Nymphs.
EDCS-21400105.summary: A dedication of a vow to Apollo and the Nymphs by Argenne Poppaea, a freedwoman of Emperor Augustus.

EDCS-21400107.translation: [...]vius Leitus willingly paid his vow to the Nitrodian Nymphs.
EDCS-21400107.summary: A dedication of a vow to the Nitrodian Nymphs by an individual whose name is partially lost.

EDCS-21400108.translation: Folia Herois gave this gift to the Nymphs. Marcus Diomedes saw to its care.
EDCS-21400108.summary: A dedication of a gift to the Nymphs, with Marcus Diomedes mentioned as the curator.

EDCS-21400109.translation: Aulus Avianius Cilo willingly and deservedly paid his vow to the Nymphs.
EDCS-21400109.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aulus Avianius Cilo to the Nymphs, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-21400110.translation: Aurelius Monnus with his family gave this gift, along with his dependents.
EDCS-21400110.summary: This is a dedication by Aurelius Monnus and his household to the Nymphs, recorded on an unspecified monument.

EDCS-21400111.translation: Capellina willingly paid her vow to the Nymphs.
EDCS-21400111.summary: This is a votive inscription by Capellina to the Nymphs.

EDCS-21400112.translation: Publius Dasimius Primus paid his vow to the Nymphs.
EDCS-21400112.summary: This is a votive inscription by Publius Dasimius Primus to the Nymphs.

EDCS-21400114.translation: Marcus Octavius Alexander willingly and deservedly paid his vow to the Nymphs.
EDCS-21400114.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Octavius Alexander to the Nymphs, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-21400115.translation: Lucius Rantius, son of Lucius, of the Tromentina tribe, to the Nymphs. Leǔkios Rhantios, son of Leǔkios, to the Nymphs.
EDCS-21400115.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Lucius Rantius to the Nymphs, featuring both a Latin and a Greek version of the text.

EDCS-21400116.translation: Titus Turranius Dionysius gave a gift to the Nymphs.
EDCS-21400116.summary: This is a dedication by Titus Turranius Dionysius to the Nymphs.

EDCS-21400118.translation: To the divine shades. To the well-deserving Venera, of Asian origin, a centurion of the 3rd Legion, who lived 44 years, 10 months, and 20 days, having completed 26 years of service. His wife Claudia Marcellina and his son Tiberius Claudius Pison made this for him.
EDCS-21400118.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tiberius Claudius Pison, a centurion, set up by his wife and son, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-21700009.translation: Sacred to the divine shades. Funisulana Helpis to her patron, Lucius Funisulanus Pius, VIvir Augustalis, who deserved well, to the one who deserved well.
EDCS-21700009.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Funisulana Helpis to her patron, Lucius Funisulanus Pius, a member of the college of VIviri Augustales.

EDCS-21700010.translation: To the divine shades. Tyche, the freedwoman, made this for her deserving husband, Lucius Faenius Ursio, a thurarius.
EDCS-21700010.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Faenius Ursio, a thurarius, set up by his freedwoman wife, Tyche.

EDCS-21700011.translation: To the departed spirits. Cominii Glyptus, Marcellina, and others [dedicated this to] their excellent mother, Albia Irena.
EDCS-21700011.summary: A funerary dedication to Albia Irena by her family, likely a tombstone inscription from the Imperial period.

EDCS-21700012.translation: Marcus Antonius Bathyllus, freedman of Augusta; Antonia Lais, freedwoman of Augusta; and Marcus Antonius Teres, freedman of Marcus, [dedicated this] for themselves and their families.
EDCS-21700012.summary: A funerary dedication for multiple individuals, possibly a tomb inscription for a household of freedmen and their patrons, from the Imperial period.

EDCS-21700013.translation: For the memory of Salluvia Naevilla, most dutiful daughter. Her parents, Geminus and Artemis, [dedicated this].
EDCS-21700013.summary: A funerary dedication for Salluvia Naevilla, set up by her parents, Geminus and Artemis, likely a tombstone from the Imperial period.

EDCS-21700018.translation: Marcus G[---] for himself [---] and for Celsus [---] lived [---] years.
EDCS-21700018.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning Marcus and Celsus, likely a tombstone from the Imperial period.

EDCS-21700019.summary: A simple tomb inscription identifying the deceased as Geladinus, a seller, from the Roman period.

EDCS-21700028.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate III consul III pater patriae refecit\n
EDCS-21700028.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, restored [this].
EDCS-21700028.summary: An inscription recording the restoration of a structure by Emperor Trajan, dated to his 3rd consulship and 3rd tribunician power, likely early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-21700033.translation: Emperor Nerva Caesar Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, arranged for this to be made. [Area] 42.
EDCS-21700033.summary: An inscription recording Emperor Nerva commissioning a construction project, dated to his 3rd consulship and tribunician power, likely 97-98 AD.

EDCS-21700034.translation: [Emperor Trajan] Dacicus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 10th time, emperor for the 5th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, arranged for [this] to be restored. [Area] 42.
EDCS-21700034.summary: An inscription recording Emperor Trajan arranging for a restoration, dated to his 5th consulship and 10th tribunician power, likely 105 AD.

EDCS-21700035.translation: 17. Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 14th time, emperor for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, paved [it] with stone from his own funds. 49.
EDCS-21700035.summary: An inscription detailing Emperor Trajan paving an area with his own funds, dated to his 6th acclamation as emperor and 5th consulship, his 14th tribunician power, likely 110 AD.

EDCS-21700037.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Nerva Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II consul III pater patriae viam a Tripontio ad forum Appii ex glarea silice sternendam sua pecunia incohavit Imperator Caesar Nerva divi Nervae filius Traianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate consul II pater patriae consummavit\n
EDCS-21700037.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, began the road from Tripontius to the Forum Appii, to be paved with stone from gravel, with his own funds. Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holding tribunician power, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, completed [it].
EDCS-21700037.summary: An inscription recording the initiation and completion of road construction between Tripontius and the Forum Appii, initiated by Emperor Nerva and completed by Emperor Trajan, dated to Trajan's 2nd consulship, likely 98 AD.

EDCS-21700038.text_edition: \n Imperator Nerva Caesar Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis consul III pater patriae faciendam curavit XLIII\n
EDCS-21700038.translation: Emperor Nerva Caesar Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, consul for the third time, father of the fatherland, saw to its construction (line 43).
EDCS-21700038.summary: This inscription records the construction of a public work initiated by Emperor Nerva, indicated by the line number XLIII.

EDCS-21700039.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Nerva Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II consul III pater patriae sua pecunia incohavit Imperator Caesar Nerva divi Nervae filius Traianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate consul II pater patriae consummavit XL\n
EDCS-21700039.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the second time, consul for the third time, father of the fatherland, began this with his own money. Emperor Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, consul for the second time, father of the fatherland, completed this (line 40).
EDCS-21700039.summary: This inscription details the initiation and completion of a project by Emperors Nerva and Trajan, funded by the emperors themselves.

EDCS-21700040.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVII imperator VI consul VI pater patriae faciundum curavit\n
EDCS-21700040.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the seventeenth time, imperator for the sixth time, consul for the sixth time, father of the fatherland, saw to its construction.
EDCS-21700040.summary: This inscription records the construction of a work commissioned by Emperor Trajan during his seventeenth tribunician power and sixth consulship.

EDCS-21700041.translation: Emperor Nerva Caesar Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, consul for the third time, father of the fatherland, saw to its construction (line 44).
EDCS-21700041.summary: This inscription records the construction of a public work initiated by Emperor Nerva, indicated by the line number XLIV.

EDCS-21700046.translation: To our Lord Emperor Marcus Valerius Maxentius, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Augustus.
EDCS-21700046.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Maxentius, a common honorific inscription for Roman emperors.

EDCS-21700047.translation: To our Lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus...
EDCS-21700047.summary: This is an incomplete dedication to Emperor Constantine.

EDCS-21700048.translation: To our Lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Augustus, son of the deified Constantius (line 50).
EDCS-21700048.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantine, mentioning his father Constantius, and possibly related to the line number L.

EDCS-21700051.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIIII imperator VI consul V pater patriae sua pecunia fecit\n
EDCS-21700051.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the fourteenth time, imperator for the sixth time, consul for the fifth time, father of the fatherland, made this with his own money.
EDCS-21700051.summary: This inscription records a project built with the emperor's own funds by Trajan during his fourteenth tribunician power and fifth consulship.

EDCS-21700082.translation: To our Lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Augustus, son of the deified Constantius.
EDCS-21700082.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantine, son of the deified Constantius.

EDCS-21700093.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the ninth time, imperator for the fourth time, consul for the fifth time, father of the fatherland, restored this.
EDCS-21700093.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a monument by Emperor Trajan during his ninth tribunician power and fifth consulship.

EDCS-21700094.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, restored the bridge work, which had collapsed from age.
EDCS-21700094.summary: This is a building inscription by Emperor Antoninus Pius, dating to his fourth tribunician power and third consulship (around 138-145 CE), recording the restoration of a bridge.

EDCS-21800047.summary: A very fragmentary inscription, likely a request or petition related to an aedile named Marcellus.

EDCS-21800048.summary: A very brief inscription, possibly identifying an aedile named Pansa.

EDCS-21800054.translation: Anyone [---] VII [---] may the dog beware.
EDCS-21800054.summary: A fragmentary inscription with a warning about a dog, possibly from a private property boundary.

EDCS-21800057.summary: A very brief inscription identifying Emperor Otho, likely part of a larger monument.

EDCS-21800058.summary: A fragmentary inscription beginning with the name Publius Sentius, likely a dedication or ownership marker.

EDCS-21800061.translation: Names of Nychi(?): genice, thietice, dotice, onomastice, phyrriche, Byzantice, Cretice, dynastice, gymnice, Chizecae, patagricae, onagricae.
EDCS-21800061.summary: This inscription lists various types of names or rhetorical styles, possibly related to education or performance, associated with 'Nychi'.

EDCS-21800062.summary: A short greeting from Iucundus Sinurini, possibly a personal inscription or marker.

EDCS-21800063.translation: Faustus Neronianus (won) 6 fights at the amphitheater; Priscus Neronianus (won) 18 fights (and) perished; Herennius (freedman) (won) 18 fights (and) perished.
EDCS-21800063.summary: An inscription recording the successes and deaths of gladiators named Faustus, Priscus, and Herennius, likely associated with an amphitheater.

EDCS-21800064.translation: Asteropaeus Ner(onianus) won 107 fights; Oceneanus (freedman?) was let off (sent off) after 6 fights.
EDCS-21800064.summary: An inscription listing the records of two gladiators, Asteropaeus and Oceneanus, detailing their victories and fates in fights.

EDCS-21800065.translation: Spiculus the Neronian won, as a recruit. Aptonetus, his freedman, died at 16.
EDCS-21800065.summary: This epitaph records the death of Aptonetus, freedman of the gladiator Spiculus, and commemorates Spiculus's victory.

EDCS-21800067.summary: This inscription likely records the age or a score associated with a person named Panthacathus.

EDCS-21800071.translation: If any spectator of the stage has noted the performances of Serpentis, young Sepumius, whether you are a lover of horses or a connoisseur of the theatre, may you always have fair scales everywhere.
EDCS-21800071.summary: This is an epigrammatic inscription by Sepumius, likely a playwright or performer, addressing spectators and wishing them fairness.

EDCS-21800076.translation: Marcus Lollius Bithus, freedman of Marcus.
EDCS-21800076.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription identifying Marcus Lollius Bithus and his patron.

EDCS-21800095.text_edition: \n Theorius est Holconi nec tamen Mazgaba amicus est Holconi nec tamen Pyrrichus\n
EDCS-21800095.translation: Theorius belongs to Holconius, but Pyrrhicus does not belong to Holconius, nor does Mazgaba.
EDCS-21800095.summary: This inscription records ownership or affiliation, possibly in a social or legal context, concerning Theorius, Holconius, Mazgaba, and Pyrrhicus.

EDCS-21800103.summary: This inscription is a crude epitaph or statement of personal attribute for someone named Phoebus.

EDCS-21800107.summary: This inscription humorously identifies a place as a 'Labyrinth' and its inhabitant as a 'Minotaur'.

EDCS-21800114.summary: This is a partial inscription identifying a person, likely Marcus Valerius Julianus, potentially on a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-21800122.translation: I do not want [to be] with Myrtale. Whoever wants [to] may have [it]. Oceanus, come drink. This is not mine, not yours, it is two. Go out! I was here. Beggar, I was here. Go outside, stop fighting!
EDCS-21800122.summary: This inscription contains a series of fragmented, colloquial, and potentially vulgar statements, possibly from a tomb or public space, expressing personal sentiments and commands.

EDCS-21800162.translation: Quintus Petronius Octavus, 34 fights, discharged. Severus, freedman, 55, won.
EDCS-21800162.summary: This epitaph records the military careers and victories of Quintus Petronius Octavus and his freedman Severus.

EDCS-21800169.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely concerning leaders involved with wool production or trade.

EDCS-21800182.summary: This inscription records the name Floronius, identified as a member of a sodality (a collegium or association).

EDCS-21800192.translation: She was the mother of Caesar Augustus.
EDCS-21800192.summary: This inscription states that a woman was the mother of Caesar Augustus.

EDCS-21800200.text_edition: \n Lollium aedilem oramus vos faciatis tegettari sic\n
EDCS-21800200.translation: We beg you to make Lollius the aedile, that he may cover himself thus.
EDCS-21800200.summary: This is a plea from citizens asking for Lollius to be appointed aedile, with a cryptic reference to his covering.

EDCS-21800202.text_edition: \n Lucium Popium Luci filium Ampliatum virum bonum aedilem dignum rei publicae oramus vos faciatis Urbulanenses rogamus\n
EDCS-21800202.translation: We, the people of Urbula, ask that you make Lucius Popius Ampliatus, son of Lucius, a good man and an aedile worthy of the state.
EDCS-21800202.summary: This inscription is a public request from the citizens of Urbula for Lucius Popius Ampliatus to be elected as aedile.

EDCS-21800203.text_edition: \n Valentem filium aedilem oro vos faciatis\n
EDCS-21800203.translation: I beg you to make Valentem, the son, the aedile.
EDCS-21800203.summary: This inscription is a personal plea for Valentem to be appointed aedile.

EDCS-21800215.translation: Cicuta, from the accounts of Nero Augustus.
EDCS-21800215.summary: This inscription identifies Cicuta in relation to the financial accounts of Emperor Nero.

EDCS-21800250.summary: This inscription records the name Chloe, possibly a freedwoman, with a cognomen that might indicate her status or origin.

EDCS-21800251.summary: This is a simple inscription recording the name Gaius Voconius.

EDCS-21800252.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording the name Lucius Abellanus Epidius, possibly with a title or role.

EDCS-21800255.summary: A funerary inscription identifying Surus and his father Holconius, likely from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-21800263.text_edition: \n Thiophilus sic canis nnm lingere noli puellis in muro\n
EDCS-21800263.translation: Theophilus: do not lick the dog on the wall.
EDCS-21800263.summary: A playful or cautionary graffito, possibly from a public space, warning against touching a dog depicted on a wall.

EDCS-21800352.translation: Marcus Attilius, the recruit, won. Hilarus Neronia nus, 14 fights, 13 victories, was discharged.
EDCS-21800352.summary: A gladiatorial epitaph recording the victories and honorable discharge of Hilarus Neronia nus, who fought under Marcus Attilius, dating to the Neronian period.

EDCS-21800380.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 6th time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, completed the work begun by the deified Nerva, his father.
EDCS-21800380.summary: An imperial dedication by Emperor Trajan, stating he completed a public work initiated by his adoptive father, the deified Nerva, dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-21800384.translation: To our lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus, pious, fortunate, unconquered Augustus, son of the deified Constantine Pius.
EDCS-21800384.summary: A dedication to Emperor Constantine I, hailing him with imperial titles and emphasizing his divine lineage, likely from the Tetrarchic or Constantinian period.

EDCS-21800387.translation: To our lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus, pious, fortunate, unconquered Augustus, son of the deified Constantine Pius. [3]XVIII
EDCS-21800387.summary: A dedication to Emperor Constantine I, similar to the previous one, with an added numeral possibly indicating a regnal year or an ordinal number, dating to his reign (306-337 AD).

EDCS-21800393.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Traianus Hadrianus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 3rd time, made this.
EDCS-21800393.summary: A monumental inscription by Emperor Hadrian, recording his titles and his role in constructing a public work, dating to his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-21900002.translation: Emperor Caesar, Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, Germanicus maximus, Dacicus maximus, Sarmaticus maximus, with tribunician power for the 4th time, imperator for the 5th time, consul, proconsul, father of the fatherland, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, most noble Caesar, Germanicus maximus, Dacicus maximus, Sarmaticus maximus, princeps of youth, took care to have this constructed from the foundations with a marine embankment, as the shore near the Severian road, battered by the constant waves of the sea and by the ruinous collapse, was weakened, so that danger would be absent for those travelling.
EDCS-21900002.summary: A joint dedication by Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Maximus Caesar, to reinforce a coastal road against sea erosion, dating to their reign (235-238 AD).

EDCS-21900006.translation: Emperor Caesar, grandson of the deified Severus Pius, son of the deified Magnus Antoninus Pius, Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 4th time, consul designated for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, restored the bridge over the Liris, dilapidated from age, with his own funds.
EDCS-21900006.summary: An imperial dedication by Emperor Alexander Severus, recording the restoration of a bridge over the Liris river, dating to his reign (222-235 AD).

EDCS-22900959.translation: The bones of Lucius Valerius Primus, son of Lucius, of the Pollia tribe, soldier of the 8th Praetorian cohort. Titus Matinius Hymenaeus, son of Titus, made this for his comrade.
EDCS-22900959.summary: A funerary monument erected by Titus Matinius Hymenaeus for his fellow soldier Lucius Valerius Primus, a member of the Praetorian Guard, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-22901620.translation: From the pottery works of Asinia Quadrata, made by Caius Nunnius Fortunatus; dedicated to Severus and Stloga, consuls.
EDCS-22901620.summary: This is a pottery stamp from the workshop of Asinia Quadrata, likely dating to the consulship of Severus and Stloga.

EDCS-22901657.summary: This is a simple name inscription, possibly a tombstone or ownership mark.
