EDCS-24300426.summary: This is an electoral request for Gaius Cuspium Pansam to be made aedile, made by Purpurio and the Paridiani.

EDCS-24300459.summary: This is a dedication to Gaius Calventius Sittius Magnus, likely on a public monument, from the 1st century BCE.

EDCS-24300486.translation: I ask you to make Lucius Maesonius duovir iure dicundo.
EDCS-24300486.summary: This is a popular request for Lucius Maesonius to be appointed as duovir iure dicundo (a chief magistrate with judicial power), likely dating from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-24300488.summary: This names Gaius Calventius Sittius Magnus, possibly as a recipient of an honor or as a dedication, from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-24300497.translation: The people of Tibur request Lucius Popidius Secundus as aedile.
EDCS-24300497.summary: This inscription records a popular request for Lucius Popidius Secundus to be appointed as aedile, a magistrate responsible for public buildings and games, by the citizens of Tibur.

EDCS-24300560.translation: I ask you to make Gavia Rufus duovir iure dicundo.
EDCS-24300560.summary: This is a popular request for Gavius Rufus to be appointed as duovir iure dicundo, likely dating from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-24300568.translation: Gaius Calventius Sittius Magnus, duovir iure dicundo, worthy of the state.
EDCS-24300568.summary: This inscription honors Gaius Calventius Sittius Magnus as a duovir iure dicundo and states his worthiness to public office, probably from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-24300578.translation: The spectators ask for Holconius Priscus, duovir iure dicundo, for the games.
EDCS-24300578.summary: This inscription records a popular request for Holconius Priscus to serve as duovir iure dicundo, especially in relation to providing spectacles, likely from the early Empire.

EDCS-24300599.translation: I ask you to make Gaius Gavius Rufus duovir iure dicundo; he requests it.
EDCS-24300599.summary: This inscription is a popular request for Gaius Gavius Rufus to be appointed duovir iure dicundo, with the added statement that he himself is requesting it, likely from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-24300614.text_edition: \n Caium Iulium Polybium aedilem viis aedibus sacris publicis procurandis Lanternari tene scalam\n
EDCS-24300614.translation: The Lanternari (lamp makers) ask Gaius Iulius Polybius to be aedile for the care of public roads, buildings, and sacred sites.
EDCS-24300614.summary: This inscription records a request by the guild of lamp makers for Gaius Iulius Polybius to serve as aedile, responsible for public works and religious sites, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-24300638.translation: I ask you to make Lucius Ceius Secundus duovir.
EDCS-24300638.summary: This is a popular request for Lucius Ceius Secundus to be appointed duovir (a chief magistrate), likely from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-24300644.translation: Citizens, I ask you to make Ceius a duovir.
EDCS-24300644.summary: This is a proposal for the election of Ceius as a duovir, likely from Pompeii.

EDCS-24300660.translation: To Popidius Rufus, a duovir worthy of the colony of Pompeii.
EDCS-24300660.summary: This inscription honors Popidius Rufus as a duovir, deeming him worthy of Pompeii.

EDCS-24300662.text_edition: \n Aulum Trebium Valentem aedilem Cerialis Acratopinon cum Cassia rogat\n
EDCS-24300662.translation: Cerialis Acratopinon, with Cassia, asks you to make Aulus Trebius Valens an aedile.
EDCS-24300662.summary: This inscription is a request by Cerialis Acratopinon and Cassia to elect Aulus Trebius Valens as an aedile.

EDCS-24300673.translation: I ask you, citizens, to make Lucius Popidius an aedile, Secundus the innocent.
EDCS-24300673.summary: This is a plea to elect Lucius Popidius, also known as Secundus, as an aedile.

EDCS-24300676.translation: I ask you, citizens, to make Pansa, a young and most virtuous aedile, worthy of the state.
EDCS-24300676.summary: This is a plea to elect Pansa as an aedile, highlighting his youth, virtue, and worthiness for public office.

EDCS-24300678.translation: To Gaius Calventius Sittius Magnus, duovir exercising judicial power.
EDCS-24300678.summary: This inscription identifies Gaius Calventius Sittius Magnus as a duovir with judicial authority.

EDCS-24300702.translation: To Lucius Sextilius Syrticus, duovir exercising judicial power, Pinarius [---].
EDCS-24300702.summary: This inscription names Lucius Sextilius Syrticus as a duovir with judicial authority, with a partially preserved mention of Pinarius.

EDCS-24300728.translation: Citizens, I ask you to make Ceius Secundus a duovir.
EDCS-24300728.summary: This is a proposal for the election of Ceius Secundus as a duovir, likely from Pompeii.

EDCS-24300744.translation: Citizens, I ask you to make Gaius Gavius Rufus a duovir.
EDCS-24300744.summary: This is a plea to elect Gaius Gavius Rufus as a duovir.

EDCS-24300753.summary: This incomplete inscription likely honors Lucius Nonius Severus in some official capacity.

EDCS-24300756.translation: Lucius Sextilius Syrticus, duumvir with judicial authority.
EDCS-24300756.summary: This is an inscription honoring Lucius Sextilius Syrticus for his role as duumvir, a high-ranking magistrate in a Roman municipality, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-24300757.translation: Lucius Nonius Severus, worthy aedile for the state.
EDCS-24300757.summary: This inscription praises Lucius Nonius Severus for his service as an aedile, a magistrate responsible for public buildings and games, probably dating to the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-24300771.text_edition: \n Caium Cuspium Pansam aedilem Floronius rogat sodalis\n
EDCS-24300771.translation: Floronius, a fellow member, requests Caius Cusprius Pansa to be aedile.
EDCS-24300771.summary: This text is a personal request from Floronius to his fellow member, Caius Cusprius Pansa, to become an aedile, dating likely to the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-24300785.text_edition: \n Ceium Secundum IIvirum iure dicundo Veneriosi rogant iuvenem\n
EDCS-24300785.translation: The Veneriosi request Ceius Secundus, a young man, to be a duumvir with judicial authority.
EDCS-24300785.summary: This inscription records a request from a group identified as the 'Veneriosi' for Ceius Secundus to serve as a duumvir, a high municipal magistrate, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-24300835.text_edition: \n Vettium Firmum aedilem quactiliarii sic rogant\n
EDCS-24300835.translation: The quactiliarii request Vettius Firmus to be aedile.
EDCS-24300835.summary: This is a request from a group called the 'quactiliarii' for Vettius Firmus to assume the office of aedile, a municipal magistrate, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-24300838.text_edition: \n Caium Iulium Polybium IIvirum Cuculla rogat\n
EDCS-24300838.translation: Cuculla requests Caius Iulius Polybius to be a duumvir.
EDCS-24300838.summary: This inscription is a personal request from 'Cuculla' for Caius Iulius Polybius to serve as a duumvir, a chief magistrate in a Roman municipality, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-24300847.translation: I ask you, make Cuspianus an aedile.
EDCS-24300847.summary: This is a plea for Cuspianus to be appointed or elected as an aedile, a municipal official, likely dating from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-24300886.translation: The 7th day before the Ides of November. Four beams. Tertius.
EDCS-24300886.summary: This is a brief record possibly noting a construction or repair project involving four beams, dated to November 5th, and attributed to Tertius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-24300888.summary: This inscription appears to be a simple count or tally, possibly related to goods or labor, with the number six being the final entry, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-24400391.translation: Emperor Caesar Augustus Servius Galba, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul designate for the second time, granted honorable discharge and citizenship to veterans who served in the First Adiutrix Legion, to them, their children, and their descendants, and the right to marry wives whom they had at the time citizenship was granted, or if they were unmarried, to marry women whom they would later marry, provided they married only one woman each. This was on the 11th day before the Kalends of January, when Gaius Bellicus Natale and Publius Cornelius Scipio were consuls. [This document was] transcribed and reviewed from a tablet which is fixed in Rome on the Capitol at the altar. Gaius Iulius Agrippa of Apamea; Gaius Iulius Sacerdos of Antioch; Lucius Velina Nauta of Antioch; Tiberius Claudius Chaerea of Antioch; Lucius Cornelius Optatus of Antioch; Lucius Secura Alexandrus, veteran; Marcus Valerius Diodorus, veteran.
EDCS-24400391.summary: This is an official document from Emperor Servius Galba, dated to December 22nd, 68 AD, recording the grant of honorable discharge and citizenship to veterans of the First Adiutrix Legion, with the text also listing witnesses and individuals from Antioch and veterans, possibly for enrollment or verification.

EDCS-24501559.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, possibly a dedication or an epitaph, with uncertain reading.

EDCS-24501685.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a personal motto or a comment on writing.

EDCS-24501723.translation: Successus the weaver loves Hiredem, Coponia's slave, but she does not care for him; rather, she pities him. A rival writes: farewell, you envious one, do not break yourself wanting someone more beautiful. And he is the wickedest and cutest man.
EDCS-24501723.summary: This is a humorous epitaph or a graffito written by a rival about a weaver's unrequited love, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-24600150.translation: I scorn, I mock your Latona, Salvilla.
EDCS-24600150.summary: This is a short inscription expressing contempt for someone named Salvilla and her association with Latona, possibly of late antique or early medieval date.

EDCS-24600289.summary: This inscription records a saying or proverb attributed to someone who lived to the age of 109, likely a personal memorial or a wisdom saying.

EDCS-24600291.translation: Oliva buried on the 17th day before the Kalends of November.
EDCS-24600291.summary: This inscription records the burial date of someone named Oliva.

EDCS-24600293.translation: On the 17th day before the Kalends of February, Eu[por(?)] all 20.
EDCS-24600293.summary: This inscription likely records the date of an event involving 20 people, possibly a group burial or a commemoration.

EDCS-24600294.translation: On the 11th day before the Kalends of February, Eupor, all 20.
EDCS-24600294.summary: This inscription likely records the date of an event involving 20 people, possibly a group burial or a commemoration.

EDCS-24600302.summary: This inscription appears to record numbers and a personal name, possibly related to inventory or a count.

EDCS-24600306.translation: To Decimus Lucretius, happily to his children.
EDCS-24600306.summary: This is a dedication, likely an epitaph, wishing happiness to the children of Decimus Lucretius.

EDCS-24600307.translation: Gulus to Publius Prothymius, greetings to Aemilius Prothymius.
EDCS-24600307.summary: This is a personal greeting between individuals, likely a letter fragment.

EDCS-24600308.translation: To Marcus Atutius, son of Timelius.
EDCS-24600308.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Marcus Atutius, likely on a tombstone or monument.

EDCS-24600316.translation: Priscus the engraver wishes Campanus the gem-cutter good fortune.
EDCS-24600316.summary: This is a message of good wishes from one craftsman, Priscus, to another, Campanus.

EDCS-24600349.translation: Caius Vesturius, the aedile, to Caesi.
EDCS-24600349.summary: This inscription likely records a public building or dedication made by the aedile Caius Vesturius.

EDCS-24600351.summary: This appears to be a personal dedication or message from Apsyrtus to Valentina.

EDCS-24600433.summary: This is a religious dedication to the Great Mother, specifically referencing her Eleusinian aspect.

EDCS-24600449.translation: The sator Arepo holds the wheels in his work.
EDCS-24600449.summary: This is a famous magic square or palindrome inscription, likely intended for protection or as a word puzzle.

EDCS-24600480.translation: Quartio Stratus Venustus, Marcus Licinius Saturninus.
EDCS-24600480.summary: This inscription lists names, possibly of a family or individuals associated with a property or monument.

EDCS-24600522.translation: Rufus to Rufus, son of Nonius Felicioni.
EDCS-24600522.summary: This inscription is likely a dedication or memorial, with a repetition of the name Rufus.

EDCS-24600524.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication to a person named Musicus.

EDCS-24600526.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely naming Lucius Acutius and possibly his office as 'magister'.

EDCS-24600532.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that appears to name Quintus Antonius.

EDCS-24600533.summary: This inscription records the name Quintus Antonius and his office as 'magister'.

EDCS-24600534.summary: This fragmentary inscription records the name Publius Coelius.

EDCS-24600542.summary: This is a highly fragmentary inscription that likely contains the name Sentius.

EDCS-24600545.summary: This inscription records the name Publius Siculus and his freedwoman Hermia.

EDCS-24600546.translation: Vettius, Optatus (perhaps a freedman) licked the...? (obscure).
EDCS-24600546.summary: This is a very fragmentary and obscure inscription mentioning Vettius and Optatus.

EDCS-24600776.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. For Ragonia Zmaragdida, daughter of Publius, who lived 27 years, 3 months, and 8 days. Publius Ragonius Fortunatus (dedicated this) to his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-24600776.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ragonia Zmaragdida, dedicated by her husband Publius Ragonius Fortunatus.

EDCS-24600777.translation: Dulcissimus, who lived [3] years, and for himself and Concordia [---].
EDCS-24600777.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for a person or animal named Dulcissimus, and possibly also for Concordia.

EDCS-24700041.text_edition: \n Dexter Veneri Opsequenti sic libens merito donavit\n
EDCS-24700041.translation: Dexter willingly and deservedly dedicated this to Venus the Compliant.
EDCS-24700041.summary: This is a dedication of an object or image to the goddess Venus Opsequens by Dexter.

EDCS-24700042.text_edition: \n Manio Sabidio Mani filio legato cives Romani in Sicilia Panhormi qui negotiantur\n
EDCS-24700042.translation: To Manius Sabidius, son of Manius, Roman citizens trading in Panormus, Sicily.
EDCS-24700042.summary: This is a dedication to Manius Sabidius, a Roman citizen, by other Roman citizens who were merchants in Panormus, Sicily.

EDCS-24700058.translation: Gnaeus Arbuxseius Bassus, son of Gnaeus, has given this gift to Diana, willingly and deservedly. Lucius Lucretius Sedulus, son of Lucius, has given this gift to Diana, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-24700058.summary: This inscription records two separate dedications of gifts to the goddess Diana by Gnaeus Arbuxseius Bassus and Lucius Lucretius Sedulus.

EDCS-24700133.text_edition: \n Lucius Metilius Publius Gavius Publi filius praetores senatus consulto Publius Venerius\n
EDCS-24700133.translation: Lucius Metilius and Publius Gavius, son of Publius, as praetors, by decree of the senate, Publius Venerius.
EDCS-24700133.summary: This inscription mentions Lucius Metilius and Publius Gavius, who were praetors, and refers to a decree of the senate involving Publius Venerius.

EDCS-24700134.text_edition: \n Caius Viscius Marci filius Marcus Curius Cai filius IIviri viam lapide sternendam pedum CDXIIII ex decreto decurionum pecunia publica pretio |(denariorum) V assium III faciendum locaverunt idemque probaverunt\n
EDCS-24700134.translation: Gaius Viscius, son of Marcus, and Marcus Curius, son of Caius, as duovirs, by decree of the decurions and at public expense, contracted for and approved the paving of a road 414 feet long, for the price of 5 denarii and 3 asses.
EDCS-24700134.summary: This inscription records the work of two duovirs, Gaius Viscius and Marcus Curius, who contracted for and approved the paving of a road with public funds.

EDCS-24700196.text_edition: \n liberta ca a Marci liberta Leon Luci liberta Habra liberta Humnis sic a Marci liberta Pamphila Caedici Cai serva alium Thais ina p iea co\n
EDCS-24700196.translation: Liberta (female freedwoman) of Marcus, Liberta Leon, Liberta Habra, daughter of Lucius, Liberta Humnis, and Liberta Pamphila, daughter of Caedicius, slave Thais, daughter of Alius, in this place.
EDCS-24700196.summary: This inscription lists several freedwomen and a slave, possibly recording their ownership or association with individuals named Marcus, Lucius, Caedicius, and Alius.

EDCS-24700664.summary: This is a simple dedication or naming inscription for Publius Sittius Vappa.

EDCS-24700667.summary: This inscription appears to be a short, possibly fragmentary, dedication or statement involving the word 'mandatus' (mandate/commissioned).

EDCS-24700668.translation: Ratio, of Marcus Ugoni, on the day before the Nones.
EDCS-24700668.summary: This inscription records a name, Ratio, and her relation to Marcus Ugoni, with a date reference.

EDCS-24700670.summary: This is a greeting inscription from Lucilius to Lucida, expressing good wishes.

EDCS-24700675.summary: This inscription appears to record the name Felicula and her occupation or action of selling.

EDCS-24700676.translation: I will place on my shoulders, accustomed to arms, the offerings owed to you, Venus Syntrophus, if you confirm my prosperous vows, and may you increase them.
EDCS-24700676.summary: This is a votive inscription to Venus Syntrophus, likely from an individual seeking continued favor and prosperity.

EDCS-24700677.summary: This is a tombstone inscription commemorating Sabidia, set up by Abonius.

EDCS-24700680.summary: This is likely a tombstone inscription or ownership mark for Aulus Licinius.

EDCS-24700681.summary: This inscription seems to record the unfortunate arrival or demise of a person named Iucundus.

EDCS-24700683.summary: This inscription is a personal greeting or dedication by Lucilius, wishing health to all.

EDCS-24700694.summary: This is a brief inscription possibly indicating possession or responsibility held by Beliatus.

EDCS-24700701.translation: Lucius Statius Modestus and Quintus Postumius.
EDCS-24700701.summary: This inscription likely records the names of two individuals, possibly as dedicators, property owners, or deceased persons.

EDCS-24700704.summary: This inscription consists of multiple instances of the name Severus, possibly indicating a family or multiple individuals with that name.

EDCS-24700708.summary: This inscription records the name Lucius Decidius along with the number thirty, which could refer to an age, a quantity, or a payment.

EDCS-24700709.summary: This is an inscription identifying Publius Cornelius Faventinus by name and profession as a barber.

EDCS-24700716.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young girl named Mula.

EDCS-24700721.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication or a private transaction, possibly relating to a person named Xomanus and Homulo.

EDCS-24700724.summary: This inscription appears to be a personal identification, possibly on a tombstone or a dedication by Quintus Vesuvius.

EDCS-24700728.summary: This inscription likely refers to the ownership or dedication by Nericulus Albus.

EDCS-24700729.summary: This inscription names two individuals, Antiochus and Actius, possibly as dedicants or subjects of the monument.

EDCS-24700730.summary: This inscription identifies Anthus as a 'destillator' (likely a distiller or perfumer) in the service of Cossinus.

EDCS-24700732.summary: This inscription appears to be a personal name, Marcus Magonius Vesbinus, possibly on a tomb or dedication.

EDCS-24700736.summary: This inscription mentions Auctus twice and refers to taxes, possibly recording an official or a transaction related to tax collection.

EDCS-24700741.summary: This inscription names two individuals, Liberalis and Prima, possibly as dedicants or recipients of a monument.

EDCS-24700742.translation: Lucius Istacydius with Primigenia, here Prima, follow Volumnius.
EDCS-24700742.summary: This inscription records a familial or social relationship, mentioning Lucius Istacydius, Primigenia, Prima, and Volumnius, possibly in a funerary context.

EDCS-24700743.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or mention of two individuals, Primigenia and Prima.

EDCS-24700745.summary: This inscription records Liberalis and likely Rufinus, possibly in a dedicatory or memorial context.

EDCS-24700746.summary: This inscription names Dorcas, identifying her parent as Largus Atius, likely a dedication or family record.

EDCS-24700747.translation: Prima, daughter of Quintus Quintus Quintus (?).
EDCS-24700747.summary: This inscription records Prima and her father, identified by a repeated patronymic, likely a dedication or family record.

EDCS-24700748.summary: This inscription mentions Dorcas and Sollemine, possibly indicating their presence or a shared dedication.

EDCS-24700750.summary: This inscription is a simple farewell or greeting to Iucunda.

EDCS-24700755.summary: This inscription expresses affection from Amicus towards Viccia.

EDCS-24700759.summary: This inscription records a weight of 24 pounds and names Mopsus, possibly related to a transaction or delivery.

EDCS-24700760.translation: Liberalis, Liberalis, Liberalis, we have come.
EDCS-24700760.summary: This inscription humorously repeats the name Liberalis, indicating their arrival.

EDCS-24700763.summary: This inscription offers greetings to both Amicus and Umbricia.

EDCS-24700765.translation: Narcissus the thirsty, Narcissus the thirsty.
EDCS-24700765.summary: This is a brief personal inscription, possibly a graffiti, of Narcissus, emphasizing his thirst.

EDCS-24700769.translation: Samnis, how much do you owe Felicio? Greetings.
EDCS-24700769.summary: This is a short inscription, likely a debt reminder or a note, from an unknown sender to Samnis regarding a debt to Felicio.

EDCS-24700770.summary: This inscription records a payment or debt of nine units from Verus to Felicio, possibly on a small object or marker.

EDCS-24700771.translation: Verus, six and a half asses, how much? Verus, three asses.
EDCS-24700771.summary: This inscription appears to be a record of two separate transactions or debts involving Verus and amounts of six and a half asses and three asses.

EDCS-24700772.summary: This inscription likely records a quantity of ten, associated with the name Livius Verus, possibly a debt or a count.

EDCS-24700774.summary: This is a very brief inscription, possibly a farewell or a label, mentioning a 'charioteer' with a question mark indicating uncertainty in the reading.

EDCS-24700780.summary: This is a simple funerary or ownership inscription identifying Pitamnus as the freedman of someone named Marcus.

EDCS-24700781.summary: This inscription, likely a graffito, mentions Severus and an action or state related to Pompeii, with a lacuna obscuring the full meaning.

EDCS-24800028.translation: On the 7th day before the Ides of September, Quintus Postumius asked Aulus Attius to sodomize him.
EDCS-24800028.summary: This is a legal or personal inscription from the Roman Republic or early Empire, recording a request made by Quintus Postumius to Aulus Attius on a specific date.

EDCS-24800029.summary: This inscription is very fragmentary and its meaning is unclear, but it seems to mention the names Auctus and Popidius.

EDCS-24800030.translation: To Ceius and many dolls, a beautiful one.
EDCS-24800030.summary: This is a dedication to the god Ceius and to dolls, likely from a child or admirer of dolls.

EDCS-24800031.summary: This is an ownership inscription indicating possession by Gaius Asinius Proculus.

EDCS-24800035.translation: The Augustalis Pierus Celadus treated Papirius Amandus.
EDCS-24800035.summary: This inscription records a medical treatment provided by Pierus Celadus, an Augustalis, to Papirius Amandus.

EDCS-24800037.summary: This inscription likely records a payment or price of 27 asses associated with someone named Arruntius.

EDCS-24900609.summary: This is a fragmentary ownership or identification inscription likely related to Aurelius Telesphorus.

EDCS-25000563.summary: This inscription contains an uninterpretable sequence of letters, possibly a name or title.

EDCS-25000564.summary: This inscription contains an uninterpretable sequence of letters, possibly a name or title.

EDCS-25000566.summary: This inscription contains an uninterpretable sequence of letters, possibly a name or title with an unusual notation.

EDCS-25100588.summary: This is likely an ownership or identification inscription for Gaius Sextus Vetalis.

EDCS-25101009.translation: 27 and a half asses, allia, Numulus the boy. On the 15th day before the Kalends of January.
EDCS-25101009.summary: This inscription records a payment of 27.5 asses for 'allia' made by Numulus, a boy, on December 18th.

EDCS-25401280.translation: Citizens, I ask you to make Lucius Ceius a duovir; Innullus, his client, asks.
EDCS-25401280.summary: This is a dedication by a client requesting his patron, Lucius Ceius, be appointed duovir.

EDCS-25401281.translation: Hinnulus asks you, together with Papilio, to make Lucius Ceius Secundus a duovir, as he is well deserving.
EDCS-25401281.summary: This is a dedication by Hinnulus and Papilio requesting Lucius Ceius Secundus be appointed duovir.

EDCS-25401283.translation: I ask you to make Lucius Ceius Secundus a duovir for dispensing justice; [---]unus asks.
EDCS-25401283.summary: This is a dedication requesting Lucius Ceius Secundus be appointed duovir for dispensing justice.

EDCS-25401289.translation: I ask you to make Popidius a duovir.
EDCS-25401289.summary: This is a dedication requesting Popidius be appointed duovir.

EDCS-25401294.translation: Lucius Ceius Secundus, duovir for dispensing justice.
EDCS-25401294.summary: This is an inscription naming Lucius Ceius Secundus as duovir for dispensing justice.

EDCS-25401295.translation: I ask you to make Numerius Popidius Rufus aedile.
EDCS-25401295.summary: This is a dedication requesting Numerius Popidius Rufus be appointed aedile.

EDCS-25401299.translation: They ask that Popidius Rufus be made aedile; [---]R[---]n.
EDCS-25401299.summary: This inscription requests that Popidius Rufus be appointed aedile.

EDCS-25401308.translation: I ask you to make Lucius Popidius Secundus aedile.
EDCS-25401308.summary: This is a dedication requesting Lucius Popidius Secundus be appointed aedile.

EDCS-25401311.summary: This inscription names Lucius Ceius Secundus as duovir, followed by an unknown abbreviation.

EDCS-25900656.translation: Marcus Avianius Coniunctus, son of Marcus, duovir for the second time, rebuilt the roof at his own expense. The work specifications are as follows: a law for building a wall in the area opposite the temple of Serapis, across the road, for whoever contracts for it; the contractor must provide guarantors and sign under the supervision of the duovirs. In the area across the road, the wall next to the road, in that wall, the opening of the doorway must be made 6 feet wide and 7 feet high; from that wall, two pilasters facing the sea must project 2 feet long and 1 foot 3/4 inch thick; on top of these, a robust lintel 8 feet long, 1 foot 3/4 inch wide, and 1 foot 7/8 inch high must be placed; on top of this and the pilasters, 2 robust mutules 1/3 of a foot thick and 1 foot high must be projected outwards from the wall on each side; painted simas must be fastened with iron; on top of this, two pine beams 1/2 foot thick must be placed on both sides, fastened with iron, and covered with pine boards 1/3 of a foot thick, not more than 1 foot 1/4 inch; and pine cover boards must be placed, made from a 1-foot-square timber; pine front-boards 1/4 foot wide and 1/2 inch thick must be made and put in place; and a small door must be covered with flat iron; all the front roof tiles must be fastened to the front-boards with iron, and a margin must be added; two barred doors with corner posts made of oak must be made and set up, and sealed with pitch, in the same way as the ones made for the temple of Honos; the outer boundary wall must be made 10 feet high, and the entrance doorway into the area and the windows that are in the wall next to that area must be blocked up, and a continuous margin must be added to the wall that is now there and to the margins; and all those walls and margins that are not plastered with lime must be coated with sand, polished, and whitewashed with wet lime; any masonry work done in the ground must contain one-quarter part of slaked lime, and the aggregate must not weigh more than 15 pounds, and the corner stones must not be higher than 1/3 foot 1/2 inch 1/16 inch; and the place must be cleared for this work; also, any small shrines, altars, and statues that are in the field and are designated must all be removed, transported, arranged, and set up where the place is designated, all under the supervision of the duovirs; this entire work must be done under the supervision of the duovirs and the board of duovirs who are usually in council; at Puteoli, as long as at least twenty are present when the matter is consulted, whatever the twenty sworn men approve shall be approved, and whatever they disapprove shall be disapproved. The work days are the first Kalends of November. Half of the payment will be given when the properties are duly guaranteed, the other half will be paid when the work is completed and approved. Gaius Blossius, son of Quintus, for 1500 sesterces, also guarantees. Quintus Fuficius, son of Quintus; Gnaeus Tetteius, son of Quintus; Gaius Granius, son of Gaius; Tiberius Crassicius.
EDCS-25900656.summary: This is a detailed building contract and specification from Puteoli, likely from the late Republican or early imperial period, outlining the reconstruction of a wall, doorway, and roof by Marcus Avianius Coniunctus, specifying materials, dimensions, and supervisory processes, with financial guarantees provided by several individuals.

EDCS-26400152.summary: This inscription appears to be a list of names or words, possibly a personal note, a list of laborers, or a fragment of a larger text.

EDCS-26400192.translation: May Damoeta grant me these things, happier than Pasiphae. Zosimus wrote this.
EDCS-26400192.summary: This is a personal inscription where Zosimus dedicates something or expresses a wish for Damoeta to grant him happiness, comparing it to the mythical figure Pasiphae.

EDCS-26400247.translation: Here is the house of Papirius Sabinius.
EDCS-26400247.summary: This is a boundary or ownership marker indicating the property of Papirius Sabinius.

EDCS-26400580.translation: 8th day before the Ides: 1 portion of cheese / 8 portions of bread / 3 portions of oil / 3 portions of wine / 7th day before the Ides: / 8 portions of bread / 5 portions of oil / 5 portions of onions / 1 pot / 2 portions of bread for the boy / 2 portions of wine / 6th day before the Ides: 8 portions of bread / 4 portions of bread for the boy / 3 portions of halica / 5th day before the Ides: / 1 portion of wine for the master | (denarius) / 8 portions of bread, 2 portions of wine / 4th day before the Ides: / 1 portion of Hxeres | (denarius) 2 portions of bread / 8 portions of female cheese / 1 portion of triticum | (denarius) / 1 portion of beef shin, 1 palm-sized portion / 1 portion of incense, 2 portions of cheese / 1 sausage / 4 portions of soft cheese / 7 portions of oil // For Servatus: 1 portion of montana | (denarius) / 1 portion of oil | (denarius) 9 portions / 4 portions of bread, 4 portions of cheese / 1 leek for the pan / 7 portions of sittuae, 1 portion of oil / 3rd day before the Ides: 2 portions of bread / 2 portions of bread for the boy / day before the Ides: 2 portions of bread for the boy / 1 portion of bread for eating / 1 leek / 2 portions of cheese / 2 portions of bread // Ides: 2 portions of bread / 2 portions of cibarium bread / 5 portions of oil / 3 portions of halica / 2 portions of fish stew for the master.
EDCS-26400580.summary: This extensive inscription is a detailed list of provisions and their quantities, dated by the Roman calendar (days before the Ides), likely representing a household's supplies or an account of food distribution.

EDCS-26400592.translation: Acti, beloved of the people, return quickly, be well.
EDCS-26400592.summary: This is a short, personal inscription, possibly a dedication or a farewell, expressing affection for someone named Acti and wishing them well.

EDCS-26500233.translation: Publius, I beg you, do not in vain... you, always dear...
EDCS-26500233.summary: This is a fragment of a personal inscription, likely an appeal or a message of affection, addressing someone named Publius.

EDCS-26500761.translation: Gaius Gavienus, son of Quintus / Gaius Hirtius, son of Gaius.
EDCS-26500761.summary: This inscription lists the names and patronymics of two individuals, Gaius Gavienus and Gaius Hirtius, likely identifying them for a legal or public record.

EDCS-26500764.text_edition: \n Caius Falcius Marci filius dat Lucius Caeseius Luci filius faciundum coiravit Olus Calidius Luci filius tutor ]\n
EDCS-26500764.translation: Caius Falcius, son of Marcus, arranged for it to be made. Lucius Caeseius, son of Lucius, supervised its construction. Olus Calidius, son of Lucius, was the guardian.
EDCS-26500764.summary: This inscription records the commissioning and supervision of a construction project, naming the contractor (Caius Falcius), the supervisor (Lucius Caeseius), and the guardian (Olus Calidius), likely related to a public building or a significant private work.

EDCS-26500765.translation: Ummidius, son of Lucius, Strabo, aedile / Fulvia, daughter of Marcus, his wife.
EDCS-26500765.summary: This inscription identifies Ummidius Strabo as an aedile and names his wife Fulvia, likely serving as a dedication or a record of their status.

EDCS-26500767.translation: Gaius Annius, son of Sextus, is buried here. Lucius Annius, son of Sextus (of the Teretina tribe), is alive.
EDCS-26500767.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Gaius Annius, noting his burial and the continued life of Lucius Annius.

EDCS-26500770.translation: The bones of Faustus, darling of the Numerii Granii, are here. Numerius Granius Artemo, the father, set this up. I beg you, traveler, do not harm me.
EDCS-26500770.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Faustus, set up by his father Numerius Granius Artemo, with a plea to travelers not to disturb the remains.

EDCS-26500771.text_edition: \n Caius Atatius Cai libertus Faustus pius in suuis sic\n
EDCS-26500771.translation: Gaius Atatius Faustus, freedman of Gaius, pious towards his own.
EDCS-26500771.summary: This inscription commemorates Gaius Atatius Faustus, a freedman, highlighting his piety.

EDCS-26601059.translation: Gaius Minucius, son of Gaius, quaestor.
EDCS-26601059.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely referring to Gaius Minucius, son of Gaius, in his capacity as quaestor.

EDCS-27400006.translation: I ask you, make Albucius and Casellius aediles.
EDCS-27400006.summary: This is a political appeal, likely a graffito, urging the public to elect Albucius and Casellius as aediles.

EDCS-27400007.translation: Epidia, not without Cosmo, asks for Marcus Samellius Modestus as aedile.
EDCS-27400007.summary: This inscription records a request or endorsement by Epidia (with Cosmo) for Marcus Samellius Modestus to be elected aedile.

EDCS-27400015.translation: I ask you, make Marcus Samellius Modestus aedile.
EDCS-27400015.summary: This is a political appeal, likely a graffito, urging the public to elect Marcus Samellius Modestus as aedile.

EDCS-27400028.translation: Fronto makes Marcus Samellius Modestus aedile.
EDCS-27400028.summary: This inscription indicates that Fronto is responsible for the election or appointment of Marcus Samellius Modestus as aedile.

EDCS-27400029.summary: This is a very brief inscription identifying Fuscus as an aedile, possibly a campaign notice or record.

EDCS-27400037.summary: This brief inscription identifies Lucius Albucius Celsus as an aedile, likely a campaign notice or record.

EDCS-27400039.translation: I ask you, elect Casellius as aedile.
EDCS-27400039.summary: This is a public notice or election slogan urging the election of Casellius as aedile.

EDCS-27400042.translation: Crocus wants to elect Lucius Albucius as aedile.
EDCS-27400042.summary: This is an election notice or slogan supporting Lucius Albucius for the position of aedile.

EDCS-27400052.translation: I ask you, elect Modestus of Samellium as aedile, a good and worthy man.
EDCS-27400052.summary: This is a public notice or election slogan endorsing Modestus of Samellium for aedile.

EDCS-27400062.summary: This is an election notice, likely a campaign slogan, promoting Samellium for the position of aedile.

EDCS-27400063.translation: I ask you, elect Gaius Gavius Rufus as IIvir, a worthy and useful young man of good character for the state.
EDCS-27400063.summary: This is a political endorsement urging the election of Gaius Gavius Rufus as a duovir (IIvir), highlighting his suitability for public office.

EDCS-27400064.translation: Marcus Samellius, a good and worthy young man for the state.
EDCS-27400064.summary: This inscription is a commendation or endorsement for Marcus Samellius, highlighting his character and suitability for public service.

EDCS-27400095.summary: This is a brief, informal personal inscription, possibly graffiti, indicating someone's presence and departure.

EDCS-27400107.translation: Ninety-three (measures) for Lacinia; Julia Clodia, four; four (measures) for Lacertus; it holds eight (measures); two measures of bread for Lacertus; Elias.
EDCS-27400107.summary: This inscription appears to be a record of quantities, possibly of goods or provisions, attributed to different individuals or recipients.

EDCS-27400110.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely the name of an individual, possibly a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-27400113.translation: Five and four (measures) of onions for Fortunatus, weighing 5 pounds; 7 and 7 (measures) of beans; 7 and 6 (measures) of barley seed.
EDCS-27400113.summary: This inscription records quantities of agricultural produce and their weights, likely a record of sale or distribution.

EDCS-27400150.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus R. B., likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400202.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary or dedication text identifying Cornelia Orestina, daughter of Publius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400238.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary or dedication text identifying Gaius Vettius Firmus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400267.translation: May the Lupercalia be successful for Marcus and Pacatus.
EDCS-27400267.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription wishing success for Marcus and Pacatus during the Lupercalia festival, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400275.translation: Xanthus, the unctor, accustomed to you with playful and jesting sport.
EDCS-27400275.summary: This is a funerary inscription likely for a slave named Xanthus, who worked as an unctor (anointing/massage practitioner), from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400284.translation: We ask the two Fabii, merchants.
EDCS-27400284.summary: This inscription appears to be a request or plea addressed to two merchants named Fabius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400420.summary: This is a very brief inscription possibly related to pregnancy, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400421.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription identifying Corinthus of Nola, likely a funerary or dedication text from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27400424.translation: I ask you, Albucius and Casellius, as aediles, to do [it].
EDCS-27400424.summary: This is a plea or petition addressed to the aediles Albucius and Casellius, likely from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-27400426.translation: I ask you, Celsus, as aedile, to do [it].
EDCS-27400426.summary: This is a plea or petition addressed to Celsus, who served as an aedile, likely from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-27400428.translation: I ask you to make Celsus an aedile.
EDCS-27400428.summary: This is a proposal for Celsus to become an aedile, likely from a political election notice.

EDCS-27400435.summary: This is a simple declaration or notice for Marcus Samellius Modestus to serve as aedile.

EDCS-27400440.summary: This is a simple declaration or notice for Marcus Samellius Modestus to serve as aedile, with a minor textual correction.

EDCS-27400444.translation: The neighbors ask you to make Albucius an aedile.
EDCS-27400444.summary: This is a neighborhood petition or announcement urging the election of Albucius as aedile.

EDCS-27400445.translation: I ask you to make Samellius, a young man of integrity, an aedile.
EDCS-27400445.summary: This is a political appeal to elect Samellius as aedile, emphasizing his youth and good character.

EDCS-27400446.summary: This inscription simply identifies Casellius and Albucius as having served as aediles.

EDCS-27400450.translation: I ask you to make Samellius, worthy of public office, an aedile.
EDCS-27400450.summary: This is a political endorsement for Samellius to become aedile, highlighting his suitability for public service.

EDCS-27400453.summary: This is a vulgar or crude inscription, likely a personal remark or curse.

EDCS-27400454.summary: This is likely a name, possibly referring to a person or a place, with no further context provided.

EDCS-27400461.text_edition: \n Aedilem Proculam sic CR cunctorum turba probavit hoc pudor ingenuus postulat et pietas\n
EDCS-27400461.translation: The crowd of citizens approved Procula as aedile; this is what honest shame and duty demand.
EDCS-27400461.summary: This inscription declares the approval of Procula as aedile by the citizens, framed as a matter of public virtue and propriety.

EDCS-27500189.translation: To Rutilia, mother, daughter of Lucius.
EDCS-27500189.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Rutilia, identified as a mother.

EDCS-27500194.translation: Quintus Iulius Verecundus, son of Quintus, of the Voltinia tribe, a soldier of the cohort I of the Praetorian Guard, and a soldier of the VI Praetorian cohort, with Lucius Augustus.
EDCS-27500194.summary: This inscription records a soldier, Quintus Iulius Verecundus, who served in the Praetorian Guard, likely on a tombstone.

EDCS-27500197.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Flavius Chrestus lived for 50 years.
EDCS-27500197.summary: A funerary dedication to Flavius Chrestus, who lived for 50 years.

EDCS-27500198.translation: [...]ius Hilarus, his foster-son, lived for 10 months and 27 days, from his own resources.
EDCS-27500198.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of an individual and is dedicated by their foster-son, Hilarus.

EDCS-27500200.translation: To the divine spirits. For Hostilia Tyches. [a mark indicating earth] with light earth. (?)
EDCS-27500200.summary: A funerary dedication to Hostilia Tyches, with a possible indication of the burial practice.

EDCS-27500203.translation: Soterichianus sends his best wishes to the boys.
EDCS-27500203.summary: A short greeting from Soterichianus to some boys, possibly on a personal item or tablet.

EDCS-27500218.translation: Afro, also known as Syphax. A public sum of money. The place was granted by decree of the decurions of the colony of Iuliensium (?) because of his outstanding poetic skill. Lucius Valerius Valerianus, a perfect man.
EDCS-27500218.summary: This inscription records the granting of public land to Afro (Syphax) for his poetic talent, decreed by the local council.

EDCS-27500219.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Marcus Voconi[us] Polybianus lived for 5 months and 24 days. Quintus Voconius Polybius and Claudia Synoris, most unhappy parents.
EDCS-27500219.summary: A funerary dedication by parents for their child, Marcus Voconi[us] Polybianus, who died at a very young age.

EDCS-27500220.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Annius Fortunatus, son of Lucius, of the Palatina tribe, lived for 4 years, 7 months, and 1 day.
EDCS-27500220.summary: A funerary inscription for a young child, Lucius Annius Fortunatus, who died before the age of five.

EDCS-27500222.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Pontia Daphne. Lucius Octavius Asiaticus, freedman of Lucius, to his most devoted wife, and for himself, and for their descendants.
EDCS-27500222.summary: A funerary dedication by Lucius Octavius Asiaticus for his wife Pontia Daphne, and also for himself and their descendants.

EDCS-27500223.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Publius Caecilius Rufinus made this for his most excellent uncle, Publius Sextilius Iucundus, son of Quintus.
EDCS-27500223.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a nephew for his uncle, Publius Sextilius Iucundus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27500224.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. The freedmen Onesiphorus, Agathangelus, and Acutus, freedmen of Aulus Umbricius Successus, son of Aulus, made this for their deserving patron.
EDCS-27500224.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by freedmen for their patron, Aulus Umbricius Successus, indicating a familial or patronage relationship and likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27500230.translation: Afinia Phieris, priestess, made this for herself while alive.
EDCS-27500230.summary: This is a funerary epitaph of a priestess, Afinia Phieris, who dedicated it for herself during her lifetime, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27500232.translation: To Caius Herius Sabinus, son of Caius, of the Voltinia tribe, [made by] Ofilliae, freedwoman of Caius, [and others, for] the seviri of Aesernia, Caius Herius Alexae[s]...
EDCS-27500232.summary: This inscription appears to be a funerary monument or dedication related to a seviri of Aesernia, with partial names and tribal affiliations preserved, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27601645.translation: Quintus Vibius Ampliatus, son of Quintus (?), gave Quintus Natalis as his proxy. Executed at Nola on the sixth day before the Kalends of January, when Spurius Oppius and Quintus Curtius Rufus were consuls. // [Someone named] Atticus, [proxy] of Sexti...
EDCS-27601645.summary: This is a legal document from Nola, recording the appointment of a proxy (cognitor) in the consulship of Spurius Oppius and Quintus Curtius Rufus, likely in the late Republic or early Imperial period.

EDCS-27601655.summary: This is a brief funerary epitaph recording the name and age of Lucius Munnius Celer, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700333.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Lucius Albucius Celsus, likely part of a larger dedication or epitaph from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700351.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription mentioning Caius Sallustius, likely part of a dedication or epitaph from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700363.translation: I ask you to make Popidius Rufus aedile.
EDCS-27700363.summary: This is a political appeal or election slogan urging the voters to elect Popidius Rufus as aedile, likely dating to the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-27700369.translation: I ask you to make Suettius and Epidius, who are worthy, iudices. I ask you to make Herennius and Verus aediles.
EDCS-27700369.summary: This inscription contains political slogans urging the voters to elect Suettius and Epidius as iudices and Herennius and Verus as aediles, indicative of electoral activity in a Roman town, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27700383.translation: I ask you, people, to make Marcellus aedile.
EDCS-27700383.summary: This is a request, likely from an election notice, for Marcellus to be elected aedile.

EDCS-27700387.translation: I ask you, people, to make Marcus Holconius Priscus aedile.
EDCS-27700387.summary: This is a request, likely from an election notice, for Marcus Holconius Priscus to be elected aedile.

EDCS-27700392.translation: Casellius, worthy of the public office, aedile.
EDCS-27700392.summary: This is a political slogan or recommendation, likely from an election notice, supporting Casellius for the position of aedile.

EDCS-27700402.translation: Marcus Holconius and Gaius Gavius, duo-virs.
EDCS-27700402.summary: This inscription likely records the names of two individuals, Marcus Holconius and Gaius Gavius, who served as duo-virs (magistrates).

EDCS-27700404.summary: This inscription records the name of Holconius Priscus who served as a duo-vir (magistrate).

EDCS-27700408.translation: I ask you, people, to make Marcus Holconius Priscus aedile.
EDCS-27700408.summary: This is a request, likely from an election notice, for Marcus Holconius Priscus to be elected aedile.

EDCS-27700410.translation: I ask you, people, to make Casellius aedile.
EDCS-27700410.summary: This is a request, likely from an election notice, for Casellius to be elected aedile.

EDCS-27700418.translation: I ask you, people, to make Lucius Veranius Hypsaeus, quinquennial, worthy of public office. Primus asks.
EDCS-27700418.summary: This is an election appeal, likely from an election notice, for Lucius Veranius Hypsaeus to be elected to the quinquennial office, with Primus as the endorser.

EDCS-27700420.summary: This inscription likely records the name of Marcus Gaius Marcellus, who served as an aedile.

EDCS-27700421.summary: This inscription likely records the name of Marcellus, who served as an aedile.

EDCS-27700426.translation: Marcellus, aedile worthy of the state.
EDCS-27700426.summary: This is a dedication or acclamation for an aedile named Marcellus, likely from a public building or monument.

EDCS-27700434.translation: To Lucius Veranius Hypsaeus, duovir for the third time with judicial authority, quinquennial, and Casellius Marcellus, aedile, most excellent colleagues.
EDCS-27700434.summary: This dedication honors Lucius Veranius Hypsaeus and Casellius Marcellus for their public service, likely as part of a collegium or association.

EDCS-27700435.summary: This is a dedication to Lucius Albucius for his service as an aedile, possibly on a public building or marker.

EDCS-27700438.summary: This is a request or acclamation urging the people to elect Veranius to the quinquennial office.

EDCS-27700441.translation: I ask you to make Casellius an aedile.
EDCS-27700441.summary: This is a plea or acclamation encouraging the populace to elect Casellius as an aedile.

EDCS-27700444.translation: I ask you to make Lucius Albucius an aedile, worthy of the state.
EDCS-27700444.summary: This inscription is a public acclamation urging the election of Lucius Albucius as aedile, highlighting his suitability for public office.

EDCS-27700445.translation: I ask you to make Gaius Gavius Rufus duovir with judicial authority.
EDCS-27700445.summary: This is a public acclamation encouraging the election of Gaius Gavius Rufus to the office of duovir iuris dicundi.

EDCS-27700446.summary: This is a simple dedication to Marcus Holconius Priscus, possibly honoring his achievements or public service.

EDCS-27700447.translation: I ask you to make Lucius Veranius Hypsaeus quinquennial.
EDCS-27700447.summary: This is a public acclamation encouraging the election of Lucius Veranius Hypsaeus to the quinquennial office.

EDCS-27700453.translation: The fruit vendors ask that Marcus Holconius Priscus be made aedile.
EDCS-27700453.summary: This inscription records a request from the fruit vendors' association to appoint Marcus Holconius Priscus as aedile.

EDCS-27700456.translation: Memor, a fellow member, makes this for Casellius Marcellus, at his request.
EDCS-27700456.summary: This inscription, likely a funerary monument or a dedication, records a request made by Memor for Casellius Marcellus.

EDCS-27700458.translation: I ask you, make Casellius aedile.
EDCS-27700458.summary: This is a political appeal urging the populace to elect Casellius as aedile.

EDCS-27700462.translation: Cognatus asks that you elect Marcus Casellius aedile. I ask you to make him aedile.
EDCS-27700462.summary: This inscription is a political appeal by Cognatus, asking the people to elect Marcus Casellius as aedile.

EDCS-27700470.summary: This inscription simply identifies Marcus Casellius as having served as an aedile.

EDCS-27700483.translation: I ask you to make Casellius aedile, worthy of public office.
EDCS-27700483.summary: This is a political appeal urging the populace to elect Casellius as aedile, emphasizing his worthiness for public service.

EDCS-27700485.summary: This inscription identifies Marcus Casellius Marcellus as having served as an aedile.

EDCS-27700507.translation: Maercus Holconius and Caius Gavius, aediles.
EDCS-27700507.summary: This inscription lists Maercus Holconius and Caius Gavius as having served as aediles.

EDCS-27700513.summary: This inscription likely records the name of Lucius Ceius Secundus, possibly on a tomb or dedication.

EDCS-27700515.translation: Hypsaeus, quinquennial, worthy of public office.
EDCS-27700515.summary: This inscription praises Hypsaeus, who held the quinquennial office, as worthy of public service.

EDCS-27700520.translation: Saturninus, with his students, asks that you make Gaius Cuspius Pansa aedile, worthy of public office.
EDCS-27700520.summary: This inscription is a political appeal by Saturninus and his students to elect Gaius Cuspius Pansa as aedile, noting his suitability for the role.

EDCS-27700521.translation: To Gaius Calventius Sittius, twice-man with the right to judge.
EDCS-27700521.summary: This is a dedication to Gaius Calventius Sittius, who held the office of twice-man with judicial authority, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-27700525.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Holconius Priscus, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-27700526.translation: I ask you to make Cuspia Pansa.
EDCS-27700526.summary: This is a plea to elect or honor Cuspia Pansa, possibly as a magistrate.

EDCS-27700530.summary: This is a dedication to Popidius Secundus, who served as an aedile, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-27700534.summary: This is a dedication to Cuspia Pansa, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-27700538.translation: I ask you to make Lucius Numisius, aedile.
EDCS-27700538.summary: This is a plea to elect or honor Lucius Numisius, who served as an aedile, possibly on a public monument.

EDCS-27700542.translation: Marcus Holconius Priscus and Gaius Gavius Rufus, twice-men. [---]ellus made this.
EDCS-27700542.summary: This records the erection of a monument by [---]ellus in honor of Marcus Holconius Priscus and Gaius Gavius Rufus, who served as twice-men.

EDCS-27700544.summary: This is a dedication to Gaius Sallustius, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-27700550.translation: To Marcus Holconius Priscus. Philippus, rejoicing.
EDCS-27700550.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Holconius Priscus, set up by Philippus out of joy, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-27700551.translation: I ask you to make Popidius Secundus, aedile.
EDCS-27700551.summary: This is a plea to elect or honor Popidius Secundus, who served as an aedile, possibly on a public monument.

EDCS-27700561.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary dedication to Lucius Numisius Rarus.

EDCS-27700563.translation: I ask you, voters, to make Gaius Cupsius Pansa, worthy of public affairs for managing public roads and sacred buildings, a young man of integrity, aedile.
EDCS-27700563.summary: This is a political appeal from a supporter to elect Gaius Cupsius Pansa as aedile, likely from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-27700565.translation: Casellius, a young man worthy of public affairs for managing public roads and sacred buildings...
EDCS-27700565.summary: This is a fragmentary political appeal to elect Casellius as aedile, emphasizing his suitability for managing public works.

EDCS-27700567.translation: To Marcus Holconius Priscus, worthy of public affairs.
EDCS-27700567.summary: This is an incomplete commendation of Marcus Holconius Priscus for his public service, likely from Pompeii.

EDCS-27700568.translation: They ask you to elect Sallustius Capito as aedile...
EDCS-27700568.summary: This is a fragmented electoral appeal asking voters to elect Sallustius Capito as aedile.

EDCS-27700569.translation: I ask you, voters, to make Casellius Marcellus, worthy of public affairs, aedile.
EDCS-27700569.summary: This is a political appeal from a supporter asking voters to elect Casellius Marcellus as aedile.

EDCS-27700570.summary: This is a simple electoral notice supporting Casellius for the position of aedile.

EDCS-27700574.summary: This is a simple electoral notice supporting Casellius for the position of aedile.

EDCS-27700582.translation: I ask you, voters, to make Sallustius Capito aedile; innkeepers, make him so.
EDCS-27700582.summary: This is an electoral appeal from a supporter, specifically urging innkeepers to vote for Sallustius Capito to become aedile.

EDCS-27700585.summary: This is a fragmented electoral notice supporting Cupsius Pansa for the position of aedile.

EDCS-27700587.translation: To Marcus Holconius Priscus, twice a magistrate.
EDCS-27700587.summary: This inscription records an honorific dedication to Marcus Holconius Priscus, who held the office of duovir twice.

EDCS-27700589.translation: Secunda Sozomene asks you everywhere to make Cassellius aedile.
EDCS-27700589.summary: This is a personal request from Secunda Sozomene to elect Cassellius as aedile.

EDCS-27700597.translation: I ask you, sirs, to make Popidius Secundus worthy of public office.
EDCS-27700597.summary: This is a plea to elect Popidius Secundus as aedile, deeming him fit for public service.

EDCS-27700604.translation: Primus asks you to make Lucius Albucius aedile.
EDCS-27700604.summary: This inscription is a personal request from Primus to elect Lucius Albucius as aedile.

EDCS-27700611.translation: I ask you, sirs, to make Sallustius aedile.
EDCS-27700611.summary: This is a request to elect Sallustius as aedile.

EDCS-28000131.translation: Marcus Acilius Priscus, son of Aulus, of the Voturia tribe, Egrilius Plarianus, prefect of the military treasury, pontifex of Vulcan and sacred buildings, patron of the colony, has dedicated as a gift a silver shield with a golden image, a place having been granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-28000131.summary: This inscription records a donation of a silver shield with a golden image by Marcus Acilius Priscus, a prominent official and priest, to the colony, with the space for its display granted by the town council.

EDCS-28000306.summary: This is a simple dedication or designation for Cassellius as aedile.

EDCS-28000308.translation: To Gaius Calventius Sittius, duovir with judicial authority.
EDCS-28000308.summary: This inscription honors Gaius Calventius Sittius for his service as a duovir with judicial powers.

EDCS-28000314.summary: This inscription begins the dedication to two individuals, Pansam and Popidium, for the office of aedile, but is incomplete.

EDCS-28000317.summary: This inscription indicates that Posido is dedicating something to Cuspium, who is serving as aedile.

EDCS-28000324.summary: This is a dedication to Popidius Rufus, likely for his role as aedile.

EDCS-28000334.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Popidius Rufus, possibly a dedication.

EDCS-28000346.summary: This is a dedication to Casellius, likely for his role as aedile.

EDCS-28000352.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Popidius Secundus, possibly a dedication.

EDCS-28000353.translation: We ask you to make Samellius, as aedile...
EDCS-28000353.summary: This inscription is a public request for Samellius to be made an aedile.

EDCS-28000359.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Popidius Secundus as aedile.

EDCS-28000362.translation: We ask you to make Marcellus, as aedile...
EDCS-28000362.summary: This inscription is a public request for Marcellus to be made an aedile.

EDCS-28000364.translation: To Albucius and Marcellus, as aediles, worthy of public office for the care of public roads, buildings, and sacred places, we ask you to make them.
EDCS-28000364.summary: This inscription is a public request for Albucius and Marcellus to be made aediles, citing their qualification for managing public affairs.

EDCS-28000369.summary: This is a dedication to Cuspius Pansa, likely for his role as aedile.

EDCS-28000372.translation: Hermes(?) asks for Samellius, as aedile.
EDCS-28000372.summary: This inscription records a request by Hermes(?) for Samellius to serve as aedile.

EDCS-28000380.summary: This inscription names Casellius, indicating his candidacy or qualification for the position of aedile.

EDCS-28000382.summary: This inscription is a plea or request involving a person named Simplex.

EDCS-28000392.summary: This inscription names Lucius Popidius Secundus, indicating his candidacy or qualification for the position of aedile.

EDCS-28000396.translation: I ask you, make Marcus Samellius Modestus, worthy of the republic, an aedile.
EDCS-28000396.summary: This inscription is a public plea to elect Marcus Samellius Modestus as aedile, highlighting his suitability for public office.

EDCS-28000403.summary: This inscription names Marcellus, indicating his candidacy or qualification for the position of aedile.

EDCS-28000404.summary: This inscription names Casellius, indicating his candidacy or qualification for the position of aedile.

EDCS-28000406.translation: I ask you, make Lucius Naevius Rufus, a man of [M...], an aedile.
EDCS-28000406.summary: This inscription is a public plea to elect Lucius Naevius Rufus as aedile, mentioning a patron or descriptor 'M.'.

EDCS-28000407.translation: I ask you, make Popidius Secundus an aedile.
EDCS-28000407.summary: This inscription is a public plea to elect Popidius Secundus as aedile.

EDCS-28000412.summary: This inscription names Samellius, indicating his candidacy or qualification for the position of aedile.

EDCS-28000413.summary: This inscription names Albicius, indicating his candidacy or qualification for the position of aedile.

EDCS-28000415.translation: I ask you to make Albucius aedile.
EDCS-28000415.summary: This is a request for the election of Albucius as aedile, likely from a public notice or informal plea.

EDCS-28000416.translation: The woodworkers and wagon-makers ask you to make Marcellus aedile, along with Lassi and Crimio and Gaius Nisio Infantio, everywhere.
EDCS-28000416.summary: This inscription records a plea from the wood and wagon guilds for the election of Marcellus as aedile, mentioning other associated individuals.

EDCS-28000421.translation: The farmers ask you to make Marcus Casellus Marcellus aedile, Fisinerficus.
EDCS-28000421.summary: This is a plea from farmers for the election of Marcus Casellus Marcellus as aedile.

EDCS-28000422.translation: I ask you to make Casellius and Albucius good, worthy men for the Republic as aediles, Secanis[--] and all [?].
EDCS-28000422.summary: This inscription urges the election of Casellius and Albucius as aediles, describing them as worthy of public office.

EDCS-28000425.summary: This inscription simply names Gaius Sallustius, possibly as a candidate or a person of importance.

EDCS-28000426.translation: Novici the innkeeper makes Marcellus and Celsus aediles.
EDCS-28000426.summary: This inscription states that the innkeeper Novici is responsible for the election of Marcellus and Celsus as aediles.

EDCS-28000429.summary: This inscription simply names Marcus Samellius, possibly as a candidate or a person of importance.

EDCS-28000437.translation: Gaius Gavius Rufus asks for Marcus Holconius.
EDCS-28000437.summary: This inscription records a request from Gaius Gavius Rufus concerning Marcus Holconius, possibly related to an election or appointment.

EDCS-28000440.translation: I ask you to make Gaius Cuspius Pansa aedile.
EDCS-28000440.summary: This is a request for the election of Gaius Cuspius Pansa as aedile, likely from a public notice or informal plea.

EDCS-28000441.summary: This inscription nominates or supports Casellius for the position of aedile.

EDCS-28000442.summary: This inscription records the name Quintus Numisius.

EDCS-28000446.translation: Euodus asks for Pansam to be made aedile.
EDCS-28000446.summary: This inscription records a request for Pansam to be appointed aedile, made by Euodus.

EDCS-28000447.summary: This inscription records the name and office of Pansam as aedile.

EDCS-28000450.translation: Marcellus, worthy of public office, as aedile.
EDCS-28000450.summary: This inscription recommends Marcellus for the position of aedile, describing him as worthy of public office.

EDCS-28000451.summary: This inscription records the name and office of Marcus Casellius Marcellus as aedile.

EDCS-28000455.translation: Marcellus asks for [him] to be made aedile.
EDCS-28000455.summary: This inscription records a request for Marcellus to be appointed aedile.

EDCS-28000456.translation: I ask you to make Pansam aedile, and Popidius.
EDCS-28000456.summary: This inscription is a plea to appoint Pansam and Popidius as aediles.

EDCS-28000457.translation: I ask you to make Gaius Calventius, duumvir, and Sittius Magnus.
EDCS-28000457.summary: This inscription is a plea to appoint Gaius Calventius as duumvir and Sittius Magnus.

EDCS-28000458.summary: This inscription records the name and office of Marcus Casellius Marcellus as aedile.

EDCS-28000460.summary: This inscription records the name and office of Casellius as aedile.

EDCS-28000463.translation: Vettius asks for Samellius to be made aedile.
EDCS-28000463.summary: A private request, likely from a wall painting or informal inscription, for Samellius to be elected aedile.

EDCS-28000464.summary: A simple designation, possibly a label or a notice, identifying Pansam as an aedile.

EDCS-28000465.summary: A simple designation, possibly a label or a notice, identifying Casellius as an aedile.

EDCS-28000466.translation: I ask you to make Casellius aedile.
EDCS-28000466.summary: A private request, likely from a wall painting or informal inscription, for Casellius to be elected aedile.

EDCS-28000469.translation: Fidelis Ebrli makes Marcus Casellius a worthy and suitable aedile for the state.
EDCS-28000469.summary: A declaration of support, likely from a wall painting or informal inscription, endorsing Marcus Casellius for the office of aedile.

EDCS-28000470.translation: I ask you to make C(aius) Marcellus, a good and worthy man, suitable for the state as aedile.
EDCS-28000470.summary: A private request, likely from a wall painting or informal inscription, for C(aius) Marcellus to be elected aedile.

EDCS-28000471.translation: C(aius) Cusp(ius) Pansam, worthy and suitable for the state as aedile.
EDCS-28000471.summary: A declaration of support, likely from a wall painting or informal inscription, endorsing C(aius) Cusp(ius) Pansam for the office of aedile.

EDCS-28000475.summary: A designation of Casellius as aedile, with 'Venus' possibly indicating a patron, a location, or a personal connection.

EDCS-28000488.translation: He asks for Marcellus to be made aedile.
EDCS-28000488.summary: A private request, likely from a wall painting or informal inscription, for Marcellus to be elected aedile.

EDCS-28000490.summary: A simple designation, possibly a label or a notice, identifying C(aius) Cusp(ius) Pansam as an aedile.

EDCS-28000491.summary: This is a dedication to Numerius Popidius, likely on a monument or tomb, whose date is not specified.

EDCS-28000492.translation: I urge you to make Gaius Cuspius Pansas an aedile worthy of the state.
EDCS-28000492.summary: This is a plea to voters to elect Gaius Cuspius Pansas as aedile, portraying him as a candidate deserving of public office, likely from the Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-28000496.translation: I urge you to make Cuspius Pansas, a young man, honest and worthy of the state, an aedile.
EDCS-28000496.summary: This inscription urges voters to elect Cuspius Pansas as aedile, emphasizing his youth, integrity, and worthiness for public office, likely from the Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-28000500.translation: I urge you to make Holconius Priscus an aedile worthy of the state; he is worthy.
EDCS-28000500.summary: This inscription advocates for the election of Holconius Priscus as aedile, asserting his worthiness for the role, likely from the Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-28100001.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Ariecinus Onesimus made this for his deserving patron, Ariecinus Hermes.
EDCS-28100001.summary: This is a tomb inscription by Ariecinus Onesimus for his well-deserving patron Ariecinus Hermes, dedicated to the gods of the underworld, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100002.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aristida, son of Artemidorus, from Rhodes, made this for himself and for his well-deserving wife Petronia Hygia, with whom he lived for 39 years and 11 months, and for his most dutiful son Petronius Dexter, and for his freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-28100002.summary: This tomb inscription is dedicated to the Spirits of the Departed by Aristida, a Rhodian, for himself, his wife Petronia Hygia, their son Petronius Dexter, and their freedmen and descendants, indicating a family burial plot from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100003.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Arrius Herax made this for his excellent patroness Arria Moschis, who deserved well.
EDCS-28100003.summary: This tomb inscription by Gaius Arrius Herax is for his excellent patroness Arria Moschis, dedicated to the gods of the underworld, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100004.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Arsinous lived for 25 years.
EDCS-28100004.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Arsinous, stating the deceased's name and age at death, dedicated to the gods of the underworld, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100005.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus hic iacet exanimis puer nomine Atenio qui vixit annis XVIII mensis sic VIII diebus VIII Aurelius Crescens |(centurio) cohortis III vigilum Iulia Manteiane pratroni carrissimo alumno fecerunt\n
EDCS-28100005.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Here lies lifeless a boy named Atenius, who lived 18 years, 8 months, and 8 days. Aurelius Crescens, centurion of the 3rd cohort of vigiles, and Iulia Manteiane made this for their dearest patron and foster-son.
EDCS-28100005.summary: This tomb inscription is a dedication by Aurelius Crescens, a centurion, and Iulia Manteiane for their beloved foster-son Atenius, who died at the age of 18 years, 8 months, and 8 days, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100006.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Atilia Artemisia dedicated this tomb intended for Marcellus Secundus. Flavia Eutycheti gave an offering of oil.
EDCS-28100006.summary: This tomb inscription records a dedication by Atilia Artemisia for Marcellus Secundus, with Flavia Eutycheti making a separate offering, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100007.translation: To the departed spirits. [---]a Statilia [to her son St]atiliano, who lived 10 years, 3 months, 3 days. [---] who lived 7 months, 12 days.
EDCS-28100007.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Statilia and her son Statiliano, detailing their ages at death.

EDCS-28100008.translation: To the departed spirits. Attia Psyche and Gaius Attius Alexander, freedpersons, made this monument for themselves, their freedpersons, freedwomen, and their descendants; it shall not follow an heir and is not for sale; entrusted to their good faith, nor shall a trustee have access. In front 18 feet, in depth 38 feet.
EDCS-28100008.summary: A funerary monument inscription by freedpersons Attia Psyche and Gaius Attius Alexander, establishing rules for inheritance and sale of the property.

EDCS-28100009.translation: To the departed spirits. Atticius Procleianus to his son Atticius Hilarus, who lived 1 year, 9 months, 15 days.
EDCS-28100009.summary: A funerary inscription by Atticius Procleianus for his son Atticius Hilarus, recording his short lifespan.

EDCS-28100011.translation: To the departed spirits. Aufidia Secunda made this for her most excellent patron, Lucius Aufidius Paternus.
EDCS-28100011.summary: A funerary dedication by Aufidia Secunda to her patron Lucius Aufidius Paternus.

EDCS-28100012.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Aurelius Alexander lived 30 years, 8 months. Publius Petronius Pelagius made this for his incomparable friend.
EDCS-28100012.summary: A funerary inscription by Publius Petronius Pelagius for his friend Marcus Aurelius Alexander, noting his age at death.

EDCS-28100013.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Aurelius Athenio to his incomparable, deserving wife Sabina.
EDCS-28100013.summary: A funerary dedication by Marcus Aurelius Athenio to his wife Sabina.

EDCS-28100014.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Aurelius Calocaerus [---] to Titus Aelius Felicissimus [---] to Malia Blunda, who [lived ---]. If anyone [---] in this monument [---] 10,000 sesterces for the treasury of the Roman people. In front [---] feet.
EDCS-28100014.summary: A funerary inscription with details about the deceased and a penalty for disturbing the monument, possibly dating to the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-28100015.translation: If anyone places or introduces a corpse of an outsider into this sarcophagus, a penalty of 20,000 sesterces for the treasury of the people. To the departed spirits. Aurelius Irenion, also called Ammonianus, and Aurelia Arpocratiana, siblings.
EDCS-28100015.summary: An inscription on a sarcophagus that stipulates a penalty for unauthorized burials and also serves as a dedication to the deceased siblings Aurelius Irenion and Aurelia Arpocratiana.

EDCS-28100016.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Axius Crispus, son of Gaius Axius, made this for his freedwoman Axiaes Nicene, and for himself, his freedwoman Axia Margarita, and their freedpersons, freedwomen, and their descendants; this monument shall not follow an heir.
EDCS-28100016.summary: A funerary monument inscription by Gaius Axius Crispus for his freedwoman Axiaes Nicene, himself, and other family members and freedpersons, with a clause against it following an heir.

EDCS-28100017.translation: To the departed spirits. Myron made this for his brother Basso, well-deserving, most dutiful.
EDCS-28100017.summary: A funerary dedication by Myron for his brother Basso.

EDCS-28100018.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Betiliena Antiochis made this for her deserving husband, Publius Betilienus Synegdemus, and for his freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-28100018.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a wife for her husband and their descendants.

EDCS-28100019.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Publius Betilienus Synegdemus and Betiliena Antiochis, under the care of Marcus Cosconius Hyginus.
EDCS-28100019.summary: This inscription records the funerary care provided by Marcus Cosconius Hyginus for Publius Betilienus Synegdemus and Betiliena Antiochis.

EDCS-28100020.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Caecilius Victor donatum sibi pavimentum purum virgineum monumenti huius a Perella sic Hieronide et Anniis Vitale et Decia in quo sarcophaga nova sumptibus suis inst<r>uxit fecitque sibi et Caeciliae Hygiae coniugi suae et libertis libertabusque posterisque eorum\n
EDCS-28100020.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Caecilius Victor, having made and furnished the pure and virgin floor of this monument with new sarcophagi at his own expense, made it for himself and for his wife Caecilia Hygia and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-28100020.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Lucius Caecilius Victor for himself, his wife, and their descendants, including the installation of new sarcophagi and a decorated floor.

EDCS-28100021.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caecilia Maxima made this for her son Socrates and grandson, from the estate of Aurelia.
EDCS-28100021.summary: This funerary inscription was set up by Caecilia Maxima for her son and grandson, funded by the estate of Aurelia.

EDCS-28100022.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Calpurnia Ptolemais, daughter of Lucius, made this for herself and for her husband Lucius Calpurnius Ianuarius and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants; this monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-28100022.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Calpurnia Ptolemais for herself, her husband, and their descendants, with a clause preventing inheritance by others.

EDCS-28100023.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Tiberius Claudius Arphocration, in a plot of land granted to himself by Julia Successa.
EDCS-28100023.summary: This funerary inscription records a burial plot granted by Julia Successa to Tiberius Claudius Arphocration.

EDCS-28100024.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Tiberius Claudius Epagathus and Claudia Olympias made this for her husband, who lived with her for 52 years.
EDCS-28100024.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Claudia Olympias for her husband, Tiberius Claudius Epagathus, noting their 52 years of marriage.

EDCS-28100025.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Tiberius Claudius Eumenes, freedman of the Emperor, for himself and for his daughters Claudia Phoebe and Fadia Tethis, and for his son Claudius Phoebus, and for Julia Heuresis, his wife, and their descendants.
EDCS-28100025.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Tiberius Claudius Eumenes, a freedman of the emperor, for his family members.

EDCS-28100026.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Tiberius Claudius Eutychus made this for his deserving wife, Claudia Memnonis, and for himself, their children, freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants; this monument will not pass to an heir. In front 15 feet, in the plot 15 feet.
EDCS-28100026.summary: This funerary monument was created by Tiberius Claudius Eutychus for his wife, children, and descendants, with specific dimensions and a non-inheritance clause.

EDCS-28100028.translation: Octavius Felix and Iunia Maxima and Iunia [---] purchased for themselves in a plot granted by Iulius Zoticus and by Iulia Artemidora, to the spirits of the departed.
EDCS-28100028.summary: This inscription records the purchase of a burial plot by Octavius Felix, Iunia Maxima, and Iunia [---] from Iulius Zoticus and Iulia Artemidora.

EDCS-28100029.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Herennius Secundus and Cornelia Secunda made this for their most devoted daughter, Herennia Procla, who lived for two years, eight months, and twenty days.
EDCS-28100029.summary: A funerary epitaph for Herennia Procla, set up by her parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100030.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ianuaria Chreste CI Haore Cai fili- HAE Chaerea annos IIII menses V\n
