EDCS-28100030.translation: To the departed spirits. Ianuaria Chreste, daughter of Cai Haores, and HAE Chaerea, lived for four years, five months.
EDCS-28100030.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Ianuaria Chreste, with parentage specified, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100032.summary: A very brief inscription, possibly a name or a funerary dedication, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100034.translation: Samus, son of Samius, made this for Maecenia Crusopolis.
EDCS-28100034.summary: A funerary epitaph for Maecenia Crusopolis, dedicated by Samus, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100035.translation: To the departed spirits. Kania Olympias made this for her most beloved husband, Lucius Malius Natalis, who was placed here, lived for ten months and twenty-nine days.
EDCS-28100035.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Malius Natalis, set up by his wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100036.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Orbius Scapula, an evocatus of Augustus, made this for Onesimus, his excellent slave.
EDCS-28100036.summary: A funerary epitaph for the slave Onesimus, dedicated by his master Marcus Orbius Scapula, an imperial official, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100038.translation: To the departed spirits. Pater Euhodus, his son Euhodianus, his daughter Euhodia, and Philete made this for themselves and for Ostorius Euhodus and his children, and their freedmen and freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-28100038.summary: A family funerary epitaph dedicating a tomb for multiple individuals, including Pater Euhodus and Ostorius Euhodus, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100039.translation: To the departed spirits. May the earth be light upon you, Parthenia, my excellent soul. She lived about eighteen years. Marcus Antonius Corymbus made this in the second half of the month of August, when Servianus was consul.
EDCS-28100039.summary: A funerary epitaph for Parthenia, set up by Marcus Antonius Corymbus, with a consular date likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28100040.text_edition: \n Petronia Erotis sibi et Tito Flavio Pharnaci karissimo coniugi suo fecit et is quibus id ius possi dendi testamemto suo legavit posterisque eorum libertis libertabusque in fronte pedes in agro pedes XXV et Zethioni Artemidori filio Rhodio itum ambitum donaverunt\n
EDCS-28100040.translation: Petronia Erotis made this for herself and for her dearest husband, Titus Flavius Pharnaces, and for those to whom he bequeathed the right of burial in his will, and for their descendants, freedmen, and freedwomen; they donated twenty-five feet in front and twenty-five feet in the field, and gifted the surrounding area to Zethion, son of Artemidorus of Rhodes.
EDCS-28100040.summary: A complex funerary dedication by Petronia Erotis for herself, her husband, and others, including a land donation, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100041.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Petronius Andronicus and Petronia Maritima made this for their most devoted daughter, Petronia Stolidis, who lived 20 years, 22 days, and 4 hours.
EDCS-28100041.summary: A funerary epitaph for Petronia Stolidis, set up by her parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28100042.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aulus Plautius Primitibus and Iunia Hieronis, having been granted space by the two freedmen of Iulius Zoticus and Aetus, heirs of Iulius Prosdocimus, made two sarcophagi for themselves, and for their most beloved son Plautius Mascellio, who lived for 3 years, 4 months, and 20 days.
EDCS-28100042.summary: This funerary inscription records the construction of two sarcophagi by Aulus Plautius Primitibus and Iunia Hieronis for themselves and their son, with the space granted by freedmen.

EDCS-28100043.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Addas made this monument for himself and his wife Plotia Areninenca, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants; this monument will not follow an heir.
EDCS-28100043.summary: A funerary epitaph where Lucius Addas dedicates a monument for himself, his wife, and their descendants, stating it is not inheritable.

EDCS-28100044.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For their most dutiful daughter Pomponia Felicia. Pomponia Optata and her father Marcus Caecilius Euodus made this; she lived for 3 years, 8 months, and 3 days.
EDCS-28100044.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication of a monument by Pomponia Optata and Marcus Caecilius Euodus for their daughter, Pomponia Felicia, who died at a young age.

EDCS-28100046.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For my dearest husband. Messia Candida made this for herself, and for her freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants, and for their ancestors; the space was granted by Gavinius Crispus and Eutyches and by the two Antonii, Iulianus and Polio, with free ingress and egress.
EDCS-28100046.summary: This funerary inscription records Messia Candida dedicating a tomb for her husband, herself, and their freed dependents, with the space granted by several individuals.

EDCS-28100047.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lucius Mindius Diogae, Iulia Zoe provided for the erection of this tomb for her well-deserving husband, and for herself, and her family, and their descendants.
EDCS-28100047.summary: A funerary epitaph where Iulia Zoe arranges for a tomb for her husband Lucius Mindius Diogae, and for herself and her descendants.

EDCS-28100048.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Gaurus. Terentia Secunda and Gaurus, son of Gaurus, made this for their most dutiful son.
EDCS-28100048.summary: This funerary inscription records Terentia Secunda and Gaurus dedicating a tomb to their son, also named Gaurus.

EDCS-28100049.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Glycera, the slave woman.
EDCS-28100049.summary: A brief funerary inscription dedicated to a slave woman named Glycera.

EDCS-28100050.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Munatius Licinianus and Curtia Gemella, who is also Flavia. For their most dutiful parents, their son Marcus Munatius Marcianus made this.
EDCS-28100050.summary: This funerary inscription records Marcus Munatius Marcianus dedicating a monument to his parents, Marcus Munatius Licinianus and Curtia Gemella (also known as Flavia).

EDCS-28100051.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Titus Munatius Proclus, son of Titus, of the Quirina tribe. Iulia Procla, daughter of Tiberius, his mother, made this for her most dutiful son; he lived 6 years and 14 days.
EDCS-28100051.summary: A funerary inscription by a mother, Iulia Procla, for her son Titus Munatius Proclus, who died at the age of six.

EDCS-28100053.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Murdia Ampelidis. With the care of the freedmen Ampliatus and Erotus, and the freedwomen Ampelide and Nice.
EDCS-28100053.summary: This funerary inscription records a tomb set up for Murdia Ampelidis, with arrangements managed by her freedmen and freedwomen.

EDCS-28100054.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Gaius Marcius Abascantus the younger, who lived 29 years, 2 months, 20 days. Gaius Marcius Abascantus and Marcia Helpis made this for their most dutiful son.
EDCS-28100054.summary: This is a tombstone set up by parents for their deceased son, Gaius Marcius Abascantus the younger.

EDCS-28100055.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Gaius Marcius Irenaeus, who lived 16 years and 45 days. His father, Gaius Marcius Nymphius, made this.
EDCS-28100055.summary: This is a tombstone set up by a father for his deceased son, Gaius Marcius Irenaeus.

EDCS-28100056.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Marcius Olympus made this for his most dutiful daughter, Marcia Olympias.
EDCS-28100056.summary: This is a tombstone set up by a father for his deceased daughter, Marcia Olympias.

EDCS-28100058.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcia (?) Donatus [---
EDCS-28100058.summary: This is a tombstone fragment, likely dedicated to a person named Donatus, part of the Marcius family, with incomplete information.

EDCS-28100059.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Felicissima, who lived 14 years, 4 months, 9 days. Felicissimus made this for his most dutiful sister.
EDCS-28100059.summary: This is a tombstone set up by a brother for his deceased sister, Felicissima.

EDCS-28100060.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Frimus Caesaris nostri servus fecit Ius<ti>no vernae suo bene merenti qui vixit annos II menses VII dies VI\n
EDCS-28100060.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Frimus, slave of our Caesar, made this for Iustinus, his own freedman, who deserved well, who lived 2 years, 7 months, 6 days.
EDCS-28100060.summary: This is a tombstone set up by a slave for his freedman, indicating a familial or close relationship within a household likely connected to the imperial family.

EDCS-28100061.translation: To the spirits of the departed. [---] Flavius (?) Callist[us (?)
EDCS-28100061.summary: This is a tombstone fragment with a partial name, likely Flavius Callistus, and incomplete dedication information.

EDCS-28100062.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Claudia Daphine and Titus Flavius Severus made this for their deserving wife and father, Titus Flavius Felix, freedman of Titus Flavius Aug(ustus).
EDCS-28100062.summary: This is a tombstone set up by a wife and son for their deceased husband and father, Titus Flavius Felix, a freedman of an imperial patron.

EDCS-28100066.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulius Apollinaris made this for his sweetest son, Gavius Iulius Paulinus, who deserved well. Iunia Gallite conceded (?) the place for (a period) of 7 years.
EDCS-28100066.summary: This is a tombstone set up by a father for his son, with a note about the concession of the burial plot.

EDCS-28100067.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Iulius Philippus made this for his freedman, Iason, who deserved well.
EDCS-28100067.summary: This is a tombstone set up by a patron for his deceased freedman, Iason.

EDCS-28100069.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Flavia Tyche made this. Flavius Thinnus.
EDCS-28100069.summary: A funerary dedication by Flavius Thinnus for Flavia Tyche.

EDCS-28100071.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Publius Fulcinius Abascantus, with his freedman Fulcinius Hermippus, each contributing half, made this for Fulcinia Callistes, and for himself, and for his daughter Fulcinia Bassilla, and for his wife Fulcinia Daphnis, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants. This monument will not be followed by an external heir of the family. This monument will not be followed by an heir.
EDCS-28100071.summary: A complex funerary monument set up by Publius Fulcinius Abascantus and his freedman for family members, with clauses to prevent an external heir from inheriting it.

EDCS-28100072.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Publius Gabinius Longinus and Annia Epictesis made this for their most devoted son, who lived 16 years.
EDCS-28100072.summary: A funerary dedication by Publius Gabinius Longinus and Annia Epictesis for their son, who died at age 16.

EDCS-28100073.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Publius Gabinius Annianus. Annia Ionice for her dearest brother.
EDCS-28100073.summary: A funerary dedication by Annia Ionice for her brother, Publius Gabinius Annianus.

EDCS-28100074.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Caius Galgestius Helius, from a pure place bought from Valeria Trophime, made this little shrine for himself and his family, joined to the wall, on the right side upon entering, in which are 14 jars, in addition to the jar which Trophime donated to Vitalis Galgestius; from which I have donated one jar as a gift to Pomponius Chrysoplus.
EDCS-28100074.summary: A funerary dedication for Caius Galgestius Helius and his family, describing the construction of a burial shrine with a specific number of jars and a donation of one jar.

EDCS-28300194.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 3rd time, the Optimus Maximus Princeps. The decurions and citizens of Surrentum, with collected funds.
EDCS-28300194.summary: An inscription dedicating a monument to Emperor Hadrian by the town council and citizens of Surrentum, likely dating to his fifth tribunician power and third consulship.

EDCS-28300195.translation: The courier of Claudia Capitolina lived 29 years.
EDCS-28300195.summary: A funerary inscription for the courier of Claudia Capitolina, who died at age 29.

EDCS-28300196.translation: Chila, freedwoman of Augustus, lived 65 years.
EDCS-28300196.summary: A funerary inscription for Chila, a freedwoman of Augustus, who died at age 65.

EDCS-28300197.translation: Crispus, slave of Augustus, lived 40 years.
EDCS-28300197.summary: A funerary inscription for Crispus, a slave of Augustus, who died at age 40.

EDCS-28300199.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Germo, freedman of Augustus, lived 85 years.
EDCS-28300199.summary: A funerary dedication for Germo, a freedman of Augustus, who died at age 85.

EDCS-28300200.translation: Ianuarius, freedman of the Emperor, lived 1 year.
EDCS-28300200.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Ianuarius, a freedman of the Emperor, at the age of one year.

EDCS-28300201.translation: For Iulia Tertia, the Nymph, whom Nympheros, freedman of the Emperor, greatly esteemed; she lived 25 years.
EDCS-28300201.summary: A tombstone set up by Nympheros, a freedman of the Emperor, for his well-deserving wife Iulia Tertia, who died at 25.

EDCS-28300203.translation: Nicephor, freedman of the Emperor, lived 75 years.
EDCS-28300203.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Nicephor, a freedman of the Emperor, at the age of 75.

EDCS-28300204.translation: Onesimus, freedman of the Emperor, lived 80 years.
EDCS-28300204.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Onesimus, a freedman of the Emperor, at the age of 80.

EDCS-28300205.translation: Primigenius, freedman of Philodespotus the Emperor, lived 11 years.
EDCS-28300205.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Primigenius, a freedman of Philodespotus the Emperor, at the age of 11 years.

EDCS-28300207.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Abascantus at the age of 4 years.

EDCS-28300209.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Claudia Tyche at the age of 40 years.

EDCS-28300210.translation: To Primigenia. Tiberius Claudius Abascantus for his well-deserving wife, lived 40 years.
EDCS-28300210.summary: A tombstone set up by Tiberius Claudius Abascantus for his wife Primigenia, who died at 40 years of age.

EDCS-28300211.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Claudius Abascantus at the age of 50 years.

EDCS-28300212.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Tiberius Claudius Stephanus at the age of 15 years.

EDCS-28300213.translation: Hilarus, son of Plocamus, lived 5 years.
EDCS-28300213.summary: This is a tombstone for Hilarus, son of Plocamus, who died at the age of 5.

EDCS-28300214.summary: This is a tombstone for Iulia Fortunata, who died at the age of 30.

EDCS-28300217.translation: Plocamus, son of Plocamus, lived 6 years.
EDCS-28300217.summary: This is a tombstone for Plocamus, son of Plocamus, who died at the age of 6.

EDCS-28300219.summary: This is a tombstone for Elemonia Iusta, who died at the age of 35.

EDCS-28300220.summary: This is a tombstone for Flavius Galata, who died at the age of 15.

EDCS-28300221.summary: This is a tombstone for Gellia Soteri, who died at the age of 29.

EDCS-28300222.translation: To the departed spirits, Iunia Myrsine lived 65 years.
EDCS-28300222.summary: This is a tombstone dedicated to the departed spirits for Iunia Myrsine, who lived 65 years.

EDCS-28300223.summary: This is a tombstone for Neoptolemus, identified as a 'collactius' (shared nursling), who died at the age of 9.

EDCS-28300224.translation: To the departed spirits, [he/she] made [this] for Pontia Auxonia, a well-deserving wife.
EDCS-28300224.summary: This is a tombstone dedicated to the departed spirits by an unnamed individual for his/her deserving wife, Pontia Auxonia.

EDCS-28300226.translation: To the departed spirits, Valeria Epiteusis made [this] for her husband Zoticus.
EDCS-28300226.summary: This is a tombstone dedicated to the departed spirits by Valeria Epiteusis for her husband Zoticus.

EDCS-28300269.translation: Claudia Aster of Jerusalem, a captive, managed the affairs. Tiberius Claudius Proculus(?), freedman of the Emperor, I beg you, by law, do not remove my monument; take care of it. She lived 25 years.
EDCS-28300269.summary: A funerary epitaph for Claudia Aster, a captive from Jerusalem, setting conditions for her tombstone and recording her age at death.

EDCS-28300278.translation: To the Divine Shades. For her son T(itus) Flavius Lucrio, and for herself and her kin and freedmen and their descendants.
EDCS-28300278.summary: A funerary dedication (Dis Manibus) by an unknown person for her son T. Flavius Lucrio, and for herself and her family and descendants.

EDCS-28300280.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Iulia Amandas, wife, and Caecilia Cara, aged 13, and Quintus Caecilius Sentianus, aged 9. Quintus Caecilius Sentianus, the father, made [this] for his most cherished wife and most devoted sons.
EDCS-28300280.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulia Amandas, Caecilia Cara, and Quintus Caecilius Sentianus, commissioned by the father and husband, Quintus Caecilius Sentianus.

EDCS-28300281.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Iulia Apollonia, daughter of Lucius, most devoted mother. Gaius Annius Proculus and Gaius Nymphidius Ogulnianus made [this] for themselves and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants, in the front 25 feet, in the land 25 feet.
EDCS-28300281.summary: A funerary dedication for Iulia Apollonia, daughter of Lucius, by Gaius Annius Proculus and Gaius Nymphidius Ogulnianus, who also provided for themselves and their descendants, with dimensions for the plot.

EDCS-28300282.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Iulia Apollonia, daughter of Lucius, most devoted mother. Gaius Annius Proculus and Gaius Nymphidius Ogulnianus.
EDCS-28300282.summary: A funerary dedication for Iulia Apollonia, daughter of Lucius, by Gaius Annius Proculus and Gaius Nymphidius Ogulnianus.

EDCS-28300283.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Iulia Arsinoe. Iulia Asia made [this] for her patroness who deserved well of her, and for herself and her kin and freedmen and their descendants, added, in the front 25 and a half feet, in the land 21 feet.
EDCS-28300283.summary: A funerary dedication for Iulia Arsinoe by her freedwoman Iulia Asia, who also provided for herself and her family, with dimensions for the plot.

EDCS-28300284.translation: Iulia Arsinoe made [this] for herself and her kin and freedmen and their descendants, in the land 16 feet, in the front 13 feet.
EDCS-28300284.summary: A funerary dedication by Iulia Arsinoe for herself and her family, with dimensions for the plot.

EDCS-28300285.translation: To the Divine [Shades]. For Iulia [---]. Gaius Julius [---] for his wife [---] and for himself and [---]
EDCS-28300285.summary: A fragmentary funerary dedication (Dis Manibus) for Iulia [---], possibly by her husband Gaius Julius [---].

EDCS-28300287.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Iulia Paullina, a wife who deserved well. Sextus Iulius Armenius made [this].
EDCS-28300287.summary: A funerary dedication by Sextus Iulius Armenius for his deserving wife, Iulia Paullina.

EDCS-28300288.translation: To the Divine Shades. Iulia Phronime made [this] for her husband who deserved well of her.
EDCS-28300288.summary: A funerary dedication by Iulia Phronime for her deserving husband.

EDCS-28300291.translation: To Iulia Procula, daughter of Tiberius, who lived 29 years, 11 months. Her mother Munatia Helpis made this for her most devoted daughter.
EDCS-28300291.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulia Procula, set up by her mother.

EDCS-28300297.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Iunius Gl[---] made this for himself and for Iunia Trophime, his patroness, his deserving benefactor, and [---]
EDCS-28300297.summary: A funerary inscription likely for Marcus Iunius Gl[---] and his patroness, recording a family burial plot.

EDCS-28300298.translation: To the departed spirits. Caius Iulius Iulianus made this for his well-deserving wife, Iunia Chelidon, and for their son, Iunius Crescens.
EDCS-28300298.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Caius Iulius Iulianus for his wife and son.

EDCS-28300299.translation: To the departed spirits. Iunia Primitiba made this for her relative Sextilia Maxima, and for her relative and excellent husband Annio Maximo, to whom I will give thanks as long as I live.
EDCS-28300299.summary: A funerary inscription by Iunia Primitiba for her relative Sextilia Maxima and her husband Annio Maximo.

EDCS-28300301.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Liburcius Marinus, the father, made this for his son Titus Liburcius Marcius Marinus, the best, who lived 26 years, 2 months, and 23 days.
EDCS-28300301.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Titus Liburcius Marinus for his deceased son.

EDCS-28300302.translation: To the departed spirits. Quintus Licinius Repentinus made this for his most devoted son, Quintus Licinius Primitivus, who lived 5 years and 30 days.
EDCS-28300302.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Quintus Licinius Repentinus for his young son.

EDCS-28300303.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Valerius Celer made this for his dearest wife, Licinia Fortunata, and for himself, and for his descendants and their posterity.
EDCS-28300303.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Lucius Valerius Celer for his wife, himself, and their descendants.

EDCS-28300306.translation: To the departed spirits of Titus Flavius Nearchus.
EDCS-28300306.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to Titus Flavius Nearchus.

EDCS-28300307.text_edition: \n Tito Flavio Augusti liberto Pharnaci fecit Petronia Erotis carissimo viro suo\n
EDCS-28300307.translation: Petronia Erotis made this for her dearest husband, Titus Flavius Augusti liberto Pharnaces.
EDCS-28300307.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Petronia Erotis for her husband, Titus Flavius Pharnaces.

EDCS-28300308.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Flavius Polytimus made this for himself and for his dearest wife Posidonia Tertia, and for his children Flavius Polytimus and Flavia Tertia, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their posterity; also Marcus Pistorius Thallus made this monument for himself and his posterity, placed on the right side upon entering, and for Antonia Onesima, his wife.
EDCS-28300308.summary: A complex funerary inscription by Titus Flavius Polytimus for his family and for Marcus Pistorius Thallus and his family.

EDCS-28300309.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Ulpius Proclianus, father, made this for his most devoted son, Titus Flavius Proclus, who lived 21 years, 11 months, and 15 days.
EDCS-28300309.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Flavius Proclus, set up by his father, Marcus Ulpius Proclianus, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28300310.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Flavius Saturninus, freedman of the emperor, dedicated this for Estaberius Hilarus and Caledia Aphrodite and their children, and their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants, and he commands that his own heir not be troubled.
EDCS-28300310.summary: A funerary dedication by Titus Flavius Saturninus for a family and their descendants, possibly from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28300313.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Flavius Alexander, father, made this for Flavia Efia.
EDCS-28300313.summary: A funerary epitaph for Flavia Efia, made by her father Titus Flavius Alexander, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28300314.translation: To Flavia Lamyra, who lived 25 years. Gaius Julius Spiclus permitted the place, which had been granted, to be possessed with access and entrance.
EDCS-28300314.summary: An epitaph for Flavia Lamyra, granting rights of passage and access to her burial plot, set up by Gaius Julius Spiclus.

EDCS-28300315.translation: To the departed spirits. Tiberius Claudius Primigenius, son of Tiberius Claudius, made this. His son Tiberius Claudius Quintilianus, his stepson Aulus Rutilius Pudens, and his freedmen made this from their own shares.
EDCS-28300315.summary: A funerary monument funded by Tiberius Claudius Primigenius' son, stepson, and freedmen, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28300316.translation: To the departed spirits. Claudius Sotericus Junior, who lived four years, six months, and twenty-one days.
EDCS-28300316.summary: A funerary epitaph for Claudius Sotericus Junior, a young child, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28300317.translation: To the departed spirits. For Tiberius Claudius Telesphorus.
EDCS-28300317.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Tiberius Claudius Telesphorus.

EDCS-28300318.translation: To the departed spirits. For his daughter Tattia. Tiberius Claudius [---] and Tattia [---] made this from their own shares. From Quintus Septius Abascantus, permitted to have access.
EDCS-28300318.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning Tattia, her daughter, Tiberius Claudius, and Quintus Septius Abascantus, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28300319.translation: To the departed spirits. For Claudia Eumenia.
EDCS-28300319.summary: A brief funerary epitaph for Claudia Eumenia.

EDCS-28300321.translation: To the departed spirits. For Claudia Hammonilla. Claudius Severinus and Claudia Hammonilia made this for their most devoted father.
EDCS-28300321.summary: A funerary monument for Claudia Hammonilla and Tiberius Claudius Severus, made by their son Claudius Severinus, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28300322.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Claudia Helpidi.
EDCS-28300322.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Claudia Helpidi, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300325.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Claudia Nice made this for herself and for her son, Claudius Lupercus. Made with permission of Claudius Lupercus. Gaius Nunnidius Fortunatus made this for himself, his children, freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-28300325.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument set up by Claudia Nice for herself and her son, with a second section by Gaius Nunnidius Fortunatus for his own family, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300327.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Claudia Sabina, who lived for 3 years, 10 months, and 22 days. Tiberius Claudius Lusimacus and Claudia Ionice made this for their most dutiful daughter.
EDCS-28300327.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Claudia Sabina, a young child, by her parents Tiberius Claudius Lusimacus and Claudia Ionice, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300328.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Claudia Soteris. Ampliatus made this for his well-deserving wife, who lived for 19 years.
EDCS-28300328.summary: This funerary dedication was made by Ampliatus for his wife Claudia Soteris, who died at the age of 19, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300329.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Claudia Tyche made this for Claudia Victoria, her granddaughter, and for herself.
EDCS-28300329.summary: This funerary dedication was made by Claudia Tyche for her granddaughter Claudia Victoria and for herself, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300330.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Clodia Me[---], by Gelas[---] and her son, who made this for themselves. For Lucius Valerius [---] of Eucaris [---]
EDCS-28300330.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning Clodia, Gelas(ia) and her son, and Lucius Valerius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300331.translation: Clodia Prepusa, freedwoman of Lucius, made this for herself and for her husband Lucius Clodius Atimetus, well-deserving, and for her freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants. This monument will not pass to the heir of the family. Frontage 20 feet, depth 40 feet.
EDCS-28300331.summary: This funerary epitaph for Clodia Prepusa and her husband Lucius Clodius Atimetus includes specific land dimensions and a clause about inheritance, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300332.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Cluentia Successa, granddaughter. Tiberius Claudius Felix made this for his most dutiful daughter, who lived 9 years.
EDCS-28300332.summary: This funerary dedication was made by Tiberius Claudius Felix for his daughter Cluentia Successa, who died at the age of 9, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300333.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Cocceia Dynate made this for her well-deserving husband, Marcus Cocceius Crescens, with whom she lived for 28 years, and for herself, and for their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-28300333.summary: This funerary epitaph records Cocceia Dynate's dedication to her husband Marcus Cocceius Crescens and for herself and their descendants, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300334.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Cocceius Daphnus made this for himself and his descendants, and for Marcus Antonius Agathias and his descendants, and for Marcus Ulpius Domitus and his descendants. Frontage 40 feet, depth 40 feet.
EDCS-28300334.summary: This funerary dedication by Marcus Cocceius Daphnus covers multiple individuals and families, including Marcus Antonius Agathias and Marcus Ulpius Domitus, specifying plot dimensions and dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300335.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Antonius Callistianus, son of Marcus, made this for his most devoted grandmother, Cocceia Doridis, who lived 21 years and 2 months.
EDCS-28300335.summary: A funerary dedication by a grandson for his grandmother, specifying her age at death.

EDCS-28300336.translation: To [---] and Cocceia Secunda for her grandson, who lived 17 years, 9 months, and 5 days.
EDCS-28300336.summary: A funerary dedication for a grandson, mentioning the surviving relative Cocceia Secunda and the deceased's age.

EDCS-28300337.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Cocceia Tyche made this for Sextus Iulius Armhenius, a deserving friend.
EDCS-28300337.summary: A funerary dedication by Cocceia Tyche for her well-deserving friend, Sextus Iulius Armhenius.

EDCS-28300338.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Considius Secundus and Beronice made this for their deserving father, Gaius Considius Ianuarius.
EDCS-28300338.summary: A funerary dedication by children (Considius Secundus and Beronice) for their deserving father, Gaius Considius Ianuarius.

EDCS-28300339.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Licinius Hermes made this for his deserving wife, Considiae Saturninae, and for himself.
EDCS-28300339.summary: A funerary dedication by Marcus Licinius Hermes for his deserving wife, Considiae Saturninae, and for himself.

EDCS-28300340.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Corinthus, his [---] deserving [---] his mother and [---] of the same [---] and [---].
EDCS-28300340.summary: A fragmentary funerary dedication mentioning a deceased named Corinthus and his mother, with incomplete details about the dedicator(s).

EDCS-28300341.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. And to Sentius Statianus, my pupil. Publius Cornelius Fortunatus has donated to Terentius Bitalinus and Sentia Laid me (this sarcophagus), with 12 sarcophagi, of which three must be made with his own expenses, the one on the right side of the Via Flavia, given with the key, and I want it to be donated to those listed above and to my freedmen and freedwomen.
EDCS-28300341.summary: A complex funerary inscription detailing the donation of 12 sarcophagi, with specific instructions for their construction and dedication, by Publius Cornelius Fortunatus.

EDCS-28300342.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Cornelius Pistos made this for his deserving wife, Cornelia Eutychia.
EDCS-28300342.summary: A funerary dedication by Cornelius Pistos for his deserving wife, Cornelia Eutychia.

EDCS-28300343.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Culciae Metropoli tympanistriae Matris Deum Magnae Virius Quinti Porius\n
EDCS-28300343.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Culcia Metropol, tympanist of the Great Mother Goddess, Virius Quinti Porius.
EDCS-28300343.summary: A funerary dedication for Culcia Metropol, a musician associated with the cult of the Great Mother Goddess.

EDCS-28300344.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Gaius P. Dexter made this for his most devoted wife, Baebia Ingenua, deserving.
EDCS-28300344.summary: A funerary dedication by Gaius P. Dexter for his devoted and deserving wife, Baebia Ingenua.

EDCS-28300345.translation: To the departed spirits of Didia Helpidis and her freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-28300345.summary: This is a tomb inscription dedicated to Didia Helpidis and her descendants.

EDCS-28300348.translation: Euhodus, slave of our Emperor, and Vennonia Apphis made this, having bought the spot from Valeria Trophime, for themselves and their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-28300348.summary: This is a tomb inscription set up by Euhodus and Vennonia Apphis for themselves and their descendants, indicating the purchase of burial space.

EDCS-28300349.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuli Eutychetis dius Deuther ori optimo\n
EDCS-28300349.translation: To the departed spirits of Iulius Eutyches, the most excellent Deuther, his associate.
EDCS-28300349.summary: This is a tomb inscription dedicated to Iulius Eutyches and his associate Deuther.

EDCS-28300376.summary: This inscription likely honors Caius Cuspius Pansa, who held the office of aedile.

EDCS-28300377.translation: To Casellius, a worthy aedile for the state, and Albucius.
EDCS-28300377.summary: This inscription honors two individuals, Casellius, an aedile deemed worthy for public service, and Albucius.

EDCS-28300383.translation: Magonius requests [this] for Cuspius Pansa, aedile.

EDCS-28800242.translation: Hysocryse, the boy Verpa greets you.
EDCS-28800242.summary: This inscription is a personal greeting from Verpa to Hysocryse, likely from a young boy.

EDCS-28800245.translation: Vettius, a true lover of cooking.
EDCS-28800245.summary: This is a playful epitaph for Vettius, a cook, highlighting his passion for his profession.

EDCS-28800265.translation: Invincible Castrensis, may your three gods be favorable to you, and also to whoever reads this. Hedone wishes well to the reader. Hedone says: here drinks are bought with money; if you give a penny, you will drink better; if you give wine, you will drink Falernian wine. Greetings to the good Castrensis. [1]
EDCS-28800265.summary: This is an epitaph or a dedication, possibly for a tavern owner named Castrensis, with a humorous plea for patronage and good fortune, dated to the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-28800343.summary: This is a brief inscription likely marking the grave or shop of Verecunnus, a bookseller, with a number possibly indicating his age or quantity.

EDCS-28800415.translation: Whoever you are, from now on forget your lost Greeks; Narcissus writes [this].
EDCS-28800415.summary: This is an enigmatic inscription, possibly a literary jest or a statement about forgetting classical Greek, attributed to Narcissus.

EDCS-28800437.translation: Samius, to hang up for Cornelius.

EDCS-28800566.translation: With spells, Circe changed her companions into animals. Olyxis.
EDCS-28800566.summary: This is a literary allusion to Homer's Odyssey, referencing Circe's magical transformation of Odysseus' men, possibly inscribed by someone named Olyxis.

EDCS-28800893.translation: Greetings, Iucunda and Ias, of August! [or Greetings, Iucunda and Ias, belonging to the Augustiani.]
EDCS-28800893.summary: This is a simple greeting to two individuals, Iucunda and Ias, possibly associated with the 'Augustiani' group or the imperial household.

EDCS-28800894.translation: Iucunda, Augustiana, whoever you are, whoever you are.

EDCS-28800897.summary: A short personal note wishing Pyramis well.

EDCS-28800908.translation: Veneria vomited out a penis for Maximo during the entire vintage and left a full of excrement belly. [---]

EDCS-28800914.summary: This inscription appears to be a complex funerary text or dedication involving multiple names and possibly a plea or statement about relationships.

EDCS-28800919.summary: This is a brief inscription wishing well-being to Spendusa and Bessaxla.

EDCS-28800922.translation: Pusina, many love you. Anicetus [loves?] your... [obscene reference].

EDCS-28800925.summary: This is a simple inscription wishing well-being to Synoris.

EDCS-28800927.inscription_type: tituli operum, tituli possessionis, tituli sacri
EDCS-28800927.translation: For Venus is a trickster, because she asks for my blood, she wishes for herself in the tumult of the streets that she may navigate well, which Ario also asks for his wife?
EDCS-28800927.summary: This inscription is a curse or a complex personal reflection involving Venus, possibly a woman, and a desire for safe travels, perhaps related to a lawsuit or dispute.

EDCS-28800928.translation: I put forth [this] because he desires to give birth to my blood.
EDCS-28800928.summary: This inscription seems to be a personal statement concerning a desire for offspring or a biological connection.

EDCS-28800929.summary: This is a dedication or address to Aulus Cominius.

EDCS-28800934.translation: Passerina, one and a half shares; Romana, one and a half shares. Eroticus sends greetings to Leucas.
EDCS-28800934.summary: This inscription appears to be a record of property division or inheritance shares between Passerina and Romana, with a greeting from Eroticus to Leucas.

EDCS-28800938.translation: Eros, be well, may it be fortunate.

EDCS-28800945.summary: A very brief inscription, possibly names or place markers, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28801099.summary: This inscription records the name and marital status of Eppia, wife of Lucius Numisius Rarhi[---].

EDCS-28801102.summary: This inscription records the name 'Tiburtinus' associated with a grove.

EDCS-28801105.summary: This inscription records the name 'Crescentis' associated with a place.

EDCS-28801110.translation: Eulalus to Vera, greetings everywhere. Eulalus, a man. Eulalus, a woman. Farewell, Eulale.
EDCS-28801110.summary: This is a personal note, likely a graffito, from Eulalus to Eulale, mentioning Vera and possibly indicating gender roles or identities.

EDCS-28801111.translation: Eulale, fare well with your wife Vera, and may you have good intercourse with her and Favonius, the equestrian.
EDCS-28801111.summary: This is a personal wish or perhaps a curse, conveying good wishes for the sexual health and relationships of Eulale, Vera, and Favonius.

EDCS-28801112.summary: This is a very brief funerary or ownership inscription identifying Romanus as the son of Plancus.

EDCS-28801114.summary: This is a simple name inscription, likely identifying an individual named Livius Balbinus, possibly on a tombstone or object.

EDCS-28801123.translation: To Numerius, son of Com(minus) [or other abbreviated name].
EDCS-28801123.summary: This is a dedication or ownership inscription identifying Numerius, the son of Com(minus) or a similarly abbreviated name.

EDCS-28801124.summary: This is a dedication or ownership inscription identifying Marcus Sindius.

EDCS-28801126.translation: Aphrodite, the 'issa' (a term of endearment or status).
EDCS-28801126.summary: This inscription names Aphrodite, possibly a freedwoman or slave, with an affectionate or status-related epithet.

EDCS-28801129.summary: This is a personal inscription, possibly a gift or dedication, from Genialis to Euch(e).

EDCS-28801131.translation: Livia to Alexander, greetings. If you are well, I am not greatly concerned. If you are happy, I rejoice.
EDCS-28801131.summary: This is a personal letter or message, possibly scratched on a wall, from Livia to Alexander expressing conditional well-being and affection.

EDCS-28801138.summary: This inscription names Potens and links him to 'Ambition', possibly representing a person, a concept, or a place.

EDCS-28801140.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription that begins to name a dedicator, Successus, and likely lists others associated with him.

EDCS-28801141.translation: You seek Falacem and Fabius in the tribe of Cotinus.
EDCS-28801141.summary: This inscription appears to be a record of individuals, Falacem and Fabius, belonging to the tribe of Cotinus.

EDCS-28801144.translation: Carus, son of Tio[...], and Cacus.
EDCS-28801144.summary: This inscription lists two individuals, Carus and Cacus, with a partial patronymic for Carus.

EDCS-28801148.summary: This is a dedicatory or greeting inscription to Publius Cornelius Iullus, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-28801152.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Publius G. Nymphius.

EDCS-28801154.summary: This inscription appears to be a personal name, Menedemeruminus.

EDCS-28801169.summary: This is a short funerary or farewell inscription for a person named Trophime.

EDCS-28801171.summary: This inscription records the name Cerialis Regentinus, possibly a father and son or a cognomen.

EDCS-28801174.summary: This is a short funerary or farewell inscription for two individuals, Cerialis and Faustila.

EDCS-28801175.summary: This inscription is a personal name, Menedemerumenus, likely a variation of EDCS-28801154.

EDCS-28801176.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a slave named Iarinus.

EDCS-28801186.summary: This inscription likely refers to a person named Chloe associated with a military camp.

EDCS-28801188.summary: This is an incomplete inscription, likely a personal name, possibly a Roman citizen with a cognomen.

EDCS-28801192.translation: Gaius Julius Primigenius [lies] here. Why do you delay?
EDCS-28801192.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Gaius Julius Primigenius, inviting passersby to reflect.

EDCS-28801196.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly the beginning of a narrative or legal text.

EDCS-28900203.translation: Maxis, son of A[---], son of Maxis, son of Maxis.
EDCS-28900203.summary: This inscription records a patronymic for someone named Maxis, possibly indicating multiple generations.

EDCS-28900279.translation: To Iustus, Felicitas, and the gods, and Munius. On the 13th day before the Kalends of December, for Licarius, son of [---]EAVG, Catetumoi[---], son of I Cnias.
EDCS-28900279.summary: This is a dedication dated to November 18th, possibly funerary or dedicatory, mentioning several individuals.

EDCS-28900297.translation: Farewell, Castrensis; farewell, Castrensis; Farewell, Actius; farewell, Castrensis; farewell, Anicetus.
EDCS-28900297.summary: This inscription contains a series of farewells, likely funerary epitaphs or dedications, mentioning individuals associated with 'Castrensis'.

EDCS-28900363.summary: This inscription is likely a playful or affectionate funerary message from Victrix to Victory.

EDCS-29100350.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely a funerary dedication to members of the Vidii family for Mavinia.

EDCS-29100351.translation: T. V. Formosus, tunics, a slave of ISSF[3]TIIIITFNTRIQ, son of N[3]ICI[3]IAAISISSVS. Postima. Caius.
EDCS-29100351.summary: This inscription appears to be a funerary epitaph with names and possibly familial relationships, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29300348.translation: He who writes is loved, he who reads is buggered, he who listens is itchy, he is a sodomite, he who passes by is a sodomite. May bears eat me and may I (have sex with) the writing one who reads.
EDCS-29300348.summary: This is a humorous and crude graffiti, likely from the Roman Imperial period, found scratched on a surface.

EDCS-29300397.summary: A personal note, likely a greeting or short message between individuals, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29300423.translation: Ania Acti, the camp follower, farewell. Anicetiane.
EDCS-29300423.summary: This is a farewell message from Ania Acti, identified as a 'castrensis' (likely a camp follower or someone associated with a military camp), to Anicetiane, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29300433.translation: Propero, farewell my Sava. Make me love you.
EDCS-29300433.summary: A personal message expressing affection and a desire for love, likely a dedication or a note between lovers, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29300434.translation: Tertiani live here. Crescens, the sodomite, farewell.
EDCS-29300434.summary: This is likely a marker for a dwelling, possibly a tomb or a house, identifying its inhabitants and including a farewell and a potentially offensive nickname, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29300435.translation: Miccio, you broke your bag while shitting for your father. Consider the state of Miccio.
EDCS-29300435.summary: A crude and humorous inscription likely found as graffiti, mocking someone named Miccio, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29300441.summary: A short farewell message, possibly on a tombstone or a personal note, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29300445.translation: ] / Of CAECEI C[3] / Sex() 130 C / Farewell() 25 25 / Secun() 75 75 / Amon() 75 / Thirst [3] / Scava LX[3] / Sedlatus [3] / Hispanus [3] / Iutto L[3] L[
EDCS-29300445.summary: This inscription appears to be a list of names with associated numbers, possibly related to accounts, goods, or measurements, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29300446.translation: Ser() 50 / Amonium 75 / Valerium 25 / Vetium 150 / Iulium 75 / Lucius Plotus 100 / Sex() 50
EDCS-29300446.summary: This inscription is a list of names with numerical values, likely related to accounts, debts, or quantities, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29300449.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name Faustus Foannus.

EDCS-29300451.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name Quintus Mucius.

EDCS-29300457.translation: Caius Alleius Astragalus was curator from the fourth day before the Nones of December until the sixth day before the Ides of December, when Marcus Agrippa and Titus Statilius were consuls, for the turma of Ce[---]ria, daughter of Caius Iulius Helenus, belonging to Caius Iulius Hilarus.
EDCS-29300457.summary: An inscription recording the tenure of office of Caius Alleius Astragalus as curator for a specific period during the consulship of Agrippa and Statilius.

EDCS-29300470.translation: [...] Viriota 150, Valerius? 125, Amonius 175, Servilius 100, Marcus 150, Sequanus 175, Sedulatus 125, Viriodus? 175, Ltofag? 100, Onarto 175 // [...] Sextius 100, Viriota 150, Valerius? 175, Valerius? 175.
EDCS-29300470.summary: A list of names with numerical values, possibly recording military personnel or rations.

EDCS-29300473.summary: A funerary inscription recording the names Lucius Postumius and his father.

EDCS-29300475.translation: On November 6th, Surus Petilius provided the regalia for Marcus Faustus Silon, for reasons of honor, as a freedman of Surus.
EDCS-29300475.summary: An inscription recording the presentation of honors by Surus Petilius to Marcus Faustus Silon.

EDCS-29300476.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name Gaius Gaudanus.

EDCS-29300477.translation: May Methe and Cominiaes, who love the Atellan play, cherish Chrestus in their hearts; may Venus Pompeiana be favorable to both, and may they live always in harmony.
EDCS-29300477.summary: A dedicatory or wishful inscription for Methe and Cominiaes, expressing hopes for their love of theatre and their relationship, under the favor of Venus Pompeiana.

EDCS-29300478.summary: A dedicatory inscription to Iustus, wishing him good fortune.

EDCS-29300480.translation: To the divine Augustus, with good fortune.
EDCS-29300480.summary: A dedicatory inscription to the deified Augustus, wishing him good fortune.

EDCS-29300516.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Marcus Maius, possibly a dedication or funerary text.

EDCS-29600510.translation: Verus taught me to give and to receive and to prohibit...
EDCS-29600510.summary: This inscription records a lesson from Verus about interaction, possibly from a school setting.

EDCS-29600512.translation: Secundus, you did not keep yourself sober, lest the reading would not have been...
EDCS-29600512.summary: This inscription seems to be a personal note or admonition, possibly related to study or a literary gathering.

EDCS-29600513.translation: Quintus Naevius asks you, Tullius Severus, to bring him back to the [?], the veteran asks you, farewell.
EDCS-29600513.summary: This is a personal request from Quintus Naevius to Tullius Severus, asking for a veteran to be brought back, likely a private message on a small object.

EDCS-29600627.translation: Amplatus asks that Gaius Calventius Sittius, the duovir for dispensing justice, [honor] Holconius Priscus.
EDCS-29600627.summary: This inscription is a request for a magistrate, the duovir Gaius Calventius Sittius, to honor Holconius Priscus, likely from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-29600628.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Popidius Cuspis, likely a personal request or dedication.

EDCS-29600649.translation: To Numerius Popidius Rufus, duovir for dispensing justice.
EDCS-29600649.summary: This is an official inscription honoring Numerius Popidius Rufus, who held the office of duovir iuris dicundi, likely in a Roman colony or municipium.

EDCS-29600651.summary: This is an inscription honoring Lucius Albucius, who served as an aedile, likely marking a public work or benefaction.

EDCS-29600661.translation: I ask you, make Cuspis an aedile.
EDCS-29600661.summary: This is a public appeal or plea for Cuspis to be appointed as an aedile, likely from a voting context.

EDCS-29600662.translation: I ask you, make Gavius Rufus duovir.
EDCS-29600662.summary: This is a public appeal or plea for Gavius Rufus to be appointed as a duovir, likely from a voting context.

EDCS-29600690.translation: I ask you to make Fervenium Celerem an aedile.
EDCS-29600690.summary: A public request to elect Fervenium Celerem as an aedile, likely from the Roman Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-29600695.translation: I ask you to make Secundus IIvir. [---] B [---] asks.
EDCS-29600695.summary: A public request to elect Secundus as a duovir (IIvir), with an incomplete indication of the proposer, likely from a Roman municipality.

EDCS-29600697.translation: I ask you to make Cornelius an aedile, worthy of public office. Anice makes this proposal.
EDCS-29600697.summary: A public request to elect Cornelius as an aedile, emphasizing his worthiness, proposed by Anice, likely from a Roman municipality.

EDCS-29600706.summary: An inscription identifying Ceius Secundus as a duovir (IIvir), likely from a Roman municipality and dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-29600709.translation: I ask you to make Lucius Ceius Secundus duovir. Demetrius asks.
EDCS-29600709.summary: A public request to elect Lucius Ceius Secundus as a duovir (IIvir), proposed by Demetrius, likely from a Roman municipality.

EDCS-29600713.summary: An inscription recording that Anicetus wrote 'Isthmus', possibly referring to a literary work or a geographical location, with uncertain dating.

EDCS-29600744.summary: A record of numerical counts, possibly related to votes, land divisions, or military units, with uncertain context and date.

EDCS-29600753.summary: An incomplete inscription mentioning the name 'Quisquis A[---]rius', likely a personal name or a dedication, with unknown context and date.

EDCS-29600760.summary: An inscription listing two personal names, Secundus and Primus, possibly indicating individuals involved in a transaction or event, with uncertain context and date.

EDCS-29600773.translation: To a man, Marcus Casellius Marcellus.
EDCS-29600773.summary: A dedication to Marcus Casellius Marcellus, likely a funerary or honorific inscription, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29600781.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Verus, identified as Ikarus, a pederast, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29600956.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary text, likely a compliment or endearment, with its context and date unknown.

EDCS-29600959.summary: This is a maker's inscription, possibly for an object, with the maker identified as Narnius, from an unknown date and context.

EDCS-29900343.translation: To the Divine Shades. Maxxima passed away in peace, having lived sweetly for 6 years and 7 months and 1 day, married to Vincentius, the builder.
EDCS-29900343.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Maxxima, who died at the age of 6 years, 7 months, and 1 day, and was married to a builder named Vincentius, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-29900347.translation: [---] called [---]nia lived for [---] years, to the well-deserving Ia/[---]us, deposited on the [---] April, under consuls Rufinus and Eusebius.
EDCS-29900347.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for a woman named [---]nia, who died in April during the consulship of Rufinus and Eusebius, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100392.translation: Under consuls Apronianus and Paetinus. The roof tile of Marcus Rutilius, of Brutian make.
EDCS-30100392.summary: This inscription records a roof tile belonging to Marcus Rutilius, made of Brutian material, dated to the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus (likely 164 AD).

EDCS-30200050.translation: From the estates of Arria Fadilla's Caepionian properties, under consuls Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-30200050.summary: This inscription indicates property ownership or management related to Arria Fadilla's Caepionian estates, dated to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus (likely 164 AD).

EDCS-30200051.translation: From the estates of Arria Fadilla's Caepionian properties, belonging to Cassia Dorides, under consuls Glabrio and Torquatus.
EDCS-30200051.summary: This inscription records property belonging to Cassia Dorides, from Arria Fadilla's Caepionian estates, dated to the consulship of Glabrio and Torquatus (likely 91 AD).

EDCS-30200052.translation: From the estates of Arria Fadilla's Caepionian properties, belonging to Julius Lupio, under consuls Apronianus and Paetinus.
EDCS-30200052.summary: This inscription notes property belonging to Julius Lupio, from Arria Fadilla's Caepionian estates, dated to the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus (likely 164 AD).

EDCS-30200482.translation: To Annia Agrippina, wife of Gaius Iulius Apollonius, a decurial [official] in Rome, also a tribune and aedile's clerk, honored with a veil, [responsible for] the curator of the gladiatorial games for three days. The place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-30200482.summary: This is a funerary monument honoring Annia Agrippina, wife of Gaius Iulius Apollonius, detailing her husband's civic and administrative roles, including his curatorship of gladiatorial games, and indicating the burial place was granted by the decurions, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30301526.translation: From the estates of Sentius Caepio, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-30301526.summary: This inscription records the origin of goods from the estates of Sentius Caepio, dated to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-30301528.translation: From the estates of Caius Curiatius Cosanus, of the Caepionian family, and of Sextus Alphius Amandus, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-30301528.summary: This inscription indicates the origin of goods from the estates of Caius Curiatius Cosanus and Sextus Alphius Amandus, dated to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-30301529.translation: From the estates of Caius Curiatius Cosanus, of the Caepionian family, and of Sextus Alphius Amandus, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-30301529.summary: This inscription indicates the origin of goods from the estates of Caius Curiatius Cosanus and Sextus Alphius Amandus, dated to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-30301641.translation: To Gnaeus Sentius Felix, son of Gnaeus, grandson of Gnaeus, of the Teretina tribe, elected by decree of the decurions as aedile, elected by decree of the decurions as a decurion, quaestor of the Ostian treasury, twice duovir, quaestor of the youth, who in this year was the first ever to be elected a decurion and to become quaestor of the treasury and was designated for the next year as duovir, quinquennalis curator of maritime ships, elected gratis among the shipowners of the Adriatic Sea and for the chariot races of the wine market, patron of the guild of scribes of wax tablets, and librarians, and lictors, and messengers, and also of the money-changers and wine merchants from the city, and also of the measurers of grain for Ceres Augusta, and also of the guild of boatmen and ferrymen for the crossing of Lucullus, and of the dendrophori and the togati from the forum, and from the sacomarium, and of public freedmen and slaves, and of oil merchants and of the youth, and of the cisiani and veterans of Augustus, and also of the beneficiarii of the procurator of Augustus, and of fishermen and boatmen, curator of the youth games. To his most indulgent father, Gnaeus Sentius Lucilius Gamala Clodianus.
EDCS-30301641.summary: This is a lengthy funerary inscription honoring Gnaeus Sentius Felix, detailing his extensive career in public office and guild memberships, dedicated by his son.

EDCS-30400513.translation: Chresimus, slave of Lucius Munatius Crescentis, [his] doliare work, from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-30400513.summary: This inscription records doliare (likely pottery) work originating from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, attributed to Chresimus, and dated to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-30400523.translation: Chresimus, slave of Lucius Munatius Crescentis, [his] doliare work, from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-30400523.summary: This inscription records doliare (likely pottery) work originating from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, attributed to Chresimus, and dated to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-30700001.summary: This is a simple tombstone recording the name and age at death of Titus Cornelius Quartio.

EDCS-30700002.translation: Dassius lived 80 years, slave of Secundus(?).
EDCS-30700002.summary: This tombstone records the name, age at death, and possible owner of a slave named Dassius.

EDCS-30700012.translation: Vicaria Calybe, freedwoman of Publius, lived 35 years.
EDCS-30700012.summary: This is a tombstone recording the name and age at death of Vicaria Calybe, a freedwoman.

EDCS-30700017.translation: Lucius Cassius Kapitonianus, who lived 3 years and 20 days. Lucius Cassius [---]dus and Volcasia Spatale, parents, made [this] for their well-deserving son.
EDCS-30700017.summary: This tombstone records the death of Lucius Cassius Kapitonianus at a young age, dedicated by his parents.

EDCS-30700021.translation: To Gaius Publicius Quintus, who lived 13 years. Clodia Atteis made this for her sweetest son and for herself.
EDCS-30700021.summary: A tombstone erected by Clodia Atteis for her son Quintus, who died at the age of 13.

EDCS-30700022.summary: An epitaph recording the lifespan of a woman named Tyche.

EDCS-30700025.translation: Quintus Minucius Optatus, eye doctor, is buried here.
EDCS-30700025.summary: A tombstone for Quintus Minucius Optatus, an oculist.

EDCS-31200042.translation: Belonging to Narcissus, freedman of the Emperor.
EDCS-31200042.summary: An ownership mark indicating property belonging to Narcissus, a freedman of the emperor.

EDCS-31200121.translation: Arunceia Acte, daughter of Spurius, as magistrate, donated two tunics and a cloak, shaved linen cloths, and an aerian lamp to the Bona Dea.
EDCS-31200121.summary: A dedication by Arunceia Acte, a magistrate, to the Bona Dea, listing several donated items.

EDCS-31200128.translation: Here lie the remains of Marcus Aemilius Soter, son of Marcus, of the Poblilia voting tribe, from the town of Osca, who was honored with torcs, armbands, and phalerae by the Emperor, a discharged soldier, a veteran of the Ninth Legion of Hispania.
EDCS-31200128.summary: A tombstone for Marcus Aemilius Soter, a veteran of the Ninth Legion, who received military honors.

EDCS-31200409.translation: From the estates of Marcus Annius Verus, workshop of Annius Zosimus, of Germanic origin, under the consulship of Pontianus and Atilianus.
EDCS-31200409.summary: An inscription related to the production of pottery from the estates of Marcus Annius Verus, workshop of Annius Zosimus, during the consulship of Pontianus and Atilianus.

EDCS-31200412.translation: Pottery work from the Iviliarian potteries of Aelia Severa, L. C. [initials or names].
EDCS-31200412.summary: A mark indicating pottery produced in the Iviliarian potteries belonging to Aelia Severa.

EDCS-31200479.translation: Under the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus, from the estates of Tiberius Claudius Maximus, Isiacum [possibly a type of ware or estate name].
EDCS-31200479.summary: An inscription noting pottery production from the estates of Tiberius Claudius Maximus during the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-31200485.translation: Pottery work from the Iviliarian potteries of Aelia Severa, L. C. [initials or names].
EDCS-31200485.summary: A mark indicating pottery produced in the Iviliarian potteries belonging to Aelia Severa.

EDCS-31300216.translation: In the consulship of Appius Iunius Silanus and Publius Silio Nerva, it was the day before the Nones of April in the Putela district. On the Ides of April, she was placed here.
EDCS-31300216.summary: A funerary inscription recording the burial date of a woman in the Putela district, dating to the consulship of Silanus and Nerva.

EDCS-31300247.translation: To the gods of the departed. Publius Cornelius Proculus, son of Publius, a scribe of duumviral rank in Capua, ordered this to be made by his will, under the supervision of Cornelia Procula, daughter of Publius.
EDCS-31300247.summary: A funerary inscription for Publius Cornelius Proculus, a scribe of duumviral rank in Capua, funded by his will and overseen by Cornelia Procula.

EDCS-31300257.translation: Gratus, freedman of Caesar, minister, gave this gift to the Annona Augusta of the Roman people by order of Quintus Cotrius, duumvir with jurisdiction, and Caius Annius Marulus and Decimus Alfidius Hypsaeus, duumvirs for the care of public roads, buildings, and sacred sites, in the consulship of Marcus Servilius and Lucius Aelius.
EDCS-31300257.summary: A dedication to Annona Augusta by a freedman and minister, commissioned by local magistrates, dating to the consulship of Servilius and Aelius.

EDCS-31300258.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-31300258.translation: For Alleia Maia, daughter of Maia, priestess of Venus and Ceres, at public expense, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-31300258.summary: An inscription recording a public commission for Alleia Maia, priestess of Venus and Ceres, funded by the decurions.

EDCS-31300259.translation: Marcus Mundicius Malchio and Marcus Clodius Agatho, as magistrates, had this made from pooled funds.
EDCS-31300259.summary: An inscription indicating that two magistrates, Marcus Mundicius Malchio and Marcus Clodius Agatho, funded and supervised a construction or monument.

EDCS-31300260.translation: To Lucius Laturnius Gratus, a villager and minister.
EDCS-31300260.summary: A dedication to Lucius Laturnius Gratus, identified as a paganus (villager/local resident) and a minister.

EDCS-31300313.translation: For the safety of Augustus. To Iuppiter Olbius Sabaeus. Longus, [a soldier?] of the legion [serving under?] Aurelius Commodus.
EDCS-31300313.summary: A religious dedication to Iuppiter Olbius Sabaeus for the emperor's safety, mentioning a soldier and potentially dating to the reign of Commodus.

EDCS-31500397.translation: To the gods of the departed. For Marcus Antonius Isido, a pausarius (sickle-bearer?) and duplicarius (sergeant) of a quadrireme, who served in the Dacian and Egyptian fleets. He lived 48 years and served 26 years. Maria Eutychia to her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-31500397.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Antonius Isido, a naval officer who served in the Dacian and Egyptian fleets, with his age and service length recorded.

EDCS-31500398.translation: To the gods of the departed. For Gaius Lysius Tertullus, a soldier of the praetorian fleet at Misenum, of the ship Minerva, nationality Dalmatian, in the century of Pius, who served for 3 years. To his well-deserving husband.
EDCS-31500398.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaius Lysius Tertullus, a Dalmatian soldier serving in the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, noting his ship and service duration.

EDCS-31600156.translation: From the barrels of Cn. Domitius Carpus, from the property of Domitia Lucilla, in the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.
EDCS-31600156.summary: A record of goods or property transfer related to barrels belonging to Cn. Domitius Carpus and from the estate of Domitia Lucilla, dated by the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-31600189.translation: From the property of Cn. Domitius Carpus, to be worked by Licinianian stonecutters for Domitia Lucilla, under consuls Apronianus and Paetinus.
EDCS-31600189.summary: This inscription records a stonecutting commission for Domitia Lucilla during the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-31600190.translation: From the property of Myrtilus, slaves of Domitia Lucilla, to be worked by Licinianian stonecutters for Paetinus and Apronianus, under consuls.
EDCS-31600190.summary: This inscription records a stonecutting commission for Paetinus and Apronianus, attributed to slaves of Domitia Lucilla, during their consulship.

EDCS-31600192.translation: The work of Q. Oppius Iustus, to be worked by Licinianian stonecutters for Domitia Lucilla, under consuls Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31600192.summary: This inscription records a stonecutting commission for Domitia Lucilla by Q. Oppius Iustus, during the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31600197.translation: From the property of Anicetus, slave of Domitia Lucilla, to be worked by Licinianian stonecutters for Paetinus and Apronianus, under consuls.
EDCS-31600197.summary: This inscription records a stonecutting commission for Paetinus and Apronianus, attributed to Anicetus, a slave of Domitia Lucilla, during their consulship.

EDCS-31600198.translation: Myrtilus, from the property of Domitia Lucilla, from the Licinianian estate, under consuls Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31600198.summary: This inscription records a transaction or work involving Myrtilus and Domitia Lucilla's property during the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31600199.translation: The work of Q. Oppius Iustus, to be worked by Licinianian stonecutters for Domitia Lucilla, under consuls Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31600203.translation: From the property of Q. Oppius Verecundus, to be worked by Licinianian stonecutters for Domitia Lucilla, under consuls Apronianus and Paetinus.
EDCS-31600203.summary: This inscription records a stonecutting commission for Domitia Lucilla by Q. Oppius Verecundus, during the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-31600204.translation: From the property of Anicetus, slave of Domitia Lucilla, to be worked by Licinianian stonecutters for Paetinus and Apronianus, under consuls.
EDCS-31600204.summary: This inscription records a stonecutting commission for Paetinus and Apronianus, attributed to Anicetus, a slave of Domitia Lucilla, during their consulship.

EDCS-31600205.translation: From the property of Anicetus, slave of Domitia Lucilla, to be worked by Licinianian stonecutters for Paetinus and Apronianus, under consuls.
EDCS-31600205.summary: This inscription records a stonecutting commission for Paetinus and Apronianus, attributed to Anicetus, a slave of Domitia Lucilla, during their consulship.

EDCS-31600206.translation: Anicetus, for Domitia Lucilla, to be made into jars (doliare) in the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31600206.summary: This inscription records the production of jars (doliare) for Domitia Lucilla by Anicetus, dated to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31600207.translation: From the Licinian lands of Domitia Lucilla, for Gnaeus Domitius Carpus, to be made into jars (doliare) in the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.
EDCS-31600207.summary: This inscription records the production of jars (doliare) from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, for Gnaeus Domitius Carpus, dated to the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-31600208.translation: From the Licinian lands of Domitia Lucilla, for the slaves of Myrtilus, to be made into jars (doliare) in the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31600208.summary: This inscription records the production of jars (doliare) from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, for the slaves of Myrtilus, dated to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31600209.translation: From the Licinian lands of Domitia Lucilla, the work of Quintus Oppius Iustus, to be made into jars (doliare) in the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31600209.summary: This inscription records the production of jars (doliare) from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, attributed to Quintus Oppius Iustus, dated to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31600210.translation: From the Licinian lands of Domitia Lucilla, the work of Quintus Oppius Iustus, to be made into jars (doliare) in the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31600210.summary: This inscription records the production of jars (doliare) from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, attributed to Quintus Oppius Iustus, dated to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31600221.translation: From the Licinian lands of Domitia Lucilla, for Gnaeus Domitius Carpus, to be made into jars (doliare) in the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.
EDCS-31600221.summary: This inscription records the production of jars (doliare) from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, for Gnaeus Domitius Carpus, dated to the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-31600228.translation: From the Licinian lands of Domitia Lucilla, for Gnaeus Domitius Trophimus, to be made into jars (doliare) in the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.
EDCS-31600228.summary: This inscription records the production of jars (doliare) from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, for Gnaeus Domitius Trophimus, dated to the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-31700052.translation: To the departed spirits. To Hilarion Pammus, whom the gods have taken, who lived 15 years, 11 months, and 23 days. [---] most beloved. [---]
EDCS-31700052.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Hilarion Pammus, who died at the age of 15, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-31700091.translation: To be made into jars (doliare) from the estates of our Caesar, Caius Aquilius Aprilis, in the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31700091.summary: This inscription records the production of jars (doliare) from the estates of Caius Aquilius Aprilis, dated to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31700095.translation: From the potteries of our Caesar, for Caius Calpetanius Hermet, to be made into jars (doliare) in the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31700095.summary: This inscription records the production of jars (doliare) from the imperial potteries, for Caius Calpetanius Hermet, dated to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31700096.translation: Tiles from the potteries of our Caesar, from Gaius Calpetanius Hermes, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31700096.summary: This inscription records tiles produced by Gaius Calpetanius Hermes from the imperial potteries during the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31700097.translation: Tiles from the potteries of our Caesar, from Gaius Calpetanius Hermes, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31700097.summary: This inscription records tiles produced by Gaius Calpetanius Hermes from the imperial potteries during the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31700098.translation: Tiles from the estates of our Caesar, from Gaius Aquilius Aprilis, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31700098.summary: This inscription records tiles produced by Gaius Aquilius Aprilis from imperial estates during the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31700110.translation: Tiles from the potteries of our Caesar, from Gaius Calpetanius Hermes, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31700110.summary: This inscription records tiles produced by Gaius Calpetanius Hermes from the imperial potteries during the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31700121.translation: Tiles from the estates of our Caesar, from Gaius Aquilius Aprilis, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31700121.summary: This inscription records tiles produced by Gaius Aquilius Aprilis from imperial estates during the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31700122.translation: Tiles from the estates of our Caesar, from Gaius Aquilius Aprilis, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31700122.summary: This inscription records tiles produced by Gaius Aquilius Aprilis from imperial estates during the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31700652.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Gaius Caesar Augustus Germanicus (Caligula).

EDCS-31800208.translation: Lucius Bruttidius Augustalis made this tiled work from the potteries of our Caesar, under the consulship of Propinquus and Ambibulus.
EDCS-31800208.summary: This inscription records a tiled work made by Lucius Bruttidius Augustalis from the imperial potteries during the consulship of Propinquus and Ambibulus.

EDCS-31800209.translation: Lucius Bruttidius Augustalis made this tiled work from the potteries of our Caesar, under the consulship of Propinquus and Ambibulus.
EDCS-31800209.summary: This inscription records a tiled work made by Lucius Bruttidius Augustalis from the imperial potteries during the consulship of Propinquus and Ambibulus.

EDCS-31800228.translation: Tiled work from Claudius Celsus, under the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.
EDCS-31800228.summary: This inscription records tiled work by Claudius Celsus during the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-31800239.translation: Lucius Bruttidius Augustalis made this brickwork from the tiles of our Caesar, in the consulship of Propinquus and Ambibulus.
EDCS-31800239.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription by Lucius Bruttidius Augustalis for brickwork made from Imperial tiles, dated to the consulship of Propinquus and Ambibulus.

EDCS-31800244.translation: Lucius Bruttidius Augustalis made this brickwork from the tiles of our Caesar, in the consulship of Propinquus and Ambibulus.
EDCS-31800244.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription by Lucius Bruttidius Augustalis for brickwork made from Imperial tiles, dated to the consulship of Propinquus and Ambibulus.

EDCS-31800245.translation: Lucius Bruttidius Augustalis made this brickwork from the tiles of our Caesar, in the consulship of Propinquus and Ambibulus.
EDCS-31800245.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription by Lucius Bruttidius Augustalis for brickwork made from Imperial tiles, dated to the consulship of Propinquus and Ambibulus.

EDCS-31900256.translation: Lucius Mettius Maximus, son of Lucius, of the Camilia tribe, from Atria, a soldier of the 9th Praetorian Cohort, lies here. He lived for 28 years and served for 11 years, returning the principal of his life to death. 12 feet in front, 16 feet in depth.
EDCS-31900256.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Mettius Maximus, a soldier in the Praetorian Guard, detailing his age, service length, and burial plot dimensions.

EDCS-32200923.translation: In the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus; from the properties of Eurytus (of the Quintiana tribe?).
EDCS-32200923.summary: This inscription records properties belonging to Eurytus, dated to the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-32300370.translation: In the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus; from the estates of Turranius Pr... from Salarese.
EDCS-32300370.summary: This inscription concerns properties from Turranius Pr... in Salarese, dated to the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-32301002.translation: In the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus; from the estates of Pomponius Vitalis, from the Quintian estates of Annus Verus.
EDCS-32301002.summary: This inscription details property transfers from Annus Verus' Quintian estates to Pomponius Vitalis, dated to the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-32301004.translation: From the estates of Agathyrsus, freedman of Augustus, of Quintus Pomponius Ianuaris, in the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-32301004.summary: This inscription records property from Agathyrsus, a freedman of Augustus, belonging to Quintus Pomponius Ianuaris, dated to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-32301015.translation: In the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus; from the figlinae of Aulus Gabinius Successus, from Salarese.
EDCS-32301015.summary: This inscription records tiles from the figlinae of Aulus Gabinius Successus in Salarese, dated to the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-32301016.translation: In the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus; from the figlinae of Cornelius Malia[...], from Salarese. P. P. B.
EDCS-32301016.summary: This inscription records tiles from the figlinae of Cornelius Malia[...] in Salarese, dated to the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-32301017.translation: In the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus, from the estates of Iulius Eutactus of Salarese.
EDCS-32301017.summary: This inscription records a delivery from the estates of Iulius Eutactus, dated to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-32301018.translation: In the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus, from the estates of Iulius Eutactus of Salarese.
EDCS-32301018.summary: This inscription records a delivery from the estates of Iulius Eutactus, dated to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-32301019.translation: In the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus, from the figlinae of Annius Verus, by Publius P. B. of Salarese.
EDCS-32301019.summary: This inscription records a delivery from the figlinae of Annius Verus, attributed to Publius P. B. of Salarese, dated to the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-32301022.translation: In the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus, from the estates of Iulius Eutactus of Salarese.
EDCS-32301022.summary: This inscription records a delivery from the estates of Iulius Eutactus, dated to the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
